topics.title: Casting - Characteristics of Sand
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT404 - MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Casting - Characteristics of Sand

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Sand Casting** (one of the most widely used expendable mold casting processes in KTU syllabus Module 1), the **Characteristics of Sand** refer to the measurable physical, mechanical, and thermal properties of the **molding sand** (also called **foundry sand** or **green sand**) that determine its suitability to form a refractory cavity capable of receiving molten metal without failure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PBMRT404 - Module 1):**
> *Molding sand is a mixture of silica sand (SiO₂), clay (bond), moisture, and special additives. Its characteristics govern the quality, dimensional accuracy, and surface finish of the final casting.*

The **six primary characteristics** of molding sand, as prescribed by the KTU 2024 Scheme, are:

| # | Characteristic | Governing Property |
|---|----------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Strength / Green Strength** | Mechanical load-bearing capacity |
| 2 | **Permeability** | Ability to vent gases |
| 3 | **Moisture Content** | Water percentage |
| 4 | **Refractoriness** | Heat resistance |
| 5 | **Grain Size & Shape** | Surface finish & flow |
| 6 | **Collapsibility** | Shrinkage accommodation |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Building a Sand Castle on the Beach**

Imagine you are building a **sand castle** at the beach:

- If the sand is **too dry**, the castle crumbles → *Low Strength*.
- If the sand is **too wet**, the water cannot drain and the base becomes muddy → *Low Permeability*.
- If you pour boiling water on a dry-sand mold, the walls melt and collapse → *Low Refractoriness*.
- If the sand grains are sharp and large, the castle surface is rough → *Large Grain Size*.
- After the castle dries, it must be easy to break apart for removal → *Good Collapsibility*.

The **molding sand** in a foundry behaves exactly like this sand castle, except that instead of being filled with water, it is filled with **molten metal at 1200°C–1600°C**. Hence, the sand must possess a **specific balance of strength, permeability, refractoriness, and collapsibility** to produce a defect-free casting.

## 1.3 Standard Foundry Sand Composition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical Green Sand Composition (by mass):**
> - **Silica Sand (SiO₂)** : 85% – 95%
> - **Clay (Bentonite)** : 5% – 11%
> - **Water (Moisture)** : 2% – 8%
> - **Additives (Coal dust, dextrin, sea coal)** : < 2%

**Physical Constants used in Foundry Practice:**
- **Density of Silica (SiO₂)** = **2.65 g/cm³**
- **Melting Point of Pure Silica** = **1710°C**
- **Sintering Temperature of Foundry Sand** ≈ **1300°C**
- **Standard Test Temperature** = **Room Temperature (27°C) for green strength tests**
- **P_N (Permeability Number) standard range** = **80 – 200**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Primary Characteristics — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Strength (Green Strength & Dry Strength)

**Definition:** The ability of molding sand to retain its shape and resist deformation under the pressure of molten metal.

- **Green Strength:** Strength of sand in the *moist (as-molded)* condition.
- **Dry Strength:** Strength of sand after *complete drying* in an oven (105°C – 110°C for 2 hours).
- **Hot Strength:** Strength at elevated temperature (above 600°C).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** Insufficient strength causes *sand collapse, mold wall failure, and casting defects like sand drops*.

**Operational Logic:**
1. Clay + water acts as a **bonding bridge** between silica grains.
2. When the sand is rammed, the clay-water film coats each grain.
3. Upon compression, these films bind grains together like cement between stones.
4. **Higher clay content → Higher strength** (but lower permeability and refractoriness).

---

### 2.1.2 Permeability

**Definition:** The ability of molding sand to allow **gases and steam** to escape through the pores when molten metal is poured.

**Why it matters:** Molten metal releases huge volumes of gas (H₂, N₂, CO, CO₂). If trapped, they cause **blow holes, gas porosity, and pinholes** in the casting.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Misconception:** Permeability is *not* the same as porosity. **Porosity** is the void fraction; **Permeability** is the ease of gas flow through those voids.

---

### 2.1.3 Moisture Content

**Definition:** The percentage of water present in the molding sand mixture.

- Controlled between **2% – 8%** (typically ~4%).
- Measured by the **dry-weight method** using a moisture teller.

**Why it matters:**
- Too little water → clay cannot bond → low strength.
- Too much water → steam explosion on pouring → surface defects and reduced permeability.

---

### 2.1.4 Refractoriness

**Definition:** The ability of molding sand to **withstand high temperatures** without melting, sintering, or fusing.

- Determined by the **sintering point** of the sand.
- For steel casting: refractoriness must be **> 1500°C**.
- For cast iron: refractoriness must be **> 1300°C**.

**Why it matters:** If the sand fuses to the casting surface, it forms a hard layer called **burn-on** or **sand fusion defect**, leading to poor surface finish and difficult cleaning.

---

### 2.1.5 Grain Size, Shape & Distribution

| Grain Shape | Code | Flowability | Surface Finish | Strength |
|-------------|------|-------------|----------------|----------|
| **Round** | R | Best | Rough | Lowest |
| **Sub-angular** | SA | Good | Better | Higher |
| **Angular** | A | Poor | Best (fine) | Highest |

**AFS (American Foundrymen's Society) Fineness Number:**
- **AFS No. = Total % of sand retained on standard sieves (cumulative) × Multiplication factor / Total % of sand retained.**
- **Lower AFS Number = Coarser grains.**
- **Higher AFS Number = Finer grains.**

---

### 2.1.6 Collapsibility

**Definition:** The ability of the sand mold to **collapse (deform easily)** after the casting solidifies and cools, allowing the casting to contract freely during solidification.

**Why it matters:** Poor collapsibility causes:
- **Hot tears / Hot cracking** (solidification cracks)
- **Internal stresses** in the casting
- **Difficulty in shake-out** (removal of casting from mold)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hot Strength is the INVERSE of Collapsibility.** When hot strength is high (e.g., due to excessive clay), collapsibility is poor. Foundrymen balance both by using additives like **wood flour, sawdust, or cereal binders**.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbols | Unit | Application |
|---|--------------------|---------|------|-------------|
| 1 | **Moisture Content (%)** = $\dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$ | $W_w$ = Wet weight, $W_d$ = Dry weight | % | Foundry moisture test |
| 2 | **Permeability Number (P_N)** = $\dfrac{V \cdot h}{A \cdot P \cdot t}$ | $V$ = Volume of air, $h$ = Specimen height, $A$ = Cross-section area, $P$ = Pressure, $t$ = Time | dimensionless | Gas venting ability |
| 3 | **Green Compression Strength (GCS)** = $\dfrac{F}{A}$ | $F$ = Rupture force, $A$ = Cross-sectional area | N/cm² or kPa | Mold strength test |
| 4 | **Dry Compression Strength (DCS)** = $\dfrac{F_{dry}}{A}$ | $F_{dry}$ = Rupture force of dry sample | N/cm² or kPa | Dry strength test |
| 5 | **Shear Strength (τ)** = $\dfrac{F_s}{A}$ | $F_s$ = Shear force | N/cm² | Lateral mold failure |
| 6 | **AFS Fineness Number** = $\dfrac{\sum (\% \text{ retained} \times \text{Multiplier})}{\sum \% \text{ retained}}$ | Sieve analysis data | dimensionless | Grain fineness |
| 7 | **Bulk Density (ρ_b)** = $\dfrac{m}{V}$ | $m$ = mass, $V$ = volume | g/cm³ | Compactness of sand |
| 8 | **Specific Surface Area (S)** = $\dfrac{6}{\rho \cdot d} \cdot N$ | $d$ = avg. grain dia, $\rho$ = density, $N$ = no. of grains | cm²/g | Surface area calculation |
| 9 | **Porosity (ε)** = $1 - \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_s}$ | $\rho_b$ = bulk density, $\rho_s$ = true density | dimensionless | Void fraction |
| 10 | **Shrinkage Allowance** = $\dfrac{L_{mold} - L_{cast}}{L_{cast}} \times 100$ | $L_{mold}$ = Mold length, $L_{cast}$ = Casting length | % | Pattern allowance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:**
> The permeability formula and AFS number are used daily in **foundries of Kerala (e.g., Steel and Industrial Forgings Ltd, Thrissur; Kerala State Coir Machinery; local MSME foundries)** to qualify incoming silica sand and to control mold quality. Mechanical engineers use these to design gating systems, risers, and chills for industrial castings like pump housings, valve bodies, and machine tool beds.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Permeability Number ($P_N$)

The **Permeability Number** is the most important sand characteristic tested in KTU laboratory viva and university exam numericals. It is derived from **Darcy's Law** for fluid flow through porous media.

### Step 1: Start with Darcy's Law

Darcy's Law states that the volumetric flow rate of a fluid through a porous medium is:

$$
Q = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}
$$

where:
- $Q$ = Volumetric flow rate (cm³/s)
- $k$ = Permeability coefficient (cm²)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area of the specimen (cm²)
- $\Delta P$ = Pressure difference across the specimen (dyne/cm²)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity of the fluid (poise)
- $L$ = Length of the specimen (cm)

### Step 2: Rearrange to Solve for $k$

$$
k = \dfrac{Q \cdot \mu \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta P}
$$

This gives permeability in **cm²**, which is too small for foundry use.

### Step 3: Apply Foundry Standard Conditions

The American Foundrymen's Society (AFS) standardizes permeability by:
- Using **air** as the test fluid (so $\mu$ is fixed).
- Standard specimen geometry: **$A$ = 20 cm²**, **$L$ = 5.08 cm (2 inches)**.
- Standard **orifice** in the test apparatus gives $\Delta P$ corresponding to a defined flow rate.

Under these standard conditions, the dimensional analysis simplifies $k$ to a **dimensionless Permeability Number**:

$$
P_N = \dfrac{V \cdot h}{A \cdot P \cdot t}
$$

where:
- $V$ = Volume of air passing through (cm³)
- $h$ = Height of specimen (cm)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area (cm²)
- $P$ = Air pressure (cm of water)
- $t$ = Time (minutes)

### Step 4: Standard Numerical Substitution

For a standard AFS permeability specimen:

$$
A = 20 \text{ cm}^2, \quad h = 5.08 \text{ cm}
$$

Substituting:

$$
P_N = \dfrac{V \cdot 5.08}{20 \cdot P \cdot t} = \dfrac{V \cdot 0.254}{P \cdot t}
$$

### Step 5: Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem:** In a permeability test, 2000 cm³ of air passed through a standard sand specimen (area = 20 cm², height = 5.08 cm) in 4 minutes, under a pressure of 10 cm of water. Calculate the Permeability Number.

**Solution:**

$$
P_N = \dfrac{V \cdot h}{A \cdot P \cdot t}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
P_N = \dfrac{2000 \times 5.08}{20 \times 10 \times 4}
$$

$$
P_N = \dfrac{10160}{800}
$$

$$
P_N = 12.7
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical Permeability Range in Foundries:** $P_N = 80 - 200$ for steel casting; $P_N = 100 - 300$ for cast iron. Lower values indicate dense, fine sand; higher values indicate coarse, open sand.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Moisture Content

### Step 1: Definition Setup

Moisture content $M$ is the ratio of water mass lost on drying to the mass of dry sand:

$$
M = \dfrac{\text{Mass of water}}{\text{Mass of dry sand}} \times 100
$$

### Step 2: Express Water Mass

The water lost on drying is the difference between wet and dry weights:

$$
m_{water} = W_w - W_d
$$

### Step 3: Substitute

$$
M = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100
$$

### Step 4: Worked Example

> **Problem:** A green sand sample weighs 200 g in the wet condition. After oven drying at 110°C for 2 hours, it weighs 184 g. Find the moisture content.

**Solution:**

$$
M = \dfrac{200 - 184}{184} \times 100
$$

$$
M = \dfrac{16}{184} \times 100
$$

$$
M = 8.696 \% \approx 8.7\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Excess Moisture Alert:** A value of 8.7% is on the higher side. For cast iron (typical KTU exam scenario), 4–6% is ideal. The instructor may deduct marks if the student does not comment on the suitability.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: AFS Fineness Number

### Step 1: Sieve Analysis Data

A representative sand sample is passed through a stack of standard sieves. The weight retained on each sieve is recorded.

| Sieve No. | Multiplier (AFS Standard) | % Retained |
|-----------|---------------------------|------------|
| 6 | 3 | 0.0 |
| 12 | 5 | 0.5 |
| 20 | 10 | 4.0 |
| 30 | 20 | 15.0 |
| 40 | 30 | 30.0 |
| 50 | 40 | 25.0 |
| 70 | 50 | 12.0 |
| 100 | 70 | 8.0 |
| 140 | 100 | 3.5 |
| 200 | 140 | 1.5 |
| 270 | 200 | 0.5 |
| Pan | 300 | 0.0 |

### Step 2: Multiply and Sum

$$
\sum (\% \text{ retained} \times \text{Multiplier}) = (0.5 \times 5) + (4 \times 10) + (15 \times 20) + (30 \times 30) + (25 \times 40) + (12 \times 50) + (8 \times 70) + (3.5 \times 100) + (1.5 \times 140) + (0.5 \times 200)
$$

$$
= 2.5 + 40 + 300 + 900 + 1000 + 600 + 560 + 350 + 210 + 100
$$

$$
= 4062.5
$$

### Step 3: Sum of % Retained

$$
\sum \% \text{ retained} = 0.5 + 4 + 15 + 30 + 25 + 12 + 8 + 3.5 + 1.5 + 0.5 = 100
$$

### Step 4: Compute AFS Number

$$
\text{AFS Fineness No.} = \dfrac{4062.5}{100} = 40.6 \approx 41
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** An AFS number of **41** corresponds to a **medium-fine sand**, typically used for **cast iron and non-ferrous castings**. Steel casting usually requires AFS 25–35 (coarser).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Test Apparatus | Standard Value (Cast Iron) | Defect if Failed |
|----------|----------------|----------------------------|------------------|
| Green Compression Strength | Universal Sand Tester | **35 – 50 kPa** | Mold collapse, sand drop |
| Dry Compression Strength | Universal Sand Tester + Oven | **80 – 150 kPa** | Surface erosion |
| Permeability Number | Permeability Ramming Device | **100 – 200** | Blow holes, gas porosity |
| Moisture Content | Moisture Teller / Oven | **4% – 6%** | Steam explosion, low strength |
| AFS Fineness | Sieve Shaker | **40 – 70** | Rough surface or poor venting |
| Refractoriness | Sintering Test | **> 1300°C** | Burn-on, sand fusion |
| Collapsibility | Special Restrained Test | Qualitative (Good/Poor) | Hot tearing, cracking |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Sand Testing in a Foundry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Silica Sand Sample] --> B[Moisture Test]

    A --> D[Strength Test]
    A --> E[Permeability Test]
    A --> F[Refractoriness Test]

    B --> G[Moisture Content 4-6 percent]
    C --> H[AFS Fineness Number]
    D --> I[Green and Dry Strength]
    E --> J[Permeability Number P N]
    F --> K[Sintering Point Celsius]

    G --> L{Quality Check}
    H --> L
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L

    L -->|Pass| M[Approved for Molding Line]
    L -->|Fail| N[Reclaim and Recondition]

    M --> O[Pattern Placement]
    O --> P[Sand Ramming]
    P --> Q[Mold Hardness]
    Q --> R[Pouring Molten Metal]
    R --> S[Solidification]
    S --> T[Shake Out]
    T --> U[Final Casting]
```

## 4.2 Hierarchy of Sand Characteristics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Characteristics of Molding Sand] --> S1[Mechanical Properties]
    R --> S2[Thermal Properties]
    R --> S3[Physical Properties]
    R --> S4[Chemical Properties]

    S1 --> P1[Green Strength]
    S1 --> P2[Dry Strength]
    S1 --> P3[Hot Strength]
    S1 --> P4[Hardness]

    S2 --> T1[Refractoriness]
    S2 --> T2[Thermal Conductivity]
    S2 --> T3[Collapsibility]

    S3 --> PH1[Permeability]
    S3 --> PH2[Flowability]
    S3 --> PH3[Grain Size and Shape]
    S3 --> PH4[Moisture Content]
    S3 --> PH5[Bulk Density]

    S4 --> C1[Chemical Stability]
    S4 --> C2[Acid Demand Value]
    S4 --> C3[pH Value]
```

## 4.3 Cause-Effect Diagram: Casting Defects from Sand Properties

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sand Property Deficiency] --> B[Low Permeability]
    A --> C[Low Strength]
    A --> D[Low Refractoriness]
    A --> E[Excess Moisture]
    A --> F[Poor Collapsibility]

    B --> G[Blow Holes and Gas Porosity]
    C --> H[Mold Collapse and Sand Drop]
    D --> I[Burn On and Sand Fusion]
    E --> J[Steam Explosions and Pinholes]
    F --> K[Hot Tears and Cracking]
```

## 4.4 Process Flow: From Raw Sand to Molding Sand

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Raw Silica Sand] --> B1[Screening and Grading]
    B1 --> C1[Addition of Bentonite Clay]
    C1 --> D1[Addition of Water 4-6 percent]
    D1 --> E1[Addition of Additives]
    E1 --> F1[Mulling Process]
    F1 --> G1[Quality Tests]
    G1 --> H1[Ready Molding Sand]

    H1 --> I1[Mold Making]
    I1 --> J1[Pouring Metal]
    J1 --> K1[Shake Out]
    K1 --> L1[Reclamation of Used Sand]
    L1 --> C1
```
