# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define the term "Permeability" of molding sand. Why is it an essential characteristic in sand casting?**

**Model Answer:**

**Permeability** is the property of molding sand that allows gases, steam, and air to escape through the pores of the sand mold when molten metal is poured into the cavity.

It is essential because:
1. When molten metal contacts the mold, it generates large volumes of gases (H₂, CO, CO₂, N₂) due to moisture evaporation and volatilization of additives.
2. If these gases are **not vented**, they become trapped in the solidifying metal, causing defects such as **blow holes, pinholes, and gas porosity**.
3. Permeability depends on **grain size, grain shape, clay content, and moisture content**. Coarser, rounder grains yield higher permeability, while excessive clay reduces it.
4. It is measured by the **Permeability Number ($P_N$)** using a standard permeability testing apparatus, with the typical foundry range being **$P_N = 80 - 200$**.

**Tags:** CO1, Bloom Level: Remember/Understand

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**List and briefly explain any THREE primary characteristics of molding sand.**

**Model Answer:**

The three primary characteristics of molding sand are:

**1. Strength (Green Strength):** The ability of the sand mixture to retain its shape and resist deformation under the load of the molten metal. It depends on the **clay and moisture content**. Insufficient strength leads to mold wall failure and sand-drop defects.

**2. Refractoriness:** The ability of the molding sand to **withstand high temperatures** without melting, sintering, or chemical reaction with the molten metal. It is determined by the silica content (SiO₂ ≥ 95% for steel casting) and the absence of low-melting impurities like feldspar, mica, and lime.

**3. Collapsibility:** The ability of the sand mold to **deform or collapse** easily after the casting has solidified, thereby allowing the casting to contract freely and avoid **hot tearing** or **cracking**. It is enhanced by adding combustible additives like wood flour or sawdust.

**Tags:** CO1, Bloom Level: Understand

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **six important characteristics of molding sand** used in a foundry. State the standard test apparatus and acceptable value range for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A green sand specimen is tested in a standard permeability apparatus. The following data is observed:
- Volume of air passed, $V = 1500 \text{ cm}^3$
- Specimen height, $h = 5.08 \text{ cm}$
- Cross-sectional area, $A = 20 \text{ cm}^2$
- Pressure head, $P = 8 \text{ cm of water}$
- Time, $t = 3 \text{ minutes}$

Calculate the **Permeability Number** and comment on the suitability of the sand for **cast iron molding**.

---

### Model Answer for Question A

#### Part (a) — Solution

The six important characteristics of molding sand are:

**1. Strength:** Resistance to deformation under the pressure of molten metal. Tested using a **Universal Sand Strength Testing Machine**. Acceptable Green Compression Strength (GCS) is **35 – 50 kPa** for cast iron.

**2. Permeability:** Ability to allow gases to escape. Tested using a **Permeability Ramming Device**. Standard $P_N$ range is **100 – 200** for cast iron.

**3. Moisture Content:** Percentage of water in the sand. Tested using a **Moisture Teller** or by **oven drying**. Acceptable range is **4% – 6%** for cast iron.

**4. Refractoriness:** Heat resistance without fusing. Tested by the **sintering point test** using a furnace. Required sintering temperature **> 1300°C** for cast iron and **> 1500°C** for steel.

**5. Grain Size and Shape (Fineness):** Distribution of grain sizes. Tested using a **sieve shaker** with AFS standard sieves. AFS Fineness Number between **40 – 70** is preferred for cast iron.

**6. Collapsibility:** Ability to deform after solidification. Tested qualitatively by **restrained casting test** or by observation during shake-out. Enhanced by adding **wood flour** or **cereal binders**.

#### Part (b) — Solution

Given:
- $V = 1500 \text{ cm}^3$
- $h = 5.08 \text{ cm}$
- $A = 20 \text{ cm}^2$
- $P = 8 \text{ cm of water}$
- $t = 3 \text{ min}$

Using the Permeability formula:

$$
P_N = \dfrac{V \cdot h}{A \cdot P \cdot t}
$$

Substituting:

$$
P_N = \dfrac{1500 \times 5.08}{20 \times 8 \times 3}
$$

$$
P_N = \dfrac{7620}{480}
$$

$$
P_N = 15.875 \approx 15.9
$$

**Comment on Suitability:** A Permeability Number of **15.9 is extremely low** and well below the recommended range of **100 – 200** for cast iron molding. The sand has **insufficient permeability** and would lead to **blow holes and gas porosity** in the casting. The sand must be **reconditioned by reducing clay content, using coarser grains, or adding surfacing agents** to increase $P_N$ to the acceptable range.

**Tags:** CO1, CO2, Bloom Level: Apply/Analyze

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define **Green Strength, Dry Strength, and Hot Strength** of molding sand. How are they measured? Discuss the effect of clay content and moisture on each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The following sieve analysis data was obtained for a molding sand sample. Calculate the **AFS Fineness Number** and identify whether the sand is suitable for **steel casting**.

| Sieve No. | Multiplier | % Retained |
|-----------|------------|------------|
| 30 | 20 | 2 |
| 40 | 30 | 18 |
| 50 | 40 | 30 |
| 70 | 50 | 25 |
| 100 | 70 | 15 |
| 140 | 100 | 6 |
| 200 | 140 | 2.5 |
| 270 | 200 | 1 |
| Pan | 300 | 0.5 |

---

### Model Answer for Question B

#### Part (a) — Solution

**1. Green Strength:**
The strength of the molding sand in its **moist (as-molded) condition** at room temperature. It is the most critical strength parameter because the mold must retain its shape during handling, closing, and pouring.

- **Measurement:** Using a **Universal Sand Strength Testing Machine** with a standard cylindrical specimen (5.08 cm diameter, 5.08 cm height). Load is applied gradually until failure. The compressive load divided by cross-sectional area gives the **Green Compression Strength (GCS)**.
- **Effect of clay:** More bentonite clay increases green strength up to an optimum (~10%); beyond that, permeability drops sharply.
- **Effect of moisture:** Green strength increases with moisture up to a peak (~6%) and then decreases.

**2. Dry Strength:**
The strength of the sand after **complete drying in an oven at 105°C – 110°C for 2 hours**. It represents the bond strength of clay alone (without water).

- **Measurement:** A green specimen is dried, then loaded in the same Universal Sand Tester. The load at failure gives **Dry Compression Strength (DCS)**.
- **Effect of clay:** Higher clay content sharply increases dry strength.
- **Effect of moisture:** Moisture is removed, so it has no direct effect on dry strength; it determines how well the specimen can be dried without cracking.

**3. Hot Strength:**
The strength of the sand at **elevated temperatures (typically 600°C – 1000°C)**, simulating the conditions in the mold cavity during pouring.

- **Measurement:** A hot-strength tester with a small furnace heats the specimen to the test temperature, and load is applied.
- **Effect of clay:** Excessive clay increases hot strength, which is **undesirable** because it reduces collapsibility and causes hot tearing.
- **Effect of moisture:** Moisture evaporates below 100°C, so it has no effect on hot strength.

#### Part (b) — Solution

Given sieve analysis data, compute the products and sum.

| Sieve No. | Multiplier (M) | % Retained (R) | R × M |
|-----------|----------------|----------------|-------|
| 30 | 20 | 2 | 40 |
| 40 | 30 | 18 | 540 |
| 50 | 40 | 30 | 1200 |
| 70 | 50 | 25 | 1250 |
| 100 | 70 | 15 | 1050 |
| 140 | 100 | 6 | 600 |
| 200 | 140 | 2.5 | 350 |
| 270 | 200 | 1 | 200 |
| Pan | 300 | 0.5 | 150 |
| **Total** | | **100** | **5380** |

Applying the formula:

$$
\text{AFS Fineness No.} = \dfrac{\sum (R \times M)}{\sum R}
$$

$$
\text{AFS Fineness No.} = \dfrac{5380}{100} = 53.8 \approx 54
$$

**Suitability Comment:** An AFS Fineness Number of **54** is **medium-fine sand**, which is suitable for **cast iron and non-ferrous castings**. However, **steel casting** typically requires a coarser sand with AFS Fineness Number between **25 and 40** for better permeability and resistance to high temperatures. Hence, this sand is **NOT ideally suitable for steel casting** and should either be re-screened with coarser sieves or blended with coarser silica sand.

**Tags:** CO1, CO2, Bloom Level: Apply/Analyze

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Sand Characteristics Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Permeability with Porosity:** Many students incorrectly write "permeability is the void fraction" in 3-mark questions. **Permeability** is the *ease of gas flow*; **Porosity** is the *fraction of voids*. They are related but not identical. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Units in Numericals:** A permeability calculation without stating $P_N = 15.9$ as a *dimensionless* number loses 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Not Commenting on Suitability:** KTU examiners award **partial marks** for "interpretation" of numerical answers. A calculated AFS number without a "suitable / not suitable" comment loses 2 marks.
>
> 4. **Mixing Up Green and Dry Strength:** Green strength = moist condition. Dry strength = oven-dried at 105°C. Examiners often test this distinction.
>
> 5. **Ignoring Hot Strength vs Collapsibility Trade-off:** The single most-tested concept in 14-mark questions. Always state that **high hot strength = poor collapsibility = hot tearing risk**.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to state the apparatus used:** "Strength" without mentioning "Universal Sand Strength Testing Machine" or "AFS Standard Specimen" loses 1 mark in 7-mark sub-parts.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Molding sand is a refractory granular material (mostly **silica SiO₂**) bonded by **clay (bentonite)** and **moisture**, used to form molds in **sand casting**.
- **Composition (Green Sand):** **85–95% Silica + 5–11% Bentonite + 2–8% Water + <2% Additives**.
- **Six Primary Characteristics:** Strength, Permeability, Moisture Content, Refractoriness, Grain Size & Shape, Collapsibility.
- **Strength Types:** Green (moist) → Dry (oven) → Hot (elevated). Each measured on a **Universal Sand Strength Testing Machine**.
- **Permeability Number $P_N$** = $V \cdot h / (A \cdot P \cdot t)$, dimensionless. Ideal range: **80–200**.
- **Moisture Content** = $(W_w - W_d) / W_d \times 100$, ideal **4–6%** for cast iron.
- **AFS Fineness Number** uses cumulative sieving; **lower = coarser**, **higher = finer**.
- **Refractoriness** must exceed the pouring temperature of metal: **>1500°C** for steel, **>1300°C** for cast iron.
- **Grain Shape:** Round (best flow, low strength) vs Angular (best strength, poor flow) vs Sub-angular (balanced).
- **Collapsibility is inversely related to Hot Strength** — high hot strength causes hot tearing.
- **Common Defects due to poor sand characteristics:** Blow holes (low permeability), sand drop (low strength), burn-on (low refractoriness), hot tears (low collapsibility), pinholes (excess moisture).
- **Standard Tests:** Permeability test, compression test, sieve analysis, moisture teller, sintering point test.
- **Density of silica** = **2.65 g/cm³**; **Melting point of pure SiO₂** = **1710°C**.
- **KTU Exam Tip:** Always quote the **apparatus name**, **standard test specimen dimensions (5.08 cm dia, 5.08 cm height)**, and **acceptable value range** in 7-mark answers.
- **Key Equation to Memorize for KTU:**
$$
P_N = \dfrac{V \cdot h}{A \cdot P \cdot t} \quad ; \quad M = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100
$$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Patterns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT404 - MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Patterns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 1: PATTERNS IN FOUNDRY

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PATTERN (KTU Definition):** A pattern is a replica of the **final cast product** (with certain modifications/allowances) used to create a **cavity** in the sand mould, into which molten metal is poured to obtain the desired casting. It is the **primary tooling** of any sand casting operation and dictates the geometry, surface finish, and dimensional accuracy of the casting.

A pattern is **not** an exact copy of the finished casting. It is a **slightly oversized, slightly modified master tool** that incorporates engineering allowances (draft, shrinkage, machining, etc.) to compensate for the physical and metallurgical phenomena that occur during moulding, pouring, solidification, and cooling.

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy 🎂
> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a **cake mould** in a bakery. The plastic cake mould (pattern) is not the cake itself. It is shaped **slightly larger** because the cake shrinks while baking. It has **drafted (tapered) walls** so the cake slides out easily. The baker applies **release agent** (akin to parting compound) so the cake (casting) does not stick. Just as no bakery can produce a cake without a mould, no foundry can produce a casting without a pattern — it is the **DNA of the casting**.

### 1.3 Functions of a Pattern
A pattern must serve **six critical functions** in the foundry:
1. **Cavity Formation** — Provides the negative space into which molten metal flows.
2. **Shape & Size Definition** — Establishes the geometry and dimensions of the final casting.
3. **Cope & Drag Surface Definition** — Splits the mould into two halves (where applicable).
4. **Core Support & Placement** — Locates the cores via *core prints* to form internal cavities.
5. **Runner & Riser Provision** — Incorporates channels for metal flow and risers for feeding.
6. **Ejection & Stripping** — Permits clean withdrawal from the sand mould without damage.

### 1.4 Standard Pattern Terminology
- **Impression:** The part of the pattern that actually forms the casting cavity.
- **Core Print:** A projection on the pattern that forms the seat (support) for the core in the mould. It does NOT appear on the final casting.
- **Parting Line / Parting Plane:** The line/plane that separates cope (upper) and drag (lower) halves of the mould.
- **Draft / Taper:** The slight taper (1°–3°) on vertical walls of the pattern to allow clean withdrawal from sand.
- **Drag Half:** The lower half of a pattern.
- **Cope Half:** The upper half of a pattern.
- **Cheek:** The intermediate section of a three-part mould.

### 1.5 Pattern Color Coding (IS 12476 / KTU Convention)
> [!NOTE]
> Foundries follow a **standard colour code** to identify machined and unmachined surfaces on patterns. This is a **direct KTU examination question**.

| Color | Surface Type | Indication |
|-------|--------------|------------|
| **Black** | Un-machined (as-cast) | No further machining needed |
| **Red** | Surface to be machined | Machining allowance provided |
| **Yellow** | Core prints | Locates cores in the mould |
| **Blue** | Surface to be polished/chipped | Extra finishing allowance |
| **Green** | Seats for loose pieces or inserts | Loose pieces location |

---

## 2. Material Used for Patterns

The choice of pattern material directly affects **dimensional accuracy, surface finish, pattern life, and cost**. KTU examiners frequently test this topic.

| Material | Relative Cost | Pattern Life | Application / Use |
|----------|--------------|--------------|-------------------|
| **Wood (Soft/Hard)** | Low | Short (≈ 100 castings) | Prototype, small-batch, large patterns (loam, skeleton) |
| **Aluminium Alloys** | Medium | Medium (≈ 5,000–10,000) | Medium-volume production, light, easy to machine |
| **Cast Iron / Steel** | High | Long (≈ 50,000+) | Mass production, high-strength, wear-resistant |
| **Plaster / Gypsum** | Low | One-time use | Match plates, master patterns for foundry tools |
| **Plastics (Epoxy, Phenolic, Urethane, Polystyrene)** | Medium | Moderate | Match plates, medium runs, excellent surface finish |
| **Brass / Bronze** | High | Long | Small intricate patterns requiring fine detail |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** **Wood** is the most economical and commonly used pattern material. **Cast iron and steel** are preferred for large-volume production. **Plastics** offer the best compromise of surface finish and cost for medium runs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — Types of Patterns & Allowances

## 2.1 Classification of Patterns (KTU High-Yield Taxonomy)

Patterns are classified based on their **construction, complexity, moulding method, and number of pieces**. This is one of the **most frequently asked 14-mark questions** in KTU ESE.

### 🔹 Level 1: Simple / Single-Piece Patterns
A pattern made from a **single solid piece**, used when:
- The pattern can be withdrawn from the mould in **one direction only**.
- The casting has a **flat parting plane** with no undercuts.

**Advantages:** Simple, low cost, easy to manufacture.
**Disadvantages:** Limited to simple castings, requires careful ramming and rapping.

> *Example:* Simple rectangular block, gear blank, simple flange.

### 🔹 Level 2: Two-Piece (Split) Patterns
The pattern is split into **cope half and drag half** along the parting line. A **dowel pin** ensures alignment.
- Used when the parting line passes through the **middle** of the casting.
- Both halves are placed in their respective mould boxes during moulding.

> *Example:* Flanged pipe, simple coupling, pulley.

### 🔹 Level 3: Multi-Piece (Three-Piece) Patterns
The pattern has **cope, drag, and cheek (middle) sections**. Used for castings with **complex geometry and intricate parting**.
> *Example:* Castings with projecting flanges at intermediate heights.

### 🔹 Level 4: Loose-Piece Patterns
When a small portion of the pattern **cannot be withdrawn** due to undercuts, it is made as a **loose insert** (loose piece / loose bar) attached by **wooden pins**.
- The loose piece is first withdrawn, then the main pattern.
- Disadvantage: **Operator-dependent**; requires skilled moulder.

> *Example:* T-shaped, L-shaped, or cruciform castings.

### 🔹 Level 5: Gated Patterns
Multiple identical patterns (cavities) are **joined through a common runner system** in a single pattern assembly.
- Used for **mass production** to save moulding time and increase productivity.
- Also called **gang / multiple / cluster patterns**.

> *Example:* Small brackets, connecting rod blanks, hinges — cast in groups of 4, 6, 8, or 16.

### 🔹 Level 6: Match-Plate Patterns
Both cope and drag halves of the pattern are **rigidly mounted on opposite sides of a metal/plastic plate** (the match plate). The plate itself contains the **runner and gating system**.
- Mounted on a **match plate molding machine** for high productivity.
- Ideal for **medium to high volume** small castings.

> *Example:* Connecting rods, small brackets, automotive components.

### 🔹 Level 7: Cope and Drag Patterns
A **modified version of match-plate** where cope and drag halves are mounted on **separate plates** (cope plate and drag plate). Used for larger castings than match-plate patterns.

### 🔹 Level 8: Skeleton (Frame) Patterns
Used for **very large castings** (e.g., lathe beds, machine tool housings). Only the **outline frame (skeleton)** is made, and the cavity is formed by **striking the sand with a strickle board** that sweeps the contour.
- Economical for one-off large castings.

> *Example:* Lathe bed castings, large gear housings.

### 🔹 Level 9: Sweep Patterns
A **sweep (template)** is revolved about a fixed axis to generate the **rotationally symmetric cavity**. Used for cylindrical castings of large size.
> *Example:* Large bell-shaped castings, large pulleys.

### 🔹 Level 10: Follow-Board Patterns
The pattern is mounted on a **wooden board (follow board)** that follows the contour of the parting surface. Used for castings with **irregular parting surfaces**.
- The follow board helps to compact the sand on the irregular parting and is removed before pouring.

> *Example:* Curved or twisted castings.

### 🔹 Level 11: Segmental Patterns
A **section of the full pattern** is used to mould the entire casting by **repeating the segment in rotation**. Saves material and machining for large circular castings.
> *Example:* Large gear rims, large flange rings.

### 🔹 Level 12: Shell Patterns
A thin metal shell (1–3 mm) made by **investment casting** is used as a permanent pattern.
- Long life, excellent finish, used in high-volume automated foundries.

---

## 2.2 Pattern Allowances (The Heart of the Topic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pattern Allowance** is the **deliberate dimensional deviation** built into the pattern to compensate for various physical, thermal, and processing phenomena. KTU examiner often asks: *"Explain any six pattern allowances with diagrams."* — typically worth **7 marks**.

### Allowance 1: Shrinkage Allowance (Contraction Allowance)
- **Definition:** Compensation for the **volumetric contraction** of metal as it cools from solidification temperature to room temperature.
- **Magnitude:** Depends on the metal (see table).
- **Application:** Pattern dimensions are made **larger** than the final casting by the shrinkage percentage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why shrinkage occurs:** During solidification, metal atoms rearrange from a disordered liquid to an ordered crystalline solid. As atoms pack more closely, **volume decreases**. Further cooling from freezing point to room temperature causes **thermal contraction**.

| Metal | Shrinkage Allowance (%) |
|-------|------------------------|
| Cast Iron (Gray) | 1.0 % |
| Cast Iron (White) | 2.0 % |
| Steel (Carbon) | 2.0 % |
| Brass | 1.5 % |
| Bronze | 1.5 % |
| Aluminum Alloys | 1.25 % |
| Magnesium Alloys | 1.2 % |
| Lead | 0.5 % |
| Zinc | 1.25 % |

> **Rule of Thumb (KTU):** A **1 m long cast iron bar** requires pattern length of **1.010 m**.

### Allowance 2: Machining Allowance (Finish Allowance)
- **Definition:** Extra material provided on the pattern to allow for **subsequent machining** of critical surfaces to achieve precise dimensions and finish.
- **Magnitude:** **1.5 mm to 8 mm** depending on casting size, metal, and machining operation.
- **Indication on pattern:** Marked with **RED** colour.

> **Note:** Non-machined (as-cast) surfaces need NO machining allowance.

### Allowance 3: Draft (Taper) Allowance
- **Definition:** Slight **taper on vertical walls** of the pattern to enable clean withdrawal from the sand mould without damaging the cavity edges.
- **Magnitude:**
  - **External surfaces:** 1° to 3° (typically 1.5°)
  - **Internal surfaces (cavity):** 3° to 5° (typically 3°)
- **Mechanism:** When the pattern is rapped (lightly tapped) before withdrawal, the cavity widens marginally — the draft taper accommodates this.
- **If draft is excessive:** Casting becomes wedge-shaped, requires more machining.

> *Mnemonic:* "**E**xternal **1-3°, I**nternal **3-5°**" (E-I-I-I).

### Allowance 4: Shaking (Rapping) Allowance
- **Definition:** To facilitate pattern withdrawal, the moulder **raps (taps)** the pattern with a rapping rod to slightly **vibrate and expand** the sand cavity. This causes the casting to be **slightly larger** than the pattern.
- **Magnitude:** Usually **included within draft** for external surfaces, but may require an **extra 0.5 mm to 2 mm** on the deep internal sections of the pattern.
- **Compensation:** Pattern is made slightly smaller on internal deep cavities.

### Allowance 5: Distortion (Camber) Allowance
- **Definition:** Compensation for **warpage** of the casting during cooling due to **non-uniform shrinkage** of different sections.
- **Example:** A flat, slender casting tends to **bow or twist** because thin sections cool faster than thick sections.
- **Pattern is made "oppositely distorted"** — pre-bent in the opposite direction to neutralize the cast warpage.
> *Example:* Long flat castings, T-sections, U-sections.

### Allowance 6: Finishing (Grinding) Allowance
- **Definition:** Extra material provided on critical surfaces that will be **ground, polished, or chipped** to high precision.
- **Magnitude:** 1.5 mm to 6 mm additional over and above machining allowance.
- **Indication on pattern:** Marked with **BLUE** colour.

### Allowance 7: Mould / Draw (Stripping) Allowance
- Related to draft but accounts for the **slight dimensional changes** that occur due to the moisture content and compression of the sand mould.

### Allowance 8: Patternmaker's Shrinkage Rule
A **slightly oversized ruler** used by the pattern maker so that all dimensions, when read normally, are **automatically enlarged** by the required shrinkage percentage.
- E.g., for cast iron (1% shrinkage), a 1 m shrink rule actually measures **1.01 m** to **look like 1 m** to the eye.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick-Recall Table — Pattern Allowances**

| Allowance | Purpose | Magnitude | Direction (Pattern vs Casting) |
|-----------|---------|-----------|--------------------------------|
| Shrinkage | Volume contraction on cooling | 0.5%–2.5% (metal dependent) | **Larger** than casting |
| Machining | For subsequent machining | 1.5–8 mm | **Larger** than casting |
| Draft | Pattern withdrawal from sand | 1°–5° (° angle) | **Tapered** walls |
| Shaking | Compensation for rapping | 0.5–2 mm (internal) | **Smaller** on deep internals |
| Distortion | Counter warpage | Variable | **Oppositely curved** |
| Finishing | For grinding/polishing | 1.5–6 mm | **Larger** than casting |
| Stripping | Sand mould release | Tiny (included in draft) | **Tapered** walls |

---

## 2.3 Cores in Pattern Making

> [!NOTE]
> **Core:** A sand mass (or ceramic) placed in the mould cavity to form **internal hollow sections, undercuts, and complex internal features** of the casting. Cores are **NOT formed by the pattern** directly — they are placed using **core prints** on the pattern.

### Core Terminology
- **Core Print:** Projection on the pattern that forms a seat to hold the core.
- **Core Box:** A wooden/metal box in which the core sand is rammed to form the core.
- **Core Sand:** Specially bonded sand (with oil, resin, or sodium silicate binder) for high strength.
- **Core Vent:** Thin vent hole in the core to allow gases to escape during pouring.
- **Chaplets:** Metallic (steel/iron) supports used to **hold the core in position** inside the mould against buoyancy forces of molten metal.

> **Chaplet Formula (Buoyancy):** The buoyant force on a sand core in liquid metal must be supported by chaplets.
> $$F_{buoyancy} = V_{core} \times (\rho_{metal} - \rho_{core})$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

| Equation | Description | Application |
|----------|-------------|-------------|
| $$L_{pattern} = L_{casting} \times (1 + s)$$ | Pattern length with shrinkage | $s$ = shrinkage fraction |
| $$A_{machining} = \pi D \times t$$ | Machining area on a cylindrical surface | $D$ = diameter, $t$ = allowance thickness |
| $$V_{machining} = A_{surface} \times t$$ | Volume of material to be removed | Cuboidal sections |
| $$F_{buoyancy} = \rho_{metal} \cdot g \cdot V_{displaced}$$ | Buoyant force on core | Core/chaplet design |
| $$\theta_{draft} = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\Delta h}{H}\right)$$ | Draft angle calculation | $H$ = depth, $\Delta h$ = taper height |
| $$\eta_{mould} = \frac{V_{casting}}{V_{mould\ sand}}$$ | Mould yield (volumetric) | Productivity analysis |
| $$t_{solidification} = K \cdot \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^2$$ | Chvorinov's rule | Cooling time estimate |

> **Solved Numerical Example (Shrinkage):**
> A cast iron gear blank must have an outer diameter of **400 mm**. Shrinkage allowance for gray cast iron is **1.0%**. What is the pattern diameter?
> $$D_{pattern} = D_{casting} \times (1 + s) = 400 \times (1 + 0.01) = 400 \times 1.01 = \mathbf{404\ mm}$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Calculations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Numerical Derivations (Step-by-Step)

### 📐 Problem 1: Total Allowances on a Cast Iron Flange
**Given:**
- Casting outer diameter: $D = 300$ mm
- Casting height: $H = 150$ mm
- Material: Gray Cast Iron
- Shrinkage allowance: $s = 1.0\%$
- Machining allowance: $t_m = 3$ mm (on top and bottom faces)
- Draft angle: $\theta = 1.5°$ on external walls
- Number of castings per pattern: 1

**Step 1: Calculate Shrinkage on the Outer Diameter**
$$D_{shrink} = D \times s = 300 \times 0.01 = 3\ \text{mm added to radius}$$
$$D_{pattern,after\ shrinkage} = D + 2 \times 3 = 306\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 2: Apply Machining Allowance on the Height (Top & Bottom)**
Machining is required on both top and bottom faces. So we add $t_m$ to **both** ends:
$$H_{machining} = H + 2 \times t_m = 150 + 2 \times 3 = 156\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 3: Apply Draft on the External Wall (Height Direction)**
The draft taper is applied on the vertical wall. The vertical height increase due to draft:
$$\Delta H_{draft} = H \times \tan(\theta) = 156 \times \tan(1.5°)$$
$$\tan(1.5°) \approx 0.02619$$
$$\Delta H_{draft} = 156 \times 0.02619 \approx 4.085\ \text{mm}$$
$$H_{final,pattern} = 156 + 4.085 \approx 160.09\ \text{mm}$$

> **Note (Important):** The draft allowance is **added** to the height of the pattern on external walls (and added on the radius too). The rule is:
> - **External wall:** Add draft (pattern is bigger).
> - **Internal wall (cavity):** Subtract draft from the bore (pattern is smaller).

**Step 4: Apply Draft on the Diameter**
The taper is applied on the outer cylindrical wall. Taper spread is:
$$\Delta D_{draft} = 2 \times H \times \tan(\theta) = 2 \times 156 \times 0.02619 \approx 8.17\ \text{mm}$$
$$D_{final,pattern} = 306 + 8.17 \approx 314.17\ \text{mm}$$

**Final Pattern Dimensions:**
- **Outer Diameter:** $\approx 314.2$ mm
- **Height:** $\approx 160.1$ mm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Order of Applying Allowances (KTU Convention):** 
> 1. **Shrinkage → first** (because it is metallurgical and inevitable).
> 2. **Machining → second** (depends on customer requirement).
> 3. **Draft → third** (depends on moulding process).
> 4. **Distortion → applied as per casting shape**.

---

### 📐 Problem 2: Buoyancy Force on a Cylindrical Core
**Given:**
- Core dimensions: Length = 400 mm, Diameter = 80 mm
- Core density: $\rho_{core} = 1600\ kg/m^3$ (typical baked sand core)
- Molten metal: Aluminum, $\rho_{Al,liquid} = 2400\ kg/m^3$ at 700°C
- $g = 9.81\ m/s^2$

**Step 1: Core Volume**
$$V_{core} = \pi \cdot r^2 \cdot L = \pi \times (0.04)^2 \times 0.40$$
$$V_{core} = \pi \times 0.0016 \times 0.40 = 0.002011\ m^3$$

**Step 2: Weight of Core (downward)**
$$W_{core} = \rho_{core} \cdot V_{core} \cdot g = 1600 \times 0.002011 \times 9.81$$
$$W_{core} \approx 31.57\ N$$

**Step 3: Buoyant Force (upward)**
$$F_{buoyancy} = \rho_{metal} \cdot V_{core} \cdot g = 2400 \times 0.002011 \times 9.81$$
$$F_{buoyancy} \approx 47.36\ N$$

**Step 4: Net Upward Force (must be supported by chaplets)**
$$F_{net} = F_{buoyancy} - W_{core} = 47.36 - 31.57 = 15.79\ N$$

> **Conclusion:** A chaplet or core support must bear at least **15.79 N** of upward buoyant load, plus a **safety factor of 2–3×** → design chaplet capacity ≥ **47 N**.

---

### 📐 Problem 3: Gated Pattern Productivity
**Given:** A hinged bracket has a finished volume of **150 cm³**. A **6-cavity gated pattern** is used. Each mould requires **2 minutes** to make, and pouring + cooling = 4 minutes.

**Step 1: Volume per mould (6 castings)**
$$V_{mould} = 6 \times 150 = 900\ cm^3$$

**Step 2: Castings per cycle**
$$N = 6\ \text{castings per cycle}$$

**Step 3: Cycle time**
$$t_{cycle} = t_{mould} + t_{pour\ cool} = 2 + 4 = 6\ \text{min}$$

**Step 4: Production rate**
$$R = \frac{N}{t_{cycle}} = \frac{6}{6} = 1\ \text{casting/min} = 60\ \text{castings/hr}$$

> **Comparison:** A single-piece pattern would yield **1 casting per 6 min = 10 castings/hr** — so the gated pattern gives **6× productivity**.

---

## 3.2 Pattern Selection Analytical Matrix (KTU Application Tool)

> [!NOTE]
> The following decision matrix helps students solve *"Which pattern is best suited for X casting?"* type questions — a **favourite 7-mark KTU question**.

| Casting Requirement | Best Pattern Type | Reason |
|---------------------|-------------------|--------|
| One-off, very large (e.g., lathe bed) | **Skeleton + Sweep** | Strikle forms large contours economically |
| 100–5000 castings, simple shape, large | **Wooden Single / Split** | Cheapest, easy to repair |
| 5000–50000 castings, small to medium | **Match Plate (Aluminium)** | Fast, automated moulding |
| 100,000+ castings, very small | **Shell pattern / Metal Gated** | Wear resistance, automation |
| Casting with undercuts or T-shape | **Loose-Piece Pattern** | Cannot withdraw in one direction |
| Irregular parting surface | **Follow-Board Pattern** | Follow board forms parting contour |
| Many small parts at once | **Gated / Multi-Cavity** | Productivity × n |
| Cylindrical symmetry, large | **Sweep / Segmental** | Use rotational symmetry |
| 3 parting surfaces (top, middle, bottom) | **Three-Piece (Cope-Cheek-Drag)** | Multiple parting planes |
| Castings with hollow sections | **Pattern + Core Box** | Cores form internal features |

---

## 3.3 Comparative Table: Pattern vs. Casting Dimensional Math

| Feature | Casting (Final) | Pattern (Before Pouring) | Why? |
|---------|------------------|---------------------------|------|
| Outer Length (steel) | 1000 mm | 1020 mm | Shrinkage 2% on steel |
| Outer Length (CI) | 1000 mm | 1010 mm | Shrinkage 1% on cast iron |
| Machined diameter | 200 mm | 200 + 2t_m = 206 mm (if t_m=3) | Machining allowance |
| Vertical wall taper | None (straight) | 1.5° draft | To withdraw pattern |
| Flat slender (long) | Slightly bowed | Pre-curved opposite | Distortion compensation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid)

## 4.1 Master Pattern Manufacturing & Moulding Flow

This block-level architecture illustrates the **role of the pattern in the foundry system** — from design to casting.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Customer Drawing / 3D Model] --> B[Pattern Design]
    B --> C[Apply Shrinkage Allowance]
    C --> D[Apply Machining Allowance]
    D --> E[Apply Draft Taper]
    E --> F[Add Core Prints]
    F --> G[Apply Distortion Allowance]
    G --> H[Final Pattern Manufactured]
    H --> I[Pattern Stored with Shrink Rule]
    I --> J[Mould Making - Sand Ramming]
    J --> K[Pattern Withdrawn - Cavity Formed]
    K --> L[Core Placed via Core Prints]
    L --> M[Mould Closed - Pouring]
    M --> N[Cooling and Solidification]
    N --> O[Shakeout - Casting Retrieved]
    O --> P[Fettling and Inspection]
    P --> Q[Final Casting Delivered]
```

## 4.2 Pattern Allowance Application Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Nominal Casting Dimension] --> B[Add Shrinkage Allowance]
    B --> C[Add Machining Allowance]
    C --> D[Add Draft Taper Allowance]
    D --> E[Add Distortion Allowance]
    E --> F[Final Pattern Dimension]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style F fill:#d4f4dd
    style B fill:#fff4cc
    style C fill:#fff4cc
    style D fill:#fff4cc
    style E fill:#fff4cc
```

## 4.3 Pattern Classification — Mind Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PATTERNS] --> B[Single Piece]
    A --> C[Two Piece Split]
    A --> D[Multi Piece 3 Piece]
    A --> E[Loose Piece]
    A --> F[Gated Multiple]
    A --> G[Match Plate]
    A --> H[Cope and Drag]
    A --> I[Skeleton Frame]
    A --> J[Sweep]
    A --> K[Follow Board]
    A --> L[Segmental]
    A --> M[Shell]
    style A fill:#ffe6e6
```

## 4.4 Sand Mould Assembly with Pattern (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph COPE[COPE - UPPER MOULD BOX]
        C1[Sand Rammed in Cope]
        C2[Cope Half Impression]
        C3[Runner System - Sprue]
    end
    subgraph DRAG[DRAG - LOWER MOULD BOX]
        D1[Sand Rammed in Drag]
        D2[Drag Half Impression]
        D3[Core Print Seat]
    end
    COPE -.Separation for Pattern Withdrawal.-> DRAG
    CORE[Core - Forms Internal Cavity] -. Sits on Core Print.-> DRAG
    POURED[Molten Metal Poured via Sprue] --> COPE
    COPE --> MOLDCLOSED[Mould Closed]
    DRAG --> MOLDCLOSED
    MOLDCLOSED --> COOL[Cooling and Solidification]
    COOL --> CAST[Final Casting with Hollow Core]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Moulding Sequence (KTU Standard):**
> 1. Place pattern on moulding board.
> 2. Place drag box over pattern, fill with sand, ram, strike off.
> 3. Invert drag, place cope box, apply parting sand, set cope, ram.
> 4. Remove cope, withdraw pattern, place core via core prints.
> 5. Close mould, pour metal, cool, shakeout.

## 4.5 Allowance Direction Logic (Mathematical Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pattern Wall Type] --> B{External or Internal?}
    B -- External --> C[Add Draft Positive Direction]
    B -- Internal --> D[Add Draft Negative Direction]
    C --> E[Pattern is Larger than Casting]
    D --> F[Pattern is Smaller than Casting]
    E --> G[After Metal Shrinks - Final Casting Dimension Achieved]
    F --> G
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]**

### Q1. Define a pattern. List any four functions of a pattern in foundry practice.
**Model Answer:**
A pattern is a **replica of the casting** with necessary allowances, used to create a mould cavity in sand for pouring molten metal.

**Four functions:**
1. Forms the **cavity** of the desired shape and size.
2. Provides **core prints** to position cores for internal hollows.
3. Establishes the **parting line** between cope and drag.
4. Incorporates **runner and gating system** for molten metal flow (in match-plate and gated patterns).

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]**

### Q2. What is the significance of **colour coding** on patterns? State the colours used to indicate (i) unmachined surfaces, (ii) machined surfaces, and (iii) core prints.
**Model Answer:**
Colour coding provides a **visual, standardized communication** between pattern maker, moulder, and fettler about the surface treatment required on a casting.

| Color | Indication |
|-------|------------|
| **Black** | Un-machined (as-cast) surface — no further work |
| **Red** | Surface to be machined — machining allowance provided |
| **Yellow** | Core prints — locates sand cores in mould |

> Additional: **Blue** = polished/chipped, **Green** = loose piece seats.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — ESE Module Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1 Full Question]**

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A.(a)** With neat sketches, explain the construction and application of the following patterns: **(7 Marks)**
   (i) **Two-piece (split) pattern**
   (ii) **Gated pattern**
   (iii) **Match-plate pattern**

**Q.A.(b)** What are pattern allowances? Explain any **five** pattern allowances with their significance and typical magnitudes. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q.A.(a):

**(i) Two-Piece (Split) Pattern:**
- Pattern is split into **cope half and drag half** along the parting line.
- The two halves are joined by **dowel pins** (alignment) and **filler/cleats** (sand support).
- During moulding, drag half is placed on moulding board, sand is rammed. The cope half is then placed on top (after parting sand is dusted), and the cope is rammed.
- Both halves are withdrawn separately, leaving the impression in their respective mould boxes.
- **Application:** Flanged pipes, valve bodies, couplings — castings with a flat parting line through the middle.

**(ii) Gated Pattern:**
- Multiple identical pattern impressions (cavities) are connected through a **common runner** (gate) in a single pattern block.
- All cavities are filled simultaneously through the central sprue.
- **Number of impressions:** 2, 4, 6, 8, 12, 16 — depends on casting size and mould box capacity.
- **Advantages:** Higher productivity, less sand per casting, uniform quality.
- **Application:** Mass production of small castings like hinges, brackets, small gear blanks, connecting rod caps.

**(iii) Match-Plate Pattern:**
- Both cope and drag halves are **rigidly mounted on opposite sides of a metal or plastic plate** (the match plate).
- The plate itself contains the **runner, gates, and risers**.
- A central **locating bush** fits into the moulding machine to position the plate accurately.
- Moulding is done on a **match-plate moulding machine** — pattern is not manually placed; the entire plate is flipped between drag and cope.
- **Application:** Medium to high volume production of small to medium castings — connecting rods, pistons, valve bodies.

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q.A.(b):

**Pattern Allowances:** (5 detailed)

1. **Shrinkage Allowance:** Compensation for thermal contraction of metal from solidification to room temperature. Typical values: Cast iron 1%, Steel 2%, Brass 1.5%, Aluminum 1.25%. Pattern is made **larger** by shrinkage %.

2. **Machining Allowance:** Extra material on machined surfaces (1.5–8 mm) for subsequent turning, milling, drilling. Marked in **RED** on pattern.

3. **Draft (Taper) Allowance:** 1°–3° on external walls, 3°–5° on internal walls, to enable clean pattern withdrawal from sand mould. Applied as **tapered walls**.

4. **Shaking Allowance:** Compensation for the slight **enlargement of the cavity** caused by the moulder's rapping of the pattern before withdrawal. 0.5–2 mm on deep internal sections.

5. **Distortion Allowance:** Pre-cambering of the pattern in the **opposite direction** to the expected warpage of the casting during cooling. Critical for flat, slender, or asymmetric castings.

> **Additional allowance (for bonus):** Finishing allowance for grinding/polishing surfaces (BLUE colour, 1.5–6 mm).

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B.(a)** Explain in detail the different **types of pattern materials** used in foundry, comparing their properties, life, and applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Q.B.(b)** What are **cores and core prints**? With a neat diagram, explain the role of **chaplets** in supporting cores during casting. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q.B.(a):

**Pattern Material Comparison:**

| Material | Life (castings) | Surface Finish | Cost | Use |
|----------|----------------|----------------|------|-----|
| **Wood (Pine, Teak, Mahogany)** | 100–1000 | Moderate | Low | Prototypes, large patterns, master patterns |
| **Aluminium alloys** | 5,000–10,000 | Excellent | Medium | Match plates, medium runs |
| **Cast Iron / Steel** | 50,000+ | Excellent (machined) | High | Mass production, large patterns |
| **Plastics (Epoxy, Phenolic, Urethane)** | 1,000–5,000 | Very Good | Medium | Match plates, intricate shapes |
| **Plaster / Gypsum** | Single use | Excellent | Low | Master patterns, prototype tools |
| **Brass / Bronze** | 10,000+ | Excellent | High | Small intricate patterns |

**Selection Criteria:**
- **Casting volume** (1-off vs. mass production)
- **Casting accuracy** required
- **Surface finish** required
- **Lead time** for pattern manufacture
- **Cost per casting** economics

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q.B.(b):

**Core:** A pre-formed sand mass (bonded with oil, resin, or sodium silicate) placed inside the mould cavity to create **internal hollows, undercuts, and passages** that the pattern cannot directly form.

**Core Print:** Projection on the pattern that forms a **bearing seat** in the mould for accurate placement of the core. It does NOT appear on the final casting.

**Chaplets:** Metallic supports (steel, iron) placed in the mould cavity to **hold the core in position** against:
- **Buoyant force** of molten metal (Archimedes' principle).
- **Erosion forces** from flowing metal.
- **Self-weight of the core** during pouring.

Chaplets are of two types:
- **Sand Core Chaplets:** Designed to embed fully in the casting — used where the chaplet location will be machined away.
- **Stay Bolts / Spreader Chaplets:** Used where the chaplet remains in the casting (e.g., in heavy machinery castings).

> **Buoyancy Formula (must be quoted):** $F_b = V_{core} \cdot (\rho_{metal} - \rho_{core}) \cdot g$
> Chaplets must be designed to support **2× to 3× the calculated buoyancy force** as a safety factor.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Patterns:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to mention COLOUR CODING** (1 mark lost in most questions). Always end pattern answer with: *"The pattern uses IS-standard colour coding — RED for machined, BLACK for unmachined, YELLOW for core prints."*
> 
> 2. **Confusing Draft vs Shrinkage direction:** Draft is in **degrees (°)**. Shrinkage is in **percentage (%)** or **mm/mm**. Do not mix them.
> 
> 3. **Missing the **order of allowances**:** Examiners award **1 mark** specifically for stating that shrinkage is applied first, then machining, then draft.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting that draft on INTERNAL walls is OPPOSITE:** External = pattern bigger, Internal = pattern smaller. Many students write draft unconditionally as "make pattern bigger".
> 
> 5. **Forgetting shrinkage value of **1 m cast iron = 1.01 m pattern** numerical.** Always carry the standard metal table.
> 
> 6. **Chaplet Buoyancy calculation is skipped** — it carries **2–3 marks** in a 7-mark part. Always derive the formula.
> 
> 7. **Gated pattern productivity numerical** — students forget to divide by cycle time. Always show: $R = N_{cavities}/t_{cycle}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📝

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist — Patterns**

- ✅ **Pattern = Replica of casting with allowances** (not exact copy).
- ✅ **Six functions:** Cavity formation, size, parting, core support, gating, stripping.
- ✅ **Most common material:** Wood (low volume), Aluminum (medium), Cast Iron/Steel (high).
- ✅ **Pattern Colour Code:** **Red** = machined, **Black** = as-cast, **Yellow** = core print, **Blue** = finish, **Green** = loose piece.
- ✅ **Shrinkage values to memorize:**
  - Gray Cast Iron = **1.0%**
  - White Cast Iron = **2.0%**
  - Steel = **2.0%**
  - Brass = **1.5%**
  - Aluminum = **1.25%**
- ✅ **Order of allowance application:** Shrinkage → Machining → Draft → Distortion.
- ✅ **Draft rule:** External **1°–3°** (E), Internal **3°–5°** (I) — pattern must be tapered to release.
- ✅ **12 Types of patterns:** Single, Split, Multi-piece, Loose-piece, Gated, Match-plate, Cope-Drag, Skeleton, Sweep, Follow-board, Segmental, Shell.
- ✅ **Gated pattern productivity** = (Number of cavities) / (Total cycle time).
- ✅ **Core Print** is on the pattern; **Chaplets** are inside the mould to support the core.
- ✅ **Chaplet Buoyancy formula:** $F_b = V_{core} \cdot (\rho_{metal} - \rho_{core}) \cdot g$.
- ✅ **Patternmaker's Shrink Rule** is a pre-scaled ruler — **1 m of shrink rule = 1.01 m** for cast iron.
- ✅ **Match-plate pattern** is for high productivity; **Skeleton pattern** is for one-off large castings.
- ✅ **Loose-piece pattern** is used for **T or L-shaped** castings with undercuts.
- ✅ **Cores** are made in **core boxes** (separate from the pattern itself).
- ✅ **Sweep pattern** uses a template rotated about a fixed axis (for circular/rotational castings).
- ✅ **Follow-board pattern** is used when the parting line is **irregular / non-planar**.

> **End of Module 1 — Patterns | KTU PBMRT404 | 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cores
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT404 - MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cores

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DEFINITION (KTU Standard)**
> A **Core** is a preformed, consolidated mass of refractory granular material (predominantly silica sand bonded with organic or inorganic binders) that is inserted into the mold cavity of a sand casting to produce **internal cavities, recesses, undercuts, holes, or hollow sections** in the finished casting which cannot be generated by the pattern alone.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework (PBMRT404 – Manufacturing Process), cores are studied as an integral subset of **Foundry Technology / Sand Casting**, falling under the broader domain of "Moulding Materials, Patterns, and Special Features" in Module 1.

### 1.2 Core Terminology Vocabulary (Must Memorize for KTU)

| Term | Meaning (Examiner-Standard) |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Core** | Sand mass placed in the mould to form internal feature. |
| **Core Print** | Extra projection on the pattern that forms a seat (cavity) in the mould to support/position the core. |
| **Core Box** | Wooden or metal tooling (split type) used to shape the core. |
| **Chaplet** | Metal support used inside the mould to hold the core against flotation by molten metal. |
| **Baking** | Thermal curing of green cores in an oven at controlled temperature to develop strength. |
| **Collapsibility** | Property of the core to disintegrate after casting cools, allowing the casting to shrink freely. |
| **Permeability** | Ability of the core sand to allow gases/escape during pouring. |
| **Green Strength** | Strength of the unbaked (raw) core sand mix. |
| **Dry Strength** | Strength of the core after baking/curing. |
| **Hot Strength** | Strength retained by the core at elevated temperature. |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy – "The Ice Cube Inside a Cake"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy**
> Imagine you are baking a **Bundt cake** (ring-shaped cake with a central hole). The hollow centre cannot be created by the outer mould alone — you need something occupying the centre space while the batter is poured around it, and which is then **removed later**, leaving a clean cavity.
>
> In a sand casting, the **mould** is the outer pan, the **molten metal** is the cake batter, and the **core** is the central object that occupies space inside the mould. Once the metal solidifies and cools, the core must be **shakeout-able** (collapsible) so that the internal cavity is left behind — exactly like scooping out the central object from the cake.

### 1.4 Why Cores are Used in Castings

A pattern can produce **external geometry** perfectly, but it cannot reach into the casting to form **internal features**. Cores solve this problem. Common features that *require cores*:

1. **Cylindrical bores** (e.g., engine cylinder block, pipe fittings).
2. **Internal passages** (e.g., water jackets, oil galleries).
3. **Undercuts and re-entrant features**.
4. **Bosses with central holes** that are not perpendicular to parting plane.
5. **Hollow structural sections** to reduce weight without losing strength.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Approximately **60–70%** of all castings produced globally require at least one core. Therefore, understanding core materials and processes is essential for foundry practice — a frequent Part A question in KTU exams.

### 1.5 Classification of Cores – Snapshot View

Cores are broadly classified based on:

* **Binding system used** (Oil-bonded, Resin-bonded, Inorganic-bonded, Clay-bonded).
* **State of cure** (Green cores, Dry/Baked cores).
* **Method of production** (Hand-rammed, Machine-made, Shell, Hot-box, Cold-box, CO₂-set).
* **Function in the mould** (Horizontal core, Vertical/Cope core, Balanced core, Hanging core, Ram-up core).

These classifications will be elaborated in Section 2 and Section 4 (visual).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constituents of Core Sand

A core sand mix is a precisely proportioned heterogeneous mixture. The standard recipe components are listed below:

1. **Silica Sand (SiO₂)** — The bulk refractory aggregate. Must be **angular, clean, uniformly sized, and of high GFN control (typically 50–70 AFS)**. High silica content (>95% SiO₂) and high fusion point (>1700 °C) are mandatory.
2. **Binder** — Holds the sand grains together after curing. Classified into:
   * **Organic binders:** Linseed oil, Cereal binders (dextrin, starch), Resins (furan, phenolic, urea-formaldehyde).
   * **Inorganic binders:** Sodium silicate (used in CO₂ process), Bentonite (clay).
3. **Catalyst / Hardener** — Accelerates curing (e.g., ammonium chloride for furan resin, CO₂ gas for sodium silicate).
4. **Additives**:
   * **Cereal (Dextrin/Starch):** 0.5–2% — improves green strength and collapsibility.
   * **Iron Oxide (Fe₂O₃):** 1–2% — prevents sintering/fusion burn-on, improves hot strength.
   * **Sea Coal/Pitch:** 2–4% — improves surface finish, reduces metal penetration.
   * **Wood Flour:** 0.5–1.5% — improves collapsibility.
   * **Moisture content:** Critical parameter, usually **3–5%** for oil-bonded cores.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Parameter / Formula | Standard Expression | Units | Typical Value / Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Permeability Number (AFS)** | $P = \dfrac{V \cdot h}{A \cdot p \cdot t}$ | cm³·cm / (cm²·g·min) | 80 – 150 (for cores) |
| 2 | **Grain Fineness Number (GFN)** | $\text{GFN} = \dfrac{\sum (m_i \times \text{AFS}_{i})}{M}$ | Dimensionless | 50 – 70 (core sand) |
| 3 | **Green Compressive Strength** | $\sigma_g = \dfrac{F_g}{A_c}$ | N/cm² or kg/cm² | 0.35 – 0.70 N/cm² |
| 4 | **Dry Compressive Strength** | $\sigma_d = \dfrac{F_d}{A_c}$ | N/cm² or kg/cm² | 7 – 35 N/cm² (oil-bonded) |
| 5 | **Hot Strength (at temp T)** | $\sigma_h(T) = \dfrac{F_h}{A_c}$ | N/cm² | 5 – 20 N/cm² |
| 6 | **Moisture Content (%)** | $M\% = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$ | % | 3 – 5% (oil bonded) |
| 7 | **Clay Content (AFS)** | $C\% = \dfrac{W_{clay}}{W_{sand}} \times 100$ | % | 6 – 10% (mould), 0–2% (core) |
| 8 | **Active Clay (%)** | $A_c = \dfrac{V_{EDTA} \times N \times 0.002}{W_s} \times 100$ | % | 4 – 8% (mould) |
| 9 | **Compactibility (Mouldability)** | $C\% = \dfrac{h_{unrammed} - h_{rammed}}{h_{unrammed}} \times 100$ | % | 35 – 50% (mould) |
| 10 | **Shakeout Time (approx.)** | $t_{so} \approx f(\text{core type}, T_{\text{metal}})$ | minutes | 5 – 60 min |
| 11 | **Core Sand Mix Ratio (typical)** | Sand : Binder : Cereal : Additive | Mass ratio | 100 : 1.5 : 2 : 1 (oil bonded) |
| 12 | **Permeability threshold (critical)** | $P_{crit} = k \cdot \sqrt{\dot{V}_{gas}}$ | cm³·cm/cm²·g·min | $> 100$ for ferrous castings |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM NOTE:** The **permeability** and **GFN** formulas are most frequently asked. Notice that the LaTeX rendering uses $\vert$ and $\sqrt{\cdot}$ notation — never write absolute values with the pipe symbol inside a markdown table as it breaks formatting.

### 2.3 The "Why and How" of Core Properties

Each property of a core sand mix exists for a **specific metallurgical reason**. Examiners love to test the "Why" behind each property.

* **Why HIGH permeability is required?** → When molten metal hits a cold core, the binder and moisture in the core vaporise instantly, producing a large volume of gas. If this gas cannot escape through the core, it back-pressures into the metal, causing **blow holes, gas porosity, and surface defects**. A permeable core lets the gas escape harmlessly.
* **Why HIGH dry strength is required?** → The baked core is lifted, transported, and placed in the mould cavity. It must withstand handling stresses without cracking. Also, the hydrostatic pressure of molten metal exerts significant force on the core.
* **Why LOW collapsibility is NOT desirable?** → As the casting cools, the metal contracts (shrinks). If the core is too rigid, it will **resist shrinkage**, causing **hot tears, cracks, and internal stresses** in the casting. The core must collapse gracefully after the metal has solidified.
* **Why HIGH refractoriness is required?** → The core surface is in direct contact with molten metal at 1300–1600 °C. The silica sand must not melt, fuse, or chemically react with the metal. High SiO₂ content (>95%) and low impurities (especially Fe₂O₃, alkalis) ensure refractoriness.
* **Why IRON OXIDE is added?** → At elevated temperatures, Fe₂O₃ reacts with silica to form **fayalite (2FeO·SiO₂)**, a low-melting slag that prevents the sand grains from sintering onto the casting surface. This dramatically improves **peelability and surface finish**.
* **Why is CEREAL (dextrin) added?** → It improves **green strength** (handling strength before baking) and contributes to **collapsibility** because dextrin chars and disintegrates at ~200 °C, leaving the core brittle post-casting.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Typical Core Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Automotive** | Engine block water jackets, cylinder bores, manifold ports. |
| **Pump & Valve** | Impeller flow passages, valve body internal bores. |
| **Aerospace** | Hollow turbine blade internal cooling channels. |
| **Pipe Fittings** | Elbows, tees, reducers — all require internal bores. |
| **Heavy Machinery** | Gearbox housings, hydraulic cylinder blocks. |

### 2.5 The Concept of Core Print (Repeated Emphasis — Frequently Asked)

The **core print** is the *negative seat* formed in the mould sand by an extension on the pattern. Its functions are:

1. To **locate** the core accurately in the mould.
2. To **support** the core against gravitational and metallostatic forces.
3. To provide a **seal** to prevent metal from entering the core-vent interface.
4. To transfer **venting** for the core gases into the mould vent system.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse "core print" with "core". The print is on the **pattern/mould**, not on the core itself. The print creates a **recess** in the mould into which the **core projection (core head/core seat)** fits. This distinction is a 2-mark favourite.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Procedures & Analytical Matrix

This section is reserved for **exhaustive step-by-step treatment** of three KTU-relevant analytical procedures: (A) Permeability calculation, (B) Core sand mix design, and (C) Core blow machine calculation.

---

### 3.1 Procedure 1 — Determination of Permeability Number (Detailed)

The **AFS Permeability Number** is defined as the volume of air (in cm³) that passes through a standard core sand specimen of given cross-section and height, under a standard pressure, per unit time.

**Step 1: Standard specimen preparation**
A cylindrical specimen of standard dimensions is prepared:
- Diameter $D = 50.8$ mm  (i.e., cross-sectional area $A = 20.25$ cm²)
- Height $h = 50.8$ mm

**Step 2: Apply standard pressure**
Air pressure applied at the base: $p = 10$ cm of water column (≈ 980 Pa).

**Step 3: Measure air volume per unit time**
Volume of air passing through: $V$ cm³ in time $t$ minutes.

**Step 4: Apply the AFS permeability formula**

$$
P = \frac{V \cdot h}{A \cdot p \cdot t}
$$

**Step 5: Substituting standard values**

$$
A = 20.25 \text{ cm}^2, \quad p = 10 \text{ cm of water}, \quad h = 50.8 \text{ mm}
$$

The numerical form of the AFS permeability equation (substituting the constants) reduces to:

$$
P = \frac{V \times 50.8}{20.25 \times 10 \times t} = \frac{V}{3.987 \, t}
$$

For practical KTU problems, this approximation is used:

$$
P \approx \frac{V}{4 \, t}
$$

> This is the **standard form** examiners use. A 14-mark problem can fully use this procedure.

---

### 3.2 Procedure 2 — Step-by-Step Core Sand Mix Design

Suppose the KTU question asks: *"Design a core sand mix for a small ferrous casting (5 kg). Use oil-bonded system."*

**Step 1: Define the total sand mass required.**

Let total sand mass = 100 kg (basis for easy percentage calculation).

**Step 2: Decide binder percentage.**

For an oil-bonded core sand: Linseed oil = **1.5 – 2.5%** of sand mass.
Choose: Linseed oil = 2%.

$$
\text{Mass of oil} = 0.02 \times 100 = 2 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3: Decide cereal (dextrin) percentage.**

For green strength and collapsibility: Dextrin = **1.5 – 2%**.
Choose: Dextrin = 2%.

$$
\text{Mass of dextrin} = 0.02 \times 100 = 2 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 4: Decide moisture content.**

For oil-bonded cores, moisture = **3 – 4%** to activate the dextrin and provide green workability.

$$
\text{Mass of water} = 0.04 \times 100 = 4 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 5: Decide additive (iron oxide + sea coal) percentages.**

For ferrous castings:

$$
\text{Iron oxide} = 1.5\%, \quad \text{Sea coal} = 2\%
$$

$$
\text{Mass of Fe}_2\text{O}_3 = 1.5 \text{ kg}, \quad \text{Mass of sea coal} = 2 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 6: Tabulate the recipe.**

| Component | Mass (kg) | % of Sand |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Silica sand (SiO₂) | 100.0 | 100.0% |
| Linseed oil (binder) | 2.0 | 2.0% |
| Dextrin (cereal) | 2.0 | 2.0% |
| Water (moisture) | 4.0 | 4.0% |
| Iron oxide | 1.5 | 1.5% |
| Sea coal | 2.0 | 2.0% |
| **Total mix mass** | **111.5** | — |

**Step 7: Mixing sequence (muller operation).**

1. Add dry sand to muller → mull 30 sec to break lumps.
2. Add **cereal + iron oxide + sea coal** → mull 60 sec (dry mixing).
3. Add **water** → mull 90 sec (activates cereal).
4. Add **oil binder LAST** → mull 120 sec (oil must coat the binder-loaded grains, not be absorbed by dry sand).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why oil last?** Oil coats the **outside** of the prepared sand mix. If added early, oil will penetrate dry sand grains and waste binder. This is a frequent 3-mark question.

**Step 8: Backing/Curing schedule (for oil-bonded cores).**

$$
T_{bake} = 200 \text{ °C} \pm 10 \text{ °C}, \quad t_{bake} = 1 \text{ to } 2 \text{ hours}
$$

The oil polymerises (oxidative polymerisation) at this temperature, binding the sand grains rigidly.

---

### 3.3 Procedure 3 — GFN Calculation (Detailed Worked Example)

A sand sample is sieved and the mass retained on each AFS screen is given. Compute the GFN.

**Given Data (Sieve Analysis):**

| AFS Sieve No. | Multiplier | Mass Retained (g) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 6 | 5 | 0.0 |
| 12 | 10 | 1.5 |
| 20 | 20 | 8.0 |
| 30 | 30 | 25.0 |
| 40 | 40 | 40.0 |
| 50 | 50 | 18.0 |
| 70 | 70 | 5.0 |
| 100 | 100 | 1.5 |
| 140 | 140 | 0.5 |
| 200 | 200 | 0.5 |
| 270 | 300 | 0.0 |
| Pan | — | 0.0 |

**Step 1: Compute total mass retained.**

$$
M = 0 + 1.5 + 8 + 25 + 40 + 18 + 5 + 1.5 + 0.5 + 0.5 + 0 + 0 = 100 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 2: Compute (mass × multiplier) for each sieve.**

$$
(m_i \times \text{AFS}_i) \text{ values:}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0 \times 5) & = 0 \\
(1.5 \times 10) & = 15 \\
(8 \times 20) & = 160 \\
(25 \times 30) & = 750 \\
(40 \times 40) & = 1600 \\
(18 \times 50) & = 900 \\
(5 \times 70) & = 350 \\
(1.5 \times 100) & = 150 \\
(0.5 \times 140) & = 70 \\
(0.5 \times 200) & = 100 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Sum all products.**

$$
\sum (m_i \times \text{AFS}_i) = 0 + 15 + 160 + 750 + 1600 + 900 + 350 + 150 + 70 + 100 = 4095
$$

**Step 4: Apply the GFN formula.**

$$
\text{GFN} = \frac{\sum (m_i \times \text{AFS}_i)}{M} = \frac{4095}{100} = 40.95
$$

**Step 5: Interpretation.**

Since GFN ≈ **41**, this is a **medium-coarse sand**, suitable for large ferrous castings but not for cores requiring fine surface finish (need GFN 60–70 for those).

> A fine sand (high GFN) → better surface finish, lower permeability.
> A coarse sand (low GFN) → better permeability, rougher surface.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Core Type vs. Property Requirements

| Core Type | Binder | Permeability Req. | Strength Req. | Collapsibility | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Green sand core** | Bentonite + water | High (120–200) | Low–Medium | High | Small, simple cores |
| **Dry sand (oil-bonded)** | Linseed oil + cereal | Medium (80–150) | High (dry) | Medium | General engineering castings |
| **Shell core** | Phenolic resin (no-valak) | High | Very high (thin shell) | Excellent | Cylinder bores, valve ports |
| **Hot-box core** | Furan/phenolic resin + acid catalyst | High | High | Good | Mass production |
| **Cold-box (amine)** | Phenolic resin + isocyanate + amine gas | Very high | High | Excellent | Automotive, high-volume |
| **CO₂ core (sodium silicate)** | Na₂SiO₃ + CO₂ gas | High | Medium–High | Poor (sinters) | Large ferrous castings, jigs |
| **Croning (shell) core** | Resin-coated sand | Very high | Very high | Excellent | Thin-walled complex cores |

### 3.5 Key Derivation — Why Chaplets Are Sometimes Required

When a core is **fully suspended** in a horizontal mould, buoyancy from molten metal pushes the core **upwards (floats)**. The buoyant force is:

$$
F_{buoy} = V_{core} \times \rho_{metal} \times g - m_{core} \times g
$$

where:
- $V_{core}$ = volume of core (m³)
- $\rho_{metal}$ = density of molten metal (kg/m³)
- $m_{core}$ = mass of core (kg)
- $g$ = 9.81 m/s²

If $F_{buoy} > 0$, the core will float and require **chaplets** to anchor it to the upper mould surface.

For a **balanced/horizontally self-supporting core** where the core axis is below the metal centreline, no chaplets are needed.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visual Aid Mandate:** KTU 2024 scheme emphasises visual literacy. The diagrams below use **Mermaid** (block-level schematic) since physical drawings cannot be embedded directly.


For actual cross-sectional diagrams of mould + core, please refer to your prescribed textbook figures. The KTU 2024 paper will *not* ask you to draw Mermaid diagrams, but understanding the *flow* of the process is essential for descriptive answers.
