<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

#### Question 1: Define a core. List the essential properties required of a good core sand. [3 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **core** is a preformed, consolidated mass of refractory granular material (silica sand + binder + additives) inserted into a mould cavity to produce internal features such as holes, passages, or undercuts in a casting that cannot be made by the pattern alone.

**Essential properties of good core sand:**

1. **High refractoriness** — to withstand molten metal temperature without fusion.
2. **High permeability** — to allow escape of gases generated during pouring.
3. **Adequate dry strength** — to withstand handling and metallostatic pressure.
4. **Good collapsibility** — to disintegrate after solidification, preventing hot tears.
5. **Good surface finish (low Veining/penetration tendency)** — ensured by adding iron oxide and sea coal.

---

#### Question 2: What is a core print? Why is it provided on the pattern? [3 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **core print** is a specially designed projection provided on the pattern that creates a matching recess (seat) in the mould sand. The core is positioned and supported by resting in this seat.

**Reasons for providing core prints:**

1. To **locate the core accurately** within the mould cavity.
2. To **support the core** against its own weight and the metallostatic buoyancy of molten metal.
3. To **prevent metal leakage** at the core-mould interface.
4. To maintain **proper alignment** between the core axis and the casting's internal feature axis.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Writing "core print is on the core itself" — this loses 1 mark. The print is a feature of the **pattern**, which forms a recess in the **mould sand** into which the core is placed.

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Core Sand Composition & Process

`[KTU University Exam – KTU 2024 Scheme Model Paper]`

**(a) Explain in detail the constituents of core sand and their functions. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The core sand mix is a carefully proportioned blend of refractory aggregate, binder, additives, and moisture. Each component serves a specific function:

**(i) Silica Sand (SiO₂) — 92 to 96%**

* The base refractory material that withstands high temperature of molten metal (1300–1600 °C).
* Must be **angular, clean, and uniformly graded** with GFN of 50–70 for cores.
* High SiO₂ content (>95%) and low impurities (Fe₂O₃, alkalis, lime) ensure refractoriness and prevent chemical reaction with the metal.
* Angular grains provide better mechanical interlocking and higher strength.

**(ii) Binders — 1 to 3%**

Binders are responsible for holding the sand grains together after curing. Types:
* **Linseed oil (organic):** Polymerises upon baking at 200 °C, forming a strong film around sand grains. Most common for traditional dry sand cores.
* **Resins (furan, phenolic, urea-formaldehyde):** Used in shell, hot-box, and cold-box processes. Cure rapidly with heat or gas catalysts.
* **Dextrin/Cereal (organic):** Provides green strength and improves collapsibility by charring at low temperature.
* **Sodium silicate (inorganic):** Hardens when exposed to CO₂ gas, no baking required.

**(iii) Additives — 1 to 4%**

* **Iron oxide (Fe₂O₃) — 1 to 2%:** Prevents sintering of sand onto casting surface by forming fayalite slag.
* **Sea coal/Pitch — 2 to 4%:** Generates lustrous carbon on heating, preventing metal penetration and improving surface finish.
* **Wood flour — 0.5 to 1.5%:** Improves collapsibility by burning out and leaving voids.

**(iv) Moisture — 2 to 5%**

* Activates cereal binders and provides workability.
* Critical for oil-bonded cores (added in controlled quantity before oil is added).
* Excess moisture generates steam blowholes during pouring.

---

**(b) Describe the step-by-step process of making a dry sand (oil-bonded) core, including mixing, ramming, baking, and inspection. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Core Sand Preparation**

Silica sand of GFN 50–70 is selected, sieved, and dried to remove residual moisture (to <0.5%). This ensures uniform binder coating.

**Step 2: Mixing (in Muller or Core Sand Mixer)**

The dry sand is first charged into the muller. The mixing sequence is critical:

1. Add **dry sand** → mull for 30 sec.
2. Add **cereal (dextrin) + iron oxide + sea coal** → mull for 60 sec (dry mix).
3. Add **water (3–4%)** → mull for 90 sec (wets the cereal).
4. Add **linseed oil (1.5–2.5%) LAST** → mull for 120 sec (oil coats the prepared mix).

**Step 3: Core Box Preparation**

A **core box** (wooden or metal, often split-type) is cleaned, dusted with parting powder (lycopodium or talc), and assembled. Vents (small slots or vent wires) are pre-placed to allow gas escape.

**Step 4: Ramming**

The mixed core sand is filled into the core box and rammed manually or by **core blowing machine**. The ramming must be uniform — too soft gives low strength, too hard reduces permeability. A **strike-off bar** removes excess sand.

**Step 5: Stripping**

After gentle ramming, the core box is inverted or the halves are separated, and the green core is carefully extracted. The vent wires remain embedded in the core (they are removed after baking, leaving vent passages).

**Step 6: Baking (Curing)**

The green cores are placed on baking trays and loaded into a **drying oven** at:

$$
T_{bake} = 200 \pm 10 \text{ °C}, \quad t_{bake} = 1 \text{ to } 2 \text{ hours}
$$

During baking, the linseed oil undergoes **oxidative polymerisation**, forming a rigid, strong film that binds the sand grains. The dextrin chars and provides collapsibility.

**Step 7: Inspection**

The baked cores are inspected for:
* **Strength** (visual handling test, drop test).
* **Surface finish** (no cracks, no soft spots).
* **Dimensional accuracy** (gauges/templates).
* **Vent holes clear**.
* **No surface defects** (cracks, fins, soft patches).

**Step 8: Storage**

Cores are stored in dry conditions and transported to the moulding station in **core carriers** to prevent damage.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice: Core Types & Curing Methods

`[KTU University Exam – KTU 2024 Scheme Model Paper]`

**(a) Compare the various types of cores based on binder system. Highlight the advantages and limitations of each. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

| Core Type | Binder | Curing Method | Advantages | Limitations |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Oil-bonded (dry sand)** | Linseed oil + dextrin | Oven baking at 200 °C, 1–2 hr | Simple, low cost, good collapsibility, widely applicable | Long cycle time, energy intensive, slow production |
| **Shell core (Croning)** | Phenolic resin (novolac) coated sand | Hot core box at 230 °C, 30 sec | Very high strength, thin sections, excellent surface finish, high production rate | Higher binder cost, limited to small/medium cores, requires resin-coated sand |
| **Hot-box** | Furan/phenolic resin + acid catalyst (NH₄Cl) | Heated core box at 200 °C, 30 sec | Fast cycle, good surface finish, mass production | Acid fumes (corrosive), need heated tooling |
| **Cold-box (Ashland)** | Phenolic resin + polyisocyanate (MDI) | Amine gas (TEA/DMEA) at room temp, 10 sec | Very fast, ambient cure, no heat, excellent accuracy, no baking | Complex gas handling, amine is toxic, expensive setup |
| **CO₂ silicate** | Sodium silicate (Na₂SiO₃) | CO₂ gas blown, 10–30 sec | Fast, no heat, no toxic fumes, low cost | Poor collapsibility (sinters on heating), hygroscopic, sand reuse difficult |
| **Green sand core** | Bentonite + water | None (used as is) | No baking, very low cost, high collapsibility | Low strength, limited to small cores, not for hot metal |
| **Furan no-bake** | Furan resin + acid catalyst (H₃PO₄/PTSA) | Ambient, 10–30 min | Long bench life, fast cure, no heat | Acid catalyst is corrosive, sulfur content causes gas defects in steel |

---

**(b) Explain the CO₂ sodium silicate core process. Discuss its advantages over the oil-bonded process. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:**

The CO₂ process uses **sodium silicate (water glass, Na₂SiO₃)** as the binder. When CO₂ gas is blown through the rammed core, the sodium silicate undergoes a chemical reaction that converts it from a soluble to an insoluble, rigid gel:

$$
\text{Na}_2\text{SiO}_3 + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{SiO}_2 \text{ (gel)}
$$

The **SiO₂ gel** forms a strong, rigid skeleton that bonds the sand grains together.

**Process Steps:**

1. **Mixing:** Silica sand (95–98%) is mixed with sodium silicate solution (3–5%) in a muller for 2–3 minutes.
2. **Ramming:** The mix is rammed into a core box (no heat required) using conventional methods.
3. **Gassing:** CO₂ gas at 1.5–2.0 bar pressure is blown through the core for 10–30 seconds.
4. **Stripping:** The hardened core is removed from the box immediately (no waiting time).
5. **Storage:** Cores are stored in dry conditions to prevent moisture absorption (sodium silicate is hygroscopic).

**Advantages of CO₂ Process over Oil-Bonded Process:**

1. **No baking required** → saves energy, time, and oven infrastructure.
2. **Faster production** → cycle time of 1–2 minutes vs 2+ hours for oil-bonded.
3. **Higher green strength** → cores can be handled immediately after gassing.
4. **No toxic fumes** → no polymerisation gases like oil-bonded cores.
5. **Better dimensional accuracy** → no thermal distortion from baking.
6. **Lower energy cost** → no oven operation.
7. **Suitable for large cores** → which would be difficult/impossible to bake uniformly in ovens.
8. **Easier reclamation** → sand can be mechanically reclaimed by scrubbing (silicate is water-soluble).

**Limitations:**

1. **Poor collapsibility** → sodium silicate sinters at high temperature, leading to difficulty in shakeout and risk of hot tears.
2. **Hygroscopic** → cores absorb atmospheric moisture, lose strength, and cause surface defects.
3. **Reduced storage life** → must be used within days.
4. **Sand reclamation is water-intensive** → silicate is soluble only in water, requiring drying.
5. **Not suitable for thin sections in long flow paths** → gas penetration may be incomplete.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (14-Mark Questions):**
> 1. **Missing the "Why" — the Function:** Many students *list* the core sand constituents but **do not explain WHY each is added**. Examiner's tip: For every ingredient, you must state its **function**. Listing without function = max 3 of 7 marks.
> 2. **Mixing Sequence Error:** Writing "oil is added first" in the muller — wrong. Oil is added **last** so it coats the outside of the prepared mix. Examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 3. **Units Error in Permeability Formula:** Writing the unit as "cm²/g" or omitting units entirely. Correct unit is **cm³·cm/(cm²·g·min)** or simply the dimensionless **AFS permeability number**.
> 4. **Core Print Confusion:** Many students wrongly write "core print is a part of the core." It is a part of the **pattern** that creates a **seat in the mould** for the core to sit in. Exam penalty: 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing Green Strength vs Dry Strength:** Green strength = strength of **unbaked** mix (important for handling). Dry strength = strength of **baked** mix (important for service). Mixing these up = 1 mark lost.
> 6. **No sketch in long answers:** KTU ESE paper valuation includes **sketch marks** (1–2 marks reserved). Always include a **labelled sketch** (even hand-drawn description in text) of core placement in mould.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FINAL REVISION BULLET POINTS — KTU 2024 SCHEME**

* **Definition:** Core = refractory sand mass inserted into mould to form internal features in a casting.
* **Core Components:** Silica sand (92–96%) + Binder (1–3%) + Additives (1–4%) + Moisture (2–5%).
* **Five Core Properties:** Refractoriness, Permeability, Dry Strength, Collapsibility, Surface Finish.
* **Permeability Number Formula:**

$$
P = \frac{V \cdot h}{A \cdot p \cdot t} \approx \frac{V}{4t}
$$

* **GFN Formula:**

$$
\text{GFN} = \frac{\sum (m_i \times \text{AFS}_i)}{M}
$$

* **Baking Temperature (Oil-Bonded):** 200 °C ± 10 °C for 1–2 hours.
* **Core Print:** Feature on **pattern**, creates seat in **mould**.
* **Chaplets:** Metal supports used when core tends to float due to metallostatic buoyancy.
* **Mixing Order:** Sand → Cereal → Additives → Water → **Oil LAST**.
* **CO₂ Process:** Sodium silicate + CO₂ gas → silica gel binder, no baking.
* **Shell Core:** Resin-coated sand + hot core box (230 °C, 30 sec).
* **Cold-Box Core:** Phenolic + isocyanate + amine gas catalyst, ambient temperature.
* **Iron Oxide (Fe₂O₃):** Prevents sand sintering onto casting.
* **Sea Coal:** Generates lustrous carbon, prevents metal penetration.
* **Dextrin:** Provides green strength + improves collapsibility by charring.
* **Core Box:** Split-type tooling used to shape the core.
* **Vent Wires:** Embedded in cores to provide gas escape passages.
* **Baking removes moisture, polymerises oil binder, develops dry strength.**
* **Cool sand after baking** before use to prevent thermal shock to mould.
* **Core storage:** Keep dry, use within shelf life.
* **Compensation allowance:** Cores are made slightly **oversize** to account for metal shrinkage.
* **Core sand vs Moulding sand:** Core sand has **lower clay (0–2%)** and **higher binder (oil/resin)** for higher dry strength and permeability.
* **Shakeout:** After solidification, cores must collapse to allow casting shrinkage.
* **Classification by Position:** Horizontal, Vertical, Hanging, Balanced, Ram-up.
* **Hanging core:** Suspended from cope by its own print (no support from below).
* **Balanced core:** Long horizontal core with prints at both ends, supported by both cope and drag prints.
* **Critical balance point:** When the core's centre of gravity coincides with the metal's metallostatic centre of pressure, no chaplets are needed.
* **Types of binders:** Organic (oil, resin, cereal) vs Inorganic (sodium silicate, bentonite).
* **Curing methods:** Oven bake, hot-box, cold-box (gas), no-bake, CO₂-set.
* **Shell core advantage:** Excellent collapsibility + thin walls + high strength.
* **Reclamation of core sand:** Mechanical scrubbing + thermal (oil-bonded) or water-wash (sodium silicate).
* **For ferrous castings:** Always use high SiO₂ sand (>95%) and add iron oxide to prevent burn-on.
* **For non-ferrous (brass/aluminium):** Lower baking temperature, less oil, can use silica flour for fine finish.
* **Inspection tests for cores:** Visual + drop test + scratch test + permeability test.
* **Surface defects from poor cores:** Blow holes (low permeability), hot tears (poor collapsibility), metal penetration (low refractoriness), veining (thermal expansion mismatch).
* **Most critical KTU exam topic from this section:** Core sand composition + Permeability formula + Mixing sequence.

> **END OF MODULE 1 — CORES NOTES**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme | PBMRT404 Manufacturing Process | Outcome-Based Education Aligned*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chaplets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBMRT404 - MANUFACTURING PROCESS > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Chaplets # MODULE 1 — CHAPLETS IN SAND CASTING ## 1.1 Core Technical Definition In the discipline of foundry engineering and sand casting (a core process of Manufacturing Technology), a **Chaplet** is defined as a small, purpose-built metallic insert that is placed inside the sand mold cavity to support, locate, and retain the sand core in its exact intended position throughout the pouring, solidification, and cooling stages of casting. Chaplets are designed to: 1. Resist the **buoyancy force** generated by the molten metal that tends to float the core upward. 2. Resist the **static gravitational weight** of the core itself (so the core does not sag downward). 3. Resist **mechanical displacement forces** caused by the dynamic flow of molten metal during pouring. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 HIGHLIGHT — Chaplets** > Chaplets are classified as *sacrificial supports* in foundry practice. They are made from a metal whose melting point is **lower than or equal to** the melting point of the cast metal, so they melt and **fuse metallurgically** with the casting body, becoming a part of the final casting. ### Standard Materials Used for Chaplets - Mild steel - Cast iron - Tin - Lead - Bronze - Copper-coated steel > [!NOTE] > **Core Rule:** The chaplet material must possess a melting point that is **lower than or at least equal to** the parent casting metal. This guarantees complete fusion with the casting. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview Imagine you are building a hollow concrete pillar. The hollow inside is created by placing a **hollow plastic/wooden pipe (the core)** into the formwork before pouring concrete (the molten metal). But here is the problem: the wet concrete is denser and will try to *float* the pipe upward, just as oil floats on water. To stop the pipe from floating away, you would **tie it down with metal rods** to the bottom of the formwork. **In this analogy:** - The **pipe** = the **sand core** of a casting - The **wet concrete** = the **molten metal** being poured - The **metal rods tying the pipe** = the **CHAPLETS** So, in plain English, a chaplet is simply a **"metal anchor"** that holds a sand core in its correct position inside a sand mold, so that the molten metal flows around it and forms a clean, dimensionally accurate hollow interior in the finished casting. > [!NOTE] > **Quick Intuition:** A chaplet is to a casting core what a *rope* is to a *balloon* — it holds the core where it belongs, against forces trying to displace it. # 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS ## 2.1 Why Are Chaplets Needed? — The Driving Forces Three physical forces act on a core inside the mold cavity: ### 2.1.1 Buoyancy Force (Upward Force) Molten metal is significantly denser than a sand core. According to Archimedes' principle, the molten metal exerts an upward buoyant force on the core equal to the weight of the molten metal displaced by the core. ### 2.1.2 Gravitational Force (Downward Force) The core has its own weight which pulls it downward. ### 2.1.3 Dynamic Flow Force (Lateral Force) When molten metal is poured, the high-velocity stream of liquid metal exerts a drag force on the core, which can shift it laterally. --- ## 2.2 Functions of Chaplets (Bulleted Logic) 1. **Position Holding:** Chaplets hold the core rigidly at the exact location specified in the design, ensuring **uniform wall thickness** in the casting. 2. **Buoyancy Counterbalance:** They counteract the upward thrust exerted by molten metal on the core. 3. **Fusion Ability:** They melt and fuse with the parent metal, eliminating a parting line in the final casting. 4. **Mechanical Stability:** They absorb and resist the dynamic forces during pouring. 5. **Clean Casting:** They produce clean, sharp internal cavities without core sag or shift. --- ## 2.3 Classification of Chaplets Chaplets are broadly classified based on the **location** at which they are used in the mold. | **S.No.** | **Type of Chaplet** | **Position in Mold** | **Function** | |----------:|---------------------|----------------------|--------------| | 1 | **Stud / Button Chaplet** | Bottom of the mold (on the drag face) | Supports the core from sagging due to its own weight | | 2 | **Core / Diaphragm Chaplet** | Placed on a horizontal surface of the core itself | Supports a long horizontal core from bending/sagging | | 3 | **Pipe / Cylindrical Chaplet** | Vertical surfaces between core halves | Holds the two halves of a vertical core together | | 4 | **Box / Double-end Chaplet** | Between cope and drag | Supports the core vertically between top and bottom mold halves | | 5 | **Spreader Chaplet** | Any location | Spreads the load and prevents core distortion | | 6 | **Bridge / Stake Chaplet** | Bottom of drag | Provides an extended support for long horizontal cores | --- ## 2.4 Design Considerations for Chaplets The following engineering rules must be followed when selecting or designing chaplets: - The chaplet should be made of a metal that is **chemically compatible** with the casting alloy. - The chaplet must have sufficient **load-bearing cross-sectional area** to withstand buoyancy without crushing or bending. - The number of chaplets must be sufficient to divide the load evenly. - Chaplets should be **dry and clean** (free of rust, oil, moisture) before placement. - Chaplets must be **pre-coated with a refractory wash** to prevent oxidation. --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Chaplets > [!IMPORTANT] > The following formulas are high-yield and frequently appear in KTU 2024 scheme university examinations. | **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula / Expression** | **Units** | |--------------|:----------:|--------------------------|:---------:| | Buoyancy force on core | $F_b$ | $F_b = V_c \cdot \rho_m \cdot g$ | $\text{N}$ | | Net upward force on core | $F_{net}$ | $F_{net} = V_c \cdot \rho_m \cdot g - W_c$ | $\text{N}$ | | Volume of core | $V_c$ | $V_c = A_c \cdot h_c$ | $\text{m}^3$ | | Number of chaplets | $n$ | $n = \dfrac{F_{net}}{P_a}$ | — | | Cross-sectional area per chaplet | $A_{ch}$ | $A_{ch} = \dfrac{F_{net}}{n \cdot \sigma_a}$ | $\text{m}^2$ | | Diameter of cylindrical chaplet | $d$ | $d = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot A_{ch}}{\pi}}$ | $\text{m}$ | **Where:** - $V_c$ = Total volume of core submerged in molten metal - $\rho_m$ = Density of molten metal (kg/m³) - $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m/s²) - $W_c$ = Weight of the core (N) - $A_c$ = Cross-sectional area of the core (m²) - $h_c$ = Height of the core (m) - $P_a$ = Load-carrying capacity of one chaplet (N) - $\sigma_a$ = Allowable stress of chaplet material (N/m²) - $n$ = Number of chaplets - $A_{ch}$ = Cross-sectional area of one chaplet (m²) > [!NOTE] > The density values of common metals (used as **standard constants**) in the solid/molten state: > - Cast iron: $\rho \approx 7200 \text{ kg/m}^3$ > - Mild steel: $\rho \approx 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$ > - Aluminum: $\rho \approx 2700 \text{ kg/m}^3$ > - Brass: $\rho \approx 8500 \text{ kg/m}^3$ > - Bronze: $\rho \approx 8800 \text{ kg/m}^3$ --- ## 2.6 Engineering Utility Chaplets are used extensively in: - **Foundry industry** for hollow castings (pipes, engine blocks, gear housings, valve bodies). - **Aerospace casting** for large turbine casings. - **Automotive industry** for cylinder blocks and manifolds. - **Heavy machinery** for pump and compressor bodies. Without chaplets, the dimensional accuracy of hollow castings would be impossible, and **core shift defects** would dominate production rejects. # 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATION & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation of the Required Number and Size of Chaplets ### Step 1 — Establish the Physical Situation Consider a sand core of: - Cross-sectional area $= A_c$ - Vertical height submerged in molten metal $= h_c$ - Total weight of the core $= W_c$ The molten metal has a density of $\rho_m$ and a free surface at the top. ### Step 2 — Write the Buoyancy Force Equation By **Archimedes' Principle**, the upward buoyant force on a body immersed in a fluid is equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body. $$F_b = (\text{Volume of core submerged}) \times (\text{Density of molten metal}) \times g$$ Mathematically: $$F_b = V_c \cdot \rho_m \cdot g$$ ### Step 3 — Express the Volume of the Core For a core of uniform cross-section: $$V_c = A_c \cdot h_c$$ Substituting back: $$F_b = A_c \cdot h_c \cdot \rho_m \cdot g$$ ### Step 4 — Compute the Net Upward Force The total downward force on the core is its own weight $W_c$. The net upward force that the chaplets must counteract is: $$F_{net} = F_b - W_c$$ Substituting the expression for $F_b$: $$F_{net} = A_c \cdot h_c \cdot \rho_m \cdot g - W_c$$ ### Step 5 — Apply Force Equilibrium to the Chaplet System If we use $n$ identical chaplets, the force is shared equally: $$F_{per \, chaplet} = \frac{F_{net}}{n}$$ ### Step 6 — Apply the Strength of Materials Condition Each chaplet has a cross-sectional area $A_{ch}$ and is made of a material with allowable stress $\sigma_a$. For the chaplet not to fail in compression: $$\sigma_{actual} \leq \sigma_a$$ That is: $$\frac{F_{per \, chaplet}}{A_{ch}} \leq \sigma_a$$ ### Step 7 — Derive the Required Cross-Sectional Area of One Chaplet Rearranging the inequality to find the minimum required area: $$A_{ch} \geq \frac{F_{per \, chaplet}}{\sigma_a} = \frac{F_{net}}{n \cdot \sigma_a}$$ Therefore, the design equation for the chaplet cross-sectional area is: $$\boxed{A_{ch} = \frac{A_c \cdot h_c \cdot \rho_m \cdot g - W_c}{n \cdot \sigma_a}}$$ ### Step 8 — Derive the Number of Chaplets Required The number of chaplets is usually derived from the load capacity of a single chaplet $P_a$: $$n = \frac{F_{net}}{P_a} = \frac{A_c \cdot h_c \cdot \rho_m \cdot g - W_c}{P_a}$$ ### Step 9 — Diameter of a Cylindrical Chaplet For a solid cylindrical chaplet of diameter $d$: $$A_{ch} = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$ Solving for $d$: $$d = \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot A_{ch}}{\pi}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Design Safety Factor:** In industrial practice, engineers add a **safety factor (S.F.) of 1.5 to 2.0** to the calculated $A_{ch}$ to account for unexpected load surges during pouring. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Style) ### Problem Statement A cylindrical sand core of diameter **150 mm** and height **250 mm** is to be supported inside a cast iron mold. The weight of the core is **8.5 N**. The chaplet material is mild steel with an allowable stress of **70 MPa**. Assume the density of molten cast iron is **7100 kg/m³** and a chaplet can safely support a maximum of **90 N** of load. Calculate: - (a) The buoyancy force on the core - (b) The number of chaplets required - (c) The diameter of each chaplet ### Given Data - Core diameter $D_c = 0.150$ m - Core height $h_c = 0.250$ m - Core weight $W_c = 8.5$ N - Density of molten cast iron $\rho_m = 7100$ kg/m³ - Allowable stress $\sigma_a = 70 \times 10^6$ N/m² - Capacity per chaplet $P_a = 90$ N - Acceleration due to gravity $g = 9.81$ m/s² ### Solution #### (a) Buoyancy Force The cross-sectional area of the cylindrical core: $$A_c = \frac{\pi D_c^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.150)^2}{4} = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2$$ The volume of the core: $$V_c = A_c \cdot h_c = 0.01767 \times 0.250 = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$ The buoyancy force: $$F_b = V_c \cdot \rho_m \cdot g = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 7100 \times 9.81$$ $$F_b = 307.6 \text{ N}$$ #### (b) Number of Chaplets The net upward force: $$F_{net} = F_b - W_c = 307.6 - 8.5 = 299.1 \text{ N}$$ The number of chaplets: $$n = \frac{F_{net}}{P_a} = \frac{299.1}{90} = 3.32$$ Since we cannot have a fractional chaplet, we round up: $$\boxed{n = 4 \text{ chaplets}}$$ #### (c) Diameter of Each Chaplet The cross-sectional area required per chaplet: $$A_{ch} = \frac{F_{net}}{n \cdot \sigma_a} = \frac{299.1}{4 \times 70 \times 10^6}$$ $$A_{ch} = 1.068 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$$ The diameter: $$d = \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot A_{ch}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 1.068 \times 10^{-6}}{\pi}}$$ $$d = 1.167 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.167 \text{ mm}$$ $$\boxed{d \approx 1.2 \text{ mm}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineer's Practical Note:** Although calculation gives $\approx 1.2$ mm, foundry practice typically uses standard chaplet sizes (3 mm, 5 mm, 8 mm, 12 mm) for ease of availability and placement. # 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Chaplet Placement in a Sand Mold The block diagram below represents the systematic position of chaplets in a typical sand mold with a horizontal core. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["TOP MOLD HALF
COPE"] --> nodeB["POURING CUP
AND SPRUE"] nodeB --> nodeC["MOLTEN METAL
INCOMING STREAM"] nodeC --> nodeD["UPPER MOLD
CAVITY"] nodeD --> nodeE["HORIZONTAL
SAND CORE"] nodeE --> nodeF["CHAPLET 1
TOP ANCHOR"] nodeE --> nodeG["CHAPLET 2
BOTTOM SUPPORT"] nodeE --> nodeH["CHAPLET 3
SIDE STABILIZER"] nodeF --> nodeI["TOP MOLD SURFACE"] nodeG --> nodeJ["BOTTOM MOLD SURFACE
DRAG"] nodeH --> nodeK["SIDE WALL OF
CORE BOX"] nodeI --> nodeL["FINAL CASTING
WITH HOLLOW CAVITY"] nodeJ --> nodeL nodeK --> nodeL nodeL --> nodeM["FUSED CHAPLETS
INSIDE CASTING"] ``` --- ## 4.2 Classification Flowchart — Types of Chaplets ```mermaid flowchart TD start["CHAPLETS
CLASSIFICATION"] --> q1{"Position of
Support Required"} q1 -- "Bottom of mold" --> c1["STUD / BUTTON
CHAPLET"] q1 -- "Between top & bottom" --> c2["BOX CHAPLET
DOUBLE END"] q1 -- "On horizontal core" --> c3["CORE / DIAPHRAGM
CHAPLET"] q1 -- "Between vertical core halves" --> c4["PIPE / CYLINDRICAL
CHAPLET"] q1 -- "Spreading load" --> c5["SPREADER
CHAPLET"] q1 -- "Long horizontal core" --> c6["BRIDGE / STAKE
CHAPLET"] c1 --> note1["Supports core
weight from below"] c2 --> note2["Spans the full
height of mold"] c3 --> note3["Prevents core
sagging mid-span"] c4 --> note4["Connects two
vertical core halves"] c5 --> note5["Distributes load
over wide area"] c6 --> note6["Acts like a small
beam bridge"] ``` --- ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Force System on a Core ```mermaid flowchart LR block1["GRAVITY
FORCE ON CORE
Wc - DOWNWARD"] --> addNode((+)) block2["BUOYANCY
FORCE FROM METAL
Fb - UPWARD"] --> addNode addNode --> eqNode["NET FORCE ON CORE
Fnet = Fb - Wc"] eqNode --> chapletSys["DISTRIBUTED TO
N CHAPLETS"] chapletSys --> chaplet1["CHAPLET 1
Force = Fnet / n"] chapletSys --> chaplet2["CHAPLET 2
Force = Fnet / n"] chapletSys --> chaplet3["CHAPLET 3
Force = Fnet / n"] chaplet1 --> stressCheck["STRESS CHECK
Sigma = Force / Area"] chaplet2 --> stressCheck chaplet3 --> stressCheck stressCheck --> result{"Sigma LESS
THAN Sigma_allowable"} result -- "YES" --> safe["DESIGN IS SAFE
CHAPLETS HOLD CORE"] result -- "NO" --> fail["DESIGN FAILS
INCREASE DIAMETER OR COUNT"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The architecture shows the sequential engineering logic — compute total force, divide among chaplets, then verify each chaplet's stress remains below allowable limits. # 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Question Paper]** *Define a chaplet. List any two materials commonly used for making chaplets.* **Model Answer:** A **chaplet** is a small metallic insert placed inside a sand mold to support and retain the sand core in its proper position against the buoyancy force exerted by the molten metal during pouring. It is designed to melt and fuse with the parent casting metal. **Common chaplet materials:** 1. **Mild steel** — most widely used for ferrous castings. 2. **Tin or lead** — used for non-ferrous castings (lower melting point). 3. **Cast iron** — also used for cast iron castings. *(Any two materials are sufficient. 3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for materials.)* --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Supplementary Exam]** *State the functions of chaplets. Why is it necessary for the chaplet material to have a melting point lower than the casting metal?* **Model Answer:** **Functions of chaplets:** 1. Hold the core in correct position. 2. Counteract the buoyancy force of molten metal. 3. Fuse with the casting to form a uniform metallic body. 4. Prevent core sag and core shift defects. **Why chaplet melting point must be lower:** The chaplet must **melt and metallurgically fuse** with the parent casting metal so that no gap or discontinuity exists at the location of the chaplet. If the chaplet had a higher melting point, it would remain solid, creating a **cold shut defect** or a **discontinuity in the casting body**, weakening the final component. *(3 marks: 1.5 for functions, 1.5 for melting point explanation.)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:** > 1. **Never forget the units** — In numerical problems, KTU examiners strictly deduct marks for missing units. > 2. **Always assume standard values of $g = 9.81$ m/s²** unless otherwise stated in the question paper. > 3. **Round up the number of chaplets** to the next whole number, never round down. > 4. **Show intermediate steps** — KTU valuation awards partial marks for each correct intermediate calculation. > 5. **State assumptions clearly** — Mention "Assuming core is fully submerged" or "Using standard density value". --- ### PART B — Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, University Exam]** **(a)** Explain in detail the various **types of chaplets** used in sand casting with neat sketches of their placement in the mold. State the function of each type. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A cylindrical core of diameter **200 mm** and height **300 mm** is used in a steel casting. The weight of the core is **15 N**. The chaplets are made of mild steel with an allowable stress of **75 MPa**, and each chaplet can carry a maximum load of **110 N**. The density of molten steel is **7800 kg/m³**. Calculate: - (i) The buoyancy force on the core - (ii) The number of chaplets required - (iii) The diameter of each chaplet *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution to Question A:** #### (a) Types of Chaplets (7 marks) | **Type** | **Description** | **Placement** | |----------|-----------------|---------------| | **Stud / Button Chaplet** | Small-headed nail-like chaplet | Bottom of drag to support core weight | | **Core / Diaphragm Chaplet** | Flat plate-like support | Placed on horizontal surface of core | | **Box / Double-end Chaplet** | Has a head on both ends | Spans between cope and drag | | **Pipe / Cylindrical Chaplet** | Hollow tube | Between vertical core halves | | **Spreader Chaplet** | Wide flat support | Distributes load on soft cores | | **Bridge / Stake Chaplet** | Long bar-like | Supports long horizontal cores | *Each type with proper function: ~1 mark per type × 6 types = 6 marks + 1 mark for neat sketch/schematic.* #### (b) Numerical Solution (7 marks) **Given Data:** - $D_c = 0.200$ m - $h_c = 0.300$ m - $W_c = 15$ N - $\sigma_a = 75 \times 10^6$ N/m² - $P_a = 110$ N - $\rho_m = 7800$ kg/m³ - $g = 9.81$ m/s² **Step 1: Cross-sectional area of core** $$A_c = \frac{\pi D_c^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.200)^2}{4} = 0.03142 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2: Volume of core** $$V_c = A_c \cdot h_c = 0.03142 \times 0.300 = 9.425 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 3: Buoyancy force** $$F_b = V_c \cdot \rho_m \cdot g = 9.425 \times 10^{-3} \times 7800 \times 9.81 = 721.2 \text{ N}$$ **Step 4: Net upward force** $$F_{net} = F_b - W_c = 721.2 - 15 = 706.2 \text{ N}$$ **Step 5: Number of chaplets** $$n = \frac{F_{net}}{P_a} = \frac{706.2}{110} = 6.42$$ Rounding up: $$\boxed{n = 7 \text{ chaplets}}$$ **Step 6: Cross-sectional area per chaplet** $$A_{ch} = \frac{F_{net}}{n \cdot \sigma_a} = \frac{706.2}{7 \times 75 \times 10^6} = 1.345 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 7: Diameter of chaplet** $$d = \sqrt{\frac{4 A_{ch}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 1.345 \times 10^{-6}}{\pi}} = 1.309 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.309 \text{ mm}$$ $$\boxed{d \approx 1.31 \text{ mm (use standard 3 mm chaplet)}}$$ --- ### PART B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** What is a chaplet? Discuss the **design considerations** and **material selection** for chaplets in foundry practice. List the **common defects** caused by improper chaplet use. *(7 marks)* **(b)** With the help of a labeled diagram, explain the **placement and functioning of stud chaplets and box chaplets** in a sand mold. Why is **preheating of chaplets** sometimes recommended? *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution to Question B:** #### (a) Design Considerations and Defects (7 marks) **Definition (1 mark):** A chaplet is a small metallic insert placed in a sand mold to support the core against buoyancy, gravity, and dynamic forces. **Design Considerations (3 marks):** 1. **Melting point:** Must be lower than or equal to the parent metal. 2. **Mechanical strength:** Must withstand buoyancy without failure. 3. **Corrosion resistance:** Should not oxidize excessively before pouring. 4. **Number and placement:** Sufficient chaplets must be placed symmetrically. 5. **Size standardization:** Should match available standard sizes. **Material Selection (1 mark):** Tin, lead, mild steel, cast iron, copper-coated steel — chosen based on compatibility with parent metal. **Common Defects (2 marks):** 1. **Misrun or cold shut** — if chaplet is too cold 2. **Porosity around chaplet** — gas evolution 3. **Hard spot** — if chaplet is harder than parent metal (machining problems) 4. **Crack initiation** — at fusion zone #### (b) Placement Diagram and Preheating (7 marks) **Sketch (3 marks):** Show a labeled cross-section of a sand mold with: - Cope (top) - Drag (bottom) - Sand core - Stud chaplet on the bottom supporting core - Box chaplet spanning the entire mold height **Functioning of stud chaplet (1.5 marks):** Holds the core from below, preventing it from sagging under its own weight. The head of the stud provides a wide base for contact. **Functioning of box chaplet (1.5 marks):** Spans the entire depth of the mold, holding the core at a fixed elevation between the cope and drag. **Preheating (1 mark):** Preheating removes moisture, prevents thermal shock to the molten metal, and reduces gas generation that would otherwise cause porosity around the chaplet location. --- ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (Rapid Fire):** - **Definition:** A chaplet is a **metallic insert** that supports a **sand core** in a sand mold. - **Purpose:** To counteract **buoyancy**, **gravity**, and **flow forces** on the core. - **Material rule:** Chaplet melting point ≤ Casting metal melting point. - **Buoyancy formula:** $F_b = V_c \cdot \rho_m \cdot g$ - **Net force formula:** $F_{net} = F_b - W_c$ - **Number of chaplets:** $n = F_{net} / P_a$ (always round UP) - **Cross-sectional area:** $A_{ch} = F_{net} / (n \cdot \sigma_a)$ - **Diameter of cylindrical chaplet:** $d = \sqrt{4 A_{ch} / \pi}$ - **Six types of chaplets:** Stud, Core, Box, Pipe, Spreader, Bridge. - **Stud chaplet:** Used at the bottom of the drag to support core weight. - **Box chaplet:** Spans between cope and drag. - **Common defects from chaplets:** Cold shut, porosity, hard spots, fusion cracks. - **Standard molten metal densities (high yield):** Cast iron 7100, Steel 7800, Aluminum 2700 kg/m³. - **Always use $g = 9.81$ m/s²** unless otherwise stated. - **Engineer's safety factor:** 1.5 to 2.0 in industrial design. - **Preheating chaplets** reduces moisture and gas defects. - **Coating chaplets** with refractory wash prevents oxidation. - **Chaplets are sacrificial** — they melt and become part of the casting.

topics.title: Risers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT404 - MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Risers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🏭 RISERS IN SAND CASTING — KTU 2024 SCHEME COMPLETE NOTES

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Riser (Exothermic Feeder / Header):** A controlled reservoir of molten metal, attached to the casting cavity, that **compensates for solidification shrinkage** by feeding additional liquid metal into the casting as it cools and contracts.

Formally, a riser is the **last portion of the casting system to solidify**. Its primary engineering objective is to supply feed metal to the casting body to prevent the formation of **shrinkage cavities (voids, pipes, or porosity)** during the liquid-to-solid transformation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):** *“A riser is a mass of metal, usually larger in cross-section than the casting itself, which is allowed to remain molten (in a liquid state) longer than the casting and from which molten metal flows to compensate for solidification shrinkage.”*

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

Imagine a melting ice cube stuck to a hot frying pan. As it melts, the water escapes sideways leaving a hollow gap. Now imagine you pour **extra water on top of the ice cube continuously** from a small overhead cup — the water fills every gap as the ice melts, leaving no hollow space.

That overhead cup is exactly what a **riser** does in a casting:
- The **frying pan** = the casting (solidifies first).
- The **overhead cup** = the riser (solidifies last, sits on top).
- The **water** = liquid metal.
- The **gap formed by melting** = shrinkage cavity.

**Without a riser → shrinkage void inside the casting. With a riser → sound casting with internal integrity.**

### 1.3 Functions of a Riser (Board Favourite — 3-Mark Question)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Four Engineering Functions of a Riser:**
> 1. **Feed molten metal** to compensate for volumetric contraction during solidification.
> 2. **Provide a reservoir of liquid metal** that remains molten after the casting has solidified.
> 3. **Act as a gas vent**, allowing trapped gases and inclusions to escape upward into the riser (rather than into the casting).
> 4. **Indicate pouring completeness** — when metal overflows the riser, the foundry worker knows the mould is full.

### 1.4 Essential Properties of an Ideal Riser

| Property | Requirement | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Solidification Time** | Must be **longer** than the casting | So liquid metal is available until casting is fully solid |
| **Size (Volume)** | Must contain **≥ 3% to 5%** extra feed metal (typical) | Sufficient to fill shrinkage volume |
| **Position** | Placed at the **last-to-solidify** (hot spot) region | Ensures directional solidification |
| **Connection (Neck)** | Adequate gating/connection to casting | Allows liquid metal to flow freely before the neck freezes |
| **Shape** | Compact with **high surface-area-to-volume ratio minimization** | Cylindrical (height = diameter) is most efficient |
| **Yield** | Minimise metal in riser (it gets cropped) | Economic efficiency |

### 1.5 Concept of Solidification Shrinkage (Why Risers Are Needed)

> [!NOTE]
> **Shrinkage occurs in THREE stages during casting solidification:**
> 1. **Liquid Shrinkage** — contraction of liquid metal from pouring temperature to freezing point.
> 2. **Solidification Shrinkage** — volumetric contraction during phase change (liquid → solid). **Largest contributor.**
> 3. **Solid Shrinkage** — contraction of solid metal from freezing point to room temperature.

For **ferrous castings (cast iron, steel):** Total volumetric shrinkage ≈ **3% – 6%**.
For **non-ferrous castings (aluminum, copper):** Total shrinkage ≈ **4.5% – 6.5%**.

A riser is engineered to supply feed metal to offset the **solidification shrinkage**, since liquid and solid contractions are accommodated by **risers + chills + proper pouring rate**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Chvorinov's Rule — The Heart of Riser Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chvorinov's Rule (1938):** The solidification time of a casting is **directly proportional to the square of its volume-to-surface-area ratio**, known as the **modulus (M)**.

### Mathematical Form:

$$t_s = B \cdot \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^{n} = B \cdot M^{n}$$

Where:
- $t_s$ = **Solidification time** of the casting (in seconds or minutes)
- $B$ = **Mould constant** (depends on mould material, pouring temperature, latent heat, etc.)
- $V$ = **Volume** of the casting (m³ or cm³)
- $A$ = **Surface area** of the casting (m² or cm²)
- $M$ = **Modulus** of the casting = $V/A$ (units: metres or centimetres)
- $n$ = **Exponent** (typically $n = 2$ for sand castings)

### Physical Meaning:

The **Modulus (M = V/A)** is a measure of the **"casting's ability to retain heat."** A higher modulus means a larger volume per unit surface area → the casting has more thermal mass relative to its cooling surface → it solidifies **slower**.

For a riser to function correctly:

$$M_{Riser} \geq M_{Casting} \implies \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)_{Riser} \geq \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)_{Casting}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Strict inequality required.** The riser modulus must be **strictly greater (1.1 to 1.3 times)** than the casting modulus to ensure the riser is the **last to freeze**.

## 2.2 Riser Efficiency

> [!NOTE]
> **Riser Efficiency (η):** The fraction of the total feed metal actually used to compensate shrinkage; the rest becomes the riser itself and is wasted (cropped and remelted).

$$\eta = \frac{V_{feed}}{V_{riser} + V_{feed}}$$

Where:
- $V_{feed}$ = Volume of metal that actually flows into the casting to compensate shrinkage
- $V_{riser}$ = Volume of the riser itself (waste metal to be remelted)

A more commonly quoted formula uses the **volumetric shrinkage fraction (S)**:

$$\eta = \frac{S \cdot V_{casting}}{V_{riser} + S \cdot V_{casting}}$$

Typical efficiency values:
- **Top riser** → η = **10% – 15%**
- **Side riser** → η = **15% – 25%**
- **Sleeve riser** (insulated) → η = **30% – 50%**
- **Exothermic riser** → η = **40% – 60%**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M = \dfrac{V}{A}$ | Modulus definition | m or cm |
| 2 | $t_s = B \cdot M^{2}$ | Chvorinov's Rule (n=2) | seconds |
| 3 | $M_R \geq M_C$ | Riser design criterion | dimensionless ratio |
| 4 | $\eta = \dfrac{S \cdot V_c}{V_R + S \cdot V_c}$ | Riser efficiency | % |
| 5 | $V_R = \dfrac{S \cdot V_c}{\eta - S}$ | Riser volume (rearranged) | m³ or cm³ |
| 6 | $H : D = 1 : 1$ | Optimum cylindrical riser | — |
| 7 | $A : V \to \text{min}$ | Design goal (minimise surface for given volume) | — |
| 8 | $D = \left(\dfrac{6 V}{\pi}\right)^{1/3}$ | Diameter of cylindrical riser (H = D) | m |
| 9 | $H = D$ | Height of cylindrical riser | m |
| 10 | $A_{cyl} = \pi D H + 2 \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | Surface area of cylinder (incl. top & bottom) | m² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Note:** A **sphere has the lowest surface area for a given volume**, so theoretically a spherical riser is most efficient. However, spheres are difficult to mould → **cylinders with H = D are used in practice**, and sometimes **breather cores (insulating sleeves)** are added.

## 2.4 Types of Risers (KTU Board-Favourite)

> [!NOTE]
> **1. Top Riser (Headed Riser)**
> - Placed on **top of the casting**.
> - Gravity-fed (metal flows downward into shrinkage cavity).
> - Easy to remove (cropped). Low efficiency (10–15%).

> [!NOTE]
> **2. Side Riser (Lateral Riser)**
> - Placed on the **side** of the casting.
> - Liquid metal flows horizontally into the casting.
> - Higher efficiency than top riser (15–25%). Common in steel casting.

> [!NOTE]
> **3. Bottom Riser**
> - Placed beneath the casting (less common). Used for heavy ferrous castings.

> [!NOTE]
> **4. Blind Riser (Pop-off Riser)**
> - Fully enclosed in the mould (no top contact with sand).
> - Slow heat loss → high modulus → **higher efficiency**.
> - Difficult to inspect; common in automated foundries.

> [!NOTE]
> **5. Insulated (Sleeve) Riser**
> - Lined with insulating material (ceramic fibre blanket, exothermic sleeve).
> - Reduces heat loss → smaller riser size required.
> - Efficiency up to 50%.

> [!NOTE]
> **6. Exothermic Riser**
> - Contains exothermic compounds (Al + Fe₂O₃ thermite reaction).
> - Generates heat → stays liquid longer.
> - Highest efficiency (40–60%).

### 2.5 Directional vs. Non-Directional Solidification

| Concept | Description | Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| **Directional Solidification** | Solidification proceeds from **thinnest section to thickest, and from casting to riser** | Sound casting (no shrinkage voids) ✓ |
| **Non-Directional Solidification** | Random solidification throughout the casting | Shrinkage voids scattered internally ✗ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Critical Point:** The riser must always be placed at the **thermal centre / hot spot** of the casting — the location furthest from any chill or mould surface. This guarantees **directional solidification** from the casting body → toward the riser.

### 2.6 Caine's Method (Alternative Riser Sizing)

> [!NOTE]
> **Caine's Criterion:** A more empirical approach to riser design using section thickness ratios.

For a casting with section thickness $T_c$ and a riser of thickness $T_R$:

$$\frac{T_R}{T_c} \geq 1.5 \quad \text{(minimum ratio for steel castings)}$$

This is a **simpler, rule-of-thumb** method used in preliminary design before the modulus approach is applied.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Chvorinov's Rule (From First Principles)

Chvorinov's empirical observation is based on **Newton's Law of Cooling** and the principle of **latent heat extraction**.

### Step 1: Heat Balance
The total heat to be extracted from a casting of volume $V$ to completely solidify it:

$$Q = \rho \cdot V \cdot L$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = Density of molten metal (kg/m³)
- $L$ = Latent heat of fusion (J/kg)

### Step 2: Rate of Heat Extraction
According to Newton's Law of Cooling, the rate of heat extraction is proportional to the **surface area** and the **temperature difference**:

$$\frac{dQ}{dt} = h \cdot A \cdot (T_{metal} - T_{mould})$$

Where:
- $h$ = Heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $A$ = Surface area (m²)
- $(T_{metal} - T_{mould})$ = Temperature gradient (K)

### Step 3: Integration
Integrating the heat transfer equation (assuming $h$ and the temperature difference are approximately constant):

$$Q = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T \cdot t_s$$

### Step 4: Equate the Two Expressions for Q

$$\rho \cdot V \cdot L = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T \cdot t_s$$

Solving for $t_s$:

$$t_s = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{h \cdot \Delta T} \cdot \frac{V}{A}$$

### Step 5: Group the Constants

Define the mould constant $B$ as:

$$B = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{h \cdot \Delta T}$$

Substituting:

$$\boxed{t_s = B \cdot \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^{2} = B \cdot M^{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** The exponent n is empirically 2, but may vary from 1.5 to 2.0 depending on mould conditions. KTU uses **n = 2** by default.

## 3.2 Derivation: Riser Diameter (H = D Cylindrical Riser)

For a cylinder with $H = D$:
- Volume: $V_R = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot H = \dfrac{\pi D^3}{4}$
- Surface area (top + bottom + side): $A_R = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} + \pi D H = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{2} + \pi D^2 = \dfrac{3\pi D^2}{2}$
- Modulus: $M_R = \dfrac{V_R}{A_R} = \dfrac{\pi D^3 / 4}{3\pi D^2 / 2} = \dfrac{D}{6}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For a cylinder with H = D, the modulus is simply D/6.** This is a powerful shortcut used in KTU board problems.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (Typical KTU Style):**
> A cylindrical steel casting has a diameter of 200 mm and length 500 mm. Pouring temperature is 1620 °C; mould is green sand (B = 3.5 min/cm² for steel). The volumetric solidification shrinkage is **3%**. Design a **top riser** (cylindrical, H = D) using Chvorinov's rule. The riser efficiency is **12%**. Take a safety factor of 1.2 on the modulus.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Calculate the Modulus of the Casting (Cylinder)**

For a cylinder with $D_c = 20$ cm, $L_c = 50$ cm:

$$V_c = \frac{\pi D_c^2}{4} \cdot L_c = \frac{\pi (20)^2}{4} \cdot 50 = 15708 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$A_c = \pi D_c L_c + 2 \cdot \frac{\pi D_c^2}{4} = \pi (20)(50) + 2 \cdot \frac{\pi (400)}{4} = 3141.6 + 628.3 = 3769.9 \text{ cm}^2$$

$$M_c = \frac{V_c}{A_c} = \frac{15708}{3769.9} = 4.166 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 2: Required Riser Modulus (with safety factor 1.2)**

$$M_R = 1.2 \times M_c = 1.2 \times 4.166 = 5.0 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Riser Diameter (Using H = D shortcut)**

Since $M_R = D_R / 6$:

$$D_R = 6 \times M_R = 6 \times 5.0 = 30 \text{ cm}$$

$$H_R = D_R = 30 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 4: Calculate Riser Volume**

$$V_R = \frac{\pi (30)^3}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 27000}{4} = 21206 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 5: Verify with Shrinkage / Efficiency Check**

Required feed metal:

$$V_{feed} = S \cdot V_c = 0.03 \times 15708 = 471.2 \text{ cm}^3$$

Efficiency check:

$$\eta = \frac{V_{feed}}{V_R + V_{feed}} = \frac{471.2}{21206 + 471.2} = 0.0218 = 2.18\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **2.18% is far below the stated efficiency of 12%.** This means the given efficiency of 12% is too high for the chosen dimensions. We must **either increase the riser size OR use an insulated/exothermic sleeve to improve efficiency.**

**Step 6: Resize Using Efficiency Formula**

Rearranging the efficiency formula for $V_R$:

$$V_R = \frac{S \cdot V_c}{\eta - S} = \frac{0.03 \times 15708}{0.12 - 0.03} = \frac{471.2}{0.09} = 5236 \text{ cm}^3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a more realistic riser volume. From $V_R = \pi D^3 / 4$, the corrected diameter is $D_R \approx 18.8$ cm. But we must also verify the modulus criterion is satisfied.

**Step 7: Final Verification (Modulus check for D = 18.8 cm)**

$$M_R = \frac{18.8}{6} = 3.13 \text{ cm} \quad \text{(modulus requirement: } 5.0 \text{ cm)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Modulus criterion is NOT satisfied.** This means we must select the **larger** of the two diameters: **D = 30 cm (modulus-controlled) OR D = 18.8 cm (efficiency-controlled).**
>
> **Engineer's decision:** Choose the **larger** of the two for safety → **D = 30 cm, H = 30 cm**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Riser Type

| Casting Type | Riser Type | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Large steel casting | Side riser + exothermic sleeve | High feed requirement, slow cooling |
| Thin-section cast iron | Top riser (small) | Quick solidification, small shrinkage |
| Heavy-section cast iron | Blind riser + sleeve | Slow cooling → high modulus required |
| Aluminium alloy casting | Insulated top riser | High thermal conductivity, fast cooling |
| Continuous casting | No riser (water-cooled chill zone) | Directional cooling by design |

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define a riser. What are the functions of a riser in a sand casting process?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
A riser is a reservoir of molten metal, generally larger in cross-section than the casting itself, which is allowed to solidify later than the casting to feed molten metal into the casting to compensate for solidification shrinkage.

**Functions (2 Marks):**
1. **Feed molten metal** to compensate solidification shrinkage and prevent shrinkage cavities.
2. **Act as a vent** for gases and inclusions, allowing them to rise out of the casting body.
3. **Indicate pouring completeness** by overflowing.
4. **Provide directional solidification** by maintaining the last-to-freeze zone at the riser location.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is Chvorinov's rule? Write the formula and explain its significance in riser design.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement (1 Mark):**
Chvorinov's rule states that the solidification time of a casting is proportional to the square of its volume-to-surface-area ratio (modulus).

**Formula (1 Mark):**

$$t_s = B \cdot \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^{2} = B \cdot M^{2}$$

**Significance (1 Mark):**
The rule is the **fundamental design criterion for risers** — a riser must have a higher modulus (M) than the casting to ensure it solidifies **after** the casting, providing continuous feed metal.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Common student mistakes on riser design problems:
> 1. **Forgetting to use M = D/6 for H = D cylinder** — this shortcut saves 4 minutes and 2 marks.
> 2. **Not applying safety factor** on modulus (typically 1.1 to 1.3).
> 3. **Confusing volume and area** in the modulus formula.
> 4. **Ignoring the efficiency check** — students compute size from modulus but forget to verify against shrinkage-based efficiency.
> 5. **Drawing riser of equal size to casting** — riser diameter should be **~1.1 to 1.5 times** casting thickness.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**a) (7 Marks)** Explain Chvorinov's rule in detail. Derive the relationship between solidification time and modulus. How is this rule applied in the design of a riser?

**b) (7 Marks)** A cylindrical casting is 150 mm diameter and 400 mm long. The volumetric shrinkage of the metal is 4%. Design a top riser (cylindrical, H = D) such that the riser modulus is 1.2 times the casting modulus. Take B = 3.0 min/cm² for the metal-mould system.

#### **Solution to (a):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Chvorinov's rule (1938) is an empirical rule which states that the solidification time of a casting section is **directly proportional to the square of its modulus (V/A)**.

**Mathematical Statement (1 Mark):**

$$t_s = B \cdot \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^{2}$$

**Derivation (3 Marks):**

Based on heat balance:
- Heat to be removed: $Q = \rho V L$
- Rate of heat removal: $dQ/dt = h A \Delta T$
- Equating and integrating:

$$t_s = \frac{\rho L}{h \Delta T} \cdot \frac{V}{A} = B \cdot M^{2}$$

**Application to Riser Design (1 Mark):**
A riser must have a **modulus greater than that of the casting** to ensure directional solidification — i.e., the casting solidifies first while the riser remains molten and feeds shrinkage voids.

#### **Solution to (b):**

**Step 1: Casting dimensions**
- $D_c = 15$ cm, $L_c = 40$ cm
- $V_c = \dfrac{\pi (15)^2}{4} \cdot 40 = 7068.6 \text{ cm}^3$
- $A_c = \pi (15)(40) + 2 \cdot \dfrac{\pi (15)^2}{4} = 1884.96 + 353.43 = 2238.4 \text{ cm}^2$

**Step 2: Casting Modulus**

$$M_c = \frac{V_c}{A_c} = \frac{7068.6}{2238.4} = 3.158 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 3: Required Riser Modulus**

$$M_R = 1.2 \times M_c = 1.2 \times 3.158 = 3.79 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 4: Riser Dimensions (H = D)**

Since $M_R = D_R / 6$:

$$D_R = 6 \times 3.79 = 22.74 \text{ cm} \approx 23 \text{ cm}$$

$$H_R = 23 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 5: Riser Volume**

$$V_R = \frac{\pi (23)^3}{4} = 9547 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 6: Verification**

Required feed metal: $V_{feed} = 0.04 \times 7068.6 = 282.7 \text{ cm}^3$

Efficiency check:

$$\eta = \frac{V_{feed}}{V_R + V_{feed}} = \frac{282.7}{9547 + 282.7} = 2.88\%$$

> This is a low efficiency (acceptable for top risers in steel), and the **modulus criterion is satisfied** → the design is **safe**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] — Alternative Choice**

**a) (7 Marks)** What are the different types of risers used in foundries? Explain the difference between top riser, side riser, and blind riser with neat sketches.

**b) (7 Marks)** A cube casting of side 200 mm is made of cast iron. The volumetric shrinkage is 3.5% and the riser efficiency is 20%. Design a suitable cylindrical riser (H = D) and verify the design using the modulus method. Assume a safety factor of 1.2.

#### **Solution to (a):**

**Types of Risers (3 Marks):**

| Type | Position | Efficiency | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Top Riser | Above casting | 10–15% | Small castings, simple shapes |
| Side Riser | On side of casting | 15–25% | Steel castings, heavy sections |
| Blind Riser | Embedded in mould | 20–30% | Mass production, automatic lines |

**Detailed Explanation (4 Marks):**

1. **Top Riser:** Positioned directly above the casting cavity. Liquid metal flows **downward** under gravity to feed shrinkage. The riser neck is short. Easy to crop and reuse, but loses heat to the atmosphere → low efficiency.

2. **Side Riser:** Attached laterally to the casting. Metal flows **horizontally** through a feeder channel. Higher efficiency than top riser because the riser is surrounded by sand, losing heat more slowly. Commonly used for **steel castings** where directional solidification is critical.

3. **Blind Riser:** Fully enclosed in the sand mould; no atmospheric exposure. Retains heat the longest → highest modulus for given volume. Highest efficiency among simple risers. Used in **high-volume automated foundries**; difficult to visually inspect.

#### **Solution to (b):**

**Step 1: Cube Dimensions**
- Side = 20 cm
- $V_c = (20)^3 = 8000 \text{ cm}^3$
- $A_c = 6 \times (20)^2 = 2400 \text{ cm}^2$

**Step 2: Casting Modulus**

$$M_c = \frac{V_c}{A_c} = \frac{8000}{2400} = 3.33 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 3: Required Riser Modulus (with SF 1.2)**

$$M_R = 1.2 \times 3.33 = 4.0 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 4: Riser Dimensions (H = D)**

$$D_R = 6 \times M_R = 6 \times 4.0 = 24 \text{ cm}$$

$$H_R = 24 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 5: Riser Volume**

$$V_R = \frac{\pi (24)^3}{4} = 10857 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 6: Efficiency Verification**

$$V_{feed} = 0.035 \times 8000 = 280 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$\eta = \frac{280}{10857 + 280} = 2.52\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The efficiency (2.52%) is far below the required 20%.** The riser must be **resized** using the efficiency formula:
>
> $$V_R = \frac{S \cdot V_c}{\eta - S} = \frac{0.035 \times 8000}{0.20 - 0.035} = \frac{280}{0.165} = 1697 \text{ cm}^3$$
>
> $D_R = \left(\dfrac{4 \times 1697}{\pi}\right)^{1/3} = 12.97 \approx 13 \text{ cm}$
>
> $H_R = 13 \text{ cm}$
>
> **Final Design:** Choose the **larger** of (24 cm from modulus) and (13 cm from efficiency) → **D = H = 24 cm** (conservative engineering choice for a sound casting).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — RISERS**

### **Core Concepts (Must Memorise):**
- [ ] **Definition:** A riser is a molten metal reservoir that compensates for solidification shrinkage.
- [ ] **Primary Goal:** Riser must solidify **AFTER** the casting → modulus of riser > modulus of casting.
- [ ] **Chvorinov's Rule:** $t_s = B \cdot (V/A)^2 = B \cdot M^2$
- [ ] **Exponent n = 2** for sand castings (default in KTU problems).
- [ ] **Mould constant B** depends on metal, mould material, pouring temperature, latent heat.

### **Critical Shortcuts (Save Time in Exam):**
- [ ] For cylinder with **H = D** → $M = D/6$ (3 marks saved in 14-mark problems).
- [ ] For cube of side **a** → $M = a/6$ (same formula).
- [ ] For sphere of diameter **D** → $M = D/6$ (lowest possible modulus for a given volume).
- [ ] For plate of thickness **t** (large L & W) → $M = t/2$.

### **Riser Design Procedure (Step-by-Step):**
1. Calculate casting modulus $M_c = V_c / A_c$.
2. Apply safety factor (typically 1.2): $M_R = 1.2 \times M_c$.
3. For H = D cylinder, $D_R = 6 \times M_R$.
4. Calculate $V_R$ and verify using **efficiency check**:
   $$V_R = \dfrac{S \cdot V_c}{\eta - S}$$
5. **Choose the LARGER of the two** calculated diameters.

### **Common Pitfalls (Avoid These):**
- [ ] ❌ Forgetting safety factor on modulus.
- [ ] ❌ Confusing top riser efficiency (~12%) with side riser (~20%) or exothermic (~50%).
- [ ] ❌ Using diameter instead of radius in formulas.
- [ ] ❌ Forgetting to subtract shrinkage metal from feed requirement.
- [ ] ❌ Placing riser on a thin section instead of a hot spot.

### **Types of Risers (Recap Table):**

| Type | Efficiency | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Top Riser | 10–15% | Simple castings |
| Side Riser | 15–25% | Steel, heavy castings |
| Blind Riser | 20–30% | Automated foundries |
| Insulated Sleeve Riser | 30–50% | Premium castings |
| Exothermic Riser | 40–60% | Critical feed applications |

### **Key Terminology (For 2-Mark Definitions):**
- [ ] **Modulus (M):** Volume-to-surface-area ratio, indicator of solidification time.
- [ ] **Directional Solidification:** Controlled heat flow from casting → riser.
- [ ] **Hot Spot:** Thickest region / thermal centre — location where riser must be placed.
- [ ] **Neck (Riser Neck):** Connecting channel between riser and casting.
- [ ] **Shrinkage Cavity / Pipe / Void:** Internal defect due to inadequate feed metal.
- [ ] **Caine's Method:** Empirical section thickness ratio method for riser sizing.

### **One-Line Memory Aid:**
> *"Modulus of Riser > Modulus of Casting → Riser feeds the shrinkage → Sound casting."* 🎯

---

**END OF KTU 2024 SCHEME NOTES — RISERS (MODULE 1)**

topics.title: Chills
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBMRT404 - MANUFACTURING PROCESS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Chills # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In KTU 2024 scheme foundry engineering terminology, **Chills** are defined as **metallic or non-metallic inserts of high thermal conductivity** that are deliberately embedded inside the sand mold (internal chill) or attached to the mold cavity wall (external chill) at locations where the casting section is too thick or where rapid heat extraction is essential. They are manufactured from materials possessing **thermal conductivity far superior to green sand molds**, such as **cast iron, copper, graphite, brass, or aluminum**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > "Chills are auxiliary refractory/metal components placed in a sand mold to increase the local rate of heat extraction from the molten metal, thereby promoting directional solidification, eliminating shrinkage porosity, and improving the mechanical/metallurgical properties of the casting." ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine you are drinking hot tea from a thin porcelain cup versus a thick steel tumbler. The steel tumbler feels much hotter on the outside because **steel conducts heat much faster than porcelain**. Now think of a casting: the molten metal in a thick region cools slowly because sand is a poor conductor of heat (acts like porcelain). A **Chill is essentially the "steel tumbler" of the casting world** — we deliberately introduce a high-conductivity object next to the hot spot in the mold. This object rapidly sucks the heat out of the molten metal, just like a heat sink in electronics, ensuring that the metal solidifies quickly in that region before shrinkage cavities can form. > [!NOTE] > **Simple Memory Aid:** *Chill = Localized Heat Sink in Mold* — its sole purpose is to accelerate heat removal from a specific region of the casting. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Property | Typical Value (KTU Reference) | |----------|-------------------------------| | Thermal conductivity of **silica sand mold** | **0.0026 W/mm·K** (≈ 2.6 W/m·K) | | Thermal conductivity of **cast iron chill** | **46–55 W/m·K** | | Thermal conductivity of **copper chill** | **385 W/m·K** | | Standard chill coating | **Refractory wash (chalk + clay + water)** — thickness **0.5–1.5 mm** | | Standard chill placement temperature | **Pre-heated to 100–200 °C** to avoid thermal shock | | Chill working life | **50–500 molding cycles** for cast iron chills | The thermal conductivity ratio between a copper chill and sand is roughly **150:1**, which clearly demonstrates why chills drastically modify the local solidification pattern. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic of a Chill — Step-by-Step The action of a chill in a sand mold follows a precise thermodynamic sequence. Understanding this sequence is critical for KTU board questions. - **Step 1 — Heat Transfer Initiation:** When molten metal (typically 1300–1450 °C for cast iron) fills the mold cavity, it contacts the chill surface. Due to the massive conductivity differential, heat instantly begins to flow from the liquid metal into the chill. - **Step 2 — Local Directional Solidification:** The metal adjacent to the chill drops below the liquidus temperature first. Solidification front advances **from the chill face inward** toward the riser. This is the *opposite* of natural cooling (which would progress from the casting surface inward) and ensures liquid feed metal flows inward from the riser. - **Step 3 — Temperature Equalization:** After several minutes, the chill and the surrounding solidified metal reach thermal equilibrium. The chill has now served its purpose of extracting the "superheat + latent heat of fusion" from the local region. - **Step 4 — Metallurgical Integration (Internal Chills Only):** For internal chills (placed inside the mold cavity), the chill **partially fuses/melts into the casting**, becoming part of the final product. The interface forms a metallurgical bond. - **Step 5 — Coating Protection (External Chills):** External chills are coated with refractory wash (chalk/kaolin) to prevent them from welding with the casting surface, allowing them to be reused after shakeout. > [!NOTE] > **Why use chills instead of just making thinner sections?** > Chills allow engineers to use thick sections for **structural strength** while still achieving **rapid solidification** for metallurgical soundness — combining the best of both worlds. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet The following table consolidates **all examinable formulas** related to chill design for the KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments. Every symbol and unit is KTU-board-exact. | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Description & KTU Application | |--------------|------------------------|------------------------------| | **Chill Effectiveness (N.Ch.)** | $N_{Ch} = \dfrac{A_{Ch}}{V_{Ch}}$ | Ratio of heat-extracting surface to chill mass volume; higher value = better cooling efficiency. | | **Brande's Area Factor (BA)** | $BA = \dfrac{V_c}{V_{wr}}$ | Where $V_c$ = casting volume to be chilled, $V_{wr}$ = chill volume; governs proper sizing. | | **Combined Modulus (M_total)** | $M_{total} = \dfrac{V_c + \eta \cdot V_{chill}}{A_c + A_{chill}}$ | Effective modulus of casting + chill system; $\eta$ = chill efficiency factor (0.5 to 0.75). | | **Heat Balance Equation** | $V_c \cdot \rho_c \cdot [L + c_p \Delta T] = h \cdot A_{chill} \cdot t$ | Energy extracted by chill equals energy released by solidifying metal. | | **Sand-to-Chill Heat Ratio** | $R_{hs} = \dfrac{K_{chill}}{K_{sand}}$ | Typically **15–150**; shows relative cooling power. | | **Biot Number Check (Foam Moulding Only)** | $Bi = \dfrac{h \cdot L_c}{K_{chill}}$ | Verifies lumped heat capacity validity (must be less than **0.1**). | | **Shrinkage Porosity Volume** | $V_{porosity} = \dfrac{V_{c} \cdot \%_{shrinkage}}{100}$ | Used to estimate the minimum chill volume required. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to "design a chill", always calculate the **effective modulus** ($M_{total}$) and compare it to the riser modulus. The chill is correctly sized only if $M_{total} \le M_{riser}$. ## 2.3 Engineering Utility — Where Chills are Used in Industry - **Heavy Steel Castings** — Railroad wheels, rolling mill housings, ship propeller hubs. - **Iron Castings** — Engine cylinder blocks, machine tool beds, gear blanks. - **Non-Ferrous Castings** — Bronze valves, aluminum alloy pump bodies. - **Defect Remediation** — Eliminating shrinkage porosity at junctions (L, T, X, Y joints). - **Grain Refinement** — Producing fine-grained structures in specific zones for wear resistance (e.g., cam lobes). - **Substitute for Risers** — Reducing riser size, increasing casting yield. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Combined Modulus of Casting + Chill System The fundamental design principle is that when a chill is attached to a casting section, the **effective modulus** of that section is modified. Let us derive this. ### Step 1: Define individual moduli The modulus of the casting section alone is: $$M_c = \frac{V_c}{A_c}$$ where: - $V_c$ = volume of the casting region to be chilled (m³) - $A_c$ = surface area of the casting region through which heat is lost (m²) The modulus of the chill itself is: $$M_{ch} = \frac{V_{ch}}{A_{ch}}$$ where: - $V_{ch}$ = volume of the chill (m³) - $A_{ch}$ = total surface area of the chill (m²) ### Step 2: Combine the two bodies as a single heat-extracting system When the casting and chill are in perfect thermal contact, the combined volume that must release heat is the **casting volume plus a fraction of the chill volume** (because the chill only absorbs heat up to a fraction of its full thermal capacity during the solidification window). $$V_{combined} = V_c + \eta \cdot V_{ch}$$ where $\eta$ is the **chill efficiency factor**, typically taken as $0.5$ for first-time calculations (KTU-recommended value) and can go up to $0.75$ for short solidification times. ### Step 3: Combined heat-loss surface area The combined surface area through which heat is being lost includes both the casting surfaces and the exposed chill surfaces. The casting-chill interface acts as an internal conductor and is excluded from the heat-loss surface. $$A_{combined} = A_c + A_{ch}$$ ### Step 4: Final combined modulus $$M_{combined} = \frac{V_c + \eta \cdot V_{ch}}{A_c + A_{ch}}$$ This is the **fundamental design equation** that KTU examiners expect in derivation questions. > [!NOTE] > **Design Rule:** For the chill to function as a "heat sink", the combined modulus of the chilled region must be **equal to or less than** the modulus of the feeding riser: > $M_{combined} \le M_{riser}$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Chill Volume for Defect-Free Casting ### Step 1: Calculate the shrinkage volume to be compensated For a cylindrical casting section of diameter $D$ and length $L$, the volume is: $$V_c = \frac{\pi D^2 L}{4}$$ The volumetric shrinkage for gray cast iron is approximately **1.0% to 2.0%** (use 1.5% for KTU calculations unless specified). $$V_{shrink} = \frac{V_c \cdot S}{100}$$ where $S$ = volumetric shrinkage percentage. ### Step 2: Equate shrinkage volume to chill absorption capacity The chill must absorb heat equal to the latent heat released by the volume that would have caused shrinkage. $$V_{ch} = \frac{V_{shrink} \cdot \rho_m \cdot L_f}{C_{ch} \cdot \Delta T_{ch}}$$ where: - $\rho_m$ = density of molten metal (kg/m³) - $L_f$ = latent heat of fusion (J/kg) - $C_{ch}$ = specific heat capacity of chill material (J/kg·K) - $\Delta T_{ch}$ = allowable temperature rise of chill (K) ### Step 3: Solve for minimum chill mass $$m_{ch} = V_{ch} \cdot \rho_{ch}$$ This gives the minimum mass of chill required. In practice, KTU problems expect students to add a **safety factor of 1.2 to 1.5**. > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Valuation Mistake:** Students often forget to convert percentage shrinkage (1.5%) to decimal form (0.015) when substituting into the formula. Always write the percentage explicitly in the numerator to avoid this trap. ## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 2024 Style) **Problem:** A steel casting has a hot spot region of volume $V_c = 8.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$ and exposed surface area $A_c = 0.04 \text{ m}^2$. Calculate the combined modulus if a cast-iron chill of volume $V_{ch} = 4.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$ and area $A_{ch} = 0.025 \text{ m}^2$ is attached. Take chill efficiency $\eta = 0.6$. Is the chill adequate if the riser modulus is $M_r = 0.015 \text{ m}$? ### Step 1 — Compute combined volume $$V_{combined} = V_c + \eta \cdot V_{ch} = 8.0 \times 10^{-4} + 0.6 \times 4.0 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$V_{combined} = 8.0 \times 10^{-4} + 2.4 \times 10^{-4} = 10.4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$ ### Step 2 — Compute combined area $$A_{combined} = A_c + A_{ch} = 0.04 + 0.025 = 0.065 \text{ m}^2$$ ### Step 3 — Compute combined modulus $$M_{combined} = \frac{10.4 \times 10^{-4}}{0.065} = 0.016 \text{ m}$$ ### Step 4 — Comparison with riser Since $M_{combined} = 0.016 \text{ m}$ is **slightly greater** than $M_r = 0.015 \text{ m}$, the chill is **marginally inadequate**. Increase chill volume by approximately 8% or reduce $\eta$ assumption to 0.5. **Final Answer:** Chill is **not adequate**; redesign required. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Classification Flow Diagram of Chills ```mermaid flowchart TD A["CHILLS CLASSIFICATION"] --> B["BY POSITION"] A --> C["BY MATERIAL"] A --> D["BY SHAPE"] B --> B1["Internal Chill
Placed inside cavity
Fuses with casting"] B --> B2["External Chill
Placed on mold wall
Removable and reusable"] C --> C1["Cast Iron Chill
Most common
K = 46 to 55 W/mK"] C --> C2["Copper Chill
High conductivity
K = 385 W/mK"] C --> C3["Graphite Chill
Non metallic
K = 120 W/mK"] C --> C4["Aluminum Chill
Lightweight
K = 200 W/mK"] D --> D1["Plate Chill"] D --> D2["Rod or Pin Chill"] D --> D3["Block Chill"] D --> D4["Cylindrical Chill"] D --> D5["Tailor Made Chill
Custom geometry"] B1 --> E["Function: Promote
Directional Solidification"] B2 --> E C1 --> E D1 --> E ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Chill Action Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart LR MOLT["Molten Metal
1300 to 1450 C"] -->|Heat Transfer| CHILL["CHILL
High K Material"] CHILL -->|Heat Storage| SAND["Surrounding Sand Mold
