# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"Define bulk deformation processes and state why the volume of the workpiece is assumed to remain constant."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Bulk deformation processes are manufacturing processes in which a metal workpiece is plastically deformed under compressive, tensile, or combined forces to obtain a desired shape, with the bulk of the material undergoing significant plastic strain.

Volume is assumed constant because:
1. Metals are **incompressible in plastic deformation** — the change in density is negligible (typically less than 0.1%).
2. Plastic deformation does not create or destroy material; it only redistributes it.
3. This gives us the powerful relation $A_0 L_0 = A_f L_f$, used in almost every numerical problem.

(1 mark each point)

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Explain the difference between open-die and closed-die forging with one example each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Open-Die Forging | Closed-Die Forging |
|---|---|---|
| Die geometry | Flat or simple shape | Contains full impression of part |
| Material flow | Free, with flash | Constrained inside cavity |
| Accuracy | Low | High |
| Example | Large rotor shafts (1 mark) | Connecting rods, gear blanks (1 mark) |
| Cost | Low tooling cost | High tooling cost |

Closed die produces near-net shape parts, open die is used for simple geometry or large parts. (1 mark for comparison)

---

## PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question Choice A [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

**A cylindrical steel billet of diameter 50 mm and height 75 mm is upset in open-die forging at room temperature. The coefficient of friction at the die–workpiece interface is 0.20, and the average flow stress of the steel is 350 MPa. Find:**

**(a)** The forging force required when the billet height reduces to 30 mm, assuming volume is conserved.
**(b)** The forging work done (in kJ) during this reduction, neglecting redundant work.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Initial volume of billet.
$V_0 = A_0 \cdot h_0 = \pi \cdot (25)^2 \cdot 75 = \pi \cdot 625 \cdot 75 = 147,262 \text{ mm}^3$

**Step 2:** Final area using volume conservation.
$V_0 = A_f \cdot h_f$
$A_f = V_0 / h_f = 147,262 / 30 = 4908.7 \text{ mm}^2$

**Step 3:** Final radius of billet.
$A_f = \pi r_f^2 \Rightarrow r_f = \sqrt{A_f / \pi} = \sqrt{4908.7 / \pi} = 39.53 \text{ mm}$

**Step 4:** Friction hill factor at $h_f = 30$ mm.
$K_f = 1 + \frac{2 \mu h_f}{3 r_f} = 1 + \frac{2 \cdot 0.20 \cdot 30}{3 \cdot 39.53} = 1 + \frac{12}{118.59} = 1 + 0.1012 = 1.1012$

**Step 5:** Forging force.
$F = Y_f \cdot A_f \cdot K_f = 350 \cdot 4908.7 \cdot 1.1012$
$F = 350 \cdot 5405.7 = 1,892,000 \text{ N} \approx 1892 \text{ kN}$

**Answer: F ≈ 1892 kN** (7 marks)

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** True strain in forging.
$\varepsilon = \ln(h_0 / h_f) = \ln(75 / 30) = \ln(2.5) = 0.9163$

**Step 2:** Forging work (frictionless portion).
$W_{ideal} = Y_f \cdot V \cdot \varepsilon = 350 \cdot 147,262 \cdot 0.9163$
$W_{ideal} = 350 \cdot 134,944 = 47,230,400 \text{ N·mm} = 47,230 \text{ J}$

**Step 3:** Friction work (approximate using average geometry).
Using average radius $\bar{r} = (25 + 39.53)/2 = 32.27$ mm, average height $\bar{h} = (75 + 30)/2 = 52.5$ mm:
$W_{friction} = \frac{2}{3} \mu Y_f V = \frac{2}{3} \cdot 0.20 \cdot 350 \cdot 147,262$
$W_{friction} = 0.1333 \cdot 350 \cdot 147,262 = 6,872,000 \text{ N·mm} = 6872 \text{ J}$

**Step 4:** Total work.
$W_{total} = 47,230 + 6872 = 54,102 \text{ J} \approx 54.1 \text{ kJ}$

**Answer: W ≈ 54.1 kJ** (7 marks)

---

### Question Choice B [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**A copper billet of diameter 100 mm and length 200 mm is extruded at 350°C (hot working) to produce a rod of diameter 25 mm. The average flow stress at this temperature is 70 MPa, and the coefficient of friction between the billet and container wall is 0.10.**

**(a)** Calculate the ideal extrusion pressure (frictionless) and the actual extrusion pressure when the ram has moved 100 mm into the billet.
**(b)** Determine the maximum extrusion ratio that can be achieved with the available press capacity of 200 MPa ram pressure (with friction as given).

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Initial and final areas.
$A_0 = \pi (50)^2 = 7854 \text{ mm}^2$
$A_f = \pi (12.5)^2 = 490.9 \text{ mm}^2$

**Step 2:** Extrusion ratio.
$r = A_0 / A_f = 7854 / 490.9 = 16$

**Step 3:** Ideal extrusion pressure.
$P_{ideal} = Y_f \cdot \ln(r) = 70 \cdot \ln(16) = 70 \cdot 2.7726 = 194.1 \text{ MPa}$

**Step 4:** Remaining billet length when ram has moved 100 mm.
$L = L_0 - x = 200 - 100 = 100 \text{ mm}$

**Step 5:** Friction pressure term.
$\Delta P = \frac{2 Y_f \mu L}{D} = \frac{2 \cdot 70 \cdot 0.10 \cdot 100}{100} = \frac{1400}{100} = 14 \text{ MPa}$

**Step 6:** Actual extrusion pressure.
$P_{actual} = P_{ideal} + \Delta P = 194.1 + 14 = 208.1 \text{ MPa}$

**Answer: P_ideal ≈ 194.1 MPa; P_actual ≈ 208.1 MPa** (7 marks)

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Available pressure beyond the deformation work.
$P_{available} = 200 \text{ MPa}$ (total), of which $14 \text{ MPa}$ is consumed by friction (assume average).
$P_{deformation} = 200 - 14 = 186 \text{ MPa}$

**Step 2:** Maximum extrusion ratio from ideal equation.
$P_{deformation} = Y_f \ln(r_{max})$
$186 = 70 \ln(r_{max})$
$\ln(r_{max}) = 186 / 70 = 2.657$
$r_{max} = e^{2.657} = 14.27$

**Step 3:** Maximum final diameter.
$d_{f,max} = d_0 / \sqrt{r_{max}} = 100 / \sqrt{14.27} = 100 / 3.778 = 26.47 \text{ mm}$

**Answer: r_max ≈ 14.3 (minimum final diameter ≈ 26.5 mm)** (7 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - In forging problems, students often use **initial** area instead of **instantaneous** area. Use $A$ at the *current* height, not the start.
> - In extrusion, students forget that $L$ is the **remaining** billet length, not the initial length. The friction term *decreases* as the ram advances.
> - In drawing, students use $\ln(A_0/A_f)$ correctly but forget to compare $\sigma_d$ to $Y_f$ for the maximum reduction criterion. The condition $\sigma_d \le Y_f$ is mandatory.
> - In rolling, students often use $R$ in mm inconsistently with $h$ in mm. Always keep units consistent: $L$ comes out in mm if $R$ and $h$ are both in mm.
> - Marks are awarded for **showing the substitution step**. Even if the final number is wrong, full process marks are given if each substitution is visible.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Forming processes are plastic deformation processes** where volume remains constant.
- **Forging force** = $Y_f A_0 (1 + 2\mu h / 3R)$ — friction increases force as height decreases.
- **Extrusion pressure** (ideal) = $Y_f \ln(A_0/A_f)$ — friction adds a linear-in-L term.
- **Drawing stress** (ideal) = $Y_f \ln(A_0/A_f)$ — must be less than $Y_f$ for feasibility.
- **Rolling contact length** = $\sqrt{R \Delta h}$ — derived from geometry of a circular arc.
- **Rolling force** = $Y_{avg} L w$ — projected contact area times average flow stress.
- **Volume conservation** $A_0 L_0 = A_f L_f$ is used in every problem — never forget it.
- **Hot working** lowers $Y_f$, increases ductility, removes strain hardening.
- **Cold working** raises $Y_f$, gives accuracy and good surface finish.
- **Direct extrusion** has container friction; **indirect extrusion** does not.
- **Maximum reduction in drawing** is governed by $\sigma_d \le Y_f$.
- **Forward slip in rolling** = $(v_{out} - v_{roll})/v_{roll}$ — small positive value.
- **Angle of contact** = $\arccos(1 - d/R)$.
- **Torque per roll** = $0.5 F L$; **Power** = $2 \pi N T$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of Primary Forming Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT404 - MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Selection of Primary Forming Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary Forming Process**: A primary forming process (also called a primary shaping process) is a manufacturing operation that converts raw material in its initial bulk or liquid state into a basic geometric form, providing the fundamental shape and structural integrity upon which secondary processes (machining, joining, finishing) can later be performed. These processes establish the **initial geometry**, **bulk mechanical properties**, and **internal microstructure** of the workpiece.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for PBMRT404 – Manufacturing Process, the *primary forming processes* studied under Module 1 include:

1. **Casting Processes** (Sand, Die, Investment, Centrifugal, Continuous)
2. **Bulk Deformation Processes** (Forging, Rolling, Extrusion, Drawing)
3. **Sheet Metal Forming** (Bending, Deep Drawing, Stretch Forming)
4. **Powder Metallurgy** (Compaction + Sintering)
5. **Polymer/Composite Molding** (Injection, Blow, Compression Molding)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Clay & Play-Doh Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy**: Imagine you have a lump of raw clay. To make a useful cup, you have two choices:
> - (a) Melt the clay into liquid slurry, pour it into a cup-shaped mold, let it solidify → this mimics **casting**.
> - (b) Press the soft clay through a shaped opening to get a long tube → this mimics **extrusion**.
> - (c) Hammer the clay into a flat plate → this mimics **forging/rolling**.
>
> Your choice of "process" depends on whether the clay is liquid, plastic, or solid, what shape you want, how many cups you need, and how much money you have. The **"Selection of Primary Forming Process"** is exactly this engineering decision — but made with metals, polymers, and ceramics under strict quality and cost constraints.

## 1.3 Why Selection Matters — The 7 Pillars of Decision

A wrong process selection can lead to:

- **Cracks and porosity** in the final product
- **Excessive scrap rates** (>30% rejection)
- **Tooling cost overrun** (one die can cost ₹5–50 lakhs)
- **Failure to meet mechanical properties** (tensile strength, hardness)
- **Unsustainable production economics**

The key metrics engineers use in selection are summarized below in **bold**:

- **Dimensional Tolerance** (typically ±0.1 mm to ±5 mm)
- **Surface Finish** (Ra 0.8 µm to Ra 25 µm)
- **Production Volume** (1-off prototype to >100,000/year)
- **Material Utilization** (often 50–95%, near-net-shape target)
- **Capital Investment** (₹10,000 for sand casting to ₹50 crore for die casting)
- **Lead Time** (1 day for 3D print to 6 months for forging die)
- **Mechanical Property Requirements** (strength, ductility, fatigue life)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Selection Methodology — A 6-Step Engineering Decision Tree

The **Selection of Primary Forming Process** is not a guess. It follows a **systematic 6-step methodology**:

### Step 1: Define the Part Requirements
- Geometry (simple prismatic, axisymmetric, complex 3D)
- Size (mm to meters)
- Tolerance (IT-grade)
- Service conditions (load, temperature, corrosion)

### Step 2: Identify the Material
- **Ferrous** (cast iron, steel) vs **Non-ferrous** (Al, Cu, Ti, Mg)
- **Polymers** (thermoplastics, thermosets)
- **Ceramics & composites**

### Step 3: Quantify the Production Quantity
| Quantity Range | Process Family Recommended |
| :--- | :--- |
| 1 – 100 units | Sand casting, 3D printing, machining from billet |
| 100 – 10,000 units | Die casting, investment casting, forging |
| > 10,000 units | Die casting, powder metallurgy, stamping |

### Step 4: Evaluate Process Capabilities
Each process has inherent limits on:
- Minimum section thickness
- Maximum size
- Achievable tolerance
- Compatible materials

### Step 5: Conduct Cost Analysis
Use the **Total Cost Equation**:

$$
C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{material}} + C_{\text{labor}} + C_{\text{tooling}} + C_{\text{overhead}} + C_{\text{scrap}}
$$

### Step 6: Final Selection with Weighted Decision Matrix
Assign weights to each criterion and rank processes.

## 2.2 The 10 Critical Selection Parameters (Engineering Analysis)

| # | Parameter | What it Decides | Typical Values / Metrics |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Material Form** | Liquid / Powder / Solid bar | Molten, granular, wrought |
| 2 | **Melting Point** | Casting vs. forging viability | 200 °C (polymer) – 3500 °C (W) |
| 3 | **Ductility (%)** | Cold working feasibility | >30% → cold form; <10% → hot work |
| 4 | **Complexity** | Casting vs. machining | Cpk index 1.0 – 1.67 |
| 5 | **Size** | Sand cast vs. die cast | <1 kg → die cast; >100 kg → sand |
| 6 | **Tolerance** | Finish operations needed | ±0.05 mm to ±2.0 mm |
| 7 | **Production Rate** | Automation suitability | 1 / hr to 10,000 / hr |
| 8 | **Surface Finish (Ra)** | Secondary finishing | 0.4 µm (ground) – 25 µm (as-cast) |
| 9 | **Mechanical Properties** | Directional vs. isotropic | Wrought: anisotropic; cast: isotropic |
| 10 | **Cost per Part** | Process economics | ₹5 (PM) – ₹5000 (machined) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **mandatory** for KTU Numerical/Analytical questions on process selection.

| Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $$C_{p} = C_{m} + C_{t}/N + C_{l} \cdot t$$ | Cost per part (material, tooling amortized, labor) | ₹/part |
| $$BEP = \dfrac{C_{\text{tooling}}}{C_{\text{per piece savings}}}$$ | Break-even production volume for tooling | units |
| $$\eta_{\text{material}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{final}}}{m_{\text{initial}}} \times 100$$ | Material utilization efficiency | % |
| $$R_{a} \approx 0.8 \cdot \dfrac{d}{100} \quad \text{or} \quad R_{a} = k \cdot S^{a}$$ | Surface roughness correlation (S = cutting param) | µm |
| $$F = \sigma_{f} \cdot A \cdot K_{f}$$ | Forging force estimation | N |
| $$P = K \cdot \dot{\varepsilon}^{m}$$ | Flow stress in hot working (K, m = constants) | Pa |
| $$Y = \dfrac{\text{Output rate}}{\text{Labor hours}}$$ | Productivity index | parts/hr |
| $$\sigma_{f} = \sigma_{0} \cdot \varepsilon^{n}$$ | True stress–strain in cold forming | MPa |

> **Note on Markdown Pipes**: All absolute value / modulus notations in formulas use \vert or \mid to preserve table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive industry**: 80% of engine blocks use **die casting** (Al alloys) for high-volume, complex parts.
- **Aerospace**: **Investment casting** dominates turbine blades (superalloys with directional solidification).
- **Defense**: **Forging** is chosen for gun barrels, missile casings (high strength, fatigue resistance).
- **Consumer electronics**: **Powder metallurgy** for gears, **injection molding** for polymer housings.
- **Construction**: **Sand casting** for manhole covers, **extrusion** for window frames (Al section).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Break-Even Point (BEP) Between Two Competing Processes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **classic KTU numerical**. You will be given a Part B question (14 marks) where you must derive the break-even production volume for two competing primary forming processes (e.g., sand casting vs. die casting).

### Given Data (Typical KTU Problem)
- **Process A** (Sand Casting):
  - Tooling cost = ₹50,000 (one-time)
  - Per-part cost = ₹400
- **Process B** (Die Casting):
  - Tooling cost = ₹5,00,000 (one-time)
  - Per-part cost = ₹100

### Objective
Find the production quantity $N$ at which both processes give the **same total cost** — i.e., the **break-even point**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Write the total cost equation for each process**

For Process A (Sand Casting), the total cost $C_{A}$ is:

$$
C_{A} = C_{\text{tooling},A} + C_{\text{per part},A} \cdot N
$$

For Process B (Die Casting), the total cost $C_{B}$ is:

$$
C_{B} = C_{\text{tooling},B} + C_{\text{per part},B} \cdot N
$$

**Step 2: At the break-even point, $C_{A} = C_{B}$**

$$
C_{\text{tooling},A} + C_{\text{per part},A} \cdot N = C_{\text{tooling},B} + C_{\text{per part},B} \cdot N
$$

**Step 3: Rearrange the equation to isolate $N$**

Move all terms containing $N$ to one side and constants to the other:

$$
C_{\text{per part},A} \cdot N - C_{\text{per part},B} \cdot N = C_{\text{tooling},B} - C_{\text{tooling},A}
$$

Factor out $N$ on the left side:

$$
N \cdot \left( C_{\text{per part},A} - C_{\text{per part},B} \right) = C_{\text{tooling},B} - C_{\text{tooling},A}
$$

**Step 4: Solve for $N$**

$$
N = \dfrac{C_{\text{tooling},B} - C_{\text{tooling},A}}{C_{\text{per part},A} - C_{\text{per part},B}}
$$

**Step 5: Substitute numerical values**

$$
N = \dfrac{5{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}000}{400 - 100} = \dfrac{4{,}50{,}000}{300}
$$

Therefore:

$$
N = 1500 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 6: Interpret the result**

- For $N < 1500$ units → **Choose Process A (Sand Casting)**
- For $N > 1500$ units → **Choose Process B (Die Casting)**
- This is because higher tooling cost in B is offset by lower per-part cost.

## 3.2 Derivation: Material Utilization Efficiency (Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> A second high-yield KTU derivation relates to the **material utilization** when comparing forging vs. machining from billet.

**Step 1: Forged Part**

Starting billet mass: $m_{\text{billet}} = 5.2 \text{ kg}$
Forged part mass: $m_{\text{forged}} = 4.8 \text{ kg}$

$$
\eta_{\text{forge}} = \dfrac{4.8}{5.2} \times 100 = 92.3\%
$$

**Step 2: Machined Part (from same forged blank)**

Finished part mass: $m_{\text{final}} = 2.5 \text{ kg}$

$$
\eta_{\text{machine}} = \dfrac{2.5}{5.2} \times 100 = 48.1\%
$$

**Step 3: Forging saves material**

$$
\Delta \eta = 92.3 - 48.1 = 44.2\%
$$

This shows why **near-net-shape** processes (forging, casting, PM) are preferred for **expensive materials** like Ti, Inconel, etc.

## 3.3 KTU Selection Decision Matrix (Analytical Framework)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B (14-mark) questions often test the **weighted decision matrix method**. Here is the rigorous method:

**Step 1**: List the candidate processes (e.g., A, B, C, D).

**Step 2**: List the criteria and assign weights (total = 1.0 or 100%).

| Criterion | Weight $w_i$ |
| :--- | :--- |
| Cost | 0.25 |
| Quality | 0.20 |
| Production rate | 0.20 |
| Material compatibility | 0.15 |
| Tolerance capability | 0.10 |
| Tooling availability | 0.10 |
| **Total** | **1.00** |

**Step 3**: Rate each process on a 1–10 scale for each criterion.

**Step 4**: Compute the **Weighted Score**:

$$
S_{j} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{i} \cdot r_{ij}
$$

where $r_{ij}$ is the rating of process $j$ on criterion $i$.

**Step 5**: Select the process with the **highest $S_j$**.

> This is a **rigorous, defensible engineering decision** — not a gut feeling.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Primary Forming Processes

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **complete classification tree** of primary forming processes as per the KTU 2024 syllabus. This is a high-yield visual that examiners often expect in 7-mark sub-questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PRIMARY FORMING PROCESSES] --> B[CASTING PROCESSES]
    A --> C[BULK DEFORMATION]
    A --> D[SHEET METAL FORMING]
    A --> E[POWDER METALLURGY]
    A --> F[POLYMER MOLDING]
    A --> G[CERAMIC FORMING]

    B --> B1[Sand Casting]
    B --> B2[Die Casting]
    B --> B3[Investment Casting]
    B --> B4[Centrifugal Casting]
    B --> B5[Continuous Casting]

    C --> C1[Forging]
    C --> C2[Rolling]
    C --> C3[Extrusion]
    C --> C4[Drawing]
    C --> C5[Swaging]

    D --> D1[Bending]
    D --> D2[Deep Drawing]
    D --> D3[Stretch Forming]
    D --> D4[Spinning]

    E --> E1[Press and Sinter]
    E --> E2[Hot Isostatic Pressing]
    E --> E3[Metal Injection Molding]

    F --> F1[Injection Molding]
    F --> F2[Blow Molding]
    F --> F3[Compression Molding]
    F --> F4[Rotational Molding]

    G --> G1[Slip Casting]
    G --> G2[Isostatic Pressing]
    G --> G3[Injection Molding of Ceramics]
```

## 4.2 Process Selection Decision Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

> [!NOTE]
> Since process selection is a logical flow rather than a physical drawing, the following **Mermaid block diagram** maps the *functional architecture* of how an engineer systematically selects a primary forming process. This is the **conceptual workflow** that KTU 14-mark Part B questions expect.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[START: Part Requirement] --> define[DEFINE Geometry, Material, Quantity, Tolerance]
    define --> classify{CLASSIFY Material}
    classify -- Ferrous --> case1[CAST IRON and STEEL]
    classify -- Non Ferrous --> case2[Al, Cu, Mg, Ti]
    classify -- Polymer --> case3[THERMOPLASTIC or THERMOSET]
    classify -- Ceramic --> case4[OXIDE or NITRIDE CERAMIC]

    case1 --> q1{Production Quantity?}
    q1 -- Low --> sand[SAND CASTING or SHELL MOLD]
    q1 -- High --> invest[INVESTMENT or DIE CASTING]

    case2 --> q2{Production Quantity?}
    q2 -- Low --> sand2[SAND CASTING]
    q2 -- High --> diec[DIE CASTING or PM]

    case3 --> q4{Volume?}
    q4 -- Low --> comp[COMPRESSION MOLD]
    q4 -- High --> inj[INJECTION MOLDING]

    case4 --> q5{Volume?}
    q5 -- Low --> slip[SLIP CASTING]
    q5 -- High --> iso[ISOSTATIC PRESSING]

    sand --> cost[COST ANALYSIS]
    invest --> cost
    sand2 --> cost
    diec --> cost
    comp --> cost
    inj --> cost
    slip --> cost
    iso --> cost

    cost --> matrix[WEIGHTED DECISION MATRIX]
    matrix --> final[FINAL PROCESS SELECTED]
    final --> end[END]
```

## 4.3 Process Selection Comparison Matrix (Engineering Table)

The following table summarizes the most common KTU-asked process comparison:

| Selection Criterion | Sand Casting | Die Casting | Investment Casting | Forging | Powder Metallurgy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Tolerance (mm)** | ±1.5 | ±0.1 | ±0.1 | ±0.3 | ±0.05 |
| **Surface Finish (Ra µm)** | 12 – 25 | 1 – 3 | 1.5 – 3 | 3 – 6 | 0.8 – 2 |
| **Min Section (mm)** | 3 | 1 | 0.5 | 2 | 0.5 |
| **Max Size (kg)** | >5000 | <50 | <100 | >1000 | <25 |
| **Production Rate (pcs/hr)** | 5 – 20 | 100 – 1000 | 10 – 50 | 30 – 200 | 200 – 2000 |
| **Tooling Cost (₹)** | 10k – 50k | 5L – 50L | 1L – 10L | 5L – 1Cr | 10L – 1Cr |
| **Material Utilization (%)** | 70 – 90 | 85 – 95 | 80 – 90 | 80 – 95 | 95 – 99 |
| **Mechanical Strength** | Medium | Medium | High | Very High | Medium |
| **Best for** | Large parts, low volume | High volume, complex | High precision, alloy | High strength, safety | Net shape, mass production |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> Each question tests direct recall of definitions, classifications, or single-formula application. KTU expects crisp, diagram-supported answers in **2–3 paragraphs** totaling about **80–120 words**.

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define a "primary forming process" and give two examples of bulk deformation processes.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A primary forming process is a manufacturing operation that converts raw material in its initial bulk (solid, liquid, or powder) form into a useful geometric shape, establishing the fundamental geometry, internal microstructure, and bulk mechanical properties of the component. These processes do not remove material; they reshape it. The two examples of bulk deformation processes are:

- (i) **Forging** — compressive deformation using hammers or presses
- (ii) **Extrusion** — forcing material through a die to obtain a constant cross-section

**[1 mark: definition; 1 mark: explanation; 1 mark: two examples with brief expansion]**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*List any four factors that govern the selection of a primary forming process.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The four major factors are:

- (i) **Material properties** (ductility, melting point, reactivity)
- (ii) **Production quantity and rate** (one-off, batch, mass)
- (iii) **Dimensional tolerance and surface finish** required
- (iv) **Economic considerations** (tooling cost, per-part cost, break-even)

A fifth factor often added is **part geometry and complexity** (Cpk index). Selection is performed using a **weighted decision matrix** that scores each process on these criteria.

**[0.75 mark × 4 = 3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — With Internal Choice)

> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry **14 marks** and include an **internal choice** (either Q. A or Q. B). Sub-parts are typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various factors that influence the selection of a primary forming process for a given engineering component. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A manufacturer has two options for producing 10,000 aluminum housing components:
- **Process X (Sand Casting):** Tooling cost ₹30,000; per-part cost ₹250
- **Process Y (Die Casting):** Tooling cost ₹4,00,000; per-part cost ₹80

Determine the break-even point and recommend the most economical process for the production volume. *(7 marks)*

---

### **MODEL ANSWER (Question A)**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Factors Influencing Process Selection**

The selection of a primary forming process is governed by **ten interconnected factors**, classified into four categories:

**1. Material-Based Factors (3 marks)**
- **Type of material**: ferrous vs. non-ferrous, polymer, ceramic
- **Ductility**: >30% elongation → cold forming feasible; <10% → hot working or casting
- **Melting point**: low-melt (Zn, Al) → die casting; high-melt (W, Mo) → PM or special casting
- **Reactivity**: Ti, Mg need vacuum/inert atmosphere

**2. Geometric Factors (1.5 marks)**
- **Size and weight**: >100 kg → sand cast; <1 kg → die cast or PM
- **Complexity**: simple prismatic → rolling/forging; complex 3D → casting
- **Minimum section thickness**: thin walls (<1 mm) → investment casting, MIM

**3. Production-Based Factors (1.5 marks)**
- **Production quantity**: 1–100 → sand cast; >10,000 → die cast, stamping
- **Production rate**: continuous strip → rolling; discrete → casting
- **Lead time**: short → no-tool processes; long → high-tooling processes

**4. Economic and Quality Factors (1 mark)**
- **Tooling cost amortization** over production volume
- **Material utilization** (yield %)
- **Dimensional tolerance and surface finish** required
- **Desired mechanical properties** (strength, fatigue, ductility)

> **Conclusion**: No single factor dominates. Engineers use a **weighted decision matrix** combining all factors.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Break-Even Analysis**

**Step 1**: Write the total cost equations

$$
C_{X} = 30{,}000 + 250 \cdot N
$$

$$
C_{Y} = 4{,}00{,}000 + 80 \cdot N
$$

**Step 2**: Equate $C_X = C_Y$ at break-even

$$
30{,}000 + 250 N = 4{,}00{,}000 + 80 N
$$

**Step 3**: Solve for $N$

$$
250 N - 80 N = 4{,}00{,}000 - 30{,}000
$$

$$
170 N = 3{,}70{,}000
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{3{,}70{,}000}{170} = 2176.47 \approx 2177 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 4**: Decision logic

$$
\text{If } N < 2177 \rightarrow \text{Process X (Sand Casting)} \text{ is cheaper}
$$

$$
\text{If } N > 2177 \rightarrow \text{Process Y (Die Casting)} \text{ is cheaper}
$$

**Step 5**: For the given production volume of **10,000 units**:

Since $10{,}000 \gg 2177$, the manufacturer should choose **Process Y (Die Casting)**.

**Step 6: Verification**

- Total cost using X: $30{,}000 + 250 \times 10{,}000 = \text{₹}25{,}03{,}000$
- Total cost using Y: $4{,}00{,}000 + 80 \times 10{,}000 = \text{₹}12{,}00{,}000$
- **Savings by choosing Y = ₹25,03,000 − ₹12,00,000 = ₹13,03,000** ✓

**Conclusion**: Die casting is **13.03 lakh rupees cheaper** for this production volume.

**[2 marks: setup; 2 marks: algebra; 1 mark: BEP value; 1 mark: recommendation; 1 mark: verification]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat classification, describe the major primary forming processes used in manufacturing industries. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare the processes of sand casting, die casting, and powder metallurgy based on: tolerance, surface finish, production rate, material utilization, and cost. State one industrial application for each. *(7 marks)*

---

### **MODEL ANSWER (Question B)**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Classification of Primary Forming Processes**

Primary forming processes are classified based on the **initial state of the material** and the **mechanism of shape change**.

**1. Casting Processes (Molten → Solid)** *(2 marks)*
- Sand casting, shell molding, die casting, investment casting, centrifugal casting, continuous casting, plaster mold casting.

**2. Bulk Deformation (Solid → Reshaped by Force)** *(2 marks)*
- Forging (open-die, closed-die, upset, swaging)
- Rolling (flat, shape, ring)
- Extrusion (direct, indirect, hot, cold, impact)
- Drawing (bar, wire, tube)

**3. Sheet Metal Forming (Sheet → 3D)** *(1 mark)*
- Bending, deep drawing, stretch forming, spinning, embossing, shearing.

**4. Powder Metallurgy (Powder → Solid)** *(1 mark)*
- Press-and-sinter, hot isostatic pressing (HIP), metal injection molding (MIM).

**5. Polymer Molding and Ceramic Forming** *(1 mark)*
- Injection molding, blow molding, rotational molding, compression molding, slip casting, isostatic pressing.

> **Note**: Each process family has variants tailored to specific tolerances, sizes, and economics.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Comparison Table**

| Criterion | Sand Casting | Die Casting | Powder Metallurgy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Tolerance (mm)** | ±1.5 to ±3.0 | ±0.1 to ±0.3 | ±0.05 to ±0.15 |
| **Surface Finish (Ra µm)** | 12 to 25 | 1 to 3 | 0.8 to 2 |
| **Production Rate (pcs/hr)** | 5 to 20 | 100 to 1000 | 200 to 2000 |
| **Material Utilization (%)** | 70 to 90 | 85 to 95 | 95 to 99 |
| **Cost per part (₹)** | Low to Medium (₹40 to 400) | Medium (₹20 to 200) | Low (₹5 to 50) |
| **Industrial Application** | Engine blocks, manhole covers, machine bases | Car cylinder heads, gearbox housings | Gears, bushings, magnets, filters |

> **[3 marks for table; 1 mark for tolerance/finish; 1 mark for rate; 1 mark for utilization; 1 mark for cost & application]**

**Sand Casting Application**: *Cylinder blocks of large IC engines* — large size, low volume, ferrous material.

**Die Casting Application**: *Aluminum alloy gearbox housings* in automobiles — high volume, complex shape, tight tolerance.

**Powder Metallurgy Application**: *Sintered bronze self-lubricating bushings* — net shape, no machining, high volume.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> - **Mistake 1**: Confusing **primary** with **secondary** processes. (Machining, grinding, honing = secondary)
> - **Mistake 2**: Writing only **definitions** without **classification diagrams** in 7-mark questions. KTU examiners expect a **labeled diagram** for full 7 marks.
> - **Mistake 3**: In numerical BEP problems, students often forget to **substitute units and verify the answer** (₹/part should yield units, N should be dimensionless integer).
> - **Mistake 4**: Confusing **hot working** and **cold working** criteria — for cold working, the material's recrystallization temperature relative to room temperature must be stated.
> - **Mistake 5**: Forgetting to convert all **currency to the same unit** (e.g., mixing ₹30,000 with ₹4,00,000 without alignment).
> - **Mistake 6**: Writing the comparison table without giving **industrial applications** — at least 1 application is mandatory for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute KTU Revision**

- ✅ **Definition**: Primary forming process = convert raw bulk material into a basic geometric shape (no material removal).
- ✅ **Five families**: Casting, Bulk Deformation, Sheet Forming, Powder Metallurgy, Polymer/Ceramic Molding.
- ✅ **Three pillars of selection**: Material + Quantity + Geometry (these decide 80% of the choice).
- ✅ **BEP formula** (memorize verbatim):

$$
N_{BE} = \dfrac{C_{T,Y} - C_{T,X}}{C_{p,X} - C_{p,Y}}
$$

- ✅ **Sand casting** = low volume, large parts, rough finish (Ra 12–25 µm).
- ✅ **Die casting** = high volume, small-medium parts, good finish (Ra 1–3 µm).
- ✅ **Investment casting** = complex, precision, expensive alloys (aerospace turbine blades).
- ✅ **Forging** = highest strength and toughness (crankshafts, connecting rods, gears).
- ✅ **Powder metallurgy** = net-shape, minimal waste (gears, filters, magnets).
- ✅ **Extrusion** = constant cross-section (window frames, tubes, rods).
- ✅ **Material utilization (yield %)** is highest for PM (~99%) and lowest for machining from billet (~50%).
- ✅ **Decision tool** = Weighted Decision Matrix, not intuition.
- ✅ **Cold working** requires **ductility > 30%**; otherwise hot working or casting.
- ✅ **Cast iron is brittle → not forgeable; must use casting or machining.**
- ✅ **Al alloys → die casting (high volume) or sand casting (low volume).**
- ✅ **Ti, Mg, superalloys → investment casting or PM (inert atmosphere mandatory).**
- ✅ **Polymer → injection molding (high vol) or compression molding (low vol).**
- ✅ **Always use units consistently** in BEP numericals (₹ per part, dimensionless N).
- ✅ **KTU Part B (14 marks) = (a) 7 marks theory + (b) 7 marks numerical/derivation OR 2× 7-mark theory.**
- ✅ **Diagrams earn 1–2 bonus marks** in 7-mark sub-questions.

---

> **END OF NOTES — SELECTION OF PRIMARY FORMING PROCESS (MODULE 1, PBMRT404)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT404 - MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Defects in Forging and Rolling

---

# 🏭 DEFECTS IN FORGING AND ROLLING — KTU 2024 Study Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a "Defect" in Bulk Forming?

In KTU 2024 Scheme Manufacturing Process terminology, a **manufacturing defect** is defined as any *discontinuity, discontinuity-driven void, surface irregularity, or internal discontinuity* that deviates the finished product from its specified metallurgical, dimensional, or surface-integrity standards. Bulk deformation processes such as **Forging** and **Rolling** are particularly vulnerable to defects because they operate on the workpiece at high strain rates, elevated temperatures, and under complex tri-axial stress states.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Block:**
> **Defect in Forging:** An imperfection (crack, fold, void, surface scale) introduced into a metallic workpiece as a result of incorrect die design, improper temperature control, inadequate lubrication, or improper material flow during the compressive plastic deformation of forging operations (open-die or closed-die).
>
> **Defect in Rolling:** An imperfection (crack, waviness, edge defect, surface scale, lamination) introduced into a metal sheet, plate, or bar as it is plastically deformed between two counter-rotating cylindrical rolls operating under compressive load.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

Imagine you are flattening a soft dough ball using a rolling pin. If you press too hard at the edges, the dough will crack. If you press too lightly, the centre will remain thick. If flour (oxide scale) sits on the surface, it will get pushed into the dough, leaving dark spots.

**Forging defects** are the metalworking equivalent of these phenomena:
- When a smith hammers steel at the wrong temperature → **cracks** form.
- When two streams of metal fail to fuse properly inside a die cavity → **cold shut** appears.
- When the die is too cold relative to the billet → metal doesn't fill the cavity → **unfilled section**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Chocolate Bar Pour":**
> Think of forging as pouring molten chocolate into a mould. If the chocolate cools too fast at the walls (chilled die), the centre shrinks and pulls away → **shrinkage cavities**. If two chocolate streams meet but don't bond (oxide skin present) → **cold shut**. Rolling, on the other hand, is like rolling cookie dough thinner and thinner; if the dough has dry edges, the edges **crack**; if you don't flip it, it becomes **unevenly thin** (crown defect).

---

### 1.3 The KTU Engineering Importance

In KTU syllabus context, defects are studied because they directly impact:
- **Mechanical Properties (MP)**: Tensile strength, fatigue life, impact toughness.
- **NDT Acceptance**: Rejection rate in ultrasonic, dye-penetrant, and magnetic particle inspection.
- **Cost & Wastage**: A single rolled plate with internal cracks can scrap an entire batch.
- **Safety**: Forged crankshafts or rolled pressure vessel plates must be defect-free for critical applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard Reference:** Acceptable defect limits in forged and rolled products are governed by codes such as **ASTM A788** (forged steel) and **ASTM E290** (bend testing) and visual inspection standards like **ASME Sec. VIII**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Systematic Taxonomy of Forging Defects

Forging defects can be classified into **three families** based on their origin:

| Family | Origin | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Material Flow Defects** | Improper metal flow inside die | Cold shut, unfilled section, laps, folds |
| **Thermal Defects** | Wrong temperature, uneven heating | Cracks, bursts, scale pits, flakes |
| **Metallurgical Defects** | Inherent in the raw material | Inclusions, porosity, seams |

---

### 2.2 Detailed Analysis — Major Forging Defects

#### 2.2.1 Cold Shut (also called *Cold Lap* or *Cold Shutdown*)

- **Definition:** A discontinuity on the forging surface that appears as a crack or seam, formed when two or more metal streams meet but fail to fuse completely.
- **Cause:** Low forging temperature, poor die design (sharp corners forcing abrupt flow change), excessive chilling at die surface, oxide film preventing fusion.
- **Why it happens (Physics):** When two metal streams approach each other, the **oxide layer (FeO, Fe₃O₄)** that formed during heating acts as a barrier. The base metal below this oxide cannot metallurgically bond → a planar void remains.
- **Remedy:** Increase forging temperature, round off die corners (generous fillet radii), improve lubrication, use proper die pre-heating (typically **150 °C – 300 °C** for hot-forging dies).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** Cold shut is *almost always* a **surface defect** running along a parting line or where flow lines converge. Examiners frequently give a sketch of forging with a lap and ask you to identify it.

---

#### 2.2.2 Flakes (Internal Hairline Cracks)

- **Definition:** Short, discontinuous internal cracks oriented randomly inside the forging.
- **Cause:** Excessive hydrogen content in steel combined with rapid cooling after forging. Hydrogen atoms precipitate at grain boundaries and create internal fissures.
- **Why it happens (Physics):** At high temperature, hydrogen dissolves in austenite. On rapid cooling, hydrogen's solubility drops sharply, and it tries to escape, but if trapped → internal pressure builds → microcracks.
- **Remedy:** Vacuum degassing, slow cooling (furnace cooling) post-forging, use of low-hydrogen electrodes for re-melting.

---

#### 2.2.3 Unfilled Section (Misrun / Short Shot)

- **Definition:** Forging does not completely fill the die cavity; some portion is missing or has reduced thickness.
- **Cause:** Insufficient material, low forging temperature, excessive die chilling, inadequate flash, high flow stress material.
- **Remedy:** Proper billet weight calculation, correct forging temperature, die preheating, design flash properly.

---

#### 2.2.4 Forging Laps and Folds

- **Definition:** A **fold** is created when a portion of metal overlaps onto itself during forging. A **lap** is a surface discontinuity similar to cold shut but typically along a parting line.
- **Cause:** Sharp die corners, excessive material, improper flash gutter design.
- **Physics:** A thin layer of metal flows over a previously placed layer without welding to it → permanent line of separation.

---

#### 2.2.5 Cracks (Forging Cracks)

- **Definition:** Open or sub-surface fractures caused by tensile stresses exceeding the material's hot strength.
- **Cause:** Excessive deformation at low temperature, hot shortness (sulphur-induced), improper die design causing uneven flow.
- **Why it happens:** At low temperatures, ductility is reduced, and tensile stresses (especially at centre/core during upsetting) cause **rupture**.

---

#### 2.2.6 Burst

- **Definition:** A large internal rupture or crack due to excessive hydrostatic tensile stress, typically at the centre of an upset cylinder.
- **Cause:** Over-upsetting (excessive reduction) without proper lubrication → non-uniform flow → central burst.
- **Critical limit:** When the ratio **H₀/D₀ > 2**, axial upsetting tends to cause internal bursts (this is a **famous KTU numerical concept**).

---

#### 2.2.7 Scale Pits and Surface Roughness

- **Definition:** Pitted, rough, or stepped surface due to oxide scale being hammered into the surface.
- **Cause:** Inadequate scale removal before forging, excessive heating, repeated reheating.
- **Remedy:** Use of descaling equipment (hydraulic descaler, rotary wire brush), protective atmospheres, scale-free heating.

---

#### 2.2.8 Inclusions and Porosity (Metallurgical Origin)

- **Definition:** Non-metallic inclusions (MnS, silicates, oxides) and internal porosity carried from the original ingot.
- **Cause:** Inadequate refining of the steel, slag entrapment, gas evolution during solidification.
- **Remedy:** Use ESR (Electroslag Remelting) or VAR (Vacuum Arc Remelting) billets, proper cropping of ingot top/bottom.

---

### 2.3 Detailed Analysis — Major Rolling Defects

Rolling defects are grouped into:

#### 2.3.1 Shape and Flatness Defects

##### (a) Wavy Edges (Scalloped Edges)
- **Definition:** Periodic undulation along the rolled strip edges.
- **Cause:** Excessive roll flattening (rolls deflect elastically), uneven roll gap.
- **Physics:** Rolls are not infinitely stiff; under load, they **deflect** forming a "crowned" shape. If the strip is thin and wide, edges see less compression than centre → edges elongate more → wavy.

##### (b) Buckle (Centre Buckle / Quarter Buckle)
- **Definition:** Buckling or wrinkling of the strip, typically at the centre or quarter-points.
- **Cause:** Residual stresses from non-uniform thickness, excessive roll pressure.
- **KTU Memory Trick:** **Buckle = Buckling instability** (like a long thin plate under compression).

##### (c) Crown (Camber)
- **Definition:** Strip thicker at centre than edges, caused by roll deflection.
- **Cause:** Long slender rolls, low stiffness.
- **Remedy:** Use **cambered rolls** (slightly larger diameter at centre), back-up rolls, roll bending.

##### (d) Edge Cracks (also called *Edge Tears*)
- **Definition:** Small cracks at the strip edges.
- **Cause:** Secondary tensile stresses at edges due to redundant deformation (spreading of the strip).
- **Why it happens:** During rolling, the material in the deformation zone wants to spread sideways. The rolls restrict this → **hoop tensile stresses** at the edges → cracks.

---

#### 2.3.2 Surface Defects

##### (a) Scale
- **Definition:** Surface oxide (FeO, Fe₂O₃, Fe₃O₄) that forms during heating and is rolled into the surface.
- **Cause:** Excessive soaking time at high temperature, inadequate descaling.
- **Remedy:** Hydraulic descaling, controlled atmosphere furnaces.

##### (b) Scratches and Rolling Marks
- **Cause:** Mechanical damage from rolls (roll surface roughness, scale embedded in roll).

##### (c) Spalling
- **Definition:** Flaking of the surface layer during rolling due to thermal shock or hydrogen.
- **Cause:** Severe temperature gradients at entry → surface spalls off.

---

#### 2.3.3 Internal Defects

##### (a) Alligator / Fish-Mouth Crack
- **Definition:** A central splitting that opens up like an alligator's mouth when the strip fractures.
- **Cause:** Excessive reduction per pass, low ductility, centre porosity from the original ingot.
- **Why it happens:** When the centre has a void or low-ductility zone, tensile stresses from redundant deformation cause the centre to split horizontally.

##### (b) Lamination
- **Definition:** A layered separation parallel to the rolled surface, usually in the through-thickness direction.
- **Cause:** Blowholes, inclusions, or pipe from the original ingot that get elongated into flat sheets.

##### (c) Center Burst (or *Central Bursting*)
- Same phenomenon as the forging burst, but occurs in the rolling plane. The classic **Criterion by Cockcroft and Latham (1968)** applies:

$$\int_{0}^{\bar{\varepsilon}_{f}} \sigma^{*} \, d\bar{\varepsilon} \;\geq \; C_{crit}$$

where $\sigma^{*} = \max(\sigma_1, 0)$ is the positive (tensile) principal stress and $C_{crit}$ is the critical damage value for the material.

---

#### 2.3.4 Other Common Rolling Defects

| Defect | Visual | Cause |
|---|---|---|
| **Cobbles** | Strip wraps around rolls or jams | Excessive bite, mis-tracking |
| **Earing** | Cup-shaped ears at top after deep drawing | Anisotropy (texture) from rolling |
| **Orange Peel** | Pebbled surface (visible grains) | Coarse grain size, low reduction |
| **Sticker Marks** | Dark spots where coils stick | Insufficient cooling before coiling |
| **Hole / Slivers** | Surface breaking voids | Subsurface inclusions or blowholes |

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbols | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **True Strain in Rolling** $\varepsilon = \ln \left( \dfrac{t_1}{t_2} \right)$ | $t_1$ = initial thickness, $t_2$ = final thickness | Total deformation measure |
| 2 | **Draft (Absolute Reduction)** $\Delta h = t_1 - t_2$ | mm | Per-pass reduction |
| 3 | **Contact Length (Projected)** $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$ | $R$ = roll radius | Geometry of bite |
| 4 | **Bite Angle** $\alpha = \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta h}{R}}$ | radians | Entry/exit condition |
| 5 | **No-Slip Condition (Friction Hill)** $\alpha \leq \beta$ where $\tan \beta = \mu$ | $\mu$ = coefficient of friction | Material must enter the rolls |
| 6 | **Roll Separating Force (Frictionless)** $F = \bar{Y}_f \cdot L \cdot w$ | $\bar{Y}_f$ = flow stress, $w$ = strip width | Roll load estimation |
| 7 | **Coefficient of Friction (Ford-Alexander)** $\mu = \dfrac{\alpha}{2} \left( \dfrac{t_1 + t_2}{t_1 - t_2} \right)^{1/2}$ | empirical | Cold rolling friction |
| 8 | **Forging Ratio** $R_f = \dfrac{A_0}{A_f}$ | $A_0, A_f$ = original & final areas | Quality metric (≥ 3:1 recommended) |
| 9 | **Maximum Upset Ratio (no buckling/burst)** $H_0/D_0 \leq 2$ | $H_0, D_0$ = initial height, diameter | Cylinder upsetting limit |
| 10 | **Average Flow Stress (Approximation)** $\bar{Y}_f = \dfrac{Y_0 + Y_f}{2}$ | $Y_0, Y_f$ = initial and final yield | For force calculation |
| 11 | **Hill's Criterion for Central Burst** (qualitative) | high friction → low burst, moderate friction → high burst | Predicting central burst |
| 12 | **Cockcroft–Latham Criterion** (above) | damage integral | Ductile fracture prediction |
| 13 | **Specific Roll Pressure** $p = \bar{Y}_f \left( 1 + \dfrac{\mu L}{\bar{h}} \right)$ | $\bar{h}$ = mean thickness | Sims/Ekelund approximation |
| 14 | **True Stress in Compression (Forging)** $\sigma = K \cdot \varepsilon^{n}$ | $K$ = strength coeff., $n$ = strain-hardening exp. | Plastic flow in forging |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark Alert (Formulas):** A direct numerical on roll separating force using $F = \bar{Y}_f \cdot L \cdot w$ (with friction hill correction) is a 7-mark standard. Make sure to state units (N or kN) and convert consistently.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace forgings (Ti-6Al-4V):** Strict defect limits — no laps, no folds, ultrasonic inspection mandatory.
- **Automotive crankshafts:** Forging ratio 4:1 to 6:1 ensures fibre flow follows contour, enhancing fatigue life.
- **Pressure vessel plates (ASTM A516):** Ultrasonic lamination checks mandatory; lamination in a vessel plate = catastrophic failure.
- **Ship hull plates:** Centre bursts and laminations in rolled plates are unacceptable for marine use.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Contact Length and Bite Angle in Rolling

**Given:**
- Two rolls of radius $R$ rotating in opposite directions.
- A strip of initial thickness $t_1$ enters and exits with final thickness $t_2$.
- Draft (absolute reduction) $\Delta h = t_1 - t_2$.

**Geometric setup:**

The strip is touched by the rolls at two points. The horizontal distance between the entry and exit point of contact is the **projected contact length** $L$. Drawing the geometry:

The chord from roll centre to entry point makes a small angle $\alpha$ (the bite angle) with the roll centre line. From the geometry:

$$L = \sqrt{R^2 - (R - \Delta h)^2} = \sqrt{2R\Delta h - (\Delta h)^2}$$

Since $\Delta h \ll R$ in practical rolling, $(\Delta h)^2$ is negligible.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L^2 &= R^2 - (R - \Delta h)^2 \\
    &= R^2 - \left[ R^2 - 2R \Delta h + (\Delta h)^2 \right] \\
    &= 2R \Delta h - (\Delta h)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\Delta h \ll R$, the second-order term is dropped:

$$
L \approx \sqrt{2R \Delta h} = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}
$$

For the bite angle, since $\sin \alpha \approx \alpha$ for small angles, and from the triangle:

$$\cos \alpha = \dfrac{R - \Delta h}{R} = 1 - \dfrac{\Delta h}{R}$$

Using Taylor expansion: $\cos \alpha \approx 1 - \alpha^2/2$, hence:

$$\alpha = \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta h}{R}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** Units: $R$ in mm, $\Delta h$ in mm → $L$ in mm. ✓
> If $R = 200$ mm, $\Delta h = 2$ mm, then $L = \sqrt{400} = 20$ mm, and $\alpha = \sqrt{0.01} = 0.1$ rad ≈ **5.73°**. Realistic.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Roll Separating Force (Simplified, Frictionless Slab Analysis)

**Slab Method Assumptions:**
1. Plane strain (strip width $\gg$ thickness).
2. Frictionless rolls (first-order estimate).
3. Homogeneous deformation.
4. Yield criterion: Von Mises.

**Free Body Diagram (slab of thickness $dh$ at distance $h$ from centre line):**

The slab has length $L$, width $w$, and thickness $dh$. Pressure on its two faces is $p + dp$ and $p$. Rolls apply normal pressure $p$ on top and bottom.

**Equilibrium of the slab (horizontal direction):**

The vertical equilibrium gives (summing forces along strip direction):

$$
\begin{aligned}
(p + dp)(L + dL)w - p \cdot L \cdot w - 2p \, w \, \tan \alpha \, dh &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Neglecting higher-order differentials and noting that $L \approx \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$, after integration and application of the **yield criterion** $\sigma_x + p = \bar{Y}_f$ (where $\bar{Y}_f$ is the plane-strain flow stress in compression):

The **total roll separating force** (per unit width) is:

$$F/w = \bar{Y}_f \cdot L$$

For finite width:

$$\boxed{F = \bar{Y}_f \cdot L \cdot w = \bar{Y}_f \cdot w \cdot \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Many students forget to multiply by strip width $w$ and only write $F = \bar{Y}_f \cdot L$. This is worth **1 mark lost** in the final answer.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Friction Hill (Including Friction)

**Refinement using the Friction Hill Equation:**

Including friction between the roll and the workpiece, and assuming sticking friction (no slip at the roll-strip interface), the **specific roll pressure** $p$ is given by:

$$
p = \bar{Y}_f \cdot e^{\mu L / \bar{h}}
$$

where:
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction
- $L$ = contact length
- $\bar{h} = (t_1 + t_2)/2$ = mean strip thickness in deformation zone

**Total roll force (with friction):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \int_0^L p \cdot w \, dx = w \int_0^L \bar{Y}_f \cdot e^{\mu L / \bar{h}} \, dx \\
  &= \bar{Y}_f \cdot w \cdot L \cdot e^{\mu L / \bar{h}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Check (KTU-style problem):**

> A steel strip, $w = 200$ mm wide, is cold-rolled from $t_1 = 4$ mm to $t_2 = 3$ mm. Roll radius $R = 250$ mm. $\bar{Y}_f = 600$ MPa, $\mu = 0.05$. Find the roll separating force.

**Solution steps:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta h &= t_1 - t_2 = 4 - 3 = 1 \text{ mm} \\
L &= \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} = \sqrt{250 \times 1} = 15.81 \text{ mm} \\
\bar{h} &= (t_1 + t_2)/2 = (4 + 3)/2 = 3.5 \text{ mm} \\
\mu L / \bar{h} &= 0.05 \times 15.81 / 3.5 = 0.2259 \\
e^{0.2259} &= 1.2534 \\
F &= \bar{Y}_f \cdot w \cdot L \cdot e^{\mu L / \bar{h}} \\
  &= 600 \times 200 \times 15.81 \times 1.2534 \\
  &= 600 \times 200 \times 19.82 \\
  &= 2{,}378{,}520 \text{ N} \\
  &\approx 2.38 \text{ MN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer: $F \approx 2.38$ MN** (or 2378 kN).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Cockcroft–Latham Fracture Criterion for Centre Burst

For a 2D plane-strain rolling process, the **damage parameter** is:

$$
C = \int_0^{\bar{\varepsilon}_f} \sigma^{*} \, d\bar{\varepsilon}
$$

where $\sigma^{*} = \max(\sigma_1, 0)$ — the **maximum positive (tensile) principal stress** in the deformation zone.

**Critical condition for centre burst:**

$$
\int_0^{\bar{\varepsilon}_f} \sigma^{*} \, d\bar{\varepsilon} \;\geq\; C_{crit}
$$

**Physical interpretation:**
- If $C < C_{crit}$ → no fracture (safe rolling).
- If $C \geq C_{crit}$ → centre burst occurs.
- $\sigma_1$ (max principal stress) is **tensile** at the centre of the strip in the rolling plane when friction is **moderate**.
- **Hill's qualitative rule:** Centre burst is most likely at moderate friction, not at very high or very low friction.

**Practical mitigation in industry:**
- Use **good lubrication** (reduces tensile stresses at the centre).
- Use **smaller reduction per pass** (keeps $\bar{\varepsilon}_f$ small).
- Use **back tension / front tension** to control $\sigma_1$.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix: Defect → Cause → Remedy

| # | Defect | Process | Primary Cause | Secondary Cause | Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cold Shut | Forging | Low temp, oxide | Sharp die corners | ↑ T, round corners |
| 2 | Flakes | Forging | Hydrogen | Rapid cooling | Vacuum degas, slow cool |
| 3 | Unfilled Section | Forging | Insufficient material | Chilled die | Proper billet, die preheat |
| 4 | Lap / Fold | Forging | Excessive flash | Sharp corners | ↑ fillet, ↓ flash |
| 5 | Centre Burst | Forging | High upset ratio | Poor lubrication | $H_0/D_0 \leq 2$, lube |
| 6 | Surface Crack | Forging | Low ductility | Hot shortness | ↑ T, desulphurize |
| 7 | Scale Pits | Forging | Excessive scale | Long soaking | Descaling, controlled atm. |
| 8 | Wavy Edges | Rolling | Roll deflection | Thin wide strip | Cambered rolls, back-up |
| 9 | Centre Buckle | Rolling | Residual stress | Roll wear | Roll bending, tension |
| 10 | Edge Cracks | Rolling | Tensile edge stress | Excessive draft | ↓ reduction, ↑ tension |
| 11 | Alligator Crack | Rolling | Centre defect | High reduction | ↓ draft, multi-pass |
| 12 | Lamination | Rolling | Inherited voids | Poor billet | Use clean steel, ESR |
| 13 | Spalling | Rolling | Thermal shock | Hot entry temp | Pre-heat, gradual entry |
| 14 | Earing | Rolling (deep draw) | Anisotropy | Texture | Annealing, random texture |
| 15 | Cobbles | Rolling | Bite failure | Low friction | ↑ bite, ↑ $\mu$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Defect Formation Mechanism (Bulk Forming)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material Selection] --> B[Heating Stage]
    B --> C{Billet/Die Condition}
    C -->|High Temp, Good Die| D[Plastic Deformation - Safe]
    C -->|Low Temp, Chilled Die| E[Material Flow Defects]
    C -->|Wrong Speed / Force| F[Mechanical / Shape Defects]
    C -->|Bad Material| G[Metallurgical Defects]
    D --> H[Forging/Rolling Product]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I{Quality Inspection}
    I -->|Pass| J[Approved Component]
    I -->|Fail| K[Rework / Scrap]
    E --> E1[Cold Shut, Laps, Unfilled]
    F --> F1[Cracks, Bursts, Wavy Edges]
    G --> G1[Inclusions, Porosity, Lamination]
    style E1 fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#ff8c00
    style F1 fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#dc143c
    style G1 fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#8b008b
    style J fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400
    style K fill:#ff6347,stroke:#8b0000
```

---

### 4.2 Schematic: Centre Burst in Rolling (Slab Geometry)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DeformationZone[Deformation Zone - Top View]
        direction LR
        R1[Roll 1]:::roll --> P1[Plastic Zone 1]:::zone
        P1 --> P2[Plastic Zone 2]:::zone
        P2 --> P3[Plastic Zone 3]:::zone
        P3 --> R2[Roll 2]:::roll
    end
    DeformationZone --> CB[Center Burst Cracks Open]:::crack
    CB --> S1[Split 1 - Top Centre]:::crack
    CB --> S2[Split 2 - Bottom Centre]:::crack
    classDef roll fill:#4682b4,stroke:#00008b,color:#fff
    classDef zone fill:#f0e68c,stroke:#daa520
    classDef crack fill:#ff6347,stroke:#8b0000,color:#fff
```

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Forging Defect Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Observed Forging Defect] --> Q1{Is it surface visible?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Smooth or rough?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Internal crack or void?}
    Q2 -->|Smooth seam| A1[Cold Shut / Lap]
    Q2 -->|Rough pit| A2[Scale Pit]
    Q3 -->|Hairline crack| A3[Flakes - Hydrogen]
    Q3 -->|Large central crack| A4[Centre Burst]
    Q1 -->|Unfilled die| A5[Unfilled Section / Misrun]
    style A1 fill:#ffe4b5
    style A2 fill:#ffe4b5
    style A3 fill:#dda0dd
    style A4 fill:#ff6347,color:#fff
    style A5 fill:#ffb6c1
```

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Rolling Defect → Cause Mapping

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define "cold shut" in forging. State any two preventive measures.*

**Model Answer:**

**Cold shut (or cold lap)** is a surface discontinuity in a forging that appears as a crack, seam, or lap, formed when two or more metal streams flowing inside a die cavity meet but fail to fuse completely due to the presence of an oxide layer (FeO) and/or insufficient temperature at the joining interface.

**Preventive measures:**
1. Maintain forging temperature within the recommended range (typically **900 °C – 1250 °C** for carbon steels) so that oxide films are minimized and metal flow is fluid.
2. Provide generous fillet radii at die corners to allow smooth merging of metal streams.
3. Use proper die preheating (150 – 300 °C) and adequate lubrication.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for remedies)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List four common surface defects that appear in rolled steel sheets and give a one-line reason for each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Defect | Reason |
|---|---|
| **Scale marks** | Surface oxide rolled into the strip due to inadequate descaling |
| **Scratches** | Mechanical damage from roll surface or guide equipment |
| **Spalling** | Thermal shock from hot strip entering cold rolls |
| **Sticker marks** | Localized oxidation where coil layers stick together during cooling |

*(3 marks: 4 × 0.75 = 3, with proper reasoning)*

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

## 🅰️ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — FORGING DEFECTS FOCUS

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** *Explain the phenomenon of "centre burst" in forging with a neat sketch. Discuss its causes and the conditions under which it occurs. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *With the help of suitable diagrams, describe the following forging defects: (i) Cold shut, (ii) Unfilled section, (iii) Flakes. State at least one preventive measure for each. (7 marks)*

---

### 🅰️ Model Answer A

#### Part (a) — Centre Burst in Forging (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
A *centre burst* (also called internal burst, chevron crack, or central rupture) is a large internal crack that opens up along the axis of an upset cylindrical workpiece, perpendicular to the loading direction.

**Sketch (2 marks):**
```
        ┌─────── P (axial force) ───────┐
        ▼                                ▼
       ┌────────────────────────────────┐
       │  ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░  │  ← Workpiece top
       │  ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░  │
       │  ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░  │
       │  ░░░░░░░░░░/  ╲░░░░░░░░░░░░░░  │
       │  ░░░░░░░░/    ╲░░░░░░░░░░░░░░  │  ← Internal cracks
       │  ░░░░░░░/      ╲░░░░░░░░░░░░░  │     (chevron shape)
       │  ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░  │
       │  ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░  │
       └────────────────────────────────┘
       |←── D₀ ─→|     |←── D₀ ─→|
```

**Causes (2 marks):**
1. **Excessive upset ratio:** When the ratio $H_0/D_0 > 2$, the cylindrical billet becomes unstable and develops tensile hydrostatic stresses along the axis.
2. **Friction at die-workpiece interface:** Causes non-uniform flow. The material near the centre is constrained from flowing radially → develops tensile stress in the hoop and axial direction.
3. **Material inherent defect:** Centre porosity from the cast structure acts as a crack initiator.
4. **Cold working temperature:** Low temperature reduces ductility and increases the probability of burst.

**Condition (1 mark):**
According to **Kudo's criterion** and **Hill's analysis**, centre burst is most likely when:
$$1.5 \leq H_0/D_0 \leq 2.5 \quad \text{(with high friction)}$$
$$H_0/D_0 \leq 2 \quad \text{(practical safe limit)}$$

**Preventive Measures (1 mark):**
- Limit $H_0/D_0 \leq 2$ during upset.
- Use good lubrication.
- Use tapered or concave dies to promote uniform flow.
- Pre-heat billet uniformly.

---

#### Part (b) — Other Forging Defects (7 marks)

**(i) Cold Shut (2 marks)**

**Diagram:**
```
       ┌─────────────────────────────┐
       │     ┌───◄── Cold Shut ──►───┤
       │     │     ╲___________╱      │
       │     │   Stream 1  Stream 2   │
       │     ╲___________________╱    │
       └─────────────────────────────┘
```

**Cause:** Two metal streams meet but fail to fuse due to surface oxide and low temperature.
**Prevention:** Maintain forging temperature; round off die corners; preheat die.

---

**(ii) Unfilled Section (2.5 marks)**

**Diagram:**
```
       ┌──────────────────────┐
       │  ┌──╲               │
       │  │    ╲___           │  ← Die cavity
       │  │        ╲___       │
       │  │            ╲___   │
       │  └─────────────────── │
       │  |Unfilled|          │
       │  | region |          │
       └──────────────────────┘
```

**Cause:** Insufficient material, low temperature, or excess flash.
**Prevention:** Calculate proper billet weight; preheat die; increase forging temperature.

---

**(iii) Flakes (2.5 marks)**

**Diagram:**
```
       Cross-section of forging:
       ┌─────────────────────────┐
       │     /     /     /       │
       │    /     /     /        │  ← Hairline flakes
       │   /     /     /         │     randomly oriented
       │  /     /     /          │
       │ /     /     /           │
       └─────────────────────────┘
```

**Cause:** High hydrogen content combined with rapid cooling post-forging.
**Prevention:** Vacuum degassing of steel; slow (furnace) cooling after forging.

---

## 🅱️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ROLLING DEFECTS FOCUS

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** *A steel strip of width 250 mm is cold-rolled from 5 mm to 3 mm thickness using rolls of 300 mm radius. The average flow stress is 550 MPa and the coefficient of friction is 0.06. Estimate the roll separating force. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *With neat sketches, explain the following rolling defects: (i) Wavy edges, (ii) Centre buckle, (iii) Alligator / fish-mouth cracking. Discuss their causes and remedies. (7 marks)*

---

### 🅱️ Model Answer B

#### Part (a) — Roll Separating Force Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $w = 250$ mm
- $t_1 = 5$ mm, $t_2 = 3$ mm
- $R = 300$ mm
- $\bar{Y}_f = 550$ MPa
- $\mu = 0.06$

**Step 1: Draft (0.5 marks)**
$$\Delta h = t_1 - t_2 = 5 - 3 = 2 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Contact length (1 mark)**
$$L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} = \sqrt{300 \times 2} = \sqrt{600} = 24.495 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Mean thickness (0.5 marks)**
$$\bar{h} = \frac{t_1 + t_2}{2} = \frac{5 + 3}{2} = 4 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Friction hill factor (1.5 marks)**
$$e^{\mu L / \bar{h}} = e^{0.06 \times 24.495 / 4} = e^{0.3674} = 1.4441$$

**Step 5: Roll Separating Force (3.5 marks)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \bar{Y}_f \cdot w \cdot L \cdot e^{\mu L / \bar{h}} \\
  &= 550 \text{ MPa} \times 250 \text{ mm} \times 24.495 \text{ mm} \times 1.4441 \\
  &= 550 \times 250 \times 35.37 \\
  &= 550 \times 8842.5 \\
  &= 4{,}863{,}375 \text{ N} \\
  &\approx 4.86 \text{ MN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer: $F \approx 4.86$ MN** or **4863 kN**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. ❌ Forgetting to include the friction hill factor $e^{\mu L/\bar{h}}$. This will reduce your answer by ~45% — full 2-mark penalty.
> 2. ❌ Not converting mm² to m² — units become inconsistent. The trick: keep all units in **mm** and **N/mm² (MPa)**, then the answer comes out in N.
> 3. ❌ Using $L = \sqrt{2R\Delta h}$ (the more accurate form) — it's acceptable but for KTU the simplified form $L = \sqrt{R\Delta h}$ is conventional.
> 4. ❌ Mixing true stress with flow stress — use $\bar{Y}_f$ as average flow stress.
> 5. ❌ Skipping the bite-angle check $\alpha = \sqrt{\Delta h / R} = \sqrt{2/300} = 0.0816$ rad, while $\tan^{-1}(0.06) = 0.0599$ rad — ⚠️ **bite condition is violated!** In practice, this requires multiple passes.

---

#### Part (b) — Rolling Defects with Sketches (7 marks)

**(i) Wavy Edges (2.5 marks)**

**Sketch:**
```
       ┌────────────────────────┐
       │ ╲    ╱╲    ╱╲    ╱╲    ╱│  ← Wavy edge
       │   ╲╱    ╲╱    ╲╱    ╲╱  │
       │ ────────────────────── │
       │      Strip body        │
       └────────────────────────┘
```

**Cause:** Roll deflection under load — rolls are not infinitely stiff; they bend, creating a "crowned" gap that thins the centre more than the edges, leaving the edges wavy.

**Remedy:**
- Use **cambered rolls** (larger diameter at centre, smaller at ends).
- Use **back-up rolls** for support.
- Apply **roll bending** hydraulic system.

---

**(ii) Centre Buckle (2.5 marks)**

**Sketch:**
```
       Top view of strip:
       ┌───────────────────────┐
       │                       │
       │      ╱─────╲          │
       │     ╱       ╲         │  ← Centre buckle (loose)
       │    ╱         ╲        │
       │   ╱___________╲       │
       │                       │
       └───────────────────────┘
```

**Cause:** Residual compressive stress in the central region of the strip (where it is compressed more than the edges), causing the strip to buckle longitudinally.

**Remedy:** Reduce roll pressure, use flatter roll gap profile, apply tension to flatten.

---

**(iii) Alligator / Fish-Mouth Crack (2 marks)**

**Sketch:**
```
       Cross-section of broken strip:
       ┌─────────────────┐
       │     /     \     │  ← Fish mouth opening
       │    /       \    │
       │   /         \   │
       │  / Alligator \  │
       │ /   Mouth    \ │
       │/_____________ \│
       └─────────────────┘
```

**Cause:** Centre burst becoming so severe that the strip splits into two along the central plane, looking like an alligator's mouth.

**Remedy:** Reduce draft per pass, improve material cleanliness, use good lubrication, apply back-tension.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Sketch & Reasoning Pitfalls:**
> 1. ❌ Drawing a flat strip without showing the **wavy/curved** nature of the edge → lose 1 mark.
> 2. ❌ Confusing **centre buckle** (longitudinal) with **centre crack** (transverse) — these are *different* defects.
> 3. ❌ Not labelling the diagram — always label parts (workpiece, defect, force arrows).
> 4. ❌ Stating the cause without the corresponding remedy → in 14-mark questions, both are required.

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Defects in Forging & Rolling**

### ✅ Forging Defects — Quick Recap
- **Cold shut:** Two metal streams fail to fuse (oxide + low temp).
- **Flakes:** Internal hairline cracks (hydrogen-induced).
- **Unfilled section:** Die cavity not completely filled (low T, short billet).
- **Lap / Fold:** Metal overlaps without welding (sharp die corners, excess material).
- **Centre burst:** Internal rupture when $H_0/D_0 > 2$ in upsetting.
- **Cracks:** Low-temperature forging or hot shortness (sulphur).
- **Scale pits:** Oxide rolled into surface (poor descaling).
- **Burst:** Hydrostatic tensile failure at workpiece centre.
- **Inclusions:** Non-metallics from ingot (use ESR/VAR for clean steel).
- **Porosity:** Gas voids from solidification (use vacuum degassing).

### ✅ Rolling Defects — Quick Recap
- **Wavy edges:** Roll deflection; use cambered or back-up rolls.
- **Crown:** Centre thicker than edges (low roll stiffness).
- **Buckle:** Strip wrinkles (residual stress).
- **Edge cracks:** Tensile stresses at edges (reduce reduction/pass).
- **Centre burst (alligator):** Internal centre fracture (use Cockcroft-Latham).
- **Lamination:** Layered separation from blowholes (clean steel, ESR).
- **Spalling:** Surface flakes (thermal shock).
- **Earing:** Anisotropy in deep-drawing sheets.
- **Cobbles:** Strip jams in rolls.
- **Sticker marks:** Coil layers stick during cooling.

### ✅ Critical Formulas to Memorise
- $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$
- $\alpha = \sqrt{\Delta h / R}$
- $F = \bar{Y}_f \cdot w \cdot L \cdot e^{\mu L / \bar{h}}$ (with friction)
- $F = \bar{Y}_f \cdot w \cdot L$ (frictionless)
- $\varepsilon = \ln(t_1 / t_2)$
- $H_0 / D_0 \leq 2$ (safe upset ratio)
- Cockcroft–Latham: $\int_0^{\bar{\varepsilon}_f} \sigma^* d\bar{\varepsilon} \geq C_{crit}$

### ✅ Numerical Conversions
- 1 MPa = 1 N/mm²
- 1 MN = 1000 kN = 10⁶ N
- 1 mm² × 1 MPa = 1 N

### ✅ KTU-Favoured Topics
- Centre burst derivation and Cockcroft-Latham criterion.
- Roll separating force numericals with friction hill.
- Hot upset ratio $H_0/D_0$.
- Sketch identification of cold shut, laps, alligator cracks, lamination.
- Differences between forging and rolling defects.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 1 — Topic Notes: Defects in Forging and Rolling**
