## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this checklist 5 minutes before the exam to lock in your marks.**

- ☐ **SMAW definition** = arc + consumable flux-coated electrode + dual shielding (gas + slag).
- ☐ Arc temperature ≈ **6000 °C**; Arc efficiency ≈ **0.75–0.85**.
- ☐ **AWS format:** **E – XX – Y – Z** → Tensile strength (ksi) – Position – Coating.
- ☐ **XX** is in **ksi**, not MPa (60 ksi = 415 MPa).
- ☐ **Y = 1** → all positions; **Y = 2** → flat & horizontal only.
- ☐ **Z = 0** → deep penetration, DC+; **Z = 3** → smooth, AC/DC; **Z = 8** → low-hydrogen.
- ☐ **E6013** = *the* general-purpose electrode (most common in KTU lab exams).
- ☐ **E7018** = low-hydrogen, used for HSLA steel, pressure vessels, bridges.
- ☐ **Heat input formula:** $H = \dfrac{\eta V I}{1000 S}$ **in kJ/mm** — convert S to **mm/min** always.
- ☐ ↑ Current → ↑ penetration; ↑ Voltage → ↑ bead width; ↑ Speed → ↓ penetration.
- ☐ **4-H causes of porosity:** **H**ydrogen, **H**umidity, **H**igh speed, **H**eavy rust.
- ☐ Flux coating provides **3 functions:** gas shield, slag blanket, arc stabilization.
- ☐ Always **chip slag** between passes in multi-pass welds.
- ☐ **SMAW advantage:** no external gas → outdoor/wind-friendly. **SMAW limitation:** low deposition rate, slag removal.
- ☐ In KTU numerical problems, **always state the units** in your final answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Submerged Arc Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT404 - MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Submerged Arc Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)** is an *arc welding process* in which coalescence of metals is produced by heating with an electric arc (or arcs) between a bare (or metal-cored) consumable electrode (or electrodes) and the workpiece. The arc and the molten weld pool are **completely submerged** (shielded/buried) under a blanket of fusible, granular flux. The flux is fed from a hopper either by gravity or by compressed air pressure, forming a thick protective mound over the weld zone. The process is standardized under **AWS A5.17 / A5.23** (carbon and low-alloy steels) and **ISO 14171** internationally.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Syllabus Highlight:** SAW is classified as a **semi-automatic or fully-mechanized arc welding process** (often called a "high-deposition-rate" process) where the arc is **invisible to the operator** because the granular flux blanket completely covers it. The flux not only shields the arc but also **decomposes** to provide deoxidizers, alloying elements, and a protective slag cover on the solidified weld bead.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Campfire Buried in Snow":** Imagine a roaring campfire in a snowstorm. As the fire burns, the surrounding snow melts and forms a wet, granular blanket that completely covers and smothers the flame. The fire still burns hot underneath, but you can no longer see it, hear the crackle, or feel the intense UV radiation. SAW works exactly this way. The arc burns at temperatures of about **1,800 °C – 2,400 °C** (the "campfire"), while the granular flux — typically 25–50 mm thick — forms a molten "snow blanket" that:
> 1. **Shields** the arc from atmospheric oxygen and nitrogen.
> 2. **Smothers** the harmful UV/IR radiation (no arc-flash hazard to welder).
> 3. **Dissolves** deoxidizers (Mn, Si) into the molten pool, like rain seeping nutrients into soil.
> 4. **Freezes** into a removable slag crust on top, just like frozen snow crust over a fire.

This is why a SAW operator can stand right next to a high-current (often **600 A – 1,200 A**) arc without needing a dark face shield — the flux "blanket" handles all the protection.

### 1.3 Salient Process Metrics & Standard Constants

| Parameter | Typical Value | KTU Exam Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Current range | **200 A – 2,000 A** | Use **600 A, 800 A, 1,000 A** in numericals |
| Voltage range | **20 V – 44 V** | Standard problems use **28 V – 32 V** |
| Travel speed | **30 cm/min – 150 cm/min** | Often given as **30 cm/min** |
| Flux mound height | **25 mm – 50 mm** | Required for full shielding |
| Electrode diameter | **1.6 mm – 6.4 mm** | Most common: **3.2 mm, 4.0 mm** |
| Deposition rate | **Up to 25 kg/hour** | Single-wire, single-arc baseline |
| Heat input efficiency | **~60–70 %** absorbed by plate | Compared to ~80 % for stick, ~50 % for GTAW |
| Filler metal recovery | **~95–100 %** | (with active flux) |
| Power source | Constant Voltage (CV), **DC+ preferred** | AC possible for special applications |

### 1.4 Distinguishing Features of SAW

- **Invisible Arc:** Operator cannot see or strike the arc manually — must use **arc-strike tab** (run-on/run-off tabs).
- **High Deposition Rate:** Up to **5–10× higher** than Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW/stick welding).
- **Flux-Cored, Not Gas Shielded:** Self-shielding — no external shielding gas required (unlike MIG/GMAW).
- **Mechanized/Automatic:** Almost always used with a **tractor/carriage, boom-and-column, or welding lathe** — handheld SAW is rare.
- **Slag Formation:** Granular flux melts → covers weld → solidifies as slag → must be **chipped off** after each pass.
- **Single-pass capability:** Thick plates up to **25 mm can be welded in a single pass** without edge beveling (square butt joint).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" & "How" of the Process — Stepwise Logic

#### Step 1 — Flux Deposition
- Granular flux is gravity-fed (or pneumatically fed) from a **flux hopper** mounted above the welding head.
- A mound of flux **25–50 mm** thick is deposited just ahead of the welding arc, **completely burying** it.
- **Why?** The flux acts as a *physical* and *chemical* shield, replacing the gas shield of MIG and the gas+slag combination of stick welding.

#### Step 2 — Arc Establishment
- A **constant-voltage (CV) DC power source** (or AC transformer with drooping characteristic) maintains voltage.
- Wire is fed at a **constant rate** by a servo-driven wire feeder; current adjusts automatically to maintain arc length.
- **How?** The arc melts the continuously fed **bare, copper-coated mild-steel electrode wire** (e.g., AWS classification **EL8, EM12K, EH14**) at a controlled feed speed (typically **1.5–15 m/min**).

#### Step 3 — Arc & Weld Pool Submersion
- The arc and the molten weld pool sit **entirely beneath** the flux layer.
- The intense heat melts a thin layer of the **flux granules directly above the arc**, forming a **molten flux envelope** (a tiny lava-tube of liquid flux) that conducts current and contains the arc plasma.
- **Why is this important for KTU exams?** The flux envelope gives SAW a **lower voltage** requirement than open-arc processes (typically 28–32 V) because the arc length is *virtually fixed* by the flux layer — the operator cannot "long-arc" the process.

#### Step 4 — Metallurgical Reactions
As the flux melts, several reactions occur:

1. **Oxide reduction** (deoxidation): 
   - MnO, SiO₂ in the flux remove dissolved O from the molten pool.
   - Mn, Si from the flux transfer into the weld metal (acts as a **killing agent**).
2. **Slag formation**: Flux components (CaO, Al₂O₃, SiO₂, MnO, MgO) form a **low-density slag** that floats on top of the weld pool.
3. **Alloying**: Specific fluxes (called "**active fluxes**") can add Cr, Ni, Mo to the weld metal, making SAW capable of producing **stainless-steel and high-strength alloy welds**.
4. **Gas shield**: Organic components in the flux decompose to release CO, H₂ — these gases displace air and prevent oxidation.

#### Step 5 — Solidification & Slag Removal
- As the arc moves forward, the molten pool solidifies into a **dense, porosity-free weld bead**.
- The slag crust (now **5–10 mm thick**) solidifies on top.
- After cooling, slag is **chipped off manually** with a chipping hammer or **deslagged automatically** in multi-pass systems (e.g., tandem-arc, hot-wire systems).

### 2.2 Process Variables — The Four Independent Parameters

KTU exams often ask: *"What are the independent variables in SAW?"* — They are:

| # | Variable | Symbol | Range | Effect on Heat Input |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Arc voltage | V (V) | 20–44 V | **Directly proportional** ↑ V → ↑ H |
| 2 | Welding current | I (A) | 200–2000 A | **Directly proportional** ↑ I → ↑ H |
| 3 | Travel speed | S (cm/min or mm/min) | 30–150 cm/min | **Inversely proportional** ↑ S → ↓ H |
| 4 | Electrode extension (stickout) | e (mm) | 20–50 mm | Affects resistance heating of electrode |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following five formulas — they account for ~70% of all numerical questions on SAW.**

**Formula 1 — Heat Input (HI) in Welding**

$$H = \eta \times \frac{V \times I}{S \times 1000} \times 60 \quad \text{(kJ/mm if S in mm/min)}$$

Or equivalently:

$$H = \eta \times \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000} \quad \text{kJ/mm}$$

where $\eta$ = arc efficiency factor, $V$ = arc voltage (V), $I$ = current (A), $S$ = travel speed (mm/min).

| Process | $\eta$ (Efficiency) |
|---|---|
| **SAW (Submerged Arc Welding)** | **0.95 – 1.00** (often taken as **1.0** for KTU) |
| GMAW (MIG) | 0.75 – 0.85 |
| SMAW (Stick) | 0.75 – 0.85 |
| GTAW (TIG) | 0.40 – 0.60 |
| PAW (Plasma) | 0.55 – 0.65 |

**Formula 2 — Heat Input (kJ/cm form, very common in KTU textbooks)**

$$H = \eta \times \frac{V \times I}{S \times 10} \quad \text{kJ/cm}$$

(when $S$ is in cm/min — multiply denominator by 10 instead of 1000).

**Formula 3 — Deposition Rate (Mass per unit time)**

$$D = \frac{\pi \times d^2}{4} \times v_f \times \rho \times \eta_m \quad \text{(kg/min or kg/h)}$$

where $d$ = electrode wire diameter (m or mm), $v_f$ = wire feed speed (m/min or mm/min), $\rho$ = density of electrode material ($\approx 7.85 \times 10^3$ kg/m³ for steel), $\eta_m$ = metal transfer efficiency ($\approx 0.95$ for SAW).

**Formula 4 — Wire Feed Rate from Current**

For SAW, an empirical linear relation holds:

$$v_f = k \times I + C$$

where $k$ is the wire-feed coefficient (mm/min per amp) and $C$ is a small constant. In standard KTU problems, this is often given in a problem-specific table.

**Formula 5 — Penetration / Bead Width correlations (qualitative)**

- **Bead width (W):** $W \propto \dfrac{V \times I}{S}$ — increases with VI, decreases with S.
- **Penetration (P):** $P \propto \dfrac{I}{S}$ — increases with current, decreases with speed.
- **Reinforcement height (R):** Inversely related to S.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Industry | Application |
|---|---|
| **Shipbuilding** | Welding long, thick hull plates (single-pass up to 25 mm) |
| **Pressure vessels** | Boiler shells, LPG cylinders, nuclear pressure vessels |
| **Pipeline welding** | Spiral-pipe mills (used inside and out) |
| **Heavy structural** | Bridge girders, crane booms, offshore platforms |
| **Locomotive / rolling stock** | Bogie frames, chassis, axle housings |
| **Wind tower fabrication** | Tapered tubular sections, flange welds |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Heat Input Formula in SAW

The **heat input (H)** is the total thermal energy delivered to the workpiece per unit length of weld. It is the most fundamental quantity in welding metallurgy because it directly controls:
- Weld bead size (penetration, width, reinforcement)
- Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ) microstructure
- Residual stresses and distortion
- Mechanical properties (toughness, hardness)

#### Derivation Start

The electrical power supplied by the welding source is:

$$P_{elec} = V \times I \quad \text{(watts, W)}$$

where $V$ = arc voltage (V) and $I$ = welding current (A).

The energy supplied per unit time is $P_{elec}$. As the weld progresses along the joint at travel speed $S$, the time taken to traverse 1 mm of weld length is:

$$t = \frac{1 \text{ mm}}{S \text{ mm/min}} = \frac{1}{S} \text{ minutes} = \frac{60}{S} \text{ seconds}$$

Therefore, the **total energy delivered per 1 mm of weld length** is:

$$E_{gross} = P_{elec} \times t = V \times I \times \frac{60}{S} \quad \text{(W·s/mm)} = V \times I \times \frac{60}{S} \quad \text{(J/mm)}$$

Note that $1 \text{ W·s} = 1 \text{ J}$. To express in **kilojoules per mm (kJ/mm)**, divide by 1,000:

$$E_{gross} = \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000} \quad \text{(kJ/mm)}$$

Not all of this gross energy reaches the workpiece — a fraction is lost as **radiation, spatter, convection, and flux heating**. The fraction absorbed is captured by the **arc efficiency $\eta$**:

$$H = \eta \times \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000} \quad \text{kJ/mm}$$

For SAW, $\eta \approx 0.95$ to $1.00$. In most KTU problems, $\eta = 1.0$ is assumed unless stated otherwise. This completes the derivation.

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — KTU-Style Heat Input Calculation

**Problem:** In a SAW operation, the arc voltage is **32 V**, welding current is **800 A**, and the travel speed is **40 cm/min**. Calculate the heat input in (a) kJ/mm and (b) kJ/cm. Take $\eta = 0.95$.

**Solution — Part (a): kJ/mm**

Given: $V = 32$ V, $I = 800$ A, $S = 40$ cm/min $= 400$ mm/min, $\eta = 0.95$.

$$H = \eta \times \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000}$$

$$H = 0.95 \times \frac{32 \times 800 \times 60}{400 \times 1000}$$

$$H = 0.95 \times \frac{1{,}536{,}000}{400{,}000}$$

$$H = 0.95 \times 3.84 = 3.648 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

**Solution — Part (b): kJ/cm**

When $S$ is in cm/min, the formula becomes:

$$H = \eta \times \frac{V \times I}{S \times 10}$$

$$H = 0.95 \times \frac{32 \times 800}{40 \times 10} = 0.95 \times \frac{25{,}600}{400} = 0.95 \times 64 = 60.8 \text{ kJ/cm}$$

**Verification by unit consistency:** $3.648$ kJ/mm $\times 10$ mm/cm $= 36.48$ kJ/cm. 
Wait — this does not match! Let me recheck the conversion.

> **Cross-check:** $1$ kJ/mm $= 10$ kJ/cm. So $3.648$ kJ/mm $= 36.48$ kJ/cm.

Re-examining Part (b): The kJ/cm formula derived by dividing numerator and denominator of the kJ/mm formula by 10:

$$H_{kJ/cm} = \eta \times \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 10{,}000} = \eta \times \frac{V \times I}{S \times 166.67}$$

But the standard textbook form $H = \eta V I / (S \times 10)$ is only valid when $S$ is in cm/min and the answer is desired in **J/cm** (not kJ/cm). Let me re-derive carefully.

Energy per unit length in J/cm:

$$E = V \times I \times \frac{60}{S_{cm/min}} \quad \text{(J/cm)} = \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \text{ (cm/min)}} \text{ J/cm}$$

So in kJ/cm:

$$H = \eta \times \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000} \quad \text{kJ/cm}$$

For $S = 40$ cm/min:

$$H = 0.95 \times \frac{32 \times 800 \times 60}{40 \times 1000} = 0.95 \times \frac{1{,}536{,}000}{40{,}000} = 0.95 \times 38.4 = 36.48 \text{ kJ/cm}$$

This **matches** the kJ/mm answer multiplied by 10. The student's textbook formula $H = \eta V I / (S \times 10)$ actually gives J/cm, not kJ/cm — a common KTU confusion point. **Final answer: 36.48 kJ/cm.**

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Deposition Rate from Wire Feed

**Problem:** A SAW machine feeds a **3.2 mm diameter** mild-steel electrode wire at **5 m/min**. The metal transfer efficiency is **95%**. Calculate the deposition rate in **kg/hour**. (Density of steel $\rho = 7.85 \times 10^3$ kg/m³.)

**Solution:**

Cross-sectional area of wire:

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (3.2 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 1.024 \times 10^{-5}}{4} = 8.042 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$$

Volume of wire fed per minute:

$$V_{min} = A \times v_f = 8.042 \times 10^{-6} \times 5 = 4.021 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$$

Mass per minute (gross):

$$m_{min,gross} = V_{min} \times \rho = 4.021 \times 10^{-5} \times 7.85 \times 10^3 = 0.3157 \text{ kg/min}$$

Mass per hour (gross):

$$m_{hr,gross} = 0.3157 \times 60 = 18.94 \text{ kg/h}$$

Net deposition rate (after efficiency loss):

$$D = m_{hr,gross} \times \eta_m = 18.94 \times 0.95 = 17.99 \text{ kg/h} \approx 18 \text{ kg/h}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Process Variables

| Parameter Increased | Heat Input H | Penetration P | Bead Width W | HAZ Size | Distortion | Risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Voltage (V)** | ↑↑ | ↑ | ↑↑ | ↑ | ↑ | Undercut, concavity |
| **Current (I)** | ↑↑ | ↑↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | Spatter, burn-through |
| **Travel Speed (S)** | ↓↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | Lack of fusion, porosity |
| **Stickout (e)** | Slight ↑ | ↓ | ↑ | — | — | Stubbing if too long |
| **Flux depth** | No effect | No effect | No effect | No effect | No effect | Insufficient if <25 mm |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a SAW System

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below is a **functional block diagram** of a Submerged Arc Welding workstation. It is rendered using Mermaid `flowchart` syntax with clean uppercase alphanumeric node labels (no markdown formatting inside labels, per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguards).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PS[CONSTANT VOLTAGE DC POWER SOURCE]
    WF[WIRE FEED MOTOR WITH SERVO CONTROL]
    FH[FLUX HOPPER WITH GRAVITY FEED]
    EL[ELECTRODE WIRE COPPER COATED MILD STEEL]
    WH[WELDING HEAD AND CONTACT TUBE]
    WFP[WORKPIECE ON COPPER BACKING OR FLUX BED]
    ARC[ARC ZONE BURIED UNDER FLUX BLANKET]
    WP[MOLTEN WELD POOL]
    SLG[FLUID SLAG CRUST FORMING ON TOP]
    SWB[SOLIDIFIED WELD BEAD WITH HAZ]
    TZ[TRAVERSE MECHANISM TRACTOR OR BOOM COLUMN]
    CS[COOLING STATION AIR OR WATER SPRAY]

    PS --> WH
    WF --> WH
    FH --> WH
    WH --> EL
    EL --> WFP
    WFP --> ARC
    ARC --> WP
    WP --> SWB
    WP --> SLG
    SWB --> CS
    TZ --> WFP
    CS --> SLG
```

### 4.2 Process Flow of a Single SAW Pass

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[START FLUX FLOW] --> B[STRIKE ARC ON RUN ON TAB]
    B --> C[SUBMERGE ARC UNDER FLUX MOUND]
    C --> D[MELT ELECTRODE AND BASE METAL]
    D --> E[DEOXIDIZE AND ALLOY WELD METAL]
    E --> F[SOLIDIFY WELD POOL]
    F --> G[CRUST SLAG ON TOP]
    G --> H[TRAVERSE TO NEXT BEAD LENGTH]
    H --> I{REACHED RUN OFF TAB}
    I --> NO --> C
    I --> YES --> J[EXTINGUISH ARC AND CRACK SLAG]
    J --> K[INSPECT WELD BY VISUAL AND NDT]
```

### 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic of the Arc Zone (Annotated)

The arc zone in SAW is a three-layer sandwich, from top to bottom:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[UNMELTED FLUX GRANULES 25 TO 50 MM]
    L2[MOLTEN FLUX ENVELOPE CONDUCTS ARC 2 TO 5 MM]
    L3[ARC PLASMA COLUMN 1800 TO 2400 DEG C]
    L4[MOLTEN WELD POOL 1700 TO 2000 DEG C]
    L5[PARTIALLY MELTED ZONE PMZ]
    L6[HEAT AFFECTED ZONE HAZ]
    L7[UNAFFECTED BASE METAL]

    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 --> L6 --> L7
```

### 4.4 Equipment Layout in Industrial SAW

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FRONT[FRONT VIEW OF SAW STATION]
        T1[FLUX RECOVERY HOPPER]
        T2[FLUX SUPPLY HOSE]
        T3[WIRE SPOOL HOLDER]
        T4[SAW TORCH ON CARRIAGE]
        T5[WORKPIECE LONGITUDINAL SEAM]
        T6[COPPER BACKING STRIP]
        T7[RUN ON AND RUN OFF TABS]
        T8[SLAG COLLECTION TRAY]
    end

    T1 --> T2 --> T4
    T3 --> T4
    T4 --> T5
    T5 --> T6
    T5 --> T7
    T5 --> T8
```

### 4.5 Variants of SAW (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SAW[SAW FAMILY]
    SAW --> SW[1 SINGLE WIRE SINGLE ARC]
    SAW --> TW[2 TWIN WIRE TANDEM ARC]
    SAW --> MW[3 MULTI WIRE UP TO 5 WIRES]
    SAW --> CS[4 COMBINATION SAW STT OR HOT WIRE]
    SAW --> NA[5 NARROW GAP SAW]
    SAW --> EW[6 ELECTROSLAG WELDING ESW VARIANT]

    SW --> SW1[UP TO 25 KG PER HR]
    TW --> TW1[UP TO 50 KG PER HR]
    MW --> MW1[UP TO 80 KG PER HR]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**

**Q1.** List **any three distinguishing features** of Submerged Arc Welding that differentiate it from Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW). **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Arc is invisible** in SAW because the granular flux completely buries the arc, eliminating arc-flash hazard; in SMAW, the arc is exposed and the welder must use a dark face shield.
2. **No external shielding gas** is required in SAW — the flux itself decomposes to provide both gas and slag shielding, whereas SMAW relies on flux coating decomposition only.
3. SAW has a **much higher deposition rate (up to 25 kg/h)** compared to SMAW (~3 kg/h) because of higher current capacity (up to 2,000 A) and continuous wire feed versus consumable stick electrodes.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**

**Q2.** What is the role of **granular flux** in Submerged Arc Welding? State any **two functions** of the slag formed on top of the weld bead. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
**Role of Granular Flux:**
The flux (1) shields the arc and molten pool from atmospheric contamination, (2) deoxidizes the weld metal by transferring Mn and Si, (3) forms a protective slag crust, and (4) can add alloying elements (Cr, Ni, Mo) when active fluxes are used.

**Functions of the Slag:**
1. The slag **protects the solidifying weld bead from atmospheric oxidation** by physically blanketing the hot metal until it cools below the oxidation threshold.
2. The slag **shields the cooling weld from rapid temperature fluctuations**, slowing the cooling rate and improving bead shape and mechanical properties.

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **INSTRUCTIONS:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

#### ✅ QUESTION A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat **functional block diagram**, explain the **working principle of Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)**. List **four advantages** of SAW over conventional arc welding processes. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle:**
SAW is an arc welding process in which coalescence is produced by heating with an electric arc between a continuously fed **bare, consumable electrode wire** and the **workpiece**. The arc and the molten weld pool are **completely submerged** under a thick blanket (25–50 mm) of **granular fusible flux** that is gravity-fed from a hopper. The flux melts at the arc to form a protective envelope and a slag cover. A **constant-voltage (CV) DC power source** maintains voltage, while a **servo-driven wire feeder** maintains arc length by varying current. The molten pool solidifies into a sound weld as the carriage traverses the joint; the slag is chipped off after cooling.

**Reference Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 above for the canonical Mermaid block diagram of the SAW system.)

**Four Advantages of SAW over Conventional Arc Welding:**
1. **Very high deposition rate** (up to 25 kg/h single wire; up to 80 kg/h multi-wire) — 5–10× faster than SMAW.
2. **Excellent weld quality** — minimal porosity, no spatter, no arc flash, low fume emission.
3. **High current capacity (up to 2,000 A)** enables single-pass welding of thick plates (up to 25 mm) without edge preparation.
4. **Mechanized/automated** — suitable for long, straight seams (e.g., ship hulls, pressure vessels) with consistent quality and minimal operator skill requirement.

---

**(b)** A SAW operation uses **arc voltage = 30 V**, **current = 900 A**, and **travel speed = 50 cm/min**. The **arc efficiency is 0.95**. Calculate the **heat input per unit length in (i) kJ/mm and (ii) kJ/cm**. If the operator increases the travel speed to **70 cm/min** while keeping V and I constant, by what **percentage** does the heat input change? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $V = 30$ V, $I = 900$ A, $S_1 = 50$ cm/min $= 500$ mm/min, $S_2 = 70$ cm/min $= 700$ mm/min, $\eta = 0.95$.

**(i) Heat input in kJ/mm at $S_1 = 500$ mm/min:**

$$H_1 = \eta \times \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S_1 \times 1000} = 0.95 \times \frac{30 \times 900 \times 60}{500 \times 1000}$$

$$H_1 = 0.95 \times \frac{1{,}620{,}000}{500{,}000} = 0.95 \times 3.24 = 3.078 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

**(ii) Heat input in kJ/cm at $S_1 = 50$ cm/min:**

$$H_1 = \eta \times \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S_1 \times 1000} = 0.95 \times \frac{30 \times 900 \times 60}{50 \times 1000}$$

$$H_1 = 0.95 \times \frac{1{,}620{,}000}{50{,}000} = 0.95 \times 32.4 = 30.78 \text{ kJ/cm}$$

**Cross-check:** $3.078$ kJ/mm $\times 10 = 30.78$ kJ/cm. ✓ **Verified.**

**Heat input at $S_2 = 700$ mm/min:**

$$H_2 = 0.95 \times \frac{30 \times 900 \times 60}{700 \times 1000} = 0.95 \times \frac{1{,}620{,}000}{700{,}000} = 0.95 \times 2.314 = 2.199 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

**Percentage change in heat input:**

$$\Delta H\% = \frac{H_2 - H_1}{H_1} \times 100 = \frac{2.199 - 3.078}{3.078} \times 100 = \frac{-0.879}{3.078} \times 100 = -28.56\%$$

**The heat input decreases by approximately 28.56 %** when travel speed is increased from 50 to 70 cm/min.

---

#### ✅ QUESTION B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Compare **Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)** with **Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW/MIG)** under the following heads: **(i) Shielding mechanism, (ii) Typical current range, (iii) Deposition rate, (iv) Slag formation, (v) Operator skill required, (vi) Outdoor usability, (vii) Typical applications.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **SAW (Submerged Arc Welding)** | **GMAW / MIG** |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Shielding mechanism** | Granular flux blanket + flux decomposition gases | External shielding gas (Ar, CO₂, or Ar/CO₂ mix) |
| **(ii) Typical current range** | 200 – 2,000 A (very high) | 50 – 500 A (moderate) |
| **(iii) Deposition rate** | Very high (up to 25 kg/h single-wire; up to 80 kg/h multi-wire) | Moderate (3 – 8 kg/h) |
| **(iv) Slag formation** | Yes — solid slag crust must be chipped off | None (spatter may form, but no slag) |
| **(v) Operator skill** | Low (mechanized) | Moderate to high (manual short-circuit transfer is tricky) |
| **(vi) Outdoor usability** | **Excellent** — flux is self-shielding, wind cannot blow it away | **Poor** — external shielding gas is dispersed by wind |
| **(vii) Typical applications** | Thick-plate welding: ship hulls, pressure vessels, pipelines, bridges, locomotive frames | Thin to medium plates: automotive bodies, sheet metal, structural fabrication |

---

**(b)** Calculate the **deposition rate (kg/h)** for a SAW process using a **4.0 mm diameter** mild-steel wire fed at **8 m/min**. Take **metal transfer efficiency = 0.96** and **density of steel = 7.85 × 10³ kg/m³**. If the **arc efficiency is 0.95** and the welding parameters are $V = 32$ V, $I = 1{,}000$ A, $S = 40$ cm/min, also calculate the **heat input in kJ/mm** and comment on whether the heat input is **within the AWS-recommended range** for the plate thickness you would typically use with these parameters. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $d = 4.0$ mm $= 4.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $v_f = 8$ m/min, $\eta_m = 0.96$, $\rho = 7.85 \times 10^3$ kg/m³, $V = 32$ V, $I = 1{,}000$ A, $S = 400$ mm/min, $\eta = 0.95$.

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of wire:**

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (4.0 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 1.6 \times 10^{-5}}{4} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Volume of wire fed per minute:**

$$V_{min} = A \times v_f = 1.2566 \times 10^{-5} \times 8 = 1.0053 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$$

**Step 3 — Gross mass per minute:**

$$m_{min,gross} = V_{min} \times \rho = 1.0053 \times 10^{-4} \times 7.85 \times 10^3 = 0.7892 \text{ kg/min}$$

**Step 4 — Net deposition rate (kg/h):**

$$D = m_{min,gross} \times \eta_m \times 60 = 0.7892 \times 0.96 \times 60 = 45.46 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 5 — Heat input in kJ/mm:**

$$H = \eta \times \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000} = 0.95 \times \frac{32 \times 1000 \times 60}{400 \times 1000}$$

$$H = 0.95 \times \frac{1{,}920{,}000}{400{,}000} = 0.95 \times 4.8 = 4.56 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

**Step 6 — Comment on AWS Recommended Range:**

For a single-pass SAW on a **20–25 mm thick** carbon-steel plate (typical for these parameters), the AWS D1.1 recommended heat input range is **2.0 – 4.0 kJ/mm** for standard structural welds. A heat input of **4.56 kJ/mm** is **slightly above the upper limit** and may lead to:
- Coarse grain HAZ
- Reduced toughness in the heat-affected zone
- Possible softening in the weld metal

**Recommendation:** Reduce either $V$, $I$, or increase $S$ to bring the heat input into the **2.0–4.0 kJ/mm** window. For example, increasing travel speed to **S = 45 cm/min (450 mm/min)** would yield:

$$H = 0.95 \times \frac{32 \times 1000 \times 60}{450 \times 1000} = 0.95 \times 4.267 = 4.05 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

This is much closer to the acceptable AWS range.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in SAW Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in Heat Input Formula:** The single biggest error. If travel speed $S$ is given in **cm/min**, use $H = \eta V I \times 60 / (S \times 1000)$ to get **kJ/cm**. If $S$ is in **mm/min**, the same formula gives **kJ/mm**. Never mix units mid-calculation. The conversion $1$ kJ/mm $= 10$ kJ/cm is a frequent cross-check.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting Arc Efficiency $\eta$:** SAW has $\eta \approx 0.95$ – $1.0$, but if the examiner specifies it as 0.85 (mimicking stick welding), using $\eta = 1.0$ will give the wrong answer. **Always read the problem carefully.**
> 
> 3. **Confusing Deposition Rate Formulas:** The two common formulas are (i) volume × density × efficiency (for kg/h) and (ii) the simple linear $D = kI$ empirical form. KTU expects the **physical (volume-based) derivation** unless the problem explicitly says "use the empirical formula."
> 
> 4. **Forgetting Slag Removal Step in Process Description:** When asked to "explain the SAW process," most students forget to mention the **slag chipping step**. This is a frequently tested 1-mark point.
> 
> 5. **Calling SAW a Gas-Shielded Process:** Many students wrongly say "SAW uses an inert gas shield." It does **NOT** — SAW is **flux-shielded**, with the flux itself generating shielding gases on decomposition. This is worth **1 mark** if missed.
> 
> 6. **Confusing Penetration vs Bead Width Effects:** Higher current → more penetration; higher voltage → wider bead. Reversing these is a classic 1-mark penalty in "compare" questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for SAW — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ✅ SAW = **Submerged Arc Welding**; arc is **invisible**, buried under **granular flux**.
- ✅ Power source: **Constant Voltage (CV) DC+** preferred; AC for special cases.
- ✅ Electrode: **bare, copper-coated mild-steel wire**; not flux-cored (that's FCAW).
- ✅ **No external shielding gas** required — flux is the shield.
- ✅ **Four independent process variables:** Voltage (V), Current (I), Travel Speed (S), Electrode Extension (e).
- ✅ **Heat Input formula:** $H = \eta \times V I \times 60 / (S \times 1000)$ kJ/mm; **remember the unit of S**.
- ✅ **Arc efficiency $\eta$ for SAW = 0.95 – 1.00** — the highest among all arc processes.
- ✅ **Deposition rate = up to 25 kg/h (single wire)**, up to **80 kg/h (multi-wire)**.
- ✅ **Single-pass capability up to 25 mm plate** — no edge beveling needed.
- ✅ Flux does **4 jobs:** shield arc, deoxidize weld, form slag, add alloying elements.
- ✅ Slag must be **chipped off manually** after each pass.
- ✅ **Run-on and run-off tabs** are mandatory (since the arc is invisible, you cannot strike on the actual workpiece).
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** (1) Limited to **horizontal/flat positions** (flux falls off if vertical/overhead), (2) Requires **post-weld slag removal**, (3) High **heat input** may cause **coarse-grain HAZ** in thick sections.
- ✅ **Key applications:** Shipbuilding, pressure vessels, pipelines, bridges, wind towers, locomotive frames, spiral pipe mills.
- ✅ **Variants:** Single-wire, Twin-wire (tandem), Multi-wire, Narrow-gap SAW, Hot-wire SAW, Electroslag welding (ESW — related high-deposition process).
- ✅ **Memorize the comparison table** with SMAW and GMAW — these questions appear every KTU exam cycle.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ultrasonic Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT404 - MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ultrasonic Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ultrasonic Welding (USW)** is a *solid-state welding process* in which two workpieces are joined by the application of **high-frequency vibratory energy (typically 20 kHz to 70 kHz)** under a static compressive force, without the use of external filler metal, flux, or significant macroscopic melting of the base materials. The localized heat required for bonding is generated *internally* at the faying interface through a combination of **surface friction, plastic deformation, and viscoelastic/plastic shear flow**.

According to the **KTU 2024 Manufacturing Process syllabus (PBMRT404)**, ultrasonic welding is classified under **Non-Conventional Welding Processes** and is a high-priority topic in **Module 2**, which deals with advanced joining technologies.

### Classification Snapshot (as per KTU taxonomy)

| Feature | Specification |
|---|---|
| Process Family | Solid-State Welding |
| Energy Type | Mechanical (Vibratory) |
| Heat Source | Internal frictional/dissipative |
| Frequency Band | Ultrasonic (> 20 kHz) |
| Typical Weld Temperature | 30% to 50% of base metal melting point |
| Filler Material | None required |
| Shielding Gas | Not required |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Jiggling Palm" Analogy**
>
> Imagine pressing two sheets of paper together flat on a table and rapidly *shaking* (jiggling) the top sheet back and forth sideways with your palm, while pressing down firmly. Within a few seconds, the interface between the papers gets warm, the surfaces deform microscopically, and the two sheets stick together as if glued. **That is essentially ultrasonic welding.**
>
> Replace the palm with a precisely tuned titanium horn vibrating at **20,000 to 40,000 cycles per second** (far too fast to see, just beyond human hearing — hence *ultra*-sonic), and replace the papers with metal foils or plastic films, and you have industrial ultrasonic welding.

### Why "Ultrasonic" and not just "Vibration Welding"?

| Parameter | Ultrasonic Welding | Vibration Welding |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency | **20–70 kHz** | 100–240 Hz |
| Amplitude | 10–50 µm (microscopic) | 1–3 mm (visible) |
| Weld area | Small, localized (spot/linear) | Large seam |
| Energy carrier | Acoustic wave in horn | Bulk mechanical shake |

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard operating frequency:** 20 kHz, 35 kHz, 40 kHz, 70 kHz (most common: **20 kHz** and **35 kHz**).
- **Vibration amplitude at horn tip:** 10 µm to 50 µm.
- **Static clamping pressure:** 0.2 MPa to 1.5 MPa.
- **Weld time:** 0.1 s to 3 s.
- **Power consumption:** 50 W to 3000 W.
- **Weld zone temperature rise:** Up to **50% of absolute melting point (Tm)** in localized micro-regions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold Frequency of Human Hearing = 20 kHz.** Ultrasonic welding operates ABOVE this threshold, hence the name. The acoustic energy is concentrated in a very small region, giving high power density (often **> 10⁶ W/cm²** at the weld interface).

---

## 1.4 KTU Module 2 Context Map

> [!NOTE]
> **Where USW fits in Module 2 of PBMRT404:**
>
> Module 2 typically covers: *Resistance Welding, Arc Welding variants, Solid-State Welding (Friction Stir, Ultrasonic, Explosive, Diffusion), Electron Beam, Laser Beam, and Plasma Arc Welding.* Ultrasonic Welding comes under the **Solid-State Welding** sub-cluster and is often paired with Friction Welding for comparison questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The Four-Stage Mechanism

The joining action in ultrasonic welding is best understood as a **four-stage temporal sequence**. Examiners frequently ask for a description of this sequence.

### Stage 1 — Initial Contact & Asperity Engagement
- The two workpieces are pressed together by the static *clamping force* of the anvil.
- The mating surfaces are microscopically rough (asperities of 1–10 µm peak-to-valley).
- True contact area is only **~5–10%** of the apparent contact area.

### Stage 2 — Acoustic Softening & Viscoelastic Heating
- The horn transmits **transverse shear vibrations** parallel to the weld interface.
- The asperities experience **interfacial friction** at velocity $v = A \cdot 2\pi f$, where $A$ = amplitude, $f$ = frequency.
- Heat is generated **internally** by:
  1. Plastic deformation of asperities.
  2. Viscoelastic hysteresis (in polymers/metals near softening point).
  3. Friction at micro-contact points.
- Material near the interface **softens** (acoustic softening effect), reducing flow stress.

### Stage 3 — Plastic/Shear Flow & Inter-Mixing
- Softened material flows laterally, breaking oxide layers and contaminants.
- Clean metal-to-metal (or polymer-to-polymer) contact is established.
- Grain boundaries interpenetrate — this is the **bonding** step.

### Stage 4 — Cooling Under Pressure
- Vibration is switched OFF; clamping force is held briefly.
- Joint cools under pressure; atomic/molecular bonds form the final weld.
- The horn retracts; the cycle ends.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** The interface never reaches the bulk melting point in true ultrasonic welding of metals. It is a *solid-state* bond, which means the original metallurgical properties of the base metal are largely preserved.

## 2.2 The Acoustic Stack (Heart of the USW System)

The acoustic assembly converts **electrical energy → mechanical vibratory energy** in three precise stages:

| Component | Function | Typical Material |
|---|---|---|
| **Transducer** (Converter) | Converts high-frequency electrical signal into mechanical vibration via *piezoelectric effect* (Lead Zirconate Titanate — PZT crystals) | PZT ceramic |
| **Booster** (Velocity Transformer/Amplifier) | Amplifies or attenuates the vibration amplitude | Titanium alloy, Monel, Aluminium |
| **Horn** (Sonotrode) | Final amplitude tuning + delivers vibration to the workpiece | Tool steel, Titanium, Hardened steel |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why titanium for the horn?** Titanium has an excellent combination of high fatigue strength, low acoustic loss, and moderate density — critical for sustaining millions of cycles per minute without cracking. Aluminium is used for low-power polymer welding.

## 2.3 Process Parameters — The "Five Levers" of USW

A KTU examiner may ask: *List and explain the process parameters affecting weld quality.*

1. **Vibration Frequency (f)** — Hz; affects depth of penetration. Higher f = shallower weld.
2. **Vibration Amplitude (A)** — µm; primary driver of interfacial shear. Higher A = more heat.
3. **Clamping Pressure (P)** — MPa; ensures intimate contact. Too high → cracks; too low → weak bond.
4. **Weld Time (t)** — seconds; cumulative energy input.
5. **Power Input (W)** — Watts; electrical drive to the transducer.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!WARNING]
> **Memorize the equations below. They are tested almost every KTU ESE cycle for this module.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Peak velocity of horn tip** | $v_{peak} = 2 \pi f A$ | m/s | $A$ = amplitude, $f$ = frequency |
| 2 | **Acoustic power delivered to interface** | $P = F \cdot v_{peak} = 2 \pi f A F$ | W | $F$ = clamping force |
| 3 | **Total energy input per weld** | $E = P \cdot t = 2 \pi f A F t$ | Joules | $t$ = weld time |
| 4 | **Power density at interface** | $\rho_{p} = \dfrac{P}{A_{contact}}$ | W/cm² | $A_{contact}$ = weld spot area |
| 5 | **Weld time (energy-based)** | $t = \dfrac{E}{2 \pi f A F}$ | s | Rearranged form of Eq. 3 |
| 6 | **Force from horn pressure** | $F = P_{clamp} \cdot A_{horn}$ | N | $P_{clamp}$ = clamping pressure |
| 7 | **Heat generated per unit volume** | $q = \tau \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | W/m³ | $\tau$ = shear stress, $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate |
| 8 | **Acoustic wavelength in horn** | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | m | $c$ = sound velocity in horn material |
| 9 | **Horn length (half-wavelength)** | $L = \dfrac{\lambda}{2} = \dfrac{c}{2f}$ | m | Resonant length for longitudinal mode |

> [!CAUTION]
> In markdown tables, never use the pipe `|` symbol inside a cell (e.g., do not write $|x|$). Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead, as shown in formulas above.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive industry:** Wire harness splicing, battery tab welding in EVs, sensor sealing.
- **Electronics:** PCB component lead soldering alternative, microchip packaging, flex circuit joining.
- **Medical devices:** Joining thin stainless-steel foils for catheter balloons and implants.
- **Packaging industry:** Hermetic sealing of toothpaste tubes, blister packs (over **80%** of plastic packaging uses USW).
- **Aerospace:** Joining of aluminium honeycomb to skins in non-structural applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why do manufacturers love USW for EV batteries?**
> 1. It produces **no sparks, no fumes, no heat-affected zone** in the bulk — safe near lithium chemistries.
> 2. Cycle time is **< 1 second per joint** — perfect for high-volume production.
> 3. It joins **dissimilar metals** (Cu-Al, Al-Ni) that are hard to weld by conventional fusion processes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Peak Velocity of the Horn Tip (Worked Analytical Proof)

**Given:** The horn tip executes simple harmonic motion (SHM) along the weld interface.
- Frequency: $f$ (Hz)
- Amplitude: $A$ (m)

**To Prove:** $v_{peak} = 2 \pi f A$

**Derivation (Step-by-Step):**

Step 1 — Equation of displacement in SHM:

$$
x(t) = A \sin(2 \pi f t)
$$

This is the standard textbook form where $A$ is the peak displacement and $2\pi f = \omega$ (angular frequency in rad/s).

Step 2 — Velocity is the time-derivative of displacement:

$$
v(t) = \dfrac{dx}{dt} = A \cdot (2 \pi f) \cos(2 \pi f t)
$$

Differentiating $\sin(\cdot)$ brings out the angular frequency multiplier.

Step 3 — The maximum (peak) velocity occurs when $\cos(2\pi f t) = 1$:

$$
v_{peak} = A \cdot (2 \pi f) \cdot 1 = 2 \pi f A
$$

**Therefore:**

$$
\boxed{v_{peak} = 2 \pi f A}
$$

**Unit Check:** $f$ is in s⁻¹, $A$ is in m, so $v_{peak}$ comes out in m·s⁻¹. ✅

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example (KTU-style):** If $f = 20,000$ Hz and $A = 30 \, \mu m = 30 \times 10^{-6}$ m, then $v_{peak} = 2 \pi \cdot 20000 \cdot 30 \times 10^{-6} \approx 3.77$ m/s. Even a tiny 30-micron amplitude generates nearly **4 m/s of rubbing velocity** — that's how the heat is built up so quickly.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Acoustic Power Transmitted Across the Weld Interface

**Given:**
- Clamping force normal to the interface: $F$ (N)
- Relative sliding velocity at interface: $v_{peak}$ (m/s)
- The horn drags the top workpiece sideways against the bottom (held by anvil).

**Assumption:** The friction force at the interface $F_f = \mu F$ (Coulomb friction model). For visco-elastically softened contact, $\mu$ is replaced by an effective shear coefficient $\eta$.

**Derivation:**

Step 1 — Instantaneous power dissipated by friction:

$$
P(t) = F_f \cdot v(t) = \mu F \cdot v(t)
$$

Power = Force × Velocity (general physics definition).

Step 2 — Substitute the velocity expression $v(t) = 2\pi f A \cos(2\pi f t)$:

$$
P(t) = \mu F \cdot 2\pi f A \cos(2\pi f t)
$$

Step 3 — Average power over one full cycle (the cosine averages to zero over a symmetric cycle, so the *mean* is taken as the RMS, $\frac{1}{2}$ of the peak-to-peak):

$$
P_{avg} = \mu F \cdot 2 \pi f A \cdot \overline{\vert \cos(2\pi f t) \vert} = \mu F \cdot 2 \pi f A \cdot \dfrac{2}{\pi}
$$

Step 4 — Simplifying the cosine average:

$$
P_{avg} = \mu F \cdot 2 \pi f A \cdot \dfrac{2}{\pi} = 4 \mu f A F
$$

Step 5 — A simpler engineering approximation (used in KTU textbooks) drops the rectification factor and writes:

$$
\boxed{P = 2 \pi f A F}
$$

This treats the power as $F \times v_{peak}$, a standard KTU treatment.

**Unit Check:** $f$ in s⁻¹, $A$ in m, $F$ in N → m·N/s = W (Joules/s). ✅

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When the problem gives $f$ in kHz, **convert to Hz first** before substituting. A common mistake costs students 1–2 marks.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Weld Energy and Required Hold Time

**Given:** Required bonding energy $E$ (J), frequency $f$, amplitude $A$, clamping force $F$.

**Derivation:**

Step 1 — Total weld energy = Power × Time:

$$
E = P \cdot t_w
$$

Step 2 — Substitute $P = 2 \pi f A F$:

$$
E = (2 \pi f A F) \cdot t_w
$$

Step 3 — Solve for the required weld time:

$$
t_w = \dfrac{E}{2 \pi f A F}
$$

**Therefore:**

$$
\boxed{t_w = \dfrac{E}{2 \pi f A F}}
$$

**Numerical Example:** 
- Energy required: $E = 50$ J
- $f = 20$ kHz = 20,000 Hz
- $A = 25 \, \mu m = 25 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $F = 500$ N

Substitute:

$$
t_w = \dfrac{50}{2 \pi \cdot 20000 \cdot 25 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 500}
$$

$$
t_w = \dfrac{50}{2 \pi \cdot 20000 \cdot 25 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 500}
$$

Compute the denominator:

$$
2 \pi \cdot 20000 = 125663.7
$$

$$
125663.7 \cdot 25 \times 10^{-6} = 3.1416
$$

$$
3.1416 \cdot 500 = 1570.8
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{t_w = \dfrac{50}{1570.8} \approx 0.0318 \text{ s} \approx 32 \text{ ms}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why ultrasonic welding is so fast — typical weld times are **0.05 to 0.5 seconds**, an order of magnitude faster than resistance spot welding.

---

## 3.4 KTU Analytical Matrix — Process Parameter Effects

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the directional effect of each parameter on weld quality. This is a KTU favorite 7-mark question.**

| Parameter ↑ (Increase) | Effect on Heat Input | Effect on Bond Strength | Risk if Excessive |
|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency (f) | ↑ (more cycles/sec) | ↑ to a point, then ↓ | Surface over-heating, shallow weld |
| Amplitude (A) | ↑ (linear) | ↑ | Cracking, fatigue of horn |
| Clamping Pressure (F) | ↑ (more friction force) | ↑ to optimum | Flash, deformation, cracks |
| Weld Time (t) | ↑ (linear) | ↑ to optimum | Burn-through, over-aging |
| Power (W) | ↑ | ↑ | Weld thinning |

## 3.5 Comparison Matrix — USW vs Other Solid-State Processes

> [!TIP]
> **Differentiate clearly in your answer.** Examiners give full marks only when differences are tabulated.

| Feature | Ultrasonic Welding | Friction Welding | Diffusion Bonding |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy form | High-freq. vibration | Continuous rotation | Heat + pressure (no vibration) |
| Temperature | 30–50% of Tm | 80–90% of Tm | 50–70% of Tm |
| Time | < 1 s | Seconds to minutes | Minutes to hours |
| Atmosphere | Air | Air | Vacuum or inert gas |
| Surface prep | Minimal | Machined face | Mirror polish + cleaning |
| Typical use | Foils, wires, plastic films | Shafts, tubes, rods | Aerospace Ti/Al components |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow of an Ultrasonic Welding System

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below maps the **energy-conversion chain** of a USW system, from the AC mains input to the welded joint. This is a Block-Level Functional Architecture suitable for KTU 14-mark answers and engineering viva explanations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Mains Supply 230V 50Hz] --> B[High Frequency Generator 20kHz to 40kHz]
    B --> C[Piezoelectric Transducer PZT Crystals]
    C --> D[Booster Velocity Transformer Amplifier]
    D --> E[Horn Sonotrode Tool Tip]
    E --> F[Top Workpiece Aluminium Foil Sheet]
    G[Anvil Clamping Block] --> F
    F --> H[Bottom Workpiece Substrate]
    H --> G
    E --> I[Weld Interface Faying Surface]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Heat Plastic Flow Bond Formation]
    J --> K[Welded Joint Output]
    L[Programmable Logic Controller PLC] --> B
    L --> G
    M[Compressed Air Pneumatic Cylinder] --> G
    N[Coolant Circulation Block] --> C
    N --> D
    O[Linear Encoder Feedback] --> L
```

**Block-by-Block Description (for KTU answer writing):**

- **AC Mains → High Frequency Generator:** Standard 50 Hz AC is converted to 20 kHz (or 35 kHz) high-frequency AC using solid-state inverters.
- **High-Frequency Generator → Piezoelectric Transducer:** Electrical oscillations drive the PZT stack, producing **mechanical vibrations of the same frequency** (reverse piezoelectric effect).
- **Transducer → Booster:** The booster amplifies the displacement amplitude by 1.5×, 2.5×, or 3× depending on design.
- **Booster → Horn (Sonotrode):** The horn further tunes and shapes the vibrational output and couples it to the workpiece.
- **Horn → Top Workpiece → Weld Interface → Bottom Workpiece → Anvil:** The mechanical energy flows through the joint; friction and plastic flow at the interface generate the bond.
- **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller):** Controls time, pressure, and energy cut-off for process repeatability.
- **Pneumatic Cylinder → Anvil:** Provides the **static clamping force** during welding.
- **Coolant Block:** Cools the transducer and booster to prevent PZT depoling and horn fatigue.
- **Encoder → PLC:** Closed-loop feedback of horn displacement (vital for "energy-mode" or "displacement-mode" welding).

## 4.2 Horn Geometry Variants (Block-Level Mapping)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The horn (sonotrode) is the most engineered component in the USW system. Its **profile** determines how vibration is delivered. Three standard types are tested in KTU:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cylindrical Horn Constant Amplitude] --> A1[Used for small spot welds]
    B[Conical Horn Amplifier Type] --> B1[Most common in industry]
    C[Catenoïdal Horn Exponential] --> C1[For high amplitude large area welds]
    D[Stepped Horn High Gain] --> D1[Maximum amplitude boost]
```

| Horn Type | Amplification | Frequency Range | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cylindrical | 1:1 (none) | All | Small precision welds, polymer films |
| Conical | 1.5:1 to 3:1 | 20–35 kHz | **Most common** for general metal welding |
| Catenoïdal / Exponential | 2:1 to 4:1 | 20 kHz | Large-area welds (e.g., solar panel ribbon) |
| Stepped | 4:1 to 6:1 | 35–40 kHz | Wire harness, micro-electronics |

## 4.3 Modes of Ultrasonic Welding

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ultrasonic Welding Process] --> B[Direct Weld Energy Director]
    A --> C[Indirect Weld Through Transmitting Member]
    A --> D[Transmission Weld Through Top Sheet]
    B --> B1[Easiest method]
    C --> C1[Used for large flat parts]
    D --> D1[For thin to thick joints]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **For a 14-mark question, explain at least two modes.** The most common combination is *Direct Welding* (for plastics) and *Spot Welding* (for metals).

## 4.4 Process Sequence Timeline (State Machine)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Load
    Load --> Clamp
    Clamp --> Vibrate
    Vibrate --> Hold
    Hold --> Release
    Release --> [*]
    Load: Load Workpiece
    Clamp: Pneumatic Clamp Closes
    Vibrate: Horn Vibrates 0.1 to 1.0s
    Hold: Clamp Holds 0.05 to 0.2s
    Release: Clamp Opens Part Ejects
```

**Typical cycle times:**
- Load: 0.3 s
- Clamp: 0.1 s
- Vibrate (weld): 0.1–1.0 s
- Hold: 0.1 s
- Release: 0.2 s
- **Total: ~1 second per joint** — extremely high throughput.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1: Define Ultrasonic Welding. State any two of its industrial applications. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model)]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Ultrasonic Welding** is a solid-state welding process in which two workpieces are joined by applying a **static clamping force** and **high-frequency vibratory energy (typically 20–70 kHz)** to the interface. The heat required for bonding is generated *internally* by interfacial friction and plastic deformation, without the use of external heat, filler, or flux.

**Two industrial applications:**
1. **Automotive wire harness splicing** — joining copper wire terminations to aluminium or brass connectors in vehicle electrical systems.
2. **Packaging industry** — hermetic sealing of plastic tubes, blister packs, and food-grade laminate pouches (e.g., toothpaste tubes, ketchup sachets).

**Mark distribution:** 2 marks for definition, 1 mark for applications. ✅

---

### Question 2: List the main components of an ultrasonic welding system and state the function of any one. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Adapted)]**

**Model Answer:**

The main components of an ultrasonic welding system are:
1. **High-frequency generator (power supply)**
2. **Piezoelectric transducer (converter)**
3. **Booster (velocity transformer)**
4. **Horn (sonotrode)**
5. **Anvil (clamping block with pneumatic cylinder)**
6. **PLC controller and feedback sensors**

**Function of the Transducer:** It converts the high-frequency electrical signal from the generator into **mechanical vibrations of the same frequency** by exploiting the **inverse piezoelectric effect** of PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate) crystals.

**Mark distribution:** 1.5 marks for the list, 1.5 marks for the function. ✅

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** In Module-level Part B questions, you get an **internal choice** — usually one of two sub-questions. Both options are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** [Choice Option 1]

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model Paper Adapted)]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of ultrasonic welding. List the process parameters that affect weld quality. [**7 Marks**]

**(b)** Derive the expressions for **(i)** peak velocity of the horn tip and **(ii)** the acoustic power delivered to the weld interface. A USW machine operates at 20 kHz with horn amplitude 30 µm and clamping force 600 N. Calculate the power delivered. [**7 Marks**]

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Working Principle (Block diagram description):** [4 marks]

The system works on the principle of converting high-frequency electrical energy into mechanical vibratory energy at the joint interface. The conversion chain is:

1. **High-frequency generator** (AC mains → 20 kHz AC).
2. **Piezoelectric transducer** — converts 20 kHz AC into mechanical vibration at the same frequency using PZT crystals.
3. **Booster** — amplifies vibration amplitude.
4. **Horn (sonotrode)** — couples vibration to the top workpiece.
5. **Anvil** — holds the bottom workpiece under static clamping pressure.
6. The **transverse shear vibrations** parallel to the interface create friction at asperities; heat is generated *internally*; the surface layers plasticize and bond.

**Stages of Bond Formation:** [2 marks]

- **Stage 1:** Initial asperity contact under static load.
- **Stage 2:** Frictional and viscoelastic heating → material softens.
- **Stage 3:** Plastic/viscous flow → clean surface-to-surface contact.
- **Stage 4:** Cooling under pressure → metallurgical bond.

**Process Parameters:** [1 mark]

The four key parameters are:
- Vibration frequency ($f$)
- Vibration amplitude ($A$)
- Clamping pressure ($P$ or $F$)
- Weld time ($t$)

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Derivation of peak velocity:** [2 marks]

Given SHM: $x(t) = A \sin(2 \pi f t)$

Differentiating: $v(t) = 2 \pi f A \cos(2 \pi f t)$

Maximum at $\cos = 1$:

$$
\boxed{v_{peak} = 2 \pi f A}
$$

**(ii) Derivation of acoustic power:** [2 marks]

Power = Force × Velocity:

$$
P = F \cdot v_{peak} = F \cdot 2 \pi f A = 2 \pi f A F
$$

**Numerical Calculation:** [3 marks]

Given:
- $f = 20,000$ Hz
- $A = 30 \, \mu m = 30 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $F = 600$ N

Substitute:

$$
P = 2 \pi \cdot 20000 \cdot 30 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 600
$$

Step 1: $2 \pi \cdot 20000 = 125663.7$

Step 2: $125663.7 \cdot 30 \times 10^{-6} = 3.77$ (m/s, this is $v_{peak}$)

Step 3: $P = 3.77 \cdot 600 = 2261.9$ W

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{P \approx 2262 \text{ W} \approx 2.26 \text{ kW}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Mistake:** Students forget to convert kHz → Hz, leaving $f = 20$ in the formula. This gives an answer **1000× too small** and costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Micron to Meter:** Forgetting $A = 30 \times 10^{-6}$ m and using $A = 30$ m gives a preposterously high power — guaranteed zero on this sub-part.
> 3. **Diagrams:** If you don't draw a block diagram, deduct 1 mark even if the text is correct. Always include a labeled block diagram in 7-mark answers.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** [Choice Option 2]

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 (Adapted)]**

**(a)** Explain the different **modes of ultrasonic welding** (Direct, Indirect, and Transmission). State one application of each. [**7 Marks**]

**(b)** Compare ultrasonic welding with **friction welding** and **resistance spot welding** in terms of **(i)** heat source, **(ii)** temperature reached at the joint, **(iii)** typical cycle time, and **(iv)** suitable material combinations. [**7 Marks**]

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Direct (Contact) Welding:** [2.5 marks]

In this mode, the **horn contacts the top workpiece directly**, transmitting vibrations into it. The bottom workpiece rests on the anvil. Most common for spot welding of metals and small plastic parts.

> **Application:** Joining copper wire terminations in automotive wiring harnesses.

**2. Indirect (Welding Through a Transmitting Member) Welding:** [2.5 marks]

The horn does **not touch the top workpiece**; vibration is transmitted through an intermediate metal plate or "transmitting stud" that is pre-bonded to the top part. Used when the top part is large, soft, or has a complex shape.

> **Application:** Sealing lids onto cosmetic jars and food containers.

**3. Transmission (Through-Welding) Welding:** [2 marks]

The **horn contacts only one workpiece**, and vibration must travel **through the top sheet** to reach the interface. Used for joining **thin sheet to thick substrate** (e.g., solar panel ribbon to back-sheet).

> **Application:** Tab welding of lithium-ion battery cells — copper tab is the transmitting sheet, aluminium can is the substrate.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table:** [Each row 1.75 marks]

| Criterion | Ultrasonic Welding | Friction Welding | Resistance Spot Welding |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Heat Source** | Internal frictional + viscoelastic from high-frequency vibration | Continuous rotational friction at the interface | Joule heating ($I^2R$) from high current |
| **(ii) Joint Temperature** | 30%–50% of melting point (no bulk melting) | 80%–90% of melting point (below $T_m$) | Exceeds melting point locally; nugget melts and re-solidifies |
| **(iii) Cycle Time** | 0.1 – 1.0 s | 5 – 30 s | 0.1 – 0.5 s (per spot) |
| **(iv) Material Suitability** | Dissimilar metals (Cu-Al, Al-Ni), plastics, thin foils | Similar/dissimilar cylindrical parts (rods, tubes) | Sheet metal of similar grades (steel-steel, Al-Al) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Errors in this comparison:**
> 1. Saying USW *melts* the metal — this is wrong; it is a **solid-state** process.
> 2. Confusing **friction welding** (rotational) with **friction stir welding** (linear with a non-consumable tool).
> 3. Omitting **dissimilar metal capability** of USW — this is its biggest commercial advantage and earns a bonus point with the examiner.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (General for this Topic)

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students LOSE marks in Ultrasonic Welding questions:**
> 1. **Calling it "ultrasound welding"** — there is no such thing. The correct term is *ultrasonic welding*.
> 2. **Stating USW needs external heat or filler** — both are false.
> 3. **Mixing up vibration welding and ultrasonic welding** — frequency, amplitude, and weld size are entirely different.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert kHz to Hz** in numerical problems.
> 5. **Not labeling the block diagram** — even a beautiful diagram gets zero marks if the blocks are not labeled. Always label: Generator → Transducer → Booster → Horn → Workpiece.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "must remember" 15 points for the night before your exam:**

- [x] **Ultrasonic Welding (USW)** is a **solid-state** welding process — **no melting, no filler, no shielding gas**.
- [x] Operating **frequency range: 20 kHz to 70 kHz** (most common: 20 kHz, 35 kHz).
- [x] **Peak velocity of horn:** $v_{peak} = 2 \pi f A$.
- [x] **Acoustic power:** $P = 2 \pi f A F$.
- [x] **Total energy per weld:** $E = 2 \pi f A F t$.
- [x] **Acoustic stack order:** Generator → Transducer (PZT) → Booster → Horn → Workpiece.
- [x] **Transducer** uses the **inverse piezoelectric effect** of PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate) crystals.
- [x] **Horn material:** Titanium alloy (for high-power metal welding), Aluminium (for low-power plastic welding).
- [x] **Joint temperature:** Only **30–50% of the absolute melting temperature** — no bulk melting.
- [x] **Three modes of USW:** Direct, Indirect, Transmission.
- [x] **Key process parameters:** Frequency, Amplitude, Clamping pressure, Weld time.
- [x] **Typical cycle time:** **< 1 second per joint** — the fastest spot-welding process in industry.
- [x] **Best application:** **Dissimilar metal joints** (Cu-Al, Al-Ni) and **plastic packaging** (toothpaste tubes, EV battery tabs).
- [x] **Major advantage over fusion welding:** No heat-affected zone, no sparks, no fumes, no porosity.
- [x] **Disadvantage:** Limited to **thin sections** (< 3 mm); not suitable for thick structural members.
- [x] **State machine order:** Load → Clamp → Vibrate → Hold → Release.

---

> **End of Module 2 — Ultrasonic Welding Notes**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme (PBMRT404) | KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10*

topics.title: Thermit Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT404 - MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Thermit Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Thermit Welding** (also called **Thermite Welding** or **Aluminothermic Welding**) is a fusion welding process that uses an **exothermic chemical reaction** between a metal oxide and a reactive metal (usually aluminum) to produce molten metal and a very high temperature slag. The molten metal produced is used as the filler material to join two metal parts, while the heat liberated is sufficient to bring the parent metal to a forgeable/plastic state at the joint.

The classical reaction is:

$$2Al + Fe_2O_3 \rightarrow 2Fe + Al_2O_3 + Heat \;(\approx 850 \; kJ/mol)$$

The process was invented by the German chemist **Dr. Hans Goldschmidt in 1895**, and it remains one of the few welding processes capable of producing molten steel (~2500 °C) **without using any external electrical or fuel-based heat source**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PBMRT404 – Module 2):**
> Thermit welding is grouped under *"Special welding and allied joining processes"*. Examiners frequently test the chemical equation, the temperature achieved, the inventor's name, and the steps/process sequence.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you have **two huge broken railway tracks** in the middle of a remote jungle — no electricity, no gas cylinder, no welding machine. You cannot take the track to a factory because it is too heavy. What do you do?

You simply **mix a "magic powder"** in a crucible placed above the joint, light it with a small magnesium ribbon (like striking a match), and within **30 seconds**, the mixture burns so violently that it produces **white-hot liquid steel** (≈ 2500 °C) — hotter than a typical electric arc. This molten steel flows into the gap, melts the edges of the parent metal, and fuses them into a single continuous track once cooled.

That "magic powder" is the **thermite mixture (Al + Fe₂O₃)** — a self-contained, portable furnace that travels with you.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the name "Thermit"?**
> The term derives from the Greek *therme* (heat). The mixture is the *thermit* — the heat-generating agent — and the process using it is *thermit welding*.

## 1.3 Essential Process Metrics (Board-Exam Standard)

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Maximum Temperature Achieved** | **2500 °C – 3000 °C** | Higher than oxy-acetylene flame (~3200 °C for acetylene, but with less localized intensity in steel) |
| **Heat of Reaction** | **~850 kJ per mole of Fe₂O₃** | Drives the fusion |
| **Reaction Time** | **20 – 30 seconds** | Very fast — entire pour is over in < 1 minute |
| **Atmosphere Required** | **No shielding gas** | Self-protected by Al₂O₃ slag |
| **External Power** | **None** | Truly portable |
| **Typical Filler Volume** | **5 – 50 kg per joint** | Bulk quantity for large sections |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — The Underlying "Why" and "How"

The principle of thermit welding is grounded in **thermodynamics of oxide reduction**. Aluminum has a stronger affinity for oxygen than iron has. Therefore, when a mixture of finely divided aluminum powder and iron oxide (Fe₂O₃ or Fe₃O₄) is ignited, aluminum "steals" the oxygen from iron oxide, liberating the iron in a molten state and producing aluminum oxide (Al₂O₃) slag. The reaction is **highly exothermic**.

### Step-by-Step Logic of the Reaction

- **Step 1 — Mixing:** Aluminum powder and iron oxide are blended in stoichiometric ratio (Al : Fe₂O₃ ≈ 1 : 3 by mass, or 54 : 160 molar).
- **Step 2 — Ignition:** A small magnesium ribbon or a chemical "ignition paste" (BaO₂ + Mg) is lit. Activation energy is supplied (~ 1000 °C).
- **Step 3 — Exothermic Reaction:** Once triggered, the reaction is self-sustaining because the heat released is much greater than the activation energy.
- **Step 4 — Separation by Density:** Molten iron (density ≈ 7.0 g/cm³) settles at the bottom; molten Al₂O₃ slag (density ≈ 3.5 g/cm³) floats on top — natural density-driven separation.
- **Step 5 — Tapping:** The crucible's bottom plug (graphite/titanium) is opened, and molten iron is poured into the preheated mould around the joint.
- **Step 6 — Solidification & Finishing:** The metal solidifies; excess is trimmed off by grinding/oxy-cutting.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** The following equations are the *core numerical toolkit*. Practice writing them from memory — they appear every cycle.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stoichiometric mass ratio Al : Fe₂O₃ | $m_{Al} / m_{Fe_2O_3} = 2(27) / 160 = 0.3375$ | dimensionless | Use for mixture preparation |
| 2 | Moles of Fe produced per mole Fe₂O₃ | $n_{Fe} = 2$ | mol | From reaction stoichiometry |
| 3 | Mass of Fe produced per kg Fe₂O₃ | $m_{Fe} = (2 \times 56) / 160 = 0.70 \; kg$ | kg | Useful for filler estimation |
| 4 | Heat released per kg of thermite | $Q = 850 \times 10^3 / 0.160 = 5.31 \; MJ/kg$ | J/kg | Approximate; varies with purity |
| 5 | Heat balance (sensible heating) | $Q_{released} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | J | For temperature rise calculations |
| 6 | Temperature reached (adiabatic) | $T_{max} = T_{ambient} + Q / (m \cdot c_p)$ | K or °C | Adiabatic flame temperature |
| 7 | Volume of molten metal | $V = m / \rho$ | m³ or cm³ | $\rho_{Fe} \approx 7.0 \; g/cm^3$ |
| 8 | Approximate adiabatic flame temperature | $T_{ad} \approx 2500 \; \text{°C}$ | °C | Empirical reference value |

> [!NOTE]
> In all KTU numerical problems, the reaction heat is usually **given** in the question. Do not memorize 850 kJ/mol blindly — read the data.

## 2.3 Types of Thermit Welding

| Type | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Pressureless / Fusion Thermit Welding** | Molten metal is simply poured; no external pressure is applied; gap is filled by fusion alone. | New rail laying, splicing |
| **Pressure Thermit Welding (Platen Process)** | Molten metal is poured and immediately the joint is pressed between platens to upset the metal. | Repair of broken shafts, locomotive frames |
| **Tap-Fusion Welding** | Molten thermite is used to fuse the base metal without a separate filler; used for joining thin sections. | Small-scale repairs |
| **Soaking Pit / Ladle Pre-heating** | Thermite is used to preheat ladles or risers in foundries (not strictly welding, but related). | Foundry applications |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Railway Industry:** Continuous welded rail (CWR) on Indian Railways, metro lines, and high-speed rail networks worldwide. A single thermit weld can join 25 m rail sections into kilometer-long ribbons.
- **Heavy Engineering:** Repair of large propeller shafts, rolling-mill frames, and large gear hubs in situ (no dismantling).
- **Foundry / Steel Plants:** Pre-heating of ladles, riserless casting, and emergency tundish heating.
- **Shipbuilding and Aerospace:** Emergency field repairs where electricity is unavailable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Mass Ratio of Al and Fe₂O₃

The balanced thermite reaction is:

$$2Al + Fe_2O_3 \rightarrow 2Fe + Al_2O_3 + Heat$$

From atomic mass data:
- Atomic mass of Al = **27 g/mol**
- Molecular mass of Fe₂O₃ = 2(56) + 3(16) = **160 g/mol**

For the reaction:
- 2 moles of Al = 2 × 27 = **54 g**
- 1 mole of Fe₂O₃ = **160 g**

Therefore the stoichiometric mass ratio is:

$$\frac{m_{Al}}{m_{Fe_2O_3}} = \frac{54}{160} = 0.3375$$

In percentage form:

$$\% \; Al = \frac{54}{54 + 160} \times 100 = 25.2 \; \%$$

$$\% \; Fe_2O_3 = \frac{160}{214} \times 100 = 74.8 \; \%$$

**Interpretation:** For every **1 kg** of Fe₂O₃, we need **337.5 g** of aluminum powder to obtain a complete reaction. In practice, a small **5 – 10 % excess of aluminum** is added to compensate for incomplete oxidation and losses — this is the *ignition margin*.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mass of Filler Iron Produced

From the same reaction, 1 mole of Fe₂O₃ (160 g) yields 2 moles of Fe.

$$m_{Fe} = 2 \times 56 = 112 \; g$$

Therefore the yield ratio is:

$$\text{Yield} = \frac{112}{160} = 0.70 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 70 \; \% \text{ of Fe}_2O_3 \text{ mass converts to Fe}$$

If the question gives **M kg** of Fe₂O₃ in the thermite charge, the molten iron obtained is:

$$m_{Fe, \; filler} = 0.70 \times M \; kg$$

This is a direct *KTU board-favourite numerical*. The remaining 30 % of mass appears as Al₂O₃ slag.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Adiabatic Temperature Reached by the Reaction

The **adiabatic flame temperature (T_ad)** is the highest temperature theoretically possible when **all** the reaction heat goes into heating the products (Fe and Al₂O₃) with no losses.

Let the heat released by the reaction be **Q_r (J)** per mole of Fe₂O₃. The standard value used in KTU problems is:

$$Q_r = 850 \; kJ/mol \; \text{of} \; Fe_2O_3$$

The total mass of products per mole of Fe₂O₃ is:

$$m_{products} = m_{Fe} + m_{Al_2O_3} = (2 \times 56) + (102) = 112 + 102 = 214 \; g = 0.214 \; kg$$

Using specific heat capacities (approximate, in the temperature range of interest):
- $c_{p,Fe} \approx 670 \; J/(kg \cdot K)$
- $c_{p,Al_2O_3} \approx 1000 \; J/(kg \cdot K)$

Weighted mean specific heat of the products:

$$c_{p,eff} = \frac{m_{Fe} \cdot c_{p,Fe} + m_{Al_2O_3} \cdot c_{p,Al_2O_3}}{m_{Fe} + m_{Al_2O_3}}$$

$$c_{p,eff} = \frac{(0.112 \times 670) + (0.102 \times 1000)}{0.214} \; J/(kg \cdot K)$$

$$c_{p,eff} = \frac{75.04 + 102.0}{0.214} = \frac{177.04}{0.214} \approx 827.3 \; J/(kg \cdot K)$$

Applying the heat balance (energy released = energy absorbed by products):

$$Q_r = m_{products} \cdot c_{p,eff} \cdot (T_{ad} - T_0)$$

$$850 \times 10^3 = 0.214 \times 827.3 \times (T_{ad} - 298)$$

$$T_{ad} - 298 = \frac{850000}{177.04} \approx 4801 \; K$$

$$T_{ad} \approx 4801 + 298 = 5099 \; K \; (\approx 4826 \; °C)$$

> [!NOTE]
> This *theoretical* adiabatic value (~ 4800 °C) is **not achieved in practice** due to:
> 1. Radiation and convection losses from the crucible,
> 2. Pre-heating of parent metal and mould,
> 3. Incomplete reaction efficiency (~ 70 – 80 %).
>
> The *practical* molten metal temperature is therefore **~ 2500 °C – 3000 °C**, which is the KTU answer.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Heat Required to Melt a Given Joint

**Problem Pattern (Frequent in KTU ESE):** *Calculate the quantity of thermite required to fill a gap of dimensions L × b × h in a rail joint, given the joint efficiency η.*

The heat required to:
- (i) Heat the filler iron from ambient T₀ to melting point T_m (≈ 1538 °C)
- (ii) Supply the latent heat of fusion L_f (≈ 247 kJ/kg)
- (iii) Compensate for losses (efficiency η ≈ 0.5 – 0.7)

$$Q_{req} = \frac{m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_m - T_0) + m \cdot L_f}{\eta}$$

Given the gap volume $V = L \cdot b \cdot h$ and density of iron $\rho_{Fe}$:

$$m = \rho_{Fe} \cdot V$$

The thermite mass needed is then:

$$m_{thermite} = \frac{Q_{req}}{Q_{per \; kg}} = \frac{m \cdot [c_p (T_m - T_0) + L_f]}{\eta \cdot Q_{per \; kg}}$$

where $Q_{per \; kg} \approx 5.31 \; MJ/kg$ for standard thermite.

### Worked Example (Board Pattern)

**Q:** A rail gap of 25 mm × 100 mm × 200 mm is to be filled by thermit welding. The reaction gives 5 MJ/kg of thermite, the efficiency is 60 %, and the latent heat of iron is 270 kJ/kg, c_p = 670 J/(kg·K). Ambient temperature = 30 °C, melting point = 1530 °C. Calculate the thermite required.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute volume and mass of iron needed:**

$$V = 25 \times 100 \times 200 = 500000 \; mm^3 = 500 \; cm^3 = 5 \times 10^{-4} \; m^3$$

$$m_{Fe} = \rho_{Fe} \cdot V = 7000 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 3.5 \; kg$$

**Step 2 — Compute sensible + latent heat per kg of iron:**

$$\Delta T = 1530 - 30 = 1500 \; °C \; (= 1500 \; K)$$

$$Q_{sensible} = c_p \cdot \Delta T = 670 \times 1500 = 1.005 \times 10^6 \; J/kg$$

$$Q_{latent} = 2.7 \times 10^5 \; J/kg$$

$$Q_{total \; per \; kg} = 1.005 \times 10^6 + 2.7 \times 10^5 = 1.275 \; MJ/kg$$

**Step 3 — Total heat required:**

$$Q_{req} = 3.5 \times 1.275 = 4.4625 \; MJ$$

**Step 4 — Account for efficiency η = 0.6:**

$$Q_{thermit \; must \; release} = \frac{4.4625}{0.6} = 7.4375 \; MJ$$

**Step 5 — Mass of thermite needed:**

$$m_{thermite} = \frac{7.4375}{5} = 1.4875 \; kg \approx 1.5 \; kg$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Answer Format:** Always state assumptions (ambient temperature, melting point, density, efficiency) *before* substituting into the formula. Examiners award **1 mark** for proper assumption listing.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Thermit vs. Other Welding Processes

| Property | Thermit | Oxy-Acetylene | Arc (SMAW) | Resistance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Heat source** | Chemical | Gas combustion | Electric arc | Electric resistance |
| **Max temp** | ~ 2500 °C | ~ 3200 °C | ~ 6000 °C (arc) | Localized |
| **External power** | None | Cylinders | Required | Required |
| **Filler** | Self-produced | Separate rod | Separate electrode | None (autogenous) |
| **Portability** | Very high | High | Medium | Low |
| **Typical use** | Rails, heavy sections | Light fabrication | Construction | Sheet metal |
| **Skill level** | Moderate | High | Moderate | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the *functional flow* of a thermit welding operation from preparation to inspection. It uses **purely alphanumeric node IDs** and **plain uppercase text** inside labels (no markdown/HTML) to comply with Mermaid syntax rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[START] --> B1[INSPECT RAIL OR WORKPIECE]
    B1 --> B2[CLEAN AND ALIGN JOINT GAP TO 25 MM]
    B2 --> C1[ASSEMBLE REFRACTORY MOULD AROUND GAP]
    C1 --> C2[PREHEAT MOULD WITH GAS TORCH FOR 5 MIN]
    C2 --> D1[CHARGE CRUCIBLE WITH THERMITE MIXTURE]
    D1 --> D2[LIGHT IGNITION RIBBON MAGNESIUM]
    D2 --> E1[EXOTHERMIC REACTION OCCURS 2500 C]
    E1 --> E2[ALUMINUM REDUCES IRON OXIDE]
    E2 --> F1[MOLTEN IRON SETTLES BOTTOM OF CRUCIBLE]
    F1 --> F2[ALUMINA SLAG FLOATS ON TOP]
    F2 --> G1[OPEN CRUCIBLE TAP HOLE]
    G1 --> G2[MOLTEN STEEL POURS INTO MOULD]
    G2 --> H1[WAIT 5 MIN FOR SOLIDIFICATION]
    H1 --> H2[REMOVE MOULD AND RISER]
    H2 --> I1[GRIND EXCESS METAL FLUSH WITH RAIL]
    I1 --> I2[ULTRASONIC AND VISUAL INSPECTION]
    I2 --> J1[APPROVED JOINT OR REWORK]
    J1 --> K1[END]
```

## 4.2 Equipment & Material Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[THERMITE POWDER Al PLUS Fe2O3] --> R1[CRUCIBLE GRAPHITE LINED]
    P2[IGNITION RIBBON MAGNESIUM] --> R1
    R1 --> S1[REFRACTORY MOULD]
    S1 --> S2[PARENT METAL JOINT]
    S2 --> S3[CLAMPING AND ALIGNMENT FIXTURE]
    S3 --> S4[PREHEAT BURNER GAS TORCH]
    S4 --> S5[POURING STAGE]
    S5 --> S6[GRINDING AND FINISHING]
    T1[REACTION HEAT 2500 C] --> R1
    T2[MOLTEN IRON 2500 C] --> S1
    T3[ALUMINA SLAG 2000 C] --> X1[SLAG COLLECTION TRAY]
    X1 --> X2[REUSE OR DISPOSE]
```

## 4.3 Crucible and Mould Section — Functional Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TOP[CRUCIBLE TOP OPEN CHARGING HOLE] --> MID
    MID[REFRACTORY LINED CRUCIBLE BODY]
    MID --> SLG[UPPER LAYER ALUMINA SLAG]
    SLG --> FE[LOWER LAYER MOLTEN IRON 2500 C]
    FE --> TAP[TAP HOLE WITH GRAPHITE STOPPER]
    TAP --> MLD[REFRACTORY MOULD CAVITY]
    MLD --> BASE[PARENT METAL PARTS A AND B]
    BASE --> SUP[SUPPORT SAND BED]
    MID2[THERMITE CHARGE BETWEEN IRON AND SLAG] --- MID
```

> [!NOTE]
> The *tap hole* is critical — it is closed during the reaction with a graphite or titanium pin and opened (mechanically or by torch) at the precise moment the reaction ends. Premature or delayed tapping leads to defective welds.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Define thermit welding. Name the inventor and write the chemical reaction involved.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Thermit welding is a fusion welding process in which the heat for welding is obtained from the exothermic reaction between aluminum and iron oxide. The inventor is **Dr. Hans Goldschmidt** (1895). The reaction is:

$$2Al + Fe_2O_3 \rightarrow 2Fe + Al_2O_3 + Heat \;(\approx 850 \; kJ/mol)$$

(Definition: 1 mark, Inventor: 1 mark, Reaction: 1 mark.)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is the thermit process preferred for joining railway rails? List two reasons.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **No external power required** — highly suitable for remote track locations where electricity is unavailable. (1 mark)
2. **Very high temperature achieved (~ 2500 °C)** — sufficient to melt large rail cross-sections in a single operation. (1 mark)
