3. **Portable equipment** — the entire setup (crucible, mould, thermite) can be carried to the field. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (READ CAREFULLY):**
> - *Do not* confuse the **inventor's name** (Goldschmidt, 1895) with Edison or other inventors of welding.
> - *Always* state the **practical temperature (~ 2500 °C)** rather than the theoretical adiabatic value (~ 4800 °C) when describing what the process achieves in real life.
> - For numericals, *always* list **assumptions** (ambient temp, density, efficiency, latent heat) before substitution. Skipping this loses **1 to 2 marks** every time.
> - In diagram questions, label the **tap hole**, **crucible**, **mould**, **parent metal**, and **slag** clearly. Half-mark cuts for unlabeled diagrams.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **step-by-step procedure of thermit welding used for joining railway rails**. (7 marks)

**(b)** A thermite mixture contains 2 kg of Fe₂O₃ and 0.7 kg of aluminum powder. The reaction releases 850 kJ per mole of Fe₂O₃. Given that the latent heat of iron is 270 kJ/kg and specific heat is 670 J/(kg·K), calculate: (i) the mass of molten iron produced, and (ii) the heat available per kg of filler. Assume the reaction efficiency is 70 %. (7 marks)

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Procedure (7 marks)

1. **Preparation of Joint (1 mark):** The ends of the two rails to be joined are cleaned by grinding to remove rust, scale, and grease. A uniform gap of **20 – 25 mm** is maintained.
2. **Alignment and Clamping (1 mark):** The rails are aligned using a pulling jack to maintain perfect straightness, and the joint is held in a clamping fixture to prevent displacement during pouring.
3. **Mould Assembly (1 mark):** A preformed **refractory (silica-based) mould** is fitted around the gap. The mould has a sprue, riser, and preheating vents.
4. **Preheating (1 mark):** The mould and rail ends are preheated with an **oxy-acetylene torch for 5 – 8 minutes** to remove moisture and bring the parent metal to a forgeable temperature (~ 800 – 1000 °C).
5. **Thermite Charging and Ignition (1 mark):** The thermite mixture is charged into a graphite-lined crucible placed above the mould. A magnesium ignition ribbon is lit.
6. **Reaction and Pouring (1 mark):** Reaction completes in **20 – 30 seconds**; the tap hole is opened, and molten steel at ~ 2500 °C fills the mould cavity.
7. **Solidification, Finishing, and Inspection (1 mark):** After 5 minutes, the mould is stripped. The riser and excess metal are removed by **grinding or oxy-cutting**. The joint is inspected by **ultrasonic testing (UT)** for soundness.

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify limiting reactant (1 mark):**
Stoichiometric Al required for 2 kg Fe₂O₃ = $0.3375 \times 2 = 0.675 \; kg$. Since 0.7 kg is supplied (slight excess), Fe₂O₃ is the limiting reactant.

**Step 2 — Moles of Fe₂O₃ reacting (1 mark):**

$$n_{Fe_2O_3} = \frac{2000 \; g}{160 \; g/mol} = 12.5 \; mol$$

**Step 3 — Mass of molten iron produced (2 marks):**

$$m_{Fe} = 12.5 \times 2 \times 56 = 1400 \; g = 1.4 \; kg$$

(With 70 % efficiency: $m_{Fe, \; useful} = 0.7 \times 1.4 = 0.98 \; kg$.)

**Step 4 — Total heat released (1 mark):**

$$Q_{released} = 12.5 \times 850 \; kJ = 10625 \; kJ$$

**Step 5 — Useful heat available (accounting for 70 % efficiency) (1 mark):**

$$Q_{useful} = 0.7 \times 10625 = 7437.5 \; kJ$$

**Step 6 — Heat per kg of filler (1 mark):**

$$\text{Heat per kg} = \frac{7437.5 \; kJ}{0.98 \; kg} \approx 7590 \; kJ/kg \approx 7.59 \; MJ/kg$$

This value is sufficient to both heat and melt the iron (compare with required 1.275 MJ/kg from Section 3.4 example), confirming the joint can be successfully poured.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Compare **fusion thermit welding** and **pressure thermit welding**. Discuss the role of **preheating** in thermit welding. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain with a diagram the **construction and working of a thermit crucible**. List any **three advantages** and **two limitations** of thermit welding. (7 marks)

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Comparison and Preheat Role (7 marks)

| Parameter | Fusion Thermit Welding | Pressure Thermit Welding |
|---|---|---|
| External pressure | **Not applied** (1 mark) | **Applied by hydraulic platen** (1 mark) |
| Joint quality | Cast structure, less ductile | Wrought + cast, stronger (1 mark) |
| Use | New rail laying, splicing (1 mark) | Repair of broken shafts, frames (1 mark) |

**Role of preheating (3 marks):**
- Removes **moisture** from the mould and parent metal — prevents steam blowholes.
- Brings the parent metal to a **forgeable temperature (~ 800 °C)** so that the molten thermite steel fuses with it metallurgically.
- Reduces **thermal shock** when the hot metal (2500 °C) is poured, minimizing cracking.
- Saves a portion of the heat otherwise needed to warm the joint, improving efficiency.

#### Part (b) — Crucible Construction and Advantages/Limitations (7 marks)

**Crucible construction (4 marks):** A thermit crucible is a **refractory-lined (magnesia or alumina) steel shell** with:
- A **top charging opening** for thermite powder and ignition ribbon.
- A **refractory lining** that withstands the 2500 °C reaction.
- A **tap hole at the bottom** closed by a **graphite or titanium pin**, opened by tapping with a rod or by an oxy-flame.
- A **supporting stand** with a tilting or fixed design.

**Three advantages (2 marks):**
1. No external power/electricity required.
2. Suitable for joining very large sections in situ.
3. Highly portable; can be used in remote locations.

**Two limitations (1 mark):**
1. Joint quality is inferior to arc welding (cast structure, lower fatigue strength).
2. Process is **inherently dangerous** due to the violent exothermic reaction; requires trained personnel and strict safety.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Thermit Welding**

- **Inventor:** Hans Goldschmidt, **1895** (Germany).
- **Reaction:** $2Al + Fe_2O_3 \rightarrow 2Fe + Al_2O_3 + Heat$ — memorize word-for-word.
- **Practical temperature achieved:** **~ 2500 °C** (not the theoretical ~ 4800 °C adiabatic value).
- **Heat released:** **~ 850 kJ/mol** of Fe₂O₃ — but always use the value *given* in the question.
- **Filler is self-generated:** No external rod is consumed.
- **External energy source:** **None** (only a small magnesium ignition ribbon).
- **Primary applications:** Railway rail joining, in-situ repair of heavy shafts, foundry ladle pre-heating.
- **Key equipment:** Crucible (refractory-lined), ignition ribbon, refractory mould, tapping pin, preheat torch.
- **Two main types:** **Fusion (pressureless)** and **Pressure (platen)** thermit welding.
- **Crucial process steps (in order):** Clean → Align → Mould → Preheat → Charge → Ignite → Pour → Solidify → Grind → Inspect (UT).
- **Density separation principle:** Molten Fe (ρ ≈ 7.0 g/cm³) settles; Al₂O₃ slag (ρ ≈ 3.5 g/cm³) floats.
- **Numerical shortcuts:**
  - Mass of Fe produced = 0.70 × mass of Fe₂O₃.
  - Stoichiometric Al : Fe₂O₃ = 54 : 160 ≈ 1 : 3 by mass.
  - Adiabatic flame temperature formula: $T_{ad} \approx 2500 \; \text{°C}$ (use empirical value for board answers).
- **Common mistake to avoid:** Confusing thermit welding with **resistance** or **arc** welding — they are *fundamentally different* (chemical vs. electrical heat source).
- **Safety:** The reaction is violent and produces **UV radiation, sparks, and toxic fumes** (BaO₂ ignition) — PPE is mandatory.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Friction Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT404 - MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Friction Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Friction Welding (FW)** is a **solid-state welding process** in which the heat required for joining two metallic components is generated purely by the **mechanical friction** between the contacting surfaces, brought into relative motion under a controlled **axial compressive force (upset force)**. No external heat source, filler metal, or shielding gas is required. Once the interface reaches a plastic state, the friction is arrested and a higher **forging force** is applied to forge the parts together, producing a metallurgical bond.

The American Welding Society (AWS) classifies Friction Welding as a **solid-phase welding process** under the broader family of pressure welding, in which coalescence occurs at a temperature **below the melting point** of the base metals.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of rubbing two dry sticks together rapidly in a forest to generate a spark and start a fire. The mechanical rubbing (friction) generates intense heat at the interface. Friction welding uses the **exact same principle**, but the heat is *contained* and *controlled* between two metal parts held together under pressure, so that instead of burning, the surfaces **soften, intermix, and forge together**.

A second helpful analogy is **brake-disc heating** in a car: when you press the brake pedal, the friction between the pad and the disc converts kinetic energy into heat — exactly what a friction welding machine does, except the heat is *confined to the joint line* and is *used to make a weld*, not to stop a vehicle.

### 1.3 Salient Features of Friction Welding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Solid-state process** — No melting, no fusion zone, no cast structure.
> - Heat source: **mechanical friction only**.
> - Joining of **dissimilar metals** is possible (e.g., aluminium to copper, steel to titanium).
> - Process is **autogenous** (no filler metal).
> - High repeatability and excellent weld quality.
> - Typical heat-affected zone (HAZ) is **narrow and well-defined**.

### 1.4 Standard Process Metrics

| Metric | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|
| Rotational speed ($N$) | **200 – 2000 rpm** |
| Friction pressure ($P_f$) | **30 – 100 MPa** |
| Forging pressure ($P_g$) | **60 – 200 MPa** |
| Burn-off length | **1 – 5 mm** |
| Friction time | **1 – 30 s** |
| Upset (axial shortening) | **2 – 10 mm** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The "Why" and "How"

The principle of friction welding can be decomposed into the following logical chain:

1. **Mechanical Energy Input** — One component is held stationary (in a clamp) while the other is rotated (or both moved linearly) at a programmed speed.
2. **Contact under Axial Load** — An axial compressive force (friction pressure) is applied, ensuring the asperities of the two surfaces are in intimate contact.
3. **Frictional Heat Generation** — Relative sliding against the asperities dissipates mechanical energy as **frictional heat** at the interface.
4. **Thermal Softening (Plasticization)** — As the interface temperature rises (typically **0.6 – 0.9 $T_m$**, where $T_m$ is the melting point in Kelvin), the material at the interface yields and becomes plastic.
5. **Material Expulsion (Flash Formation)** — The plasticized metal is squeezed out radially, forming a characteristic **upset flash / circumferential burr**.
6. **Forging Phase** — Rotation is stopped abruptly and a much higher **forging force** consolidates the joint, breaks up oxide films, and produces a fine-grained weld.
7. **Cooling and Solidification of Bond** — Heat is conducted into the bulk, and the joint solidifies under pressure, completing coalescence.

### 2.2 Phases of Friction Welding (Process Timeline)

| Phase | Duration | Friction Torque | Axial Pressure | Material Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **I — Initial (Dry Friction)** | 0.2 – 1 s | High, fluctuating | Low (friction pressure) | Asperity contact; rapid temperature rise |
| **II — Transition (Mixed Friction)** | 1 – 5 s | Drops sharply | Constant | Partial plasticization; some flash forms |
| **III — Steady-State (Plastic Flow)** | 2 – 20 s | Low, stable | Constant | Steady burn-off; flash grows radially |
| **IV — Forging (Brake + Upset)** | 0.1 – 1 s | Zero (rotation stops) | High (forging pressure) | Consolidation; oxide film is extruded |

> [!NOTE]
> The **transition point** (end of Phase I / start of Phase II) is detected in modern machines by a **torque drop** or a **flash-formation sensor** and is the **principal process-control trigger**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Definition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat generated (uniform pressure) | $Q = \dfrac{\pi \mu F N R \, t}{45}$ | $\mu$=coeff. of friction, $F$=axial force, $N$=rpm, $R$=outer radius, $t$=time | J |
| 2 | Mean interface velocity | $v_m = \dfrac{\pi N R}{60}$ | $R$=outer radius, $N$=rpm | m/s |
| 3 | Local heat flux | $q(r) = \mu \, p \, \omega \, r$ | $p$=local pressure, $\omega$=angular vel., $r$=radial coord. | W/m² |
| 4 | Rotational power | $P_{rot} = 2 \pi N T / 60$ | $T$=torque | W |
| 5 | Friction power (avg) | $P_f = \mu F v_m$ | — | W |
| 6 | Energy to reach forging temp. | $E_f = m \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | $m$=mass at interface, $c_p$=specific heat | J |
| 7 | Burn-off rate | $\dot{L} = \dfrac{\mu \, F \, \omega \, R}{3 \, \sigma_y}$ | $\sigma_y$=yield stress at interface | m/s |
| 8 | Forging pressure ratio | $K = P_g / P_f$ | typically 1.5 – 3 | dimensionless |
| 9 | Interface temperature (approx.) | $T_i \approx T_{amb} + \dfrac{0.9 \, Q}{m \, c_p}$ | steady-state approximation | K |
| 10 | Angular velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ | $N$ in rpm | rad/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on units:** When using the heat-generation formula $Q = \pi \mu F N R t / 45$, keep $F$ in **newtons (N)**, $N$ in **rev/min**, $R$ in **metres (m)**, and $t$ in **seconds (s)** to obtain $Q$ directly in **joules (J)**. The coefficient $\mu$ is dimensionless and is typically **0.3 – 0.5** for clean steel–steel contact.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Friction welding is used in industries where **reliability, repeatability, and metallurgical compatibility of dissimilar joints** matter more than cosmetic weld appearance:

- **Automotive:** Drive shafts, propeller shafts, gear blanks, steering columns, hydraulic piston rods.
- **Aerospace:** Engine turbine discs to shafts, blisks (blade + disc), landing-gear pins.
- **Oil & Gas:** Drill pipes, sucker rods, tool joints, downhole tubulars.
- **Power Generation:** Rotor shafts, valve bodies, dissimilar metal transitions (stainless-to-carbon-steel).
- **Tooling:** Bimetallic tools, drill bits with carbide shanks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Rate of Heat Generation at the Interface

**Statement:** For two cylindrical workpieces of outer radius $R$, one rotating at $N$ rpm under uniform contact pressure $p$ with coefficient of friction $\mu$, derive the total rate of frictional heat generation, $\dot{Q}$.

**Assumptions:**
- Contact pressure $p$ is uniform across the annular face.
- Coefficient of friction $\mu$ is constant.
- Heat is generated only at the sliding interface.

**Step 1 — Set up the kinematics.**
The angular velocity is:

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} \quad \text{[rad/s]}$$

The local linear sliding velocity at radius $r$ is:

$$v(r) = \omega \, r = \frac{2 \pi N r}{60} \quad \text{[m/s]}$$

**Step 2 — Express the local frictional power per unit area.**
For a pressure $p$ acting at the surface, the local frictional shear stress is $\tau = \mu p$. The local heat flux (power per unit area) is:

$$q(r) = \tau \cdot v(r) = \mu \, p \, \omega \, r = \frac{2 \pi \mu p N r}{60} \quad \text{[W/m}^2\text{]}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate over the full contact area.**
A differential annular ring has area $\mathrm{d}A = 2 \pi r \, \mathrm{d}r$. The total rate of heat generation is:

$$\dot{Q} = \int_{0}^{R} q(r) \, \mathrm{d}A = \int_{0}^{R} \frac{2 \pi \mu p N r}{60} \cdot 2 \pi r \, \mathrm{d}r$$

**Step 4 — Perform the integration.**

$$\dot{Q} = \frac{4 \pi^{2} \mu p N}{60} \int_{0}^{R} r^{2} \, \mathrm{d}r$$

$$\dot{Q} = \frac{4 \pi^{2} \mu p N}{60} \cdot \frac{R^{3}}{3}$$

$$\dot{Q} = \frac{4 \pi^{2} \mu p N R^{3}}{180}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{Q} = \frac{\pi^{2} \mu p N R^{3}}{45}} \quad \text{[W]}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to a function of total axial force $F$.**
The total axial force is related to pressure by $F = p \cdot \pi R^{2}$, so $p = F / (\pi R^{2})$. Substituting:

$$\dot{Q} = \frac{\pi^{2} \mu N R^{3}}{45} \cdot \frac{F}{\pi R^{2}} = \frac{\pi \mu F N R}{45}$$

Therefore the **total heat generated over time $t$** is:

$$Q_{total} = \dot{Q} \cdot t = \frac{\pi \mu F N R \, t}{45} \quad \text{[J]}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Simplified (Mean-velocity) Approximation

A commonly used, simpler engineering approximation uses the **mean sliding velocity** at the interface:

$$v_{m} = \frac{\omega R}{2} = \frac{\pi N R}{60} \quad \text{[m/s]}$$

The mean velocity is the average of 0 (at the centre) and $\omega R$ (at the rim). Then:

$$\dot{Q}_{approx} = F \cdot \mu \cdot v_{m} = \frac{\pi \mu F N R}{60} \quad \text{[W]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The mean-velocity form gives $\dot{Q} = \pi \mu F N R / 60$, while the rigorous integral gives $\dot{Q} = \pi \mu F N R / 45$. The ratio is $60/45 = 4/3$, i.e., the **rigorous result is ~33% higher** because the actual velocity distribution weights the outer radius (higher $r$, higher $v$) more heavily than a simple mean. For KTU problems, **use the mean-velocity form** unless the question explicitly asks for the rigorous integral.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Burn-off (Axial Shortening) Rate

The axial shortening rate $\dot{L}$ (m/s) at the interface equals the volumetric plastic flow rate divided by the interface area. Using the plastic-flow relation $\sigma_y = 3 \tau = 3 \mu p$ and $p = F/(\pi R^{2})$:

$$\dot{L} = \frac{2 \mu F \omega R}{3 \pi R^{2} \sigma_y} = \frac{2 \mu F \omega}{3 \pi R \sigma_y} = \frac{\mu F \omega R}{3 \pi R^{2} \cdot (\sigma_y/2)}$$

For a clean derivation, the standard engineering result is:

$$\dot{L} = \frac{\mu \, F \, \omega \, R}{3 \, \sigma_y \, A} = \frac{\mu \, F \, \omega \, R}{3 \, \sigma_y \, \pi R^{2}}$$

Simplifying:

$$\boxed{\dot{L} = \frac{\mu \, F \, \omega}{3 \pi R \, \sigma_y}} \quad \text{[m/s]}$$

The total **burn-off length** during the friction phase is $L = \dot{L} \cdot t_{friction}$, and is typically 1–5 mm.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Required Rotational Torque

The torque required to overcome friction at the interface is obtained from the moment of the frictional shear stress about the rotation axis:

$$T = \int_{0}^{R} (\mu p) \cdot r \cdot \mathrm{d}A = \int_{0}^{R} \mu p \cdot r \cdot 2 \pi r \, \mathrm{d}r = 2 \pi \mu p \int_{0}^{R} r^{2} \, \mathrm{d}r$$

$$T = 2 \pi \mu p \cdot \frac{R^{3}}{3} = \frac{2 \pi \mu p R^{3}}{3}$$

Substituting $p = F / (\pi R^{2})$:

$$\boxed{T = \frac{2 \mu F R}{3}} \quad \text{[N·m]}$$

This is the **mean-radius torque**, and the required **rotational power** is then:

$$P_{rot} = T \cdot \omega = \frac{2 \mu F R}{3} \cdot \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \mu F R N}{90} = \frac{\pi \mu F R N}{45} \quad \text{[W]}$$

(consistent with the heat-generation rate, as expected from energy conservation).

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Two steel shafts of diameter 30 mm are to be joined by rotary friction welding. Friction pressure = 50 MPa, coefficient of friction = 0.4, rotational speed = 1000 rpm, friction time = 5 s. Calculate: (i) total heat generated, (ii) torque required, and (iii) burn-off length, given $\sigma_y = 350$ MPa at the interface.

**Given:** $D = 0.030$ m, $R = 0.015$ m, $p = 50 \times 10^{6}$ Pa, $\mu = 0.4$, $N = 1000$ rpm, $t = 5$ s, $\sigma_y = 350 \times 10^{6}$ Pa.

**(i) Total heat generated** (rigorous form):

$$Q = \frac{\pi \mu F N R t}{45}$$

First, compute axial force:

$$F = p \cdot \pi R^{2} = 50 \times 10^{6} \cdot \pi \cdot (0.015)^{2} = 50 \times 10^{6} \cdot 7.0686 \times 10^{-4} \approx 35{,}343 \text{ N}$$

Now substitute:

$$Q = \frac{\pi \cdot 0.4 \cdot 35{,}343 \cdot 1000 \cdot 0.015 \cdot 5}{45}$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \cdot 0.4 \cdot 35{,}343 \cdot 75}{45} = \frac{\pi \cdot 1{,}060{,}290}{45} \approx 74{,}000 \text{ J} \approx 74 \text{ kJ}$$

**(ii) Torque required:**

$$T = \frac{2 \mu F R}{3} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 35{,}343 \cdot 0.015}{3} = \frac{424.1}{3} \approx 141.4 \text{ N·m}$$

**(iii) Burn-off length:**

First, $\omega = 2 \pi N / 60 = 2 \pi \cdot 1000 / 60 \approx 104.72$ rad/s.

$$\dot{L} = \frac{\mu F \omega}{3 \pi R \sigma_y} = \frac{0.4 \cdot 35{,}343 \cdot 104.72}{3 \pi \cdot 0.015 \cdot 350 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$\dot{L} = \frac{1{,}480{,}300}{4.948 \times 10^{7}} \approx 0.0299 \text{ m/s} = 29.9 \text{ mm/s}$$

$$L = \dot{L} \cdot t = 0.0299 \cdot 5 \approx 0.150 \text{ m} = 150 \text{ mm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> The burn-off length of 150 mm is unrealistically large — this signals that, in practice, the friction phase is **stopped much earlier** (typically when flash is observed, or when torque drops). In real KTU numericals, friction time is usually chosen so that $L \approx 2$–$5$ mm. The KTU examiner may ask you to **re-compute the friction time** for a desired burn-off.

**Correction for realistic burn-off:** For $L_{desired} = 3$ mm $= 0.003$ m:

$$t_{friction} = \frac{L_{desired}}{\dot{L}} = \frac{0.003}{0.0299} \approx 0.10 \text{ s}$$

The corrected heat generated:

$$Q_{corrected} = 74{,}000 \cdot \frac{0.10}{5} \approx 1{,}480 \text{ J} \approx 1.48 \text{ kJ}$$

This is a much more realistic energy input for a small-diameter shaft joint.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process-Block Diagram of a Rotary Friction Welding Machine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Motor Drive System] --> B[Clamp Chuck Rotating]
    B --> C[Workpiece 1 Rotating Component]
    D[Hydraulic / Pneumatic Cylinder] --> E[Thrust Bearing]
    E --> F[Moving Carriage]
    F --> G[Clamp Chuck Stationary]
    G --> H[Workpiece 2 Stationary Component]
    I[Control Panel PLC] --> A
    I --> D
    J[Force Sensor Load Cell] --> I
    K[Torque Sensor] --> I
    L[Axial Displacement Sensor LVDT] --> I
    A -. rotational speed .-> I
    D -. axial pressure .-> I
    F -. burn-off length .-> L
    B -. torque feedback .-> K
    F -. upset force .-> J
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **Motor** drives a rotating chuck that holds one workpiece. The **hydraulic cylinder** advances the other (stationary) workpiece axially under controlled pressure. **Load cell**, **torque sensor**, and **LVDT** provide real-time feedback to the **PLC** that triggers the forging phase when the transition point is detected.

### 4.2 Friction-Welding Process State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> InitialContact
    InitialContact --> DryFriction: Axial pressure applied
    DryFriction --> TransitionPhase: Temperature rises to 0.6 Tm
    TransitionPhase --> SteadyState: Torque drop detected
    SteadyState --> BrakeStop: Burn-off target reached
    BrakeStop --> Forging: Rotation halted, forging pressure applied
    Forging --> Cooling: Pressure held for dwell time
    Cooling --> JointComplete: Coalescence complete
    JointComplete --> [*]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Process Variables

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[Process Inputs]
        I1[Rotational Speed N]
        I2[Friction Pressure Pf]
        I3[Forging Pressure Pg]
        I4[Friction Time tf]
        I5[Forging Time tg]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS[Heat Generation Zone]
        P1[Friction Heat Qf]
        P2[Plastic Deformation]
        P3[Flash Formation]
        P4[Material Expulsion]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Weld Outputs]
        O1[Interface Temperature Ti]
        O2[Upset / Burn-off Length L]
        O3[Flash Geometry]
        O4[Microstructure Grain Refinement]
        O5[Joint Strength]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    I4 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> O1
    P4 --> O2
    P4 --> O3
    P2 --> O4
    O4 --> O5
    I3 --> P2
    I5 --> O5
```

### 4.4 Typical Temperature–Time Profile at the Interface

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Interface Temperature vs Time during Friction Welding"
    x-axis "Time (s)" [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7]
    y-axis "Temperature (°C)" 0 --> 1400
    line [25, 350, 780, 1100, 1280, 1320, 1280, 900]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the curve:** The temperature rises sharply during **Phase I (dry friction)**, then continues to climb more slowly through **Phase II–III** as the interface approaches the forging temperature (here ~ 0.9 $T_m$ for steel). When rotation is braked and forging pressure is applied, the **peak temperature is reached** before the joint cools rapidly by conduction into the bulk material. This thermal cycle determines the **final grain size and HAZ width**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define friction welding. With a neat sketch, explain its working principle.**

**Model Answer:**
Friction welding is a **solid-state pressure welding process** in which the heat required for coalescence is generated by **mechanically rubbing** two components together under an axial compressive force. The steps are:

1. One component is rotated and brought into contact with the other (held stationary) under a programmed **friction pressure** $P_f$.
2. Frictional heat raises the interface temperature to **0.6 – 0.9 $T_m$**, plasticizing the material.
3. Rotation is braked, and a higher **forging pressure** $P_g$ is applied to consolidate the joint.
4. A characteristic **circumferential flash** is expelled, and a metallurgical bond forms below the melting point.

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **List any six advantages and three limitations of friction welding.**

**Model Answer (Advantages):**
1. **Solid-state process** — no fusion, no cast structure, no solidification cracks.
2. Suitable for **dissimilar metal joining** (e.g., Al–Cu, steel–Ti) with no filler.
3. **High repeatability** and easy automation.
4. **Narrow HAZ** and fine-grained weld zone.
5. **No shielding gas or flux** required.
6. **Short cycle time** (a few seconds).

**Limitations:**
1. One component must be rotationally symmetric (in rotary FW).
2. Requires **high initial capital** for the machine.
3. **Flash formation** is unavoidable and may need post-machining.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Question 1 [14 Marks]** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PBMRT404]*
>
> **(a)** Explain the **four phases of rotary friction welding** with a labelled time–torque and time–axial-displacement diagram. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a neat schematic, describe the construction and working of a **continuous-drive friction welding machine**. List the functions of its main components. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q1(a)

The four phases of rotary friction welding are:

**Phase I — Initial (Dry Friction) Phase:**
The two surfaces first touch at asperities. Coefficient of friction is high ($\mu \approx 0.4$–$0.6$), and the **frictional torque is high and fluctuating**. Rapid heat generation occurs at discrete contact spots. The axial pressure is held at the *friction pressure* $P_f$, and the burn-off rate is low. This phase lasts **0.2 – 1 s**.

**Phase II — Transition Phase:**
The interface temperature rises to **0.6 – 0.8 $T_m$**, asperities begin to yield, and contact area increases. The **friction coefficient drops sharply** (because of plasticized-film lubrication), causing a measurable **torque dip**. This dip is the **trigger** for the control system to begin the forging countdown. Some flash begins to form.

**Phase III — Steady-State (Plastic Flow) Phase:**
Material at the interface flows plastically and is **expelled radially as flash**. The burn-off is approximately linear in time. Torque stabilises at a lower value. This phase determines most of the **upset / burn-off length** (typically 2 – 5 mm).

**Phase IV — Forging Phase:**
Rotation is braked (deceleration ~ 100 – 1000 rad/s²), and the **forging pressure** $P_g$ (≈ 1.5 – 3 × $P_f$) is applied for a short dwell (~ 0.1 – 1 s). Plasticized material is consolidated, oxide films are squeezed out, and the joint solidifies under pressure.

**Diagrams expected by the examiner:**

- **Torque–time graph:** high–fluctuating → sharp drop → low–stable → instant zero
- **Burn-off–time graph:** near-zero → accelerating → linear → sudden jump (at forging)

#### Model Solution — Q1(b)

**Construction (with reference to the block diagram in Section 4.1):**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Motor drive** (AC/Servo with VFD) | Provides programmable rotational speed (200 – 2000 rpm) and braking torque. |
| **Rotating chuck** | Holds the rotating workpiece; transmits torque from motor. |
| **Stationary chuck** | Holds the other workpiece on a sliding carriage. |
| **Hydraulic/pneumatic cylinder** | Applies axial friction and forging pressure; programmable profile. |
| **Thrust bearing** | Isolates the rotating spindle from the advancing carriage so that axial force is transmitted without spinning the carriage. |
| **Load cell** | Measures axial force for closed-loop pressure control. |
| **Torque sensor / current sensor** | Detects the transition point. |
| **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)** | Measures axial displacement (burn-off). |
| **PLC / Microcontroller** | Runs the welding sequence: spin-up → friction → brake → forge → retract. |
| **Flash tray / enclosure** | Catches the expelled hot flash. |

**Working Cycle:**
1. Load both workpieces in chucks.
2. Set the **friction speed $N$**, **friction pressure $P_f$**, **friction time $t_f$** (or **burn-off target $L$**), and **forging pressure $P_g$** on the HMI.
3. Motor accelerates rotating chuck to set $N$.
4. Hydraulic cylinder advances the stationary workpiece; pressure ramps to $P_f$.
5. Friction begins. PLC monitors torque and displacement.
6. At trigger condition (burn-off reached, or time elapsed), motor is braked.
7. Hydraulic pressure is increased to $P_g$; forging dwell begins.
8. After dwell, cylinder retracts; the welded assembly is unloaded.
9. Flash is removed (often by post-machining).

---

> **Question 2 [14 Marks — Alternative Choice]** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PBMRT404]*
>
> **(a)** Discuss the **principal process parameters** of friction welding (rotational speed, friction pressure, forging pressure, burn-off, friction time) and their effects on **weld quality**. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare **rotary, inertia, and linear friction welding** processes with respect to heat-generation mechanism, machine complexity, component geometry, and typical applications. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q2(a)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Effect on Weld Quality |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rotational speed** | $N$ | 200 – 2000 rpm | Higher $N$ → faster heating, narrower HAZ, but risk of over-heating and reduced torque (because $\mu$ falls); too low → insufficient heat, cold weld. |
| **Friction pressure** | $P_f$ | 30 – 100 MPa | Higher $P_f$ → faster interface temperature rise, shorter friction time, but greater flash loss; too low → insufficient heat. |
| **Forging pressure** | $P_g$ | 60 – 200 MPa | $P_g$ must be sufficient to **consolidate the joint** and **extrude the oxide film**; too low → porosity, weak bond. |
| **Burn-off length** | $L$ | 1 – 5 mm | Acts as a **process signature** — sufficient $L$ guarantees that all contaminated surface material is expelled; too low → defective joint, too high → wastage. |
| **Friction time** | $t_f$ | 1 – 30 s | Indirectly controlled (via burn-off); affects thermal cycle and HAZ. |
| **Forging time / dwell** | $t_g$ | 0.1 – 1 s | Must be long enough for joint solidification under pressure. |

**Key principle:** The product $P_f \times t_f$ (or equivalently, $P_f \times L$) is a measure of **heat input per unit area**. Consistency of this product across joints is the **principal quality criterion** in production.

#### Model Solution — Q2(b)

| Feature | Rotary (Continuous Drive) | Inertia (Storage) | Linear |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Heat-generation mechanism** | Continuous frictional heating at a programmed speed. | A **flywheel** stores kinetic energy; welding consumes the stored energy. | One component oscillates linearly against the other. |
| **Drive system** | Motor + chuck, continuous. | Flywheel + spindle; motor spins flywheel, then disengages. | Hydraulic or mechanical linear oscillator. |
| **Component geometry** | Round bars / tubes; rotational symmetry required. | Round bars / tubes; rotational symmetry required. | **Non-circular cross-sections** possible (rectangular, irregular). |
| **Control parameter** | Time or burn-off. | Flywheel speed (energy stored) + axial pressure. | Stroke length, frequency, axial pressure. |
| **Energy delivery** | Continuous, can be long. | One short burst (typically 0.5 – 3 s). | Pulsed per stroke. |
| **Machine complexity** | Moderate. | High (flywheel, bearings, brake). | High. |
| **Typical applications** | Drive shafts, drill pipes, valve bodies, gear blanks. | Automotive axle components, small precision parts. | Aircraft wing-skin stringers, large non-circular sections. |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in friction welding questions:**
> 1. **Units mismatch** — The most common error. Mixing $R$ in mm with $F$ in N without conversion. The heat-generation formula $Q = \pi \mu F N R t / 45$ requires $R$ in **metres**.
> 2. **Confusing friction pressure and forging pressure** — These are *two different* pressures. Forging pressure is **1.5 – 3 times** the friction pressure. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **Omitting the flash** — A friction-welded joint **always has flash**. Failing to mention it indicates an incomplete understanding.
> 4. **Saying "fusion welding"** — Friction welding is **solid-state**. Calling it a fusion process will cost full credit on definition questions.
> 5. **Confusing burn-off with upset** — *Burn-off* is the axial shortening during the friction phase; *upset* is the total axial shortening including the forging phase.
> 6. **Forgetting the temperature ratio** — KTU examiners expect the student to mention that interface temperature is **0.6 – 0.9 $T_m$**, *not* the melting point.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Friction welding = solid-state pressure welding**; heat source is **mechanical friction only**; no melting, no filler.
> - Principal **process parameters**: rotational speed $N$, friction pressure $P_f$, forging pressure $P_g$, burn-off length $L$, friction time $t_f$.
> - **Forging pressure > Friction pressure** (typically 1.5 – 3 × $P_f$).
> - **Heat generated (rigorous):** $Q = \pi \mu F N R t / 45$. **Mean-velocity approx.:** $Q = \pi \mu F N R t / 60$.
> - **Mean interface velocity:** $v_m = \pi N R / 60$.
> - **Torque required:** $T = 2 \mu F R / 3$.
> - **Rotational power:** $P_{rot} = \pi \mu F N R / 45 = T \omega$.
> - **Burn-off rate:** $\dot{L} = \mu F \omega / (3 \pi R \sigma_y)$.
> - **Four phases:** Initial (dry) → Transition → Steady-state → Forging.
> - **Transition point** detected by a **sharp drop in torque** — used as forging trigger.
> - Interface temperature = **0.6 – 0.9 $T_m$** (NOT $T_m$).
> - **Flash** is the characteristic radial upset that is **expelled contaminated surface material** — its presence is a *quality indicator*.
> - **Types:** Rotary (continuous drive), Inertia (flywheel), Linear, Friction Stir, Orbital.
> - **Key applications:** drive shafts, drill pipes, gear blanks, dissimilar-metal joints (Al–Cu, steel–Ti), aerospace blisks, automotive axle components.
> - **Advantages:** solid-state, dissimilar-metal capability, automation-friendly, narrow HAZ, no shielding gas.
> - **Limitations:** rotational symmetry (for rotary type), high capital cost, flash must be removed.
> - **Examiner's mantra:** remember to convert $R$ from mm to m before plugging into energy / power formulas.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resistance Spot Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT404 - MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Resistance Spot Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition

**Resistance Spot Welding (RSW)** is a resistance welding process in which coalescence (metallic fusion bonding) is produced at a *single localized point* by the heat generated from the resistance offered by the workpieces to the flow of a low-voltage, high-amperage electric current. The workpieces are held together under a controlled **electrode force**, and the entire operation is completed in a fraction of a second (typically 0.1 to 0.5 seconds per spot).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Key Phrase:** *"Coalescence produced at the surface of contact of two or more parts by the heat obtained from resistance to the flow of electric current through the parts held together under pressure by electrodes."* — This is the exact KTU board definition worth 3-7 marks.

**Key Engineering Constants & Standards:**
- **Welding Current Range:** 1000 A to 100,000 A (typically 3000–20,000 A for sheet metal)
- **Electrode Voltage:** 1 V to 12 V (very low voltage, high current principle)
- **Open Circuit Voltage:** typically **415 V** (3-phase full-wave rectified to DC)
- **Electrode Force Range:** 200 N to 50,000 N
- **Welding Time:** 0.1 s to 2.0 s (most common 0.2 s)
- **Spot Diameter:** 3 mm to 25 mm

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — "The Toaster Principle with Two Fingers":**
> Imagine you are pinching a small piece of paper between your thumb and forefinger and try to pull it apart. Now imagine applying a very brief, high electric current through that pinch point — the paper would heat up and char (burn) exactly at the contact point, fusing the fibers together. That charred bond IS a spot weld.
> 
> In actual RSW: Two copper "fingers" (electrodes) pinch two metal sheets together. A huge current passes through. The highest resistance (and hence heat) is at the **faying surface** (interface between the two sheets) — not at the electrode-sheet contact. The metal at the interface melts, mixes, and forms a **fused nugget** which becomes the spot weld.

### 1.3 Why Resistance Spot Welding Matters in KTU Curriculum

This topic is a **High-Yield Module 2 topic** because it:
1. Carries 7–14 mark direct theory questions in **End Semester Examinations (ESE)**
2. Is a prerequisite for understanding **Projection Welding, Seam Welding, and Flash Butt Welding**
3. Tests student's understanding of **Joule's heating law (I²R)** — a fundamental physics application
4. Has direct industrial relevance in **automotive body assembly** (a typical car has **3000–5000 spot welds**)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — The Three Pillars of RSW

Resistance spot welding is governed by **THREE simultaneously controlled variables**, often called the *Welding Triad*:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Role |
|-----------|--------|---------------|------|
| **Welding Current** | I | 3000 – 20,000 A | Generates the heat (I²R) |
| **Weld Time** | t | 0.1 – 2.0 s | Duration of current flow |
| **Electrode Force** | F | 200 – 8000 N | Holds sheets, controls contact resistance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Concept:** Heat is NOT generated at the electrode tips. Heat is concentrated at the **faying interface** because that is where the highest resistance exists. The electrodes are water-cooled copper so they don't melt.

### 2.2 Stages of Spot Weld Formation (Sequential Logic)

The formation of a sound spot weld follows a precise **4-stage sequence**:

**Stage 1 — Initial Contact (Pre-Squeeze):**
- Electrodes descend and apply force F on the sheets
- Surface asperities flatten; intimate metal-to-metal contact begins
- Contact resistance is HIGH at this moment

**Stage 2 — Heat Build-up (Welding Time):**
- Current I flows; heat H = I²Rt develops
- Temperature at the faying interface rises to **0.5 × T_melt (in Kelvin)**
- At ~50% of melting temperature, a thin molten **nugget** nucleates
- Nugget grows radially outward

**Stage 3 — Forging / Consolidation (Hold Time):**
- Current is **cut off** but electrode force is **maintained**
- Molten nugget solidifies under pressure (similar to forging)
- This eliminates shrinkage porosity / cracks — *this is why electrode force is critical*

**Stage 4 — Release (Off Time):**
- Electrodes retract
- Weld cools, electrode indent is left on the sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Hold Time" is just as important as the "Weld Time"** in KTU theory. Many students forget to mention this in answers.

### 2.3 Heat Generation — The Core Physics

The fundamental heat generation equation is **Joule's First Law of Heating** applied to resistance welding:

$$H = I^2 \cdot R \cdot t$$

where:
- $H$ = Heat generated (Joules, J)
- $I$ = RMS welding current (Amperes, A)
- $R$ = Total resistance at the joint (Ohms, $\Omega$)
- $t$ = Time of current flow (seconds, s)

The total resistance $R$ is the **sum of four resistance components** in the current path:

$$R_{\text{total}} = 2R_e + 2R_{es} + R_s$$

where:
- $2R_e$ = Bulk resistance of the two copper electrodes
- $2R_{es}$ = Two electrode–sheet contact resistances
- $R_s$ = Sheet–sheet (faying) contact resistance — **this is where most heat is generated**

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---------|---------|------|
| 1 | $H = I^2 R t$ | Heat generated (Joule's Law) | Joules (J) |
| 2 | $H_{\text{net}} = I^2 R t - H_{\text{losses}}$ | Effective heat for nugget formation | J |
| 3 | $H_{\text{required}} = \rho \, V \, C_p \, \Delta T$ | Heat to raise metal to melting | J |
| 4 | $I^2 t = K \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{S}{t}\right)$ | **Preece's Equation** (weldability) | A²s |
| 5 | $d_{\text{nugget}} = k\sqrt{F}$ | Empirical nugget diameter relation | mm |
| 6 | $T_{\text{interface}} = T_0 + \dfrac{I^2 R t}{m \cdot C_p}$ | Temperature rise at interface | °C |
| 7 | $R_{\text{total}} = 2R_e + 2R_{es} + R_s$ | Total joint resistance | $\Omega$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Preece's Equation (Equation #4)** is a **guaranteed KTU board question**. Here, $S$ is the sheet thickness, $t$ is the welding time, and $K$ is a material constant. It plots as the famous **Weldability Lobe Diagram**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** 100% of car bodies are joined by RSW (e.g., Tata, Maruti assembly lines)
- **Aerospace:** Aircraft fuselage skin panels
- **White Goods:** Refrigerator cabinets, washing machine drums
- **Electronics:** Battery tab welding, capacitor can sealing
- **Construction:** Welded wire mesh, rebar cages

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Industrial Linkage:** Always quote at least one industrial application in 14-mark answers to score full marks under the "application" CO mapping.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Heat Generation Equation from First Principles

We begin from the fundamental definition of electrical power dissipated as heat in a resistor.

**Step 1 — Define electrical power dissipated:**
By definition, the power dissipated in a resistor when current $I$ flows under a potential difference $V$ is:

$$P = V \cdot I$$

**Step 2 — Apply Ohm's Law to substitute V:**
Using Ohm's law, $V = I \cdot R$, where $R$ is the resistance in ohms. Substituting this back:

$$P = (I \cdot R) \cdot I = I^2 \cdot R$$

This is the instantaneous power dissipation (in Watts) due to current flow.

**Step 3 — Convert power into total heat energy:**
Heat is the integral of power over the time of current flow. For a constant current and resistance during the welding cycle, integration reduces to simple multiplication:

$$H = P \cdot t = I^2 \cdot R \cdot t$$

**Step 4 — Final boxed result (Joule's First Law for RSW):**

$$H = I^2 R t \;\; \text{[Joules]}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- Heat is **quadratically proportional** to current → doubling I quadruples H
- Heat is **linearly proportional** to time → doubling t doubles H
- This is why **current control is more critical** than time control in RSW

### 3.2 Derivation of Net Heat Available for Nugget Formation

Not all the generated heat reaches the nugget. A portion is lost via conduction to the surrounding metal, electrodes, and cooling water. Let us derive the net heat equation.

**Step 1 — Total heat generated (from previous derivation):**
$$H_{\text{gen}} = I^2 R t$$

**Step 2 — Subtract the heat losses to electrodes, sheets, and water cooling:**
$$H_{\text{net}} = H_{\text{gen}} - H_{\text{losses}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and rearrange:**
$$H_{\text{net}} = I^2 R t - H_{\text{losses}}$$

**Step 4 — The net heat must equal the heat required to form the nugget:**
The minimum heat required to raise a small mass $m$ of metal from ambient temperature $T_0$ to the melting point $T_m$ is:

$$H_{\text{required}} = m \cdot C_p \cdot (T_m - T_0)$$

where $C_p$ is the specific heat capacity of the metal. For a **volumetric** approach using density $\rho$ and volume $V$:

$$H_{\text{required}} = \rho \cdot V \cdot C_p \cdot (T_m - T_0)$$

**Step 5 — Equating net heat to required heat (Heat Balance Equation):**
For a successful weld:

$$I^2 R t - H_{\text{losses}} = \rho \cdot V \cdot C_p \cdot (T_m - T_0)$$

**Step 6 — Solving for the critical (minimum) current required:**

$$\begin{aligned}
I^2 R t &= \rho V C_p (T_m - T_0) + H_{\text{losses}} \\
I^2 &= \dfrac{\rho V C_p (T_m - T_0) + H_{\text{losses}}}{R \, t} \\
I_{\min} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho V C_p (T_m - T_0) + H_{\text{losses}}}{R \, t}}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Trick:** If $I < I_{\min}$ → **No nugget forms (cold weld).** If $I \gg I_{\min}$ → **Expulsion (metal ejected) — a defective weld.** Only when $I$ lies within the **weldability lobe** does a sound nugget form.

### 3.3 Derivation of Preece's Equation (Weldability Criterion)

**Step 1 — Empirically, the minimum current squared times time for a sound weld varies logarithmically with sheet thickness:**
Welding researchers (Preece, 1940s) plotted extensive experimental data and arrived at:

$$I^2 \cdot t = K \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{S}{t}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation of each term:**
- $I$ = Welding current in kA
- $t$ = Welding time in cycles (1 cycle = 1/50 s in India = 0.02 s)
- $S$ = Single sheet thickness in mm
- $K$ = Material constant (depends on metal type, e.g., $K \approx 70$ for low-carbon steel)

**Step 3 — The "Lobe" appears when $I^2 t$ vs. $t$ is plotted:**
- **Upper curve (Expulsion Limit):** $I^2 t$ above which metal ejects
- **Lower curve (Preece Curve):** $I^2 t$ below which no proper nugget forms
- **Between the curves** lies the **Weldability Lobe** — the safe operating zone

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style):**

> **Q:** A low-carbon steel sheet of thickness 1.5 mm is to be spot-welded. Given $K = 70$, calculate the minimum current required for a welding time of 0.4 s. Take the operating frequency as 50 Hz.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

Convert time from seconds to cycles:
$$t_{\text{cycles}} = 0.4 \times 50 = 20 \text{ cycles}$$

Apply Preece's equation:
$$I^2 \cdot t = K \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{S}{t}\right)$$

Substitute values:
$$I^2 \cdot 20 = 70 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1.5}{20}\right)$$

Calculate the logarithm:
$$\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1.5}{20}\right) = \log_{10}(0.075) = -1.1249$$

Substitute:
$$I^2 \cdot 20 = 70 \cdot (-1.1249) = -78.74$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Negative value is physically impossible.** This means the time of 20 cycles is too long for a 1.5 mm sheet. The Preece equation only gives **valid results for the right-shifted part of the lobe**. Students must comment on this in the KTU answer.

Choose a shorter time, say $t = 6$ cycles (0.12 s):
$$I^2 \cdot 6 = 70 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1.5}{6}\right) = 70 \cdot \log_{10}(0.25) = 70 \cdot (-0.602) = -42.14$$

Still negative. Try $t = 2$ cycles:
$$I^2 \cdot 2 = 70 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1.5}{2}\right) = 70 \cdot (-0.125) = -8.72$$

Try $t = 0.5$ cycles:
$$I^2 \cdot 0.5 = 70 \cdot \log_{10}(3) = 70 \cdot 0.477 = 33.4$$
$$I^2 = 66.8 \implies I \approx 8.17 \text{ kA}$$

So, the minimum current is approximately **8.2 kA** for 0.5 cycles (10 ms) on 1.5 mm steel.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Parameters vs. Weld Quality

| If we INCREASE... | Effect on Weld | KTU Keyword |
|-------------------|---------------|-------------|
| Current (I) | More heat → larger nugget → risk of **expulsion** | "I²R effect dominates" |
| Time (t) | More heat → softer weld → risk of **porosity** | "Forging action reduces" |
| Electrode Force (F) | Lower contact R → less heat → risk of **cold weld** | "Resistive heating reduced" |
| Sheet Thickness (S) | Heat dissipation increases → need more I | "Quenching effect" |
| Electrode Tip Diameter | Lower current density → smaller nugget | "Current density is critical" |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Question Pattern:** "Explain the effect of electrode force on weld quality" is a recurring 7-mark question. Use the above matrix as your answer backbone.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Resistance Spot Welding — Process Flow Block Diagram

This block diagram maps the complete functional flow of an RSW machine from input power to the finished spot weld.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Mains 415V 50Hz] --> B[Step Down Transformer]
    B --> C[Low Voltage 1-12V]
    C --> D[Contactor SCR Timer]
    D --> E[Welding Transformer Secondary]
    E --> F[Electrodes Cu Class II]
    F --> G[Workpieces Held Under Force F]
    G --> H[Current Flows Through Joint]
    H --> I[I Squared R Heat Generated]
    I --> J[Localized Melting at Faying Surface]
    J --> K[Nugget Formation and Solidification]
    K --> L[Spot Weld Complete]

    M[Air Hydraulic Pneumatic System] --> F
    N[Water Cooling Lines] --> F
    O[Microprocessor Weld Controller] --> D
```

### 4.2 Heat Distribution During RSW — Block Architecture

This diagram illustrates why heat is concentrated at the faying surface, not at the electrodes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Copper Electrode Top] --> B[Contact Resistance R es1]
    B --> C[Top Sheet Bulk Resistance]
    C --> D[Faying Contact Resistance R s HIGHEST HEAT]
    D --> E[Bottom Sheet Bulk Resistance]
    E --> F[Contact Resistance R es2]
    F --> G[Copper Electrode Bottom]

    H[Water Cooling Removes Heat] --> A
    H --> G
    I[Heat Concentrated at D forms Nugget] --> D
```

### 4.3 Weldability Lobe Diagram — Conceptual Block Representation

Since the Lobe Diagram is a graph (not easily rendered in Mermaid), here is a block-level functional representation of the operating zones:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plot I squared t vs t] --> B{Compare with Limits}
    B --> C[Above Upper Curve: EXPULSION Defect]
    B --> D[Between Curves: SOUND WELD Zone]
    B --> E[Below Lower Curve: COLD WELD No Nugget]

    F[Lower Preece Curve] --> D
    G[Upper Expulsion Curve] --> D

    H[Material Constant K affects curve] --> F
    H --> G
    I[Sheet Thickness S shifts lobes] --> F
    I --> G

    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style C fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style E fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Sequence of Operations — Timing Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Squeeze Time]:::phase --> P2[Weld Time Current On]:::active
    P2 --> P3[Hold Time Current Off]:::phase
    P3 --> P4[Off Time Electrode Retract]:::phase

    classDef phase fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef active fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

### 4.5 Equipment Layout — Pedestal Type RSW Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operator Control Panel] --> B[Microprocessor Timer]
    B --> C[SCR Contactor Bank]
    C --> D[Primary 415V 3 phase]
    D --> E[Welding Transformer 50 to 200 kVA]
    E --> F[Secondary Winding - heavy copper]
    F --> G[Upper Arm and Electrode Holder]
    F --> H[Lower Arm and Electrode Holder]
    G --> I[Upper Electrode Tip]
    H --> J[Lower Electrode Tip]
    I --> K[Workpieces clamped]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Spot Weld Formed]

    M[Air Pneumatic Cylinder] --> G
    N[Water Inlet Manifold] --> I
    N --> J
    O[Foot Pedal Switch] --> B
```

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define resistance spot welding. Mention any two industrial applications.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Resistance spot welding is a resistance welding process in which coalescence is produced at the surface of contact of two or more parts by the heat obtained from resistance to the flow of electric current through the parts held together under pressure by electrodes. The heat generated follows Joule's law: $H = I^2 R t$.
Two industrial applications: (1) Automotive body assembly (car frames have 3000–5000 spot welds), and (2) Sheet metal fabrication in white goods such as refrigerator cabinets and washing machine drums.

---

#### Q2. [KTU University Exam - July 2023] State Joule's law of heating as applied to resistance spot welding.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
According to Joule's law of heating, the heat generated during resistance spot welding is given by:
$$H = I^2 R t$$
where $H$ = heat in Joules, $I$ = welding current in Amperes, $R$ = total resistance of the joint in Ohms, and $t$ = welding time in seconds. This equation forms the basis for determining the heat input required to form a sound spot weld nugget.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of resistance spot welding. Discuss the sequence of operations during one complete welding cycle. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:**
Resistance spot welding uses the heat generated by the resistance to electric current flow through two or more metal sheets held together under pressure by water-cooled copper electrodes. A low voltage (1–12 V) but high current (3,000–20,000 A) is passed through the joint. The total resistance at the joint comprises $R_{\text{total}} = 2R_e + 2R_{es} + R_s$, where $R_s$ (faying resistance) is the largest. Hence maximum heat is generated at the interface between the two sheets, melting a small volume of metal to form a **fused nugget**, which on solidification forms the spot weld.

**Sequence of Operations (4 stages):**
1. **Squeeze Time (5–10 cycles):** Electrodes close and apply a preset force; surface asperities flatten; contact resistance is established.
2. **Weld Time (2–30 cycles):** Current is switched on. Heat $H = I^2 R t$ is generated, nugget nucleates and grows.
3. **Hold Time (5–30 cycles):** Current is cut off, but electrode force is maintained. Molten nugget solidifies under pressure (forging action eliminates porosity).
4. **Off Time:** Electrodes retract, electrode indent mark remains.

**(b)** Explain the effect of welding current, welding time, and electrode force on the quality of a spot weld. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Effect on Weld Quality |
|-----------|------------------------|
| **Welding Current (I)** | Heat varies as $I^2$. Insufficient I → no nugget (cold weld). Excessive I → metal expulsion and porosity. **Optimum current** produces a sound nugget of diameter $d = 4\sqrt{t}$ for low-carbon steel (where $t$ is sheet thickness). |
| **Welding Time (t)** | Longer time accumulates more heat, allowing nugget to grow. Too long → coarse-grained weak weld, electrode sticking. Too short → undersized nugget. |
| **Electrode Force (F)** | Higher F → lower contact resistance → less heat → smaller nugget. Lower F → high contact R → localized overheating → expulsion. **Optimum F** gives consistent contact without crushing the sheets. |

The optimum combination is determined using the **Weldability Lobe Diagram**, which plots $I^2 t$ vs $t$ with two boundaries: the lower Preece curve (no-nugget limit) and the upper expulsion curve. The safe operating zone lies between them.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - July 2022]

**(a)** What is the weldability lobe diagram? Explain how it is constructed and used to determine optimum welding parameters. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Weldability Lobe Diagram** is a plot of welding current squared times welding time ($I^2 t$) on the Y-axis versus welding time ($t$) on the X-axis, for a given sheet thickness and material. It defines the safe operating region for producing sound spot welds.

**Construction:**
1. The **lower curve (Preece curve)** is plotted using the empirical equation $I^2 t = K \log_{10}(S/t)$. Below this curve, the heat input is insufficient and no nugget forms.
2. The **upper curve (Expulsion curve)** is determined experimentally. Above it, the heat is so high that molten metal is ejected (expulsion), producing a defective weld.
3. The region enclosed between the two curves is the **Weldability Lobe** — the safe operating window.

**Usage:**
- For a given sheet thickness, the optimum welding current and time combination is selected from within the lobe.
- As sheet thickness $S$ increases, both curves shift upward and to the right (more heat needed).
- As material strength/hardness increases, the lobe shrinks (narrower safe zone) — explaining why stainless steel and aluminum are harder to spot-weld than low-carbon steel.

**(b)** Two mild steel sheets each 1.2 mm thick are to be spot-welded. Using Preece's equation with $K = 70$ and a welding time of 4 cycles (50 Hz supply), determine the minimum welding current required. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Sheet thickness $S = 1.2$ mm
- Material constant $K = 70$
- Welding time $t = 4$ cycles
- Frequency $f = 50$ Hz

**To find:** Minimum welding current $I_{\min}$.

**Step 1 — Apply Preece's Equation:**
$$I^2 \cdot t = K \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{S}{t}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known values:**
$$I^2 \cdot 4 = 70 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1.2}{4}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the logarithm:**
$$\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1.2}{4}\right) = \log_{10}(0.3) = -0.5229$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $I^2$:**
$$I^2 \cdot 4 = 70 \cdot (-0.5229) = -36.60$$

$$I^2 = \dfrac{-36.60}{4} = -9.15$$

**Step 5 — Result and Comment:**
Since $I^2$ is **negative**, this is physically impossible. This indicates that **4 cycles is too long** a welding time for 1.2 mm mild steel sheet. The negative value arises because the Preece equation is empirical and valid only for the descending portion of the lobe, where $S/t < 1$ (i.e., $t < S$ in cycle units).

**Corrected approach:** Try $t = 1$ cycle:
$$I^2 \cdot 1 = 70 \cdot \log_{10}(1.2) = 70 \cdot 0.0792 = 5.544$$
$$I = \sqrt{5.544} \approx 2.35 \text{ kA}$$

Thus, a minimum current of about **2.35 kA** for 1 cycle (20 ms) is required for a sound weld on 1.2 mm mild steel.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks in RSW Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Hold Time** — Many students describe only Squeeze + Weld + Off, missing the critical **Hold (Forging) Time**. Deducts 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Resistance Location** — A common error is stating "heat is generated at the electrodes." Correct answer: **maximum heat is at the faying interface (Rs)**, not at the electrodes (which are water-cooled copper).
> 3. **Preece's Equation Sign Error** — Students blindly substitute and forget to check the **sign of the logarithm**. Always verify $S/t < 1$ for valid result.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert seconds to cycles** — Preece's equation uses cycles (1 cycle = 1/50 s in India), not seconds. Marks are lost for unit error.
> 5. **Drawing Electrode as Solid Block** — Examiners expect a labeled sketch showing water cooling inlet/outlet, force direction, current arrows, and the nugget location.
> 6. **Not Mentioning Electrode Class** — Always state that electrodes are made of **Class II Copper Alloy (RWMA)** for spot welding of steel.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** RSW is a resistance welding process producing **coalescence at a single point** by I²R heat under electrode pressure. *(1-mark definition word-for-word practice required.)*
- **Core Formula:** $H = I^2 R t$ — derived from Joule's First Law. Heat ∝ $I^2$, ∝ $R$, ∝ $t$.
- **Total Resistance:** $R_{\text{total}} = 2R_e + 2R_{es} + R_s$. **Faying resistance Rs is dominant for heat generation.**
- **Three Control Parameters (Welding Triad):** Current $I$, Time $t$, Electrode Force $F$.
- **Four Stages of Operation:** Squeeze → Weld → **Hold (Forging)** → Off. *Hold time is non-negotiable for sound welds.*
- **Preece's Equation:** $I^2 t = K \log_{10}(S/t)$ — defines the **lower boundary of the weldability lobe.**
- **Weldability Lobe:** Plot of $I^2 t$ vs $t$ with two curves (Preece & Expulsion). Safe zone is **between** the curves.
- **Current Range:** 3000–20,000 A typical; voltage only 1–12 V.
- **Electrodes:** Class II **Copper-Chromium-Zirconium alloy**, water-cooled internally.
- **Lobe Shape Shift:** Higher sheet thickness → lobe shifts **up and right** (more heat needed).
- **Defects to remember:** Expulsion (too much heat), Cold weld (too little), Porosity (insufficient hold time), Stick weld (excessive force).
- **Industrial Use Case:** Automotive bodies, white goods, aircraft panels.
- **Nugget Diameter Rule:** $d = 4\sqrt{t}$ for low-carbon steel (where $t$ = single sheet thickness in mm).
- **Preece's K value:** ~70 for low-carbon steel; lower for stainless steel and aluminum (these have smaller lobes).
- **KTU CO Mapping:** This topic primarily addresses **CO2 (Understand various welding processes)** and **CO3 (Apply process parameters)** of the PBMRT404 course.
- **Exam Tip:** Always draw a **labeled sketch** with electrode, sheet, current arrows, force arrow, and the nugget for full marks on theory questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resistance Seam Welding
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT404 - MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Resistance Seam Welding

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Resistance Seam Welding (RSEW / RSW-Seam)** is a process variant of resistance welding in which a continuous or intermittent overlapping weld is produced on a joint between two or more metal sheets by passing an electric current through the area of contact while the workpieces are held together under mechanical pressure applied between two **circular roller electrodes** that roll along the joint line. The process follows the governing principle of **Joule Heating (I²R heating)**, where heat is generated internally within the workpiece due to the resistance offered to the flow of welding current at the interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PBMRT404):**
> Seam welding is classified under **Electric Resistance Welding (ERW)** processes. Unlike spot welding which produces discrete nugget points, seam welding produces a **continuous gas-tight / liquid-tight joint**, making it indispensable for the manufacture of fuel tanks, radiators, refrigerator shells, and automobile exhaust systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have two pieces of dough and you want to seal their edges together to form a closed pouch (like a fluid container). One way is to take a hot iron rod and press it along the edge, melting the dough together progressively. As you slide the iron along, the seal forms continuously.

**Resistance Seam Welding does exactly the same thing — but instead of a hot iron rod, it uses two rotating metal wheels (roller electrodes) that:**

1. **Squeeze** the sheets together with mechanical force.
2. **Pass a heavy electric current** through the contact zone, causing the metal to heat up to a plastic state (≈ 0.8–0.9 of the melting temperature).
3. **Roll forward**, leaving behind a sealed, fused seam.

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Analogy:** Think of it as a **moving clothes iron** that seals fabric — except the "heat" comes from electricity resisting passage through metal (resistance heating), and the "iron" is a pair of rotating copper-alloy wheels.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Industry & KTU Reference)

| Parameter | Typical Range | Engineering Significance |
|-----------|--------------|---------------------------|
| Welding Current (I) | **5,000 – 20,000 A** | Low voltage (~2–10 V), high amperage |
| Electrode Force (F) | **1.5 – 10 kN** | Must exceed upsetting force to prevent expulsion |
| Welding Speed (v) | **0.5 – 5 m/min** | Depends on material thickness |
| Roll Diameter (D) | **50 – 300 mm** | Copper or Cu-Cd alloy |
| Material Thickness (t) | **0.5 – 3.0 mm** | Thin-gauge sheet metal |
| Frequency | **50 Hz (LF) or 1 kHz–10 kHz (HF)** | HF for stainless & high-resistivity alloys |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Heat Generation (The Welding Triangle)

Resistance seam welding operates on the simultaneous occurrence of three conditions. If any one of them is missing, no weld forms:

> **Pillar 1 — Current (I):** A low-voltage, high-amperage AC current flows through the workpieces.
> **Pillar 2 — Resistance (R):** The interface resistance (R_interface) and bulk resistance (R_bulk) oppose current flow.
> **Pillar 3 — Time (t):** Current flows for a finite duration as rollers traverse the joint.

These three combine via **Joule's First Law** to produce heat at the interface, raising local temperature until forging consolidation occurs.

## 2.2 Heat Generation Stages in Seam Welding (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Mechanical Contact Stage:** Roller electrodes compress sheets, reducing surface asperities and creating metallic contact points.
2. **Current Application Stage:** Welding current passes; heat is generated at the highest-resistance zone (the interface between sheets, not within the rollers).
3. **Plastic Deformation Stage:** The interface material reaches plastic state (~0.8 T_melting).
4. **Forging / Coalescence Stage:** Pressure causes atomic diffusion and grain growth across the interface → metallurgical bond forms.
5. **Cooling Stage (Under Pressure):** Rollers continue rolling; the molten/plastic nugget solidifies as a fused seam.

## 2.3 Why Heat Localizes at the Sheet Interface (The "Why" Insight)

The current path is: **Roller 1 → Sheet 1 → Sheet 1–Sheet 2 Interface → Sheet 2 → Roller 2.**

- **R_roller and R_bulk_sheet** are LOW (large cross-section, copper rollers).
- **R_interface** is HIGH (oxide layers, micro-gaps, asperity contact).

Since $H = I^2 R t$ and $I$ is **constant** through the series circuit, heat is concentrated where **R is highest** — the sheet interface. This is why the rollers remain relatively cool.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Notice:** All formulas below are directly examinable in KTU 2024 ESE for PBMRT404. Memorize symbols and units exactly.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Governing Equations of Resistance Seam Welding} \\
&\hline
&(1)\quad \text{Heat Generated:}\quad H \;=\; I^2 R t \quad [\text{Joules}] \\
&(2)\quad \text{Heat in calories:}\quad H \;=\; 0.24\, I^2 R t \quad [\text{cal}] \\
&(3)\quad \text{Electrical Power:}\quad P \;=\; I^2 R \;=\; V I \quad [\text{Watts}] \\
&(4)\quad \text{Welding Speed:}\quad v \;=\; \dfrac{\pi D N}{60} \quad [\text{m/s}] \\
&(5)\quad \text{Energy per unit length:}\quad E_{L} \;=\; \dfrac{V I}{v} \quad [\text{J/m}] \\
&(6)\quad \text{Interface Resistance:}\quad R_i \;=\; \rho_s \cdot \dfrac{L_i}{A_i} \quad [\Omega] \\
&(7)\quad \text{Electrode Force (Empirical):}\quad F \;\approx\; K \cdot t \quad [\text{N}] \\
&(8)\quad \text{Heat Balance:}\quad H_{\text{gen}} \;=\; H_{\text{useful}} + H_{\text{loss}} \\
&(9)\quad \text{Weldability Criterion:}\quad \text{Successful nugget} \iff I^2 R t \;\geq\; Q_{\text{required}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Symbol Legend (Exam-Friendly):**

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit |
|--------|----------|------|
| $I$ | Welding current | Ampere (A) |
| $R$ | Total resistance (interface + bulk) | Ohm ($\Omega$) |
| $t$ | Time of current flow | Second (s) |
| $V$ | Voltage across electrodes | Volt (V) |
| $D$ | Roller electrode diameter | m |
| $N$ | Roller rotational speed | RPM |
| $v$ | Welding (traverse) speed | m/s |
| $\rho_s$ | Sheet electrical resistivity | $\Omega \cdot m$ |
| $L_i$, $A_i$ | Interface length and area | m, m² |
| $K$ | Empirical force constant | N/mm |
| $t$ (formula 7) | Sheet thickness | mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formula 7 Note:** The empirical force equation $F = K \cdot t$ is widely used in KTU numerical problems. $K \approx 1500\text{–}3000 \text{ N/mm}$ for mild steel, depending on alloy and machine capacity.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Mass production of fuel tanks, oil sumps, mufflers, and air filters.
- **Domestic Appliances:** Washing-machine drums, refrigerator evaporators, and air-conditioner heat exchangers.
- **Aerospace:** Thin-wall titanium and stainless-steel ducts in jet engines.
- **Construction:** Roofing sheets and galvanized ductwork.
- **Packaging:** Tin-can bodies (historically the largest application — hence the term "tin can" line production).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Joule's Law to the Seam Welding Energy Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Mandate:** Every step is shown explicitly. No step is skipped. This is the exact logical chain expected in a 14-mark KTU question.

### Step 1 — Statement of Joule's First Law of Heating

When a steady current $I$ flows through a conductor of resistance $R$ for a time $t$, the heat dissipated in the conductor is:

$$
H \;\propto\; I^2 R t
$$

### Step 2 — Introduction of the Proportionality Constant

Experiments by James Prescott Joule established that the proportionality constant is exactly **1** when $H$ is measured in Joules, $I$ in Amperes, $R$ in Ohms, and $t$ in Seconds. Therefore:

$$
H \;=\; I^2 R t \quad \text{(in SI units)}
$$

### Step 3 — Conversion to Thermal Calories (Engineering Practice)

In older texts and certain KTU problem sets, heat is expressed in **calories**. The mechanical equivalent of heat gives:

$$
1 \text{ cal} \;=\; 4.186 \text{ J} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 1 \text{ J} \;=\; \frac{1}{4.186} \text{ cal} \;\approx\; 0.24 \text{ cal}
$$

Thus:

$$
H_{\text{cal}} \;=\; 0.24 \cdot I^2 R t
$$

### Step 4 — Expressing Power Dissipated at the Weld Interface

Power is energy per unit time. Dividing the heat equation by $t$:

$$
P \;=\; \dfrac{H}{t} \;=\; I^2 R \quad \text{[Watts]}
$$

Using Ohm's Law ($V = IR$), an equivalent form is:

$$
P \;=\; V I
$$

### Step 5 — Deriving the Welding Speed Relationship

The roller electrodes of diameter $D$ rotate at $N$ RPM. The linear distance covered by one full rotation is the circumference $\pi D$. The time for one rotation is $60/N$ minutes $= 3600/N$ seconds. Hence the **welding (traverse) speed** is:

$$
v \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Time}} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi D \cdot (N/60)}{1} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi D N}{60} \quad \text{[m/s if D in m]}
$$

### Step 6 — Deriving Energy per Unit Length of Seam

To compare processes fairly, engineers normalize energy by seam length. If the rollers advance a distance $\Delta x$ in time $\Delta t$, with $v = \Delta x / \Delta t$:

$$
E_L \;=\; \dfrac{P \cdot \Delta t}{\Delta x} \;=\; \dfrac{P}{v} \;=\; \dfrac{V I}{v} \quad \text{[J/m]}
$$

### Step 7 — The Heat Balance Equation (Energy Audit)

Not all generated heat forms the weld. Some is lost to:

- Conduction into the rollers and surrounding metal.
- Convection to ambient air.
- Radiation from the hot zone.

A complete energy audit gives:

$$
\underbrace{I^2 R t}_{\text{Heat generated}} \;=\; \underbrace{Q_{\text{nugget}}}_{\text{Useful weld heat}} \;+\; \underbrace{Q_{\text{loss, conduction}}}_{\text{To rollers}} \;+\; \underbrace{Q_{\text{loss, convection}}}_{\text{To air}} \;+\; \underbrace{Q_{\text{loss, radiation}}}_{\text{To environment}}
$$

In **well-tuned industrial seam welding**, the useful heat fraction is typically **60–80%**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Classification of Seam Welding Variants

| Variant | Current Application | Roller Motion | Characteristic | Typical Application |
|---------|---------------------|---------------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Continuous Drive Seam Welding** | Continuous (every cycle) | Rolls continuously, no stop | Solid continuous seam | Fuel tanks, drums |
| **Intermittent Drive Seam Welding** | Intermittent (per weld cycle) | Roll–stop–roll–stop | Discrete overlapping spots | Containers requiring controlled spacing |
| **Roll Spot Welding** | Single pulse per stop | Stops at each spot | Overlapping spot welds | Where spot-weld strength is sufficient |
| **Mash Seam Welding** | None (forge welding by pressure) | Rolls continuously | Weld formed purely by pressure, no fusion | Foil / thin gauges of compatible metals |
| **Foil Butt-Seam Welding** | Continuous | One roller, foil edge to sheet | Joins sheet to foil | Battery and capacitor manufacturing |
| **High-Frequency Seam Welding** | 10–500 kHz | Rolls continuously | Skin-effect localized heating | Stainless steel tubes, ERW pipes |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** Continuous drive seam welding is the most commonly asked variant. Be prepared to draw a **current vs. time graph** showing pulsed heating (on–off pattern) for **intermittent drive** as opposed to **continuous** uninterrupted heating.

## 3.3 Sample Numerical Walkthrough (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A continuous-drive resistance seam welding machine operates on 0.9 mm thick low-carbon steel sheets. The welding current is 8,000 A, interface resistance is $90 \,\mu\Omega$, roller diameter is 200 mm, and roller speed is 30 RPM. Calculate:
(a) Welding speed in m/min.
(b) Heat generated per minute.
(c) Power dissipated at the interface.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Welding Speed:**

$$
v \;=\; \dfrac{\pi D N}{60} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \times 0.2 \times 30}{60} \;=\; 0.3142 \text{ m/s} \;\times 60 \;=\; 18.85 \text{ m/min}
$$

**Part (b) — Heat Generated per Minute:**

For continuous-drive seam welding, current flows for the full minute (in real practice it is pulsed, but in idealized analysis $t = 60$ s).

$$
H \;=\; I^2 R t \;=\; (8000)^2 \times 90 \times 10^{-6} \times 60
$$

$$
H \;=\; 64 \times 10^{6} \times 90 \times 10^{-6} \times 60 \;=\; 64 \times 90 \times 60 \;=\; 345{,}600 \text{ J/min}
$$

**Part (c) — Power Dissipated:**

$$
P \;=\; \dfrac{H}{t} \;=\; \dfrac{345{,}600}{60} \;=\; 5{,}760 \text{ W} \;=\; 5.76 \text{ kW}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Physical Setup of Resistance Seam Welding

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[AC Power Supply Transformer] --> A2[Step Down to 2 to 10 V]
    A2 --> A3[High Amperage 5k to 20k A]
    A3 --> B1[Upper Roller Electrode Copper Alloy]
    B1 --> B2[Roller 1 rotates clockwise and applies downward force F]
    B2 --> C1[Workpiece Sheet 1 top sheet]
    C1 --> C2[Weld Interface Highest Resistance zone]
    C2 --> C3[Workpiece Sheet 2 bottom sheet]
    C3 --> B3[Lower Roller Electrode Copper Alloy]
    B3 --> B4[Roller 2 rotates counter clockwise and applies upward force F]
    B4 --> A1
    C2 --> D1[Heat Generated H equals I squared R t]
    D1 --> D2[Nugget Forms at Interface]
    D2 --> D3[Continuous Seam Produced as rollers advance]
    D3 --> E1[Finished Seam Weld]
```

```mermaid
    end
```

## 4.3 Current vs. Time Pulse Patterns (KTU Diagram Required in Exams)

```mermaid
        CD1[Current ON continuously]

        ID1[Current ON for short pulse]
        ID2[Rollers move during OFF period]

    CD1 --> CD2 --> CD3

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When drawing current vs. time graphs, mark:
> - **$t_w$** = welding time (current ON)
> - **$t_c$** = cooling / roll time (current OFF)
> - **Duty cycle** = $\dfrac{t_w}{t_w + t_c} \times 100\%$
