<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Short-Answer / Definition)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

> **Q1.** Define **Resistance Seam Welding**. List any **four** industrial products manufactured using this process.

**Model Answer:**

Resistance Seam Welding is a resistance welding process in which a continuous or intermittent overlapping weld is produced between two or more sheets of metal by passing a heavy electric current through the area of contact, while the sheets are held together under pressure between two **circular roller electrodes** that rotate and traverse along the joint.

**Four industrial products:**

1. Automobile fuel tanks.
2. Refrigerator evaporators and shells.
3. Washing-machine inner drums.
4. Radiator cores and tin cans.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall (–1 mark):** Students often confuse **seam welding** with **spot welding**. The key distinguishing feature is the use of **roller electrodes** (not stationary electrodes) producing a **continuous seam** (not isolated nuggets).

---

### Question 2 (Short-Answer / Concept)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **Q2.** State **Joule's Law of Heating** as applicable to resistance seam welding. Why does the maximum heat develop at the **sheet-to-sheet interface** and not at the roller-to-sheet contact?

**Model Answer:**

**Joule's Law:** The heat generated in a resistance welding process is given by

$$
H \;=\; I^2 R t
$$

where $H$ is heat in Joules, $I$ is current in Amperes, $R$ is resistance in Ohms, and $t$ is time in seconds.

**Why heat localizes at the interface:** In a series circuit, current is constant. Heat is proportional to resistance. The **roller-to-sheet contact** has low resistance because roller electrodes are made of high-conductivity copper alloys under high pressure (large metallic contact area). The **sheet-to-sheet interface** has high resistance due to oxide layers, micro-roughness, and small real contact area. Therefore, $H = I^2 R t$ is maximum where $R$ is maximum, i.e., at the interface.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall (–1 mark):** Writing "heat is generated due to current flow" without explicitly invoking $H = I^2 R t$ and the **series-circuit constancy of $I$** will lose marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

> **Q (A). (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **principle, working, and construction** of a resistance seam welding machine. Discuss the role of **roller electrodes** and the difference between **continuous drive** and **intermittent drive** seam welding. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q (A). (b)** A resistance seam welding operation is carried out on two **1.2 mm thick mild steel sheets**. The welding current is **10,000 A**, the interface resistance is **$80 \,\mu\Omega$**, the roller diameter is **250 mm**, and the rotational speed is **24 RPM**. Calculate the **(i) welding speed in m/min**, **(ii) heat generated per minute in kJ**, and **(iii) power dissipated at the interface in kW**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**1. Principle (2 marks):**
Resistance seam welding works on the principle of **Joule heating**. When a heavy current is passed through the joint between two sheets held under roller pressure, the interface resistance generates heat that raises the local temperature to the plastic state, causing coalescence and formation of a continuous seam.

**2. Construction (3 marks):**

- **Welding Transformer:** Steps down mains voltage (415 V, 3-phase) to 2–10 V while boosting current to 5,000–20,000 A.
- **Roller Electrodes:** Two circular wheels made of **Class 1 copper alloy** (Cu-Cd or Cu-Cr-Zr) for high conductivity and wear resistance. One is driven by a motor, the other is idler.
- **Pressure System:** Pneumatic or hydraulic cylinder providing 1.5–10 kN force.
- **Control Panel:** SCR/Ignitron-based switching, current timers, and heat-cool sequencing module.
- **Cooling System:** Water circulation through rollers to dissipate conductive heat loss.

**3. Working (1 mark):**
Sheets are fed between rollers. Current + pressure → heat → plastic deformation → roll forward → continuous fused seam.

**4. Continuous Drive vs. Intermittent Drive (1 mark):**

| Feature | Continuous Drive | Intermittent Drive |
|---------|------------------|---------------------|
| Current application | Continuous | Pulsed (ON-OFF) |
| Roller motion | Continuous rolling | Roll–stop–roll |
| Heat profile | Uniform, no overlap marks | Pulsed, with visible overlap pattern |
| Weld quality | Smooth, leak-tight | Slightly ribbed, strong but with periodicity |
| Application | Fuel tanks, drums | Containers, structural panels |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $I = 10{,}000 \text{ A}$
- $R = 80 \,\mu\Omega = 80 \times 10^{-6} \,\Omega$
- $D = 250 \text{ mm} = 0.25 \text{ m}$
- $N = 24 \text{ RPM}$
- $t = 60 \text{ s}$ (per minute)

**(i) Welding Speed (2 marks):**

$$
v \;=\; \dfrac{\pi D N}{60} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \times 0.25 \times 24}{60} \;=\; \dfrac{18.85}{60} \;=\; 0.3142 \text{ m/s} \;\times 60 \;=\; 18.85 \text{ m/min}
$$

**(ii) Heat Generated per Minute (3 marks):**

$$
H \;=\; I^2 R t \;=\; (10{,}000)^2 \times 80 \times 10^{-6} \times 60
$$

$$
H \;=\; 10^{8} \times 80 \times 10^{-6} \times 60 \;=\; 8000 \times 60 \;=\; 480{,}000 \text{ J} \;=\; 480 \text{ kJ}
$$

**(iii) Power Dissipated (2 marks):**

$$
P \;=\; \dfrac{H}{t} \;=\; \dfrac{480{,}000}{60} \;=\; 8{,}000 \text{ W} \;=\; 8 \text{ kW}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

> **Q (B). (a)** Explain the **heat generation mechanism** in resistance seam welding with reference to **Joule's Law**. Discuss the **heat balance** and identify the various **heat loss mechanisms** during seam welding. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q (B). (b)** List and explain the **different types of seam welding** with neat sketches. Compare the suitability of **continuous drive** vs. **mash seam welding** for **fuel tank** and **aluminium foil** applications respectively. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**1. Joule's Law Statement (2 marks):**

$$
H \;=\; I^2 R t
$$

Heat generated at the weld interface is proportional to the square of current, the resistance offered, and the time of current flow.

**2. Heat Generation Mechanism (2 marks):**
- Current passes from upper roller → top sheet → **interface** → bottom sheet → lower roller.
- The interface presents the highest resistance due to surface oxides, micro-asperities, and limited real contact area.
- Heat is concentrated at the interface because $H \propto R$ in a series circuit.
- This heat raises the local temperature to ~0.8–0.9 of melting point, making the material plastic.

**3. Heat Balance Equation (1.5 marks):**

$$
H_{\text{generated}} \;=\; H_{\text{nugget}} + H_{\text{conduction}} + H_{\text{convection}} + H_{\text{radiation}}
$$

**4. Heat Loss Mechanisms (1.5 marks):**
- **Conduction loss:** Heat flows into the cold copper rollers and adjacent cold parent metal. This is the **dominant loss** (~50–70%).
- **Convection loss:** Natural/forced air cooling at exposed hot surfaces (~5–10%).
- **Radiation loss:** Emitted from the hot nugget zone (~2–5%). Becomes significant at higher temperatures.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (–2 marks):** Students often state "**heat is lost to the surroundings**" without categorizing losses into conduction, convection, and radiation. Always **name the three modes explicitly** and indicate which one dominates.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**1. Types of Seam Welding (4 marks):**

| Type | Description |
|------|-------------|
| **Continuous Drive Seam Welding** | Current flows continuously; rollers rotate without stopping. Smooth, leak-tight seam. |
| **Intermittent Drive Seam Welding** | Current flows in pulses; rollers advance during OFF periods. Overlapping nugget pattern. |
| **Roll Spot Welding** | One pulse per roller position; resembles spot welds placed in a line. |
| **Mash Seam Welding** | No current is used. Weld forms purely by forge pressure between rollers, bonding compatible metals. |
| **Foil Butt-Seam Welding** | One roller bonds the edge of a sheet to the edge of a foil strip. |
| **High-Frequency Seam Welding** | Uses 10–500 kHz current; heat generated by skin-effect at surface. Used for tubes. |

**2. Application Comparison (3 marks):**

**For fuel tanks → Continuous Drive Seam Welding:**
- Requires **leak-tight** and **pressure-tight** joints.
- Continuous heating provides uniform, smooth, hermetic seam.
- Suitable for low-carbon steel sheets (0.8–1.5 mm).

**For aluminium foil pouch sealing → Mash Seam Welding:**
- Foil is too thin (typically 0.02–0.1 mm) to handle high welding current without burning.
- Currentless forge-pressure bonding prevents burn-through and arcing.
- Produces strong cold-pressure weld suitable for laminated foils used in food and pharmaceutical packaging.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's General Pitfall Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones (Consolidated):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** $R = 80 \,\mu\Omega$ must be written as $80 \times 10^{-6} \,\Omega$ before substitution. (–1 to –2 marks)
> 2. **Confusing "seam welding" with "spot welding":** Always emphasize **roller electrodes** and **continuous seam**.
> 3. **Stating "heat is produced by resistance"** without writing $H = I^2 R t$: Marks are reserved for the **formula**.
> 4. **Omitting heat losses:** A heat balance answer is **incomplete** without conduction, convection, and radiation breakdown.
> 5. **Drawing the seam welder sketch without labels:** Marks for diagrams require **labels on rollers, workpieces, transformer, pressure cylinder, and current direction arrows**.
> 6. **Mixing up mash vs. projection welding:** Mash seam = no current. Projection = current + projection point. This is a **favourite KTU trick question**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read before entering exam hall):**
>
> - ☐ **Definition:** Resistance seam welding = continuous/intermittent overlapping weld using **roller electrodes**, based on **Joule heating** ($H = I^2 R t$).
> - ☐ **Three pillars:** Current, Resistance, Time — all must coexist.
> - ☐ **Heat localization:** Maximum at sheet-sheet interface because $R_{\text{interface}} \gg R_{\text{roller-contact}}$.
> - ☐ **Roller material:** Class 1 copper alloy (Cu-Cd, Cu-Cr-Zr) for high conductivity + wear resistance.
> - ☐ **Welding speed formula:** $v = \pi D N / 60$ (m/s) or $\times 60$ for m/min.
> - ☐ **Power:** $P = I^2 R = VI$ (Watts).
> - ☐ **Heat per minute:** $H = I^2 R \times 60$ (Joules) — common numerical.
> - ☐ **Energy per unit length:** $E_L = V I / v$ (J/m).
> - ☐ **Types:** Continuous drive, Intermittent drive, Roll spot, Mash seam, Foil butt-seam, High-frequency.
> - ☐ **Mash seam** = no current, only pressure; for foils and compatible metals.
> - ☐ **Continuous drive** = current always ON; for fuel tanks and leak-tight containers.
> - ☐ **Applications:** Fuel tanks, refrigerators, radiators, drums, ERW pipes, foils.
> - ☐ **Heat losses:** Conduction (dominant) > Convection > Radiation.
> - ☐ **HF seam welding** uses skin effect for stainless and high-resistivity alloys.
> - ☐ **Electrode force** $F = K \cdot t$ (N), $K \approx 1500\text{–}3000$ N/mm for mild steel.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Brazing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT404 - MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Brazing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BRAZING (AWS / KTU Standard Definition)**
> **Brazing** is a *group of welding processes* in which coalescence (metallurgical joining) of metals is produced by heating them to a suitable temperature **above 450 °C (840 °F)** and using a **filler metal** whose liquidus temperature is **above 450 °C** and **below the solidus temperature of the base metals**. The filler metal is distributed between the closely fitted faying surfaces of the joint by **capillary attraction**.

**Key distinguishing temperature threshold:**

$$T_{\text{filler, liquidus}} > 450\,^\circ\text{C} \quad \text{AND} \quad T_{\text{filler, liquidus}} < T_{\text{base, solidus}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The American Welding Society (AWS) uses **450 °C** as the critical threshold that *separates* brazing from **soldering** (which is conducted below 450 °C). The base metal is *never melted* in brazing — only the filler metal melts and wets the substrate.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to stick two pieces of **glass together** using water. The water itself is not strong, but if you push the two glass pieces very close together (a narrow gap of a few hundredths of a millimeter), the water *automatically flows into the gap* without you pushing it. This automatic "suction-like" pulling of liquid into a narrow space is called **capillary action**.

**Brazing works exactly the same way:**

1. Two metal pieces are machined with a *very tight, precise gap* (typically **0.025 mm to 0.25 mm**).
2. A low-melting filler metal (like a "metal-glue" wire or paste) is placed at the joint entrance.
3. Heat is applied until the filler melts and is literally *sucked into the gap* by capillary forces.
4. On cooling, the solidified filler creates a strong, leak-proof metallic bond.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In brazing, *no base metal melts*. Only the filler metal melts. The heat is just enough to melt the glue, not the parts. This is fundamentally different from welding, where the base metal itself melts and fuses.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants (Highlighted)

| Metric | Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Defining temperature** | **450 °C (840 °F)** | Boundary between brazing and soldering (per AWS A3.0) |
| **Typical clearance (joint gap)** | **0.025 mm – 0.25 mm** | Optimum capillary action range |
| **Maximum clearance** | **≤ 0.6 mm** | Beyond this, capillary suction fails |
| **Filler metal melting range** | **450 °C – 1200 °C** | Common operating window |
| **Capillary rise height (h)** | $\frac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$ | Governs gap-filling ability |

## 1.4 Brazing vs. Soldering vs. Welding (At a Glance)

| Feature | **Brazing** | **Soldering** | **Welding (Fusion)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Filler liquidus temperature | > 450 °C | < 450 °C | — |
| Base metal melted? | **No** | **No** | **Yes** |
| Joining mechanism | Capillary action | Capillary action | Fusion of base metal |
| Joint strength | **High** | Low–Medium | **Highest** |
| Heat input | Moderate | Low | Very High |
| Typical filler | Cu, Ag, Al-Si, brass | Pb-Sn, Sn-Ag | Same as base metal |
| Distortion of base metal | Minimal | None | Significant |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Step Mechanism of Brazing (Operational Logic)

The entire brazing process is governed by **five sequential physical steps**. KTU examiners frequently test this:

1. **Cleaning of base metal surfaces** — Removal of oxides, oil, grease, and dirt. Contaminants block molten filler from wetting the surface.
2. **Flux application (or controlled atmosphere)** — Flux chemically dissolves residual oxides and prevents new oxide formation during heating.
3. **Assembly & Fixturing** — Parts are clamped with the **correct clearance (0.025–0.25 mm)** to allow capillary action.
4. **Heating to brazing temperature** — The assembly is heated uniformly until the filler metal melts (above its liquidus) but the base metal remains solid.
5. **Filler flow, solidification & cooling** — Capillary action draws the molten filler into the gap; on cooling, the joint solidifies and develops strength.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three 'Must-Haves' for a Sound Brazed Joint:**
> 1. **Wetting** — Filler must spread on the base metal (contact angle $\theta < 90°$).
> 2. **Capillarity** — Filler must flow into the gap (depends on clearance and surface tension).
> 3. **Adhesion + Diffusion** — Filler must metallurgically bond (atomic-level diffusion at interface).

## 2.2 The Physics of Capillary Action — The Heart of Brazing

Capillary rise in a parallel-plate joint is given by **Jurin's Law (modified):**

$$h = \frac{2\,\sigma_{\text{LV}} \cos\theta}{\rho\, g\, d}$$

where:
- $h$ = vertical capillary rise (m)
- $\sigma_{\text{LV}}$ = surface tension of liquid filler (N/m)
- $\theta$ = contact angle (wetting angle) between filler and base metal
- $\rho$ = density of liquid filler (kg/m³)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m/s²)
- $d$ = gap clearance (m)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** To maximize capillary flow:
> - Keep $\theta$ **low** (use flux + clean surface).
> - Keep $d$ **small but not zero** (optimum ≈ 0.05–0.1 mm).
> - Use a filler with **high surface tension** when fully molten.
> - Hold the joint in a **horizontal or slightly inclined** position so gravity assists, not fights, the flow.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbols | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{\text{filler, liquidus}} > 450\,^\circ\text{C}$ | — | °C | Defines brazing (vs soldering) | 
| 2 | $T_{\text{filler, liquidus}} < T_{\text{base, solidus}}$ | — | °C | Ensures base metal does not melt |
| 3 | $h = \dfrac{2\,\sigma_{\text{LV}} \cos\theta}{\rho\, g\, d}$ | $h,\sigma,\theta,\rho,d$ | m, N/m, rad, kg/m³, m | Capillary rise height |
| 4 | Optimum clearance | $0.025 \le d \le 0.25$ | mm | Joint design |
| 5 | Bond shear strength approx. | $\tau = \dfrac{F}{A_{\text{joint}}}$ | MPa | Strength check |
| 6 | Filler volume in joint | $V = L \times w \times d$ | mm³ | Filler quantity estimate |
| 7 | AWS BFM temp. classification | $\text{BAg-1 to BAg-8}$, $\text{BCuP}$, $\text{BCuZn}$ | — | Filler selection |
| 8 | Rule of thumb for joint strength | $70 - 90\%$ of base metal strength | — | Design limit |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Isolation Rule:** Notice that all mathematical absolute-value bars inside the tables above have been deliberately written using the spelled-out form (e.g. "above 450 °C" instead of $\vert T \vert$) to prevent Markdown table-parser breakage. Vertical pipes inside LaTeX are reserved for fraction/evaluative contexts only.

## 2.4 Why the Clearance Matters — Analytical Reasoning

A *very small* clearance (e.g. $d \to 0$) seems intuitively good, but in practice:
- It causes **starvation** (insufficient filler to fill the gap).
- It traps flux and gas, leading to **porosity** and **voids**.

A *very large* clearance (e.g. $d > 0.6$ mm):
- **Defeats capillarity** — the liquid filler no longer rises by capillary action.
- Creates a **weak joint** because the filler joint has lower strength than the base metal.

**Conclusion:** An *intermediate clearance* (0.025–0.25 mm) gives the strongest joint. This is a classic KTU short-answer / viva question.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Honeycomb sandwich panels, turbine blade repairs.
- **Automotive:** Radiators, air-conditioning condensers, carburetor assemblies.
- **Electronics:** Hermetic sealing of vacuum tubes, power semiconductor heat sinks.
- **Plumbing & HVAC:** Copper tube joints in refrigeration.
- **Tooling:** Carbide-tipped cutting tools (brazed steel shank + carbide insert).
- **Jewellery:** Joining precious metals without distortion.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Capillary Pressure in a Brazed Joint (The Driving Force)

The **capillary pressure** $P_c$ that drives molten filler into a narrow gap between two flat plates is derived from a force balance on the liquid meniscus.

### Step 1: Define the wetting geometry

Consider two parallel base-metal plates separated by a gap $d$. The molten filler forms a concave meniscus of radius $R$ with contact angle $\theta$ against the plate.

For a parallel-plate geometry, the meniscus radius is related to the gap:

$$R = \frac{d}{2\cos\theta}$$

### Step 2: Apply the Young–Laplace equation

The pressure difference across a curved liquid–vapour interface is given by the Young–Laplace equation (2-D form for parallel plates):

$$P_c = \frac{2\,\sigma_{\text{LV}} \cos\theta}{d}$$

where:
- $P_c$ = capillary pressure (Pa)
- $\sigma_{\text{LV}}$ = surface tension (N/m)
- $\theta$ = contact angle
- $d$ = gap (m)

### Step 3: Derive Jurin's Law for capillary rise

A column of liquid of height $h$ and density $\rho$ exerts a hydrostatic pressure at its base:

$$P_{\text{hydro}} = \rho\, g\, h$$

At equilibrium, capillary pressure equals hydrostatic pressure:

$$\frac{2\,\sigma_{\text{LV}} \cos\theta}{d} = \rho\, g\, h$$

Solving for $h$:

$$h = \frac{2\,\sigma_{\text{LV}} \cos\theta}{\rho\, g\, d}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the same expression quoted in §2.2. The key takeaway for a KTU exam is the **two formulae**: capillary *pressure* $P_c$ and capillary *rise* $h$. Memorize the form, not the memorization crutch.

### Step 4: Sample numerical evaluation

**Given:**
- Filler = $\text{BAg-1}$ (silver-copper-zinc brazing alloy)
- $\sigma_{\text{LV}} = 0.9$ N/m at $650\,^\circ\text{C}$
- $\theta = 25^\circ$
- $\rho = 8500$ kg/m³
- $d = 0.10$ mm $= 1\times 10^{-4}$ m
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Calculate capillary rise:**

$$\cos 25^\circ = 0.9063$$

$$h = \frac{2 \times 0.9 \times 0.9063}{8500 \times 9.81 \times 1\times 10^{-4}}$$

**Step-by-step arithmetic:**

$$\text{Numerator} = 2 \times 0.9 \times 0.9063 = 1.6313\;\text{N/m}$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 8500 \times 9.81 \times 1\times 10^{-4} = 8.3385\;\text{N/m}^2$$

$$h = \frac{1.6313}{8.3385} = 0.1956\;\text{m} \approx 195.6\;\text{mm}$$

**Capillary pressure:**

$$P_c = \frac{2 \times 0.9 \times 0.9063}{1\times 10^{-4}} = 16\,313\;\text{Pa} \approx 0.163\;\text{atm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A capillary pressure of ~0.16 atm is more than enough to "suck" the molten filler over ~20 cm of joint length. This explains why brazing works for long joints (e.g. copper refrigerant lines) without external pumping.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Selection Criteria for Brazing Process

| Selection Parameter | Torch Brazing | Furnace Brazing | Induction Brazing | Dip Brazing | Resistance Brazing |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Heat source | Gas flame | Resistance / gas | High-freq. induction | Molten salt bath | Electric current |
| Joint size | Small–medium | Small–large | Small–medium | Small–medium | Small |
| Production volume | Low–medium | **High** | Medium–high | High | Low–medium |
| Atmosphere | Air + flux | Air / inert / vacuum | Air + flux | Flux bath | Air + flux |
| Skill required | **High** | Low | Medium | Low | Medium |
| Distortion risk | Medium | **Low** | Low | Low | Low |
| Typical filler | BAg, BCuP | BCu, BNi, BAg | BAg | BAl, BAg | BCu |
| Cost | Low | **High (capital)** | Medium | Medium | Low |

## 3.3 AWS Filler Metal Classification (High-Yield for KTU)

The **American Welding Society (AWS) A5.8** specification classifies brazing filler metals (BFM) by a letter-number code:

| Prefix | Filler Family | Typical Liquidus (°C) | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BAg** | Silver-based alloys | 600 – 900 | General purpose, food-grade, vacuum |
| **BCu** | Copper-based alloys | 1080 – 1150 | Steel, carbide, furnace brazing |
| **BCuP** | Copper–Phosphorus | 700 – 850 | Copper-to-copper (no flux needed) |
| **BNi** | Nickel-based alloys | 900 – 1200 | High-temp aerospace, vacuum |
| **BAlSi** | Aluminum–Silicon | 575 – 640 | Aluminum joining |
| **BMg** | Magnesium-based | 445 – 605 | Magnesium alloys |
| **BCuZn** | Brass / Bronze | 870 – 1030 | Steel, lower-melting filler |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BCuP Trivia (KTU favourite):** Copper–phosphorus fillers are **self-fluxing** on copper because phosphorus reduces the copper oxide to pure copper, eliminating the need for an additional flux. **However**, BCuP fillers should *not* be used on ferrous metals or copper alloys containing > 10% nickel — this is a common KTU MCQ trap.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define brazing. How is it distinguished from soldering based on temperature?**

**Model Answer:**

> Brazing is a *welding process* in which a filler metal is heated to a temperature **above 450 °C** and distributed between the closely fitted faying surfaces of the joint by **capillary action**, *without melting the base metals*.

The **defining temperature threshold of 450 °C** is the AWS-specified boundary:

- **Brazing** → filler metal liquidus is **> 450 °C**
- **Soldering** → filler metal liquidus is **< 450 °C**

In both processes the base metal remains solid; the only difference is the *temperature regime* of the filler metal, which in turn dictates filler composition, joint strength, and operating environment.

*(3 marks: Definition 1 m, 450 °C threshold 1 m, key distinction 1 m)*

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the role of flux in brazing? Name any two commonly used fluxes.**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of flux:**
1. **Dissolves and removes** existing oxides from the base metal surface.
2. **Prevents re-oxidation** by forming a protective liquid blanket over the joint during heating.
3. **Reduces surface tension** of the molten filler, improving wetting and capillary flow.

**Two common fluxes:**
- **Borax (sodium tetraborate, Na₂B₄O₇)** — used for brass and bronze fillers above 800 °C.
- **Boric acid (H₃BO₃)** — used with silver brazing fillers in the 600–900 °C range.
- *(Also acceptable: potassium fluoroborate for stainless steel; fluoride-based flux for aluminium.)*

*(3 marks: Role 2 m, Two examples 1 m)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]

**Answer the following:**

**(a)** Explain the mechanism of capillary action in brazing. Derive the expression for capillary rise in a parallel-plate joint. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **AWS classification of brazing filler metals**. List any **four families** with their typical compositions and applications. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — Capillary Mechanism and Derivation (7 marks)

**Mechanism of capillary action (4 marks):**

1. The base metals are prepared with a **very narrow, uniform clearance** (typically 0.025–0.25 mm).
2. On heating, the **filler metal melts** and is placed at the joint entrance.
3. Because the gap is narrow, the molten filler forms a **concave meniscus** with a contact angle $\theta < 90°$.
4. The curvature of this meniscus creates a **pressure difference** across the liquid–vapour interface, which **sucks the molten filler** into the gap.
5. The filler flows against gravity (or with it) until the **capillary pressure** equals the **hydrostatic back-pressure**.
6. On cooling, the filler solidifies to form a continuous, dense, metallurgically bonded joint.

**Derivation of capillary rise (3 marks):**

A concave meniscus between two parallel plates of separation $d$ has radius of curvature $R$, where geometrically:

$$R = \frac{d}{2\cos\theta}$$

The Young–Laplace pressure difference across the curved interface is:

$$P_c = \frac{2\,\sigma_{\text{LV}} \cos\theta}{d}$$

This capillary pressure must balance the hydrostatic pressure of a liquid column of height $h$:

$$P_c = \rho\, g\, h$$

Equating and solving for $h$:

$$h = \frac{2\,\sigma_{\text{LV}} \cos\theta}{\rho\, g\, d}$$

> This is the **driving force** of brazing. Higher $\sigma$ and lower $\theta$ and smaller $d$ all *increase* the rise, ensuring complete gap-filling.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — AWS Filler Metal Classification (7 marks)

The **American Welding Society (AWS A5.8)** classifies brazing filler metals (BFMs) using a **two-letter prefix code** denoting the base element, followed by a numerical composition identifier.

| AWS Code | Family | Key Composition (approx.) | Typical Liquidus | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **BAg** | Silver-based | Ag 30 – 60 %, Cu 20 – 40 %, Zn 10 – 30 % | 600 – 900 °C | General purpose, food-grade, vacuum |
| **BCuP** | Cu–P alloy | Cu 92 – 95 %, P 5 – 8 % | 700 – 850 °C | Copper-to-copper joints (self-fluxing) |
| **BNi** | Nickel-based | Ni 70 – 95 %, Cr, B, Si, P | 900 – 1200 °C | Aerospace, jet engine, vacuum |
| **BAlSi** | Aluminium–silicon | Al 88 – 92 %, Si 8 – 12 % | 575 – 640 °C | Joining aluminium alloys |
| **BCuZn** | Brass / Bronze | Cu 50 – 60 %, Zn 40 – 50 % | 870 – 1030 °C | Steel, lower-cost assemblies |
| **BMg** | Magnesium-based | Mg 88 – 95 %, Al, Zn | 445 – 605 °C | Magnesium alloy parts |

**Key features:**
- **Selection** is based on: (1) base metal compatibility, (2) service temperature, (3) corrosion resistance, (4) joint clearance, (5) heating method.
- **BCuP family is unique** in that it is *self-fluxing* on pure copper because phosphorus chemically reduces CuO.
- **BNi fillers** are used in *vacuum brazing* of aerospace parts where flux residue is unacceptable.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]

**Answer the following:**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **torch brazing process**. List its **advantages and limitations**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare **brazing, soldering, and fusion welding** in terms of (i) base metal melting, (ii) filler metal temperature, (iii) joint strength, (iv) heat input, and (v) typical applications. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — Torch Brazing (7 marks)

**Process description (4 marks):**

Torch brazing is the **most common manual brazing process**. A fuel-gas flame (oxy-acetylene, oxy-propane, or air–natural gas) is used to heat the joint area locally.

**Procedure:**
1. **Clean** the faying surfaces mechanically (grit blasting, wire brushing) and chemically (degreasing).
2. **Apply flux** to both surfaces to dissolve oxides and prevent re-oxidation.
3. **Assemble** parts with the correct clearance (0.025–0.25 mm).
4. **Heat** the joint with the torch flame — the flame should be *neutral* or slightly *reducing* to avoid oxidising the base metal.
5. **Touch the filler rod** to the joint — when the base metal is hot enough, the filler melts and is drawn in by capillary action.
6. **Cool** the assembly slowly (avoid water quenching — it can crack the joint).

**Sketch (described textually — figure expected in exam):** Two pipe sections butted together; torch flame impinging on joint; filler rod fed at joint entrance; flux visible as white paste; arrows indicating capillary flow of filler into the gap.

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- Low capital cost; portable equipment.
- Suitable for **field repairs** and small-batch production.
- Works on a wide variety of metals and joint geometries.

**Limitations (1.5 marks):**
- **Operator-skill dependent** — uneven heating causes weak joints.
- Difficult to control on **complex multi-joint** assemblies.
- **Flux residue** must be removed after brazing (corrosive).
- Not suitable for *mass production* — slower than furnace brazing.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — Comparative Table (7 marks)

| S.No. | Parameter | **Brazing** | **Soldering** | **Fusion Welding** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Base metal melting | **No** (base solidus > filler liquidus) | **No** | **Yes** (base metal melts) |
| 2 | Filler metal temperature | **> 450 °C** | **< 450 °C** | Equal to base melting point |
| 3 | Typical joint strength | High (70–90 % of base) | Low–Medium (30–60 % of base) | **Highest** (≥ base metal) |
| 4 | Heat input | Moderate | **Low** | **Very high** |
| 5 | Distortion of base | **Minimal** | Negligible | **Significant** (residual stresses) |
| 6 | Typical fillers | BAg, BCuP, BNi, BAlSi | Pb–Sn, Sn–Ag | Same as base metal |
| 7 | Mechanism | Capillary action | Capillary action | **Fusion** + epitaxial grain growth |
| 8 | Typical applications | Radiators, carbide tools, aerospace | Electronics PCB joints | Structural beams, pressure vessels, pipelines |
| 9 | Equipment cost | Low–Medium | Low | **High** |
| 10 | Skill level required | Medium | Low–Medium | High (certified welder) |

> **Key conclusion (1 mark):** Brazing is *intermediate* between soldering and fusion welding — it gives high strength without the heat-input penalty of welding, making it ideal for **dissimilar metal joints** and **mass production** of leak-tight assemblies.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 450 °C threshold.** Many students write "brazing is above 600 °C" — partial credit is *lost*. Memorize exactly **450 °C (840 °F)**.
> 2. **Stating that base metal melts in brazing.** This is *incorrect* and is a guaranteed 1–2 mark cut. The base metal must remain solid.
> 3. **Confusing BCuP with BCu.** BCuP = *Copper–Phosphorus* (self-fluxing on copper, no flux needed). BCu = pure copper filler (furnace brazing of steel). They are *not* the same.
> 4. **Omitting the derivation step in Q.A(a).** Writing the formula $h = \frac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g d}$ without showing the Young–Laplace balance loses 2 of the 3 derivation marks.
> 5. **Sketch quality.** In torch brazing, students often forget to **label the flux, filler rod, and indicate capillary flow direction** with arrows — these are explicit marking points in the KTU answer key.
> 6. **AWS codes.** Writing "Brazing alloy 1" instead of **"BAg-1"** (with the dash and number) loses a half-mark on the classification question.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Brazing (Module 2):**
> - **Definition:** Filler metal > 450 °C, base metal not melted, joining by **capillary action**.
> - **AWS boundary:** Brazing > 450 °C, Soldering < 450 °C.
> - **Capillary rise formula:** $h = \frac{2\sigma_{\text{LV}} \cos\theta}{\rho g d}$ (Jurin's Law, parallel plates).
> - **Capillary pressure formula:** $P_c = \frac{2\sigma_{\text{LV}} \cos\theta}{d}$ (Young–Laplace).
> - **Optimum clearance:** **0.025 – 0.25 mm** (too small = porosity, too large = no capillarity).
> - **Three must-haves:** Wetting ($\theta < 90°$) + Capillarity + Adhesion/Diffusion.
> - **Filler families (AWS A5.8):** BAg, BCu, BCuP, BNi, BAlSi, BCuZn, BMg.
> - **BCuP = self-fluxing on copper** (unique property — common MCQ).
> - **Flux functions:** (1) dissolve oxides, (2) prevent re-oxidation, (3) aid wetting.
> - **Process variants:** Torch, Furnace, Induction, Dip, Resistance, Vacuum.
> - **Applications:** Aerospace, automotive radiators, carbide tools, HVAC, electronics.
> - **Joint strength:** Typically **70 – 90 %** of base metal.
> - **Advantage vs. welding:** Lower heat input → less distortion, dissimilar metals possible.
> - **Advantage vs. soldering:** Higher temperature → stronger, higher service temperature.
> - **For B.Tech viva:** "Why is narrow clearance important?" → controls capillary rise and prevents porosity; "Why flux?" → dissolves oxides, prevents re-oxidation; "Why not welding?" → base metal doesn't melt, so dissimilar metals with different melting points can be joined.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT404 - MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Soldering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Soldering** is a non-fusion metal-joining process in which two or more metal parts are united by means of a **filler metal (solder)** whose melting point is **below 450°C (840°F)** and well below the melting point of the base metals being joined. The filler metal is melted and drawn into the joint by **capillary action**, wetting the base-metal surfaces without melting them, and subsequently solidifies to form a permanent mechanical and electrical bond.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Threshold (High-Yield):**
> - **Soldering** → Filler metal melts **below 450°C (840°F)**
> - **Brazing** → Filler metal melts **above 450°C** but below 850°C
> - **Welding** → Base metal itself is melted (fusion)

The filler metal used in soldering is called **solder** (derived from the Latin *solidare* – "to make solid"). Common solders include **tin-lead (Sn-Pb)** alloys and modern **lead-free** variants like **SAC (Tin-Silver-Copper)**.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy – "The Glue Stick for Metals"**
> Imagine joining two pieces of paper using a hot glue stick. The glue melts at a low temperature, spreads between the papers, and solidifies to hold them. The paper does not melt. Soldering is conceptually identical, but the "paper" is replaced with copper, brass, or steel, and the "glue stick" is replaced with a metallic alloy that melts at low temperature. Just as glue relies on surface adhesion, solder relies on **metallurgical wetting** and **capillary action**.

## 1.3 Critical Physical Constants & Standards

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Soldering temperature threshold | **< 450°C (840°F)** | Distinguishes soldering from brazing |
| Common Sn-Pb solder melting point | **183°C – 214°C** | Eutectic Sn63/Pb37 = **183°C** |
| Lead-free SAC305 melting point | **217°C – 220°C** | RoHS compliant standard |
| Wetting contact angle (ideal) | **< 30°** | Indicates good wetting |
| Typical working temperature | **30°C – 50°C** above solder liquidus | Ensures proper flow |

> [!TIP]
> The **Eutectic Composition (63% Sn / 37% Pb)** is critical because it transitions directly from solid to liquid at a single temperature (**183°C**) without a pasty range – the KTU examiner loves this fact.

## 1.4 Scope of Soldering in Manufacturing

- **Electronics Manufacturing**: PCB assembly (95% of all electronic joints are soldered)
- **Plumbing & Sheet Metal**: Joining copper pipes, sheet metal seams
- **Automotive**: Radiators, wiring harnesses
- **Aerospace & Defense**: Critical avionics assemblies
- **Jewelry & Cutlery**: Decorative and functional low-temperature joining

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Soldering Mechanism – The "Why" Behind the Bond

A successful solder joint is not merely a mechanical sticking; it is a **metallurgical bond** formed by several sequential phenomena:

### Step 1: Surface Preparation
The base metal surfaces are cleaned mechanically (abrasion) and chemically (flux) to remove **oxides, grease, and contaminants**. Oxides prevent wetting and must be displaced.

### Step 2: Heating
Heat is applied via soldering iron, hot plate, torch, or reflow oven. The base metal is heated to a temperature **above the solder's liquidus** so that on contact, the solder instantly melts.

### Step 3: Wetting
The molten solder spreads over the heated base-metal surface, replacing oxides and forming intimate atomic contact. **Wetting is quantified by the contact angle (θ)**:

> **Wetting Criterion**: $\theta < 90°$ = Good wetting; $\theta < 30°$ = Excellent wetting.

### Step 4: Capillary Action (Interfacial Flow)
Molten solder flows into the narrow joint gap due to **capillary action**. The driving force is the surface tension of the molten solder acting across the joint clearance.

> The classic capillary-rise height equation is:

$$
h = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}
$$

where:
- $h$ = capillary rise height
- $\gamma$ = surface tension of molten solder
- $\theta$ = contact angle
- $\rho$ = density of molten solder
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration
- $r$ = radius of joint gap (capillary radius)

### Step 5: Intermetallic Compound (IMC) Formation
At the atomic scale, tin from the solder diffuses into the base metal, forming a thin layer of **Intermetallic Compounds (IMCs)** such as $Cu_6Sn_5$ and $Cu_3Sn$ (when soldering copper). This IMC layer is what gives the joint its **metallurgical strength**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A thin, continuous IMC layer = strong joint. A thick, brittle IMC layer = weak joint. Excessive heating time must be avoided to prevent IMC overgrowth.

### Step 6: Solidification & Cooling
The assembly is allowed to cool. The solder solidifies, locking the base metals together. Cooling should be **gradual but not prolonged** to avoid thermal shock or large grain structures.

## 2.2 Role of Flux – The Silent Hero

**Flux** is a chemical cleaning agent that is essential for a successful solder joint. Its four-fold role is:

1. **Removes oxides** from the base metal by chemical reduction
2. **Prevents re-oxidation** during heating by forming a protective barrier
3. **Improves wetting** by lowering the surface tension of molten solder
4. **Transfers heat** uniformly across the joint

### Types of Flux (KTU High-Yield)

| Flux Type | Active Agent | Application | Corrosive? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rosin (R, RMA, RA)** | Natural pine resin | Electronics | Mild |
| **Water-soluble (organic)** | Organic acids (citric, lactic) | Industrial soldering | Yes (must be cleaned) |
| **No-clean** | Synthetic resins | Modern electronics | Negligible |
| **Inorganic (acid)** | Zinc chloride, HCl | Plumbing, sheet metal | Highly corrosive |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic**: "**R**osin is for **R**adios" – rosin flux is the original standard for electronics, derived from pine tree sap.

## 2.3 Classification of Solders

### A. Based on Composition

| Solder Type | Composition | Melting Point | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tin-Lead (Sn-Pb)** | 63/37 or 60/40 | **183°C – 214°C** | General electronics (legacy) |
| **Lead-free (SAC)** | Sn96.5/Ag3/Cu0.5 | **217°C** | RoHS-compliant electronics |
| **Silver solder (hard)** | Sn-Ag eutectic | **221°C** | Higher-strength joints |
| **Bismuth-based** | Sn-Bi | **138°C** | Low-temperature, heat-sensitive |
| **Indium-based** | In-Sn | **118°C** | Cryogenic, vacuum sealing |

### B. Based on Form

- **Wire solder** (with rosin core) – hand soldering
- **Paste solder** (Solder Paste: solder powder + flux) – reflow/SMT
- **Bar solder** – wave soldering pots
- **Preforms** – pre-cut shapes for automated placement

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = m \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | Heat required to raise base metal temperature | Joules (J) |
| 2 | $Q = m \, L_f$ | Latent heat for solder solidification | J |
| 3 | $h = \frac{2\gamma\cos\theta}{\rho g r}$ | Capillary rise of molten solder in joint | meters (m) |
| 4 | $\tau = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Shear strength of solder joint | $\text{N/mm}^2$ (MPa) |
| 5 | $P_{\text{joint}} = \tau \cdot A$ | Load-bearing capacity of a joint | N |
| 6 | $\Delta T = T_{\text{iron}} - T_{\text{liquidus}}$ | Working temperature differential | °C or K |
| 7 | $\text{Peel Strength} = \dfrac{F_{\text{peel}}}{w}$ | Force per unit width for peel test | N/mm |
| 8 | $\theta_c = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\gamma_{sv}-\gamma_{sl}}{\gamma_{lv}}\right)$ | Young's equation (contact angle) | degrees |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight (KTU favourite)**: The **capillary effect** is strongest when joint clearance is between **0.05 mm and 0.20 mm**. Too tight = no flow; too wide = weak fillet.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Soldering

- **Electrical conductivity** preserved across the joint (solder is conductive)
- **Hermetic sealing** for enclosures and connectors
- **Repairability** – joints can be desoldered and reworked
- **Low thermal distortion** of base metals (critical in PCB assemblies)
- **Cost-effectiveness** for mass production (wave/reflow soldering)

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation 1: Heat Energy Required for a Soldering Operation

When soldering a copper wire of mass $m$ to a copper pad, the total heat $Q_{\text{total}}$ required consists of:
- $Q_1$: Sensible heat to raise the base metal to soldering temperature
- $Q_2$: Latent heat to melt the solder
- $Q_3$: Heat losses to the environment

### Derivation

**Step 1: Sensible Heat to Raise Base Metal**

$$
Q_1 = m_{\text{base}} \cdot c_{p,\text{base}} \cdot (T_{\text{working}} - T_{\text{ambient}})
$$

**Step 2: Latent Heat to Melt Solder**

$$
Q_2 = m_{\text{solder}} \cdot L_f
$$

**Step 3: Total Useful Heat**

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = Q_1 + Q_2
$$

**Step 4: Practical Heat Supplied (Accounting for Losses)**

$$
Q_{\text{supplied}} = \frac{Q_{\text{total}}}{\eta_{\text{iron}}}
$$

where $\eta_{\text{iron}}$ is the **thermal efficiency** of the soldering iron tip (typically 0.6 to 0.85 for a good station).

### Numerical Example (Worked)

**Given:**
- Copper wire mass: $m_{\text{base}} = 10 \text{ g} = 0.010 \text{ kg}$
- Specific heat of copper: $c_p = 0.385 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- Ambient temp: $T_{\text{amb}} = 25°C$
- Working temp: $T_{\text{work}} = 240°C$ (for Sn-Pb solder)
- Solder mass: $m_{\text{solder}} = 0.5 \text{ g} = 0.0005 \text{ kg}$
- Latent heat of fusion of solder: $L_f = 59 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- Iron efficiency: $\eta = 0.75$

**Calculate $Q_1$:**

$$
Q_1 = 0.010 \times 0.385 \times (240 - 25)
$$

$$
Q_1 = 0.010 \times 0.385 \times 215
$$

$$
Q_1 = 0.8278 \text{ kJ} = 827.8 \text{ J}
$$

**Calculate $Q_2$:**

$$
Q_2 = 0.0005 \times 59 = 0.0295 \text{ kJ} = 29.5 \text{ J}
$$

**Calculate Total Useful Heat:**

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = 827.8 + 29.5 = 857.3 \text{ J}
$$

**Calculate Actual Heat to be Supplied:**

$$
Q_{\text{supplied}} = \frac{857.3}{0.75} = 1143.07 \text{ J} \approx 1.143 \text{ kJ}
$$

> This calculation justifies why soldering irons are rated in **watts** (a 40 W iron delivers 40 J/s, so this joint needs $\approx 28.6$ s of contact time in theory).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation 2: Shear Strength of a Solder Joint (Lap Joint)

A **lap joint** is the most common solder joint geometry in electronics.

### Derivation

**Step 1: Define Joint Geometry**
- Pad area on PCB: $A = L \times W$
- Lap length: $L$, Width: $W$

**Step 2: Shear Stress Equation**

$$
\tau = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{F}{L \cdot W}
$$

**Step 3: Maximum Load Before Failure**

$$
F_{\max} = \tau_{\text{ult}} \cdot A = \tau_{\text{ult}} \cdot L \cdot W
$$

**Step 4: Safety-Loaded Design**

$$
F_{\text{design}} = \frac{\tau_{\text{ult}} \cdot A}{n}
$$

where $n$ is the **factor of safety** (typically 2 to 4 for solder joints).

### Numerical Example

**Given:**
- Lap joint: $L = 5 \text{ mm}$, $W = 3 \text{ mm}$
- Ultimate shear strength of Sn63/Pb37 solder: $\tau_{\text{ult}} = 38 \text{ MPa}$
- Factor of safety: $n = 3$

**Calculate Area:**

$$
A = 5 \times 3 = 15 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Calculate Maximum Load:**

$$
F_{\max} = 38 \times 15 = 570 \text{ N}
$$

**Calculate Safe Design Load:**

$$
F_{\text{design}} = \frac{570}{3} = 190 \text{ N}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation 3: Capillary Flow in a Solder Joint

### Derivation (from Young-Laplace)

**Step 1: Pressure Across a Curved Liquid Interface**

$$
\Delta P = \frac{2 \gamma}{r}
$$

**Step 2: Driving Pressure for Capillary Flow**

$$
\Delta P_{\text{cap}} = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r}
$$

**Step 3: Volumetric Flow Rate (Hagen-Poiseuille for narrow gaps)**

$$
Q = \frac{\pi r^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L}
$$

**Step 4: Time to Fill Joint**

$$
t_{\text{fill}} = \frac{V_{\text{joint}}}{Q} = \frac{8 \mu L^2 V_{\text{joint}}}{\pi r^4 \cdot 2\gamma\cos\theta}
$$

> This shows that **gap radius (r)** has a **fourth-power** effect on fill time – halving the gap makes filling **16× longer**. This is why KTU examiners emphasize optimum joint clearance.

---

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix: Soldering vs Brazing vs Welding

| Parameter | Soldering | Brazing | Welding |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Filler MP** | < 450°C | 450°C – 850°C | Base metal MP |
| **Base metal melts?** | No | No | **Yes** |
| **Joint strength** | Low – Medium | Medium – High | Highest (parent metal) |
| **Heat source** | Iron, torch, oven | Torch, furnace, induction | Arc, laser, gas, resistance |
| **Flux needed?** | Yes | Yes | No (usually) |
| **Typical applications** | Electronics, plumbing | Refrigeration, hard metals | Structural, pressure vessels |
| **Metallurgical bond?** | Yes (IMC) | Yes (diffusion) | Yes (fusion) |
| **Distortion** | Minimal | Low | Significant |
| **Reversibility** | Easy (desoldering) | Difficult | Very difficult |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define soldering. State the temperature threshold that distinguishes soldering from brazing.**

**Model Answer:**

Soldering is a joining process in which two or more metal parts are united by a filler metal (solder) that has a melting point **below 450°C (840°F)**. The base metals are not melted; the molten solder is drawn into the joint by capillary action and wets the base metal surfaces before solidifying.

The **temperature threshold** distinguishing the two processes is **450°C (840°F)**:
- **Soldering** → Filler metal melts **below 450°C**
- **Brazing** → Filler metal melts **above 450°C** but below 850°C

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall**: Many students write "below 500°C" or "below 600°C" – this is wrong. The universally accepted ISO/AWS threshold is **450°C**. Memorize this value precisely.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**What is the role of flux in soldering? Mention any two types of flux used.**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Flux:**
1. **Removes oxides** from the base metal by chemical reduction.
2. **Prevents re-oxidation** during heating by forming a protective liquid barrier over the joint.
3. **Improves wetting** of the solder on the base metal.
4. **Aids in uniform heat transfer** across the joint area.

**Two Types of Flux:**
1. **Rosin Flux** – Derived from pine resin; mildly active; widely used in electronics soldering (e.g., RMA – Rosin Mildly Activated).
2. **Inorganic Acid Flux** – Contains zinc chloride or hydrochloric acid; highly active and corrosive; used in plumbing and sheet-metal soldering.

---

## PART B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the soldering process with a neat block diagram. List the different types of solders used in manufacturing.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**The Soldering Process – Step-by-Step Explanation:**

The soldering process consists of the following sequential operations:

1. **Surface Preparation**: The surfaces of the base metals to be joined are cleaned mechanically using emery paper, wire brush, or chemically using solvents to remove dirt, grease, and oxide films.

2. **Flux Application**: A suitable flux is applied to the cleaned surfaces. The flux chemically dissolves residual oxides and prevents re-oxidation during heating.

3. **Assembly and Fixturing**: The components are positioned in their final geometry and held using jigs or fixtures to prevent movement during the soldering process.

4. **Heating**: Heat is applied to the joint area using a soldering iron, hot plate, torch, or reflow oven until the base metal reaches a temperature above the liquidus of the solder.

5. **Solder Application**: Solder (in wire, paste, or preform form) is brought into contact with the heated joint. It melts, wets the base metal (contact angle less than 30°), and flows into the joint gap by capillary action.

6. **Joint Formation**: At the microscopic level, tin from the solder diffuses into the base metal forming a thin layer of Intermetallic Compounds (IMCs) such as $Cu_6Sn_5$ and $Cu_3Sn$. This metallurgical bond is the source of joint strength.

7. **Cooling and Solidification**: Heat is removed, and the solder solidifies, locking the joint. Movement during solidification causes a "cold joint" and must be avoided.

8. **Cleaning and Inspection**: Flux residue is cleaned, and the joint is inspected visually and electrically.

**Block Diagram of the Process:**

```
[Base Metal 1] + [Base Metal 2]
            |
            v
  [Step 1: Surface Cleaning]
            |
            v
   [Step 2: Flux Application]
            |
            v
   [Step 3: Positioning & Clamping]
            |
            v
   [Step 4: Heat Application]
            |
            v
   [Step 5: Solder Application & Melting]
            |
            v
  [Step 6: Capillary Flow & Wetting]
            |
            v
   [Step 7: Solidification & Bonding]
            |
            v
   [Step 8: Cleaning & Inspection]
            |
            v
       [Finished Solder Joint]
```

**Types of Solders Used in Manufacturing:**

| Category | Type | Composition | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tin-Lead** | Eutectic | 63% Sn / 37% Pb | General electronics |
| **Tin-Lead** | Non-eutectic | 60% Sn / 40% Pb | Cost-effective, broader pasty range |
| **Lead-free** | SAC alloy | Sn-Ag-Cu | RoHS-compliant modern electronics |
| **Silver solder** | Hard solder | Sn-Ag (96.5/3.5) | Higher strength, higher temp |
| **Bismuth solder** | Low-temp | Sn-Bi (42/58) | Heat-sensitive components |
| **Indium solder** | Cryogenic | In-Sn (52/48) | Cryogenic sealing, vacuum |
| **Antimony solder** | Plumbing | Pb-Sb | Pipe joining, sheet metal |

---

**(b) Explain the working of a soldering iron with a neat diagram. List the different types of soldering techniques.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Working of a Soldering Iron:**

A soldering iron is a handheld tool used to apply heat to a solder joint. Its main components are:

1. **Tip (Copper core with iron plating)**: The working end that contacts the joint. It must be **tinned** (coated with a thin film of solder) to ensure proper heat transfer.

2. **Heating Element**: Either a **nichrome wire** (in low-cost irons) or a **ceramic PTC element** (in temperature-controlled stations) that converts electrical energy into heat.

3. **Thermal Sensor (Thermocouple)**: Senses tip temperature and feeds back to the controller (in soldering stations).

4. **Barrel / Housing**: A metal or plastic body that holds the heating element and provides electrical insulation.

5. **Handle**: Heat-insulated grip, usually made of silicone rubber or high-temperature plastic.

6. **Power Cord / Connector**: Connects to a mains supply (typically 20–80 W for hand soldering).

**Working Principle:**
When the iron is switched on, the heating element raises the tip temperature to a set point (typically 200–450°C). The user then touches the tip to the joint area, transferring heat through **conduction**. After 1–3 seconds, solder wire is fed into the joint. The molten solder flows, wets, and solidifies to form the joint.

**Block Diagram of a Soldering Iron:**

```
        [Mains Power Supply 230V AC]
                  |
                  v
        [Step-Down Transformer if needed]
                  |
                  v
        [Temperature Controller PID]
                  |
                  v
        [Heating Element Nichrome or Ceramic]
                  |
                  v
        [Heat Conduction to Tip]
                  |
                  v
        [Soldering Tip Tinned Copper with Iron Plating]
                  |
                  v
        [Contact with Workpiece Joint]
                  |
                  v
        [Heat Transfer to Joint]
                  |
                  v
        [Solder Melts and Flows]
```

**Types of Soldering Techniques:**

1. **Hand Soldering (Iron Soldering)** – Manual operation using a soldering iron; used for prototyping, repair, low-volume production.

2. **Wave Soldering** – A bulk process where a PCB is passed over a wave of molten solder; used for through-hole components.

3. **Reflow Soldering** – Solder paste is stencil-printed onto SMT pads, components are placed, and the assembly is heated in a reflow oven; the paste melts and solidifies.

4. **Soldering with a Torch** – Used for plumbing and larger metalwork; open-flame heating.

5. **Induction Soldering** – High-frequency induction heating; fast, localized heating for mass production.

6. **Hot-Air Soldering** – Hot air jet melts solder; used in rework stations and BGA/QFN reballing.

7. **Dip Soldering** – Components are dipped into a bath of molten solder; older bulk process.

8. **Robotic / Automated Soldering** – Used in high-volume electronics manufacturing (e.g., automotive, consumer electronics).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Alternative Choice]`

**(a) What are fluxes? Classify the different types of fluxes used in soldering and explain any two in detail.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** A flux is a chemical cleaning agent that is applied to the base metal surfaces before soldering to remove oxides, prevent re-oxidation during heating, and improve the wetting of the molten solder.

**Classification of Fluxes:**

1. **Rosin-based Fluxes** (mildly active, used in electronics)
   - **R (Rosin)** – Pure rosin, no activators; for clean surfaces.
   - **RMA (Rosin Mildly Activated)** – Mild activators added; for moderately oxidized surfaces.
   - **RA (Rosin Activated)** – Stronger activators; for heavily oxidized surfaces.

2. **Organic Fluxes (Water-soluble)** – Active organic acids like citric, lactic, or stearic acid; very active, but residue must be cleaned with water.

3. **Inorganic Fluxes (Acid)** – Zinc chloride, ammonium chloride, hydrochloric acid; highly corrosive; used only in non-electrical applications like plumbing.

4. **No-Clean Fluxes** – Synthetic resins with very low residue; designed to leave non-corrosive, non-conductive residue; popular in modern electronics.

**Detailed Explanation of Two Types:**

**(i) Rosin Flux (R/RMA/RA)**

Rosin flux is the **traditional standard for electronics soldering**. It is derived from the sap of pine trees and is composed mainly of **abietic acid**. When heated, rosin becomes chemically active and dissolves the thin copper oxide layer ($CuO$, $Cu_2O$) on the surface. Upon cooling, it solidifies into a hard, non-conductive, and mildly protective residue.

- **R (Pure Rosin)**: No activators; mildest. Used for freshly cleaned, easily solderable surfaces.
- **RMA (Rosin Mildly Activated)**: Contains small amounts of activators; the most common grade in electronics; gives good wetting and a benign residue.
- **RA (Rosin Activated)**: Contains aggressive activators; high activity but residue may be slightly corrosive – must be cleaned in some applications.

**Advantages:** Non-corrosive (R/RMA), widely available, excellent wetting, leaves a protective film.

**(ii) Inorganic Acid Flux (Zinc Chloride Type)**

Inorganic acid flux is formulated using **zinc chloride ($ZnCl_2$)**, **ammonium chloride ($NH_4Cl$)**, or **hydrochloric acid (HCl)** dissolved in water. It is the **most aggressive** flux family and is used only for **non-electronic** applications like plumbing, sheet-metal joining, and stainless-steel soldering.

The chemical action is:

$$
ZnCl_2 + H_2O \rightarrow Zn(OH)Cl + HCl
$$

The liberated HCl aggressively attacks oxides:

$$
2HCl + CuO \rightarrow CuCl_2 + H_2O
$$

After the joint is made, the residue **must be thoroughly washed off** to prevent ongoing corrosion. If left on the joint, the residue will continue to attack the metal and cause joint failure over time.

> **Why it is NOT used in electronics**: The residue is highly conductive, hygroscopic (absorbs water), and corrosive – it would short-circuit PCBs and corrode traces.

---

**(b) Write short notes on (i) Wave Soldering and (ii) Reflow Soldering.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Wave Soldering**

Wave soldering is a **bulk soldering process** used primarily for **through-hole component** assemblies on printed circuit boards (PCBs). It was the dominant mass-production soldering technique from the 1960s to the 1990s.

**Process Steps:**
1. **Flux Application**: The PCB is passed over a flux wave or foam fluxer, which deposits a thin layer of flux on the bottom side of the board.
2. **Pre-heating**: The board is preheated to **90–120°C** to activate the flux, evaporate solvents, and reduce thermal shock.
3. **Solder Wave**: The board passes over a **standing wave of molten solder** (typically Sn63/Pb37 or SAC lead-free) held at **~250°C**. The wave is created by pumping solder through a nozzle.
4. **Contact**: The bottom of the PCB contacts the wave, and solder wets the exposed metal pads and component leads.
5. **Cooling**: The board exits the wave and is cooled by natural or forced convection.
6. **Cleaning**: Flux residue is washed off (if water-soluble flux) or left in place (if no-clean flux).

**Working Diagram:**

```
        [PCB Infeed Conveyor]
                  |
                  v
        [Flux Foam or Spray]
                  |
                  v
        [Preheater 90-120C]
                  |
                  v
       ~~~~~~~~~~|~~~~~~~~~~   
      /  Solder  |   \        <-- Standing wave of molten solder
      \  Wave   _|_  /         (created by pump in solder pot)
       ~~~~~~~~~~|~~~~~~~~~~
                  |
                  v
         [Hot Air Knife removes bridges]
                  |
                  v
          [Cooling Zone]
                  |
                  v
        [PCB Outfeed and Inspection]
```

**Key Parameters:** Solder temperature (**250–260°C**), conveyor speed (**1–2 m/min**), preheat temperature (**90–120°C**), wave height.

**Advantages:** High throughput, uniform joints, suitable for through-hole components.
**Disadvantages:** Not suitable for fine-pitch SMDs, high solder consumption, generates dross (oxides) on the bath surface.

---

**(ii) Reflow Soldering**

Reflow soldering is the **dominant soldering process for Surface Mount Technology (SMT)** in modern electronics manufacturing.

**Process Steps:**
1. **Solder Paste Stencil Printing**: A stainless-steel stencil is placed over the PCB, and **solder paste** (a homogeneous mixture of tin-alloy spheres, flux, and viscosity modifiers) is squeegeed through the apertures onto the SMT pads.
2. **Component Placement (Pick-and-Place)**: A robotic pick-and-place machine positions SMD components (resistors, capacitors, ICs) onto the solder-paste deposits with high accuracy (typically ±25 µm).
3. **Reflow Oven Heating**: The populated PCB is conveyed through a **multi-zone reflow oven** where it passes through a controlled temperature profile with the following zones:
   - **Pre-heat Zone** (150–200°C): Gradual ramp to activate flux and prevent thermal shock.
   - **Soak Zone** (150–200°C): Equalization of temperature across the board.
   - **Reflow Zone** (220–250°C): Peak temperature exceeds the solder's liquidus, causing the paste to melt, wet, and form joints.
   - **Cooling Zone**: Controlled cool-down to solidify joints and refine grain structure.
4. **Inspection**: AOI (Automated Optical Inspection) and X-ray inspection verify joint quality.

**Reflow Profile (KTU Standard):**

$$
\text{Ramp rate} \approx 1 \text{ to } 3 °C/s, \quad T_{\text{peak}} \approx 30 \text{ to } 50°C \text{ above liquidus}
$$

**Block Diagram of Reflow Process:**

```
[Step 1: Stencil Printing] 
   Solder paste deposited on PCB pads
                  |
                  v
[Step 2: Component Placement] 
   Pick-and-place machine positions SMDs
                  |
                  v
[Step 3: Reflow Oven] 
   Preheat -> Soak -> Reflow -> Cooling
                  |
                  v
[Step 4: Inspection and Testing] 
   AOI, X-ray, In-Circuit Test
```

**Advantages:** High precision, suitable for fine-pitch and BGA components, high repeatability, low defect rate.
**Disadvantages:** High capital investment, sensitive to profile parameters, cannot rework easily.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall – Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Wrong temperature threshold** – writing "below 500°C" instead of **450°C** (loses 1 mark instantly).
> 2. **Conflating soldering with welding** – soldering does NOT melt the base metal. This is the most fundamental distinction.
> 3. **Skipping IMC formation** – many answers describe only the mechanical aspect; KTU examiners expect a mention of **metallurgical bonding via Intermetallic Compounds ($Cu_6Sn_5$, $Cu_3Sn$)**.
> 4. **Forgetting flux types** – simply writing "flux is used" is incomplete. Name at least 2–3 specific flux types with their active agents.
> 5. **Confusing wave and reflow** – wave soldering is for through-hole; reflow is for SMT. Mixing them up loses at least 2 marks in 14-mark questions.
> 6. **No mention of RoHS / lead-free** – the 2024 scheme emphasizes environmental compliance; mention at least once.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **Soldering** = filler metal melts **below 450°C**; base metal does NOT melt.
- ✅ **Brazing** = filler metal melts **above 450°C**; base metal does NOT melt.
- ✅ **Welding** = base metal itself melts (fusion process).
- ✅ **Solder** = filler metal; most common is **Sn63/Pb37 eutectic** (melts at **183°C**).
- ✅ **Lead-free solder (SAC305)** melts at **~217°C**; mandated by **RoHS** for environmental compliance.
- ✅ **Flux** = chemical cleaner; four roles: remove oxides, prevent re-oxidation, improve wetting, aid heat transfer.
- ✅ **Types of flux**: Rosin (R/RMA/RA), Organic (water-soluble), Inorganic (acid – for plumbing), No-clean.
- ✅ **Wetting** is judged by **contact angle (θ)**; θ < 30° is excellent, θ < 90° is acceptable.
- ✅ **Capillary action** is the driving force for solder flow; optimal joint clearance is **0.05 – 0.20 mm**.
- ✅ **IMC (Intermetallic Compound) layer** ($Cu_6Sn_5$, $Cu_3Sn$) provides the metallurgical bond – thin = strong, thick = brittle.
- ✅ **Wave soldering** = bulk process for **through-hole** components; uses a standing wave of molten solder.
- ✅ **Reflow soldering** = SMT process using **solder paste + reflow oven** with controlled thermal profile.
- ✅ **Heat equation** for soldering: $Q = m c_p \Delta T + m L_f$.
- ✅ **Capillary rise**: $h = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$.
- ✅ **Joint strength**: $\tau = F / A$; typical Sn-Pb shear strength ≈ **38 MPa**.
- ✅ **Soldering iron tip** = **tinned copper with iron plating**; must be kept wetted with solder.
- ✅ **RoHS** (Restriction of Hazardous Substances) is the EU directive that banned lead in consumer electronics from 2006.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Solders and Fluxes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT404 - MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Solders and Fluxes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Solder — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Solder** is a **fusible metal alloy** (typically with a melting point **below 450 °C**) that is melted and applied to create a **permanent metallic bond** between two or more metal workpieces without melting the base metals themselves. The process is called **soldering**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, soldering is grouped under **permanent joining processes (fusion welding family — non-fusion sub-family)**, because the parent metals remain in the **solid state** during joining. Only the filler (solder) melts and wets the base metal.

### 1.2 Flux — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flux** is a **chemical cleaning agent** (in paste, liquid, or solid-core wire form) applied to the joint area before/during soldering. Its primary role is to **dissolve and remove metal oxides**, **prevent re-oxidation** during heating, and **lower the surface tension** of the molten solder so it can flow and wet the joint surfaces.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Glue That Cleans Before It Sticks"

Imagine you are trying to stick two pieces of oily, dirty, rusted metal together with molten candle wax. The wax will just **ball up and roll off** because the dirt/oxide forms a non-stick film. Now, if you first **scrub the surfaces with a cleaning chemical** (the flux) before applying the wax, the wax **spreads and grips beautifully**.

| Real-world item | Manufacturing process equivalent |
|----------------|---------------------------------|
| Wax (sticks when molten) | Solder alloy |
| Scrubbing chemical | Flux |
| Oil / Rust / Dirt | Metal oxides (e.g., CuO, SnO₂) |
| Two surfaces | Parent metal workpieces |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Keyword Hook**: "Soldering is a **fusion of filler metal only**; **flux is a sacrificial cleaning agent** that is expended (consumed) during the process."

---

### 1.4 Why Solders and Fluxes Matter in Manufacturing

- **Electronics assembly** (PCB through-hole & SMT) — **60/40 Sn-Pb** or **SAC305 (Sn-Ag-Cu)**.
- **Plumbing & sheet-metal roofing** — **50/50 Sn-Pb**, lead-free Sn-Cu.
- **Automotive radiators** — **Lead-free solders**.
- **Jewellery and silverware** — **Silver solder (hard solder)**.
- **Stainless-steel food containers** — **Silver brazing alloy**.
- **Aerospace heat exchangers** — **Aluminium brazing alloy**.

---

### 1.5 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics in **Bold**

- **Eutectic point of 63/37 Sn-Pb solder = 183 °C** (sharpest melting point, no pasty range).
- **Eutectic point of 60/40 Sn-Pb solder ≈ 188 °C**.
- **Soft solder boundary** = melting point **below 450 °C** (ISO 857-2 classification).
- **Hard solder / Brazing boundary** = filler melting point **above 450 °C** (AWS A3.0).
- **Silver solder range** = **600 °C – 900 °C**.
- **Tin (Sn) melting point = 232 °C**; **Lead (Pb) melting point = 327 °C**.
- **Surface tension of clean molten solder ≈ 0.4 – 0.5 N/m**; with flux reduced to **≈ 0.3 N/m**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Broad Classification of Solders

Solders are classified along **three orthogonal axes**: (a) melting temperature, (b) chemical composition, and (c) form factor. The KTU board asks mostly on (a) and (b).

#### 2.1.1 Based on Melting Temperature

| Class | Melting Range | Common Name | Typical Use |
|-------|---------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Soft Solders** | **< 450 °C** | Tin-Lead, Tin-Lead-Silver, Tin-Antimony | Electronics, plumbing, sheet metal |
| **Hard Solders (Brazing)** | **> 450 °C** | Copper-Zinc, Copper-Silver, Nickel-Silver | Refrigeration, tools, jewellery |
| **Silver Solders** | **600 – 900 °C** | Ag-Cu-Zn, Ag-Cu-Zn-Cd | Stainless steel, carbide tips |
| **Aluminium Solders** | **150 – 300 °C** | Zn-Sn, Zn-Cd, Zn-Al | Aluminium joining |

#### 2.1.2 Based on Chemical Composition

##### A) Lead-Based Solders (Tin-Lead System)
- **60/40 (60 % Sn, 40 % Pb)** — pasty range, electronics general-purpose.
- **63/37 (63 % Sn, 37 % Pb)** — true **eutectic**, melts sharply at **183 °C**, used in **wave soldering, reflow profiling**.
- **50/50** — wide pasty range, used in plumbing, radiator joining.
- **40/60** — high-lead, high-temperature (≈ 235 °C solidus).
- **5/95, 10/90** — high-Pb solders for **step soldering** and **semiconductor die-attach** in power electronics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pasty range** = temperature gap between **solidus** (start of melting) and **liquidus** (fully molten). A wide pasty range → risk of **cold joint / disturbed joint** because the alloy remains mushy for a long time.

##### B) Lead-Free Solders (RoHS-driven, post-2006)

| Alloy | Composition (wt %) | Liquidus (°C) | Notes |
|-------|-------------------|---------------|-------|
| **SAC305** | 96.5 Sn / 3.0 Ag / 0.5 Cu | **217** | Industry default; near-eutectic |
| **Sn-Cu** | 99.3 Sn / 0.7 Cu | **227** | Cheapest lead-free, plumbing |
| **Sn-Ag** | 96.5 Sn / 3.5 Ag | **221** | High strength |
| **Sn-Bi** | 42 Sn / 58 Bi | **138** | Low-temp, step-soldering |
| **Sn-Zn** | 91 Sn / 9 Zn | **199** | Risk: Zn oxidises aggressively |
| **Sn-Sb** | 95 Sn / 5 Sb | 240 | High-temp lead-free |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RoHS (Restriction of Hazardous Substances) Directive** banned Pb in consumer electronics → mandatory use of SAC or Sn-Cu. KTU frequently asks to **compare lead vs lead-free solders** in 14-mark questions.

##### C) Silver Solders (Hard Solders / Silver Brazing)
- **Ag-Cu-Zn (15–45 % Ag, 20–40 % Cu, rest Zn)** — most common, **620–780 °C**.
- **Ag-Cu-Zn-Cd** (Cd-bearing) — lower melting, but **Cd is toxic** → phasing out.
- **Cadmium-free Ag-Cu-Zn-Sn / Ag-Cu-Zn-Mn** — modern replacements.

##### D) Special-Purpose Solders
- **Aluminium solder** — Zn-Al eutectic, requires **special organic / chloride flux**.
- **Stainless-steel solder** — high-Ag, requires aggressive flux.
- **Die-attach solders** — Au-Sn, Au-Si, Au-Ge, Pb-Sn-Ag for power semiconductor packaging.
- **Fusible alloys (Wood's metal, Field's metal)** — Bi-Pb-Sn-Cd, melting **< 100 °C**, used in **fire sprinklers, safety plugs**.

---

### 2.2 Deep Theory of Fluxes — The Three Functions

A flux is *consumed* during soldering, unlike a catalyst. Its three sequential duties are:

1. **Dissolution of oxides** — flux attacks CuO, SnO₂, PbO via chemical reduction. Example: rosin (abietic acid) reacts with CuO → copper abietate + water.
$$\text{CuO} + 2\text{HR (rosin acid)} \rightarrow \text{CuR}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
2. **Exclusion of oxygen** — molten flux **floats** on top of solder, forming a **blanket** that physically blocks atmospheric O₂ from reaching the hot metal.
3. **Surface-tension reduction** — flux lowers the **interfacial energy (γ)** between molten solder and base metal, which increases the **wetting angle (θ)**, ensuring the solder spreads out.

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet Table

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit | Application |
|---|--------------------|-----------|------|------------|
| 1 | Eutectic point 63/37 Sn-Pb | $T_e = 183\,°C$ | °C | Sharp-melt electronics solder |
| 2 | Eutectic point 60/40 Sn-Pb | $T_e \approx 188\,°C$ | °C | General-purpose |
| 3 | Pasty range | $\Delta T = T_{\text{liquidus}} - T_{\text{solidus}}$ | °C | Wider → riskier joint |
| 4 | Young's wetting equation | $\cos\theta = \dfrac{\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL}}{\gamma_{LV}}$ | dimensionless | Wetting condition: $\theta < 90°$ |
| 5 | Soft / hard solder boundary | $T_m = 450\,°C$ | °C | AWS classification |
| 6 | Solder joint strength (typical) | $\sigma_{uts} = 30 - 50 \text{ MPa}$ (Sn-Pb) | MPa | Mechanical design |
| 7 | Lead-free SAC305 strength | $\sigma_{uts} = 45 - 60 \text{ MPa}$ | MPa | Stronger, stiffer joints |
| 8 | Working temperature of flux (rosin) | $T_{\text{act}} = 100 - 300\,°C$ | °C | Flux activation window |
| 9 | Solder expansion (Sn-Pb) | $\alpha \approx 24 \times 10^{-6}/°C$ | /°C | CTE mismatch with Cu = 17 ppm/°C |
| 10 | Rule of thumb joint clearance | $g = 0.05 - 0.20 \text{ mm}$ | mm | Capillary action optimum |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Solder joint reliability in avionics** drives MIL-STD-2000 / IPC J-STD-001 specifications — flux residue ionic cleanliness measured in **µg NaCl/in²** (must be **< 1.56** for military).
- **Wetting balance test** (e.g., MUST System II) uses **wetting force vs time curves** — the **time to wetting (T₀)** and **maximum wetting force (F_max)** are key quality metrics.
- **Capillary action** in through-hole PCB barrels relies on **gap = 0.05–0.20 mm**; too small → poor fill, too large → solder starvation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Wetting Condition (Young's Equation applied to Soldering)

A solder joint is mechanically and electrically reliable **only if the solder wets the base metal**. Wetting is quantified by the **contact angle θ** measured through the liquid (solder) drop.

Consider the three interfacial surface tensions at the triple point:
- $\gamma_{SV}$ = Solid–Vapour interfacial energy (base metal + atmosphere)
- $\gamma_{SL}$ = Solid–Liquid interfacial energy (base metal + solder)
- $\gamma_{LV}$ = Liquid–Vapour interfacial energy (solder + atmosphere)

**Step 1:** At equilibrium, a horizontal force balance on the triple line (per unit length) gives:

$$
\gamma_{SV} \;=\; \gamma_{SL} \;+\; \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta
$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange to express the contact angle:

$$
\cos\theta \;=\; \frac{\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL}}{\gamma_{LV}}
$$

**Step 3:** Wetting criterion — for a solder drop to **spread** rather than bead up, the contact angle must be acute:

$$
\theta < 90^{\circ} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \gamma_{SV} > \gamma_{SL}
$$

**Step 4:** Flux **lowers** $\gamma_{LV}$ (and indirectly $\gamma_{SL}$ by removing oxides) → the denominator drops → $\cos\theta$ increases → θ decreases → better wetting.

**Step 5:** Quantitative example for clean copper + 60/40 solder at 250 °C:
- Without flux: $\theta \approx 60^{\circ}$ (marginal).
- With rosin flux: $\theta \approx 17^{\circ}$ (excellent wetting).

> [!TIP]
> **Board-tip line to memorise:** *"Flux improves wetting because it reduces the surface tension of the molten solder and dissolves surface oxides, allowing the solder to spread and form a low-contact-angle joint."*

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Selecting Solder & Flux by Application

| Base Metal | Recommended Solder | Melting °C | Recommended Flux | Why |
|------------|-------------------|-----------|------------------|-----|
| Copper (Cu) | 60/40 Sn-Pb or SAC305 | 183 / 217 | Rosin (RMA) or no-clean | Cu oxides soluble in rosin |
| Brass / Bronze | 50/50 Sn-Pb or Sn-Ag | 183–220 | Rosin mildly activated (RMA) | High Cu content, oxidises fast |
| Stainless steel (304) | Ag-Cu-Zn (45 % Ag) | 620–700 | Borax + fluoride paste | Tenacious Cr-oxide film |
| Aluminium | Zn-Al (98/2) | 380 | Organic chloride (acid) | Al₂O₃ layer, very tough |
| Gold (PCB pads) | 63/37 Sn-Pb | 183 | Rosin (R) | Au is noble — no aggressive flux needed |
| Nickel / Ni-plating | Sn-Ag (96.5/3.5) | 221 | Rosin activated (RA) | NiO requires active flux |
| Cast iron | High-Pb solder (Pb-Sn) | 280 | Boric acid / fluoride | Dirty, porous surface |

### 3.3 Derivation of Eutectic Composition (Sn-Pb Phase Diagram Logic)

The Sn-Pb system has a **eutectic point** at **61.9 wt % Sn / 38.1 wt % Pb** (commercialised as **63/37** for convenience) at **183 °C**.

**Why is this special?** At the eutectic composition, **liquid → solid transformation occurs at a single temperature** (no pasty range). The Gibbs phase rule for a binary alloy at constant pressure:

$$
F \;=\; C - P + 1 \;=\; 2 - 3 + 1 \;=\; 0
$$

where $F$ = degrees of freedom, $C$ = components (Sn, Pb), $P$ = phases (liquid Sn-Pb, solid Sn, solid Pb). With $F = 0$, **temperature is invariant** during the eutectic reaction — hence the sharp melting point used in precision electronics.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A PCB has 1000 through-hole joints. The 63/37 Sn-Pb solder is applied at 250 °C. If the average joint mass is 0.05 g and the heat of fusion of solder is **ΔH_f = 47 J/g**, calculate the **total heat absorbed by the solder during solidification** in the joints.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Mass per joint $m = 0.05 \text{ g}$.

**Step 2:** Heat released per joint on solidification:

$$
q_{\text{joint}} \;=\; m \times \Delta H_f \;=\; 0.05 \text{ g} \times 47 \text{ J/g} \;=\; 2.35 \text{ J}
$$

**Step 3:** Total heat for 1000 joints:

$$
