| 7 | $\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{r \cos\alpha}{1 - r \sin\alpha}\right)$ | Shear angle from cutting ratio | degrees |
| 8 | $\tau_s = \dfrac{F_s \sin\phi}{t_1 \, w}$ | Shear stress on shear plane | N/mm² |
| 9 | $\gamma = \cot\phi + \tan(\phi - \alpha)$ | **Shear strain** in the chip | dimensionless |
| 10 | $F_c = F \cos(\beta - \alpha)$ | Cutting force component | N |
| 11 | $F_t = F \sin(\beta - \alpha)$ | Thrust (feed) force component | N |
| 12 | $P = F_c \times V$ | Cutting power | W (if V in m/s) |
| 13 | $U = \dfrac{F_c}{t_1 \, w}$ | Specific cutting energy | N/mm² |
| 14 | $MRR = t_1 \cdot w \cdot V$ | Material removal rate | mm³/s |
| 15 | $F_{c,\text{oblique}} = F_{c,\text{ortho}} \cos i$ | Oblique cutting force (approx.) | N |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - **Force equations (10, 11)** are used to size the **spindle motor and bearings** of a lathe/milling machine.
> - **Specific cutting energy (13)** helps select a tool material (HSS, carbide, ceramic) and estimate energy bills in mass production.
> - **Merchant's equation (4)** is the foundation of **CAM software** for predicting chip formation, tool wear, and chatter stability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Velocity Relationship (V_s and V_c)

**Given:** Orthogonal cutting geometry, cutting velocity **V** acts horizontally; chip flows up the rake face at angle **α** above horizontal; shear plane is at angle **φ** to the cutting velocity.

**Step 1 — Define the velocity triangle.**
The tool moves with velocity V relative to the workpiece. The material element being sheared moves along the shear plane with velocity **V_s** (relative to the workpiece), and the resulting chip slides up the rake face with velocity **V_c** (also relative to the workpiece).

**Step 2 — Apply the law of sines to the velocity triangle.**
The interior angles of the velocity triangle are:
- Between **V** and **V_s** = φ
- Between **V_s** and **V_c** = 90° − φ + α
- Between **V_c** and **V** = 90° − α

**Step 3 — Use the law of sines.**

$$\frac{V}{\sin(90° - \phi + \alpha)} = \frac{V_s}{\sin(90° - \alpha)} = \frac{V_c}{\sin\phi}$$

Since **sin(90° − x) = cos x**, this simplifies to:

$$\frac{V}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)} = \frac{V_s}{\cos\alpha} = \frac{V_c}{\sin\phi}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for V_s and V_c.**

$$V_s = \frac{V \cos\alpha}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}$$

$$V_c = \frac{V \sin\phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}$$

**Step 5 — Sanity check.**
For α = 0 and φ = 45°, we get V_s = V_c = V/cos 45° ≈ 1.414 V, which is physically reasonable because the chip and shear flow must be *faster* than the tool to conserve mass.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Cutting Ratio (r) from Geometry

**Step 1 — Set up undeformed and deformed chip geometry.**
Consider a small element of workpiece of length **L** along the cutting direction. Before cutting, its thickness is **t₁**; after cutting, it forms a chip of thickness **t₂** and length **L_c**.

**Step 2 — Apply conservation of volume (incompressible plastic flow).**
Volume before = Volume after:

$$t_1 \cdot L \cdot w = t_2 \cdot L_c \cdot w$$

The width *w* cancels:

$$t_1 \cdot L = t_2 \cdot L_c$$

**Step 3 — Define the cutting ratio.**

$$r = \frac{t_1}{t_2} = \frac{L_c}{L}$$

**Step 4 — Relate lengths to the shear angle using the shear-plane geometry.**
By projecting onto the shear plane direction:

$$L \sin\phi = L_c \sin(\phi - \alpha)$$

Solving for the ratio **L_c / L**:

$$\frac{L_c}{L} = \frac{\sin\phi}{\sin(\phi - \alpha)}$$

**Step 5 — Equate the two expressions for the ratio.**

$$r = \frac{\sin\phi}{\sin(\phi - \alpha)}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for φ.**

Using the identity $\sin(\phi - \alpha) = \sin\phi \cos\alpha - \cos\phi \sin\alpha$:

$$r = \frac{\sin\phi}{\sin\phi \cos\alpha - \cos\phi \sin\alpha}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $\sin\phi \cos\alpha$:

$$r = \frac{1}{\cos\alpha - \cot\phi \sin\alpha}$$

$$\frac{1}{r} = \cos\alpha - \cot\phi \sin\alpha$$

$$\cot\phi \sin\alpha = \cos\alpha - \frac{1}{r}$$

$$\cot\phi = \frac{\cos\alpha}{\sin\alpha} - \frac{1}{r \sin\alpha} = \frac{r \cos\alpha - 1}{r \sin\alpha}$$

$$\tan\phi = \frac{r \sin\alpha}{r \cos\alpha - 1}$$

**Final result:**

$$\boxed{\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{r \cos\alpha}{1 - r \sin\alpha}\right)}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Merchant's Shear Angle Equation

**Step 1 — Construct the Merchant force circle.**
At equilibrium, the four forces **F, F_s, N_s, F_c, F_t, N** must close on a force diagram. The key result from geometry is that the shear force **F_s** and normal-to-shear-plane force **N_s** form a resultant **F'**, and similarly the friction force **F** and normal **N** form a resultant **F**, all acting through the common point of tool-workpiece contact.

**Step 2 — Express the resultant force in terms of F_s and F.**
By the law of sines applied to the force circle, the angle between **F** and the shear plane is **(φ + β − α)**, so:

$$F_s = F \cos(\phi + \beta - \alpha)$$

**Step 3 — Express the cutting force in terms of F.**
The angle between **F** and the horizontal cutting direction is **(β − α)**:

$$F_c = F \cos(\beta - \alpha)$$

**Step 4 — Take the ratio.**

$$\frac{F_s}{F_c} = \frac{\cos(\phi + \beta - \alpha)}{\cos(\beta - \alpha)}$$

**Step 5 — Assume shear stress is maximized at the shear plane (Merchant's principle).**
Merchant proposed that the actual shear angle **φ** is the one that **minimizes the cutting force F_c** (or equivalently the energy), which yields:

$$\frac{dF_c}{d\phi} = 0$$

Carrying out this differentiation (with constant α and β) gives the famous result:

$$2\phi + \beta - \alpha = 90°$$

**Step 6 — Solve for φ.**

$$\phi = 45° + \frac{\alpha}{2} - \frac{\beta}{2}$$

This is **Merchant's First Solution**. An alternate, equally valid form based on minimizing the resultant shear force is:

$$\phi = 45° - \frac{\alpha}{2} + \frac{\beta}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The Ernst–Merchant equation (**2φ + β − α = 90°**) is the *most-frequently tested* formula in KTU Module 3. Always state the assumption "minimum energy" when writing it.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of the Shear Strain (γ) in the Chip

**Step 1 — Geometric setup.**
A material element ABCD in the workpiece becomes a sheared parallelogram AB'C'D' in the chip. The shear strain is the change in angle at vertex A.

**Step 2 — Use chip-thickness ratio r and the angles.**

$$\gamma = \cot\phi + \tan(\phi - \alpha)$$

**Step 3 — Express in terms of r only (eliminating α and φ).**
Substituting the geometric relations derived above:

$$\gamma = \frac{\cos\alpha}{\sin\phi \cos\phi - \sin\alpha \cos^2\phi / \cos(\phi - \alpha)} \;\cdots \text{(after algebra)}$$

A simpler and widely-used form:

$$\gamma = \frac{\cos\alpha}{\sin\phi \cos(\phi - \alpha)}$$

**Step 4 — Pure geometric form (no α):**

$$\gamma = \frac{1}{r} - 1 + \text{(angle corrections)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Practical use:** Shear strain is a measure of *how severely* the chip is deformed. Higher γ ⇒ harder machining, more tool wear, more heat generated.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem (KTU 2022 Pattern):**
In an orthogonal cutting operation, the following data are given:
- Undeformed chip thickness: **t₁ = 0.5 mm**
- Cutting ratio: **r = 0.4**
- Rake angle: **α = 10°**
- Coefficient of friction: **μ = 0.5**
- Width of cut: **w = 3 mm**
- Cutting velocity: **V = 100 m/min**

Find: (a) Shear angle φ, (b) Chip thickness t₂, (c) Friction angle β, (d) Merchant's shear-angle check.

**Solution:**

(a) **Shear angle** from cutting-ratio formula:

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.4 \times \cos 10°}{1 - 0.4 \times \sin 10°}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.4 \times 0.9848}{1 - 0.4 \times 0.1736}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.3939}{0.9306}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.4233) \approx 22.94°$$

(b) **Chip thickness:**

$$t_2 = \frac{t_1}{r} = \frac{0.5}{0.4} = 1.25 \text{ mm}$$

(c) **Friction angle:**

$$\beta = \tan^{-1}(0.5) \approx 26.57°$$

(d) **Merchant's equation check:**

$$2\phi + \beta - \alpha = 2(22.94°) + 26.57° - 10° = 62.45°$$

This is **not** exactly 90°, indicating that the given data does not satisfy Merchant's minimum-energy condition exactly — a real-world scenario where α and β are not perfectly optimized. The deviation is **27.55°**, which is significant, and the actual shear angle is therefore governed by the cutting ratio.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Orthogonal Cutting — Functional Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Workpiece Surface] --> B[Tool Cutting Edge Engages Workpiece]
    B --> C[Primary Shear Zone: Material Shears along Plane at angle phi]
    C --> D[Material Crosses Shear Plane and Becomes Chip]
    D --> E[Secondary Deformation Zone: Chip Slides on Rake Face]
    E --> F[Built-up Edge May Form or Detach]
    F --> G[Continuous Chip Flows Up Rake Face at Velocity Vc]
    G --> H[New Workpiece Surface Exposed]
    H --> I[Tool Advances by Feed = t1]
    I --> B
```

### 4.2 Merchant's Force Circle — Force Vector Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Resultant Force F on Tool] --> B[Fc: Cutting Force horizontal]
    A --> C[Ft: Thrust Force vertical]
    B --> D[Fs: Shear Force along Shear Plane]
    C --> E[Ns: Normal Force on Shear Plane]
    D --> F[Friction Force Friction on Rake Face]
    E --> G[Normal Force N on Rake Face]
    F --> H[Friction Angle beta = arctan mu]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Closed Force Polygon = Equilibrium]
    B -.angle beta minus alpha.-> A
    D -.angle phi plus beta minus alpha.-> A
```

### 4.3 Velocity Vector Diagram (Merchant's Velocity Triangle)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V[Cutting Velocity V horizontal] --> V1{Velocity Triangle}
    V1 --> Vs[Shear Velocity Vs along Shear Plane]
    V1 --> Vc[Chip Velocity Vc along Rake Face]
    Vs --> Eq[By Law of Sines: V / cos phi minus alpha = Vs / cos alpha = Vc / sin phi]
    Vc --> Eq
    V --> Eq
    Eq --> Calc[Compute phi from r and alpha]
```

### 4.4 Orthogonal vs Oblique Cutting — Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ORTHO[Orthogonal Cutting - 2D]
        O1[Tool Edge perpendicular to V]
        O2[Chip flows in vertical plane]
        O3[Forces: Fc and Ft only]
        O4[Single shear plane analysis]
    end
    subgraph OBLIQ[Oblique Cutting - 3D]
        P1[Tool Edge inclined at angle i]
        P2[Chip flows obliquely on rake face]
        P3[Forces: Fc, Ff, Fr - three components]
        P4[Effective normal rake angle alpha_n used]
    end
    ORTHO --> TRANS[Transform using inclination angle i]
    TRANS --> OBLIQ
```

### 4.5 Tool Geometry Nomenclature Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Single Point Cutting Tool] --> B[Rake Face top surface]
    A --> C[Flank Face clearance surface]
    A --> D[Cutting Edge sharp boundary]
    B --> E[Rake Angle alpha]
    C --> F[Clearance Angle gamma]
    D --> G[Inclination Angle i]
    D --> H[Cutting Edge Angle lambda]
    A --> I[Nose Radius r small radius at tip]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define orthogonal cutting. List the four basic tool angles used in its analysis.**

**Model Answer:**
Orthogonal cutting is a chip-removal process in which the cutting edge of the tool is *straight* and *perpendicular* to the direction of the cutting velocity, and the chip flows in a single vertical reference plane. The four basic tool angles are:
1. **Rake angle (α)** – angle between the rake face and a plane normal to the workpiece surface.
2. **Clearance angle (γ)** – angle that prevents rubbing between the tool flank and the workpiece.
3. **Cutting edge angle (λ)** – orientation of the cutting edge relative to the feed direction.
4. **Nose radius (r)** – small rounded edge at the tool tip improving surface finish and reducing stress concentration.

---

**Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**What is the significance of the friction angle (β) in orthogonal cutting? How is it related to the coefficient of friction?**

**Model Answer:**
The friction angle **β** represents the inclination of the resultant tool-chip interface reaction force from the normal to the rake face. It is a direct measure of the frictional resistance between the chip and the tool face. A larger β means more friction, more heat generation, and a higher tendency for built-up edge formation.

It is related to the coefficient of friction **μ** at the tool-chip interface by:

$$\beta = \tan^{-1}(\mu)$$

This relation is essential in applying Merchant's shear angle equation. Typical values of β in machining range from **25° to 45°**.

---

### PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** divided into sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU 2022 ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain **Merchant's circle force analysis** in orthogonal cutting. Derive the relationship $2\phi + \beta - \alpha = 90°$. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Sketch the cutting geometry**: Show the cutting edge, the rake face at angle α, the shear plane at angle φ, the cutting velocity V, and the forces F, F_c, F_t, F_s, N_s acting at the tool tip.

2. **Identify the four forces and their directions**:
   - F_c (horizontal, opposing V)
   - F_t (vertical, pushing tool away from workpiece)
   - F_s (along the shear plane)
   - N_s (normal to the shear plane)
   - On the tool face: Friction force F and Normal force N

3. **Apply the law of sines to the closed force polygon.** The angle between F and the shear plane is (φ + β − α). Therefore:

$$F_s = F \cos(\phi + \beta - \alpha)$$

4. **Apply Merchant's energy principle:** The cutting process will naturally select the shear angle that *minimizes* the resultant force F (or the energy required). Differentiating F with respect to φ and setting dF/dφ = 0:

$$\frac{dF}{d\phi} = 0 \implies 2\phi + \beta - \alpha = 90°$$

5. **Solve:**

$$\phi = 45° + \frac{\alpha}{2} - \frac{\beta}{2}$$

This is **Merchant's shear angle equation**.

---

**(b)** In an orthogonal cutting operation, the cutting ratio is **r = 0.45**, the rake angle is **α = 8°**, and the coefficient of friction at the tool-chip interface is **μ = 0.6**. Determine:
(i) the shear angle φ,
(ii) the friction angle β,
(iii) check whether Merchant's condition $2\phi + \beta - \alpha = 90°$ is satisfied. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

(i) **Friction angle:**

$$\beta = \tan^{-1}(0.6) = 30.96°$$

(ii) **Shear angle from cutting ratio:**

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.45 \times \cos 8°}{1 - 0.45 \times \sin 8°}\right)$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.45 \times 0.9903}{1 - 0.45 \times 0.1392}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.4456}{0.9374}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.4754)$$

$$\phi \approx 25.43°$$

(iii) **Merchant's check:**

$$2\phi + \beta - \alpha = 2(25.43°) + 30.96° - 8° = 50.86° + 30.96° - 8° = 73.82°$$

The value **73.82°** is significantly less than **90°**, meaning the given data does not strictly satisfy Merchant's minimum-energy condition. The actual shear angle is governed by the measured cutting ratio, and the tool-chip friction is higher than the optimum.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU 2023 ESE Pattern — Internal Choice Alternative]**

**(a)** Distinguish clearly between **orthogonal and oblique cutting**. Show how the cutting force in oblique cutting is related to that in orthogonal cutting. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

| Basis | Orthogonal Cutting | Oblique Cutting |
|------|--------------------|------------------|
| Cutting edge orientation | Perpendicular to V | Inclined at angle *i* to the normal of V |
| Dimensionality | 2D | 3D |
| Chip flow | In vertical reference plane | Across the rake face, perpendicular to cutting edge |
| Practical example | Idealized, narrow tool | Actual turning, drilling, milling |
| Effective rake angle used | α (vertical plane) | α_n (normal plane) |

**Force relation:**
The cutting force in oblique cutting is the projection of the orthogonal cutting force on the cutting-velocity direction:

$$F_{c,\text{oblique}} = F_{c,\text{ortho}} \cdot \cos i$$

The other two components (feed force F_f and radial force F_r) arise solely due to the inclination.

---

**(b)** A turning operation uses a single-point tool with **rake angle α = 12°**, **inclination angle i = 10°**, and **cutting velocity V = 150 m/min**. The orthogonal cutting force was calculated to be **F_c,ortho = 1200 N**. The chip thickness is observed to be **t₂ = 1.2 mm** for an undeformed chip thickness of **t₁ = 0.4 mm**.
Calculate:
(i) the oblique cutting force,
(ii) the cutting ratio,
(iii) the material removal rate per minute, assuming the width of cut **w = 4 mm**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

(i) **Oblique cutting force:**

$$F_{c,\text{oblique}} = 1200 \times \cos 10° = 1200 \times 0.9848 = 1181.76 \text{ N}$$

(ii) **Cutting ratio:**

$$r = \frac{t_1}{t_2} = \frac{0.4}{1.2} = 0.333$$

(iii) **MRR per minute:**

The undeformed chip cross-section area is $A = t_1 \times w = 0.4 \times 4 = 1.6 \text{ mm}^2$.

Converting V to mm/min: $V = 150 \text{ m/min} = 150{,}000 \text{ mm/min}$.

$$MRR = A \times V = 1.6 \times 150{,}000 = 240{,}000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 240 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}$$

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in this topic (KTU Board Examiner Feedback):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units of V** — Cutting velocity is often given in m/min but must be converted to m/s before computing power (P = F_c × V).
> 2. **Sign convention for rake angle** — Always state whether α is positive or negative. A negative rake (e.g., for hard materials) flips the sign in Merchant's equation.
> 3. **Confusing rake angle (α) with clearance angle (γ)** — The clearance angle does not appear in the cutting-force equations; do not include it in Merchant's formula.
> 4. **Wrong angle subtractions** — The formula is $\phi = 45° + \alpha/2 - \beta/2$, **not** $45° - \alpha/2 + \beta/2$. Memorize the sign carefully.
> 5. **Unit mismatch in MRR** — If V is in m/min and dimensions in mm, MRR will be in mm³/min, not mm³/s. Convert consistently.
> 6. **Skipping the assumption** — Always state **"Assuming minimum energy principle"** before writing $2\phi + \beta - \alpha = 90°$, or lose 1 mark.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Orthogonal cutting = 2D**, **Oblique cutting = 3D** with inclination angle *i*.
- The **cutting ratio r = t₁/t₂ ≤ 1** measures how much the chip thickens; it is the most important experimental quantity.
- **Merchant's shear angle equation:** $\phi = 45° + \alpha/2 - \beta/2$ (minimum-energy principle).
- **Friction angle:** $\beta = \tan^{-1}\mu$; typical range **25° to 45°**.
- **Velocity relations** (KTU high-yield): $V_s = V\cos\alpha / \cos(\phi - \alpha)$ and $V_c = V\sin\phi / \cos(\phi - \alpha)$.
- **Forces:** $F_c = F\cos(\beta - \alpha)$, $F_t = F\sin(\beta - \alpha)$, $F_s = F\cos(\phi + \beta - \alpha)$.
- **Power:** $P = F_c \times V$ (with V in m/s for Watts).
- **MRR:** $MRR = t_1 \cdot w \cdot V$.
- **Specific cutting energy (U)** is used to compare machinability of materials; lower U means easier to cut.
- **Built-up edge (BUE)** forms at low speeds and high friction — avoid by increasing cutting speed, using coolant, or selecting a coated tool.
- **Continuous vs. discontinuous chip:** continuous for ductile materials (e.g., mild steel); discontinuous for brittle materials (e.g., cast iron).
- **Oblique cutting force:** $F_{c,\text{oblique}} \approx F_{c,\text{ortho}} \cos i$ (approximate practical rule).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rake and Clearance Angle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT404 - MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Rake and Clearance Angle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Tool Geometry

In metal cutting, the geometry of a single-point cutting tool (SPCT) directly governs the **machining efficiency**, **surface finish**, **tool life**, and **cutting forces**. Among the various tool angles, the **Rake Angle** and the **Clearance Angle** are the two most fundamental and influential parameters that define how the cutting edge interacts with the workpiece material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PBMRT404 Module 3):** The tool nomenclature, orthogonal cutting, chip formation, and the role of rake and clearance angles in chip flow and tool life form the core of this module. These are **HIGH-FREQUENCY** topics in KTU University Examinations.

---

## 1.2 Rake Angle — Formal Definition

The **Rake Angle (α)** is defined as the **angle between the rake face (top face) of the cutting tool and a reference plane**, measured in the plane of motion (the orthogonal cutting plane for most analytical work).

**Reference Planes Used:**
- **Plane A (Reference Plane):** Horizontal plane through the cutting edge, perpendicular to the cutting velocity.
- **Orthogonal Plane (Plane P-P):** Plane perpendicular to the cutting edge.
- **Normal Plane (Plane N-N):** Plane normal to the cutting edge, measured perpendicular to the projected length of the cutting edge.

**Three Reference Systems (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
1. **Tool Reference System (TRS)** – based on the shank of the tool.
2. **Cutting Plane Coordinate System (CPCS)** – based on the cutting edge geometry.
3. **Working Plane Coordinate System** – based on the work surface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rake Angle Classification (By Sign):**
> - **Positive Rake Angle (α > 0):** Rake face slopes downward toward the cutting edge from the reference plane. *Commonly used for ductile materials.*
> - **Zero Rake Angle (α = 0):** Rake face coincides with the reference plane. *Used for cast iron and brass.*
> - **Negative Rake Angle (α < 0):** Rake face slopes upward away from the cutting edge. *Used for hard, brittle materials and interrupted cuts.*

---

## 1.3 Clearance Angle — Formal Definition

The **Clearance Angle (also called the Lip Angle component, denoted γ or α_c)** is the angle between the **flank (clearance) face** of the tool and a reference surface (typically the machined surface of the workpiece).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Board Standard):** The **Primary Clearance Angle** is the angle between the primary flank face of the tool and a plane tangent to the machined work surface, measured in the **plane of motion**. The **Secondary Clearance Angle** is provided just behind the primary flank to provide additional relief.

**Key Functions of Clearance Angle:**
1. **Prevents rubbing** between the flank face and the newly machined surface.
2. **Reduces flank wear** and heat generation.
3. **Provides clearance** for the cutting edge to engage the workpiece without interference.

**Typical Recommended Values (Engineering Practice):**
- **Roughing operations:** 5° to 7°
- **Finishing operations:** 10° to 15°
- **High-speed cutting:** 6° to 10°

---

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

### 🍕 The Pizza Cutter Analogy

Imagine you are slicing through a thick piece of cheese with a **flat kitchen knife**:

- The **Rake Face** is the **top slanted surface** of the knife. If you tilt the top of the knife so that the **front face slopes down toward the cutting edge** (like the angled blade of a Japanese chef's knife), you are creating a **positive rake angle**. The cheese peels off the top surface easily — that's how a chip flows off a cutting tool.

- The **Clearance Face** is the **underside of the knife** that faces the freshly cut surface of the cheese. If this underside is **completely flat and parallel** to the cheese surface, it will **drag and rub**, making the cut rough. By angling the underside slightly away (grinding a small clearance angle), the knife **lifts off** the surface, allowing a clean slice.

> **The "Lifting the Pencil" Analogy for Clearance Angle:**
> If you try to draw a line on a table while keeping the pencil absolutely horizontal, the side of the pencil will rub against the table. But if you **tilt the pencil slightly** (creating a clearance angle), the tip alone contacts the surface — clean contact, no friction. **This is precisely the role of the clearance angle on a cutting tool.**

### 🏏 The Cricket Bat Analogy for Rake Angle

- **Positive Rake** = A bat with a "sweet spot" tilted forward — easier to slice through air (chip flows easily, less force).
- **Negative Rake** = A vertical or backward-tilted bat — requires more force, but the edge is stronger (suitable for hard materials).

---

## 1.5 Summary of Definitions in Tabular Form

| Parameter | Symbol | Plane of Measurement | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Orthogonal Rake Angle** | α | Orthogonal Plane (⊥ to cutting edge) | Governs chip flow and shear angle |
| **Normal Rake Angle** | αₙ | Normal Plane (⊥ to chip flow) | True chip-tool interaction angle |
| **Side Rake Angle** | αₛ | Longitudinal-vertical plane | Helps chip curl direction |
| **Back Rake Angle** | αᵦ | Transverse-vertical plane | Controls chip flow across tool |
| **Primary Clearance Angle** | γ or α_c | Plane of motion | Prevents flank rubbing |
| **Secondary Clearance Angle** | γ' | Plane of motion | Additional relief |
| **Cutting Edge Inclination Angle** | λ | Reference plane (Plane A) | Strengthens cutting edge |
| **Cutting Edge Angle (Approach Angle)** | ψᵣ | Reference plane | Affects chip thickness |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Basic Tool Angles (ASA / ISO Standard)

The **American Standards Association (ASA)** and **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** define **seven fundamental tool angles** measured in three mutually perpendicular reference planes:

### A. Angles Measured in Reference Plane (Plane A)
- **Back Rake Angle (αᵦ)** — controls chip flow across the nose.
- **Side Rake Angle (αₛ)** — controls chip flow sideways; main chip-controlling angle.
- **Cutting Edge Inclination Angle (λ)** — inclination of cutting edge w.r.t. horizontal.

### B. Angles Measured in Cutting Plane (Plane V-V)
- **Cutting Edge Angle (Approach Angle, ψᵣ)** — angle between cutting edge and feed direction.
- **Side Cutting Edge Angle (ψₛ)** — the approach angle in the longitudinal-vertical plane.

### C. Angles Measured in Plane of Motion (Plane P-P)
- **Rake Angle (α)** — angle between rake face and horizontal.
- **Clearance Angle (γ or α_c)** — angle between flank face and machined surface.

---

## 2.2 The Three Rake Angle Systems (KTU Must-Know)

There are **three systems** for defining the rake angle, depending on the reference plane used:

### 🔹 System 1: Orthogonal Rake Angle (ORS)
The rake angle measured in the **orthogonal plane (P-P)** — perpendicular to the cutting edge.
- **Symbol:** α
- **Most commonly used** in analytical force analysis and KTU problems.

### 🔹 System 2: Normal Rake Angle (NRS)
The rake angle measured in the **normal plane (N-N)** — perpendicular to the cutting edge and the cutting velocity vector.
- **Symbol:** αₙ
- **Used for grinding wheels and form tools** where the cutting edge has a complex 3D shape.

### 🔹 System 3: Maximum Rake Angle (MRS)
The maximum possible rake angle considering the actual contact geometry.
- **Symbol:** αₘ
- **Equals ORS** when the cutting edge is straight.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Between Systems (KTU Frequently Asked):**
> - **Normal to Orthogonal:** $\alpha_n = \tan^{-1}(\tan \alpha \cdot \cos \lambda)$
> - **Side Rake to Orthogonal:** $\tan \alpha = \tan \alpha_s \cos \psi_r + \tan \alpha_b \sin \psi_r$
> - Where **λ = Cutting Edge Inclination Angle** and **ψᵣ = Side Cutting Edge Angle**.

---

## 2.3 Detailed Analysis: Effect of Rake Angle

### ✅ Positive Rake Angle Effects:
1. **Reduces cutting force** (F_c) — chip shears easily.
2. **Reduces power consumption.**
3. **Produces thinner, smaller chips** for the same feed.
4. **Improves surface finish** in ductile materials.
5. **Lower cutting temperatures.**
6. **Smoother chip flow** — sharper cutting action.

### ❌ Negative Consequences of Excessive Positive Rake:
1. **Weaker cutting edge** — prone to chipping.
2. **Poor tool life** in interrupted cuts.
3. **Vibration tendency** at high speeds.

### ✅ Negative Rake Angle Effects:
1. **Stronger cutting edge** — resists fracture.
2. **Better for hard, brittle materials** (e.g., hardened steel, cast iron).
3. **Suitable for interrupted cuts** (e.g., milling).
4. **Higher compressive stress on chip** — chip slides on rake face.

### ❌ Negative Consequences of Excessive Negative Rake:
1. **Increased cutting force** (~20-30% higher).
2. **Higher power consumption.**
3. **Increased heat generation.**
4. **Poor surface finish.**

| Material | Recommended Rake Angle (Orthogonal) |
|---|---|
| **Aluminum, Brass, Soft Steel** | +20° to +30° |
| **Mild Steel (Low Carbon)** | +10° to +20° |
| **High Carbon Steel, Alloy Steel** | +5° to +10° |
| **Hardened Steel (HRC > 50)** | -5° to -10° |
| **Cast Iron, Bronze** | 0° to +10° |

---

## 2.4 Detailed Analysis: Effect of Clearance Angle

### Critical Function:
The **clearance angle** ensures the **flank face does NOT contact** the newly machined surface. Without adequate clearance:
- **Flank wear increases rapidly.**
- **Heat generation rises** → thermal damage to tool.
- **Surface finish deteriorates.**
- **Built-up edge formation** is promoted.

### Trade-off in Clearance Angle Selection:

| Clearance Angle | Effect on Cutting | Effect on Tool |
|---|---|---|
| **Too Small (< 3°)** | Rubbing, vibration, poor finish | Excessive flank wear, tool failure |
| **Optimum (5°-15°)** | Clean cut, good finish | Long tool life, low wear |
| **Too Large (> 20°)** | Weak edge, chatter | Edge chipping, reduced strength |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse **Clearance Angle** with **Rake Angle**. Remember:
> - **Rake Angle** is on the **TOP face** of the tool (where the chip slides).
> - **Clearance Angle** is on the **BOTTOM/FLANK face** of the tool (facing the workpiece).
> - These are **DIFFERENT angles on DIFFERENT faces** of the tool.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas are organized for quick last-minute revision. Memorize these for KTU 2024 ESE.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Shear Angle (Merchant)** | $\phi = 45° + \dfrac{\alpha}{2} - \dfrac{\beta}{2}$ | Classic Merchant's equation for shear angle |
| 2 | **Friction Angle Relation** | $\tan \beta = \mu$ | Where μ is the friction coefficient at tool-chip interface |
| 3 | **Normal-Orthogonal Rake Conversion** | $\tan \alpha_n = \tan \alpha \cdot \cos \lambda$ | Convert between NRS and ORS |
| 4 | **Side Rake to Orthogonal Rake** | $\tan \alpha = \tan \alpha_s \cos \psi_r + \tan \alpha_b \sin \psi_r$ | For oblique cutting tools |
| 5 | **Back Rake to Orthogonal Rake** | $\tan \alpha = \tan \alpha_s \cos \psi_r + \tan \alpha_b \sin \psi_r$ | Component form |
| 6 | **Cutting Velocity** | $V = \pi D N$ | m/min, where D is diameter, N is RPM |
| 7 | **Uncut Chip Thickness** | $t_1 = f \sin \psi_r$ | For oblique cutting |
| 8 | **Chip Thickness Ratio Relation** | $r = \dfrac{\sin \phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}$ | Connects r to φ and α |
| 9 | **Shear Strain** | $\gamma = \cot \phi + \tan(\phi - \alpha)$ | Strain in primary shear zone |
| 10 | **Cutting Force Component (Merchant)** | $F_s = F \cos(\phi + \beta - \alpha)$ | Shear force on shear plane |
| 11 | **Cutting Force Component (Merchant)** | $F = F_s \cos(\phi + \beta - \alpha)$ | Tangential cutting force |
| 12 | **Merchant's Force Circle** | $F \sin \alpha + N \cos \alpha = F_s \cos(\phi + \beta)$ | Force equilibrium on tool face |
| 13 | **Specific Cutting Energy** | $E_c = \dfrac{F_c}{A_c}$ | Energy per unit volume |
| 14 | **Cutting Power** | $P = F_c \cdot V$ | Watts (W) |
| 15 | **Nose Radius Effect on Clearance** | Effective $\gamma_{eff} \approx \gamma + \dfrac{\sin^{-1}(f/r_n)}{2}$ | Where $r_n$ is nose radius |

### 🧠 Engineering Utility of These Equations

1. **Tool Design:** Manufacturers use the conversion formulas (Equations 3, 4) to design tool inserts for standard holders.
2. **Process Optimization:** Merchant's equation (Eq. 1) predicts chip formation geometry for given tool-workpiece pairs.
3. **CNC Programming:** Cutting power (Eq. 14) determines the spindle power required and feed limits.
4. **Tool Selection:** Engineers select rake/clearance angles based on material hardness charts (similar to Section 2.3 table).
5. **Wear Prediction:** Clearance angle reduction over time predicts tool life using the Taylor equation.

---

## 2.6 Cutting Tool Nomenclature Diagram (Description)

Imagine the cutting tool as a **3D wedge** with the following key features:

```
        RAKE FACE (where chip slides)
       / 
      / α (Rake Angle)
     /________________________
    |                        /|
    |                       / |
    |__CUTTING EDGE________/  |
    |                      \  |
    |                       \ |
    |__MACHINED SURFACE_____\|
              \  γ (Clearance Angle)
               \_________________________ (Workpiece)
```

The **rake face** is on the **upper-front surface**, and the **clearance face (flank)** is on the **lower-front surface**, separated by the **cutting edge**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Conversion Between Normal and Orthogonal Rake Angles

This is a **KTU 2024 Module 3 high-priority derivation**. The relationship between the normal rake angle (αₙ) and the orthogonal rake angle (α) is governed by the cutting edge inclination angle (λ).

### Given:
- α = Orthogonal rake angle (in plane P-P)
- αₙ = Normal rake angle (in plane N-N)
- λ = Cutting edge inclination angle (in reference plane A)
- V = Cutting velocity vector
- Chip flow direction at angle η from the cutting edge

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1: Define the geometry.**

The normal plane (N-N) is perpendicular to the cutting edge and contains the chip flow vector. The orthogonal plane (P-P) is also perpendicular to the cutting edge but contains the cutting velocity vector.

**Step 2: Establish the projection of the rake face.**

The rake face, when viewed in the normal plane, has a steeper angle than when viewed in the orthogonal plane, because the normal plane is "tilted" relative to the orthogonal plane by the inclination angle λ.

**Step 3: Use trigonometric relations.**

The tangent of the normal rake angle is the projection of the orthogonal tangent onto the normal plane:

$$
\tan \alpha_n = \tan \alpha \cdot \cos \lambda
$$

**Step 4: Algebraic expansion for verification.**

Substituting $\alpha = 10°$ and $\lambda = 15°$:

$$
\tan \alpha_n = \tan 10° \cdot \cos 15°
$$

$$
\tan \alpha_n = (0.1763) \cdot (0.9659)
$$

$$
\tan \alpha_n = 0.1703
$$

$$
\alpha_n = \tan^{-1}(0.1703) = 9.66°
$$

**Conclusion:** The normal rake angle is **slightly less** than the orthogonal rake angle when the cutting edge has a positive inclination. This is the form most commonly tested in KTU 2024 ESE.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Merchant's Shear Angle Equation

The **Merchant equation** relates the shear angle (φ) to the rake angle (α) and the friction angle (β). This is foundational for KTU problem-solving.

### Given:
- α = Rake angle of tool
- β = Friction angle at tool-chip interface (where $\tan \beta = \mu$)
- φ = Shear angle (angle of shear plane with cutting velocity)

### Derivation Steps:

**Step 1: Force balance at the cutting tool.**

Forces acting on the chip:
- F = Tangential cutting force (along V)
- Fₛ = Shear force (along shear plane)
- N = Normal force on tool face
- F_f = Friction force on tool face

**Step 2: Equilibrium of forces at the shear plane.**

Resolving forces along the shear plane:

$$
F_s \cos \phi = F \sin \phi + N \sin \beta
$$

**Step 3: Resolving forces perpendicular to the shear plane.**

$$
F_s \sin \phi = F \cos \phi - N \cos \beta
$$

**Step 4: Divide equation (1) by equation (2) to eliminate Fs.**

$$
\dfrac{\cos \phi}{\sin \phi} = \dfrac{F \sin \phi + N \sin \beta}{F \cos \phi - N \cos \beta}
$$

**Step 5: Apply tool-chip friction relation.**

The ratio of friction force to normal force on the tool face gives:

$$
\dfrac{F}{N} = \cos \alpha \cdot \tan \beta - \sin \alpha
$$

**Step 6: Substitute into the equation and simplify.**

After trigonometric simplifications using angle sum identities:

$$
2\phi + \beta - \alpha = 90°
$$

**Step 7: Solve for φ.**

$$
\phi = 45° + \dfrac{\alpha}{2} - \dfrac{\beta}{2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the most cited equation in metal cutting theory** and is **guaranteed** to appear in some form in KTU 2024 ESE.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Chip Thickness Ratio and Shear Strain

The **chip thickness ratio (r)** is defined as:

$$
r = \dfrac{t_1}{t_2}
$$

Where:
- $t_1$ = Uncut chip thickness
- $t_2$ = Chip thickness after cutting

### Derivation Steps:

**Step 1: Geometry of the shear plane.**

From the cutting geometry, the shear plane is inclined at angle φ to the cutting velocity. The relationship between chip thickness and shear angle is:

$$
t_1 = s \sin \phi
$$

**Step 2: The deformed chip thickness.**

$$
t_2 = s \sin(\phi - \alpha)
$$

Where s is the shear plane length projected.

**Step 3: Form the ratio.**

$$
r = \dfrac{t_1}{t_2} = \dfrac{s \sin \phi}{s \sin(\phi - \alpha)} = \dfrac{\sin \phi}{\sin(\phi - \alpha)}
$$

**Step 4: Express the shear strain γ.**

The shear strain in the primary shear zone is:

$$
\gamma = \cot \phi + \tan(\phi - \alpha)
$$

This is the **non-dimensional strain** representing how much the material has been deformed during cutting.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 ESE-Style Problem)

### Problem:
A turning operation is performed on a **mild steel workpiece** with the following parameters:
- **Rake angle (α)** = +15°
- **Friction coefficient (μ)** at tool-chip interface = 0.5
- **Uncut chip thickness (t₁)** = 0.25 mm
- **Cutting velocity (V)** = 120 m/min

### Find:
1. The **friction angle (β)**.
2. The **shear angle (φ)** using Merchant's equation.
3. The **chip thickness (t₂)**.
4. The **chip thickness ratio (r)**.

### Solution:

**Part (1): Friction Angle**

$$
\tan \beta = \mu = 0.5
$$

$$
\beta = \tan^{-1}(0.5) = 26.57°
$$

**Part (2): Shear Angle (Merchant's Equation)**

$$
\phi = 45° + \dfrac{\alpha}{2} - \dfrac{\beta}{2}
$$

$$
\phi = 45° + \dfrac{15°}{2} - \dfrac{26.57°}{2}
$$

$$
\phi = 45° + 7.5° - 13.28°
$$

$$
\phi = 39.22°
$$

**Part (3): Chip Thickness**

From the chip thickness ratio relation:

$$
r = \dfrac{\sin \phi}{\sin(\phi - \alpha)}
$$

$$
r = \dfrac{\sin 39.22°}{\sin(39.22° - 15°)} = \dfrac{\sin 39.22°}{\sin 24.22°}
$$

$$
r = \dfrac{0.6321}{0.4102} = 1.541
$$

Therefore:

$$
t_2 = \dfrac{t_1}{r} = \dfrac{0.25 \text{ mm}}{1.541} = 0.1622 \text{ mm}
$$

**Part (4): Verification of the Result**

Since $t_2 < t_1$, the chip has **thinned** during cutting — this is consistent with a **positive rake angle** where the chip is sheared cleanly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight:** A chip thickness ratio of ~1.5 indicates a **medium-strength material** being machined with good chip formation. Ratios close to 1.0 indicate ductile materials, while ratios > 2.0 indicate brittle materials (chip breaks into segments).

---

## 3.5 Effect of Rake Angle on Cutting Force — Analytical Proof

### Setup:
Consider a 2D orthogonal cutting model. The horizontal component of the resultant cutting force (R) is:

$$
F_c = R \cos(\beta - \alpha)
$$

### Effect of Increasing Positive Rake:
- As α increases positively, $(\beta - \alpha)$ decreases.
- $\cos(\beta - \alpha)$ increases (since the angle becomes smaller).
- Therefore, $F_c$ **decreases** for the same shear strength.

### Effect of Negative Rake:
- As α becomes negative, $(\beta - \alpha)$ becomes larger.
- $\cos(\beta - \alpha)$ decreases.
- Therefore, $F_c$ **increases**.

> [!TIP]
> **This is why positive rake is preferred for soft, ductile materials** — it reduces cutting force and hence power consumption, while a **strong cutting edge (slightly negative rake)** is needed for hard, abrasive materials to prevent edge chipping.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Study of Rake Configurations

| Property | Positive Rake (+α) | Zero Rake (α = 0) | Negative Rake (-α) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cutting Force (F_c)** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| **Power Consumption** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| **Cutting Edge Strength** | Weakest | Medium | Strongest |
| **Heat Generation** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| **Surface Finish** | Best (in ductile) | Good | Moderate |
| **Tool Life** | Lower in hard mat. | Moderate | Best in hard mat. |
| **Chip Curl** | Tighter curl | Medium | Looser, flatter |
| **Best For** | Aluminum, mild steel | Brass, cast iron | Hardened steel, ceramics |
| **Vibration Tendency** | Higher | Lower | Lowest |
| **Recommended Speed** | Higher speeds OK | Moderate | Lower speeds preferred |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Rake Angle and Clearance Angle. State their typical values for machining mild steel. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (Point-wise, KTU Board Standard):**

**Rake Angle (α):** The angle between the rake face of the cutting tool and a reference plane (usually the horizontal plane through the cutting edge), measured in the orthogonal plane. It governs chip flow and shear deformation.

**Clearance Angle (γ):** The angle between the flank (clearance) face of the cutting tool and a plane tangent to the machined work surface. It prevents rubbing of the flank face on the workpiece.

**Typical Values for Mild Steel (Orthogonal Cutting):**
- Rake Angle (α) = +10° to +20° (positive rake preferred for ductile mild steel).
- Primary Clearance Angle (γ) = 6° to 10°.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Positive, Zero, and Negative Rake Angles. State one application for each. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Type | Definition | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Positive Rake (α > 0)** | Rake face slopes downward toward the cutting edge from the reference plane. | Machining of **aluminum alloys, brass, mild steel** — reduces cutting force and improves finish. |
| **Zero Rake (α = 0)** | Rake face coincides with the reference plane. | Machining of **cast iron, bronze** — provides moderate strength with average chip control. |
| **Negative Rake (α < 0)** | Rake face slopes upward away from the cutting edge. | Machining of **hardened steel (HRC > 50), interrupted cuts (milling)** — strengthens cutting edge against chipping. |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions are 14 marks with **internal choice** — students must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A: 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Merchant's Equation & Shear Angle Derivation

**Q:** With the help of a neat sketch, derive **Merchant's shear angle equation**. State its significance in metal cutting.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Diagram Description (2 marks):**

A 2D orthogonal cutting diagram showing:
- Cutting velocity **V** horizontal.
- Rake face inclined at angle **α** above the horizontal.
- Shear plane inclined at angle **φ** to V.
- Cutting force **F** along V, friction force **F_f** along rake face, normal force **N** perpendicular to rake face.
- Shear force **F_s** along shear plane.

**2. Derivation Steps (5 marks):**

**Step 1: Force balance on the chip.**

Forces acting on the chip in equilibrium:
- Friction force: $F_f = \mu N = N \tan \beta$
- Resultant: $R = \sqrt{F^2 + N^2 - 2FN \cos(90° - \alpha + \beta)}$

**Step 2: Shear plane force equilibrium.**

Resolving forces along the shear plane:

$$
F \sin \phi = F_s \cos \phi - N \sin \beta
$$

**Step 3: Perpendicular to shear plane:**

$$
F \cos \phi = F_s \sin \phi + N \cos \beta
$$

**Step 4: Elimination of F_s and N.**

Dividing and simplifying with the friction relation:

$$
2\phi + \beta - \alpha = 90°
$$

**Step 5: Solving for shear angle:**

$$
\boxed{\phi = 45° + \dfrac{\alpha}{2} - \dfrac{\beta}{2}}
$$

**3. Significance (included in 5 marks):**

The shear angle φ determines:
- **Chip thickness** (thinner chips for larger φ).
- **Cutting force magnitude.**
- **Power consumption.**
- **Heat generation in the primary shear zone.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Common Marks Loss):**
> - **MISTAKE 1:** Students often write $\phi = 45° + \alpha/2 + \beta/2$ (sign error). The correct form has a **MINUS** sign for β/2.
> - **MISTAKE 2:** Forgetting to define the friction angle β relation: $\tan \beta = \mu$.
> - **MISTAKE 3:** Skipping the diagram — at least **2 marks** are reserved for the neat sketch in KTU valuation.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Numerical Problem on Tool Geometry

**Q:** In an orthogonal turning operation, the tool has an orthogonal rake angle of **+20°** and a normal rake angle of **+18°**. The cutting edge inclination angle is **15°**. Verify the relationship between the rake angles. If the uncut chip thickness is **0.3 mm** and the friction angle is **30°**, calculate the **shear angle** and **chip thickness ratio**.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Verification of Rake Angle Relation (3 marks):**

The standard relation is:

$$
\tan \alpha_n = \tan \alpha \cdot \cos \lambda
$$

Substituting the given values:

$$
\tan \alpha_n = \tan 20° \cdot \cos 15°
$$

$$
\tan \alpha_n = (0.3640) \cdot (0.9659)
$$

$$
\tan \alpha_n = 0.3516
$$

$$
\alpha_n = \tan^{-1}(0.3516) = 19.36° \approx 18° \text{ (within tolerance)}
$$

**The relation is verified** (small difference is due to rounding of the given data).

**2. Shear Angle Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
\phi = 45° + \dfrac{\alpha}{2} - \dfrac{\beta}{2}
$$

$$
\phi = 45° + \dfrac{20°}{2} - \dfrac{30°}{2}
$$

$$
\phi = 45° + 10° - 15° = 40°
$$

**3. Chip Thickness Ratio (2 marks):**

$$
r = \dfrac{\sin \phi}{\sin(\phi - \alpha)} = \dfrac{\sin 40°}{\sin(40° - 20°)} = \dfrac{\sin 40°}{\sin 20°}
$$

$$
r = \dfrac{0.6428}{0.3420} = 1.879
$$

**4. Final Result:**

The **shear angle φ = 40°** and the **chip thickness ratio r = 1.879**. The chip thickness after cutting:

$$
t_2 = \dfrac{t_1}{r} = \dfrac{0.3}{1.879} = 0.1596 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:**
> - **MISTAKE 1:** Using degrees and radians inconsistently in the sine calculation.
> - **MISTAKE 2:** Forgetting to convert angles properly in the chip thickness ratio formula.
> - **MISTAKE 3:** Not mentioning the units (mm) in the final answer.

---

### Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Tool Nomenclature and ASA System

**Q:** With the help of a labeled diagram, explain the **ASA (American Standards Association) system** of tool nomenclature. List all **seven basic tool angles** and state the reference plane for each.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Seven Basic Tool Angles — Tabular List (4 marks):**

| # | Tool Angle | Symbol | Reference Plane | Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Back Rake Angle | αᵦ | Plane A (Longitudinal-Vertical) | Controls chip flow across the nose |
| 2 | Side Rake Angle | αₛ | Plane A (Longitudinal-Vertical) | Main chip-controlling angle |
| 3 | Cutting Edge Inclination | λ | Plane A (Reference Plane) | Strengthens cutting edge |
| 4 | Side Cutting Edge Angle (Approach Angle) | ψᵣ or ψₛ | Plane V-V (Cutting Plane) | Affects chip thickness and feed direction |
| 5 | End Cutting Edge Angle | ψₑ | Plane V-V (Cutting Plane) | Affects surface finish at nose |
| 6 | Rake Angle (Orthogonal) | α | Plane P-P (Plane of Motion) | Governs chip deformation |
| 7 | Clearance Angle | γ | Plane P-P (Plane of Motion) | Prevents flank rubbing |

**2. Diagram Description (3 marks):**

The diagram should show:
- A 3D view of the single-point cutting tool.
- Three mutually perpendicular reference planes: **Plane A** (horizontal through cutting edge), **Plane V-V** (parallel to cutting velocity and feed), and **Plane P-P** (perpendicular to cutting edge).
- All seven angles labeled with their standard symbols.

**Reference Plane Summary:**
- **Plane A (Reference Plane):** Horizontal plane through the cutting edge, perpendicular to the cutting velocity.
- **Plane V-V (Cutting Plane):** Plane containing the cutting velocity and the feed direction.
- **Plane P-P (Plane of Motion / Orthogonal Plane):** Plane perpendicular to the cutting edge projection.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Effect of Rake and Clearance Angles

**Q:** Explain the effect of **rake angle** and **clearance angle** on cutting performance. State the recommended values for machining **aluminum** and **hardened steel**.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Effect of Rake Angle (3.5 marks):**

**Positive Rake:**
- Reduces cutting force and power.
- Improves chip flow and surface finish.
- Lowers cutting temperature.
- Weakens cutting edge.

**Negative Rake:**
- Strengthens cutting edge.
- Suitable for hard materials and interrupted cuts.
- Increases cutting force and power.
- Higher heat generation.

**2. Effect of Clearance Angle (2 marks):**

- **Too small clearance:** Flank rubs against workpiece, causing excessive wear, poor finish, and heat buildup.
- **Optimum clearance (5°-15°):** Clean cutting, long tool life, good surface finish.
- **Too large clearance:** Weak cutting edge, prone to chipping and vibration.

**3. Recommended Values (1.5 marks):**

| Material | Rake Angle (α) | Clearance Angle (γ) |
|---|---|---|
| **Aluminum (Soft, Ductile)** | +25° to +30° | 10° to 12° |
| **Hardened Steel (HRC > 50)** | -5° to -10° | 5° to 8° |

**Justification:**
- Aluminum is soft and ductile — high positive rake reduces cutting force and prevents built-up edge.
- Hardened steel is hard and abrasive — negative rake strengthens the edge to resist chipping, with lower clearance to provide support.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:**
> - **MISTAKE 1:** Confusing rake angle with clearance angle in the table.
> - **MISTAKE 2:** Not justifying the recommended values (always explain WHY a particular value is chosen for a material).
> - **MISTAKE 3:** Writing vague statements like "rake angle is important" without specifying the mechanism.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision — Bookmark This Section!**

### 🔑 Definitions You MUST Memorize:
- ☐ **Rake Angle (α):** Angle between rake face and reference plane (orthogonal plane).
- ☐ **Clearance Angle (γ):** Angle between flank face and machined surface.
- ☐ **Shear Angle (φ):** Angle between shear plane and cutting velocity.
- ☐ **Friction Angle (β):** $\tan \beta = \mu$ (friction coefficient).
- ☐ **Cutting Edge Inclination (λ):** Inclination of cutting edge in reference plane.

### 🔑 Key Formulas (Must Know by Heart):
- ☐ **Merchant's Equation:** $\phi = 45° + \alpha/2 - \beta/2$
- ☐ **Chip Thickness Ratio:** $r = \sin \phi / \sin(\phi - \alpha)$
- ☐ **Shear Strain:** $\gamma = \cot \phi + \tan(\phi - \alpha)$
- ☐ **NRS to ORS:** $\tan \alpha_n = \tan \alpha \cdot \cos \lambda$
- ☐ **Shear Force:** $F_s = F \cos(\phi + \beta - \alpha)$

### 🔑 Three Types of Rake Angles:
- ☐ **Positive Rake:** +10° to +30° for soft materials.
- ☐ **Zero Rake:** 0° for cast iron and brass.
- ☐ **Negative Rake:** -5° to -10° for hard materials.

### 🔑 Three Rake Systems:
- ☐ **ORS (Orthogonal Rake System)** — most common.
- ☐ **NRS (Normal Rake System)** — for complex cutting edges.
- ☐ **MRS (Maximum Rake System)** — equals ORS for straight edges.

### 🔑 Seven Basic Tool Angles (ASA System):
- ☐ Back Rake, Side Rake, Cutting Edge Inclination.
- ☐ Side Cutting Edge Angle (Approach), End Cutting Edge Angle.
- ☐ Rake Angle (Orthogonal), Clearance Angle.

### 🔑 Three Reference Planes:
- ☐ **Plane A** (Reference Plane) — horizontal, perpendicular to V.
- ☐ **Plane V-V** (Cutting Plane) — contains V and feed direction.
- ☐ **Plane P-P** (Orthogonal Plane) — perpendicular to cutting edge.

### 🔑 Material-to-Angle Quick Reference:
- ☐ **Aluminum:** +25° rake, 10° clearance.
- ☐ **Mild Steel:** +15° rake, 8° clearance.
- ☐ **Cast Iron:** 0° rake, 6° clearance.
- ☐ **Hardened Steel:** -5° rake, 5° clearance.

### 🔑 Common Examiner Traps to Avoid:
- ☐ **Sign error** in Merchant's equation (β/2 is MINUS, not PLUS).
- ☐ **Confusing rake and clearance** — they are on DIFFERENT faces.
- ☐ **Skipping the diagram** in derivation questions (loses 2 marks).
- ☐ **No units** in numerical answers (loses 0.5-1 mark).
- ☐ **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians** in trig calculations.

### 🔑 Engineering Applications (For "Real-World" Questions):
- ☐ **Tool holder design** uses NRS-ORS conversion.
- ☐ **CNC programming** uses shear angle for chip load estimation.
- ☐ **Tool wear analysis** uses clearance angle reduction as a wear indicator.
- ☐ **Cutting parameter optimization** uses Merchant's force analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Always draw a **neat labeled diagram** before any derivation question. Examiners reward clarity, and a well-drawn diagram can earn **partial marks** even if the derivation has minor errors. Also, **state assumptions** explicitly (e.g., "assuming orthogonal cutting, steady-state, and continuous chip formation") — this demonstrates professional engineering practice and earns full valuation marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In metal cutting theory, a **chip** is defined as the layer of workpiece material that is removed by the cutting edge of a single-point or multi-point cutting tool during a machining operation. The **type of chip** is fundamentally determined by the **ductility of the work material**, the **cutting conditions** (speed, feed, depth of cut), the **tool geometry** (rake angle), and the presence or absence of a built-up edge.

According to the standard KTU 2024 classification framework, the three primary categories of chips formed during orthogonal and oblique metal cutting are:

1. **Type 1 — Continuous Chip** (also called ribbon chip or flow chip)
2. **Type 2 — Discontinuous Chip** (also called segmented or serrated chip)
3. **Type 3 — Continuous Chip with Built-Up Edge (BUE)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Chip morphology is a *direct visual diagnostic* of the machinability of a workpiece, the integrity of the cutting tool, and the efficiency of the cutting parameters. Examiners frequently test the relationship between chip type and cutting conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Engineering Definition:** A chip is the plastically deformed, sheared-off workpiece material that slides up the rake face of a tool. Its shape, thickness, and color carry vital information about the cutting process.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **peeling a potato with a vegetable peeler**:

- If the potato is **soft and moist**, the peel comes off as one **long, unbroken ribbon** that curls smoothly — this is exactly like a **continuous chip** (ductile material, sharp tool, high cutting speed).
- If the potato is **cold, hard, and brittle**, the peel breaks into **small, jagged fragments** that fly off — this mimics a **discontinuous chip** (brittle material like cast iron, low cutting speed, large feed).
- If the peeler is slightly **dull or sticky** with residue, a small lump of potato pulp sticks to the blade edge, and the ribbon comes out with a small white lump attached — this is the **built-up edge (BUE)** phenomenon.

This analogy makes the three chip types instantly memorable for KTU viva and short-answer questions.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

Key chip-formation metrics that must be memorized for KTU 2024:

- **Shear Angle ($\phi$):** typically **$25°$ to $75°$** in conventional cutting.
- **Cutting Ratio ($r$):** dimensionless, defined as the ratio of chip thickness before cut to chip thickness after cut.
- **Standard Chip Thickness Coefficient:** $r = \dfrac{t_1}{t_2}$ where $t_1$ is uncut chip thickness and $t_2$ is cut chip thickness.
- **Built-Up Edge Temperature Range:** typically forms between **$300°C$ to $600°C$** at the tool-chip interface for ferrous materials.

> [!NOTE]
> All values above are **empirical ranges** verified from standard manufacturing textbooks used in KTU-affiliated colleges. They are accepted as correct for university examination answers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Primary Types of Chips — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Type 1 — Continuous Chip (Ribbon / Flow Chip)

- **Formation Logic:** Material flows plastically and continuously over the tool's rake face without fracture. The shear plane remains stable.
- **Visual Appearance:** A long, curled, unbroken ribbon. The underside (in contact with rake face) is smooth and shiny; the upper side shows a saw-tooth pattern from the shear steps.
- **Conditions that Produce It:**
  * Ductile workpiece material (e.g., mild steel, aluminum, copper).
  * **High cutting speed** (typically > 60 m/min for steel).
  * **Large positive rake angle** (sharp tool).
  * **Light feed** and adequate cooling.
- **Preferred For:** Finish machining and high productivity turning because cutting forces are lowest and surface finish is best.
- **Tool Wear Pattern:** Crater wear is the dominant mode because the chip slides continuously over the rake face.

### 2.1.2 Type 2 — Discontinuous Chip (Segmented / Serrated Chip)

- **Formation Logic:** The material is brittle and fractures repeatedly along the shear plane before the chip can flow continuously. Each segment is produced by a discrete crack event.
- **Visual Appearance:** A series of small, separate, wedge-shaped pieces. The fracture surface appears rough and crystalline.
- **Conditions that Produce It:**
  * Brittle workpiece material (e.g., cast iron, bronze).
  * **Low cutting speed**.
  * **Negative or small positive rake angle**.
  * **Heavy feed** and insufficient cutting fluid.
- **Preferred For:** Rough machining of castings where chip disposal is easy and continuous chip tangling must be avoided.
- **Tool Wear Pattern:** Flank wear dominates. Cutting forces fluctuate, which can cause vibration and chatter.

### 2.1.3 Type 3 — Continuous Chip with Built-Up Edge (BUE)

- **Formation Logic:** At low-to-moderate cutting speeds, the **temperature and pressure** at the tool-chip interface cause workpiece atoms to **weld (micro-weld)** to the tool tip. This welded lump is the BUE. As the chip flows over, the BUE intermittently breaks off and reforms, leaving a fragment embedded in the chip (and a corresponding void in the tool).
- **Visual Appearance:** The chip looks continuous, but a bright, irregular lump is visible at the free surface of the chip (the side originally against the tool).
- **Conditions that Produce It:**
  * Ductile materials at **low cutting speed** (roughly 15–30 m/min for mild steel).
  * **Dull or improperly ground tools** with small rake angle.
  * **Inadequate or wrong cutting fluid**.
- **Why It Is Undesirable:** The BUE periodically shears off, carrying away tiny chunks of tool material, accelerating **flank wear**. The BUE also leaves a poor surface finish because it momentarily substitutes for the cutting edge.
- **How to Eliminate BUE:** Increase cutting speed, use a sharper tool, increase rake angle, use proper cutting fluid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Trick Question Warning:** Students often confuse BUE with crater wear. Remember: BUE is workpiece material welded to the tool; crater wear is a depression formed on the rake face by the flowing chip.

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' — Logical Flow

The chip type transitions as follows when cutting speed is increased on a ductile material:

$$
\text{Discontinuous (low v)} \rightarrow \text{Continuous with BUE} \rightarrow \text{Continuous (high v)}
$$

- **Why?** At low speeds, the tool tip pressure welds material (BUE forms). At higher speeds, the increased temperature softens the BUE and it can no longer adhere, producing a clean continuous chip.
- **How is this observed in the lab?** A turning experiment on mild steel at 20 m/min shows BUE; the same job at 80 m/min produces a clean curled ribbon.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Symbol Notes | KTU Use |
|---|-------------------|---------|--------------|---------|
| 1 | $r = \dfrac{t_1}{t_2}$ | Cutting Ratio (Chip Thickness Ratio) | $t_1$ = uncut chip thickness; $t_2$ = chip thickness after cut | Determines degree of plastic deformation |
| 2 | $\tan\phi = \dfrac{r \cos\alpha}{1 - r \sin\alpha}$ | Shear Angle Equation | $\alpha$ = rake angle; $\phi$ = shear angle | Most-tested formula in KTU 2024 |
| 3 | $r = \dfrac{\sin\phi}{\cos(\phi-\alpha)}$ | Alternative shear-angle form | Derived from Merchant's circle | Quick substitution use |
| 4 | $\tau_s = \dfrac{F_s \sin\phi}{t_1 \, w}$ | Average Shear Stress | $F_s$ = shear force; $w$ = width of cut | Numerical problems |
| 5 | $V_c = \pi D N$ | Cutting Speed (m/min) | $D$ = work diameter (mm); $N$ = spindle rpm | Process parameter design |
| 6 | $F_c = \tau_s \, A_s$ | Cutting Force component | $A_s$ = shear area | Force analysis problems |
| 7 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\cos\alpha}{\sin\phi \cos(\phi-\alpha)}$ | Strain in chip | $\varepsilon$ = shear strain | Merchant's analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, the symbol $\vert$ has been deliberately avoided. For absolute values use the latex command `\vert` or `\mid` inside equations instead of the vertical pipe, so that KTU markdown renderers do not break the table layout.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CNC Machining Centers:** Operators visually inspect the chip color and shape to verify whether the recommended cutting parameters are being followed. A blue or purple chip indicates excessive heat; a straw-colored chip is the sweet spot for HSS tools.
- **Automotive Industry:** Chip morphology control is critical in transfer-line production of engine blocks and crankshafts to ensure consistent tool life.
- **Aerospace Machining of Titanium:** Discontinuous chips are often intentionally encouraged because continuous titanium chips are highly flammable and can cause tool entanglement.
- **Industry 4.0 Smart Machining:** Modern CNC systems integrate **chip morphology sensors** (vision-based) that auto-adjust feed and speed in real time to maintain a desired chip type — a direct industrial application of this KTU topic.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Shear Angle Relationship from Chip Geometry

This is the **most heavily tested KTU 2024 derivation** for chip formation. We shall derive it step-by-step from the geometry of orthogonal cutting.

### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions

Consider a single-point turning tool cutting a ductile workpiece at rake angle $\alpha$, shear angle $\phi$, and uncut chip thickness $t_1$. The chip emerges with thickness $t_2$ after plastic deformation along the shear plane.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Volume constancy assumption.**
The metal removed per unit width must equal the metal in the chip (incompressible plastic flow):

$$
t_1 \cdot l_1 \;=\; t_2 \cdot l_2
$$

where $l_1$ is the uncut chip length and $l_2$ is the cut chip length. The cutting ratio is therefore:

$$
r = \frac{t_1}{t_2} = \frac{l_2}{l_1}
$$

**Step 2 — Geometric construction of the shear plane.**
Draw the orthogonal cutting diagram: tool tip at origin, rake face inclined at angle $\alpha$ above horizontal, shear plane inclined at angle $\phi$ from the cutting velocity direction. The uncut chip thickness $t_1$ lies along the vertical, and the cut chip thickness $t_2$ lies perpendicular to the rake face.

**Step 3 — Apply the sine rule in the velocity triangle.**
The chip velocity $V$ is along the rake face, the shear velocity $V_s$ is along the shear plane, and the cutting velocity $V_c$ is horizontal. The angle between $V$ and $V_c$ is $\alpha$, and the angle between $V_s$ and $V_c$ is $\phi$.

By the sine rule on the velocity triangle:

$$
\frac{V_c}{\sin(90°+\theta-\alpha)} = \frac{V_s}{\sin(90°-\alpha)} = \frac{V}{\sin\phi}
$$

where $\theta$ is the tool-included angle (not required in 2-D orthogonal cutting, equal to 90°).

**Step 4 — Relate $r$, $\phi$, and $\alpha$ through chip thickness.**
From the geometry of the shear plane cutting across thickness $t_1$ and producing $t_2$:

$$
t_2 = \frac{t_1 \cos(\phi-\alpha)}{\sin\phi}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
r = \frac{t_1}{t_2} = \frac{\sin\phi}{\cos(\phi-\alpha)}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\phi$ explicitly.**
Cross-multiply:

$$
r \cos(\phi-\alpha) = \sin\phi
$$

Expand $\cos(\phi-\alpha)$ using the cosine subtraction identity:

$$
r \left[ \cos\phi \cos\alpha + \sin\phi \sin\alpha \right] = \sin\phi
$$

Divide throughout by $\cos\phi$:

$$
r \left[ \cos\alpha + \tan\phi \sin\alpha \right] = \tan\phi
$$

Collect the $\tan\phi$ terms on one side:

$$
r \cos\alpha = \tan\phi \, (1 - r \sin\alpha)
$$

Therefore:

$$
\tan\phi = \frac{r \cos\alpha}{1 - r \sin\alpha}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **canonical Merchant shear-angle equation** and is the formula most often expected in KTU 2024 Part B (14-mark) questions.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 2024 Style)

**Problem:** In an orthogonal cutting operation, the uncut chip thickness is $t_1 = 0.50$ mm and the cut chip thickness is $t_2 = 0.80$ mm. The tool rake angle is $\alpha = 10°$. Determine the shear angle $\phi$.

**Solution — Step 1: Compute the cutting ratio.**

$$
r = \frac{t_1}{t_2} = \frac{0.50}{0.80} = 0.625
$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the shear-angle formula.**

$$
\tan\phi = \frac{r \cos\alpha}{1 - r \sin\alpha}
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the trigonometric components.**

$$
\cos 10° = 0.9848, \qquad \sin 10° = 0.1736
$$

$$
\tan\phi = \frac{0.625 \times 0.9848}{1 - 0.625 \times 0.1736}
$$

**Step 4: Compute numerator and denominator separately.**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 0.625 \times 0.9848 = 0.6155
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 1 - 0.1085 = 0.8915
$$

**Step 5: Final division and inverse tangent.**

$$
\tan\phi = \frac{0.6155}{0.8915} = 0.6904
$$

$$
\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.6904) = 34.62°
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This result (≈ 35°) lies within the **expected KTU range of 25°–75°**, confirming the answer is physically realistic. Always verify your shear-angle value falls in this range.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of the Three Chip Types

| Property | Continuous Chip | Discontinuous Chip | Continuous with BUE |
|----------|----------------|--------------------|---------------------|
| Material behaviour | Highly ductile | Brittle | Ductile |
| Cutting speed | High | Low | Low to moderate |
| Rake angle preferred | Large positive | Negative or small positive | Any |
| Surface finish produced | Best | Acceptable | Poorest |
| Cutting force fluctuation | Steady, low | High, fluctuating | Moderate, irregular |
| Tool wear mode | Crater wear | Flank wear | Flank wear + crater |
| Chip disposal | Difficult (long ribbon) | Easy (small pieces) | Moderate |
| Power consumption | Lowest | Highest | Moderate |
| Typical material | Mild steel, aluminium, copper | Cast iron, bronze | Mild steel, stainless steel |
| KTU 2024 exam weightage | High (most asked) | Medium | High (often confused) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Define the term "built-up edge" in metal cutting. List two conditions that favor its formation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **built-up edge (BUE)** is a wedge-shaped lump of work-material that welds to the cutting edge of the tool due to high temperature and pressure at the tool-chip interface. It is not part of the original tool geometry but forms dynamically during cutting.

**Two conditions favoring BUE formation (1 mark each):**
1. **Low cutting speed** (typically 15–30 m/min for mild steel), which produces sufficient heat but not enough to soften and dislodge the welded layer.
2. **Dull or improperly ground tool** with a **small positive or negative rake angle**, which raises contact stress at the tool tip. Inadequate cutting fluid also encourages BUE.

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Distinguish between a continuous chip and a discontinuous chip with respect to (i) work material, (ii) cutting force, and (iii) surface finish.**

**Model Answer (1 mark per correct pair):**

| Criterion | Continuous Chip | Discontinuous Chip |
|-----------|-----------------|--------------------|
| (i) Work material | Ductile (e.g., mild steel, aluminium) | Brittle (e.g., cast iron, bronze) |
| (ii) Cutting force | Steady and low | Fluctuating and high |
| (iii) Surface finish | Smooth, good finish | Rougher, occasional marks |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B-A. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **three types of chips** formed during metal cutting. Discuss the effect of cutting speed and rake angle on chip morphology. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** In an orthogonal cutting operation, the uncut chip thickness is **$0.40$ mm** and the chip thickness after cutting is **$0.75$ mm**. The tool rake angle is **$12°$**. Compute the **shear angle** and the **shear strain**. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**[2 marks] Definition and Type 1 — Continuous Chip:**
A chip is the plastically deformed workpiece material removed by the cutting tool. Three types are recognised. The **continuous chip** (ribbon chip) forms when ductile materials are cut at high speed with a sharp, large-positive rake tool. The chip emerges as one unbroken curled ribbon, giving the lowest cutting force and best surface finish.

**[2 marks] Type 2 — Discontinuous Chip:**
The **discontinuous chip** forms when brittle materials (cast iron, bronze) are cut, or when cutting speed is very low and feed is heavy. The shear zone cracks repeatedly, producing small, separate segments. Cutting force fluctuates; chip disposal is easy but vibration and chatter are common.

**[2 marks] Type 3 — Continuous Chip with BUE:**
A **continuous chip with built-up edge** forms at low-to-moderate cutting speed on ductile materials. Workpiece material welds to the tool tip, producing a lump (BUE) that periodically shears off. It degrades surface finish and accelerates tool wear. Eliminated by raising cutting speed, sharpening the tool, or improving rake angle.

**[1 mark] Effect of speed and rake angle:**
Increasing cutting speed transitions discontinuous → continuous-with-BUE → continuous chip. Increasing positive rake angle lowers cutting force and promotes continuous flow.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Cutting ratio (1 mark):**

$$
r = \frac{t_1}{t_2} = \frac{0.40}{0.75} = 0.5333
$$

**Step 2 — Shear angle using Merchant's equation (3 marks):**

$$
\tan\phi = \frac{r \cos\alpha}{1 - r \sin\alpha}
$$

Substitute $\alpha = 12°$, $\cos 12° = 0.9781$, $\sin 12° = 0.2079$:

$$
\tan\phi = \frac{0.5333 \times 0.9781}{1 - 0.5333 \times 0.2079} = \frac{0.5216}{1 - 0.1109} = \frac{0.5216}{0.8891} = 0.5867
$$

$$
\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.5867) = 30.39° \quad \text{(approximately } 30.4°\text{)}
$$

**Step 3 — Shear strain (3 marks):**
The shear strain in the chip is given by:

$$
\gamma = \cot\phi + \tan(\phi - \alpha)
$$

$$
\cot\phi = \frac{1}{\tan\phi} = \frac{1}{0.5867} = 1.7045
$$

$$
\phi - \alpha = 30.39° - 12° = 18.39° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tan(18.39°) = 0.3325
$$

$$
\gamma = 1.7045 + 0.3325 = 2.037
$$

**Final Answer:** Shear angle $\phi \approx 30.4°$ and shear strain $\gamma \approx 2.04$.

---

### Question B-B. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the formation of a **built-up edge** during metal cutting. Why is BUE undesirable, and what remedial measures are recommended? **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Derive the **Merchant shear-angle relationship**:

$$
\tan\phi = \frac{r \cos\alpha}{1 - r \sin\alpha}
$$

starting from the assumption of **volume constancy of the chip**. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**[2 marks] Mechanism of BUE formation:**
During cutting of ductile materials at low-to-moderate speeds, the tool-chip interface reaches a temperature of about **300–600 °C** for ferrous workpieces. At this range, the work material exhibits **athermal softening** but remains chemically reactive. The intimate contact between freshly cut work-material atoms and the tool face produces localized **micro-welds**. These welds accumulate at the cutting edge as a wedge-shaped lump called the built-up edge (BUE). The BUE is *not* permanent — it periodically fractures under chip-flow shear, sending fragments into the chip and leaving a corresponding crater on the tool.

**[2 marks] Why BUE is undesirable:**
- Surface finish is ruined because the BUE momentarily substitutes for the true cutting edge, leaving a torn surface.
- Dimensional accuracy of the workpiece is lost.
- BUE fragments entrained in the chip cause **flank wear** and rapid tool failure.
- Cutting force becomes irregular, increasing vibration.

**[2 marks] Remedial measures (any four):**
1. **Increase cutting speed** above the BUE formation range.
2. **Use a sharper tool** with a larger positive rake angle.
3. **Apply a suitable cutting fluid** (sulphurised or chlorinated oil) to reduce interface temperature.
4. **Use coated tools** (TiN, Al₂O₃) to reduce friction and welding tendency.
5. **Reduce feed rate** if BUE persists.

**[1 mark] Conclusion:**
Eliminating BUE is essential for high surface integrity, especially in finish machining of steels and aluminium alloys.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the full derivation. The step-by-step structure expected in a 7-mark answer is:

- **Step 1 (1 mark):** State volume constancy: $t_1 l_1 = t_2 l_2$, hence $r = t_1 / t_2 = l_2 / l_1$.
- **Step 2 (1 mark):** Sketch the orthogonal cutting diagram showing rake face, shear plane, $t_1$, $t_2$, $\alpha$, $\phi$.
- **Step 3 (2 marks):** Use sine rule on velocity triangle to relate $V_c$, $V_s$, and $V$, identifying angles $\alpha$ and $\phi$.
- **Step 4 (2 marks):** From chip geometry, $t_2 = t_1 \cos(\phi-\alpha) / \sin\phi$, hence $r = \sin\phi / \cos(\phi-\alpha)$.
- **Step 5 (1 mark):** Expand and rearrange to obtain:

$$
\tan\phi = \frac{r \cos\alpha}{1 - r \sin\alpha}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't confuse the uncut and cut chip thickness.** $t_1$ is the *uncut* (workpiece) thickness and $t_2$ is the *cut* (chip) thickness. The cutting ratio $r = t_1 / t_2$ is **always less than 1** because the chip is thicker than the original layer (material expands sideways after plastic deformation). If your answer gives $r > 1$, you have inverted the ratio — instant loss of 1 mark.
> 2. **Always state units explicitly.** KTU examiners allocate a half-mark for "Units" in numerical answers. Writing "$\phi = 30°$" without the degree symbol is a common mark-loser.
> 3. **Sketch quality matters.** In 7-mark answers, the examiner expects a *neat labeled sketch* of the shear zone, rake face, and chip thickness. A missing or sloppy sketch costs at least 1 to 2 marks.
> 4. **Built-up edge is NOT a tool failure mode** — it is a transient workpiece weld. Conflating BUE with crater wear or flank wear will lose marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Three types of chips:** Continuous, Discontinuous, Continuous with BUE. (★★★★★ most important)
- **Cutting Ratio:** $r = t_1 / t_2$ is always less than 1. (★★★★★)
- **Merchant's shear-angle formula:** $\tan\phi = r \cos\alpha / (1 - r \sin\alpha)$. (★★★★★)
- **Shear strain** in chip: $\gamma = \cot\phi + \tan(\phi-\alpha)$. (★★★★)
- **Higher cutting speed** transitions chip type: Discontinuous → Continuous with BUE → Continuous. (★★★★)
- **Higher positive rake angle** lowers cutting force and promotes continuous chip. (★★★★)
- **BUE temperature range** for steel: **300 °C – 600 °C**. (★★★)
- **BUE is undesirable** because it ruins surface finish, accelerates tool wear, and causes dimensional errors. (★★★★)
- **Remedies for BUE:** increase speed, sharpen tool, raise rake angle, use coated tools, apply cutting fluid. (★★★★)
- **Continuous chip is preferred** for finish machining; **discontinuous chip is preferred** for roughing brittle materials. (★★★★)
- **Chip colour coding (industry standard):**
  * Straw / golden ≈ 200–250 °C → good for HSS.
  * Blue / purple ≈ 300–400 °C → excessive heat, reduce speed.
- **KTU 2024 hot keywords:** shear plane, primary deformation zone, secondary deformation zone, chip thickness ratio, Merchant circle, orthogonal cutting, oblique cutting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Strategy:** Memorize the three-chip table, the shear-angle formula, the cutting ratio definition, and the BUE causes and remedies. These four items cover more than 80% of typical KTU questions on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tool Life - Taylor's Tool Life Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT404 - MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tool Life - Taylor's Tool Life Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Tool Life

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tool Life (T):** The actual cutting time (in minutes) during which a cutting tool performs its intended function satisfactorily, before it reaches a predefined **tool failure criterion** (most commonly a limiting **flank wear land width, VB = 0.3 mm** for HSS, or VB = 0.6 mm for carbide tools).

Tool life is one of the most critical performance metrics in any machining operation because it directly governs:
- **Productivity** (parts produced per hour)
- **Surface finish** and **dimensional accuracy**
- **Downtime** due to tool changes
- **Manufacturing cost per component**

The end of tool life is signalled by one of the following failure modes:
- **Flank wear** (VB reaches the critical value)
- **Crater wear** (depth of crater exceeds threshold)
- **Built-up edge (BUE)** formation affecting surface finish
- **Chipping or fracture** of the cutting edge
- **Excessive cutting temperature** causing plastic deformation

---

## 1.2 Taylor's Tool Life Equation – Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
