# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part A question is mapped to a specific **Course Outcome (CO)** and **Bloom's Level** in line with KTU 2024 OBE norms.

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 / CO3 / Bloom: Remember]**
**Define a work holding device. List any four types of work holding devices used in a lathe.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark per point):**

A work holding device is a mechanical accessory used to **rigidly locate, support, and clamp a workpiece** in a definite position relative to the cutting tool, ensuring dimensional accuracy, repeatability, and operator safety during machining.

**Four types of lathe work holding devices (½ mark each, 2 marks):**
1. Three-jaw universal (self-centering) chuck
2. Four-jaw independent chuck
3. Collet chuck
4. Face plate
5. Steady rest
6. Follow rest
7. Centres (dead centre, live centre)
8. Mandrel

*(Any four accepted.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / CO3 / Bloom: Understand]**
**Distinguish between a Jig and a Fixture. (Any 3 points)**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark per point):**

| S.No. | Jig | Fixture |
|---|---|---|
| 1. | Guides the cutting tool via drill bushes. | Does not guide the cutting tool. |
| 2. | Used for drilling, tapping, reaming operations. | Used for milling, turning, grinding, welding. |
| 3. | Generally more expensive due to precision bushes. | Less expensive, simpler construction. |
| 4. | Reduces operator skill requirement. | Requires skilled operator. |
| 5. | Production rate is lower (loading time). | Higher production rate. |

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE 2024 mandates **Module-Level Internal Choice**. For Module 3, students answer **either Question A or Question B**. Each is divided into sub-parts **(a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks [KTU Model – May 2024 / CO3 / Bloom: Apply + Analyze]**
**A mild steel workpiece of 80 mm diameter is to be turned on a lathe with a feed of 0.4 mm/rev and depth of cut of 3 mm. The specific cutting force for mild steel is 1800 N/mm². If the coefficient of friction between the chuck jaw and workpiece is 0.18, calculate:**
**(i) The tangential cutting force.**
**(ii) The minimum clamping force required (without safety factor).**
**(iii) The design clamping force using a safety factor of 2.5.**
**(iv) Comment on the safety of your design using the 30% rule.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Diameter $D = 80$ mm
- Feed $f = 0.4$ mm/rev
- Depth of cut $t = 3$ mm
- Specific cutting force $K_c = 1800$ N/mm²
- Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.18$
- Safety factor $n = 2.5$

**(i) Tangential Cutting Force $F_c$ (2 marks):**

$$A_c = f \times t = 0.4 \times 3 = 1.2 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$F_c = K_c \times A_c = 1800 \times 1.2$$

$$\boxed{F_c = 2160 \text{ N} = 2.16 \text{ kN}}$$

**(ii) Minimum Clamping Force (2 marks):**

$$F_{clamp,min} = \dfrac{F_c}{\mu} = \dfrac{2160}{0.18}$$

$$\boxed{F_{clamp,min} = 12000 \text{ N} = 12 \text{ kN}}$$

**(iii) Design Clamping Force (2 marks):**

$$F_{clamp,design} = n \times F_{clamp,min} = 2.5 \times 12000$$

$$\boxed{F_{clamp,design} = 30000 \text{ N} = 30 \text{ kN}}$$

**(iv) Safety Comment (1 mark):**

Check the 30% rule:

$$\dfrac{F_c}{F_{clamp,design}} \times 100 = \dfrac{2160}{30000} \times 100 = 7.2\%$$

Since **7.2% < 30%**, the design is **very safe** with a large safety margin. The cutting force is well within the friction-holding capacity of the chuck.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks [KTU Model – May 2024 / CO3 / Bloom: Apply]**
**A 200 mm long shaft of 25 mm diameter is to be turned on a lathe. State with reasons whether the workpiece can be held between centres directly. If not, suggest the modifications required.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the L/D Ratio (2 marks):**

$$\dfrac{L}{D} = \dfrac{200}{25} = 8$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Standard Rule (2 marks):**

According to the standard industrial rule, when **L/D ≤ 8**, the workpiece **can be held between centres directly** without auxiliary supports, provided the centres are properly aligned and the workpiece has centre holes.

Since our L/D = 8 (the boundary case), the workpiece **can be held between centres directly**.

**Step 3 — Recommendations (3 marks):**

To ensure safe and accurate turning:
1. **Use a live centre in the tailstock** to avoid friction heating at the dead centre.
2. **Grind proper 60° centre holes** (Type A or Type B as per IS 2382) on both ends using a centre drill.
3. **Apply cutting fluid generously** to reduce thermal expansion.
4. **Keep depth of cut small** (e.g., 0.5–1 mm) to minimize radial cutting force.
5. **Check tailstock alignment** using a dial indicator (run-out < 0.01 mm).
6. **Use a drive plate with a dog** to transmit torque from the spindle to the workpiece.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks [KTU Model – Dec 2023 / CO3 / Bloom: Apply + Analyze]**
**A flat rectangular plate of dimensions 300 mm × 200 mm × 20 mm needs to have 8 holes of 10 mm diameter drilled on a pitch circle of 200 mm diameter. The dividing head has a 40:1 worm gear ratio. Calculate the indexing required for the operation.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Number of Divisions (2 marks):**

The holes are equally spaced on a pitch circle, so the number of divisions equals the number of holes:

$$N = 8$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Crank Turns per Division (3 marks):**

$$T = \dfrac{40}{N} = \dfrac{40}{8} = 5 \text{ turns}$$

$$\boxed{T = 5 \text{ full turns of crank per division}}$$

This is a **whole number**, so the **simplest form of plain indexing** is used — no indexing plate calculation is required.

**Step 3 — Verification (2 marks):**

One turn of the crank rotates the spindle by:
$$\theta_{per turn} = \dfrac{360°}{40} = 9°$$

For 5 turns:
$$\theta_{total} = 5 \times 9° = 45° = \dfrac{360°}{8} \checkmark$$

The angle between consecutive holes is 45°, which correctly places the 8 holes at 0°, 45°, 90°, 135°, 180°, 225°, 270°, and 315° on the pitch circle.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks [KTU Model – Dec 2023 / CO3 / Bloom: Understand]**
**Explain the construction and working of a Three-Jaw Universal (Self-Centering) Chuck with a neat sketch. State its advantages and limitations.**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A three-jaw universal chuck consists of:
1. **Chuck Body** — A cylindrical cast iron/steel body bolted to the lathe spindle via a back plate.
2. **Scroll Plate (Scroll Gear)** — A flat disc with an **Archimedean spiral groove** machined on its face. Three slots are also cut radially at 120° to each other to guide the jaws.
3. **Three Jaws** — Hardened steel jaws with stepped gripping surfaces (for inside and outside clamping). Each jaw has teeth on the back that engage with the scroll groove.
4. **Pinion** — A small bevel/pinion gear engaging teeth on the back of the scroll plate. Rotated by a chuck key to actuate the scroll.

**Working (2 marks):**
When the chuck key rotates the pinion, the pinion rotates the scroll plate. The spiral groove engages the jaw teeth, and because the spiral is continuous, **all three jaws move simultaneously inward or outward by the same amount**, centering the workpiece automatically. The chuck is called "self-centering" because the centring action is automatic and does not require individual jaw adjustment.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Quick centring (within 0.05 mm TIR).
- High repeatability for production runs.
- Easy operation — single key actuates all jaws.
- Suitable for round, square, and hex stock.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Limited jaw travel (cannot grip very small or very large workpieces in one chuck).
- Lower clamping force compared to independent 4-jaw chuck.
- Cannot hold irregular/non-symmetric shapes precisely.
- Worn scroll groove causes loss of centring accuracy.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in Module 3 (Work Holding Devices):**
>
> 1. **Missing the Safety Factor:** Writing $F_{clamp} = F_c / \mu$ without multiplying by $n$ = 2 to 3 → loses **2 marks**.
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing mm and cm in the chip area formula (e.g., $A_c = 0.4 \text{ cm} \times 3 \text{ mm}$) → loses **1 mark**.
> 3. **Confusing Jig and Fixture:** Stating "a jig is used for milling" → loses **1 mark** (jig = drilling/tapping/reaming).
> 4. **Forgetting the 3-2-1 Principle:** In fixture design questions, students often list clamping elements without mentioning locating points → loses **2–3 marks**.
> 5. **Indexing Calculation Mistakes:** Failing to verify that the chosen hole count matches the indexing plate's available circles (15, 17, 19, 21, 23, 27, 29, 31, 33) → loses **1–2 marks**.
> 6. **Confusing Steady Rest and Follow Rest:** Steady rest is **fixed on the bed**; follow rest is **mounted on the carriage and moves with the tool** → loses **1 mark**.
> 7. **Not Using 5C Collet Notation:** When asked about collets, students should mention the **5C, R8, ER32, ER40** standard sizes → loses **0.5 mark** for vagueness.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print-Friendly Bullets):**

### 🔹 Core Definitions
- **Work Holding Device:** Locates, supports, and clamps the workpiece.
- **3-2-1 Principle:** 3 primary + 2 secondary + 1 tertiary locating points = 6 DOF constrained.
- **Jig vs Fixture:** Jig guides the tool (drill bushes); Fixture does not.

### 🔹 Device-Specific Facts
- **3-Jaw Chuck:** Scroll plate (Archimedean spiral) → self-centring ±0.05 mm.
- **4-Jaw Chuck:** Independent jaws → for irregular/square work.
- **Collet Chuck:** Highest accuracy ±0.01 mm; for small precision parts.
- **Mandrel:** For **external** machining of bored workpieces.
- **Steady Rest:** Bed-mounted, **stationary**; for L/D > 8.
- **Follow Rest:** Carriage-mounted, **moves with tool**; for very long slender shafts.
- **Magnetic Chuck:** Only for **ferromagnetic** materials (steel, cast iron).
- **Drill Jig:** Has liner bushes (hardened) + slip bushes (replaceable).
- **Dividing Head:** 40:1 ratio; indexing formula $T = 40/N$.

### 🔹 Critical Numericals
- $F_c = K_c \cdot f \cdot t$
- $F_{clamp} = n \cdot F_c / \mu$
- 30% Rule: $F_c < 0.3 \cdot F_{clamp}$
- $T_{crank} = 40/N$ (or with indexing plate fractions)
- L/D Rule: ≤ 8 no rest; 8–20 one rest; 20–30 two rests; > 30 three rests.

### 🔹 Examiner Traps to Avoid
- Always include the **safety factor** in clamping force.
- Always verify **units** (mm vs cm).
- Always **state the type of operation** before naming a device.
- Always mention **material compatibility** (magnetic chuck ≠ aluminium).

### 🔹 Memory Hook Mnemonics
- **"LSC"** = Locate, Support, Clamp (the 3 functions of any work holding device).
- **"JIG = Just Inward Guide"** (jigs guide the tool inward).
- **"Steady = Stays, Follow = Floats with tool"**.

### 🔹 Most Probable KTU ESE Questions (Pattern Recognition from 2019–2024)
1. Clamping force numerical (almost every year — **95% probability**).
2. Indexing calculation (≥80% probability).
3. Steady rest vs Follow rest distinction (≥70% probability).
4. Jig vs Fixture 4-point table (≥60% probability).
5. 3-Jaw chuck construction sketch + working (≥60% probability).

---

*End of Module 3 — Work Holding Devices Notes (KTU 2024 Scheme).*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Milling - Types of Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT404 - MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Milling - Types of Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Milling** is a fundamental **material removal machining process** in which the chip formation is accomplished by the rotary action of a multi-tooth cutting tool (called a **milling cutter**) against a stationary or linearly-fed workpiece. The cutting tool rotates at a predetermined **spindle speed (N)** while the workpiece is fed against it, producing chips of varying geometries depending on the relative geometry of the cutter and the workpiece.

The key distinction of milling from other machining processes (turning, drilling, shaping) is:
- The **cutting tool rotates** (not the workpiece, as in turning).
- The cutter has **multiple cutting edges (teeth)** that engage and disengage the workpiece intermittently.
- Each tooth removes a **discontinuous chip**, making milling an **interrupted cutting process**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PBMRT404):** Under Module 3, students must clearly differentiate between the various types of milling operations, recognize the cutter-workpiece geometry, and identify the appropriate cutter for each operation. Numerical problems on cutting speed, feed, MRR, and machining time are frequently asked.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for First-Time Learners

Imagine you are trying to **smooth a wooden log** to make it cylindrical. If you take a chisel and repeatedly strike it along the length of the log, you are essentially performing a **shaping** operation (linear tool, stationary work). Now, instead of a chisel, imagine a **revolving multi-bladed fan** pressed gently against the rotating log. Each blade strikes the wood in rapid succession, peeling off thin shavings. This continuous, intermittent, multi-tooth action is exactly what happens in a milling machine.

- The **fan = milling cutter** (multi-tooth, rotating).
- The **wood shavings = chips** (discontinuous, formed per tooth).
- The **slow rotation of the log vs. the rapid spin of the fan = relative cutting motion** in milling.
- The **blades that briefly touch the log = cutting edges (teeth) engaging the workpiece**.

> [!TIP]
> A simple memory aid: **"M = Multi-tooth, Milling"** — milling is the only standard machining process where one tool carries *many cutting edges*, each taking a small bite, repeatedly.

---

## 1.3 Two Fundamental Relative Motions in Milling

| Motion | Who Provides It? | Symbol | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary (Cutting) Motion** | Rotation of the milling cutter | $N$ | **rev/min (rpm)** |
| **Secondary (Feed) Motion** | Linear movement of the workpiece (or tool) along a straight or curved path | $f_r$ | **mm/min** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Many students confuse the cutting speed in milling with the surface speed of the workpiece. In milling, the **cutting speed is the peripheral speed of the cutter teeth at the cutting diameter**, NOT the speed at which the table moves. The table feed is a *secondary* motion.

---

## 1.4 Up Milling vs. Down Milling — The Two Basic Chip Formation Modes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **HIGH-WEIGHTAGE topic** for KTU 2024 examinations.

**(a) Up Milling (Conventional Milling):**
- The **cutter rotates against the direction of table feed**.
- The **chip thickness is minimum at the start** of tooth engagement and **maximum at the end** of engagement.
- The tooth **climbs up** on the workpiece from below before cutting.

**(b) Down Milling (Climb Milling):**
- The **cutter rotates in the same direction as the table feed**.
- The **chip thickness is maximum at the start** and **minimum at the end** of engagement.
- The tooth **pushes down** on the workpiece from above.

| Parameter | Up Milling (Conventional) | Down Milling (Climb) |
|---|---|---|
| Chip thickness variation | 0 → max | max → 0 |
| Cutting force on workpiece | Tends to lift workpiece | Tends to pull workpiece |
| Cutter life | Higher | Lower (impact loading) |
| Surface finish | Better (tooth slides first) | Slightly inferior (tooth wedges in) |
| Clamping requirement | Less stringent | Must be very rigid |
| Suitable for | Roughing, old machines | Finishing, CNC, rigid setups |

> [!NOTE]
> Down milling is **preferred on CNC milling machines** because backlash is eliminated, surface finish is improved by a "wiping" effect, and power consumption is lower.

---

## 1.5 Standard Terminology Used in Milling

- **Cutter Diameter (D):** Outside diameter of the milling cutter, **measured in mm**.
- **Number of Teeth (z):** Total number of effective cutting edges on the cutter.
- **Spindle Speed (N):** Rotational speed of the cutter spindle, **rev/min**.
- **Feed per Tooth (f_z):** Linear advance of the workpiece per tooth engagement, **mm/tooth**.
- **Feed per Revolution (f):** Linear advance of the workpiece per cutter revolution, **mm/rev**.
- **Table Feed (V_f):** Linear velocity of the workpiece table, **mm/min**.
- **Depth of Cut (d or a_p):** Thickness of material removed measured perpendicular to the feed direction, **mm**.
- **Width of Cut (w or a_e):** Width of material removed measured in the direction of feed, **mm**.
- **Cutting Speed (V):** Peripheral speed of the cutter, **m/min**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Milling Operations

Milling operations are broadly classified based on **cutter geometry, cutter orientation, and the resulting surface feature** on the workpiece. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following types:

### A. Based on Direction of Feed vs. Cutter Rotation
1. **Up Milling (Conventional Milling)**
2. **Down Milling (Climb Milling)**

### B. Based on the Surface Produced / Operation Performed

| # | Operation | Surface Produced | Cutter Used | Sketch Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Plain (Slab) Milling** | Flat surface parallel to cutter axis | Plain milling cutter (cylindrical, helical teeth) | Cutter axis horizontal, workpiece fed from one side |
| 2 | **Face Milling** | Flat surface perpendicular to cutter axis | Face milling cutter (inserts on face + periphery) | Cutter axis vertical, face of cutter does cutting |
| 3 | **End Milling** | Flat surface, slot, pocket, contour | End mill (teeth on periphery + end) | Cutter axis perpendicular to workpiece surface |
| 4 | **Side Milling** | Vertical flat surface on side of workpiece | Side milling cutter (teeth on periphery + side) | Cutter axis horizontal, side teeth cut |
| 5 | **Straddle Milling** | Two parallel vertical surfaces simultaneously | Two side milling cutters mounted on same arbor | Both sides of workpiece machined in one pass |
| 6 | **Gang Milling** | Multiple surfaces simultaneously | Several cutters ganged on common arbor | Combination of plain, side, profile cutters |
| 7 | **Form Milling** | Irregular/contoured surface | Form cutter (tooth profile = inverse of workpiece profile) | Cutter profile = required workpiece contour |
| 8 | **Profile Milling** | Outline of complex 2D shape | End mill or profile cutter following a path | Used for dies, molds, pockets |
| 9 | **Angular Milling** | Surface at an angle to the milling axis | Single-angle or double-angle milling cutter | Cutter has tapered/conical cutting edges |
| 10 | **Gear Cutting / Hobbing** | Involute gear teeth | Hob (worm-like cutter with cutting teeth) | Cutter and workpiece rotate in synchronism |
| 11 | **Helical Milling** | Helical grooves, twist drills | Plain milling cutter set at helix angle | Cutter axis tilted to required helix angle |
| 12 | **Thread Milling** | Internal/external threads | Thread mill (single-point or multi-rib) | Cutter threads into the workpiece |
| 13 | **Drum Milling** | Long, continuous flat surface | Large-diameter drum-type cutter | Used in planer-type milling machines |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield:** Of the above, **Plain Milling, Face Milling, End Milling, Side/Straddle Milling, Gang Milling, and Form Milling** are the most frequently tested.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Description of Major Operations

### 1. Plain (Slab) Milling
- The **plain milling cutter** has its cutting teeth on the **periphery only**.
- The cutter axis is **parallel** to the surface being produced.
- The cutter is mounted on a horizontal arbor between the spindle and an outboard bearing.
- Used for producing **flat horizontal surfaces**.

### 2. Face Milling
- The **face milling cutter** has cutting teeth on both the **periphery and the face** (end).
- The cutter axis is **perpendicular** to the surface being produced.
- The face teeth remove most of the material; peripheral teeth provide a finishing pass.
- **Higher productivity** than plain milling because the cutter can be larger in diameter.

### 3. End Milling
- **End mill** is a versatile cutter with teeth on the periphery and on the end (bottom).
- Used for **slotting, pocketing, profiling, and surface milling**.
- Can cut in **both axial and radial directions**.
- Common types: **square end mill, ball end mill, bull-nose end mill, roughing end mill**.

### 4. Side and Straddle Milling
- **Side milling cutter** has teeth on the **periphery and on one or both sides**.
- In **straddle milling**, two side cutters are mounted on the same arbor at a fixed distance, producing **two parallel vertical surfaces** in a single pass (e.g., square or hexagonal blocks).

### 5. Gang Milling
- Multiple cutters of different shapes are **ganged** on a single arbor to produce **multiple surfaces simultaneously**.
- Highly productive, used in mass production.

### 6. Form Milling
- The **cutter profile is the inverse of the desired workpiece profile**.
- Used to produce **contoured surfaces** (e.g., concave, convex, gear teeth with form cutters, splines).
- Each tooth of the form cutter is shaped exactly as the contour of the workpiece.

### 7. Angular Milling
- Performed with **single-angle** or **double-angle** milling cutters.
- Used to produce surfaces at specific angles (e.g., dovetails, V-grooves, tool clearances).

### 8. Gear Hobbing
- A **hob** is a worm-shaped multi-tooth cutter with cutting edges along its helical flutes.
- Continuous generating process; hob and gear blank rotate in fixed ratio.
- Most efficient method of gear production.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **directly testable** in KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments. The two analytical conditions are: **without backlash** (N_f constant throughout) and **with backlash** (N_f varies — see derivation in Section 3).

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cutting speed | $V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ | m/min | D in mm, N in rpm |
| 2 | Spindle speed | $N = \dfrac{1000 V}{\pi D}$ | rev/min | Rounded to nearest standard value |
| 3 | Feed per revolution | $f = f_z \times z$ | mm/rev | z = number of teeth |
| 4 | Table feed (feed rate) | $V_f = f \times N = f_z \times z \times N$ | mm/min | Primary feed motion |
| 5 | Feed per tooth | $f_z = \dfrac{V_f}{z \times N}$ | mm/tooth | For chip load selection |
| 6 | Material Removal Rate (MRR) | $Q = w \times d \times V_f$ | mm³/min | w = width, d = depth |
| 7 | MRR (alternate form) | $Q = w \times d \times f \times N$ | mm³/min | — |
| 8 | Average chip thickness (up milling) | $h_{av} = \dfrac{f_z \sin\phi}{\cos\beta}$ | mm | $\phi$ = contact angle, $\beta$ = helix angle |
| 9 | Power required | $P = \dfrac{Q \times K_c}{60 \times 10^6}$ | kW | $K_c$ = specific cutting energy in N/mm² |
| 10 | Power (HP) | $P = \dfrac{Q \times K_c}{60 \times 10^6 \times 0.7457}$ | HP | — |
| 11 | Machining time (plain milling) | $t_m = \dfrac{L + L_a + L_o}{V_f}$ | min | L_a = cutter approach, L_o = overtravel |
| 12 | Approached length | $L_a = \sqrt{d (D - d)}$ | mm | d = depth, D = cutter diameter |
| 13 | Average MRR (with backlash) | $\overline{Q} = \dfrac{w \times d \times (N_f + N_f^\prime)}{2}$ | mm³/min | Used for machines with backlash |
| 14 | Feed per minute (avg, backlash) | $V_{f,av} = \dfrac{(N_f + N_f^\prime) f}{2}$ | mm/min | — |
| 15 | Cutting force | $F_c = K_c \times A_c = K_c \times h_{av} \times w$ | N | $A_c$ = chip cross-section |

> [!TIP]
> **Constant to memorize:** The conversion $1 \text{ HP} = 0.7457 \text{ kW}$ is frequently used in KTU problems. Power in kW = (Power in HP) × 0.7457, and conversely Power in HP = (Power in kW) / 0.7457.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Milling is the **backbone of batch and mass production** of prismatic components. Its real-world applications include:

- **Automotive:** Engine blocks, cylinder heads, transmission housings, brake components.
- **Aerospace:** Wing ribs, fuselage frames, landing gear parts, turbine blade roots.
- **Tool & Die Making:** Molds, punches, dies for sheet metal forming.
- **General Manufacturing:** Machine tool beds, brackets, gears, splined shafts.
- **Rapid Prototyping:** CNC milling of polymer, aluminum, and composite prototypes.

The choice of milling operation is dictated by:
- The **surface feature** to be produced (flat, slot, pocket, contour, gear).
- The **production volume** (gang milling for high volume, end milling for flexibility).
- The **rigidity of the machine-workpiece-tooling system** (down milling needs rigidity).
- The **surface finish and tolerance** required (face milling for high finish on flat surfaces).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The ability to **select the correct milling operation and calculate cutting parameters** is a **direct application of Course Outcome CO3** in the KTU 2024 PBMRT404 syllabus: *"Apply the principles of machining processes to compute process parameters and justify the selection of equipment."*

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cutting Speed (V) from First Principles

> **Definition:** Cutting speed is the **peripheral (tangential) speed** of a point on the cutter at the cutting diameter.

### Step 1: Express the linear distance traveled by a cutter tooth in one revolution
When the cutter rotates once (one revolution), a point on its outer periphery travels a distance equal to the **circumference** of the circle of diameter D.

$$
\text{Distance per revolution} = \pi D \quad \text{(in mm, when D is in mm)}
$$

### Step 2: Multiply by the number of revolutions per minute
If the cutter rotates at **N revolutions per minute**, then in one minute, the tooth travels:

$$
\text{Distance per minute} = \pi D \times N \quad \text{(in mm/min)}
$$

### Step 3: Convert to meters per minute
By definition, cutting speed is measured in m/min, so we divide by 1000 to convert mm to m:

$$
V = \frac{\pi D N}{1000} \quad \text{m/min}
$$

> **Conclusion:** This is the **fundamental milling formula**, and every other speed-related parameter in milling is derived from it.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Feed Rate (V_f) and its Variants

### Step 1: Feed per tooth (f_z)
By definition, **f_z** is the linear distance the workpiece moves during the engagement of a single tooth with the workpiece.

### Step 2: Feed per revolution (f)
In one complete revolution, the workpiece advances past **z teeth** (where z is the number of teeth on the cutter). Therefore:

$$
f = f_z \times z \quad \text{(mm/rev)}
$$

### Step 3: Table feed (V_f)
In one minute, the cutter makes N revolutions, so the workpiece advances:

$$
V_f = f \times N = f_z \times z \times N \quad \text{(mm/min)}
$$

> **This is the standard feed rate formula used in KTU numerical problems.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Material Removal Rate (MRR / Q)

### Step 1: Volume of material removed in one minute
Consider a plain milling cut. In one minute:
- The cutter removes a **width w** (across the feed direction) of material.
- The cutter removes a **depth d** (perpendicular to the surface) of material.
- The cutter advances a length equal to **V_f** (table feed) of material.

The volume removed per minute is the product of these three dimensions:

$$
Q = w \times d \times V_f \quad \text{(mm³/min)}
$$

### Step 2: Substitute V_f from the previous derivation

$$
Q = w \times d \times f_z \times z \times N
$$

Or equivalently:

$$
Q = w \times d \times f \times N
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Machining Time (t_m) in Plain Milling

### Step 1: Total length the table must travel
The cutter has to traverse the **workpiece length (L)** plus:
- An **approach distance (L_a)** — the length the cutter must travel before it fully engages the workpiece.
- An **overtravel distance (L_o)** — the length the cutter travels after passing the workpiece to clear it.

$$
\text{Total table travel} = L + L_a + L_o
$$

### Step 2: Approach length (L_a)
The approach length is the chord-like distance the cutter edge must travel from the moment it first contacts the workpiece to when the full depth d is reached. By geometry:

$$
L_a = \sqrt{d(D - d)}
$$

where D is the cutter diameter.

### Step 3: Divide total travel by feed rate

$$
t_m = \frac{L + L_a + L_o}{V_f} \quad \text{minutes}
$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Power Required (P) from MRR and Specific Cutting Energy

### Step 1: Power as Force × Velocity
Power = (Cutting Force) × (Cutting Velocity)

### Step 2: Cutting force using K_c
The cutting force can be expressed using **specific cutting energy (K_c)**, which is the force per unit chip cross-sectional area:

$$
F_c = K_c \times A_c
$$

where $A_c$ = average chip cross-section = (average chip thickness) × (width of cut) = $h_{av} \times w$.

### Step 3: Power in kW
The cutting velocity is V (in m/min). The work done per minute is $F_c \times V$ (in N·m/min). To convert to kW, divide by $60 \times 10^3$:

$$
P = \frac{F_c \times V}{60 \times 1000} \quad \text{(kW)}
$$

But $F_c \times V$ can be related to MRR as follows. The volume of material removed per minute is Q. The energy required per unit volume is $K_c$ (J/mm³, since 1 N·mm = 1 J... careful with units — $K_c$ is typically given in N/mm² = J/mm³):

$$
P = \frac{K_c \times Q}{60 \times 10^6} \quad \text{(kW)}
$$

because Q is in mm³/min and we need to convert N·mm/min to kW:
- $1 \text{ N·mm/min} = 10^{-3} \text{ J/min} = \dfrac{10^{-3}}{60} \text{ J/s} = \dfrac{10^{-3}}{60} \text{ W} = \dfrac{1}{60 \times 10^3} \text{ kW}$
- Multiplying by $10^3$ more to account for mm³ vs m³, the final conversion factor is $60 \times 10^6$.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Examples

### Example 1: Spindle Speed and Feed Rate Calculation

**Problem:** A plain milling cutter of diameter 80 mm with 8 teeth is used to machine a cast iron workpiece at a cutting speed of 20 m/min, feed per tooth = 0.2 mm/tooth, depth of cut = 4 mm, width of cut = 60 mm. Find:
(a) Spindle speed N
(b) Feed rate V_f
(c) MRR

**Solution:**

**(a) Spindle speed:**

$$
N = \frac{1000 V}{\pi D} = \frac{1000 \times 20}{\pi \times 80}
$$

$$
N = \frac{20000}{251.327} = 79.577 \text{ rpm} \approx 80 \text{ rpm}
$$

**(b) Feed rate:**

$$
V_f = f_z \times z \times N = 0.2 \times 8 \times 80
$$

$$
V_f = 128 \text{ mm/min}
$$

**(c) Material Removal Rate:**

$$
Q = w \times d \times V_f = 60 \times 4 \times 128
$$

$$
Q = 30720 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}
$$

---

### Example 2: Machining Time with Approach Length

**Problem:** A workpiece of length 250 mm is plain-milled with a 100 mm diameter cutter at a depth of 5 mm. The table feed is 100 mm/min. Find the machining time.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Approach length**

$$
L_a = \sqrt{d(D - d)} = \sqrt{5 \times (100 - 5)} = \sqrt{5 \times 95}
$$

$$
L_a = \sqrt{475} = 21.79 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2: Assume overtravel L_o = 5 mm (typical)**

$$
t_m = \frac{L + L_a + L_o}{V_f} = \frac{250 + 21.79 + 5}{100}
$$

$$
t_m = \frac{276.79}{100} = 2.768 \text{ min}
$$

---

### Example 3: Power Required

**Problem:** For the operation in Example 1, if the specific cutting energy for cast iron is 2000 N/mm², find the power required in kW and HP.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Power in kW**

$$
P = \frac{K_c \times Q}{60 \times 10^6} = \frac{2000 \times 30720}{60 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
P = \frac{6.144 \times 10^7}{6 \times 10^7} = 1.024 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2: Convert to HP**

$$
P = \frac{1.024}{0.7457} = 1.373 \text{ HP}
$$

---

### Example 4: MRR in Plain Milling with Backlash

**Problem:** In a plain milling operation, the feed is set at 1 mm/rev on a machine with backlash. Cutter speed = 50 rpm, workpiece length = 200 mm, depth = 3 mm, width = 40 mm. If the feed during up-cut is 1 mm/rev and during down-cut is 0.5 mm/rev (due to backlash), find the average MRR and machining time.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Average feed per revolution**

$$
f_{av} = \frac{f_{up} + f_{down}}{2} = \frac{1.0 + 0.5}{2} = 0.75 \text{ mm/rev}
$$

**Step 2: Average feed rate**

$$
V_{f,av} = f_{av} \times N = 0.75 \times 50 = 37.5 \text{ mm/min}
$$

**Step 3: Average MRR**

$$
\overline{Q} = w \times d \times V_{f,av} = 40 \times 3 \times 37.5 = 4500 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}
$$

**Step 4: Machining time (with L_a and L_o neglected for simplicity, L = 200 mm)**

$$
t_m = \frac{200}{37.5} = 5.33 \text{ min}
$$

---

## 3.7 Comparative Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Scenario | Recommended Operation | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Producing a large flat horizontal surface | **Plain Milling** | High MRR, rigid setup, helical teeth ensure smooth cutting |
| Producing a flat surface with high finish | **Face Milling** | Inserted teeth, fine surface finish (0.8–1.6 μm Ra) |
| Machining a slot, pocket, or 3D contour | **End Milling** | Multi-functional, can plunge, profile, and slot |
| Two parallel surfaces on opposite sides | **Straddle Milling** | One pass produces both surfaces → high productivity |
| Multiple surfaces in one pass | **Gang Milling** | Combines plain + side + form cutters on a common arbor |
| Complex 2D/3D outline | **CNC End Milling / Profile Milling** | Cutter follows a programmed path |
| Gear teeth | **Gear Hobbing (hob)** or form milling | Continuous generating process |
| Angled surface (dovetail, V-groove) | **Angular Milling** | Single or double-angle cutter |
| Long workpieces in a single pass | **Drum Milling** | Wide-diameter cutter with multiple inserts |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Decision Rule for KTU Viva:** Ask three questions in order — (1) *What surface is to be produced?* → identifies the operation. (2) *What is the production volume?* → identifies the cutter style. (3) *What is the workpiece rigidity?* → identifies up vs down milling.

```mermaid
    classDef root fill:#4a148c,stroke:#4a148c,color:#ffffff
    classDef up fill:#d84315,stroke:#d84315,color:#ffffff
    classDef down fill:#1b5e20,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff
    classDef leaf fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#666666,color:#000000
```

---

## 4.4 Visual Reference — Cutter Orientation Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes the **geometric orientation of cutter vs. surface produced** for the major milling operations. This is a frequently tested topic in KTU 2024 ESE.

| Operation | Cutter Axis | Surface Produced | Cutter Has Teeth On |
|---|---|---|---|
| Plain Milling | Parallel to surface | Horizontal flat | Periphery only |
| Face Milling | Perpendicular to surface | Flat (high finish) | Periphery + Face |
| End Milling | Perpendicular to surface | Slot / Pocket / Profile | Periphery + End |
| Side Milling | Parallel to surface | Vertical flat | Periphery + One side |
| Straddle Milling | Parallel to surface | Two parallel verticals | Periphery + Both sides |
| Angular Milling | At an angle to surface | Angled surface | Conical / Tapered |
| Form Milling | Depends on profile | Irregular profile | Profile-shaped |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Viva:**
> - **Plain** = "Plane" = flat surface, parallel cutter.
> - **Face** = "Facing" = surface facing the cutter face, perpendicular cutter.
> - **End** = "End" of cutter does the work, perpendicular cutter, versatile.
> - **Straddle** = "Straddles" the workpiece — two sides at once.
> - **Gang** = "Gang" of cutters on one arbor.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q: Differentiate between up milling and down milling. Mention any three points of distinction.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Awarded for Clarity, Tabulation, and Technical Accuracy):**

| S.No. | Up Milling (Conventional) | Down Milling (Climb) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cutter rotation is **opposite** to the direction of table feed. | Cutter rotation is **in the same direction** as table feed. |
| 2 | Chip thickness varies from **zero to maximum** during tooth engagement. | Chip thickness varies from **maximum to zero** during tooth engagement. |
| 3 | Cutting force tends to **lift the workpiece** off the table. | Cutting force tends to **pull the workpiece** into the cutter. |
| 4 | Requires **less rigid clamping** of workpiece. | Requires **very rigid clamping** of workpiece. |
| 5 | Suitable for **conventional machines** with backlash. | Suitable for **CNC machines** without backlash. |

> **Marking Scheme (Typical KTU 2024):**
> - 1 mark for stating the difference in rotation vs. feed direction.
> - 1 mark for chip thickness variation.
> - 1 mark for force direction / application.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q: What is gang milling? List its advantages.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Gang milling is a milling operation in which **two or more cutters of different shapes are mounted on a common arbor (ganged together)** to produce **multiple surfaces of the workpiece simultaneously in a single pass**.

**Advantages (1.5 Marks):**
1. **High productivity** — multiple surfaces are machined at once, reducing cycle time.
2. **Better dimensional accuracy** — surfaces are produced in one setup, so relative positions are accurate.
3. **Lower machining cost per piece** — due to reduced setup time and labor.
4. **Suitable for mass production** of prismatic components like brackets, levers, and spacers.

> **Marking Scheme:** 1.5 marks for the definition with a brief explanation; 0.5 mark for each advantage (any three).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice) (1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> Following the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, each Part B question carries 14 marks, with internal choice between Question A and Question B. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) — Plain Milling, Straddle Milling, and Straddle Gang Milling

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q: (a)** Explain with neat sketches the **plain milling operation**. List the **types of plain milling cutters** and state the advantages of plain milling. (7 Marks)

**Q: (b)** Explain **straddle milling** and **gang milling** operations with sketches. Highlight their **industrial applications** and relative **advantages over plain milling**. (7 Marks)

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Plain Milling (7 Marks)

**Plain Milling (Slab Milling):**

> Plain milling is a milling operation in which a **cylindrical milling cutter with teeth on its periphery only** rotates about an axis **parallel to the surface being produced**, while the workpiece is fed perpendicular to the cutter axis. The cutter is mounted on a horizontal arbor supported between the machine spindle and an outboard bearing.

**Sketch Description (Verbal — 2 Marks):**
- Horizontal arbor with cutter at the middle.
- Workpiece on the table below, fed from one side.
- Cutting direction indicated by an arrow on the cutter.
- Feed direction indicated by an arrow on the table.

**Types of Plain Milling Cutters (2 Marks):**

1. **Light-Duty Plain Milling Cutter** — Used for shallow cuts and finishing; cutter diameter up to ~75 mm.
2. **Heavy-Duty Plain Milling Cutter** — Used for deep roughing cuts; cutter diameter > 75 mm; often with helical teeth.
3. **Helical-Tooth Plain Milling Cutter** — Teeth are arranged in a helix around the periphery; reduces impact loading and chatter.
4. **Staggered-Tooth Plain Milling Cutter** — Alternating right- and left-hand helical teeth; suitable for cutting deep slots and roughing.

**Advantages of Plain Milling (3 Marks):**

1. **High material removal rate** due to multi-tooth engagement.
2. **Good surface finish** (helical teeth give a smooth cutting action).
3. **Long cutter life** — chips are formed gradually by helical teeth.
4. **Versatile** — can be used on horizontal, vertical, and universal milling machines.
5. **Easily resharpened** — only peripheral teeth need grinding.

---

#### Part (b) — Straddle Milling and Gang Milling (7 Marks)

**Straddle Milling (3.5 Marks):**

> Straddle milling is a milling operation in which **two side-milling cutters are mounted on the same arbor at a fixed distance apart** so that the workpiece is **straddled** between them. Both sides of the workpiece are machined simultaneously in a single pass, producing **two parallel vertical surfaces**.

**Sketch Description:** Two side cutters on a common arbor; workpiece in between, fed longitudinally.

**Industrial Applications:**
- Production of **square or hexagonal blocks** (e.g., nuts, wrench flats).
- Machining **parallel flanges** of brackets and levers.
- Production of **slide rails and guide ways**.

**Advantages over Plain Milling:**
- Two surfaces produced in one pass → **higher productivity**.
- **Dimensional accuracy** between the two parallel surfaces is excellent because both are machined simultaneously against the same arbor setup.

---

**Gang Milling (3.5 Marks):**

> Gang milling is a milling operation in which **multiple cutters of different shapes are mounted (ganged) on a single arbor** to produce **multiple surfaces of the workpiece in a single pass**.

**Sketch Description:** A combination of cutters (plain, side, profile, form) on the same arbor; workpiece below, fed once.

**Industrial Applications:**
- Mass production of **prismatic parts** like brackets, levers, and hinges.
- Production of **complex profiles** in one setup.

**Advantages over Plain Milling:**
- **Multiple surfaces** produced simultaneously → drastically reduced cycle time.
- **Lower per-piece cost** in high-volume production.
- **Better interchangeability** of parts due to consistent setup.

> **Marking Scheme Summary for Question A:**
> - Part (a): 2 marks for plain milling definition + sketch, 2 marks for cutter types, 3 marks for advantages.
> - Part (b): 3.5 marks for straddle milling (with industrial application), 3.5 marks for gang milling (with industrial application).

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Face Milling, End Milling, and Form Milling

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q: (a)** Describe with a sketch the **face milling operation**. Compare **face milling with plain milling**. (7 Marks)

**Q: (b)** Explain **end milling** and **form milling** operations with neat sketches. State **at least two industrial applications** of each. (7 Marks)

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Face Milling (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 Marks):**

> Face milling is a milling operation in which the **cutting tool (face milling cutter) rotates about an axis perpendicular to the surface being produced**. The cutting action is performed primarily by the **teeth on the face (end) of the cutter**, with the peripheral teeth providing a finishing pass at the edge of the machined surface.

**Sketch Description:** Vertical cutter axis; face teeth in contact with the workpiece surface; insert-type teeth.

**Types of Face Milling Cutters (1.5 Marks):**
1. **Plain Face Milling Cutter** — Teeth on the face only.
2. **Shell Face Milling Cutter** — Has a separate body and insert holder; common in modern CNC.
3. **Inserted-Tooth Face Milling Cutter** — Uses throwaway carbide inserts.

**Comparison: Face Milling vs. Plain Milling (3.5 Marks):**

| S.No. | Face Milling | Plain Milling |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cutter axis is **perpendicular** to the surface. | Cutter axis is **parallel** to the surface. |
| 2 | Cutting is done primarily by **face teeth**. | Cutting is done by **peripheral teeth** only. |
| 3 | **Higher MRR** possible due to multiple inserts. | Lower MRR compared to face milling. |
| 4 | **Better surface finish** (inserts are precisely ground). | Surface finish depends on cutter geometry. |
| 5 | **Shorter setup time** — cutter directly mounted on spindle. | Requires horizontal arbor + outboard bearing. |
| 6 | Suitable for **large flat surfaces** in finishing. | Suitable for both roughing and finishing. |
| 7 | **Vibration can be an issue** for very wide cutters. | More stable due to horizontal arbor setup. |

---

#### Part (b) — End Milling and Form Milling (7 Marks)

**End Milling (3.5 Marks):**

> End milling is a milling operation performed with an **end mill**, which is a multi-tooth cutter with **cutting edges on both the periphery and the end (bottom)**. End milling is used to produce **slots, pockets, contours, and flat surfaces**.

**Sketch Description:** End mill with helical teeth on periphery and end cutting edges; workpiece with slot/pocket.

**Types of End Mills:**
- **Square End Mill** — Produces flat-bottomed slots and pockets.
- **Ball End Mill** — Used for 3D contours and sculptured surfaces.
- **Bull-Nose End Mill** — Has a small flat at the tip.
- **Roughing End Mill** — Has serrated teeth for high MRR.

**Industrial Applications (any two):**
1. Machining **pockets and slots** in dies and molds.
2. Producing **2D/3D contours** in CNC milling.
3. **Side and face milling** in a single operation.

---

**Form Milling (3.5 Marks):**

> Form milling is a milling operation performed with a **form cutter** whose **cutting edge profile is the inverse of the desired workpiece profile**. As the cutter rotates, it reproduces its own shape on the workpiece.

**Sketch Description:** Form cutter with shaped teeth; workpiece below with corresponding contour.

**Types of Form Cutters:**
- **Circular Form Cutter** — Rotates about an axis parallel to the workpiece surface.
- **Convex/Concave Form Cutter** — Used for curved profiles.
- **Corner-Rounding Cutter** — Produces rounded edges.
- **Gear-Tooth Form Cutter** — Used for non-precision gear teeth.

**Industrial Applications (any two):**
1. Production of **contoured surfaces** on machine tool components.
2. Cutting **gear teeth, splines, and serrations** (for low-precision gears).
3. Machining **decorative profiles** on wood, plastic, and metal.

> **Marking Scheme Summary for Question B:**
> - Part (a): 2 marks for face milling definition + sketch, 1.5 marks for cutter types, 3.5 marks for comparison.
> - Part (b): 3.5 marks for end milling (with applications), 3.5 marks for form milling (with applications).

---

## 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Milling Questions — KTU 2024 ESE:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (3-mark penalty possible):** Students often forget to convert mm to m in the cutting speed formula. Always write: $V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ when D is in mm and V is in m/min.
> 2. **Confusing Feed per Tooth with Feed per Revolution:** $f_z \times z = f$. Many students multiply or divide by z incorrectly.
> 3. **Forgetting to Convert Power Units:** $1 \text{ HP} = 0.7457 \text{ kW}$. A common error is to forget this conversion.
> 4. **MRR Formula Sign Error:** MRR is always **positive**. Some students compute negative MRR due to vector confusion. MRR is a **scalar** quantity.
> 5. **Sketch Missing:** In long-answer questions (7 or 14 marks), a sketch is **mandatory**. Not drawing a sketch can cost **2–3 marks** even if the answer is textually correct.
> 6. **Up vs. Down Milling Confusion:** The definitions are frequently interchanged. **Remember:** Up milling = cutter rotates against feed = chip starts at zero thickness.
> 7. **Missing Approach Length:** In machining time calculations, ignoring $L_a$ and $L_o$ is a common 1-mark deduction in KTU valuation.
> 8. **Gang vs. Straddle Milling:** Students often describe them as the same. **Remember:** Straddle = two side cutters on a single arbor for two parallel surfaces. Gang = multiple different cutters for multiple different surfaces.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Milling: Types of Operations**

- ✅ Milling is a **multi-tooth, interrupted cutting** process where the **cutter rotates** and the **workpiece feeds** linearly.
- ✅ The **two basic relative motions** are: cutter rotation (primary) and table feed (secondary).
- ✅ **Up Milling (Conventional):** Cutter rotates **against** feed; chip thickness varies from **0 → max**; force lifts workpiece; preferred for roughing and old machines.
- ✅ **Down Milling (Climb):** Cutter rotates **with** feed; chip thickness varies from **max → 0**; force pulls workpiece; requires rigid clamping; preferred for CNC and finishing.
- ✅ **Plain Milling:** Cutter axis **parallel** to surface; cutter has teeth on **periphery only**; produces flat horizontal surface.
- ✅ **Face Milling:** Cutter axis **perpendicular** to surface; cutting by **face teeth + peripheral teeth**; produces flat surface with high finish.
- ✅ **End Milling:** Cutter axis perpendicular; teeth on **periphery + end**; used for **slots, pockets, profiles**.
- ✅ **Side Milling:** Cutter has teeth on **periphery + one or both sides**; produces vertical surfaces.
- ✅ **Straddle Milling:** **Two side cutters on same arbor**; produces **two parallel vertical surfaces** in one pass.
- ✅ **Gang Milling:** **Multiple cutters on common arbor**; produces **multiple surfaces simultaneously**; best for mass production.
- ✅ **Form Milling:** Cutter **profile = inverse of workpiece profile**; used for contoured surfaces and gear teeth.
- ✅ **Angular Milling:** Uses **single or double-angle cutters**; produces angled surfaces (dovetails, V-grooves).
- ✅ **Gear Hobbing:** Uses **hob** (worm-like cutter); most efficient gear-production process.
- ✅ **Cutting Speed:** $V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ m/min.
- ✅ **Spindle Speed:** $N = \dfrac{1000 V}{\pi D}$ rpm.
- ✅ **Feed Rate:** $V_f = f_z \times z \times N$ mm/min.
- ✅ **MRR:** $Q = w \times d \times V_f$ mm³/min.
- ✅ **Power (kW):** $P = \dfrac{K_c \times Q}{60 \times 10^6}$.
- ✅ **Power Conversion:** $1 \text{ HP} = 0.7457 \text{ kW}$.
- ✅ **Machining Time:** $t_m = \dfrac{L + L_a + L_o}{V_f}$, with $L_a = \sqrt{d(D - d)}$.
- ✅ **MRR with Backlash:** Use **average** feed rate: $V_{f,av} = \dfrac{(N_f + N_f^\prime) f}{2}$.
- ✅ **Constant to remember:** $1 \text{ HP} = 0.7457 \text{ kW}$; $\pi \approx 3.1416$.
- ✅ **Sketches are compulsory** in long-answer questions — drawing a labeled sketch is worth **2–3 marks** independently of textual explanation.

---

> **END OF NOTES — PBMRT404, Module 3 — Milling: Types of Operations**

topics.title: Types of Milling Machines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT404 - MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Milling Machines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Milling Machine (KTU 2024 Definition):** A milling machine is a **multipoint cutting machine** used primarily for shaping and machining flat, curved, or irregular surfaces by feeding a workpiece against a **rotating multipoint cutter**. The classification of milling machines is fundamentally based on the **orientation of the spindle axis**, the **design of the machine bed/column**, the **type of knee and table mechanism**, and the **degree of automation/specialization** for the intended production task.

The taxonomy of milling machines is divided by KTU into two primary super-categories:

1. **General-Purpose (Conventional) Machines** — Manually operated, versatile, used in job shops and tool rooms.
2. **Special-Purpose Machines** — Designed for high-volume, repetitive production of a specific geometry.

Under General-Purpose, the KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly recognizes the **Column-and-Knee Type** and **Bed-Type** machines. Under Special-Purpose, the syllabus highlights **Planer, Rotary Table, Duplicating, and Profile Milling Machines**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Carpenter's Workshop:**
> Imagine a master carpenter who has multiple specialized tools. A simple **hand-held chisel** represents the most basic cutting action (one point, manual). A **rotating circular saw blade** is like a horizontal milling machine — the blade spins at high speed and the wood is pushed against it. Now imagine mounting that circular saw onto a **heavy cast-iron table with a sliding trolley** — this is a knee-type milling machine. If you build a machine that mounts the spinning saw vertically downward (like a drill press with a wide blade), you have a **vertical milling machine**. If you design a machine where the wood is mounted on a giant rotating turntable (like a pottery wheel) and a stationary cutter shapes it, you have a **rotary table milling machine** — perfect for making identical circular slots. Each variant is optimized for a specific *family* of shapes, just as each carpentry tool is optimized for a specific *family* of cuts.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!TIP]
> **Key Standard Parameters Used to Classify Milling Machines:**
> - **Spindle Speed Range (N):** Typically **30 RPM to 3000 RPM** (variable via stepped pulleys or VFD drives).
> - **Table Travel (X, Y, Z axes):** Standard knee-type machines provide travels of roughly **300–900 mm** in each direction.
> - **Power Rating:** General-purpose machines range from **0.5 kW to 15 kW**; planer-type mills can exceed **75 kW**.
> - **Arbor Diameter:** Standard horizontal arbors are **27 mm, 32 mm, or 40 mm** in diameter.
> - **Number of Teeth on Cutter (z):** Standard milling cutters carry **2 to 24 teeth** depending on geometry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 3):** The classification hinges on the **spindle orientation (horizontal vs. vertical)**, the **structural support of the table (knee vs. bed vs. planer)**, and the **production volume (job shop vs. mass production)**. Always remember the mnemonic **"HVR-KBPS"** — **H**orizontal, **V**ertical, **R**otary, **K**nee, **B**ed, **P**laner, **S**pecial.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Broken Down

The classification of milling machines is governed by **three orthogonal design parameters** that the KTU examiner will expect a student to map onto any given machine diagram.

### A. Based on Spindle Orientation

- **Horizontal Milling Machine (HMM):** The cutter spindle is oriented **horizontally** (parallel to the worktable). The cutter is mounted on an **arbor** supported by the spindle and an outer arbor support. It excels at **slab milling**, **gang milling**, and **straddle milling** operations.
  - *Why:* The horizontal layout provides superior chip evacuation and allows long arbor-mounted cutters (like side-and-face mills) to engage the workpiece across a wide contact area.

- **Vertical Milling Machine (VMM):** The cutter spindle is oriented **vertically** (perpendicular to the worktable). The cutter is mounted directly into the spindle nose, eliminating the need for an arbor in most cases.
  - *Why:* The vertical layout naturally provides a **down-feed** force, eliminating the need for an arbor support and making the machine ideal for **end-milling**, **face milling**, **drilling**, and **boring** on small to medium workpieces.

### B. Based on Table/Column Construction

- **Column-and-Knee Type:** The machine consists of a **column**, a **knee** that slides vertically on the column, and a **saddle** that slides horizontally on the knee. The worktable rides on the saddle.
  - *Why:* This triple-axis (X, Y, Z) movement of the table makes the machine extremely **versatile** for tool-room and job-shop work where the workpiece is small and the cuts are varied.

- **Bed-Type Milling Machine:** The table moves **only longitudinally** (X-axis) on a fixed, heavy bed. Vertical and cross-feeds are achieved by moving the **spindle head** instead of the table.
  - *Why:* Eliminating the moving knee drastically **increases rigidity**, allowing heavier cuts on **larger and heavier workpieces**.

- **Planer-Type Milling Machine:** A very large machine where multiple cutters are mounted on a **cross-rail** and a **vertical ram**, simultaneously machining large surfaces.
  - *Why:* Designed for **ultra-heavy, large workpieces** (e.g., lathe beds, engine frames) that cannot be moved practically.

### C. Based on Specialization

- **Rotary Table Milling Machine:** The table is a large **rotary disc**. Ideal for machining **circular slots, arcs, and contours** on multiple identical parts.
- **Duplicating / Tracer Milling Machine:** A **stylus (tracer)** follows a 2D template while a **spindle-mounted cutter** reproduces the profile on the workpiece.
- **Profile Milling Machine:** Uses a **die-sink or profile cutter** to cut complex 3D contours (e.g., turbine blades).
- **CNC Milling Machine:** Computer-controlled, used in modern flexible manufacturing.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are not unique to *types* of machines, but are routinely used in numerical problems that accompany this topic. Memorize them in KTU mark-table form.**

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cutting Speed (V) | $V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | m/min | $D$ = cutter diameter (mm), $N$ = spindle RPM |
| Feed Rate (V_f) | $V_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$ | mm/min | $f_z$ = feed/tooth (mm), $z$ = number of teeth |
| Material Removal Rate (MRR) | $MRR = V_f \cdot a_p \cdot w$ | mm³/min | $a_p$ = depth of cut (mm), $w$ = width of cut (mm) |
| Spindle Power (P) | $P = \dfrac{MRR \cdot K_c}{60 \cdot 10^6 \cdot \eta}$ | kW | $K_c$ = specific cutting energy (N/mm²), $\eta$ = efficiency (typically 0.7–0.85) |
| Machining Time (T_m) | $T_m = \dfrac{L + L_a}{V_f}$ | min | $L$ = workpiece length (mm), $L_a$ = cutter approach allowance |
| Average Pitch (P) | $P = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D}{z}$ | mm | Valid for plain milling cutters only |
| Surface Roughness (R_a) | $R_a = \dfrac{f^2}{8 \cdot r}$ | µm | $f$ = feed/tooth, $r$ = nose radius of cutter |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Unit Convention:** $D$ in the cutting speed formula **MUST be in mm**, not metres, because of the division by 1000. A common KTU error is plugging in $D$ in metres, which produces a final answer 1000× too small.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The classification system is not academic — it directly drives **machine selection** in any production environment:

- A **tool-room** stocks a **knee-type vertical mill** because one operator must switch between 50 different jobs per day.
- An **automotive mass-production line** uses a **dedicated rotary-table mill** because 10,000 identical engine blocks must be machined per week.
- An **aerospace turbine-blade shop** relies on a **5-axis CNC profile mill** because the geometry is mathematically complex and the tolerance is ±0.01 mm.
- A **heavy-engineering foundry** invests in a **planer-type mill** because the workpiece (a 20-tonne lathe bed) cannot be moved easily.

The economic principle is: **match machine rigidity and specialization to production volume and part size.**

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this section exists:** KTU 2024 scheme examiners frequently attach a *numerical problem* to a descriptive question on machine types. For example: "A horizontal slab mill is used on a bed-type machine..." — the student must then calculate spindle power or MRR. Below is the exhaustive derivation of every formula in the cheat sheet, so a student can solve any variant problem.

## 3.1 Derivation — Cutting Speed (V)

### Starting Premise

Cutting speed is defined as the **linear speed at which the cutter periphery sweeps across the workpiece surface**. For a rotating body, the linear speed at the rim is given by the classical circular-motion relation.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let the cutter rotate at $N$ revolutions per minute. Then in one minute, every point on the rim travels a circumferential distance equal to:

$$C = \pi \cdot D$$

where $D$ is the cutter diameter. Since there are $N$ revolutions per minute, the total linear distance swept in one minute is:

$$L_{min} = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$$

This length, expressed in mm/min when $D$ is in mm, is the *raw* cutting speed. To convert to **m/min** (the KTU standard unit), we divide by 1000:

$$V = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$$

**Units check:**
- $\pi$ : dimensionless
- $D$ : mm
- $N$ : rev/min
- Product : mm·rev/min → rev cancels → mm/min
- After dividing by 1000 : m/min ✓

## 3.2 Derivation — Feed Rate (V_f)

### Starting Premise

Feed rate is the **distance the table advances per minute relative to the cutter**. It is the product of three independent parameters: feed per tooth, number of teeth, and spindle speed.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

In one revolution, every tooth in the cutter engages the workpiece once. If the cutter has $z$ teeth, then in one revolution, the table advances a distance equal to:

$$f_{rev} = f_z \cdot z$$

where $f_z$ is the axial advance per tooth engagement (the **feed per tooth**). Since the spindle turns $N$ revolutions per minute, the table advances:

$$V_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$$

**Units check:**
- $f_z$ : mm/tooth
- $z$ : teeth (dimensionless)
- $N$ : rev/min
- rev cancels → mm/min ✓

## 3.3 Derivation — Material Removal Rate (MRR)

### Starting Premise

MRR is the **volume of material removed per unit time**. In a peripheral (slab) milling operation, the cutter sweeps a rectangular cross-section through the workpiece.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

The cross-sectional area of the cut in a slab-milling operation equals the product of the **axial depth of cut** ($a_p$) and the **radial width of cut** ($w$):

$$A = a_p \cdot w$$

The volume removed in one minute equals this area swept along the feed distance per minute:

$$MRR = A \cdot V_f = a_p \cdot w \cdot V_f$$

Substituting the feed-rate expression:

$$MRR = a_p \cdot w \cdot f_z \cdot z \cdot N$$

**Units check:**
- $a_p \cdot w$ : mm²
- $V_f$ : mm/min
- Product : mm³/min ✓

## 3.4 Derivation — Spindle Power (P)

### Starting Premise

The power required at the spindle equals the cutting force multiplied by the cutting velocity. The cutting force is approximated by the product of the specific cutting energy and the instantaneous cross-sectional area.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

The **cutting force** $F_c$ required to shear a unit area of material is given by the specific cutting energy $K_c$:

$$F_c = K_c \cdot \text{(instantaneous chip area)}$$

For milling, the *average* chip cross-section is $f_z \cdot a_p$ (feed per tooth times axial depth). Therefore:

$$F_c = K_c \cdot f_z \cdot a_p$$

The power at the cutting zone is force times velocity:

$$P_{cut} = F_c \cdot V = K_c \cdot f_z \cdot a_p \cdot V$$

Substituting $V$ and $f_z \cdot N = V_f / z$:

$$P_{cut} = K_c \cdot a_p \cdot V_f \cdot \frac{V}{V_f / z} = K_c \cdot a_p \cdot z \cdot V \cdot \frac{N}{V_f / f_z}$$

A cleaner final form uses **MRR directly**:

$$P_{cut} = \frac{MRR \cdot K_c}{60 \cdot 10^6}$$

The factor $60 \cdot 10^6$ converts mm³/min × N/mm² → N·mm/min → J/min → W, since 1 W = 1 N·m/s. Accounting for mechanical efficiency $\eta$, the **spindle motor power** is:

$$P = \frac{MRR \cdot K_c}{60 \cdot 10^6 \cdot \eta}$$

**Units check:**
- $MRR$ : mm³/min
- $K_c$ : N/mm²
- Product : N·mm/min = mJ/s
- Divide by $10^6$ : mN·m/min
- Divide by 60 : mN·m/s = mW → multiply by appropriate scaling → kW ✓

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 3-Mark Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A horizontal milling machine uses a 75 mm diameter slab mill with 8 teeth on mild steel. Spindle speed = 200 RPM; feed per tooth = 0.15 mm; depth of cut = 4 mm; width of cut = 50 mm. Specific cutting energy $K_c$ = 2000 N/mm²; efficiency = 0.8. Calculate (a) cutting speed, (b) feed rate, (c) MRR, (d) spindle power.

### Solution

**(a) Cutting Speed:**

$$V = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} = \frac{\pi \cdot 75 \cdot 200}{1000}$$

$$\boxed{V = 47.12 \text{ m/min}}$$

**(b) Feed Rate:**

$$V_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N = 0.15 \cdot 8 \cdot 200$$

$$\boxed{V_f = 240 \text{ mm/min}}$$

**(c) Material Removal Rate:**

$$MRR = a_p \cdot w \cdot V_f = 4 \cdot 50 \cdot 240$$

$$\boxed{MRR = 48{,}000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}}$$

**(d) Spindle Power:**

$$P = \frac{MRR \cdot K_c}{60 \cdot 10^6 \cdot \eta} = \frac{48{,}000 \cdot 2000}{60 \cdot 10^6 \cdot 0.8}$$

$$\boxed{P = 2.0 \text{ kW}}$$

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Machine Types

> [!TIP]
> **How to use this matrix in the exam:** A KTU question may say *"Compare the knee-type and bed-type milling machine on five points."* This matrix is your direct template — simply convert each row into a one-sentence comparative statement.

| Feature | Column-and-Knee | Bed-Type | Planer-Type | Rotary-Table | Duplicating |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Spindle Orientation** | Horizontal or Vertical | Mainly Horizontal | Horizontal (multi-spindle) | Horizontal or Vertical | Vertical (tracer-guided) |
| **Table Axes** | 3 (X, Y, Z) | 1 (X only) | Fixed; cutter moves | 1 linear + 1 rotary | 2 (X, Y) tracer-controlled |
| **Typical Workpiece Size** | Small to Medium | Medium to Large | Very Large | Small to Medium (in batches) | Medium (templated) |
| **Rigidity** | Moderate | High | Very High | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Production Volume** | Job Shop / Low Batch | Medium Batch | Low Batch (heavy) | High Batch (rotary index) | Medium to High (templated) |
| **Key Application** | Tool-room versatility | Heavy slab milling | Machine-tool beds | Circular slots, keyways | Die cavities, contours |
| **Power Range (kW)** | 1.5 – 7.5 | 5 – 25 | 20 – 75 | 3 – 15 | 2 – 10 |
| **Operator Skill Required** | High (manual handling) | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Cost Index (relative)** | 1× | 2× | 5× | 2.5× | 3× |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Knee-Type Milling Machine

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional relationships** between the main sub-assemblies of a column-and-knee type milling machine. This is the KTU-recommended way to represent a milling machine when a true engineering drawing is not feasible in text.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Base and Column Assembly] --> B[Vertical Slide Knee]
    B --> C[Horizontal Slide Saddle]
    C --> D[Worktable with T Slots]
    A --> E[Overarm with Arbor Support]
    A --> F[Spindle Headstock]
    F --> G[Horizontal Spindle with Arbor]
    G --> H[Multipoint Milling Cutter]
    E --> I[Arbor Bearing Support]
    G --> I
    D --> J[Workpiece Clamping Vice]
    H --> K[Chip Formation Zone]
    J --> K
    F --> L[Feed Gearbox and Drive Motor]
    L --> B
    L --> C
    L --> D
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **Base and Column** (A) is the rigid foundation, fixed to the shop floor.
- The **Knee (B)** moves vertically (Z-axis) along the column face, driven by the **Feed Gearbox (L)** via a vertical leadscrew.
- The **Saddle (C)** moves horizontally (Y-axis) across the knee, driven by L via a cross-leadscrew.
- The **Worktable (D)** moves longitudinally (X-axis) along the saddle.
- The **Spindle (G)** rotates the cutter (H) at the prescribed RPM.
- The **Arbor Support (I)** braces the long horizontal arbor to prevent deflection.
- The **Chip Formation Zone (K)** is where the cutter engages the clamped workpiece (J).

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Milling Machine]
    M --> G[General Purpose]
    M --> S[Special Purpose]
    G --> CK[Column and Knee Type]
    G --> BT[Bed Type]
    G --> PT[Planer Type]
    CK --> HMM[Horizontal Milling]
    CK --> VMM[Vertical Milling]
    CK --> UM[Universal Milling]
    BT --> PHM[Plain Horizontal Bed]
    BT --> PVM[Plain Vertical Bed]
    S --> RT[Rotary Table]
    S --> DM[Duplicating Tracer]
    S --> PM[Profile Milling]
    S --> CNC[CNC Machining Centre]
```

## 4.3 Functional Signal Flow — Spindle and Feed Drive

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AC Induction Motor 5 to 15 kW] --> B[Stepped Pulley Head]
    B --> C[Spindle Gearbox]
    C --> D[Spindle and Arbor]
    D --> E[Milling Cutter]
    F[Separate Feed Motor] --> G[Feed Gearbox]
    G --> H[Table Leadscrew X axis]
    G --> I[Cross Leadscrew Y axis]
    G --> J[Vertical Leadscrew Z axis]
    H --> K[Worktable]
    I --> L[Saddle]
    J --> M[Knee]
```

**Interpretation for KTU viva:**

- A **single main motor (A)** powers the spindle through a **two-stage speed-change** (B and C). This is why older conventional mills have a *stepped* RPM range (e.g., 30, 45, 65, 95, 135, 200, 280, 400 RPM).
- A **separate feed motor (F)** is used in industrial-grade machines so that the feed rate can be varied *independently* of the spindle speed — essential for optimizing surface finish and chip load.

## 4.4 Cutter Engagement Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rotating Cutter at N RPM] --> B[Tooth 1 enters cut]
    B --> C[Tooth 1 reaches max chip thickness]
    C --> D[Tooth 1 exits cut]
    D --> E[Tooth 2 enters cut]
    E --> F[Tooth 2 reaches max chip thickness]
    F --> G[Tooth 2 exits cut]
    G --> H[Cyclical engagement at frequency zN]
```

**Why this matters:** Milling is an **interrupted cutting process** — unlike turning, each tooth alternately engages and disengages. This cyclic load is what causes **chatter** and limits the depth of cut, and it is the fundamental reason why a *rigid* machine (bed-type or planer) can take heavier cuts than a flexible knee-type machine.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Differentiate between a **column-and-knee type** and a **bed-type** milling machine in terms of table movement, rigidity, and typical application.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Column-and-Knee Type | Bed-Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Table Movement** | Moves in all 3 axes (X, Y, Z) via knee, saddle, and table | Moves only longitudinally (X-axis); spindle head moves in Y and Z |
| **Rigidity** | Moderate (moving knee reduces stiffness) | High (fixed bed, no moving knee) |
| **Typical Application** | Job-shop and tool-room work on small-to-medium parts | Medium-to-large parts requiring heavy cuts |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark allocation:** 1 mark per correct row × 3 rows = 3 marks.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** State **any three** specific applications where a **vertical milling machine** is preferred over a horizontal milling machine.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **End-milling of pockets and slots:** The vertical spindle allows the cutter to plunge directly into the workpiece — a horizontal machine cannot do this.
2. **Face milling with a large diameter cutter:** Gravity assists chip fall, and the downward cutting force keeps the workpiece firmly clamped.
3. **Drilling and boring:** The vertical spindle can accommodate twist drills and boring bars directly, eliminating the need to change machines.

> [!NOTE]
> Each valid application = 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, split as **Part (a) = 7 marks** and **Part (b) = 7 marks**. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 3 Internal Choice Q1]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **constructional features and working principle** of a **column-and-knee type horizontal milling machine**. List the various **milling operations** that can be performed on it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A horizontal milling machine is used to perform **slab milling** on a mild-steel workpiece of length 400 mm using a 100 mm diameter, 10-tooth helical milling cutter. The following data is provided:

- Spindle speed, $N$ = 120 RPM
- Feed per tooth, $f_z$ = 0.20 mm
- Axial depth of cut, $a_p$ = 5 mm
- Width of cut, $w$ = 60 mm
- Specific cutting energy, $K_c$ = 1800 N/mm²
- Cutter approach allowance, $L_a$ = 10 mm
- Mechanical efficiency, $\eta$ = 0.80

Calculate: **(i)** Cutting speed, **(ii)** Feed rate, **(iii)** Material removal rate, **(iv)** Spindle power, and **(v)** Machining time. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Constructional Features (4 marks):**

1. **Base and Column:** Heavy cast-iron foundation supporting the entire structure. (0.5 mark)
2. **Knee:** Slides vertically on the column face via a vertical leadscrew; supports saddle and table. (0.5 mark)
3. **Saddle:** Mounted on the knee; provides cross (Y-axis) movement. (0.5 mark)
4. **Worktable:** Mounted on the saddle; provides longitudinal (X-axis) movement; has T-slots for workpiece clamping. (0.5 mark)
5. **Spindle and Arbor:** The horizontal spindle drives a cylindrical **arbor** on which milling cutters are mounted. (0.5 mark)
6. **Overarm and Arbor Support:** An arm extending from the column top supports the *outer end* of the arbor via a bearing, preventing arbor deflection. (0.5 mark)
7. **Feed and Speed Gearboxes:** Provide stepped ranges of spindle speeds and table feed rates. (0.5 mark)
8. **Coolant and Lubrication System:** Flood coolant supply and centralized lubrication. (0.5 mark)

**Working Principle (1.5 marks):**

The milling cutter rotates at high speed on the horizontal arbor. The workpiece, clamped to the worktable, is fed slowly *into* or *under* the rotating cutter. The multipoint cutter removes material in the form of discrete chips, one tooth at a time, generating a machined surface whose geometry is determined by the cutter profile and the relative feed path.

**Milling Operations (1.5 marks):**
- **Plain / Slab milling** — flat surface produced by a peripheral cutter.
- **Face milling** — flat surface produced by the face of a cutter.
- **Angular milling** — producing an angular surface.
- **Form milling** — producing a contour using a profile cutter.
- **Straddle milling** — two cutters simultaneously producing two parallel surfaces.
- **Gang milling** — multiple cutters on one arbor producing several surfaces in one pass.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $D = 100$ mm, $z = 10$, $N = 120$ RPM
- $f_z = 0.20$ mm/tooth, $a_p = 5$ mm, $w = 60$ mm
- $K_c = 1800$ N/mm², $L_a = 10$ mm, $\eta = 0.80$, $L = 400$ mm

**(i) Cutting Speed:**

$$V = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} = \frac{\pi \cdot 100 \cdot 120}{1000}$$

$$V = \frac{37{,}699.1}{1000}$$

$$\boxed{V \approx 37.70 \text{ m/min}}$$ (1 mark)

**(ii) Feed Rate:**

$$V_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N = 0.20 \cdot 10 \cdot 120$$

$$\boxed{V_f = 240 \text{ mm/min}}$$ (1 mark)

**(iii) Material Removal Rate:**

$$MRR = a_p \cdot w \cdot V_f = 5 \cdot 60 \cdot 240$$

$$\boxed{MRR = 72{,}000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}}$$ (1 mark)

**(iv) Spindle Power:**

$$P = \frac{MRR \cdot K_c}{60 \cdot 10^6 \cdot \eta} = \frac{72{,}000 \cdot 1800}{60 \cdot 10^6 \cdot 0.80}$$

$$P = \frac{1.296 \times 10^8}{4.8 \times 10^7}$$

$$\boxed{P = 2.70 \text{ kW}}$$ (2 marks)

**(v) Machining Time:**

$$T_m = \frac{L + L_a}{V_f} = \frac{400 + 10}{240} = \frac{410}{240}$$

$$\boxed{T_m \approx 1.708 \text{ min} \approx 102.5 \text{ s}}$$ (2 marks)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 3 Internal Choice Q2]**

**(a)** Compare **Column-and-Knee**, **Bed-Type**, and **Planer-Type** milling machines on the basis of **(i)** spindle orientation, **(ii)** table movements, **(iii)** rigidity, **(iv)** workpiece size, **(v)** typical application, and **(vi)** production volume suitability. State **two specific operations** that uniquely require a **planer-type mill**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **rotary-table milling machine** is used to machine **eight identical keyway slots** arranged on a pitch circle of diameter 200 mm in a cast-iron indexing plate. The following data is given:

- Cutter diameter, $D$ = 60 mm
- Number of teeth, $z$ = 6
- Spindle speed, $N$ = 150 RPM
- Feed per tooth, $f_z$ = 0.10 mm
- Depth of cut, $a_p$ = 3 mm
- Width of cut, $w$ = 8 mm
- Specific cutting energy, $K_c$ = 2200 N/mm²
- Mechanical efficiency, $\eta$ = 0.75

Calculate: **(i)** Feed rate, **(ii)** MRR, **(iii)** Spindle power, and **(iv)** total indexing time if the table rotates at 2.0 RPM between cuts (overhead per index = 4 s). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Comparison Table (6 marks — 1 mark per correct row × 6 criteria):**

| Criterion | Column-and-Knee | Bed-Type | Planer-Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Spindle orientation** | Horizontal or Vertical | Mainly Horizontal | Horizontal (multi-spindle) |
| **(ii) Table movements** | 3 axes (X, Y, Z) | 1 axis (X) | Fixed table; cutter moves on cross-rail and ram |
| **(iii) Rigidity** | Moderate | High | Very High |
| **(iv) Workpiece size** | Small to Medium | Medium to Large | Very Large (e.g., lathe beds) |
| **(v) Typical application** | Tool-room, job shop | Heavy slab milling | Machining large castings |
| **(vi) Production volume** | Low (job shop) | Medium (batch) | Low (one-off heavy) |

**Two operations unique to planer-type mill (1 mark):**

1. Machining the **bed ways of a lathe** (length > 3 m) where the workpiece cannot be moved.
2. **Simultaneous multi-surface milling** on a large casting using several cutters mounted on the cross-rail, e.g., machining a large engine frame in a single setup.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $D = 60$ mm, $z = 6$, $N = 150$ RPM
- $f_z = 0.10$ mm, $a_p = 3$ mm, $w = 8$ mm
- $K_c = 2200$ N/mm², $\eta = 0.75$
- 8 slots, table index speed = 2.0 RPM, overhead = 4 s per index

**(i) Feed Rate:**

$$V_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N = 0.10 \cdot 6 \cdot 150$$

$$\boxed{V_f = 90 \text{ mm/min}}$$ (1 mark)

**(ii) Material Removal Rate:**

$$MRR = a_p \cdot w \cdot V_f = 3 \cdot 8 \cdot 90$$

$$\boxed{MRR = 2160 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}}$$ (1 mark)

**(iii) Spindle Power:**

$$P = \frac{MRR \cdot K_c}{60 \cdot 10^6 \cdot \eta} = \frac{2160 \cdot 2200}{60 \cdot 10^6 \cdot 0.75}$$

$$P = \frac{4.752 \times 10^6}{4.5 \times 10^7}$$

$$\boxed{P = 0.1056 \text{ kW} \approx 105.6 \text{ W}}$$ (2 marks)

**(iv) Total Indexing Time:**

The table must index between consecutive slots. The angular distance between two adjacent slots on an 8-slot pitch circle is:

$$\theta = \frac{360°}{8} = 45°$$

Time per index (rotation only):

$$t_{rot} = \frac{45°}{360° \cdot 2.0 \text{ RPM}} = \frac{1}{8 \cdot 2.0} = \frac{1}{16} \text{ min} = 3.75 \text{ s}$$

Total time per index including overhead:

$$t_{index} = 3.75 + 4.0 = 7.75 \text{ s}$$

For 8 slots, there are 7 inter-slot indexes (the 8th slot returns to the start):

$$T_{index,total} = 7 \cdot 7.75$$

$$\boxed{T_{index,total} = 54.25 \text{ s} \approx 0.904 \text{ min}}$$ (3 marks)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks on this topic:**
