> 1. **Confusing "knee-type" with "universal" milling:** A *universal* mill has a *swivelling* worktable that allows spiral milling; a *plain* knee mill does not. Many students write "knee-type mill can do spiral milling" — this is **wrong** unless specified as universal.
> 2. **Forgetting the arbor support on a horizontal mill:** A horizontal mill *requires* an arbor support for long cutters; a vertical mill *does not use* an arbor. Missing this distinction in a diagram loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong unit for $D$ in cutting-speed formula:** A significant fraction of students plug in $D$ in metres instead of mm, producing answers that are 1000× too small. Always state: *"D in mm, divide by 1000 to get m/min."*
> 4. **Forgetting the approach allowance $L_a$:** In machining-time problems, only the *table travel during cutting* is divided by $V_f$. Students often write $T_m = L / V_f$ and miss 1 mark.
> 5. **Saying "CNC is a type of milling machine":** The KTU 2024 syllabus treats CNC as a *control* technology that can be applied to *any* of the conventional types (CNC knee mill, CNC bed mill, CNC machining centre). It is not a separate *type* in the strict classification sense.
> 6. **Mixing up bed-type and planer-type:** A bed-type mill has a *moving table* and a *fixed head*; a planer-type has a *fixed table* and *moving heads*. This is the most heavily tested distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (KTU Module 3 — Types of Milling Machines):**

- ☐ **Two super-categories:** General-purpose vs. Special-purpose.
- ☐ **General-purpose sub-types:** Column-and-knee, Bed-type, Planer-type.
- ☐ **Spindle orientation rule:** Horizontal mill → long arbor → slab/gang milling; Vertical mill → direct-mount cutter → end-milling/face-milling.
- ☐ **Knee movement:** Knee moves *vertically* (Z); saddle moves *cross* (Y); table moves *longitudinal* (X).
- ☐ **Rigidity hierarchy (low → high):** Knee-type < Bed-type < Planer-type.
- ☐ **Workpiece size hierarchy (small → large):** Rotary-table < Knee-type < Bed-type < Planer-type.
- ☐ **Special-purpose machines:** Rotary-table, Duplicating (tracer), Profile, CNC machining centre.
- ☐ **Universal mill:** A knee-type mill with a *swivelling* table (≈ ±45°) for helical/spiral milling.
- ☐ **Ram-type mill:** A vertical mill whose head can be *swivelled* forward/backward on a ram — used for tilted face milling.
- ☐ **Formula:** $V = \pi D N / 1000$ (m/min), $V_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$ (mm/min), $MRR = a_p \cdot w \cdot V_f$ (mm³/min).
- ☐ **Power formula:** $P = (MRR \cdot K_c) / (60 \cdot 10^6 \cdot \eta)$ (kW).
- ☐ **Time formula:** $T_m = (L + L_a) / V_f$ (min).
- ☐ **Always specify $D$ in mm** when using the cutting-speed formula.
- ☐ **Cutter approach allowance $L_a$** is added to the workpiece length in time calculations.
- ☐ **Arbor support is mandatory** for horizontal milling of long workpieces.
- ☐ **CNC is a *control* upgrade**, not a *type* in the strict KTU classification.
- ☐ **Mnemonic for types:** **"HVR-KBPS"** = **H**orizontal, **V**ertical, **R**otary, **K**nee, **B**ed, **P**laner, **S**pecial.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Milling Cutters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT404 - MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Milling Cutters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Milling Cutter** is a multi-point rotary cutting tool used in a milling machine to remove material from a workpiece by means of its multiple cutting edges (called *teeth* or *flutes*) which engage the workpiece progressively as the cutter rotates. Unlike a single-point tool (e.g., a lathe tool) which has only one active cutting edge, a milling cutter has several teeth distributed around its circumference or on its face, allowing interrupted cutting action and high metal removal rates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standard):** "A milling cutter is a multipoint cutting tool having either a cylindrical or a conical or a disk-shaped body, provided with a number of cutting teeth on its periphery or face, used for producing flat or contoured surfaces by progressive removal of material from a workpiece held on a milling machine."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Pizza Cutter on Steroids!

Imagine a **rolling pizza cutter** in your kitchen. It has a single circular blade that slices through the dough as you push it forward. Now imagine an industrial version of this, but:

- Instead of one blade, it has **dozens of sharp teeth** arranged around its edge (like a circular saw, but sturdier).
- The cutter itself is spinning very fast on a horizontal axis (not your hand pushing it).
- A stationary slab of metal (the workpiece) is fed into this rotating toothed wheel.
- Each tiny tooth acts like a tiny chisel — it chips away a small piece of metal as it rotates past the workpiece.

That, in essence, is a milling cutter at work. Each tooth takes a "bite" of metal, comes out of contact, rotates around, and then comes back to take another bite. This **interrupted cutting** is what distinguishes milling from turning.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Anchors for KTU Exams:**
> - **Tooth (Flute):** A single cutting edge on the cutter.
> - **Cutter Body (Shank/Arbor Hole):** The structural part of the cutter.
> - **Rake Angle (α):** The angle of the tooth face relative to a reference plane.
> - **Clearance Angle (γ):** The angle that prevents the tooth flank from rubbing on the workpiece.
> - **Pitch:** The angular distance between adjacent teeth.

### 1.3 Physical / Geometric Constants of Standard Milling Cutters

| Parameter | Standard Range | KTU Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Cutter diameter (D) | **40 mm – 200 mm** | Module 3 standard |
| Number of teeth (Z) | **4 – 20** | Depends on cutter type |
| Rake angle (α) | **0° – 20°** | Positive, negative, or zero |
| Clearance angle (γ) | **3° – 15°** | Always positive |
| Cutter material | **HSS, Carbide, Ceramics, CBN, PCD** | Module 3 syllabus |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why milling cutters have multiple teeth:** Because the cut is *interrupted*, the cutting teeth have time to cool during the non-engagement portion of the rotation. This means milling cutters can run at **higher cutting speeds** than single-point turning tools, dramatically increasing productivity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric Nomenclature of a Milling Cutter Tooth

Each tooth on a milling cutter has the following critical geometric features, which **KTU examiners love to label and ask in diagrams**:

1. **Cutting Edge (Lip):** The actual sharp edge that contacts the workpiece. Each tooth may have 1, 2, or 3 cutting lips (e.g., a plain milling cutter has teeth on the periphery only; an end mill has teeth on periphery + face).

2. **Rake Face:** The surface of the tooth over which the chip flows.

3. **Flank (Clearance Face):** The surface behind the cutting edge that provides clearance to prevent rubbing.

4. **Rake Angle (α):** Angle between the rake face and a reference plane.
   - **Positive rake:** Rake face slopes downward from cutting edge (typical for soft materials).
   - **Negative rake:** Rake face slopes upward (used for hard materials like cast iron).
   - **Zero rake:** Vertical rake face (used for high-temperature alloys).

5. **Clearance Angle (γ):** Angle between the flank and the tangent at the cutting edge. Prevents the flank from rubbing against the machined surface.

6. **Lip Angle / Cutting Edge Angle:** The angle that the cutting edge makes with a plane perpendicular to the cutter axis (relevant for side-milling cutters).

7. **Pitch (p):** The arc distance between two adjacent teeth along the periphery. *Mathematically:* $p = \dfrac{\pi D}{Z}$

8. **Tooth Space (g):** The gap between two adjacent teeth, which must be large enough to accommodate the chip volume.

## 2.2 Classification of Milling Cutters (The Big Hierarchy)

Milling cutters are classified based on **shape, mounting, and application**. KTU module 3 expects mastery of this taxonomy:

### A. **Based on Shape / Construction**

| Type | Description | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Plain (Slab) Cutter** | Teeth on periphery only; cylindrical body | Producing flat horizontal surfaces |
| **Side Milling Cutter** | Teeth on periphery + side faces | Cutting slots, deep grooves, keyways |
| **End Mill** | Teeth on periphery + end face | Profile milling, pockets, die work |
| **Face Mill** | Large-diameter cutter with insert teeth | High MRR surface machining (CNC, VMC) |
| **Angle Cutter** | Teeth angled to cutter axis | Cutting V-grooves, dovetails, angular surfaces |
| **Form Cutter** | Tooth profile matches the desired shape | Gear teeth, concave/convex profiles |
| **T-Slot Cutter** | T-shaped profile | Cutting T-slots in machine tool tables |
| **Woodruff Cutter** | Small disk-shaped cutter | Cutting semi-circular keyways |
| **Slitting Saw** | Very thin cutter (1–3 mm) | Cutting deep narrow slots |
| **Fly Cutter** | Single-point tool mounted on a rotating body | Light surface finishing, large flat surfaces |

### B. **Based on Mounting**

- **Arbor-Mounted Cutters:** Have a central hole (e.g., plain cutter, side cutter). Mounted on a horizontal milling machine arbor.
- **Shank-Mounted Cutters:** Have an integral cylindrical (straight) or tapered (e.g., Morse taper) shank (e.g., end mills, drill-mill). Mounted directly in the spindle.

### C. **Based on Material of Cutter**

| Material | HRC / Hardness | Application | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| **High-Speed Steel (HSS)** | ~62–65 HRC | General purpose, low–medium speed | Low |
| **Cemented Carbide** | ~85 HRA | High-speed machining of steel, cast iron | Medium |
| **Coated Carbide (TiN, Al₂O₃)** | ~90 HRA | High productivity, abrasive materials | Medium-High |
| **Ceramics (Al₂O₃, Si₃N₄)** | ~92 HRA | High-temperature alloys, cast iron | High |
| **CBN (Cubic Boron Nitride)** | ~95 HRA | Hardened steels (HRC > 50) | Very High |
| **PCD (Polycrystalline Diamond)** | ~10000 HV | Non-ferrous metals, composites | Very High |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Print This!)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important cheat sheet** for KTU ESE Module 3. Memorize these equations — they appear in nearly every numerical problem.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cutting Speed (V) | $V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ | m/min | D in mm, N in rpm |
| 2 | Spindle Speed (N) | $N = \dfrac{1000 \, V}{\pi D}$ | rpm | Derived by inverting #1 |
| 3 | Feed per Tooth (f_z) | Given by tables | mm/tooth | Typical: 0.05 – 0.3 mm/tooth |
| 4 | Feed Rate (v_f) | $v_f = f_z \times Z \times N$ | mm/min | Total table feed |
| 5 | Material Removal Rate (MRR) | $\text{MRR} = w \times d \times v_f$ | mm³/min | w = width, d = depth |
| 6 | Specific Cutting Energy (K_s) | Given by tables | N/mm² | Steel ≈ 2000 N/mm² |
| 7 | Power Required (P) | $P = \dfrac{\text{MRR} \times K_s}{60 \times 10^6}$ | kW | Standard KTU form |
| 8 | Pitch of Cutter (p) | $p = \dfrac{\pi D}{Z}$ | mm | Arc length between teeth |
| 9 | Time for Milling (t_m) | $t_m = \dfrac{L + l_1 + l_2}{v_f}$ | min | L = workpiece length; l_1, l_2 = approach & overtravel |
| 10 | Centrifugal Force on Cutter Tooth | $F_c = \dfrac{m \, v^2}{r}$ | N | m = mass per tooth, v = tangential velocity |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU RULE:** Never write absolute value `|x|` inside a markdown table — it breaks rendering. Use `\vert x \vert` instead. This is a **mark-losing trap** in scanned answer sheets.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

Milling cutters are the workhorse of **discrete-parts manufacturing**. Their use cases span:

- **Aerospace:** Machining aluminum wing ribs (PCD end mills), titanium blisks (carbide end mills).
- **Automotive:** Engine block deck faces (face mills with carbide inserts), crankshaft keyways.
- **Die & Mold Making:** Profile milling of complex 3D cavities (ball-nose end mills).
- **General Engineering:** Slotting, keyway cutting, gear cutting (form cutters / hobbing).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why milling cutters matter in KTU context:** Every numerical in Module 3 (cutting force, power, MRR, time estimation) hinges on understanding cutter geometry. Without knowing D, Z, and α, you cannot solve a single problem.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the **full mathematical reasoning** behind every formula in the cheat sheet, so you can defend your work in KTU valuations.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cutting Speed (V) of a Milling Cutter

### Step 1: Set up the geometric relationship
A milling cutter of diameter **D** rotates at **N revolutions per minute**. The cutter's outermost point traces a circular path whose circumference is:

$$C = \pi D$$

where C is in **mm per revolution** (since D is in mm).

### Step 2: Scale up to per-minute
If the cutter makes **N revolutions in one minute**, then in one minute the point travels a total linear distance equal to:

$$\text{Distance per minute} = C \times N = \pi D \times N$$

This distance, expressed in **millimeters per minute (mm/min)**, is the **cutting speed** in linear units.

### Step 3: Convert to standard KTU units (m/min)
Since 1 m = 1000 mm, we divide by 1000:

$$V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000} \quad \text{(m/min)}$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula**. ✓

> **Conversion logic:** Cutting speed is fundamentally a tangential velocity at the cutter's outer edge. The circumference (πD) gives distance per revolution; multiplying by N gives per minute; dividing by 1000 gives meters.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Spindle Speed (N) from Cutting Speed

This is just a re-arrangement of the cutting-speed equation. We solve for N:

$$\pi D N = 1000 \, V$$

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \, V}{\pi D} \quad \text{(rpm)}$$

**Solved.** ✓

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Feed Rate (v_f) in Milling

The feed rate is the speed at which the workpiece advances under the rotating cutter. To derive it:

### Step 1: Define feed per tooth
Each tooth, when it engages the workpiece, advances a small distance called **feed per tooth (f_z)**, measured in mm/tooth.

### Step 2: Count total teeth engagements per revolution
In one full revolution of the cutter, all **Z** teeth engage the workpiece **once each**. So in one revolution, the workpiece advances by:

$$\text{Feed per revolution} = f_z \times Z \quad \text{(mm/rev)}$$

### Step 3: Scale to per-minute
If the cutter makes N revolutions per minute, then the feed rate is:

$$v_f = f_z \times Z \times N \quad \text{(mm/min)}$$

**Solved.** ✓

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Material Removal Rate (MRR)

MRR is the volume of material removed per unit time. For a milling operation, consider a slab of material of width **w** and depth **d** being removed as the cutter advances at feed rate **v_f**.

### Step 1: Volume removed per unit length of feed
For every 1 mm the cutter advances along the workpiece, the cross-sectional area of the removed material is:

$$A = w \times d \quad \text{(mm}^2\text{)}$$

So, per mm of feed, the volume removed is **w × d mm³**.

### Step 2: Scale up to per-minute
If the cutter advances at **v_f mm/min**, then the volume removed per minute is:

$$\text{MRR} = w \times d \times v_f \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{/min)}$$

**Solved.** ✓

> **Conversion logic:** MRR in milling equals (cross-section of cut) × (linear feed rate). This is a general principle that applies to all material-removal processes (turning, drilling, grinding).

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Power Required (P)

Power is the rate of doing work, and in machining, it equals the rate of energy consumed in shearing the material.

### Step 1: Energy consumed per unit volume
The **specific cutting energy (K_s)** is the energy required to remove a unit volume of material, expressed in N/mm² (= J/mm³). It is a material property tabulated in KTU data booklets.

### Step 2: Energy consumed per minute
If MRR is the volume removed per minute, the total energy consumed per minute is:

$$E_{\text{per min}} = \text{MRR} \times K_s \quad \text{(N·mm/min)} = \text{(J/min)}$$

### Step 3: Convert to kilowatts
Since 1 kW = 60 × 10⁶ J/min (because 1 kJ/min = 1000 J/60 s = 16.67 J/s = 16.67 W → so 1 kW = 1 kJ/s = 60,000 kJ/min = 60×10⁶ J/min):

$$P = \dfrac{\text{MRR} \times K_s}{60 \times 10^6} \quad \text{(kW)}$$

**Solved.** ✓

## 3.6 Worked Example — A Typical KTU Numerical

> **[KTU Sample Numerical — Dec 2024 Pattern]**
> A plain milling cutter of diameter **D = 80 mm** with **Z = 8 teeth** runs at a spindle speed of **N = 200 rpm**. The feed per tooth is **f_z = 0.15 mm/tooth**, the width of cut is **w = 60 mm**, and the depth of cut is **d = 4 mm**. The specific cutting energy is **K_s = 2200 N/mm²**. Find:
> (i) Cutting speed, (ii) Feed rate, (iii) MRR, (iv) Power required.

### Solution:

**(i) Cutting Speed**

$$V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000} = \dfrac{\pi \times 80 \times 200}{1000} = \dfrac{50265.5}{1000} = 50.27 \text{ m/min}$$

**(ii) Feed Rate**

$$v_f = f_z \times Z \times N = 0.15 \times 8 \times 200 = 240 \text{ mm/min}$$

**(iii) Material Removal Rate**

$$\text{MRR} = w \times d \times v_f = 60 \times 4 \times 240 = 57{,}600 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$$

**(iv) Power Required**

$$P = \dfrac{\text{MRR} \times K_s}{60 \times 10^6} = \dfrac{57600 \times 2200}{60 \times 10^6} = \dfrac{1.267 \times 10^8}{6 \times 10^7} = 2.11 \text{ kW}$$

> **Conversion logic summary:** (1) Cutting speed uses cutter diameter; (2) Feed rate aggregates per-tooth feed across all teeth and revolutions; (3) MRR is cross-section × linear feed; (4) Power scales MRR by specific energy and converts to kW.

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Forces on a Milling Cutter Tooth

Each tooth on a milling cutter experiences **three orthogonal force components**:

| Force Component | Direction | Effect | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tangential (F_t)** | Direction of cutter rotation at cutting edge | Does the actual cutting work | Used to compute power: $P = \dfrac{F_t \times v_c}{60{,}000}$ kW |
| **Radial (F_r)** | Perpendicular to cutter axis, toward center | Pushes cutter away from workpiece | Affects deflection, vibration |
| **Axial (F_a)** | Parallel to cutter axis | Affects spindle bearing load | Important for end mills |

**Total Power Equation (from tangential force):**

$$P = \dfrac{F_t \times V}{60{,}000} \quad \text{(kW)}$$

where V is cutting speed in m/min and F_t is tangential force in Newtons. This equation is often the **direct route to computing cutting force** from known power, and KTU examiners frequently set problems the other way around.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

Since the exact physical geometry of a milling cutter is complex to render in Mermaid, the diagrams below provide a **block-level functional architecture** mapping the classification hierarchy and the operational workflow — both of which are highly tested in KTU theory questions.

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Classification of Milling Cutters

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MILLING CUTTER]:::root
    A --> B[BY SHAPE]
    A --> C[BY MOUNTING]
    A --> D[BY MATERIAL]
    A --> E[BY APPLICATION]

    B --> B1[Plain Cutter]
    B --> B2[Side Cutter]
    B --> B3[End Mill]
    B --> B4[Face Mill]
    B --> B5[Angle Cutter]
    B --> B6[Form Cutter]
    B --> B7[TSlot Cutter]
    B --> B8[Woodruff Cutter]
    B --> B9[Slitting Saw]

    C --> C1[Arbor Mounted]
    C --> C2[Shank Mounted]
    C1 --> C1a[Plain, Side, Slitting Saw]
    C2 --> C2a[End Mill, TSlot, Drill Mill]

    D --> D1[HSS]
    D --> D2[Carbide]
    D --> D3[Coated Carbide]
    D --> D4[Ceramics]
    D --> D5[CBN]
    D --> D6[PCD]

    E --> E1[Slab Milling]
    E --> E2[Profile Milling]
    E --> E3[Slot Milling]
    E --> E4[Gear Cutting]

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Geometry of a Single Milling Tooth

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[TOOTH GEOMETRY]:::root
    T1 --> T2[Rake Face]
    T1 --> T3[Flank or Clearance Face]
    T1 --> T4[Cutting Edge or Lip]
    T1 --> T5[Rake Angle alpha]
    T1 --> T6[Clearance Angle gamma]
    T1 --> T7[Lip Angle phi]
    T1 --> T8[Pitch p]
    T1 --> T9[Tooth Space g]

    T2 --> T2a[Surface over which chip flows]
    T3 --> T3a[Surface that prevents rubbing]
    T4 --> T4a[Sharp edge that does the cutting]
    T5 --> T5a[Angle of tooth face to reference]
    T6 --> T6a[Angle preventing flank contact]
    T7 --> T7a[Angle of edge with cutter axis]
    T8 --> T8a[Arc distance between adjacent teeth]
    T9 --> T9a[Volume available for chip]

    classDef root fill:#0f766e,stroke:#0f766e,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Process Flow — Milling Operation Functional Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Workpiece Mounted on Table]:::step
    P1 --> P2[Spindle Rotates Cutter at N rpm]
    P2 --> P3[Cutter Teeth Engage Workpiece]
    P3 --> P4[Each Tooth Removes Chip]
    P4 --> P5[Chip Flows Up Rake Face]
    P5 --> P6[New Tooth Engages After Pitch Angle]
    P6 --> P7[Coolant Applied at Cutting Zone]
    P7 --> P8[MRR computed as w times d times vf]
    P8 --> P9[Power Computed from MRR and Ks]
    P9 --> P10[Finished Surface Produced]

    classDef step fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Tip:** When asked to draw a milling cutter in the exam, the **mandatory labels** are: cutting edge, rake face, flank, rake angle (α), clearance angle (γ), and pitch (p). Missing any one of these labels typically costs 1–2 marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Q.1.a]**
> **Q1. Define a milling cutter. List any four types of milling cutters.**

**Model Answer:**
A milling cutter is a multipoint rotary cutting tool having cutting teeth on its periphery or face, used for removing material from a workpiece in a milling machine.

Four types:
1. **Plain (slab) milling cutter** — for flat horizontal surfaces.
2. **Side milling cutter** — for slots and keyways.
3. **End mill** — for profiling and pocket milling.
4. **Face mill** — for high-MRR surface machining.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Q.1.b]**
> **Q2. What is rake angle? Differentiate between positive and negative rake angles.**

**Model Answer:**
Rake angle is the angle between the rake face of the tooth and a reference plane.

- **Positive rake angle:** Rake face slopes downward from the cutting edge. Reduces cutting force, requires less power, but weakens the cutting edge. Used for soft materials.
- **Negative rake angle:** Rake face slopes upward from the cutting edge. Strengthens the cutting edge, allows higher speeds, but increases power consumption. Used for hard materials like cast iron.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Q.6]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the geometry of a plain milling cutter. Label the following: cutting edge, rake face, flank, rake angle, clearance angle, pitch. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A plain milling cutter of diameter 100 mm with 10 teeth is used to machine a workpiece of width 80 mm at a depth of 5 mm. The cutting speed is 40 m/min and feed per tooth is 0.2 mm/tooth. Calculate the (i) spindle speed, (ii) feed rate, (iii) MRR, and (iv) power required. Take specific cutting energy K_s = 2400 N/mm². **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The sketch must show:
- A cylindrical cutter body.
- Teeth on the periphery.
- A central arbor hole.
- Labels for: **cutting edge** (the sharp edge), **rake face** (front face of tooth), **flank** (rear face of tooth), **rake angle α** (angle of rake face with radial line), **clearance angle γ** (angle of flank with tangent to the circle at cutting edge), and **pitch p** (arc distance between two consecutive teeth = πD/Z).

*Note:* In the actual exam, a clear hand-drawn sketch with all six labels is mandatory for full 7 marks. A sketch without labels scores ≤ 3 marks.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: D = 100 mm, Z = 10, w = 80 mm, d = 5 mm, V = 40 m/min, f_z = 0.2 mm/tooth, K_s = 2400 N/mm².

**(i) Spindle Speed:**

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \, V}{\pi D} = \dfrac{1000 \times 40}{\pi \times 100} = \dfrac{40000}{314.16} = 127.32 \text{ rpm}$$

**(ii) Feed Rate:**

$$v_f = f_z \times Z \times N = 0.2 \times 10 \times 127.32 = 254.65 \text{ mm/min}$$

**(iii) MRR:**

$$\text{MRR} = w \times d \times v_f = 80 \times 5 \times 254.65 = 101{,}859 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$$

**(iv) Power Required:**

$$P = \dfrac{\text{MRR} \times K_s}{60 \times 10^6} = \dfrac{101859 \times 2400}{60 \times 10^6} = \dfrac{2.445 \times 10^8}{6 \times 10^7} = 4.07 \text{ kW}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Compare HSS and Carbide as materials of milling cutters. State their typical applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A face milling cutter of diameter 150 mm with 8 inserts is used on a cast iron workpiece. The cutting speed is 250 m/min, feed per insert is 0.25 mm/tooth, depth of cut is 3 mm, and width of cut is 120 mm. If the specific cutting energy for cast iron is 1500 N/mm², calculate the (i) spindle speed, (ii) feed rate, (iii) MRR, (iv) power. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table:**

| Property | HSS | Carbide |
|---|---|---|
| Hot hardness | Loses hardness above ~600°C | Retains hardness up to ~900°C |
| Cutting speed | 30–60 m/min | 100–300 m/min |
| Tool life | Moderate | High |
| Cost | Low | Higher |
| Toughness | High (resists shock) | Lower (brittle) |
| Typical use | Low-speed, general purpose, soft materials | High-speed, mass production, hardened steels, cast iron |

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: D = 150 mm, Z = 8, V = 250 m/min, f_z = 0.25 mm/tooth, d = 3 mm, w = 120 mm, K_s = 1500 N/mm².

**(i) Spindle Speed:**

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \times 250}{\pi \times 150} = \dfrac{250000}{471.24} = 530.5 \text{ rpm}$$

**(ii) Feed Rate:**

$$v_f = 0.25 \times 8 \times 530.5 = 1061 \text{ mm/min}$$

**(iii) MRR:**

$$\text{MRR} = 120 \times 3 \times 1061 = 381{,}960 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$$

**(iv) Power Required:**

$$P = \dfrac{381960 \times 1500}{60 \times 10^6} = \dfrac{5.73 \times 10^8}{6 \times 10^7} = 9.55 \text{ kW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing mm with m, or rpm with rev/s. Always write units in **every single line** of the numerical solution.
> 2. **Missing Sketch Labels:** In part (a) of any cutter-geometry question, students draw the cutter but forget to label **rake angle** and **clearance angle** distinctly. This typically costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing feed per tooth vs. feed per revolution:** A common error is writing $v_f = f_z \times N$ (forgetting to multiply by Z). The correct form is $v_f = f_z \times Z \times N$.
> 4. **Power formula denominator:** Writing $10^6$ instead of $60 \times 10^6$ is a 1-mark deduction. The 60 is **mandatory** (conversion of /min to /s).
> 5. **Not stating assumptions:** In derivation questions, explicitly state "we neglect the effect of the built-up edge" or "we assume orthogonal cutting" for full marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Milling Cutters**

- [x] A milling cutter is a **multipoint** rotary cutting tool — distinguish it from a single-point turning tool.
- [x] **Tooth geometry** has 6 mandatory labels: cutting edge, rake face, flank, rake angle (α), clearance angle (γ), pitch (p).
- [x] **Positive rake** = for soft materials; **Negative rake** = for hard materials; **Zero rake** = for high-temperature alloys.
- [x] **Clearance angle** is *always* positive (otherwise the flank rubs the workpiece).
- [x] **Pitch** $p = \dfrac{\pi D}{Z}$ — this is the most-tested formula after cutting speed.
- [x] **Cutting speed** $V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ m/min — the **canonical** KTU equation.
- [x] **Feed rate** $v_f = f_z \times Z \times N$ mm/min — note all **three** multiplicative terms.
- [x] **MRR** = $w \times d \times v_f$ mm³/min — same form as turning MRR.
- [x] **Power** = $\dfrac{\text{MRR} \times K_s}{60 \times 10^6}$ kW — the **60** is for minutes-to-seconds; **10⁶** is for N·mm → kW.
- [x] **Milling cutter types to remember:** Plain, Side, End Mill, Face Mill, Angle, Form, T-Slot, Woodruff, Slitting Saw.
- [x] **Arbor-mounted** cutters have a central hole; **shank-mounted** cutters (end mills) have an integral shank.
- [x] **Material hierarchy (increasing hot hardness):** HSS < Carbide < Coated Carbide < Ceramics < CBN < PCD.
- [x] **Forces on tooth:** Tangential (F_t — does the cutting), Radial (F_r — pushes cutter), Axial (F_a — along spindle axis).
- [x] **Power from force:** $P = \dfrac{F_t \times V}{60{,}000}$ kW — useful when F_t is given.
- [x] **Milling time:** $t_m = \dfrac{L + l_1 + l_2}{v_f}$ — remember to **add approach and overtravel** distances.

> **Closing Note:** Mastering milling cutters means internalizing both the **geometry** (for diagram questions) and the **formulas** (for numerical questions). KTU typically asks one 7-mark diagram question and one 7-mark numerical in this module, with full internal choice. Use the cheat sheet in Section 2 as your single source of truth.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simple Indexing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT404 - MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Simple Indexing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Simple Indexing — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simple Indexing** is a fundamental operation in **milling and shaping machines** wherein a workpiece, mounted on a **Dividing Head (Index Head)**, is rotated through a precise, predetermined angular displacement between successive machining cuts, in order to produce features at **equally spaced angular positions** around a circular component.

The term "Simple" distinguishes it from **Differential Indexing** and **Compound Indexing** because it uses **only one crank rotation** of the dividing head spindle to achieve the required angular movement — no additional gear train manipulation is involved.

Mathematically, the indexing operation is governed by the **Indexing Equation**:

$$N = \frac{40}{x}$$

Where:
- $N$ = Number of full turns of the index crank
- $40$ = Reduction ratio of the standard dividing head (worm gear ratio)
- $x$ = Number of equal divisions required on the workpiece

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The Clock Face Concept

Imagine you are at a **clock with only an hour hand**, and you want to stop at every numbered hour (1, 2, 3... 12). If the hand starts at **12**, you would need to rotate it **1/12th of a full circle** twelve times. Each pause represents one equal division.

In simple indexing:
- The **crank** of the dividing head = your **hand**
- The **40:1 worm gear** of the dividing head = the mechanical arrangement that multiplies your small hand movement into a precise, magnified rotation of the workpiece
- The **number of divisions (x)** = the number of "stops" you need to make around the circle

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A dividing head is essentially a precision **mechanical calculator** — you input a fraction of a turn (via the crank and index plate), and the worm gear mechanism multiplies it by 40 to produce the required workpiece rotation.

## 1.3 Physical Components of an Indexing Setup (Preview)

The standard **Dividing Head** consists of:
- **Spindle** — holds the workpiece via a chuck or between centers
- **Worm and Worm Wheel** — provides the 40:1 ratio (40 turns of crank = 1 turn of spindle)
- **Index Crank** — the handle that the operator turns
- **Index Plate** — a circular plate with concentric circles of equally spaced holes
- **Index Pin (Sector Arm)** — a retractable pin that locks into the holes of the index plate to control the crank position
- **Footstock** — the support for the free end of long workpieces

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners often ask students to **list the components of a dividing head** and **state the standard reduction ratio (40:1)**. Memorize this — it appears in nearly every question paper.

---

## 1.4 Standard Index Plates and Their Hole Circles

A **set of index plates** is provided with each dividing head. A typical Brown & Sharpe dividing head comes with **3 plates**, each having the following hole circles:

| Plate No. | Hole Circles Available (Number of Holes) |
|:---------:|:----------------------------------------:|
| Plate 1   | 15, 16, 17, 18, 19, 20                  |
| Plate 2   | 21, 23, 27, 29, 31, 33                  |
| Plate 3   | 37, 39, 41, 43, 47, 49                  |

> [!NOTE]
> **Selection Logic:** The number of divisions $x$ must be **divisible** by at least one of the hole circle numbers. If not, the operation cannot be performed by **simple indexing** and requires **differential or compound indexing**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics Behind the 40:1 Ratio

The worm gear mechanism is the heart of the dividing head. It consists of:
- A **single-thread worm** (1 start) on the crank shaft
- A **40-tooth worm wheel** on the spindle

For every **40 revolutions** of the worm (and hence the crank), the worm wheel — and therefore the **spindle holding the workpiece** — completes exactly **1 full revolution (360°)**.

This is the fundamental reason for the "40" in the indexing equation. It is a **mechanical amplifier** that converts coarse hand movements into fine, precise rotations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 40 and not some other number?**
> 40 is highly **factorable** ($40 = 2^3 \times 5$), meaning that $40/x$ yields a clean ratio for many common division values: 2, 4, 5, 8, 10, 20, 40, etc. This makes it ideal for a wide range of practical divisions.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Indexing Operation

When a student asks, "How does the dividing head actually work?", break the answer into these structured steps:

1. **Identify the required divisions ($x$):** Determine how many equal angular features are needed (e.g., 6 for a hex bolt head, 8 for a square, 12 for gear teeth starter, etc.).

2. **Compute the required spindle rotation:** The workpiece must rotate by $360°/x$ for each indexing step.

3. **Compute the corresponding crank rotation:** Using the 40:1 ratio, the crank must rotate by $40 \times (360°/x) / 360° = 40/x$ turns.

4. **Convert to whole turns + fraction:** Express $40/x$ as a mixed number. The whole number of full turns + the fractional part.

5. **Select the appropriate index plate hole circle:** The denominator of the fraction must match an available hole circle.

6. **Engage the index pin in the chosen hole:** The sector arm is set to span exactly the number of holes in the fraction, and the pin is dropped into the final hole.

7. **Lock and machine:** The spindle is locked, the cut is made, and the process is repeated $x$ times.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is HIGH-YIELD for KTU exams. Memorize every formula and the units.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description |
|:-:|:---------:|:-------:|:-----------:|
| 1 | Number of full crank turns | $N = \dfrac{40}{x}$ | Standard simple indexing equation |
| 2 | Angle between divisions (degrees) | $\theta = \dfrac{360°}{x}$ | Workpiece rotation per indexing step |
| 3 | Angle per crank turn (degrees) | $\phi = \dfrac{360°}{40} = 9°$ | Spindle rotation per single crank turn |
| 4 | Crank rotation per division (degrees) | $\alpha = \dfrac{40}{x} \times 360°$ | Alternative expression for $N$ in degrees |
| 5 | Angular position of $n$-th division | $\theta_n = n \cdot \dfrac{360°}{x}$ | Where $n$ is the division count (1, 2, 3...) |
| 6 | Plate selection condition | $x \text{ must be divisible by an available hole circle}$ | Necessary condition for simple indexing |
| 7 | Index crank turns for partial revolution | $N = \dfrac{\theta_{req}}{9°}$ | When a specific angle (not division count) is given |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Simple indexing is the **workhorse of gear cutting on milling machines**. Specifically:

- **Cutting spur gears** (initial blanking and tooth formation when a gear hobbing machine is unavailable)
- **Cutting splines on shafts** (e.g., 6-spline, 10-spline automotive drive shafts)
- **Milling square or hexagonal heads** on bolts and fasteners
- **Cutting keyways** at specific angular positions
- **Drilling bolt-hole circles** on flanges and circular plates
- **Machining cam profiles** with multiple identical lobes

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Insight:** In modern CNC machining, indexing is performed by **servo-driven rotary tables** controlled by G-code (e.g., `A90.0` for a 4-division index). However, the **fundamental indexing equation $N = 40/x$** remains conceptually valid in CNC — the controller simply executes the same rotational logic electronically.

---

## 2.5 Worked Example Skeleton (For Mental Preparation)

Before jumping into the derivation section, here is a quick example to set the stage:

> **Problem:** Cut a **hexagonal (6-sided)** feature on a workpiece using simple indexing.
> **Solution:**
> - Divisions $x = 6$
> - $N = 40/6 = 6 + 4/6 = 6 + 2/3$ turns
> - Use a **hole circle of 6 holes** (e.g., on Plate 1 with the 18-hole circle, every 3rd hole; or note: there is no 6-circle, so use 18 ÷ 3)
> - The pin moves **2/3 of the way** around a 3-hole sector, locking into the 3rd hole

We will solve this **and more complex examples** in the next section.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the Indexing Equation

The derivation is **mandatory** for KTU 14-mark questions. Examiners expect students to start from first principles, not just write $N = 40/x$.

### Step 1: Define the Problem Geometry

The workpiece must be divided into $x$ equal angular parts. Therefore, the angular advance between two consecutive cuts is:

$$\theta = \frac{360°}{x}$$

This is the **required rotation of the spindle (workpiece)** per indexing step.

### Step 2: Apply the Worm Gear Ratio

The dividing head has a worm-to-worm-wheel ratio of $40:1$. This means:

$$1 \text{ revolution of spindle} = 40 \text{ revolutions of crank}$$

$$1° \text{ of spindle rotation} = \frac{40}{360} \text{ revolutions of crank} = \frac{1}{9} \text{ revolutions of crank}$$

### Step 3: Express Crank Rotation in Terms of $\theta$

The crank must rotate by:

$$N_{turns} = \frac{\theta}{9°} = \frac{360°/x}{9°} = \frac{360}{9x} = \frac{40}{x}$$

Thus, the **canonical simple indexing equation** is derived:

$$N = \frac{40}{x}$$

where $N$ is the **number of full revolutions of the index crank** between two consecutive divisions.

### Step 4: Express $N$ as a Mixed Number (Practical Form)

For actual operation, $N$ must be decomposed into a whole number of turns plus a fraction. For example:

$$N = \frac{40}{x} = a + \frac{b}{c}$$

where $a$ is the number of full turns, and $b/c$ is the fractional part. The denominator $c$ must match a hole circle on one of the three index plates.

---

## 3.2 Comprehensive Analytical Worked Examples

### Example 1: Indexing for a Square (4 Divisions)

**Given:** A square must be milled on a cylindrical workpiece. Compute the indexing requirement.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Identify divisions: For a square, $x = 4$.

Step 2 — Apply the formula:

$$N = \frac{40}{x} = \frac{40}{4} = 10 \text{ turns}$$

Step 3 — Interpretation: The crank must be turned **exactly 10 full revolutions** between cuts. No fractional turn is needed.

Step 4 — Verification: Spindle rotation per step $= 10/40 = 1/4$ revolution $= 90°$. ✓ (Square has $360°/4 = 90°$ between sides.)

---

### Example 2: Hexagonal Feature (6 Divisions)

**Given:** Mill a hexagonal head on a bolt blank. $x = 6$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Apply the indexing formula:

$$N = \frac{40}{6} = \frac{20}{3} = 6 \frac{2}{3} \text{ turns}$$

Step 2 — Decompose: $N = 6 + 2/3$.

Step 3 — Plate selection: We need a hole circle where a sector of 2/3 of the circle works. 
- Check Plate 1: Hole circles 15, 16, 17, 18, 19, 20. 
- The denominator 3 is not directly available. 
- **Solution:** Use the **15-hole circle** and move the index pin **10 holes** (because $10/15 = 2/3$). 
- Alternatively, use the **18-hole circle** and move **12 holes** (because $12/18 = 2/3$). ✓

Step 4 — Verification: Crank rotation $= 6 + 10/15 = 6 + 2/3 = 20/3$ turns. ✓
Spindle rotation $= (20/3)/40 = 1/6$ revolution $= 60°$. ✓ (Hexagon has $360°/6 = 60°$.)

---

### Example 3: Gear with 24 Teeth (24 Divisions)

**Given:** Cut a 24-tooth gear on a milling machine. $x = 24$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Apply the formula:

$$N = \frac{40}{24} = \frac{5}{3} = 1 \frac{2}{3} \text{ turns}$$

Step 2 — Plate selection: Denominator is 3.
- Not directly available. 
- Use **15-hole circle**: move **10 holes** ($10/15 = 2/3$).
- Or use **18-hole circle**: move **12 holes** ($12/18 = 2/3$).
- Or use **21-hole circle**: move **14 holes** ($14/21 = 2/3$).

Step 3 — Verification: Spindle rotation per step $= (5/3)/40 = 1/24$ revolution $= 15°$. ✓ (Gear has $360°/24 = 15°$ between teeth.)

---

### Example 4: 35 Divisions — A Critical Plate Selection Case

**Given:** Index for 35 equal divisions.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Apply the formula:

$$N = \frac{40}{35} = \frac{8}{7} = 1 \frac{1}{7} \text{ turns}$$

Step 2 — Plate selection: Denominator 7 — not directly available on any plate.
- **This is a key learning point:** Without a 7-hole circle, **simple indexing cannot directly perform 35 divisions**.
- However, we can use **multiples of 7** that ARE available:
  - **21-hole circle** on Plate 2: move $1/7 \times 21 = 3$ holes.
  - **49-hole circle** on Plate 3: move $1/7 \times 49 = 7$ holes.

Step 3 — Use the **21-hole circle** and move **3 holes** per indexing step.

Step 4 — Verification: Crank rotation $= 1 + 3/21 = 1 + 1/7 = 8/7$ turns. ✓
Spindle rotation $= (8/7)/40 = 8/280 = 1/35$ revolution. ✓

---

### Example 5: Direct Angle Indexing (9° Per Step)

**Given:** Rotate the workpiece by exactly **9° per step** (a common case in cam milling).

**Solution:**

Step 1 — The angle per step is 9°. Using the direct formula:

$$N = \frac{\theta_{req}}{9°} = \frac{9°}{9°} = 1 \text{ full turn}$$

Step 2 — Interpretation: The crank must be turned **exactly once** between cuts.

Step 3 — Verification: Spindle rotation $= 1/40$ revolution $= 9°$. ✓

---

### Example 6: Non-Standard Angle — 7°30' Per Step

**Given:** Rotate the workpiece by **7°30' per step**.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Convert to decimal degrees: $7°30' = 7.5°$.

Step 2 — Apply the direct formula:

$$N = \frac{7.5°}{9°} = \frac{7.5}{9} = \frac{5}{6} = 0 \frac{5}{6} \text{ turns}$$

Step 3 — Plate selection: Denominator 6 — not directly available.
- Use **18-hole circle**: move $5/6 \times 18 = 15$ holes.
- Use **24-hole circle** (if available): move $5/6 \times 24 = 20$ holes.

Step 4 — Verification: Spindle rotation $= (5/6)/40 = 5/240 = 1/48$ revolution $= 360°/48 = 7.5°$. ✓

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Simple vs. Other Indexing Methods

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** This comparison table is asked in **8-mark and 14-mark questions** almost every year.

| Feature | Simple Indexing | Differential Indexing | Compound Indexing |
|:-------:|:---------------:|:---------------------:|:-----------------:|
| Number of crank turns per index | One complete calculation | Involves intermediate gears | Involves two crank positions |
| Plate holes required | Division by an available hole circle | Any number of divisions possible | Any number of divisions possible |
| Mechanism complexity | Simplest | Medium (requires change gears) | Most complex (two index plates) |
| Accuracy | Highest | Good | Good |
| Speed of operation | Fastest | Slower (gear setup) | Slowest |
| Plate divisibility constraint | Yes — must divide | No — uses gears to compensate | No — uses two fractions |
| Typical applications | Gears, squares, hexes | Prime number divisions (e.g., 51, 127) | High-precision odd divisions |
| Example division | $x = 6, N = 6 + 2/3$ | $x = 51$ (prime) | $x = 99$ |

---

## 3.4 Common Pitfalls in Indexing Calculations (Board Pattern Analysis)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Observations — Frequent Mark-Deduction Points:**

1. **Forgetting to convert the mixed number to a hole count:** Students often write $N = 6 + 2/3$ and stop. They must say: *"On a 15-hole circle, move 10 holes."*

2. **Selecting the wrong plate:** Using a plate hole circle that doesn't have a multiple of the denominator. For $N = 6 + 2/3$, students may try to use a 3-hole circle (nonexistent) instead of 15, 18, or 21.

3. **Forgetting to add the whole turns:** Writing only the fractional part and missing the 6 full revolutions.

4. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing degrees and revolutions in the same equation. Always keep units explicit.

5. **Not stating the plate number:** Examiners want to know **which plate and which hole circle** is used, not just the fraction.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Dividing Head

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operator Hand Input] --> B[Index Crank]
    B --> C[Index Pin Engagement]
    C --> D[Index Plate Hole Circle]
    D --> E[Worm Shaft 1 start]
    E --> F[Worm Wheel 40 teeth]
    F --> G[Dividing Head Spindle]
    G --> H[Workpiece Chuck or Center]
    H --> I[Rotational Output at 40:1 Ratio]
    I --> J[Angular Position Settled]
    J --> K[Milling Cutter Engagement]
    K --> L[Feature Machined 1 of x]
    L --> M{All x divisions done?}
    M -->|No| B
    M -->|Yes| N[Component Complete]
```

**Architecture Description:**
- The **flowchart** traces the **signal/power path** from the operator's hand to the final machined feature.
- Notice the **closed feedback loop** at step M → B, which represents the **iterative nature** of indexing — the process repeats $x$ times to complete the component.
- The **40:1 reduction** is implicit in the worm-to-worm-wheel coupling (steps E → F).

---

## 4.2 Geometric Schematic of the Indexing Equation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Problem: Divide into x parts] --> Q[Compute 40 by x]
    Q --> R[Fractional Result]
    R --> S[Whole Turns Plus Fraction]
    S --> T{Plate has matching hole circle?}
    T -->|Yes| U[Move Pin by Calculated Holes]
    T -->|No| V[Use Multiple of Denominator]
    V --> U
    U --> W[Lock Pin and Machine]
    W --> X[Repeat x Times]
    X --> Y[Final Indexing Complete]
```

**Description:**
- This is a **decision-flow diagram** showing the **logical path** of an indexing problem.
- The critical decision node is the **plate availability check** — if the denominator is not directly available, the operator must find a **multiple** of the denominator among the available hole circles.

---

## 4.3 Visual Indexing Pattern — Hexagonal Example (6 Divisions)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start Position 0 degrees] --> A1[Crank Turn 6 plus 2 over 3]
    A1 --> P1[Position 1 at 60 degrees]
    P1 --> A2[Crank Turn 6 plus 2 over 3]
    A2 --> P2[Position 2 at 120 degrees]
    P2 --> A3[Crank Turn 6 plus 2 over 3]
    A3 --> P3[Position 3 at 180 degrees]
    P3 --> A4[Crank Turn 6 plus 2 over 3]
    A4 --> P4[Position 4 at 240 degrees]
    P4 --> A5[Crank Turn 6 plus 2 over 3]
    A5 --> P5[Position 5 at 300 degrees]
    P5 --> A6[Crank Turn 6 plus 2 over 3]
    A6 --> P6[Position 6 at 360 degrees Back to Start]
```

**Description:**
- This **iterative flowchart** shows the 6-step indexing cycle for a hexagonal feature.
- Each arrow represents **one full indexing operation** (6 + 2/3 crank turns).
- The cycle terminates when the spindle returns to the original angular position (0° = 360°).

---

## 4.4 Plate Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Required Division x] --> Calc[Compute N = 40 over x]
    Calc --> Mixed[Express as Mixed Number]
    Mixed --> Denom[Identify Denominator c]
    Denom --> Check{c equals available hole circle?}
    Check -->|Yes| Direct[Use Direct Hole Circle]
    Check -->|No| Mult[Find Multiple of c on Plates]
    Mult --> Found{Multiple Available?}
    Found -->|Yes| UseMult[Use Multiple Hole Circle]
    Found -->|No| Diff[Switch to Differential Indexing]
    Direct --> Done[Indexing Setup Complete]
    UseMult --> Done
    Diff --> Done
```

**Description:**
- This is the **decision tree** an operator (or student) must follow when setting up an indexing operation.
- The terminal node "Differential Indexing" is **outside the scope of simple indexing** and is covered in higher modules.
- For simple indexing, the goal is always to **land in the "Indexing Setup Complete" node** via the top two paths.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define simple indexing. State the indexing equation and explain the role of the worm gear in a dividing head.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Simple indexing is a method of rotating a workpiece through predetermined equal angular increments using a dividing head, in which **only one crank turn calculation** is required per indexing step.

**Equation (1 mark):** The indexing equation is:

$$N = \frac{40}{x}$$

where $N$ is the number of crank revolutions and $x$ is the number of divisions required.

**Role of worm gear (1 mark):** The worm and worm wheel provide a **40:1 reduction ratio**, meaning 40 turns of the index crank produce 1 turn of the spindle. This enables **precise, fine angular control** of the workpiece by converting the operator's coarse hand movement into small, accurate spindle rotations.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**List the standard hole circles available on a typical set of three index plates used in simple indexing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A standard set of **three index plates** (Brown & Sharpe type) has the following hole circles:

- **Plate 1:** 15, 16, 17, 18, 19, 20 holes
- **Plate 2:** 21, 23, 27, 29, 31, 33 holes
- **Plate 3:** 37, 39, 41, 43, 47, 49 holes

For a simple indexing operation, the number of divisions $x$ must be **divisible by at least one of these numbers**, or the operation must be performed using **differential indexing**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Derive the simple indexing equation $N = 40/x$ starting from the worm gear ratio. State all assumptions clearly. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
- The dividing head has a standard **40:1 worm-to-wheel ratio**.
- The index plate is assumed to be free from wear and the worm backlash is negligible.

**Derivation (6 marks):**

The workpiece must be divided into $x$ equal angular parts. Hence, the angular advance per division is:

$$\theta = \frac{360°}{x} \quad \text{(Step 1)}$$

The worm gear ratio gives:

$$1 \text{ spindle revolution} = 40 \text{ crank revolutions} \quad \text{(Step 2)}$$

Therefore, the crank rotation per spindle degree is:

$$1 \text{ crank revolution} = \frac{360°}{40} = 9° \text{ of spindle rotation} \quad \text{(Step 3)}$$

The required crank turns $N$ for spindle rotation $\theta$ is:

$$N = \frac{\theta}{9°} \quad \text{(Step 4)}$$

Substituting $\theta = 360°/x$:

$$N = \frac{360°/x}{9°} = \frac{360}{9x} = \frac{40}{x} \quad \text{(Step 5)}$$

Hence derived:

$$\boxed{N = \frac{40}{x}}$$

---

**(b) For a 6-tooth gear cutting operation on a milling machine, determine: (i) the number of turns of the index crank, (ii) the index plate and hole circle to be used, and (iii) the number of holes to be moved. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Number of divisions (1 mark):** $x = 6$

**Step 2 — Number of crank turns (2 marks):**

$$N = \frac{40}{x} = \frac{40}{6} = \frac{20}{3} = 6 \frac{2}{3} \text{ turns}$$

**Step 3 — Plate and hole circle selection (2 marks):**

The fractional part has denominator **3**. The hole circle **3** does not exist directly, so we use a **multiple of 3** from the available hole circles:
- **15-hole circle** (Plate 1): $15/3 = 5$, move 10 holes
- **18-hole circle** (Plate 1): $18/3 = 6$, move 12 holes
- **21-hole circle** (Plate 2): $21/3 = 7$, move 14 holes

We can use **any one** of these. Let us select the **15-hole circle on Plate 1**.

**Step 4 — Holes to move (2 marks):**

On a 15-hole circle, the pin must move:

$$n = \frac{2}{3} \times 15 = 10 \text{ holes per indexing step}$$

**Final Answer:** Rotate the crank **6 full turns plus 10 holes** on a **15-hole circle (Plate 1)** between each of the **6 cuts**.

**Verification:** Spindle rotation per step $= (6 + 10/15)/40 = 6.6667/40 = 1/6$ revolution $= 60°$ ✓ (Hexagon has $60°$ per side.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the construction and working of a universal dividing head with a neat block diagram. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Construction (4 marks):**
A universal dividing head consists of:
1. **Spindle** — a horizontal shaft that holds the workpiece via a chuck or between centers.
2. **Worm and worm wheel** — single-start worm meshing with a 40-tooth wheel, providing the 40:1 reduction.
3. **Index crank** — a handle attached to the worm shaft, turned by the operator.
4. **Index plate** — three circular plates with multiple concentric circles of equally spaced holes.
5. **Index pin and sector arms** — a spring-loaded pin that engages the holes; sector arms allow pre-setting the number of holes to be moved.
6. **Footstock** — supports the free end of long workpieces on the tailstock side.

**Working (3 marks):**
- The workpiece is mounted on the spindle.
- The operator rotates the index crank by a calculated number of turns.
- The worm gear reduces this 40-fold to rotate the spindle by a precise angular amount.
- The index pin locks into the appropriate hole on the index plate, ensuring accuracy.
- After each cut, the process is repeated until all $x$ divisions are completed.

---

**(b) A milling operation requires indexing for 28 divisions. Determine the crank turns, plate, hole circle, and number of holes to move. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Divisions (1 mark):** $x = 28$

**Step 2 — Crank turns (2 marks):**

$$N = \frac{40}{28} = \frac{10}{7} = 1 \frac{3}{7} \text{ turns}$$

**Step 3 — Plate selection (2 marks):**

The denominator is **7**. No plate has a direct 7-hole circle, so we use a **multiple of 7**:
- **21-hole circle** (Plate 2): $21/7 = 3$, move $3/7 \times 21 = 9$ holes ✓
- **49-hole circle** (Plate 3): $49/7 = 7$, move $3/7 \times 49 = 21$ holes ✓

We select the **21-hole circle on Plate 2**.

**Step 4 — Holes to move (2 marks):**

$$n = \frac{3}{7} \times 21 = 9 \text{ holes per indexing step}$$

**Final Answer:** Rotate the crank **1 full turn plus 9 holes** on a **21-hole circle (Plate 2)** between each of the **28 cuts**.

**Verification:** Spindle rotation per step $= (1 + 9/21)/40 = 1.4286/40 = 1/28$ revolution $= 12.857°$ ✓ ($360°/28 = 12.857°$).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Skipping the derivation** in 7-mark sub-questions: When asked to "derive," you **must** start from the worm ratio and reach $N = 40/x$ algebraically. Just stating the formula earns only 1-2 marks.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to specify the plate and hole circle:** A common 1-2 mark deduction. You must say: *"Use the 21-hole circle on Plate 2."*
> 
> 3. **Unit errors in verification:** Always verify by computing $N/x_{divisor} = 1/x$ (in terms of spindle revolutions).
> 
> 4. **Using a non-existent hole circle:** Students sometimes write "use the 7-hole circle" — this does not exist on standard plates. Always use a **multiple** of the denominator.
> 
> 5. **Not stating the assumption of negligible backlash:** In high-mark questions, mention this explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3**

- **Core Equation:** $\boxed{N = \dfrac{40}{x}}$ — memorize verbatim, with units (turns).
- **Worm Ratio:** 40:1 — means 40 crank turns = 1 spindle turn.
- **Spindle rotation per crank turn:** Exactly **9°** ($360°/40$).
- **Angle per division:** $\theta = 360°/x$ degrees.
- **Standard Plates:** 3 plates, hole circles listed in Section 1.4.
- **Plate Rule:** The denominator of the fractional part of $N$ must be a **factor** of an available hole circle.
- **Whole Turns Plus Fraction:** Always decompose $N$ into $a + b/c$ form before plate selection.
- **Verification Trick:** After computing, check that $(N/40) = (1/x)$, i.e., the spindle rotates by exactly one $x$-th of a revolution.
- **Applications:** Gear teeth, splines, hex/square heads, bolt circles, keyways, cam lobes.
- **Limitation:** Simple indexing works only when $40/x$ yields a fraction whose denominator is a **factor** of an available hole circle. For prime-number divisions (e.g., 47, 53, 127), **differential indexing** is required.
- **Difference from Differential/Compound:** Simple indexing uses **one crank rotation** only; differential uses change gears; compound uses two index movements per step.
- **Industrial Relevance:** The 40:1 ratio is **historically standard** (Brown & Sharpe design); some European dividing heads use 60:1 or 80:1, in which case the equation becomes $N = 60/x$ or $N = 80/x$ respectively.
- **Modern Equivalent:** CNC rotary tables perform indexing electronically, but the **same equation $N = R/x$** applies, where $R$ is the machine's gear reduction ratio.
- **Examiner's Pet Topics:** Derivation of $N = 40/x$, plate selection for 6/7/35 divisions, and the **comparison table** between simple/differential/compound indexing.

---

**End of Notes — Simple Indexing | KTU 2024 Scheme | PBMRT404 Module 3**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drilling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT404 - MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Drilling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition
**Drilling** is a material removal machining process that employs a multi-point cutting tool (typically a **twist drill**) to produce a cylindrical hole of definite diameter and depth in a solid workpiece. The process is performed on a **drilling machine (drill press)**, where the rotating drill bit is fed linearly into the stationary (or slowly rotating) workpiece along its axis.

Mathematically, drilling can be defined as an internal turning operation where a single-point or multi-point tool traces a **helical path** through the material, generating a chip with each revolution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Drilling is grouped with Boring, Reaming, Tapping, Counterboring, and Countersinking. Students must distinguish between *primary* hole-making (drilling) and *secondary* hole-finishing (boring, reaming).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine using a **corkscrew** to pierce the cork of a wine bottle. The corkscrew rotates on its own axis (like the drill) while you push it downward (like the feed motion). The threads of the corkscrew cut and lift out the cork as chips — this is exactly how a twist drill works. The **flutes** of the drill act like the spiral threads, evacuating the chips as they are cut.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics at a Glance:**
> - **N** = Spindle Speed (rev/min) — how fast the drill rotates
> - **f** = Feed per Revolution (mm/rev) — how deep the drill advances per rotation
> - **D** = Drill Diameter (mm) — size of the hole produced
> - **MRR** = Material Removal Rate (mm³/min) — volume of metal cut per minute

### The Drilling Process — At a Glance
A drilling operation involves **two simultaneous motions**:
1. **Primary Motion (Cutting Motion):** Rotation of the drill bit about its own axis.
2. **Secondary Motion (Feed Motion):** Linear advancement of the drill along the axis of rotation into the workpiece.

Together, these two motions produce a hole of diameter equal to the drill diameter.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Twist Drill — Nomenclature & Geometry

The **twist drill** is the most common drilling tool. Understanding its geometry is critical for KTU exams, as questions frequently test students on angles and parts.

| Part of Drill | Function |
|---|---|
| **Point (Dead Center)** | The sharp tip that initiates the hole. |
| **Chisel Edge** | The straight edge connecting the two cutting lips; performs ~50% of thrust force. |
| **Cutting Lips (Edges)** | The two sharp edges that perform the actual cutting. |
| **Flutes** | Helical grooves that evacuate chips and allow coolant flow. |
| **Margin (Land)** | Narrow cylindrical strip that guides the drill in the hole. |
| **Body** | The cylindrical portion with helical flutes. |
| **Shank** | The holding portion (straight, tapered, or special). |
| **Neck** | Relief area for grinding the flutes. |

### 2.2 Key Drill Angles

| Angle | Typical Value (for Mild Steel) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Point Angle (2φ)** | **118°** (general purpose); 135° (hard materials); 90° (soft, non-metallic) | Determines lip geometry; affects thrust and chip formation. |
| **Helix Angle (α)** | **30°** (general); 40° (soft materials); 10°–20° (hard materials) | Influences chip evacuation and cutting efficiency. |
| **Rake Angle (γ)** | **−10° to −15°** (for steel) | Affects chip flow and cutting force. |
| **Clearance Angle (α_c)** | **12° to 15°** (on secondary flank) | Prevents rubbing between drill flank and workpiece. |
| **Chisel Edge Angle** | ~55° | Reduces thrust force at the chisel edge. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** "**P**oint angle 118, **H**elix 30, **R**ake negative, **C**learance 12–15" — for mild steel.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula No. | Equation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N = \dfrac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \times D}$ | Spindle speed (rev/min); $V_c$ in m/min, $D$ in mm |
| 2 | $f_r = f \times N$ | Feed rate (mm/min); $f$ in mm/rev |
| 3 | $MRR = \dfrac{\pi \times D^2}{4} \times f_r$ | Material removal rate (mm³/min) |
| 4 | $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60{,}000}$ | Power (kW); $T$ in N·m |
| 5 | $F = K_s \times A$ | Thrust force; $K_s$ in N/mm², $A$ in mm² |
| 6 | $A = \dfrac{\pi \times D^2}{4}$ | Cross-sectional area of hole (mm²) |
| 7 | $T_d = \dfrac{T}{N}$ | Drilling time (min); $L$ = depth of hole |
| 8 | $L_{total} = L + 0.3 D$ | Actual drill travel (approximate pilot point allowance) |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Unit Consistency Rule:** Always keep $D$ in **mm** and $V_c$ in **m/min** in the spindle speed formula. The factor **1000** converts meters to millimeters.

### 2.4 Types of Drilling Machines

| Type | Application |
|---|---|
| **Bench Drill Press** | Small, light work; up to ~12 mm capacity |
| **Floor (Upright) Drill Press** | General purpose workshop; up to ~50 mm capacity |
| **Radial Drill Press** | Heavy, large workpieces; drill head can be moved radially |
| **Gang Drill** | Multiple spindles for mass production |
| **Multi-Spindle Drill** | Several holes drilled simultaneously |
| **Deep Hole Drilling Machine** | Holes with $L/D > 10$ (e.g., gun barrels) |
| **CNC Drilling/Machining Center** | High precision, automation, mass production |

### 2.5 Operations Performed on a Drill Press
1. **Drilling** — producing a new hole.
2. **Reaming** — finishing an existing hole to size and finish.
3. **Boring** — enlarging an existing hole on a lathe or boring machine.
4. **Tapping** — cutting internal threads.
5. **Counterboring** — enlarging the top of a hole to accommodate a socket-head screw.
6. **Countersinking** — making a conical recess for a flat-head screw.
7. **Spot Facing** — producing a flat seat for a bolt head.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Spindle Speed (N)

The **cutting velocity** $V_c$ is the linear velocity at which the cutting edge of the drill contacts the workpiece at the outer diameter.

**Step 1:** The drill of diameter $D$ completes one revolution. The distance traveled by a point on the periphery is equal to the circumference.

$$\text{Distance per revolution} = \pi \times D \quad \text{(in mm, if D is in mm)}$$

**Step 2:** If the drill rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute, the total linear distance traveled per minute is:

$$\text{Distance per minute} = \pi \times D \times N \quad \text{(in mm/min)}$$

**Step 3:** This distance per minute must equal the cutting speed $V_c$. However, $V_c$ is conventionally expressed in **meters per minute**. So we convert mm to m by dividing by **1000**.

$$V_c \text{ (m/min)} = \dfrac{\pi \times D \text{ (mm)} \times N}{1000}$$

**Step 4:** Solving for $N$:

$$\boxed{N = \dfrac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \times D}} \quad \text{(rev/min)}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Material Removal Rate (MRR) in Drilling

**Step 1:** The volume of metal removed per minute is the cross-sectional area of the hole multiplied by the linear feed rate.

$$MRR = A \times f_r$$

**Step 2:** The cross-sectional area of a hole of diameter $D$ is:

$$A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \quad \text{(mm}^2\text{)}$$

**Step 3:** The feed rate $f_r$ (mm/min) is the feed per revolution multiplied by the spindle speed:

$$f_r = f \times N \quad \text{(mm/min)}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting both into the MRR equation:

$$\boxed{MRR = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \times f \times N} \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{/min)}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Drilling Time

**Step 1:** Drilling time is the total time taken for the drill to advance the required depth.

$$T_d = \dfrac{\text{Total axial travel of drill}}{\text{Feed rate}}$$

**Step 2:** The total axial travel $L_{total}$ includes the actual hole depth $L$ plus a small allowance for the drill point (approximately **0.3 D** for a standard 118° point).

$$L_{total} = L + 0.3 D$$

**Step 3:** Substituting:

$$\boxed{T_d = \dfrac{L + 0.3 D}{f \times N}} \quad \text{(minutes)}$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A **20 mm diameter** hole is to be drilled in a mild steel workpiece. The cutting speed is **25 m/min** and the feed is **0.20 mm/rev**. Calculate: (a) Spindle speed, (b) Feed rate, (c) MRR, and (d) Drilling time for a **40 mm deep** hole.

**Given:**
- $D = 20$ mm
- $V_c = 25$ m/min
- $f = 0.20$ mm/rev
- $L = 40$ mm

**Solution (a): Spindle Speed**

$$N = \dfrac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \times D} = \dfrac{25 \times 1000}{\pi \times 20}$$

$$N = \dfrac{25{,}000}{62.832} = 397.89 \text{ rev/min}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 398 \text{ rev/min}}$$

**Solution (b): Feed Rate**

$$f_r = f \times N = 0.20 \times 397.89 = 79.58 \text{ mm/min}$$

$$\boxed{f_r \approx 79.6 \text{ mm/min}}$$

**Solution (c): Material Removal Rate**

$$MRR = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \times f_r = \dfrac{\pi \times 20^2}{4} \times 79.58$$

$$MRR = 314.16 \times 79.58 = 25{,}000.5 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$$

$$\boxed{MRR \approx 25{,}000 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}}$$

**Solution (d): Drilling Time**

$$L_{total} = L + 0.3 D = 40 + 0.3 \times 20 = 46 \text{ mm}$$

$$T_d = \dfrac{L_{total}}{f_r} = \dfrac{46}{79.58} = 0.578 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{T_d \approx 0.58 \text{ min} \approx 34.7 \text{ seconds}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Notice the beautiful coincidence — when $V_c$ and $D$ are given in standard units, the MRR numerically often equals $V_c \times A$. This is because $V_c = \pi D N$, and MRR is essentially $V_c$ in mm/min multiplied by the area divided by a factor. Always cross-check your answers for dimensional consistency.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Each Drill Point Angle

| Workpiece Material | Recommended Point Angle | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel, Cast Iron | **118°** | General purpose; balances thrust and cutting efficiency. |
| Hard Steel, Stainless Steel | **135°** (split point) | Better centering, reduced thrust, longer tool life. |
| Aluminum, Soft Alloys | **90° to 118°** | Sharper edge for clean cutting. |
| Plastics, Wood | **60° to 90°** | Very sharp; prevents grabbing. |
| Cast Iron (chipping) | **118° to 125°** | Wider lip for strength. |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Twist Drill Nomenclature (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[TWIST DRILL - COMPONENT ARCHITECTURE]
    A --> B[CUTTING END]
    A --> C[BODY]
    A --> D[SHANK END]

    B --> B1[Point - Dead Center]
    B --> B2[Chisel Edge - 50 percent thrust]
    B --> B3[Cutting Lip Left]
    B --> B4[Cutting Lip Right]
    B --> B5[Flank Face]

    C --> C1[Flutes - Helical Grooves]
    C --> C2[Margin - Land - Guide]
    C --> C3[Body Diameter]

    D --> D1[Straight Shank]
    D --> D2[Tapered Shank]
    D --> D3[Tang]
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow: The Drilling Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Workpiece Setup on Drill Press Table] --> P2[Clamping in Drill Vise]
    P2 --> P3[Drill Bit Mounted in Chuck]
    P3 --> P4[Center Punch Mark]
    P4 --> P5[Start Spindle - Primary Motion]
    P5 --> P6[Engage Feed - Secondary Motion]
    P6 --> P7{Chip Evacuation OK?}
    P7 -- No --> P8[Apply Cutting Fluid / Peck Drill]
    P8 --> P6
    P7 -- Yes --> P9[Continue to Required Depth]
    P9 --> P10[Retract Drill]
    P10 --> P11[Stop Spindle]
    P11 --> P12[Inspect Hole - Diameter and Depth]
```

### 4.3 Drill Angle Geometry (Schematic Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    G[DRILL GEOMETRY ANGLES]
    G --> G1[Point Angle 2 phi = 118 deg standard]
    G --> G2[Helix Angle alpha = 30 deg standard]
    G --> G3[Rake Angle gamma = negative 10 to 15 deg for steel]
    G --> G4[Clearance Angle alpha_c = 12 to 15 deg]

    G1 --> G1A[Lip Relief Angle]
    G2 --> G2A[Flute Geometry]
    G3 --> G3A[Chip Flow Direction]
    G4 --> G4A[Flank Clearance]
```

### 4.4 Drilling Machine Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DM[DRILLING MACHINE - BLOCK ARCHITECTURE]
    DM --> M1[Base - Foundation]
    DM --> M2[Column - Vertical Support]
    DM --> M3[Table - Workpiece Holder]
    DM --> M4[Head Assembly]
    DM --> M5[Feed Mechanism]

    M4 --> M4A[Spindle - Rotates Drill]
    M4 --> M4B[Quill - Holds Spindle]
    M4 --> M4C[Chuck - Holds Drill Bit]

    M5 --> M5A[Manual Feed Lever]
    M5 --> M5B[Power Feed - Automatic]
    M5 --> M5C[Depth Stop]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**
**Define drilling. With a neat sketch, name any six parts of a twist drill.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Drilling is a machining process used to produce a cylindrical hole in a solid workpiece by rotating a multi-point cutting tool (twist drill) and feeding it linearly along its axis.

**Six parts of a twist drill (1/2 mark each):**
1. **Point** — sharp tip for initiation.
2. **Chisel edge** — straight edge connecting cutting lips.
3. **Cutting lips** — perform the actual cutting.
4. **Flutes** — helical grooves for chip evacuation.
5. **Margin (land)** — guides the drill in the hole.
6. **Shank** — held in the drill chuck.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**
**What is the standard point angle of a twist drill for general-purpose mild steel work? Why is the helix angle important?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The standard point angle of a twist drill for mild steel is **118°** (1 mark).

The **helix angle** is important because (1 mark each):
- It facilitates **chip evacuation** from the hole.
- It influences the **rake angle** and hence the cutting force and chip flow.
- Typical value is **30°** for general-purpose work in steel (lower for hard materials, higher for soft materials).

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Q3. Question A [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**
**(a)** Explain the various drill geometry angles with the help of a neat diagram. Discuss how the point angle is selected for different workpiece materials. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **15 mm diameter** hole is to be drilled in a cast iron workpiece at a cutting speed of **20 m/min** with a feed of **0.15 mm/rev**. Calculate the spindle speed, feed rate, MRR, and drilling time for a **30 mm deep** hole. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Drill Geometry Angles (4 marks):**

| Angle | Value | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Point Angle (2φ) | 118° standard | Determines cutting lip geometry |
| Helix Angle (α) | 30° standard | Influences chip flow and rake |
| Rake Angle (γ) | −10° to −15° for steel | Affects cutting force |
| Clearance Angle (α_c) | 12° to 15° | Prevents rubbing |
| Lip Relief Angle | 5° to 7° | Relieves cutting lip behind edge |
| Chisel Edge Angle | ~55° | Reduces thrust at chisel edge |

**Selection of Point Angle for Different Materials (3 marks):**

| Material | Point Angle | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel, Cast Iron | 118° | General purpose, balanced |
| Hard Steel, Stainless Steel | 135° | Better centering, longer life |
| Soft Materials (Al, Brass) | 90°–118° | Sharper cutting |
| Plastics, Wood | 60°–90° | Prevents grabbing |

**Sketch** (1 mark reserved for the diagram showing the angles marked on the drill point).

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- $D = 15$ mm
- $V_c = 20$ m/min
- $f = 0.15$ mm/rev
- $L = 30$ mm

**Step 1: Spindle Speed (2 marks)**

$$N = \dfrac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \times D} = \dfrac{20 \times 1000}{\pi \times 15} = \dfrac{20{,}000}{47.124}$$

$$\boxed{N = 424.4 \text{ rev/min}}$$

**Step 2: Feed Rate (1 mark)**

$$f_r = f \times N = 0.15 \times 424.4 = 63.66 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Step 3: Material Removal Rate (2 marks)**

$$MRR = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \times f_r = \dfrac{\pi \times 15^2}{4} \times 63.66$$

$$MRR = 176.71 \times 63.66 = 11{,}249.5 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$$

$$\boxed{MRR \approx 11{,}250 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}}$$

**Step 4: Drilling Time (2 marks)**

$$L_{total} = L + 0.3 D = 30 + 0.3 \times 15 = 34.5 \text{ mm}$$

$$T_d = \dfrac{L_{total}}{f_r} = \dfrac{34.5}{63.66} = 0.542 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{T_d \approx 0.54 \text{ min} \approx 32.5 \text{ seconds}}$$

---

**Q3. Question B (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
**(a)** Describe the construction and working of a **radial drill press** with a neat sketch. Mention its advantages over an upright drill press. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hole of **25 mm diameter** and **50 mm depth** is drilled in an aluminum workpiece. The cutting speed is **60 m/min** and feed is **0.25 mm/rev**. Determine: (i) Spindle speed, (ii) MRR, (iii) Power required if the specific cutting force is **800 N/mm²**, and (iv) Drilling time. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Radial Drill Press — Construction (3 marks):**
- **Base:** Heavy foundation mounted on the floor.
- **Column:** Vertical pillar supporting the radial arm.
- **Radial Arm:** Horizontal arm that can rotate about the column and be raised/lowered. Carries the drill head.
- **Drill Head:** Mounted on the radial arm; can slide along the arm to position over the workpiece. Contains spindle, motor, and feed mechanism.
- **Spindle and Quill:** Holds the drill; provides rotation and feed.
- **Table:** May be circular, mounted on the column base.

**Working (2 marks):**
- The radial arm is set at the desired height and rotated to align the drill head with the workpiece location.
- The drill head is slid along the arm to position the drill exactly over the hole location.
- The spindle rotates the drill, and the quill feeds the drill downward to produce the hole.

**Advantages over Upright Drill Press (2 marks):**
1. Suitable for **heavy and large workpieces** which cannot be conveniently moved.
2. Can drill **multiple holes** at various locations on a single setup by simply rotating/swiveling the arm — no need to reposition the workpiece.
3. Greater **flexibility** in positioning the drill.
4. Higher **rigidity** for heavy-duty drilling.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- $D = 25$ mm
- $L = 50$ mm
- $V_c = 60$ m/min
- $f = 0.25$ mm/rev
- $K_s = 800$ N/mm² (specific cutting force for aluminum)

**Step 1: Spindle Speed (1 mark)**

$$N = \dfrac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \times D} = \dfrac{60 \times 1000}{\pi \times 25} = \dfrac{60{,}000}{78.54} = 763.94 \text{ rev/min}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 764 \text{ rev/min}}$$

**Step 2: Feed Rate (0.5 mark)**

$$f_r = f \times N = 0.25 \times 763.94 = 190.98 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Step 3: Material Removal Rate (1 mark)**

$$MRR = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \times f_r = \dfrac{\pi \times 25^2}{4} \times 190.98$$

$$MRR = 490.87 \times 190.98 = 93{,}743 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$$

$$\boxed{MRR \approx 93{,}743 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}}$$

**Step 4: Power Required (3 marks)**

First, calculate the cross-sectional area:

$$A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 25^2}{4} = 490.87 \text{ mm}^2$$

Cutting force (assuming rough approximation for drilling):

$$F_c = K_s \times A = 800 \times 490.87 = 392{,}699 \text{ N}$$

(For drilling, typically only a fraction acts as tangential force; using simplified approach:)

Cutting velocity:

$$V_c = \pi D N = \pi \times 25 \times 763.94 = 60{,}000 \text{ mm/min} = 60 \text{ m/min} = 1 \text{ m/s}$$

Power:

$$P = F_c \times V_c = 392{,}699 \times 1 = 392{,}699 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 392.7 \text{ kW (theoretical, simplified)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, drilling power is calculated using torque $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60{,}000}$, and the force is split between thrust and tangential components. The simplified formula here is for academic purposes; in real analysis, multiply by a tool efficiency factor (0.7–0.85).

**Step 5: Drilling Time (1.5 marks)**

$$L_{total} = L + 0.3 D = 50 + 0.3 \times 25 = 57.5 \text{ mm}$$

$$T_d = \dfrac{L_{total}}{f_r} = \dfrac{57.5}{190.98} = 0.301 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{T_d \approx 0.30 \text{ min} \approx 18.1 \text{ seconds}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (–1 to –2 marks):** Students frequently forget to multiply $V_c$ by 1000 in the spindle speed formula. Always write the formula before substitution.
> 2. **Forgetting Pilot Point Allowance (–1 mark):** Drilling time must use $L_{total} = L + 0.3D$, not just $L$.
> 3. **Confusing Rake and Clearance Angles (–1 mark):** Rake is on the face (negative for steel); clearance is on the flank (positive 12°–15°).
> 4. **MRR vs Cutting Velocity confusion:** MRR is a volume rate (mm³/min), not a linear rate.
> 5. **Point Angle confusion:** 118° is for general-purpose steel; 135° is NOT for soft materials — it is for HARD materials.
