> 6. **Power units:** Final power must be expressed in kW. Convert carefully.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Drilling** is a hole-making process using a rotating multi-point tool with linear feed.
- **Twist drill** is the standard tool; key parts include point, chisel edge, cutting lips, flutes, margin, and shank.
- **Point Angle:** 118° (steel), 135° (hard steel), 90° (soft materials), 60°–90° (plastics).
- **Helix Angle:** 30° (general), higher for soft, lower for hard materials.
- **Rake Angle:** Negative for steel (−10° to −15°).
- **Clearance Angle:** 12° to 15° on the secondary flank.
- **Spindle Speed Formula:** $N = \dfrac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \times D}$ (rev/min).
- **Feed Rate Formula:** $f_r = f \times N$ (mm/min).
- **MRR Formula:** $MRR = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \times f_r$ (mm³/min).
- **Drilling Time Formula:** $T_d = \dfrac{L + 0.3D}{f \times N}$ (minutes).
- **Power Formula:** $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60{,}000}$ (kW).
- **Drill Press Types:** Bench, Floor, Radial, Gang, Multi-spindle, Deep hole, CNC.
- **Operations on Drill Press:** Drilling, Reaming, Boring, Tapping, Counterboring, Countersinking, Spot Facing.
- **Chisel edge** generates ~50% of thrust force — point angle and chisel edge angle are critical for tool life.
- **Coolant** is essential in deep hole drilling to prevent chip welding and tool overheating.
- **Peck drilling** is used for deep holes ($L/D > 4$) to clear chips periodically.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Always draw a **labeled diagram** for full marks in long-answer questions. KTU examiners award **1 to 2 marks** specifically for neat sketches with proper labels. Memorize the standard angle values table — it appears in nearly every KTU paper.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Shaping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT404 - MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Shaping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shaping** is a material-removal machining process in which a **single-point cutting tool** removes a thin layer of metal from a **stationary workpiece** by means of a **linear reciprocating motion** of the tool, combined with a slow **intermittent cross-feed** of the workpiece (or tool) at the end of each stroke. The cutting action occurs only during the **forward (cutting) stroke**, while the return (idle) stroke is unproductive.

The shaping machine is classified under the category of **reciprocating-type machine tools** (along with planing and slotting) and is primarily used for producing **flat surfaces, grooves, keyways, slots, and contours** on small to medium-sized workpieces.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Hand-Plane of Carpentry**
> Imagine a carpenter using a hand plane on a wooden board. The carpenter pushes the plane forward (cutting stroke — wood shavings are removed), then pulls it back (return stroke — no cutting, just repositioning). After each forward push, the carpenter shifts the board slightly sideways (cross-feed) to plane a new strip of wood.
> 
> The **mechanical shaper works on exactly the same principle** — a clamped tool slides linearly across a stationary workpiece, slicing off a chip, retracts, advances again, and the workpiece is indexed sideways by a small amount. Instead of a wooden board, we have a metal workpiece, and instead of muscle power, we have a **bull gear and crank mechanism** (Whitworth quick-return mechanism) driving the ram.

### 1.3 Key Metrics and Standard Specifications

The following standard metrics are used to fully define the capacity of a shaping machine:

| Parameter | Symbol / Unit | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Maximum length of stroke** | $L$ in **mm** | 200 – 900 mm |
| **Cutting speed** | $V_c$ in **m/min** | 8 – 25 m/min |
| **Feed rate** | $f$ in **mm/stroke** | 0.05 – 3 mm/stroke |
| **Cross-slide travel** | — | 200 – 600 mm |
| **Table travel (vertical)** | — | 250 – 700 mm |
| **Power of motor** | **kW** | 1.5 – 11 kW |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The two motion parameters that uniquely define a shaping operation are **(1) Reciprocating Primary Motion** (given to the tool/ram) and **(2) Secondary Intermittent Feed Motion** (given to the workpiece/table transversely). These are the only two motions in pure shaping.

### 1.4 Classification of Shapers (Based on Type of Drive & Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Classification:**
> 
> **A. Based on the type of mechanism used:**
> 1. **Crank-type (Crank-and-Slider) shaper** — Uses a simple crank and slotted lever.
> 2. **Geared-type shaper** — Uses a bull gear and pinion (Whitworth quick-return mechanism) — *most common in industry*.
> 3. **Hydraulic shaper** — Uses hydraulic cylinder for stroke control — *used for heavy-duty, variable-speed work*.
> 4. **Oscillating-link shaper** — Rare, used in specialised applications.
> 
> **B. Based on the type of table:**
> 1. **Plain shaper** — Table has only cross and vertical slides (most common).
> 2. **Universal shaper** — Table can be swiveled (rotated) and tilted; equipped with a circular table for machining curves and contours.
> 3. **Vertical shaper (Slotters)** — Tool moves vertically instead of horizontally (actually a separate machine category).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Working Principle

The shaping operation unfolds in a **discrete, cyclic sequence**. Each complete cycle of the ram constitutes one **double stroke** (one cutting + one return stroke). Let us break this down:

1. **Clamping:** The workpiece is rigidly clamped to the machine table using **T-bolts, clamps, and steps**.
2. **Tool Mounting:** The single-point cutting tool is held in a **tool post** mounted on the **ram** (or sometimes on a clapper box for horizontal return clearance).
3. **Cutting Stroke (Forward Stroke):** The **ram moves forward** in a straight-line, horizontal, reciprocating motion. The cutting tool engages the workpiece and removes a layer of material as a **chip**.
4. **Return Stroke (Idle Stroke):** The ram retracts back to its starting position. **No cutting occurs** during this stroke. The tool is slightly lifted (via the clapper box mechanism) to avoid dragging across the finished surface.
5. **Feed Motion:** At the end of the return stroke, the **worktable (with the workpiece)** is fed **perpendicular** to the cutting direction by a small increment $f$ (feed per stroke).
6. **Repetition:** Steps 3–5 are repeated. Successive strips of material are removed until the desired depth and width of cut are achieved.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Mechanism

- **Why is the return stroke faster than the cutting stroke?**
  Because cutting happens only in one direction, the return stroke is **dead time** that wastes productivity. To minimise this waste, the **Whitworth quick-return mechanism** makes the return stroke **30–50% faster** than the cutting stroke, increasing the **strokes per minute (SPM)**.

- **Why is the feed given to the workpiece and not the tool?**
  Mechanically, it is easier to index the **table (workpiece side)** using a ratchet-and-pawl or hydraulic mechanism, while keeping the tool-carrying ram in pure reciprocating motion. This also gives better rigidity during cutting.

- **Why is a clapper box used?**
  The clapper box is a **hinged tool holder** that lifts the tool automatically during the return stroke. This prevents the tool flank from rubbing against the freshly machined surface (which would spoil surface finish and wear the tool).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains every equation, definition, and unit you will need to solve any numerical problem on Shaping in a KTU 2024 examination.

| **#** | **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Explanation** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cutting Velocity** | $V_c = \dfrac{2 L N}{1000}$ | m/min | $L$ = stroke length (mm), $N$ = strokes per minute (SPM) |
| 2 | **Quick-Return Ratio (QRR)** | $QRR = \dfrac{T_r}{T_c} = \dfrac{\text{Return stroke time}}{\text{Cutting stroke time}}$ | dimensionless | Always $QRR < 1$; typically **0.6 – 0.75** |
| 3 | **Time for one Double Stroke** | $T_{ds} = T_c + T_r = \dfrac{60}{N}$ | seconds | Total cycle time per stroke |
| 4 | **Cutting Time per Stroke** | $T_c = \dfrac{60}{N(1 + QRR)}$ | seconds | Productive time |
| 5 | **Return Time per Stroke** | $T_r = QRR \cdot T_c$ | seconds | Idle time |
| 6 | **Feed per Stroke** | $f$ | mm/stroke | Lateral displacement of table |
| 7 | **Feed Rate (per minute)** | $f_m = f \cdot N$ | mm/min | Useful for time calculations |
| 8 | **Average Cutting Speed (Geared shaper)** | $V_c = \dfrac{2 L N}{1000}$ | m/min | Same formula as #1 (constant for uniform crank-driven motion) |
| 9 | **Total Number of Strokes Required** | $N_{tot} = \dfrac{W}{f}$ | strokes | $W$ = width of cut (mm) |
| 10 | **Total Machining Time** | $T_m = \dfrac{N_{tot}}{N} \cdot \dfrac{1}{60}$ hours, or $T_m = \dfrac{W}{f \cdot N}$ minutes | min / hr | Using $f_m$ for continuous-feed variant |
| 11 | **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** | $MRR = V_c \cdot f \cdot d$ | mm³/min | $d$ = depth of cut (mm) |
| 12 | **Power Consumed** | $P = \dfrac{MRR \cdot k_s}{60 \times 10^6}$ | kW | $k_s$ = specific cutting energy (N·m/mm³) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Tip — QRR vs. Time Ratio:**
> - $QRR$ is the **time ratio** ($T_r / T_c$), NOT the speed ratio.
> - Speed ratio (return speed / cutting speed) is $V_r / V_c = 1 / QRR$.
> - Always check whether the question asks for **time ratio** or **velocity ratio**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Shaping remains a **workhorse for low-volume, large-size flat workpieces** (e.g., slide-ways of machine tools, large gear blanks, keyways in shafts, and maintenance jobs in heavy engineering). Although CNC milling has overtaken shaping in mass production, the **simplicity, low cost, and ability to handle very long strokes** make shaping indispensable in:
- **Tool rooms** (one-off jobs and prototypes).
- **Maintenance workshops** of heavy industries (railways, shipyards, power plants).
- **Foundries** for machining large castings with rough surfaces (high rigidity of stationary workpiece helps).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Average Cutting Velocity ($V_c$)

The shaping process involves **simple harmonic motion** of the ram, driven by a crank or Whitworth mechanism. The velocity of the ram is therefore **not constant** during a stroke. However, for the purpose of computing power, time, and MRR, we use the **average cutting velocity** derived as follows.

**Given:** Stroke length $= L$ mm, Strokes per minute $= N$ (SPM), Quick-return ratio $= QRR$.

**Step 1 — Distance travelled by the tool in one complete double stroke:**
The tool travels a distance $L$ on the forward (cutting) stroke and a distance $L$ on the return stroke. Thus, the total distance per double stroke $= 2L$ mm.

**Step 2 — Time taken for one double stroke:**
A double stroke consists of one cutting stroke and one return stroke. The total time per double stroke is:

$$
T_{ds} = \dfrac{60}{N} \text{ seconds}
$$

This is because $N$ double strokes occur in 60 seconds.

**Step 3 — Average Cutting Velocity:**
Average velocity is total productive distance per unit productive time. Since cutting occurs **only** during the cutting stroke (distance $L$), and the total time per double stroke is $T_{ds}$, the average velocity over the entire cycle is:

$$
V_{avg} = \dfrac{L}{T_{ds}} = \dfrac{L \cdot N}{60} \text{ mm/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to m/min** (multiply numerator and denominator appropriately):

$$
V_c = \dfrac{L \cdot N}{60} \times \dfrac{60}{1000} = \dfrac{2 L N}{1000} \text{ m/min}
$$

> This result is consistent with the standard formula. **The factor of 2 arises** because the total distance per double stroke is $2L$, and the time is $60/N$ minutes, giving $V_{avg,total} = 2LN/1000$ m/min for the *total* cycle. For purely the cutting portion, $V_{cutting,avg} = 2LN/[1000(1+QRR)]$ m/min.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A shaping machine is used to machine a flat surface of width $W = 60$ mm. The stroke length is $L = 200$ mm, strokes per minute $N = 30$ SPM, feed per stroke $f = 0.5$ mm/stroke, and depth of cut $d = 2$ mm. Calculate: (a) the average cutting velocity, (b) the total number of strokes required, and (c) the total machining time.

**Given Data:**
- $W = 60$ mm, $L = 200$ mm, $N = 30$ SPM, $f = 0.5$ mm/stroke, $d = 2$ mm.

#### (a) Average Cutting Velocity

$$
V_c = \dfrac{2 L N}{1000} = \dfrac{2 \times 200 \times 30}{1000} = \dfrac{12000}{1000} = 12 \text{ m/min}
$$

The average cutting velocity is **$V_c = 12$ m/min**.

#### (b) Total Number of Strokes Required

The workpiece has a width of 60 mm to be covered, with each stroke feeding laterally by 0.5 mm. The number of cutting strokes required to cover the full width:

$$
N_{tot} = \dfrac{W}{f} = \dfrac{60}{0.5} = 120 \text{ strokes}
$$

The total number of strokes required is **$N_{tot} = 120$ strokes**.

#### (c) Total Machining Time

Each double stroke takes $60/N$ seconds. Therefore:

$$
T_m = \dfrac{N_{tot} \times 60}{N \times 60} \text{ minutes} = \dfrac{N_{tot}}{N} = \dfrac{120}{30} = 4 \text{ minutes}
$$

The total machining time is **$T_m = 4$ minutes**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Tool Paths

| **Stroke Type** | **Tool Motion** | **Cutting?** | **Duration** | **Velocity Direction** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cutting Stroke (Forward)** | Ram moves toward workpiece | **Yes — chip removed** | $T_c$ (longer) | Left → Right (assumed) |
| **Return Stroke (Idle)** | Ram moves back from workpiece | **No — clapper lifts tool** | $T_r$ (shorter) | Right → Left |
| **Feed** | Table moves transversely | **No** | Between strokes (instantaneous) | Perpendicular to ram motion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **feed motion** in a shaping machine occurs **between two consecutive strokes** (i.e., at the end of the return stroke), not simultaneously with cutting. This is a key distinction from milling, where feed is continuous.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Architecture of a Shaper

> [!NOTE]
> The following block-level functional architecture maps the kinematic and structural relationships of a major components of a shaping machine, compliant with Mermaid `flowchart` syntax.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PowerSource[Power Source - Electric Motor 1.5 to 11 kW]:::power
    Gearbox[Reduction Gearbox and Belt Drive]:::power
    BullGear[Bull Gear - Main Drive Wheel]:::mech
    CrankPin[Crank Pin - Adjustable Radius]:::mech
    SlottedLever[Slotted Lever - Quick Return Link]:::mech
    Ram[Ram - Carries Tool Post]:::output
    ToolPost[Tool Post with Clapper Box]:::output
    CuttingTool[Single Point Cutting Tool]:::output
    Workpiece[Workpiece - Clamped on Table]:::output
    CrossSlide[Cross Slide - Provides Feed Motion]:::control
    VerticalSlide[Vertical Slide - Elevates Table]:::control
    Table[Machine Table - Holds Workpiece]:::output
    FeedMech[Feed Mechanism - Ratchet and Pawl]:::control

    PowerSource --> Gearbox
    Gearbox --> BullGear
    BullGear --> CrankPin
    CrankPin --> SlottedLever
    SlottedLever --> Ram
    Ram --> ToolPost
    ToolPost --> CuttingTool
    CuttingTool -. cuts .-> Workpiece
    Workpiece --> Table
    Table --> CrossSlide
    CrossSlide --> VerticalSlide
    FeedMech --> CrossSlide

    classDef power fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#000
    classDef mech fill:#fff3bf,stroke:#e67700,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#d3f9d8,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#d0ebff,stroke:#1971c2,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Schematic Layout — Tool–Workpiece Interaction During a Cutting Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start([Start of Stroke]):::state
    Forward[Ram moves FORWARD - Cutting Stroke]:::cut
    Chip[Chip removed from Workpiece]:::cut
    EndCut([End of Cutting Stroke]):::state
    Retract[Clapper Lifts Tool - Ram RETURNS]:::idle
    EndRet([End of Return Stroke]):::state
    Feed[Table feeds transversely by f mm]:::feed
    Repeat{Width covered?}:::state
    Stop([End of Operation]):::state

    Start --> Forward
    Forward --> Chip
    Chip --> EndCut
    EndCut --> Retract
    Retract --> EndRet
    EndRet --> Feed
    Feed --> Repeat
    Repeat -- No --> Start
    Repeat -- Yes --> Stop

    classDef state fill:#e7e7ff,stroke:#5c5cff,color:#000
    classDef cut fill:#ffd8a8,stroke:#d9480f,color:#000
    classDef idle fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#868e96,color:#000
    classDef feed fill:#c5f6fa,stroke:#0c8599,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture of a Geared Shaper — Component Flow

| **S.No** | **Component** | **Function** | **Power/Motion Path** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Motor** | Source of rotary power | Electrical → Mechanical |
| 2 | **Belt & Pulley** | First-stage speed reduction | Rotary to Rotary |
| 3 | **Bull Gear + Pinion** | Second-stage reduction + storage of inertia | Rotary to Rotary |
| 4 | **Crank Pin (Eccentric)** | Converts rotary motion to oscillating motion | Rotary to Oscillating |
| 5 | **Slotted Lever** | Quick-return mechanism (Whitworth) | Oscillating to Linear |
| 6 | **Ram** | Carries tool, provides linear reciprocation | Linear reciprocating |
| 7 | **Clapper Box** | Lifts tool on return stroke | Hinged passive |
| 8 | **Feed Mechanism** | Ratchet-pawl indexing at end of return stroke | Intermittent linear |
| 9 | **Cross & Vertical Slide** | Position the workpiece in 3D | Manual or powered |
| 10 | **Table** | Holds the workpiece rigidly | Static during cut |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Define shaping. List any two operations that can be performed on a shaper.**

**Model Answer:**
Shaping is a machining process in which a single-point cutting tool, mounted on a reciprocating ram, removes material from a stationary workpiece in the form of discrete chips. The cutting action takes place only during the forward (cutting) stroke, and the workpiece is given a small intermittent transverse feed after each return stroke.

**Two operations performable on a shaper:**
1. Machining of **flat horizontal, vertical, and inclined surfaces**.
2. Cutting of **internal keyways, slots, and grooves** using a narrow tool.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **What is quick-return ratio? Why is it used in shaping?**

**Model Answer:**
Quick-return ratio ($QRR$) is defined as the ratio of the time taken for the return (idle) stroke to the time taken for the cutting (forward) stroke:

$$
QRR = \dfrac{T_r}{T_c} \quad \text{where} \quad QRR < 1
$$

**Why it is used:** Since cutting occurs only during one direction of ram travel, the return stroke is unproductive dead time. By making the return stroke **faster** (i.e., $QRR < 1$), the overall cycle time is reduced, thereby increasing productivity. This is achieved using the **Whitworth quick-return mechanism** in geared shapers, which typically gives $QRR \approx 0.6$ to $0.75$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Question A (14 Marks):**
**(a)** Describe the working principle of a shaping machine with a neat block diagram. Explain the function of the **clapper box** and **quick-return mechanism**. (7 Marks)
**(b)** A shaping machine has a stroke length of 250 mm and operates at 25 strokes per minute with a quick-return ratio of 0.6. Calculate: (i) cutting velocity, (ii) time per cutting stroke, (iii) time per return stroke, and (iv) total machining time for a job requiring 80 cutting strokes. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for Q3A

**Part (a) — Working Principle:**
The shaping machine consists of an electric motor that drives a bull gear via a belt-pulley reduction. The bull gear carries an adjustable crank pin, the rotation of which is converted by a **slotted lever (Whitworth mechanism)** into linear reciprocating motion of the **ram**. The single-point cutting tool, held in a clapper box on the ram, makes a cutting pass over the stationary workpiece during the forward stroke, then retracts during the return stroke. The workpiece is mounted on a table that can move transversely (cross-slide) and vertically, and is fed laterally by a small increment (feed per stroke) after each return stroke.

**Function of the Clapper Box:** The clapper box is a **hinged tool holder** that allows the tool to pivot upward during the return stroke. This prevents the **clearance face of the tool from rubbing** against the freshly machined workpiece surface, which would otherwise spoil surface finish and accelerate tool wear.

**Function of the Quick-Return Mechanism:** The Whitworth quick-return mechanism ensures that the **return stroke is completed in less time** than the cutting stroke. This increases the **strokes per minute (SPM)** for a given average cutting speed, boosting productivity without raising the cutting velocity.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $L = 250$ mm, $N = 25$ SPM, $QRR = T_r / T_c = 0.6$, $N_{tot} = 80$ strokes.

**(i) Cutting Velocity:**

$$
V_c = \dfrac{2 L N}{1000} = \dfrac{2 \times 250 \times 25}{1000} = \dfrac{12500}{1000} = 12.5 \text{ m/min}
$$

**(ii) Time per Double Stroke:**

$$
T_{ds} = \dfrac{60}{N} = \dfrac{60}{25} = 2.4 \text{ s}
$$

**Time per Cutting Stroke (using $T_{ds} = T_c + T_r = T_c(1 + QRR)$):**

$$
T_c = \dfrac{T_{ds}}{1 + QRR} = \dfrac{2.4}{1 + 0.6} = \dfrac{2.4}{1.6} = 1.5 \text{ s}
$$

**(iii) Time per Return Stroke:**

$$
T_r = QRR \cdot T_c = 0.6 \times 1.5 = 0.9 \text{ s}
$$

**Verification:** $T_c + T_r = 1.5 + 0.9 = 2.4$ s ✓

**(iv) Total Machining Time for 80 Cutting Strokes:**

$$
T_m = N_{tot} \times T_{ds} = 80 \times 2.4 = 192 \text{ s} = 3 \text{ min } 12 \text{ s}
$$

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**
**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the constructional features and working of a **geared-type shaping machine**. List any **four differences between shaping and planing**. (7 Marks)
**(b)** During shaping of a mild-steel workpiece, the following data is given: stroke length = 200 mm, SPM = 30, feed = 0.6 mm/stroke, depth of cut = 2.5 mm, specific cutting energy $k_s = 2.0$ N·m/mm³. Calculate: (i) MRR, (ii) Power consumed in kW, and (iii) total time to machine a width of 90 mm. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for Q3B

**Part (a) — Geared Shaper Construction & Working:**
A geared shaper consists of:
- A **headstock** housing the motor, belt drive, and bull-gear-crank mechanism.
- A **Whiworth quick-return mechanism** (slotted lever) that converts the rotary motion of the bull gear into the linear reciprocating motion of the ram.
- A **ram** carrying the tool post, which slides on accurately machined guideways.
- A **clapper box** that lifts the tool on the return stroke.
- A **cross-slide and vertical slide** carrying the worktable.
- A **feed mechanism** (ratchet-and-pawl) that indexes the table transversely at the end of each return stroke.

**Four differences between Shaping and Planing:**

| **#** | **Shaping** | **Planing** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tool reciprocates; workpiece is stationary. | Workpiece reciprocates; tool is stationary. |
| 2 | Suitable for **small to medium** workpieces. | Suitable for **large and heavy** workpieces. |
| 3 | Stroke length is **limited** (max ~900 mm). | Stroke length can be **several meters**. |
| 4 | Quick-return ratio achievable **0.6 – 0.75**. | QRR typically **0.7 – 0.8** (slower return). |

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $L = 200$ mm, $N = 30$ SPM, $f = 0.6$ mm/stroke, $d = 2.5$ mm, $k_s = 2.0$ N·m/mm³, $W = 90$ mm.

**(i) Material Removal Rate (MRR):**

$$
MRR = V_c \times f \times d
$$

First, compute $V_c$:

$$
V_c = \dfrac{2 L N}{1000} = \dfrac{2 \times 200 \times 30}{1000} = 12 \text{ m/min} = 12 \times 1000 \text{ mm/min} = 12000 \text{ mm/min}
$$

Then:

$$
MRR = 12000 \times 0.6 \times 2.5 = 18000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}
$$

**(ii) Power Consumed:**

$$
P = \dfrac{MRR \times k_s}{60 \times 10^6} = \dfrac{18000 \times 2.0}{60 \times 10^6} = \dfrac{36000}{6 \times 10^7} = 6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kW} = 0.6 \text{ kW}
$$

**(iii) Total Machining Time:**

Number of strokes required:

$$
N_{tot} = \dfrac{W}{f} = \dfrac{90}{0.6} = 150 \text{ strokes}
$$

Total time:

$$
T_m = \dfrac{N_{tot}}{N} = \dfrac{150}{30} = 5 \text{ minutes}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Mixing up QRR definition:** The most common error is using $QRR = T_c / T_r$ (cutting-to-return) instead of $QRR = T_r / T_c$. KTU strictly follows the *time ratio* convention. Always remember: **return time / cutting time**.
> 
> 2. **Unit mismatch in $V_c$:** Forgetting to convert mm/s to m/min (factor of 60/1000) leads to silent unit errors. Show the unit conversion step explicitly.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $V_c$ formula:** Writing $V_c = LN/1000$ instead of $2LN/1000$ is a frequent slip. The factor of 2 accounts for the **double travel** per cycle (forward + return) when using $N$ as SPM.
> 
> 4. **Not verifying QRR:** After computing $T_c$ and $T_r$, always verify $T_c + T_r = 60/N$ to catch arithmetic mistakes.
> 
> 5. **Confusing shaping with planing:** Examiners often test whether students know that in **shaping the tool moves** and the workpiece is stationary, whereas in **planing the workpiece moves**. Mixing these up costs full marks in descriptive answers.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Shaping (Module 3)**
> 
> - **Shaping** is a **reciprocating-type, single-point** machining process with **stationary workpiece** and **moving tool**.
> - **Two motions:** Primary reciprocating motion (ram) + Secondary intermittent feed (table).
> - **Cutting occurs only on the forward stroke**; return stroke is **idle and faster**.
> - **Quick-Return Ratio (QRR)** = $T_r / T_c < 1$, typically **0.6 – 0.75**, achieved via the **Whitworth mechanism**.
> - **Key Formula:** $V_c = \dfrac{2 L N}{1000}$ m/min — apply this in **every numerical**.
> - **Clapper box** prevents tool flank rubbing during the return stroke — protects surface finish.
> - **Feed** is given to the **workpiece (table)**, never to the ram; feed is **intermittent** (between strokes), not continuous.
> - **Standard specifications:** $L = 200$–$900$ mm, $N = 10$–$80$ SPM, $V_c = 8$–$25$ m/min, $P = 1.5$–$11$ kW.
> - **Types of shapers:** Plain, Universal (swivelling table), Vertical (slotter).
> - **Types of drives:** Crank-and-slider, Geared (Whitworth — most common), Hydraulic (heavy duty), Oscillating-link (rare).
> - **Six main operations:** horizontal/vertical/inclined surfaces, slots, keyways, contours, parting-off, irregular surfaces.
> - **Time per cutting stroke:** $T_c = \dfrac{60}{N(1 + QRR)}$ seconds.
> - **Time per return stroke:** $T_r = \dfrac{60 \cdot QRR}{N(1 + QRR)}$ seconds.
> - **Total strokes required:** $N_{tot} = W / f$.
> - **Total machining time:** $T_m = N_{tot} \cdot 60 / N$ seconds.
> - **MRR:** $V_c \cdot f \cdot d$ (mm³/min); **Power:** $MRR \cdot k_s / (60 \times 10^6)$ kW.
> - **Shaping vs. Planing:** Shaping = small/medium jobs, tool moves; Planing = large jobs, workpiece moves.
> - **Applications:** tool rooms, maintenance, foundries, prototypes, large flat workpieces.
> - **Limitation:** low productivity due to idle return stroke; CNC milling has largely replaced it in mass production.
> - **Always verify** $T_c + T_r = 60/N$ to catch calculation errors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Planing and Slotting Machines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT404 - MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Planing and Slotting Machines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Planing Machine — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Planing Machine** is a material-removal machine tool that produces **flat surfaces** (horizontal, vertical, or inclined) on a workpiece by employing a **single-point cutting tool** that executes a **linear reciprocating motion** (straight-line cutting stroke followed by an idle return stroke), while the **workpiece is held stationary** (or fed slowly across the tool path) on a horizontal reciprocating table.

The planing operation is a **low-frequency, high-strokage machining process** primarily used to machine **large, heavy, and awkwardly shaped workpieces** that cannot be conveniently rotated or fed under a rotating multi-tooth cutter (as in milling or turning).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU High-Yield):**
> - In **planing** → *Tool moves*, *Workpiece is mostly stationary* (or reciprocates slowly).
> - In **shaping** → *Tool reciprocates*, *Workpiece is stationary* (smaller scale).
> - In **slotting** → *Tool reciprocates vertically*, *Workpiece is stationary* (vertical shaper).

### 1.2 Slotting Machine — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Slotting Machine** (also called a **vertical shaper** or **slotter**) is a reciprocating machine tool in which the **ram (carrying the single-point cutting tool) reciprocates vertically** (up-and-down motion), and the **workpiece is mounted on a rotary or longitudinal feed table**. It is primarily used for producing **internal slots, keyways, internal grooves, and dies**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Carpenter's Hand Plane vs. The Woodworker's Vertical Chisel:**
> - Imagine a **carpenter using a long hand plane** on a giant wooden door lying flat on a workbench. The carpenter pushes the plane forward (cutting stroke) and pulls it back (idle stroke). The wood is huge, so we cannot rotate it — we just keep sliding the plane back and forth. This is exactly what a **planing machine** does, but on metal at industrial scale.
> - Now imagine a **locksmith chiseling a keyway into the inside of a lock cylinder** — the chisel moves up and down vertically while the lock is held still. This is what a **slotting machine** mimics — vertical chiseling for internal features.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Cutting Velocity (V)** in planing/slotting is **not constant**; it varies throughout the stroke (high at mid-stroke, low at dead-centre positions). The quoted "cutting speed" is the **mean cutting speed** $V_m$.
- **Quick-Return Ratio (Q)** = Length of cutting stroke / Length of return stroke. Typically **2:1 to 3:1** in standard machines.
- **Planer Capacity** is specified by the **maximum stroke length (L)** and **table size (W × L)**, e.g., "1.5 m × 1.5 m" planer.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Planing Machine — Constructional Features

The major parts of a **double-housing planer** (the most common industrial type) are:

1. **Bed** — Heavy, ribbed cast-iron base on which the **table** slides on **V-and-flat ways** (or dovetail ways).
2. **Table** — A rectangular work-holding surface that **reciprocates** linearly on the bed. Holds the workpiece by T-slots and clamps.
3. **Housing (Columns)** — Two vertical columns/standards supporting the cross-rail and tool heads.
4. **Cross-Rail** — A horizontal beam mounted between the housings, carrying **side tool heads**.
5. **Tool Heads** — Usually **two side tool heads** (mounted on the housings) and **two vertical tool heads** (mounted on the cross-rail). Each head can be swivelled and fed independently.
6. **Driving Mechanism** — A bull gear, connecting rod, and rack-and-pinion arrangement converts rotary motion of the motor into reciprocating motion of the table.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Double-Housing Planer** = The table is mounted between two vertical housings → used for **heavy-duty, large workpieces** (e.g., lathe beds, large plates).
> **Open-Side (Pit) Planer** = Only one housing, allowing the workpiece to extend beyond the column → used for **extra-wide workpieces** (e.g., locomotive frames, large gear blanks).

### 2.2 Planing Machine — Working Principle

The operational sequence per cutting cycle:

- **Cutting Stroke**: The table (carrying the workpiece) advances against the **stationary single-point cutting tool** in a slow, powered feed. Material is removed in the form of a **discontinuous chip**.
- **Return Stroke (Idle)**: The table returns rapidly to its starting position, *lifted slightly* to avoid tool rubbing (called **tool relief** or **lateral clearance**).
- **Cross-Feed**: After each cutting stroke, the tool is fed **laterally (perpendicular to the cutting direction)** by a small increment `f` to engage a fresh portion of the workpiece.

### 2.3 Planing vs. Shaping — Critical Comparison Table

| Feature | Planing Machine | Shaping Machine |
|---|---|---|
| **Moving Element** | Workpiece (table) reciprocates | Tool (ram) reciprocates |
| **Stroke Length** | Long (up to several metres) | Short (typically < 900 mm) |
| **Workpiece Size** | Very large & heavy | Small to medium |
| **Power** | High (heavy cuts possible) | Low to medium |
| **Number of Tools** | Multiple (up to 4 tool heads) | Usually single tool |
| **Surface Quality** | Moderate to good | Good (shorter stroke = better accuracy) |
| **Cost** | High (₹₹₹) | Low (₹) |

### 2.4 Slotting Machine — Constructional & Working Details

Key parts of a slotter:

1. **Base & Column** — The vertical column houses the driving mechanism.
2. **Ram** — A vertical sliding member that carries the **tool slide and tool holder**. The ram reciprocates vertically.
3. **Rotary Table** — A circular work-holding table on the bed, which can be rotated and fed longitudinally or cross-wise. Essential for machining **circular/curved internal features**.
4. **Feed Mechanism** — Provides intermittent feed to the rotary table (circular feed for circular work, longitudinal feed for straight slots).
5. **Stroke Adjustment** — The **length of stroke** and **position of stroke** (relative to the workpiece) can be adjusted.

> [!NOTE]
> **Slotting Operations Performed:**
> - **Internal keyways** in pulleys, gears, and shafts.
> - **Internal slots, splines, and grooves**.
> - **Dies and punches** (sheet-metal tooling).
> - **Internal square or hexagonal holes**.
> - **Flat surfaces on small workpieces** held vertically.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All symbols are as per KTU 2024 Scheme notation.** The `vert` symbol is used to denote absolute value to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mean Cutting Speed | $V_m = \dfrac{2 \cdot L \cdot N_r}{1000}$ | m/min | $L$ = stroke length (mm), $N_r$ = double strokes/min |
| 2 | Quick-Return Ratio | $Q = \dfrac{T_c}{T_r} = \dfrac{L_c}{L_r}$ | dimensionless | $T_c$ = cutting time, $T_r$ = return time, $L_c$ = cutting length, $L_r$ = return length |
| 3 | Number of Strokes/min | $N_r = \dfrac{V_m \cdot 1000}{2 \cdot L}$ | strokes/min | Derived from Eq. 1 |
| 4 | Material Removal Rate (MRR) | $MRR = L \cdot d \cdot f \cdot N_r$ | mm³/min | $L$ = stroke length (mm), $d$ = depth of cut (mm), $f$ = feed (mm/stroke) |
| 5 | Volume of Material Removed | $V_{chip} = L \cdot d \cdot f$ | mm³/stroke | Per stroke |
| 6 | Time per Stroke | $T_{stroke} = \dfrac{60}{N_r}$ | s/stroke | — |
| 7 | Time for n Strokes | $T_n = \dfrac{60 \cdot n}{N_r}$ | seconds | For a given number of passes |
| 8 | Feed (planing) | $f = \dfrac{V_f}{N_r}$ | mm/stroke | $V_f$ = feed rate (mm/min) |
| 9 | Power Required | $P = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V_m}{60\,000}$ | kW | $F_c$ = cutting force (N) |
| 10 | Machining Time (Planing) | $T_m = \dfrac{L + L_{appr}}{V_m} \cdot \dfrac{W}{f}$ | minutes | $L_{appr}$ = approach allowance, $W$ = width of cut |

### 2.6 Engineering Utility of Planing & Slotting

- **Planing** is the **only practical method** for machining large cast-iron lathe beds, planer beds themselves, and large structural components.
- **Slotting** is **irreplaceable** for internal keyways on shaft-mounted components (where broaching is too expensive for small batches).
- Both are **reciprocating-type machines**, fundamentally distinct from rotary machines (turning, drilling, milling), and their understanding is **mandatory for KTU Module 3**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Mean Cutting Speed $V_m$ for a Reciprocating Machine

**Starting Premise:**
In a planing/slotting machine, the tool (or table) completes one **double stroke** (one cutting + one return stroke) per cycle. The number of such double strokes per minute is $N_r$.

**Step 1 — Distance covered in one cutting stroke:**
The tool travels a length equal to the **stroke length** $L$ (in mm) during the cutting stroke. The same distance $L$ is also covered during the return stroke.

**Step 2 — Total linear distance per minute:**
Since there are $N_r$ double strokes per minute, the total distance covered in one minute is:

$$
\text{Distance per minute} = 2 \cdot L \cdot N_r \quad [\text{mm/min}]
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to standard cutting speed units (m/min):**
Since $1000 \text{ mm} = 1 \text{ m}$, we divide by 1000:

$$
V_m = \frac{2 \cdot L \cdot N_r}{1000} \quad [\text{m/min}]
$$

**Step 4 — Rearranged form to find $N_r$:**

$$
N_r = \frac{V_m \cdot 1000}{2 \cdot L} \quad [\text{double strokes/min}]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The "mean" cutting speed is the average over the entire cycle. The instantaneous cutting velocity actually varies as a **simple harmonic motion** (if crank-driven) — slow at the dead-centre (top/bottom) positions and maximum at the mid-stroke position. Hence $V_m$ is the **time-averaged** speed, not the peak speed.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Quick-Return Mechanism Efficiency

**Step 1 — Define cutting and return times:**
For a crank-and-connecting-rod drive, the cutting stroke corresponds to the crank rotating through an angle $> 180°$ (typically $200°$ to $240°$), and the return stroke through the remaining angle (typically $120°$ to $160°$).

**Step 2 — Time is proportional to crank angle (constant RPM):**
Since the crank rotates at constant angular velocity, time is directly proportional to angle:

$$
T_c = \frac{\theta_c}{360} \cdot \frac{60}{N_{crank}} \quad ; \quad T_r = \frac{\theta_r}{360} \cdot \frac{60}{N_{crank}}
$$

where $\theta_c + \theta_r = 360°$.

**Step 3 — Quick-Return Ratio:**

$$
Q = \frac{T_c}{T_r} = \frac{\theta_c}{\theta_r}
$$

**Step 4 — Standard Numerical Example:**
If the crank rotates at $N_{crank} = 60$ rpm and the cutting angle is $240°$, the return angle is $120°$.

$$
T_c = \frac{240}{360} \cdot \frac{60}{60} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot 1 = 0.667 \text{ min} = 40 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_r = \frac{120}{360} \cdot 1 = \frac{1}{3} \text{ min} = 20 \text{ s}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{40}{20} = 2:1
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Material Removal Rate (MRR) in Planing

**Step 1 — Chip volume removed in ONE stroke:**
During one cutting stroke of length $L$ mm, with depth of cut $d$ mm and feed $f$ mm/stroke:

$$
V_{chip} = L \times d \times f \quad [\text{mm}^3/\text{stroke}]
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by number of strokes per minute:**

$$
MRR = V_{chip} \times N_r = L \cdot d \cdot f \cdot N_r \quad [\text{mm}^3/\text{min}]
$$

**Step 3 — Practical Numerical Example:**
A planer is set with stroke length $L = 1000$ mm, depth $d = 5$ mm, feed $f = 2$ mm/stroke, and $N_r = 10$ double strokes/min.

$$
MRR = 1000 \times 5 \times 2 \times 10 = 100\,000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 100 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Check:** $100\,000 \text{ mm}^3 = 100 \text{ cm}^3$ (since $1 \text{ cm}^3 = 1000 \text{ mm}^3$).

---

### 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Numerical — Machining Time Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem (KTU-style):** A surface of width **$W = 200$ mm** is to be planed on a casting using a planer. The cutting stroke length is $L = 400$ mm, feed $f = 1.5$ mm/stroke, depth of cut $d = 3$ mm, and the mean cutting speed $V_m = 12$ m/min. The approach allowance $L_{appr} = 50$ mm. Calculate: (a) the number of double strokes per minute, (b) the MRR, and (c) the total machining time.

**Solution:**

**(a) Number of double strokes per minute $N_r$:**

From the formula sheet (Eq. 3):

$$
N_r = \frac{V_m \cdot 1000}{2 \cdot L} = \frac{12 \cdot 1000}{2 \cdot 400}
$$

$$
N_r = \frac{12\,000}{800} = 15 \text{ double strokes/min}
$$

**(b) Material Removal Rate:**

From Eq. 4 of the formula sheet:

$$
MRR = L \cdot d \cdot f \cdot N_r = 400 \times 3 \times 1.5 \times 15
$$

$$
MRR = 400 \times 3 = 1200; \quad 1200 \times 1.5 = 1800; \quad 1800 \times 15 = 27\,000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}
$$

**(c) Total Machining Time:**

The number of strokes required to cover the width $W = 200$ mm with feed $f = 1.5$ mm/stroke:

$$
n = \frac{W}{f} = \frac{200}{1.5} = 133.33 \approx 134 \text{ strokes}
$$

The time per stroke:

$$
T_{stroke} = \frac{60}{N_r} = \frac{60}{15} = 4 \text{ s/stroke}
$$

Therefore:

$$
T_m = n \times T_{stroke} = 134 \times 4 = 536 \text{ s} \approx 8.93 \text{ min}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification using the alternative formula (Eq. 10):**

$$
T_m = \frac{(L + L_{appr})}{V_m} \times \frac{W}{f} = \frac{(400 + 50)/1000}{12} \times \frac{200}{1.5}
$$

$$
T_m = \frac{0.45}{12} \times 133.33 = 0.0375 \times 133.33 = 5.0 \text{ min}
$$

> The discrepancy arises because Eq. 10 ignores the return stroke (i.e., assumes *continuous* cutting). For accurate time including the return stroke, use $T_m = n \times (60/N_r) = 8.93$ min. **Always state the assumption in the KTU exam.**

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Double-Housing Planer

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional flow** of components in a double-housing planer, illustrating how the input power is transmitted and how cutting force is applied.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Electric Motor] --> A2[Bull Gear Drive]
    A2 --> A3[Crank Pin and Connecting Rod]
    A3 --> A4[Rack and Pinion on Table]
    A4 --> A5[Workpiece Table - Linear Reciprocation]
    A5 --> A6[Workpiece Held in T-Slots and Clamps]
    B1[Cross Rail Feed Motor] --> B2[Cross Rail Vertical Feed]
    B2 --> B3[Side Tool Head Left]
    B2 --> B4[Side Tool Head Right]
    C1[Tool Head Slide Motor] --> C2[Horizontal Feed Mechanism]
    C2 --> C3[Vertical Tool Head Front]
    C2 --> C4[Vertical Tool Head Rear]
    A6 --> CUT[Material Removal During Cutting Stroke]
    C3 --> CUT
    B3 --> CUT
    C4 --> CUT
    B4 --> CUT
    CUT --> D1[Chip Formation and Disposal]
    D1 --> D2[Finished Flat Surface on Workpiece]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Power flows from the **Motor → Bull Gear → Connecting Rod → Rack-Pinion → Table** (giving the reciprocating motion). The **tool heads** are independently fed by their own motors for cross-feed. All four tool heads converge at the **cutting zone** to remove material from the workpiece.

### 4.2 Kinematic Block Diagram of the Quick-Return Mechanism

This diagram explains how the **crank-and-connecting-rod arrangement** achieves the unequal cutting and return strokes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Main Shaft Motor Drive] --> S1[Bull Gear]
    S1 --> S2[Crank Disc with Offset Pin]
    S2 --> S3[Connecting Rod]
    S3 --> S4[Slotted Lever and Rack]
    S4 --> T1[Table Reciprocates Forward - Cutting Stroke]
    S4 --> T2[Table Returns Backward - Idle Stroke]
    S2 -.->|Angle 200 to 240 deg| T1
    S2 -.->|Angle 120 to 160 deg| T2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The crank rotates at constant RPM. Since the cutting portion occupies a larger crank angle (≈ 2/3 of the cycle), the cutting stroke is **slower** (better surface finish and chip formation), while the return stroke is **faster** (saving time, improving productivity).

### 4.3 Functional Architecture of a Slotting Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Electric Motor] --> P2[Bull Gear in Column]
    P2 --> P3[Crank Disc and Connecting Rod]
    P3 --> P4[Vertical Ram with Tool Holder]
    P4 --> P5[Single Point Cutting Tool]
    Q1[Rotary Table Feed Motor] --> Q2[Rotary Table with T-Slots]
    Q2 --> Q3[Workpiece Mounted on Rotary Table]
    Q3 --> CUT2[Internal Slot or Keyway Machined]
    P5 --> CUT2
    Q2 -->|Longitudinal Feed| CUT2
    Q2 -->|Circular Feed| CUT3[Curved Internal Feature]
    P5 --> CUT3
    Q2 -->|Cross Feed| CUT4[Rectangular Pocket]
    P5 --> CUT4
```

### 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram — Planing vs. Shaping vs. Slotting

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Reciprocating Machine Tool Family] --> R1[Planing]
    R --> R2[Shaping]
    R --> R3[Slotting]
    R1 --> R1A[Workpiece Table Moves]
    R1 --> R1B[Tool is Stationary during cut]
    R1 --> R1C[Large Workpieces up to several metres]
    R2 --> R2A[Tool Ram Moves Horizontally]
    R2 --> R2B[Workpiece is Stationary]
    R2 --> R2C[Small to Medium Workpieces]
    R3 --> R3A[Tool Ram Moves Vertically]
    R3 --> R3B[Workpiece on Rotary Table]
    R3 --> R3C[Internal Slots and Keyways]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The above comparative block diagram is a high-yield visual that examiners love. If asked to "compare and contrast" these three machines, drawing a similar flowchart with the moving/stationary elements earns full marks.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Distinguish between a planing machine and a shaping machine. Mention any three points of difference.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| S.No. | Planing Machine | Shaping Machine |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | The **workpiece (table) reciprocates** linearly, while the tool is held stationary during the cutting stroke. | The **tool (ram) reciprocates** linearly, while the workpiece remains stationary on the table. |
| 2 | Used for machining **large, heavy, and bulky workpieces** (e.g., lathe beds, large plates) with stroke length up to several metres. | Used for **small to medium-sized workpieces** with stroke length typically less than 900 mm. |
| 3 | Can have **multiple tool heads (up to 4)** — two on housings, two on cross-rail — allowing simultaneous multi-surface machining. | Usually equipped with a **single tool head**, machining one surface at a time. |
| 4 | **High power** machines capable of heavy cuts; expensive. | **Lower power**, less expensive, suitable for job-shop production. |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is a quick-return mechanism? State its purpose in a planing machine.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **quick-return mechanism** is a kinematic arrangement (commonly a **crank-and-connecting-rod** or **bull-gear and slotted-lever** type) that converts the **uniform rotary motion** of the driving motor into a **non-uniform reciprocating motion** of the ram or table. The cutting stroke occurs over a **larger crank angle** (typically $200°$–$240°$) and hence takes **more time** at a **lower mean speed**, which is desirable for proper chip formation and surface finish. The return stroke occurs over the **remaining smaller angle** ($120°$–$160°$) at a **higher mean speed**, ensuring the tool returns quickly to the start position **without cutting**, thereby improving productivity.

The **quick-return ratio (Q)** is defined as $Q = T_c / T_r$, typically ranging from **2:1 to 3:1** in standard planers.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 3 carries an internal choice. Two alternative questions are provided below.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working principle of a double-housing planer**. List the various operations that can be performed on it. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A planing operation is to be carried out on a cast iron surface of length **$L_w = 1.2$ m** and width **$W = 250$ mm**. The cutting stroke length is **$L = 1.4$ m** (including approach and over-run), the mean cutting speed is **$V_m = 15$ m/min**, the feed is **$f = 2$ mm/stroke**, and the depth of cut is **$d = 4$ mm**. Calculate the **(i)** number of double strokes per minute, **(ii)** material removal rate, and **(iii)** total machining time. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Construction (5 sub-points, 1 mark each for naming + 1 mark for neat sketch):**

A **double-housing planer** consists of the following main parts:

1. **Bed** — A heavy, ribbed cast-iron base that provides rigidity and supports the reciprocating table. It has precision-machined **V-and-flat ways** on top.
2. **Table** — A rectangular work-holding surface that slides on the bed ways. It has **T-slots** for clamping workpieces using bolts and clamps. The table is driven by a rack-and-pinion mechanism.
3. **Two Vertical Housings (Columns)** — Mounted on either side of the bed, supporting the cross-rail and providing rigidity.
4. **Cross-Rail** — A horizontal beam spanning between the two housings. It can be raised or lowered to accommodate different workpiece heights.
5. **Tool Heads** — A double-housing planer has **four tool heads**:
   - **Two side tool heads** mounted on the inner faces of the housings (for vertical surfaces).
   - **Two vertical tool heads** mounted on the cross-rail (for horizontal surfaces).
   Each tool head can swivel to cut at any angle and has independent feed.
6. **Driving Mechanism** — Comprises a motor, bull gear, crank disc, connecting rod, and rack-and-pinion that converts rotary motion into reciprocating table motion.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
The workpiece is clamped on the table. As the table reciprocates (cutting stroke), the tool (held in the tool head) removes material. During the return stroke, the tool is automatically lifted (lateral relief) to prevent rubbing. After each stroke, the tool is fed cross-wise by a small amount $f$.

**Operations Performed (1 mark):**
- Machining horizontal, vertical, and inclined flat surfaces.
- Cutting slots, grooves, and T-slots.
- Machining angular surfaces using swivelled tool heads.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given Data:**
- $L_w = 1.2$ m = 1200 mm (workpiece length)
- $W = 250$ mm
- $L = 1.4$ m = 1400 mm (cutting stroke length)
- $V_m = 15$ m/min
- $f = 2$ mm/stroke
- $d = 4$ mm

**(i) Number of double strokes per minute $N_r$:**

$$
N_r = \frac{V_m \times 1000}{2 \times L} = \frac{15 \times 1000}{2 \times 1400} = \frac{15\,000}{2800}
$$

$$
N_r = 5.357 \approx 5.36 \text{ double strokes/min}
$$

**(ii) Material Removal Rate:**

$$
MRR = L \times d \times f \times N_r = 1400 \times 4 \times 2 \times 5.357
$$

$$
MRR = 1400 \times 4 = 5600; \quad 5600 \times 2 = 11\,200; \quad 11\,200 \times 5.357 = 60\,000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}
$$

(Using $N_r = 5.36$ for clean calculation: $MRR = 1400 \times 4 \times 2 \times 5.36 = 60\,032 \approx 60\,000$ mm³/min)

**Answer:** $MRR \approx 60\,000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 60 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}$

**(iii) Total Machining Time:**

Number of strokes required:

$$
n = \frac{W}{f} = \frac{250}{2} = 125 \text{ strokes}
$$

Time per stroke:

$$
T_{stroke} = \frac{60}{N_r} = \frac{60}{5.36} = 11.19 \text{ s/stroke}
$$

Total time:

$$
T_m = n \times T_{stroke} = 125 \times 11.19 = 1398.75 \text{ s} \approx 23.3 \text{ min}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

**(a)** Explain the **construction, working, and applications of a slotting machine**. How is it different from a shaping machine? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In a slotting operation, the stroke length is **$L = 150$ mm**, the number of strokes per minute is **$N_r = 30$**, the feed is **$f = 0.5$ mm/stroke**, and the depth of cut is **$d = 2$ mm**. Calculate the **(i)** mean cutting speed, **(ii)** time for one complete cutting stroke, and **(iii)** volume of metal removed per minute. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Construction (3 marks):**

A slotting machine (vertical shaper) consists of:

1. **Base and Column** — A heavy, robust column that supports the entire mechanism and houses the drive.
2. **Ram (Sliding Head)** — A vertical reciprocating member carrying the tool holder at its bottom end. The ram slides in accurately machined vertical guideways.
3. **Rotary Table** — A circular work-holding table mounted on the base. It can be **rotated, fed longitudinally, or fed cross-wise**.
4. **Feed Mechanism** — Automatic intermittent feed to the rotary table after each return stroke.
5. **Stroke Length and Position Adjustment** — The length and vertical position of the ram stroke can be adjusted.

**Working (2 marks):**
The workpiece is clamped on the rotary table. The ram carrying the tool reciprocates vertically — downward motion is the **cutting stroke**, upward motion is the **idle return stroke**. After each return stroke, the rotary table feeds the workpiece by a small amount to expose fresh material. For circular internal features, the table rotates continuously (circular feed) instead of linear feed.

**Applications (1 mark):**
- Internal keyways, splines, and slots in gears and pulleys.
- Internal grooves in machine parts.
- Dies and punches for sheet metal.
- Square and hexagonal holes.

**Difference from Shaping Machine (1 mark):**
In a **shaping machine**, the tool reciprocates **horizontally** and the workpiece is on a flat table. In a **slotting machine**, the tool reciprocates **vertically** and the workpiece is on a **rotary table**, enabling internal and curved surface machining.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given Data:**
- $L = 150$ mm
- $N_r = 30$ strokes/min
- $f = 0.5$ mm/stroke
- $d = 2$ mm

**(i) Mean Cutting Speed $V_m$:**

$$
V_m = \frac{2 \times L \times N_r}{1000} = \frac{2 \times 150 \times 30}{1000} = \frac{9000}{1000}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_m = 9 \text{ m/min}}
$$

**(ii) Time for one complete cutting stroke:**

Note: $N_r$ is **double strokes per minute**, so one double stroke (cutting + return) takes:

$$
T_{cycle} = \frac{60}{N_r} = \frac{60}{30} = 2 \text{ seconds per double stroke}
$$

If only the **cutting portion** of the stroke is asked (assuming Q = 2:1 quick-return ratio, cutting takes 2/3 of cycle time):

$$
T_c = \frac{2}{3} \times 2 = 1.333 \text{ s/cutting stroke}
$$

If Q is not specified, the total cycle time is **2 seconds per double stroke**.

**(iii) Volume of metal removed per minute (MRR):**

$$
MRR = L \times d \times f \times N_r = 150 \times 2 \times 0.5 \times 30
$$

$$
MRR = 150 \times 2 = 300; \quad 300 \times 0.5 = 150; \quad 150 \times 30 = 4500 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}
$$

$$
\boxed{MRR = 4500 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 4.5 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** $L$ in formula must be in **mm** for the final $V_m$ in m/min. Forgetting to divide by 1000 is the #1 mistake costing 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Strokes:** A "stroke" in planing is a **double stroke** (one forward + one return). Examiners often state $N_r$ as double strokes/min — read carefully.
> 3. **Feed Direction:** In planing, the feed $f$ is given as **mm/stroke** (not mm/revolution, since there is no rotation). Writing it as mm/rev will fetch **zero marks**.
> 4. **MRR Formula:** Students often forget the factor of 2 in the cutting speed formula, or the factor of $N_r$ in MRR.
> 5. **Quick-Return Ratio:** When asked, always mention the **typical range 2:1 to 3:1** and the **cutting angle** (≈ 2/3 of the full crank rotation).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **planing machine** uses a **reciprocating workpiece** (table) and a **stationary single-point tool**; it is the largest of all reciprocating machine tools and is used for very large workpieces.
- A **shaping machine** uses a **reciprocating tool** (horizontal ram) and a **stationary workpiece**; it is for small to medium workpieces.
- A **slotting machine** uses a **vertically reciprocating tool** and a **rotary work table**; it is used for **internal features** like keyways and slots.
- The **double-housing planer** has **two columns and a cross-rail** with up to **four tool heads** for simultaneous multi-surface machining.
- The **quick-return mechanism** uses crank angles typically of **$200°$–$240°$ for cutting** and **$120°$–$160°$ for return**, giving a quick-return ratio **Q = 2:1 to 3:1**.
- The **mean cutting speed** formula $V_m = (2 L N_r)/1000$ is the **most important equation** for this module.
- The **MRR** formula $MRR = L \cdot d \cdot f \cdot N_r$ applies to all reciprocating machines (planing, shaping, slotting).
- The **machining time** is $T_m = n \times (60/N_r)$, where $n = W/f$ is the number of strokes required to cover the width.
- The **cutting velocity is variable** (harmonic), reaching maximum at mid-stroke and zero at dead-centre positions — this is why $V_m$ is called the *mean* speed.
- The **unit of $N_r$** is always **double strokes per minute** — never revolutions, since there is no rotation.
- The **planer capacity** is specified by the **maximum stroke length × table width**, e.g., "2 m × 1.5 m".
- **Slotting machines** are the only practical means of producing **internal keyways in shaft-mounted components** for small-batch production (broaching is used for large batches).
- **Tool relief during return stroke** is automatic in planers (table is slightly lowered or tool lifted) to prevent rubbing and tool wear.
- **Feed in planing** is given **per double stroke**, and the feed direction is **perpendicular** to the cutting direction.
- For the **KTU exam**, always draw a **neat labelled sketch** of the planer/shaper/slotter — it is worth a minimum of **2–3 marks** even if the rest of the answer is incomplete.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Grinding - Types of Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT404 - MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Grinding - Types of Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grinding** is a material removal process in manufacturing where abrasive particles bonded together in a grinding wheel act as micro-cutting tools. Each abrasive grain has a sharp cutting edge that removes a tiny chip from the workpiece surface through a combination of rubbing, ploughing, and cutting action. Grinding is classified as a *finishing* (and sometimes *semi-finishing*) abrasive machining process used to achieve high dimensional accuracy (tolerances of **±0.0005 mm** or better), excellent surface finish (Ra **0.1–1.6 µm**), and tight geometrical form.

Mathematically, the grinding process is governed by the interaction between three principal movements:
1. **Wheel velocity** $v_s$ (m/s) — Primary cutting motion
2. **Workpiece velocity** $v_w$ (m/s) — Secondary feed motion
3. **Infeed** $d$ (mm) — Depth of cut per pass

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine sanding a wooden plank with sandpaper. Sandpaper has tiny, hard, sharp grains glued to a flexible sheet. As you slide the sandpaper across the wood, each tiny grain scrapes off a microscopic sliver of wood. **Grinding is the industrial, precision-engineered cousin of sanding.** Instead of sand grains on paper, grinding uses ultra-hard, geometrically controlled abrasive grains (Aluminium Oxide $Al_2O_3$, Silicon Carbide $SiC$, Cubic Boron Nitride $CBN$, or Diamond) bonded in a wheel. The "sanding" is done at wheel surface speeds of **30–50 m/s** (almost 200 km/h) — that's why grinding produces very fine finishes and high accuracy.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is grinding considered a "super-finishing" process?**
> Because each abrasive grain removes a chip of thickness typically **0.001–0.025 mm** — about **50–1000 times smaller** than a turning or milling chip. This is why grinding is the *last operation* in most precision manufacturing sequences (e.g., bearings, gears, dies, cutting tools).

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standard wheel surface speed (Al₂O₃ on steel) | $v_s$ | **30 – 35** | m/s |
| Standard wheel surface speed (CBN/Diamond) | $v_s$ | **45 – 60** | m/s |
| Wheel hardness grade (Shore scale) | — | **A to Z** | Alphabetical |
| Grain size (mesh number) | — | **8 (coarse) – 600 (fine)** | — |
| Workpiece speed | $v_w$ | **0.05 – 0.30** | m/s |
| Depth of cut | $d$ | **0.005 – 0.050** | mm/pass |
| G-ratio (wheel-life metric) | $G$ | **10 – 1000** | mm³/mm³ |

### 1.4 Classification Overview

Grinding operations are broadly classified into two families:
- **Conventional / Standard Grinding** (uses bonded abrasive wheels)
- **Non-Conventional / Modern Abrasive Processes** (uses loose abrasives or specialized kinematics)

The standard bonded-wheel operations include: Surface, Cylindrical, Centerless, Internal, Tool & Cutter, and several special variants.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Principal Types of Grinding Operations

#### 2.1.1 Surface Grinding
The **flat face or periphery** of the grinding wheel grinds a flat workpiece surface. The workpiece is held on a reciprocating magnetic chuck table.

- **Wheel contact:** Peripheral (side) or Face
- **Use:** Flat surfaces, die flats, reference surfaces, tool-room work
- **Feed directions:** Longitudinal (table travel) + cross-feed + vertical infeed

**Why this works:** The reciprocating motion gives uniform spark pattern, and the magnetic chuck holds the workpiece rigidly — making it the *go-to* method for finishing machine beds, tool-room jobs, and hardened steel flat surfaces.

#### 2.1.2 Cylindrical Grinding
Used to finish the **external cylindrical surface** of a workpiece mounted between centers (or in a chuck). The wheel grinds while the workpiece rotates.

- **Wheel contact:** Peripheral
- **Sub-types:**
  - **Plunge grinding** — wheel plunges radially to full depth
  - **Traverse grinding** — wheel traverses along the workpiece length
- **Use:** Shafts, journals, pins, rollers

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical concept:** In cylindrical grinding, both wheel and workpiece rotate. The relative speed $(v_s - v_w)$ determines the cutting action. The grinding arc is where material is actually removed.

#### 2.1.3 Centerless Grinding
The workpiece is **not held between centers** — instead, it rests on a *work rest blade* between two wheels:
- **Regulating wheel** (rubber/ceramic bonded) — rotates slower, controls workpiece rotation & feed
- **Grinding wheel** (rigid, fast) — does the actual cutting

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy:** Think of a pencil rolling between your two palms while one palm presses harder. The harder palm is the grinding wheel, the softer palm is the regulating wheel. The pencil is the workpiece.

- **Variants:** Through-feed, In-feed (plunge), End-feed
- **Use:** High-volume production of pins, bushings, piston rods

#### 2.1.4 Internal Grinding
A small-diameter wheel grinds the **inner surface** of a bore/hole. The workpiece is held in a chuck and rotated while the wheel spindle rotates and reciprocates inside the bore.

- **Use:** Bearing races, cylinder bores, hydraulic sleeves
- **Challenge:** Wheel diameter is small → lower wheel speed → tendency for chatter

#### 2.1.5 Tool and Cutter Grinding
Uses specialized **profiled or diamond-dressed wheels** to sharpen or form cutting tools (drills, milling cutters, hobs, reamers).

- **Use:** Resharpening, profile grinding, form tools

#### 2.1.6 Special / Production Grinding Operations
| Type | Description | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Creep-Feed Grinding** | Very high depth of cut (1–10 mm) at very low table feed | Slots, profile grinding, aerospace blades |
| **Jig Grinding** | Precision bore finishing using a jig boring-type machine | Tool-room dies, moulds |
| **Disc / Bench Grinding** | Hand-held workpiece against large wheel | Off-hand deburring, sharpening |
| **Portable Grinding** | Wheel mounted on handheld machine | Field work, weld dressing |
| **Thread Grinding** | Threaded profile wheel grinds screw threads | Lead screws, ball screws |
| **Gear Grinding** | Form wheel grinds gear tooth flanks | Hardened gears (after heat treatment) |
| **Crankshaft / Cam Grinding** | Specialized multi-wheel machines | Automotive engines |

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all the critical formulas** the KTU examiner expects you to know for a question on grinding operations. Memorize these before reading further.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Wheel surface speed** | $v_s = \dfrac{\pi D_s N_s}{60}$ | $D_s$ = wheel diameter (m), $N_s$ = rpm | m/s |
| 2 | **Workpiece surface speed** | $v_w = \dfrac{\pi D_w N_w}{60}$ | $D_w$ = workpiece dia (m), $N_w$ = rpm | m/s |
| 3 | **MRR — Surface grinding (periphery)** | $Z_w = w \cdot d \cdot v_t$ | $w$ = width (mm), $d$ = depth (mm), $v_t$ = table speed (mm/s) | mm³/s |
| 4 | **MRR — Cylindrical grinding** | $Z_w = \pi D_w \cdot f_r \cdot v_t$ | $f_r$ = radial infeed (mm/rev), $v_t$ = traverse (mm/s) | mm³/s |
| 5 | **MRR — Internal grinding** | $Z_w = \pi D_w \cdot f_r \cdot v_t$ | Same as cylindrical, $D_w$ = bore dia | mm³/s |
| 6 | **MRR — Centerless grinding** | $Z_w = \pi D_w \cdot f \cdot v_r$ | $f$ = infeed, $v_r$ = regulating wheel pitch-line vel. | mm³/s |
| 7 | **G-ratio** | $G = \dfrac{V_{\text{work}}}{V_{\text{wheel}}} = \dfrac{Z_w}{Z_s}$ | Ratio of workpiece volume to wheel volume removed | Dimensionless |
| 8 | **Specific Energy** | $u = \dfrac{P}{Z_w}$ | $P$ = power (W), $Z_w$ in mm³/s | N·mm/mm³ (J/mm³) |
| 9 | **Equivalent chip thickness** | $h_{eq} = \dfrac{v_w \cdot \sqrt{d}}{v_s \cdot \sqrt{D_s}}$ | All in compatible SI units | m or µm |
| 10 | **Number of cutting points per area** | $C = \dfrac{c \cdot \rho_{\text{grain}}}{d_g^2}$ | $c$ = fraction of active grits, $\rho$ = density | 1/m² |
| 11 | **Equivalent diameter (centerless)** | $D_{eq} = \dfrac{D_w}{1 + \dfrac{v_r}{v_g}}$ | $v_g$ = grinding wheel pitch-line velocity | m |
| 12 | **Regulating wheel tilt angle** | $\sin\alpha = \dfrac{v_f}{v_r}$ | $v_f$ = workpiece feed, $v_r$ = regulating surface vel. | rad or ° |
| 13 | **Power required** | $P = u \cdot Z_w$ | $u$ = specific energy (J/mm³), $Z_w$ in mm³/s | W |
| 14 | **Surface roughness (theoretical)** | $R_a \approx \dfrac{h_{eq}^2}{8r}$ | $r$ = grain nose radius | µm |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not confuse MRR formula for surface grinding** ($w \cdot d \cdot v_t$) **with cylindrical grinding** ($\pi D_w \cdot f_r \cdot v_t$). This is the single most common mistake KTU examiners penalize. The rectangular cross-section in surface grinding gives $w \cdot d$, while the cylindrical swept area gives $\pi D_w \cdot f_r$.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

> [!TIP]
> - **Aerospace:** Creep-feed grinding of titanium turbine blades — single-pass profile generation from solid.
> - **Automotive:** Cylindrical grinding of camshaft journals, centerless grinding of valve stems.
> - **Bearings:** Internal grinding of raceways to sub-micron roundness.
> - **Tool-room:** Jig grinding of mould cavities to ±2 µm accuracy.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The derivations below are the **most-tested analytical content** for grinding questions in KTU university exams. You should be able to reproduce each one on paper.

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Material Removal Rate (MRR) in Surface Grinding

**Setup:** A surface grinding wheel of diameter $D_s$ rotates at $N_s$ rpm. The workpiece (width $w$, length $L$) is fixed on a reciprocating table that moves at velocity $v_t$ in the longitudinal direction. A vertical infeed of $d$ mm is given per pass.

**Step 1 — Identify the volume removed in one pass.**
The wheel removes a rectangular layer from the workpiece surface of:
- Length = $L$ (workpiece length)
- Width = $w$ (workpiece width, also width of wheel contact)
- Height (thickness) = $d$ (depth of cut)

$$
V_{\text{pass}} = L \times w \times d
$$

**Step 2 — Time taken for one pass.**
The table moves at velocity $v_t$, so the time to traverse the workpiece once is:

$$
t_{\text{pass}} = \dfrac{L}{v_t}
$$

**Step 3 — MRR definition.**
MRR is the volume removed per unit time:

$$
Z_w = \dfrac{V_{\text{pass}}}{t_{\text{pass}}} = \dfrac{L \cdot w \cdot d}{L / v_t} = w \cdot d \cdot v_t
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Z_w = w \cdot d \cdot v_t\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Unit check.**
$[w] = \text{mm}, \; [d] = \text{mm}, \; [v_t] = \text{mm/s} \;\Rightarrow\; [Z_w] = \text{mm}^3/\text{s}$ ✓

### 3.2 Derivation 2: MRR in Cylindrical Grinding

**Setup:** A cylindrical workpiece of diameter $D_w$ rotates at $N_w$ rpm. The grinding wheel moves radially in at feed $f_r$ (mm/rev) and traverses longitudinally at $v_t$ (mm/s).

**Step 1 — Radial penetration per revolution.**
In one revolution, the wheel penetrates radially by $f_r$ mm. Since the workpiece is cylindrical, the material removed is a thin cylindrical shell of thickness $f_r$ over a length corresponding to the contact.

**Step 2 — Circumferential length per revolution.**

$$
L_{\text{circ}} = \pi D_w
$$

**Step 3 — Volume removed per revolution.**
This is the area of a thin cylindrical strip of length $\pi D_w$ and radial depth $f_r$, swept over a unit length — but more rigorously, considering the workpiece rotation rate $N_w$ and traverse $v_t$:

$$
V_{\text{rev}} = \pi D_w \cdot f_r \cdot (\text{axial length per rev})
$$

The axial length advanced per revolution of the workpiece is $v_t / N_w$ (since $v_t$ is mm/s, divide by rev/s to get mm/rev). Thus:

$$
Z_w = \dfrac{V_{\text{rev}}}{1 / N_w} = \pi D_w \cdot f_r \cdot v_t
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Z_w = \pi D_w \cdot f_r \cdot v_t\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Unit check.**
$[\pi D_w] = \text{mm}, \; [f_r] = \text{mm/rev}, \; [v_t] = \text{mm/s}$ → Note: we must be consistent. Re-checking: $f_r$ in mm/rev means volume/rev = $\pi D_w \cdot f_r$ per axial unit. Multiplying by $v_t$ (mm/s) gives the correct mm³/s. ✓

### 3.3 Derivation 3: G-Ratio (Wheel-Life Metric)

**Definition:** G-ratio is the volume of workpiece material removed per unit volume of grinding wheel worn away.

**Step 1 — Workpiece volume removed per unit time** is $Z_w$ (mm³/s).

**Step 2 — Wheel volume worn per unit time** is $Z_s$ (mm³/s), measured as the decrease in wheel radius times the contact area swept.

**Step 3 — G-ratio is their ratio:**

$$
\boxed{\,G = \dfrac{Z_w}{Z_s} = \dfrac{V_{\text{workpiece removed}}}{V_{\text{wheel worn}}}\,}
$$

**Why it matters:**
- **High G-ratio** (e.g., 500–1000) → wheel lasts long → cost-effective
- **Low G-ratio** (e.g., 5–20) → wheel wears fast → dressing frequent → expensive
- Diamond and CBN wheels have G-ratios **100× higher** than Al₂O₃ on hardened steel.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Specific Energy in Grinding

**Step 1 — Definition.** Specific energy $u$ is the energy consumed per unit volume of material removed.

$$
u = \dfrac{\text{Energy input}}{\text{Volume removed}}
$$

**Step 2 — Power $P$** supplied to the grinding process (in Watts = J/s). The energy consumed in time $t$ is $P \cdot t$ (J).

**Step 3 — Volume removed in time $t$** is $Z_w \cdot t$ (mm³).

**Step 4 — Therefore:**

$$
u = \dfrac{P \cdot t}{Z_w \cdot t} = \dfrac{P}{Z_w}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,u = \dfrac{P}{Z_w}\,}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Typical values of specific energy in grinding:**
> - Soft steels (low-C, annealed): **5 – 15 J/mm³**
> - Hardened steels (HRC 60): **20 – 50 J/mm³**
> - Carbides / Ceramics: **50 – 200 J/mm³**
>
> Compare to turning: **1 – 3 J/mm³** — grinding consumes **5–50× more energy per unit volume** than turning, justifying why it is used only for finishing.

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Equivalent Chip Thickness in Grinding

This is a **KTU-favorite derivation** (asked frequently in ESE).

**Setup:** Each grit on the wheel removes a chip. The chip cross-section is approximately triangular with:
- Width (along wheel surface) = contact length $= \sqrt{D_s \cdot d}$
- Length (perpendicular to surface) = undeformed chip thickness $h_{eq}$

**Step 1 — Geometric contact length.**
For a wheel of diameter $D_s$ cutting to depth $d$, the contact arc length projected onto the wheel surface is:

$$
\ell = \sqrt{D_s \cdot d}
$$

**Step 2 — Material balance on the contact zone.**
The volume of material entering the contact zone per unit time equals the MRR:

$$
Z_w = w \cdot v_w \cdot d
$$

(where $w$ is the width, $v_w$ is the workpiece velocity, $d$ is the depth — same as surface grinding but re-expressed).

**Step 3 — Equivalently, the volume passing through the contact zone is the contact area times the chip thickness:**

$$
Z_w = (w \cdot \ell) \cdot v_s \cdot \frac{h_{eq}}{\text{(grit spacing)}}
$$

Using the **simplified equivalent chip thickness** definition (Malkin):

$$
h_{eq} = \dfrac{v_w}{v_s} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{d}{D_s}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,h_{eq} = \dfrac{v_w \cdot \sqrt{d}}{v_s \cdot \sqrt{D_s}}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This formula tells you the most important practical lesson: **to reduce $h_{eq}$** (and therefore improve surface finish), you must **increase $v_s$, increase $D_s$, or reduce $v_w$ and $d$**. This is why creep-feed grinding (high $d$, low $v_w$) still produces good finishes because $v_s$ is also high.

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Operation | Wheel Contact | Workpiece Motion | Typical MRR (mm³/s) | Typical Ra (µm) | Accuracy (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Surface grinding | Periphery/Face | Reciprocating | 5 – 200 | 0.4 – 1.6 | ±0.005 |
| Cylindrical grinding | Periphery | Rotating + reciprocating | 3 – 100 | 0.2 – 0.8 | ±0.0025 |
| Centerless grinding | Periphery | Rotating (regulating wheel) | 10 – 300 | 0.2 – 0.8 | ±0.0025 |
| Internal grinding | Periphery (small wheel) | Rotating | 1 – 30 | 0.4 – 1.6 | ±0.005 |
| Creep-feed grinding | Periphery | Slow feed, deep cut | 5 – 50 (mm³/s) per mm width | 0.4 – 1.6 | ±0.010 |
| Tool & cutter | Periphery/End | Manual/CNC | Variable | 0.2 – 0.8 | ±0.002 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Classification Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid

    A --> C[Loose Abrasive Process]:::branch

    B --> H[Tool and Cutter Grinding]:::leaf
    B --> I[Special Grinding]:::leaf

    D --> D1[Horizontal Spindle Reciprocating Table]:::sub
    D --> D2[Vertical Spindle Rotary Table]:::sub
    D --> D3[Vertical Spindle Reciprocating Table]:::sub

    E --> E2[Plunge Cylindrical]:::sub
    E --> E3[Universal Cylindrical]:::sub

    F --> F2[Infeed Plunge]:::sub

    I --> I1[Creep Feed]:::sub

    C --> J5[Abrasive Flow]:::sub

```

### 4.2 Centerless Grinding Kinematic Architecture (Mermaid Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GR[Grinding Wheel]:::driver1 -->|Peripheral contact| WP[Workpiece]:::target
    REG[Regulating Wheel]:::driver2 -->|Friction drives rotation| WP
    WRB[Work Rest Blade]:::support -->|Supports and locates| WP
    WP -->|Through feed exit| OUT[Finished Component]:::output

    GR -->|Speed vg| REL((Relative Velocity)):::math
    REG -->|Speed vr, tilt alpha| REL
    REL -->|Determines feed vf| WP

    classDef driver1 fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px
    classDef driver2 fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:2px

```

### 4.3 Surface Grinding Setup (Block Architecture)

```mermaid

    MOTOR1[Spindle Motor - Ns rpm]:::motor --> WHEEL

    TABLE[Reciprocating Table - Velocity vt]:::table
    CHUCK[Magnetic Chuck]:::chuck --> WP
    TABLE --> CHUCK
    INFEED[Vertical Infeed - Depth d]:::infeed --> WHEEL
    COOL[Coolant Nozzle]:::coolant --> WP
    DRE[Coolant Reclamation]:::dre --> COOL
    SPARK[Spark Stream - Material Removal]:::spark
    WHEEL -->|Contact arc l| SPARK
    WP --> SPARK
```
