<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Question structure follows the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**:
> - **Part A:** Short-answer (3 marks each) — typically definitions, classifications, or simple formula applications
> - **Part B:** Choice-based (14 marks each) — usually with sub-parts **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between cylindrical grinding and centerless grinding. List any two industrial applications of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Cylindrical Grinding | Centerless Grinding |
|---|---|---|
| Work holding | Between centers or in chuck | On work rest blade between two wheels |
| Setup time | Higher (centering needed) | Lower (no centering) |
| Accuracy | High (±0.0025 mm) | Slightly lower (±0.005 mm) |
| Production rate | Lower | Higher (continuous through-feed possible) |
| Best for | Long slender shafts | Small pins, bushings, high-volume |

**Applications:**
- *Cylindrical:* Crankshaft journals, spindle shafts, bearing rollers
- *Centerless:* Piston pins, hydraulic shafts, valve stems

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define G-ratio in grinding. What is its significance? What is the typical range for conventional aluminum-oxide wheels grinding hardened steel?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

G-ratio is defined as the volume of workpiece material removed per unit volume of grinding wheel worn away:

$$
G = \dfrac{Z_w}{Z_s}
$$

**Significance:** G-ratio is the primary *wheel-life metric*. A high G-ratio means the wheel lasts longer, reducing downtime, dressing frequency, and cost-per-piece.

**Typical range for Al₂O₃ on hardened steel: G = 10 – 50** (i.e., 1 mm³ of wheel wears away for every 10–50 mm³ of steel removed). For CBN or Diamond on hardened steel, G can be **200 – 1000**.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A** `[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme Pattern]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **principle and working of surface grinding** operation. State the four main movements involved and derive the **material removal rate (MRR)**.

**(b) [7 marks]** A surface grinding operation is performed on a mild-steel workpiece of width 60 mm. The grinding wheel diameter is 200 mm, wheel speed 1500 rpm, depth of cut 0.02 mm, and table speed 12 m/min. Calculate:
1. Wheel surface velocity
2. MRR
3. Specific energy if the spindle motor draws 3 kW
4. Equivalent chip thickness

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Principle of Surface Grinding:**
A horizontal-spindle surface grinder has the grinding wheel mounted on a horizontal spindle. The wheel periphery contacts the top surface of the workpiece. The workpiece is clamped to a magnetic chuck fixed on a reciprocating table. Coolant is sprayed at the contact zone.

**Four movements:**
1. **Primary motion (wheel rotation):** $v_s$ — high speed cutting action
2. **Secondary motion (table reciprocation):** $v_t$ — feeds workpiece under wheel
3. **Cross-feed:** $w_f$ — moves workpiece across wheel after each stroke
4. **Vertical infeed:** $d$ — sets depth of cut

**Derivation of MRR (already shown in §3.1):**

$$
Z_w = w \cdot d \cdot v_t
$$

Where:
- $w$ = width of workpiece (= width of wheel contact) (mm)
- $d$ = depth of cut (mm)
- $v_t$ = table speed (mm/s)

**Sketch (Mermaid-equivalent block):**

```
  ┌──────────┐
  │  WHEEL   │ ← rotates at Ns
  │  ↺       │   
  └────┬─────┘
       │ ▼ d (infeed)
═══════╪═══════════════════  ← Spark
  ┌──────────┐
  │ WORKPIECE│
  └──────────┘
  ┌──────────┐
  │ CHUCK    │
  └────┬─────┘
   ←── TABLE ──→ (reciprocates at vt)
```

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Given:**
- $w$ = 60 mm
- $D_s$ = 200 mm = 0.2 m
- $N_s$ = 1500 rpm
- $d$ = 0.02 mm
- $v_t$ = 12 m/min = **200 mm/s**
- $P$ = 3 kW = 3000 W

**(1) Wheel surface velocity:**

$$
v_s = \dfrac{\pi D_s N_s}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.2 \times 1500}{60}
$$

$$
v_s = \dfrac{942.48}{60} \approx 15.71 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(2) MRR:**

$$
Z_w = w \cdot d \cdot v_t = 60 \times 0.02 \times 200
$$

$$
Z_w = 240 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}
$$

**(3) Specific energy:**

$$
u = \dfrac{P}{Z_w} = \dfrac{3000}{240} = 12.5 \text{ J/mm}^3
$$

**(4) Equivalent chip thickness:**

$$
h_{eq} = \dfrac{v_w \cdot \sqrt{d}}{v_s \cdot \sqrt{D_s}} \quad \text{— but } v_w = v_t \text{ (table feed) in surface grinding}
$$

$$
h_{eq} = \dfrac{200 \cdot \sqrt{0.02}}{15.71 \times 1000 \cdot \sqrt{200}}
$$

*Note: convert to consistent units (mm and mm/s).*

$$
v_s = 15708 \text{ mm/s}, \quad \sqrt{0.02} = 0.1414, \quad \sqrt{200} = 14.142
$$

$$
h_{eq} = \dfrac{200 \times 0.1414}{15708 \times 14.142} = \dfrac{28.28}{222,148} \approx 1.27 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mm} = 0.127 \,\mu\text{m}
$$

**Final Answers:**
1. $v_s \approx 15.71$ m/s
2. $Z_w = 240$ mm³/s
3. $u = 12.5$ J/mm³
4. $h_{eq} \approx 0.127$ µm

---

#### **QUESTION B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme Pattern]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **principle, setup, and working of centerless grinding**. With a sketch, describe the **through-feed** and **infeed (plunge)** methods. State the role of the regulating wheel and the work rest blade.

**(b) [7 marks]** A centerless grinding operation is used to finish 25 mm diameter pins. The grinding wheel diameter is 350 mm running at 1900 rpm, the regulating wheel is 250 mm at 60 rpm with a tilt angle of 3°. Calculate:
1. Surface speed of the grinding wheel
2. Surface speed of the regulating wheel
3. Workpiece feed velocity
4. Approximate MRR if the radial infeed is 0.015 mm/pass

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Principle of Centerless Grinding:**
The workpiece is **not** held by centers or chucks. Instead, it sits on a thin **work rest blade** between two wheels whose axes are parallel and slightly inclined:

1. **Grinding wheel** (larger, harder, faster, e.g., 1200–1800 m/min): does the cutting
2. **Regulating wheel** (smaller, softer rubber/ceramic bond, slower, e.g., 50–200 m/min): rotates the workpiece by friction and provides axial feed

**Through-feed method:**
- Workpieces are fed continuously from one end to the other
- Regulating wheel is tilted (1°–3°) so its surface velocity has an axial component
- Suited for straight cylindrical parts (pins, rods)

**Infeed (Plunge) method:**
- Regulating wheel has **no tilt** (or very small) — purely rotates the workpiece
- The grinding wheel is **plunged radially** to grind a shoulder or form
- Suited for parts with a head, flange, or multiple diameters

**Role of components:**
- *Work rest blade:* Supports the workpiece; its angle (typically 30° above horizontal) determines workpiece center height
- *Regulating wheel:* Provides both rotation and axial feed
- *Tilt angle α* (typically 0°–3°): controls feed rate $v_f = v_r \cdot \sin\alpha$

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Given:**
- $D_w$ = 25 mm = 0.025 m
- $D_g$ = 350 mm = 0.35 m (grinding wheel)
- $N_g$ = 1900 rpm
- $D_r$ = 250 mm = 0.25 m (regulating wheel)
- $N_r$ = 60 rpm
- $\alpha$ = 3°
- $f_r$ = 0.015 mm/rev (radial infeed)

**(1) Surface speed of grinding wheel:**

$$
v_g = \dfrac{\pi D_g N_g}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.35 \times 1900}{60}
$$

$$
v_g = \dfrac{2089.6}{60} \approx 34.83 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(2) Surface speed of regulating wheel:**

$$
v_r = \dfrac{\pi D_r N_r}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.25 \times 60}{60}
$$

$$
v_r = \dfrac{47.12}{60} \approx 0.785 \text{ m/s} \; (47.12 \text{ m/min})
$$

**(3) Workpiece feed velocity (axial):**

$$
v_f = v_r \cdot \sin\alpha = 0.785 \times \sin(3°)
$$

$$
\sin 3° = 0.0523
$$

$$
v_f = 0.785 \times 0.0523 \approx 0.0411 \text{ m/s} \; (41.1 \text{ mm/s})
$$

**(4) MRR:**
The workpiece rotates at the regulating wheel surface speed (pitch-line assumption, ignoring slip):

$$
N_w \approx N_r \cdot \dfrac{D_r}{D_w} = 60 \times \dfrac{250}{25} = 600 \text{ rpm}
$$

MRR for cylindrical-type operation:

$$
Z_w = \pi D_w \cdot f_r \cdot v_f
$$

$$
Z_w = \pi \times 25 \times 0.015 \times 41.1
$$

$$
Z_w \approx 48.4 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Final Answers:**
1. $v_g \approx 34.83$ m/s
2. $v_r \approx 0.785$ m/s
3. $v_f \approx 41.1$ mm/s
4. $Z_w \approx 48.4$ mm³/s

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read This Carefully:**
> 1. **Unit conversions in numerical problems** are the #1 mark-loser. Convert m/min → mm/s, rpm → rev/s, kW → W *before* substituting. Most lost marks come from mixing mm³/s with cm³/s.
> 2. **Don't write $Z_w = \pi D_w f_r v_t$ for surface grinding** — that formula is for cylindrical. For surface grinding, it is $w \cdot d \cdot v_t$.
> 3. **Skipping the sketch** in a 7-mark question costs at least 1–2 marks. Even a simple block diagram earns full credit.
> 4. **In centerless grinding**, students often forget the **tilt angle**; if α = 0, there is **no axial feed**, only rotation.
> 5. **Specific energy** must be quoted in **J/mm³** (not J/m³) — examiners will deduct a mark for unit error.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Grinding = abrasive machining using bonded wheel; chips are **50–1000× smaller** than turning chips.
- ✅ **Six major types:** Surface, Cylindrical, Centerless, Internal, Tool & Cutter, Special.
- ✅ **Three principal movements:** Wheel rotation $v_s$, Workpiece motion $v_w$, Infeed $d$.
- ✅ **MRR — Surface:** $Z_w = w \cdot d \cdot v_t$
- ✅ **MRR — Cylindrical/Internal:** $Z_w = \pi D_w \cdot f_r \cdot v_t$
- ✅ **MRR — Centerless:** $Z_w = \pi D_w \cdot f \cdot v_r$
- ✅ **Wheel speed:** $v_s = \dfrac{\pi D_s N_s}{60}$ (m/s)
- ✅ **G-ratio:** $G = Z_w / Z_s$ — higher is better; Diamond/CBN ≫ Al₂O₃/SiC.
- ✅ **Specific energy:** $u = P / Z_w$ — grinding is **5–50× more energy-intensive** than turning.
- ✅ **Equivalent chip thickness:** $h_{eq} = \dfrac{v_w \sqrt{d}}{v_s \sqrt{D_s}}$ — controls surface finish.
- ✅ **Centerless key idea:** Two wheels + work rest blade; regulating wheel tilt = axial feed.
- ✅ **Creep-feed grinding** = high $d$ (1–10 mm), low $v_w$ — single-pass profile.
- ✅ **Surface finish range:** Ra = 0.1 – 1.6 µm in grinding.
- ✅ **Tolerance range:** ±0.0005 to ±0.010 mm depending on operation.
- ✅ **Coolant is mandatory** in grinding to prevent thermal damage to workpiece and wheel.
- ✅ **Wheel specifications** = Abrasive type + Grain size + Grade + Structure + Bond (e.g., 38A60H8V).
- ✅ Always **dress the wheel** before precision grinding to expose fresh sharp grains.
- ✅ **KTU-favorite numericals** = surface/cylindrical MRR + specific energy + chip thickness; centerless tilt angle + feed.
- ✅ **Don't forget units** — convert everything to mm and seconds before plugging in!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: NC and CNC Machines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT404 - MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** NC and CNC Machines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of NC and CNC Machines

## 1.1 Numerical Control (NC) — The Formal KTU Definition

**Numerical Control (NC)** is a method of automatic operating and controlling machine tools by means of **coded numerical data** (alphanumeric instructions) that are processed by a dedicated control unit (MCU) to command the machine's slides, spindles, and auxiliary functions in a predetermined sequence without manual intervention of the operator.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note (KTU Board Favourite):** NC was pioneered at the **Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT)** in **1952** by John T. Parsons and Frank L. Stulen, in collaboration with the U.S. Air Force, for machining complex aircraft components. The first prototype was a Cincinnati Hydrotel vertical milling machine retrofitted with servos and a punched-tape reader.

## 1.2 Computer Numerical Control (CNC) — The Modern Definition

**Computer Numerical Control (CNC)** is the advanced evolution of NC in which the dedicated hardwired logic controller is replaced by a **microcomputer (or microprocessor-based controller)** that interprets the part program stored in its memory, executes the motion commands through servo drives, and continuously monitors feedback to ensure dimensional accuracy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** CNC is a subset of "Soft-Wired NC." The transition from hard-wired NC to CNC occurred in the **mid-1970s**, and modern CNC machines are essentially "mechatronic systems" combining mechanics, electronics, hydraulics, pneumatics, and software.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Smart Robot Chef"

Imagine a skilled but **deaf-mute chef** who is an absolute expert in cooking but cannot hear verbal instructions. To get him to cook, you must hand him a **printed recipe card** with exact, pre-decided instructions — *"Add 200g of flour, stir clockwise 5 times, set flame to medium, bake 18 minutes."* He follows the card **blindly** but executes perfectly.

- **The chef** = the machine tool (milling, lathe, etc.)
- **The recipe card** = the part program (G-code / M-code)
- **You writing the recipe** = the part programmer
- **The chef's brain (still his own)** = the NC's hardwired logic
- **Now, replace the chef's brain with a smart tablet that can edit, optimize, and store 10,000 recipes** = the CNC controller

**This is the essence of NC → CNC evolution: replace fixed logic with a programmable computer brain.**

## 1.4 Core Technical Metrics (Memorize for KTU)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Positioning accuracy** | $A_p$ | $\pm 0.005$ mm to $\pm 0.02$ mm |
| **Repeatability** | $R$ | $\pm 0.0025$ mm to $\pm 0.01$ mm |
| **Resolution** | $r$ | **0.001 mm** (1 micron) standard |
| **Number of axes** | $n$ | 3 to 5 (up to 12 in 5-axis) |
| **Spindle speed range** | $N$ | **0–10,000 rpm** (up to 60,000 rpm) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Repeatability** is the ability of a machine to return to a previously commanded position under identical conditions, while **Accuracy** is the ability to reach the *exact* commanded position. CNC machines are typically **20–30 times more repeatable than they are accurate**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of NC/CNC Systems (The Family Tree)

**A. Based on Control Loop (KTU Frequently Asked):**

1. **Open-Loop System (NC):**
   - No feedback sensor.
   - Uses stepper motors.
   - Cheaper, but positioning error accumulates.
   - Used in low-cost machines, small EDM, wire cutting.
   - *Example:* Small desktop CNC router, hobby plotter.

2. **Semi-Closed-Loop System (CNC):**
   - Feedback only from the motor shaft (rotary encoder on servo motor).
   - Most common commercial configuration.
   - Mechanical backlash and leadscrew errors NOT corrected.

3. **Closed-Loop System (CNC):**
   - Feedback from **linear scales** mounted directly on the slide.
   - Highest accuracy.
   - Most expensive, used in precision machining centers.
   - Compensates for thermal drift, backlash, and leadscrew errors.

**B. Based on Motion Type:**

- **Point-to-Point (PTP) Control** — Tool moves to discrete positions; path is irrelevant. E.g., drilling, tapping, punching.
- **Straight-Cut Control** — Tool moves only along parallel axes. E.g., simple milling of rectangular pockets.
- **Contouring (Continuous Path) Control** — Tool follows a continuous 3D curve. E.g., circular interpolation in turning, complex die-mold surfaces.

## 2.2 Architecture of a CNC System

A CNC machine has **three fundamental subsystems:**

| Subsystem | Function | KTU Example |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Program of Instructions** | Stores the part program in alphanumeric codes (G-code/M-code) | Punched tape, USB, Ethernet |
| **2. Machine Control Unit (MCU)** | Decodes instructions, interpolates path, commands drives | Fanuc, Siemens, Mitsubishi, Haas controller |
| **3. Machine Tool (with Feedback)** | Physical slide/spindle that performs cutting | 3-axis VMC, CNC turning center |

## 2.3 Axes Nomenclature (Right-Hand Rule — KTU Mandatory)

The **3 Primary Linear Axes** follow the **Right-Hand Rule**:

- **X-axis:** Longest, primary feed direction (typically along spindle to column on mills; radial on lathes).
- **Y-axis:** Orthogonal to X and Z (perpendicular to spindle axis on mills).
- **Z-axis:** Parallel to the **spindle axis** (perpendicular to tool-holder mounting surface).

**3 Rotary Axes:**
- **A-axis:** Rotation around X.
- **B-axis:** Rotation around Y.
- **C-axis:** Rotation around Z.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Trap:** On a CNC lathe, **X is radial (diameter direction) and Z is axial (longitudinal)**. On a CNC mill, **Z is vertical (parallel to spindle)**. Students frequently confuse these.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory formulas for KTU Board Numerical Problems — 14-mark questions often test these.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Spindle Speed | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot v}{\pi \cdot D}$ | rpm | Sets the cutting velocity for a given tool/workpiece diameter |
| 2 | Cutting Velocity | $v = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | m/min | Tool manufacturer's recommended $v$ is the design input |
| 3 | Feed Rate (table) | $f_r = f \cdot z \cdot N$ | mm/min | Used for programming the F-code in CNC |
| 4 | Feed per Tooth | $f_z$ | mm/tooth | Given in tool catalog |
| 5 | Number of Teeth | $z$ | – | Milling cutter parameter |
| 6 | Material Removal Rate (MRR) | $MRR = v \cdot f \cdot a_p$ | mm³/min | Productivity metric for KTU cost calculations |
| 7 | Linear Interpolation End Point | $P_{end} = P_{start} + (X, Y, Z)$ displacement | mm | G01 command |
| 8 | Circular Interpolation Radius | $R = \dfrac{\sqrt{(X_e - X_s)^2 + (Y_e - Y_s)^2}}{2}$ | mm | G02/G03 command |
| 9 | Positioning Time (PTP) | $t_p = \dfrac{L}{v_r} + t_a$ | s | Open-loop speed analysis |
| 10 | Machining Time | $t_m = \dfrac{L}{f_r}$ | min | Used in cycle-time calculations |

**Where:**
- $v$ = cutting speed (m/min)
- $D$ = tool/workpiece diameter (mm)
- $f$ = feed per revolution (mm/rev) or per tooth (mm/tooth)
- $a_p$ = depth of cut (mm)
- $L$ = length of cut (mm)
- $v_r$ = rapid traverse (mm/min)
- $t_a$ = acceleration time (s)

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Why CNC Matters

- **Repeatability** enables mass production of identical parts (e.g., 1 million pistons with $\pm 0.01$ mm tolerance).
- **Flexibility** — same machine can produce 10,000 different parts via program changeover.
- **Complex geometry** — 5-axis simultaneous machining of turbine blades, medical implants.
- **Reduced lead time** — from design (CAD) → toolpath (CAM) → production in hours.
- **Cost savings** in skilled labor and scrap reduction.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Spindle Speed from Cutting Velocity

**Given:** Cutting velocity $v$ (m/min) and tool diameter $D$ (mm).
**Required:** Spindle speed $N$ in rpm.

**Step 1:** The cutting velocity is the tangential speed at the cutting edge. For a rotating tool of diameter $D$, the circumference traversed per revolution is $\pi D$ (in mm).

**Step 2:** The distance traversed per minute is $(\pi D \times N)$, where $N$ is in revolutions per minute. This must equal the cutting velocity $v$ converted to consistent units (mm/min):

$$v \;\; (\text{m/min}) = 1000 \cdot v \;\; (\text{mm/min})$$

**Step 3:** Equating the linear speed of the cutting edge to the prescribed cutting velocity:

$$1000 \cdot v = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$$

**Step 4:** Solving for $N$:

$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot v}{\pi \cdot D}$$

**Step 5:** **Units check:** $\dfrac{\text{m/min} \cdot \text{mm (after 1000 conversion)}}{\text{mm}} = \text{min}^{-1} = \text{rpm}$ ✓

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A Ø20 mm HSS end mill is used to cut mild steel with a recommended cutting speed $v = 30$ m/min. Calculate the spindle speed.

**Solution:**

$$N = \frac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 20}$$

$$N = \frac{30{,}000}{62.832}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 477.5 \text{ rpm} \;\; (\text{round to } 480 \text{ rpm on machine})}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Feed Rate Programming

**Given:** A 4-flute end mill, feed per tooth $f_z = 0.05$ mm/tooth, spindle speed $N = 480$ rpm.

**Step 1:** Total feed per revolution = feed per tooth × number of teeth:

$$f_{rev} = f_z \cdot z = 0.05 \times 4 = 0.20 \text{ mm/rev}$$

**Step 2:** Table feed per minute = feed per revolution × spindle speed:

$$f_r = f_{rev} \cdot N = 0.20 \times 480 = 96 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Step 3:** The CNC program line (G-code) would be:

$$\text{F96} \quad \text{(programmed as F96.0)}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Circular Interpolation End-Point Coordinates

**Problem:** A semicircular profile is to be machined. Start point $P_s = (X_s, Y_s) = (30, 0)$, end point $P_e = (X_e, Y_e) = (50, 0)$, counterclockwise (G03), radius $R = 10$ mm. Find the center.

**Step 1:** For a semicircle, the center lies on the perpendicular bisector of the chord $P_s P_e$, at the midpoint.

**Step 2:** Midpoint of chord:

$$X_m = \frac{X_s + X_e}{2} = \frac{30 + 50}{2} = 40$$

$$Y_m = \frac{Y_s + Y_e}{2} = \frac{0 + 0}{2} = 0$$

**Step 3:** Distance from midpoint to either endpoint:

$$d = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{(X_e - X_s)^2 + (Y_e - Y_s)^2}$$

$$d = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{(50-30)^2 + (0-0)^2} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{400} = 10$$

**Step 4:** For a **semicircle**, the center is at distance $R$ from the midpoint, perpendicular to the chord. Since the chord is along the X-axis and motion is counterclockwise, the center is at:

$$(X_c, Y_c) = (X_m, \; Y_m + R) = (40, \; 0 + 10) = (40, 10)$$

**Step 5:** Verify: distance from center to $P_s$:

$$\sqrt{(40-30)^2 + (10-0)^2} = \sqrt{100 + 100} = \sqrt{200} \approx 14.14 \text{ mm}$$

For a true semicircle, the chord length must equal $2R$. Here chord $= 20$ mm, $2R = 20$ mm ✓.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table (High-Yield for KTU)

| Feature | NC | CNC | DNC | MNC |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | Numerical Control | Computer Numerical Control | Direct Numerical Control | Manufacturing (Cell) NC |
| **Controller Type** | Hard-wired logic | Microcomputer | Central host + multiple CNC | FMS / CIM-integrated |
| **Storage** | Punched tape (one-time use) | RAM/ROM (editable) | Hard disk of host | Network database |
| **Program Editing** | Impossible | Possible at machine | Done at host | Done at central server |
| **Feedback** | Open-loop (stepper) | Open / Semi-closed / Closed | Depends on CNC type | Closed-loop with adaptive control |
| **Flexibility** | Very Low | High | Very High | Highest |
| **Cost** | Lowest (obsolete) | Medium | High | Very High |
| **Reliability** | High (no software) | Software bugs possible | Network-dependent | System-failure critical |
| **Year Introduced** | 1952 | 1970s | 1970s | 1980s–1990s |

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Part Programming Logic (KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

**The 7 Logical Blocks of a Part Program:**

| Block | Function | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1. **Program Header** | Identifies program | `O1001;` |
| 2. **Safety Block** | Safe start, absolute mode | `G90 G80 G40 G17;` |
| 3. **Tool Change** | Selects tool, sets spindle | `T01 M06; S1500 M03;` |
| 4. **Approach Move** | Rapid to clearance | `G00 X0 Y0 Z50;` |
| 5. **Cutting Move** | Engagement with workpiece | `G01 Z-5.0 F100;` |
| 6. **Contour / Profile** | Interpolation moves | `G01 X20 Y10; G02 ...` |
| 7. **Retract & End** | Return home, stop | `G00 Z100; M05; M30;` |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trap:** Students often forget the **"Safety Block"** and **"Cutter Compensation"** (G40/G41/G42). The examiner will deduct 2 marks for skipping G90/G17 initialization.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — CNC Machine Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef input fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    classDef controller fill:#c00000,stroke:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    classDef output fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    classDef feedback fill:#f57c00,stroke:#f57c00,color:#ffffff
    classDef physical fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#ffffff

    prog[PART PROGRAM G CODE M CODE]:::input
    edit[OFFLINE EDITING DNC NETWORK]:::input
    cadcam[CAD CAM SOFTWARE]:::input

    cnc[CNC CONTROLLER MCU MICROCOMPUTER]:::controller
    interp[INTERPOLATOR AND PATH GENERATOR]:::controller
    servo[SERVO AMPLIFIER AND DRIVE]:::controller
    spindleDrv[SPINDLE DRIVE INVERTER]:::controller

    xAxis[X AXIS SERVO MOTOR AND BALLSCREW]:::output
    yAxis[Y AXIS SERVO MOTOR AND BALLSCREW]:::output
    zAxis[Z AXIS SERVO MOTOR AND BALLSCREW]:::output
    spdl[SPINDLE MOTOR AND TOOL HOLDER]:::output

    machine[PHYSICAL MACHINE TOOL VMC LATHE]:::physical

    enc1[ENCODER ON X MOTOR SEMI CLOSED]:::feedback
    enc2[ENCODER ON Y MOTOR SEMI CLOSED]:::feedback
    enc3[ENCODER ON Z MOTOR SEMI CLOSED]:::feedback
    linScale[LINEAR SCALE ON X SLIDE CLOSED LOOP]:::feedback

    prog --> cnc
    edit --> cnc
    cadcam --> cnc
    cnc --> interp
    interp --> servo
    servo --> xAxis
    servo --> yAxis
    servo --> zAxis
    cnc --> spindleDrv
    spindleDrv --> spdl
    xAxis --> machine
    yAxis --> machine
    zAxis --> machine
    spdl --> machine
    xAxis -.-> enc1
    yAxis -.-> enc2
    zAxis -.-> enc3
    machine -.-> linScale
    enc1 -.-> servo
    enc2 -.-> servo
    enc3 -.-> servo
    linScale -.-> cnc
```

## 4.2 Flowchart — Part Programming Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[STEP 1 ANALYZE THE DRAWING]:::step1
    B[STEP 2 SELECT MACHINE AND FIXTURE]:::step2
    C[STEP 3 SELECT TOOLS AND CUTTING PARAMETERS]:::step3
    D[STEP 4 DETERMINE WORK ZERO OFFSET]:::step4
    E[STEP 5 WRITE PART PROGRAM G CODE]:::step5
    F[STEP 6 SIMULATE GRAPHICALLY]:::step6
    G[STEP 7 DRY RUN ON MACHINE]:::step7
    H[STEP 8 FIRST ARTICLE INSPECTION]:::step8
    I[STEP 9 PRODUCTION RUN]:::step9

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H --> I
    I -->|Defect| A

    classDef step2 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000000

    classDef step8 fill:#fbe9e7,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000000
    classDef step9 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1{IS HIGH ACCURACY REQUIRED}:::q
    Q2{IS BUDGET TIGHT}:::q
    Q3{IS LOAD VARIABLE}:::q

    SEMI[SEMI CLOSED LOOP SERVO AND ENCODER]:::semiStyle
    CLOSED[CLOSED LOOP SERVO AND LINEAR SCALE]:::closedStyle

    Q1 -->|NO| Q2
    Q1 -->|YES| Q3
    Q2 -->|YES| OPEN
    Q2 -->|NO| SEMI
    Q3 -->|NO| SEMI
    Q3 -->|YES| CLOSED

```

## 4.4 Interpolation Types (Comparison Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LINEAR[LINEAR INTERPOLATION G01]
        L1[STRAIGHT LINE BETWEEN TWO POINTS]
        L2[FEED CONTROLLED BY F CODE]
        L3[USED FOR SQUARE AND RECTANGULAR POCKETS]
    end

    subgraph CIRCULAR[CIRCULAR INTERPOLATION G02 G03]
        C1[G02 IS CLOCKWISE CW]
        C2[G03 IS COUNTER CLOCKWISE CCW]

        C4[USED FOR HOLES ARCS AND CIRCLES]
    end

    subgraph HELICAL[HELICAL INTERPOLATION]

        H2[USED FOR THREAD MILLING AND TAP HOLES]
    end

    LINEAR --> CIRCULAR --> HELICAL
```

For KTU, students should be able to draw a simplified block diagram showing **Input → MCU → Servo → Machine → Feedback Sensor → MCU** as the canonical feedback loop.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define CNC. Differentiate between NC and CNC machines in any 3 aspects.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Computer Numerical Control (CNC) is an NC system in which a dedicated microcomputer replaces hard-wired logic to control machine tool operations through a stored part program.

| Aspect | NC | CNC |
|---|---|---|
| **Controller** | Hard-wired logic circuits (1 mark) | Microcomputer with stored program (1 mark) |
| **Program Storage** | Punched tape (one-time) | RAM/ROM/USB (editable, reusable) |
| **Flexibility** | Cannot modify program at machine | Can edit, optimize, and store multiple programs |

**(1 mark for any valid 3rd point, e.g., feedback, cost, diagnostics.)**

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any three G-codes and M-codes commonly used in CNC part programming, with their functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark per correct entry):**

| Code | Function | Category |
|---|---|---|
| **G00** | Rapid positioning (rapid traverse to a point) | G-code |
| **G01** | Linear interpolation with controlled feed (F code) | G-code |
| **G02** | Circular interpolation — Clockwise (CW) | G-code |
| **G03** | Circular interpolation — Counter-Clockwise (CCW) | G-code |
| **M03** | Spindle ON (clockwise rotation) | M-code |
| **M05** | Spindle STOP | M-code |
| **M08** | Coolant ON (flood) | M-code |
| **M30** | Program END and rewind | M-code |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **INSTRUCTION (KTU 2024 Pattern):** *Answer ANY ONE full question from the pair. Each has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.*

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]**

**(a) Explain the classification of NC/CNC systems based on the control loop. With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an open-loop system. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Classification (3 marks):**
1. **Open-loop system:** No feedback from output to input. Uses **stepper motors**. Errors due to load variation, backlash, and motor stall are not corrected.
2. **Semi-closed loop system:** Feedback taken from a **rotary encoder on the servo motor shaft**. Compensates for motor errors but not for mechanical errors (leadscrew, backlash).
3. **Closed-loop system:** Feedback taken from a **linear scale (LVDT/glass scale) directly on the machine slide**. Highest accuracy; corrects all errors including thermal drift.

**Block Diagram of Open-Loop System (3 marks):**

$$\text{Controller (MCU)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Signal Amplifier} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Stepper Motor} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Ball Screw} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Slide/Table}$$

(There is **no feedback path** — distinguishing feature.)

**Working (1 mark):** The MCU sends a pulse train to the stepper motor driver. Each pulse rotates the motor by a fixed angle (step angle, e.g., 1.8° = 200 steps/rev). The ball screw converts rotary motion to linear motion, positioning the slide. The system **assumes** the slide has moved to the commanded position.

**(b) A Ø30 mm HSS end mill (4 teeth) is used to machine a mild steel workpiece. Recommended cutting speed $v = 25$ m/min, feed per tooth $f_z = 0.08$ mm/tooth. Calculate: (i) Spindle speed, (ii) Feed rate, (iii) MRR for $a_p = 3$ mm. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $D = 30$ mm, $z = 4$, $v = 25$ m/min, $f_z = 0.08$ mm/tooth, $a_p = 3$ mm.

**(i) Spindle Speed (3 marks):**

$$N = \frac{1000 \times v}{\pi \times D} = \frac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 30}$$

$$N = \frac{25{,}000}{94.248} \approx 265.26 \text{ rpm}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 265 \text{ rpm} \;\; (\text{round to 270 rpm})}$$

**(ii) Feed Rate (2 marks):**

$$f_r = f_z \times z \times N = 0.08 \times 4 \times 265$$

$$\boxed{f_r \approx 84.8 \text{ mm/min}}$$

**(iii) Material Removal Rate (2 marks):**

$$MRR = v \times f \times a_p$$

Note: $v$ here is cutting speed (m/min). To use mm/min, convert: $v = 25 \times 1000 = 25{,}000$ mm/min.

Alternatively, $MRR = (\pi D \cdot N) \times f_z \cdot z \times a_p$ (more direct).

$$MRR = \pi \times 30 \times 265 \times 0.08 \times 4 \times 3 / 1000$$

$$MRR = \frac{\pi \times 30 \times 265 \times 0.96}{1000}$$

$$MRR = \frac{23{,}966.4}{1000} \approx 23.97 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}$$

Or equivalently: $\boxed{MRR \approx 23{,}970 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 23.97 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}}$

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, May 2023]**

**(a) Explain the working principle of a CNC machining center with a block diagram. List the major components of the CNC machine. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle (4 marks):** A CNC machining center receives a part program (G-code/M-code) via DNC network, USB, or manual entry. The CNC controller (a microprocessor-based system) decodes the blocks, performs interpolation to generate smooth tool paths, and issues command signals to the servo amplifiers. These drive the X, Y, Z servo motors (and rotary axes if 5-axis), which rotate the ball screws to position the slides precisely. The spindle motor (driven by a VFD/inverter) rotates the tool at the programmed RPM. Coolant valves, tool changers, and pallet changers are controlled by PLC logic. Position feedback from encoders/linear scales is continuously compared with the commanded position, and corrections are issued (closed-loop).

**Block Diagram (already shown in Section 4.1 — KTU 1 mark for that).**

**Major Components (3 marks — 1 mark per group, ½ mark each item):**

1. **Input devices:** DNC port, USB, RS-232, Ethernet
2. **CNC controller:** Microprocessor, memory (RAM/ROM), interpolator, PLC
3. **Drive system:** Servo amplifiers, spindle inverter, stepper/servo motors
4. **Machine tool:** Bed, column, spindle, slides, ball screws, ATC, APC
5. **Feedback sensors:** Encoders, linear scales, resolvers, proximity switches

**(b) Explain the concept of interpolation in CNC. Differentiate between linear and circular interpolation. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept of Interpolation (3 marks):** Interpolation is the mathematical process of generating intermediate coordinate points along a desired tool path between the start point and the end point. The CNC controller performs real-time trajectory planning using these intermediate points to produce smooth, continuous motion. There are three primary types: **linear, circular, and helical**.

**Linear Interpolation (2 marks):**
- Tool moves in a **straight line** from current position to programmed end point.
- All axes move at **proportional feed rates** so that the resultant vector velocity equals the programmed F value.
- Achieved via **G01** command.
- E.g., `G01 X50 Y30 F100;` — move linearly to (50, 30) at 100 mm/min.

**Circular Interpolation (2 marks):**
- Tool moves along a **circular arc** of specified radius, center, and direction.
- **G02** = clockwise; **G03** = counter-clockwise.
- Requires specifying **end point** (X, Y) and either **R** (radius) or **I, J, K** (center offset from start point).
- E.g., `G02 X50 Y30 I20 J0 F80;` — CW arc to (50, 30) with center 20 mm in +X from start.

**Differences (1 mark summary):**

| Aspect | Linear (G01) | Circular (G02/G03) |
|---|---|---|
| Path | Straight line | Circular arc |
| Required Address | X, Y, Z, F | X, Y, I/J/K or R, F |
| Difficulty | 1-axis at a time (interpolated) | 2-axis simultaneous (X and Y) |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency (–2 marks):** Mixing $v$ in m/min with $D$ in mm without multiplying by 1000. **Always write the conversion step explicitly.**
> 2. **Wrong G02/G03 direction (–1 mark):** Direction is judged **looking from the +Z axis** (top view for horizontal mills; looking from spindle to chuck for lathes). Students flip this.
> 3. **Forgetting to round $N$ to nearest available spindle speed (–1 mark):** Real machines have discrete RPM values; 477.5 rpm becomes **480 rpm** on the dial.
> 4. **Skipping the "Safety Block" in programs (–2 marks):** KTU mandates G90/G17/G40/G80 initialization.
> 5. **Confusing X/Y/Z axes between mill and lathe (–2 marks):** On a lathe, X is **radial**, Z is **axial**, and Y is absent. On a mill, Z is **vertical (spindle axis)**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print and pin this checklist to your study wall.**

- [x] **NC = Hard-wired logic + punched tape** (obsolete since 1970s).
- [x] **CNC = Microcomputer + editable program** (modern standard).
- [x] **DNC = Central host computer + multiple CNC machines networked** (no tape at machine).
- [x] **MNC = Manufacturing cell with automated material handling + CNC + robots** (FMS precursor).
- [x] **Open loop** uses **stepper motors** (no feedback, errors accumulate).
- [x] **Closed loop** uses **servo motors + linear scales** (highest accuracy).
- [x] **Right-Hand Rule** defines +X, +Y, +Z. Z is always parallel to the **spindle axis**.
- [x] **G00** = rapid; **G01** = linear cut; **G02** = CW arc; **G03** = CCW arc.
- [x] **M03** = spindle CW; **M05** = spindle OFF; **M08** = coolant ON; **M30** = program end.
- [x] **Absolute programming = G90**; **Incremental = G91**.
- [x] **Cutter compensation:** G40 = cancel, G41 = left, G42 = right.
- [x] **Work coordinate setting:** G54–G59 (selectable zero offsets).
- [x] **Spindle speed formula:** $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot v}{\pi \cdot D}$
- [x] **Feed rate formula:** $f_r = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$
- [x] **MRR formula:** $MRR = v \cdot f \cdot a_p$ (with consistent units in mm³/min)
- [x] **NC developed at MIT, 1952** by Parsons & Stulen (3 marks guaranteed if asked).
- [x] **Resolution** of standard CNC = **0.001 mm (1 micron)**.
- [x] **Repeatability** of CNC = $\pm 0.0025$ mm to $\pm 0.01$ mm.
- [x] **APT (Automatically Programmed Tools)** was the first high-level NC language (1956, MIT).
- [x] **APT statements** use verbs like `GOTO`, `FROM`, `GO/TO`, `TLLFT`, `TLRGT`, `CIRCLE`.
- [x] **5-axis machines** have 3 linear + 2 rotary axes (A, B, or C) — used for turbine blades, impellers, medical implants.
- [x] **Tool length offset (G43/H)** and **cutter diameter offset (G41/G42/D)** are mandatory in modern programming.
- [x] **Modal codes** stay active until canceled; **non-modal codes** apply to one block only.
- [x] **CNC advantages:** Flexibility, accuracy, repeatability, complex geometry, low labor, less scrap.
- [x] **CNC disadvantages:** High initial cost, skilled programmer needed, computer breakdowns halt production, over-engineering for simple parts.

**End of Module 3 — NC and CNC Machines Notes**
