topics.title: Micromachining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT404 - MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Micromachining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Micromachining** is defined as a class of non-conventional manufacturing processes used to produce **micro-scale features, micro-holes, micro-channels, and micro-structures** with feature sizes typically in the range of **1 μm to 999 μm** (sub-millimetre range) on engineering materials, with high dimensional accuracy, low surface roughness (sub-micron Ra), and minimal heat-affected zones (HAZ).

In the KTU 2024 scheme, micromachining is treated as an extension of **non-conventional / advanced manufacturing processes** where the energy source (mechanical, thermal, chemical, or electrochemical) is precisely localized and controlled to remove material at the micro/nano scale. The process is governed by the principle that **specific energy (energy per unit volume removed)** increases dramatically as the size of the cut decreases below a critical threshold, making conventional machining impractical.

The fundamental unit metrics used in KTU examinations are:
- **Feature size (d):** 1–999 μm
- **Surface roughness (Ra):** < 0.1 μm (mirror-class finish)
- **Specific energy (U):** typically **10⁹ – 10¹² J/mm³**
- **Material Removal Rate (MRR):** often in **mm³/min** or **mg/min**

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Surgeon vs. The Carpenter:**
> Imagine a **carpenter** using a chainsaw to fell a tree — fast, powerful, but messy. This is **conventional machining** (high MRR, high force, moderate precision).
> Now imagine a **surgeon** performing eye surgery with a laser — extremely precise, localized, minimal collateral damage, slow but accurate. This is **micromachining** (low MRR, controlled energy beam, ultra-high precision).
> The key insight: as the tool gets smaller, the **force per unit area (pressure) becomes enormous**, so the energy must be delivered through a non-contact or micro-contact mechanism (laser, EDM pulse, ion beam, chemical etch) rather than a macro cutting tool.

Another powerful analogy: writing your name with a **thick marker** (conventional) vs. a **0.1 mm technical pen** (micromachining). The thin pen requires steadier hand, more controlled pressure, and finer ink chemistry — but the result is intricate and precise.

## 1.3 Why Micromachining? — The Driving Need

| Driver | Conventional Machining Limit | Micromachining Solution |
|---|---|---|
| Miniaturization of products (MEMS, micro-sensors) | Tool deflection, chatter | Low cutting forces, high stiffness |
| Hard/brittle materials (Si, ceramics, glass) | Tool wear, cracking | Non-contact energy delivery |
| Micro-features (holes, slots) | Drill breakage, burrs | Abrasive/beam-based removal |
| Burr-free, HAZ-free surfaces | Thermal/mechanical damage | Chemical/electrochemical action |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly categorizes micromachining under **"Advanced/Non-Conventional Manufacturing Processes"** and emphasizes its role in **MEMS fabrication, microelectronics, aerospace, and biomedical implants** (e.g., micro-channels in drug-delivery stents).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Micromachining Processes

Micromachining is broadly classified into **four families** based on the energy mechanism used for material removal. This taxonomy is a **high-weightage KTU exam point**.

### 2.1.1 Mechanical / Abrasive Micromachining
- Material is removed by **micro-scale mechanical impact or abrasion**.
- Examples:
  - **Micro-milling** (tool diameter < 1 mm)
  - **Micro-drilling** (drill diameter < 1 mm)
  - **Micro-grinding / Micro-abrasive jet machining (MAJM)**
  - **Abrasive Flow Machining (AFM)**
  - **Ultrasonic Machining (USM)** — uses abrasive particles in a slurry vibrated at **20–40 kHz**

### 2.1.2 Thermal / Energy-Beam Micromachining
- Material is removed by **melting, vaporization, or ablation** using focused energy.
- Examples:
  - **Laser Beam Micromachining (LBM)** — Nd:YAG, Fiber, Excimer lasers
  - **Electron Beam Micromachining (EBM)**
  - **Focused Ion Beam (FIB)**
  - **Electric Discharge Micromachining (μ-EDM)** — uses pulsed discharges with **< 1 μs pulse-on time**

### 2.1.3 Chemical / Electrochemical Micromachining
- Material is removed by **chemical dissolution or anodic dissolution** — no mechanical force, no thermal damage.
- Examples:
  - **Chemical Micromachining (CMM)** — uses etchants like **KOH, HF, TMAH** for silicon
  - **Electrochemical Micromachining (ECMM)** — uses pulsed DC current in an electrolyte
  - **Photo-chemical / Photo-assisted etching**

### 2.1.4 Hybrid Micromachining
- Combines two energy sources for superior results.
- Examples:
  - **Laser-assisted micro-milling**
  - **ECMM + USM**
  - **EDM + ECM (wire-EDM + electrochemical)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Logic — "Why so many types?"**
> The reason there is no single universal micromachining process is the **material–mechanism compatibility matrix**. For example, glass is transparent to most mechanical forces but absorbs UV (Excimer) laser energy — so **Excimer laser** is preferred. Silicon, on the other hand, etches cleanly in **KOH** (chemical) but resists most mechanical tools due to brittleness.

## 2.2 Operating Principles of Key Processes

### 2.2.1 Micro-EDM (μ-EDM)
- A **pulsed DC voltage** (open-circuit voltage **80–250 V**) is applied between a **micro-tool electrode** (cathode) and the workpiece (anode) immersed in a **dielectric fluid** (typically EDM oil or deionized water).
- Each pulse generates a **spark** at the smallest gap (typically **1–10 μm**), vaporizing a micro-volume of material.
- **Pulse duration (Ton):** 1 μs – 10 μs (for micromachining, much shorter than macro-EDM).
- **MRR** is proportional to discharge energy; **TWR (Tool Wear Rate)** is the main concern — often managed by using **rotating or orbiting tools** or **wire-electrode**.

### 2.2.2 Laser Beam Micromachining (LBM)
- A **focused laser beam** (spot size **5–50 μm**) delivers **high power density (10⁶ – 10¹² W/cm²)**.
- Three regimes of material removal:
  1. **Vaporization regime** (above vaporization temperature) — most common for micro-holes.
  2. **Melting and ejection** — for metals.
  3. **Photo-ablation** (for polymers/biological tissue using Excimer UV laser) — breaks molecular bonds without thermal damage.
- **Nozzle gas** (oxygen, nitrogen, or argon) is used to **assist ejection of molten/vaporized material** and prevent re-deposition.

### 2.2.3 Chemical Micromachining (CMM) / Wet Etching
- Workpiece is **selectively masked** (using photoresist, SiO₂, or Si₃N₄ for silicon) and immersed in an **etchant**.
- Etch rate depends on **crystallographic orientation** (anisotropy) — KOH etches Si(100) ~400× faster than Si(111).
- **Isotropic etchants** (HNA — HF + HNO₃ + CH₃COOH) etch in all directions equally; **anisotropic etchants** (KOH, TMAH, EDP) etch along preferred crystal planes.

### 2.2.4 Ultrasonic Micromachining (USM)
- A **sonotrode / tool** vibrates at **20–40 kHz** with amplitude of **15–50 μm**.
- Abrasive slurry (SiC, B₄C, Al₂O₃, **grit size 5–150 μm**) is fed between the tool and workpiece.
- Material removal is by **hammering of abrasive particles** — the tool itself does not contact the workpiece.
- Best suited for **brittle, non-conductive materials** (glass, ceramics, quartz, germanium, silicon).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the cheat sheet students must memorize for KTU ESE. All units are SI unless otherwise stated.**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Applicable Process |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specific Energy (U) | $U = \dfrac{F_c \cdot v_c}{MRR}$ | $\text{J/mm}^3$ | All machining |
| 2 | MRR (Micro-milling) | $MRR = w \cdot d \cdot f_z \cdot Z \cdot N$ | $\text{mm}^3/\text{min}$ | Mechanical |
| 3 | MRR (μ-EDM) | $MRR = K \cdot V \cdot I \cdot T_{on} \cdot f$ | $\text{mm}^3/\text{min}$ | Thermal |
| 4 | MRR (USM) | $MRR \propto \dfrac{F \cdot A \cdot f}{H_v}$ | $\text{mm}^3/\text{min}$ | Mechanical |
| 5 | MRR (LBM) | $MRR = \dfrac{P}{H_v + L_f + L_v}$ | $\text{mm}^3/\text{s}$ | Thermal |
| 6 | Power density (Laser) | $q = \dfrac{4 P}{\pi d^2}$ | $\text{W/cm}^2$ | LBM |
| 7 | HAZ depth (approx.) | $HAZ \approx \sqrt{\alpha \cdot t}$ | μm | Thermal processes |
| 8 | Etch rate (chemical) | $E_r = \dfrac{\Delta h}{\Delta t}$ | μm/min | CMM |
| 9 | TWR ratio | $TWR = \dfrac{\text{Tool volume loss}}{\text{Workpiece volume loss}} \times 100$ | % | All |
| 10 | Aspect ratio (micro-hole) | $AR = \dfrac{D}{d}$ | dimensionless | LBM, μ-EDM |

Where:
- $F_c$ = cutting force (N), $v_c$ = cutting velocity (mm/s)
- $w$ = width, $d$ = depth, $f_z$ = feed/tooth, $Z$ = teeth, $N$ = RPM
- $K$ = material constant, $V$ = voltage, $I$ = current, $T_{on}$ = pulse-on time, $f$ = pulse frequency
- $F$ = static load, $A$ = amplitude, $f$ = frequency, $H_v$ = Vickers hardness
- $P$ = laser power, $H_v$ = vaporization enthalpy, $L_f, L_v$ = latent heats
- $d$ = laser spot diameter, $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity, $t$ = interaction time
- $D$ = hole depth, $d$ = hole diameter

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Restriction:** In KTU answer sheets and even in this formula sheet, **never write absolute value as `|x|`** — use **$\vert x \vert$** in LaTeX to prevent the markdown table parser from breaking the row layout. This is a common KTU answer-script formatting trap.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems):** accelerometers, gyroscopes, pressure sensors (μ-EDM + CMM).
- **Aerospace:** cooling micro-channels in turbine blades (LBM, μ-EDM).
- **Biomedical:** drug-eluting stents, micro-needles for transdermal drug delivery (Excimer LBM).
- **Electronics:** micro-via holes in PCBs (LBM), wafer dicing (EBM/FIB).
- **Optics:** micro-lens arrays, diffractive optical elements (LBM).
- **Watch / jewellery industry:** micro-engraving, micro-drilling of jewels.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Specific Energy in Micromachining (Foundational)

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Principle:** Specific energy is the energy required to remove a unit volume of material. It is the **inverse of process efficiency** — higher U means lower efficiency, but in micromachining, high U is **accepted as the cost of precision**.

**Step 1 — Define the cutting power**

The cutting power $P_c$ delivered to the workpiece is the product of the tangential cutting force and the cutting velocity:

$$P_c = F_c \cdot v_c \quad \text{[W = N · mm/s]}$$

**Step 2 — Define MRR**

The Material Removal Rate is the volume of material removed per unit time:

$$MRR = A_c \cdot v_c \quad \text{[mm}^3/\text{s]}$$

where $A_c$ is the cross-sectional area of the cut (mm²).

**Step 3 — Divide power by MRR**

Specific energy is the ratio of cutting power to material removal rate:

$$U = \frac{P_c}{MRR} = \frac{F_c \cdot v_c}{A_c \cdot v_c}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify**

Since $v_c$ cancels out (a beautiful result — U is independent of cutting speed at the macro-level), we obtain:

$$U = \frac{F_c}{A_c} \quad \text{[J/mm}^3\text{]}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret for micromachining**

In micromachining, $A_c$ is **extremely small** (order of μm²), so even a tiny $F_c$ produces a huge $U$. For example, if $F_c = 0.1$ N and $A_c = 100$ μm² = $10^{-6}$ mm²:

$$U = \frac{0.1}{10^{-6}} = 10^{5} \;\text{J/mm}^3$$

This explains why **conventional cutting tools fracture** at micro-scales — the specific energy demand exceeds the tool's strength-to-fracture threshold. Hence, **non-contact energy beams** are preferred.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — MRR for Micro-EDM

> [!NOTE]
> **Principle:** Each spark removes a small crater of volume proportional to the discharge energy. The MRR is the product of (a) discharge energy per pulse, (b) number of pulses per unit time, and (c) a material-workpiece constant.

**Step 1 — Energy per pulse**

The energy dissipated in a single discharge pulse is:

$$E_p = V \cdot I \cdot T_{on} \quad \text{[J]}$$

where $V$ = discharge voltage (V), $I$ = discharge current (A), $T_{on}$ = pulse-on time (s).

**Step 2 — Volume removed per pulse**

Empirically, the volume $V_p$ of material removed per pulse is proportional to the pulse energy:

$$V_p = K \cdot E_p = K \cdot V \cdot I \cdot T_{on}$$

where $K$ is a material-workpiece constant (mm³/J), typically $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-4}$ mm³/J.

**Step 3 — Number of pulses per unit time**

For a pulse frequency $f$ (Hz), the number of effective pulses per second is $f$ (assuming every pulse produces a spark):

$$n = f \quad \text{[pulses/s]}$$

**Step 4 — Total MRR**

$$MRR = V_p \cdot n = K \cdot V \cdot I \cdot T_{on} \cdot f \quad \text{[mm}^3/\text{s]}$$

Converting to mm³/min:

$$MRR = 60 \cdot K \cdot V \cdot I \cdot T_{on} \cdot f \quad \text{[mm}^3/\text{min]}$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example**

Given: $V = 100$ V, $I = 5$ A, $T_{on} = 2$ μs = $2 \times 10^{-6}$ s, $f = 50$ kHz = $5 \times 10^{4}$ Hz, $K = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ mm³/J.

$$E_p = 100 \times 5 \times 2 \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-3} \;\text{J}$$

$$V_p = 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{-3} = 5 \times 10^{-9} \;\text{mm}^3/\text{pulse}$$

$$MRR = 5 \times 10^{-9} \times 5 \times 10^{4} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{mm}^3/\text{s}$$

$$MRR = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 60 = 0.015 \;\text{mm}^3/\text{min}$$

This is an **extremely low MRR** — typical of μ-EDM. It illustrates why μ-EDM is used only when **precision and zero mechanical stress** outweigh speed.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — MRR for Laser Beam Micromachining (LBM)

**Step 1 — Energy balance**

The laser delivers power $P$ (W). A fraction $\eta$ is absorbed by the workpiece; the rest is reflected/scattered. The absorbed power is:

$$P_{abs} = \eta \cdot P \quad \text{[W]}$$

**Step 2 — Energy required to vaporize a unit volume**

To remove a unit volume of material, the laser must supply enough energy to (a) heat the material to melting point ($H_v$, sensible heat), (b) supply latent heat of fusion ($L_f$), (c) heat to vaporization point, and (d) supply latent heat of vaporization ($L_v$). The total energy per unit volume is:

$$E_{vap} = \rho \cdot (c_p \cdot \Delta T + L_f + L_v) \quad \text{[J/mm}^3\text{]}$$

**Step 3 — MRR formula**

The volume vaporized per second is the absorbed power divided by the vaporization energy per unit volume:

$$MRR = \frac{\eta \cdot P}{E_{vap}} \quad \text{[mm}^3/\text{s]}$$

**Step 4 — Power density interpretation**

The laser spot diameter is $d$, so the **power density** at the focal point is:

$$q = \frac{4 P}{\pi d^2} \quad \text{[W/cm}^2\text{]}$$

A higher $q$ (smaller spot, higher power) yields a higher MRR but also a deeper HAZ. The **optimal** $q$ is just above the **vaporization threshold** of the material.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — HAZ (Heat-Affected Zone) Depth Approximation

For thermal micromachining processes, the HAZ depth is approximated by the 1-D heat diffusion length during the interaction time $t$:

$$HAZ \approx \sqrt{4 \alpha t} \quad \text{[m]}$$

where $\alpha$ is the **thermal diffusivity** of the workpiece material (m²/s).

**Worked example:** For steel, $\alpha \approx 8 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s, and a short-pulse laser interaction $t = 1$ μs = $10^{-6}$ s:

$$HAZ = \sqrt{4 \times 8 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{-6}} = \sqrt{3.2 \times 10^{-11}} \approx 5.66 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{m} = 5.66 \;\mu\text{m}$$

This **sub-10 μm HAZ** is one of the strongest selling points of **short-pulse / femtosecond laser micromachining** in biomedical applications.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Process Selection

| Material → | Metals | Ceramics / Glass | Silicon | Polymers | Composites |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Micro-milling** | ★★★ | ★ (brittle fracture) | ✗ | ★★ | ★ |
| **μ-EDM** | ★★★ (conductive) | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ★ (carbon fiber) |
| **LBM (Fiber/Nd:YAG)** | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★ |
| **LBM (Excimer UV)** | ★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ |
| **USM** | ✗ | ★★★ | ★★ | ✗ | ★ |
| **CMM (wet etch)** | ★ (acids) | ★★ (HF) | ★★★ (KOH) | ✗ | ✗ |
| **ECMM** | ★★★ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **FIB** | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★ |

Legend: ★★★ Excellent | ★★ Good | ★ Limited | ✗ Not suitable

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** This selection matrix is a **favourite 14-mark question** in the form "Select and justify a micromachining process for [given material] to achieve [given feature]." Students must justify using **mechanism compatibility, MRR, surface finish, and HAZ** criteria.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart of Micromachining Processes

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Micromachining]:::root --> B[Mechanical]:::branch
    A --> C[Thermal]:::branch
    A --> D[Chemical]:::branch
    A --> E[Hybrid]:::branch

    B --> B1[Micro-milling]
    B --> B2[Micro-drilling]
    B --> B3[Micro-grinding]
    B --> B4[Ultrasonic USM]
    B --> B5[Abrasive Jet MAJM]

    C --> C1[Laser Beam LBM]
    C --> C2[Electron Beam EBM]
    C --> C3[Focused Ion Beam FIB]
    C --> C4[Micro EDM]

    D --> D1[Chemical CMM]
    D --> D2[Electrochemical ECMM]
    D --> D3[Photo Chemical]

    E --> E1[Laser Assisted Milling]
    E --> E2[ECMM plus USM]
    E --> E3[EDM plus ECM]

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef branch fill:#d9e2f3,color:#1f3864,stroke:#5b9bd5,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU:** When you draw such a hierarchy in the exam, always use **rectangular boxes, clean arrows, and a single root node**. The examiner allocates marks for **completeness of classification** (1 mark per branch) and **correctness of examples** (1 mark per correct example).

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Generic Micromachining Workstation

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Power Supply Unit]:::power --> A2[Energy Controller / Pulse Generator]:::ctrl
    A2 --> A3[Focused Energy Source]:::src
    A3 --> A4[Workpiece Stage]:::work
    A4 --> A5[XYZ CNC Controller]:::cnc
    A5 --> A2

    A6[Coolant / Dielectric / Etchant Supply]:::aux --> A4
    A7[Vision / Microscopy Feedback]:::vis --> A5
    A8[Exhaust and Filtration]:::aux --> A4

    classDef power fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c55a11
    classDef ctrl fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000
    classDef src fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235
    classDef work fill:#ddebf7,stroke:#2e75b6
    classDef cnc fill:#f8cbad,stroke:#c55a11
    classDef aux fill:#e7e6e6,stroke:#7f7f7f
    classDef vis fill:#d9e1f2,stroke:#4472c4
```

**Block-by-block interpretation (for KTU 14-mark descriptive answers):**

- **Power Supply Unit:** Provides pulsed DC for μ-EDM, high-voltage for EBM, RF-excitation for USM, or DC for ECMM.
- **Energy Controller / Pulse Generator:** Sets pulse-on time $T_{on}$, pulse-off time $T_{off}$, frequency, current — critical for micro-feature fidelity.
- **Focused Energy Source:** Laser cavity, spark gap, ultrasonic horn, ion column, or chemical bath.
- **Workpiece Stage:** Precision XY(Z) table with **resolution < 1 μm** (often piezo-driven).
- **XYZ CNC Controller:** Closed-loop motion control with sub-micron positioning accuracy.
- **Auxiliary systems:** Dielectric/coolant for EDM, slurry for USM, etchant for CMM, gas-assist for LBM.
- **Vision / Microscopy Feedback:** In-situ monitoring for process stability and alignment.

## 4.3 Decision Flow for Process Selection (KTU Exam Favourite)

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Start: Material and Feature Specified]:::start --> S2{Is the material conductive?}
    S2 -- Yes --> S3{Is feature < 50 micrometre?}
    S2 -- No --> S4{Is material brittle or hard?}
    S3 -- Yes --> S5[Choose mu EDM or FIB]:::pick
    S3 -- No --> S6[Choose Micro milling or mu EDM]:::pick
    S4 -- Yes --> S7{Is material transparent to laser?}
    S4 -- No --> S8[Choose Laser Beam LBM]:::pick
    S7 -- Yes --> S9[Use Excimer UV Laser]:::pick
    S7 -- No --> S10{Is chemical etch available?}
    S10 -- Yes --> S11[Choose CMM with KOH or HF]:::pick
    S10 -- No --> S12[Use USM with abrasive slurry]:::pick

    classDef start fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    classDef pick fill:#70ad47,color:#ffffff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When answering "Justify the choice of micromachining process for X," always structure your answer as a **decision tree traversal** — start with **material properties → feature size → tolerance → surface finish → HAZ constraint → economic feasibility**. This is the exact pattern of a model answer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Define micromachining. List any four micromachining processes with their energy source.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Micromachining is the manufacturing of features with dimensions in the range of **1–999 μm** using precisely controlled, localized energy sources — mechanical, thermal, chemical, or electrochemical — to achieve high precision, low surface roughness, and minimal heat-affected zone.

**Four processes (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

| Process | Energy Source |
|---|---|
| Micro-EDM (μ-EDM) | Electrical (spark discharge) |
| Laser Beam Micromachining (LBM) | Photonic (focused laser beam) |
| Ultrasonic Micromachining (USM) | Mechanical (high-frequency vibration + abrasive) |
| Chemical Micromachining (CMM) | Chemical (etchant dissolution) |

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] State the role of abrasive slurry in Ultrasonic Micromachining (USM). Why is USM not suitable for machining soft metals like copper or aluminium?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Role of slurry (2 marks):** The abrasive slurry (typically **SiC, B₄C, or Al₂O₃** particles of grit size **5–150 μm** suspended in water) performs the actual material removal. The vibrating tool (sonotrode) at **20–40 kHz** hammers the abrasive particles against the workpiece. Each impact chips off a microscopic fragment of the brittle workpiece. The slurry also:
- Cools the cutting zone.
- Carries away debris (swarf).
- Provides a continuous supply of fresh sharp abrasive.

**Why unsuitable for soft metals (1 mark):** USM relies on **brittle fracture** under impact. Soft, ductile metals like copper and aluminium **absorb impact energy plastically** rather than fracturing, so the abrasive particles merely **rub** the surface without chip removal, leading to negligible MRR and possible tool/abrasive embedding.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** Most students **list** micromachining processes but **fail to compare** them on a unified axis. A 14-mark question expects **at least one full comparison table** plus a **justified selection** for a specific application. Merely writing definitions will fetch ≤ 6 marks.
>
> **Pitfall Callout #2:** When solving numerical problems on μ-EDM MRR, students often **forget to convert μs to seconds and Hz to pulses/s** — leading to MRR errors by 3–6 orders of magnitude. Always write units explicitly.

---

### 5.2.1 Question A (14 Marks) — *Micro-EDM + Chemical Micromachining Focus*

> **Q.A [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]**
> **(a)** Explain the working principle of **Micro-Electric Discharge Machining (μ-EDM)** with a neat schematic. Discuss the role of pulse-on time ($T_{on}$), pulse-off time ($T_{off}$), and discharge current in determining MRR and surface finish. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe **Chemical Micromachining (CMM)** for silicon. Compare **isotropic** and **anisotropic** etching with suitable examples of etchants. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q.A (a) [7 marks]

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
Micro-EDM removes material by **controlled spark discharges** between a micro-tool (cathode) and the workpiece (anode) immersed in a dielectric fluid. The tool is fed axially to maintain a small spark gap (**1–10 μm**). When a pulsed voltage is applied (open-circuit voltage **80–250 V**), the dielectric breaks down at the smallest gap, generating a plasma channel at temperatures of **8,000–12,000 °C**. This vaporizes/melts a micro-crater of workpiece material. The dielectric then flushes the debris, and the cycle repeats.

**Role of process parameters (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Effect on MRR | Effect on Surface Finish (Ra) |
|---|---|---|
| $T_{on}$ (pulse-on time) | Higher $T_{on}$ → more energy per pulse → higher MRR | Higher $T_{on}$ → deeper crater → **rougher** surface |
| $T_{off}$ (pulse-off time) | Shorter $T_{off}$ → more pulses/sec → higher MRR | Too short $T_{off}$ → arcing → damaged surface |
| Discharge current $I$ | Higher $I$ → higher MRR (linear at low current) | Higher $I$ → wider, deeper craters → **rougher** |

**Key relationship:** $MRR = K \cdot V \cdot I \cdot T_{on} \cdot f$ (1 mark for correct formula).

**Schematic description (1 mark):** Workpiece held on precision XY stage; tool rotates (to avoid short-circuit) and is fed by a servo-controlled Z-axis; dielectric is filtered and recirculated; debris and gas bubbles are flushed through a side nozzle.

#### Model Solution — Q.A (b) [7 marks]

**Chemical Micromachining of Silicon (3 marks):**
- A **silicon wafer** is coated with a **mask** (SiO₂, Si₃N₄, or photoresist) patterned with the desired micro-feature geometry.
- The masked wafer is immersed in an **etchant** (e.g., KOH, TMAH, EDP, or HNA).
- The etchant **dissolves the exposed silicon** but is **blocked by the mask**.
- Etching is performed at controlled temperature (e.g., **80 °C for KOH**) for a calibrated duration.
- After etching, the mask is stripped, revealing the micro-feature.

**Comparison Table — Isotropic vs. Anisotropic Etching (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Isotropic Etching | Anisotropic Etching |
|---|---|---|
| Etch direction | Equal rate in **all directions** | **Crystallographic-plane dependent** |
| Etchants (for Si) | **HNA** (HF + HNO₃ + CH₃COOH) | **KOH, TMAH, EDP** |
| Typical etch rate | 1–10 μm/min | 0.1–1 μm/min |
| Undercut (lateral etch) | **Large** — significant under mask | **Minimal** — vertical sidewalls |
| Sidewall profile | **Curved / rounded** | **Angular** (e.g., 54.74° for Si(100) in KOH) |
| Use case | Bulk micromachining, releasing membranes | Through-wafer vias, micro-channels, MEMS structures |
| Surface finish | Smoother | Rougher, may show hillocks |

---

### 5.2.2 Question B (14 Marks) — *Laser + USM + Process Selection Focus*

> **Q.B [KTU University Exam — June 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**
> **(a)** Explain the principle of **Laser Beam Micromachining (LBM)**. Discuss the effect of **power density, pulse duration, and assist gas** on the quality of micro-holes. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the working of **Ultrasonic Micromachining (USM)**. A USM process operates at a frequency of 25 kHz, amplitude 30 μm, with a static load of 5 N on a workpiece of Vickers hardness 900 HV. Estimate the relative MRR and explain the factors affecting it. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q.B (a) [7 marks]

**Principle (2 marks):** In LBM, a **focused laser beam** (spot diameter 5–50 μm) delivers power densities of **10⁶ – 10¹² W/cm²** onto the workpiece. The intense localized energy raises the material temperature above its **vaporization point**, causing material to be ejected as **vapor and molten droplets**, creating a micro-feature. A **gas-assist nozzle** (O₂, N₂, or Ar) co-axially blows the molten/vaporized material out of the kerf, preventing re-deposition on the workpiece.

**Effect of process parameters (5 marks):**

| Parameter | Effect on Micro-Hole Quality |
|---|---|
| **Power density** $q = \frac{4P}{\pi d^2}$ | Below vaporization threshold → no material removal. At optimal $q$ → clean, circular hole. Excess $q$ → **spatter, recast layer, larger HAZ**. |
| **Pulse duration** $t_p$ | Short pulses (ns, ps, fs) → **minimal HAZ** because thermal diffusion is suppressed. Long pulses (μs, ms) → larger HAZ, heat-conduction damage, tapered hole. |
| **Assist gas type** | **O₂** — exothermic reaction (boosts MRR in carbon steels). **N₂ / Ar** — inert, prevents oxidation, gives cleaner edges (used for titanium, stainless steel, refractory metals). Gas pressure too low → re-deposition; too high → plasma shielding. |
| **Focal position** | Above, on, or below the surface — affects taper and recast. |
| **Number of pulses** | More pulses → deeper hole (drilling) but also larger entrance diameter. |

**Femtosecond lasers** are the gold standard for **HAZ-free, sub-micron precision** micromachining in biomedical and aerospace applications (1 mark for application relevance).

#### Model Solution — Q.B (b) [7 marks]

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):** A USM system consists of:
- **High-frequency power supply** (20–40 kHz, typically 25 kHz).
- **Piezzoelectric transducer** (converts electrical energy to mechanical vibration).
- **Concentrator / booster** (amplifies amplitude).
- **Sonotrode / tool holder** (transmits vibration to the tool).
- **Tool** (shaped to mirror the cavity — made of soft, tough material like Monel or SS-304 to prevent tool wear).
- **Abrasive slurry pump** (recirculates SiC/B₄C slurry through the machining zone).
- **Workpiece fixture** and **static load applicator** (spring or weight providing $F$).

Material removal occurs because the vibrating tool **hammers the abrasive particles** against the workpiece, each impact chipping a micro-fragment.

**Relative MRR Estimation (4 marks):**

Using the empirical proportionality:

$$MRR \propto \frac{F \cdot A \cdot f}{H_v}$$

where $F = 5$ N, $A = 30$ μm = $30 \times 10^{-3}$ mm, $f = 25$ kHz = $25 \times 10^3$ Hz, $H_v = 900$ HV.

$$MRR \propto \frac{5 \times 30 \times 10^{-3} \times 25 \times 10^3}{900} = \frac{5 \times 750}{900} = \frac{3750}{900} \approx 4.17 \;\text{(relative units)}$$

**Factors affecting MRR (list 3 for 1.5 marks):**
1. **Abrasive grit size** — larger grit → higher MRR, but rougher surface.
2. **Abrasive material hardness** — harder abrasive (B₄C > SiC > Al₂O₃) → higher MRR.
3. **Concentration of slurry** — optimum 30–40% by weight; too dilute → low MRR; too dense → slurry damping.
4. **Static load** — higher $F$ → more impacts per cycle → higher MRR (up to a saturation point).
5. **Tool material** — soft tools wear faster; tool wear reduces accuracy but also redistributes load.
6. **Hollow vs. tapered tool** — affects slurry flow and debris evacuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — print this and revise the night before ESE.**

- ✅ **Definition:** Micromachining = features in **1–999 μm** range; specific energy **10⁹–10¹² J/mm³**.
- ✅ **Four families:** Mechanical, Thermal, Chemical, Hybrid.
- ✅ **μ-EDM:** Conductive materials only; **$T_{on}$ in μs**; dielectric oil/water; **MRR ∝ V·I·T_on·f**.
- ✅ **LBM:** Non-contact; **vaporization regime**; assist gas choice (O₂ exothermic, N₂ inert); short pulses → low HAZ.
- ✅ **USM:** **20–40 kHz** vibration; abrasive slurry; **brittle materials only** (glass, ceramics, Si); **MRR ∝ F·A·f / H_v**.
- ✅ **CMM:** Mask + etchant; **KOH** for Si(100) anisotropic; **HNA** isotropic; **no thermal/mechanical damage**.
- ✅ **ECMM:** Conductive metals only; pulsed DC; **no HAZ, no tool wear**; excellent for micro-channels in stainless steel.
- ✅ **FIB:** Highest precision (sub-100 nm); slowest; expensive; used for TEM sample prep and mask repair.
- ✅ **Key formula:** $U = \dfrac{F_c \cdot v_c}{MRR} = \dfrac{F_c}{A_c}$ — note $v_c$ cancels.
- ✅ **Key selection criteria:** Material conductivity, brittleness, feature aspect ratio, tolerance, HAZ constraint, MRR need.
- ✅ **Common confusion:** LBM does **not** require conductive material; μ-EDM **does**. USM is **not** for ductile metals.
- ✅ **Examiner trap:** Always convert units (μs → s, kHz → Hz) before plugging into MRR formulas.
- ✅ **Recent trend in KTU 2024:** Questions on **femtosecond laser**, **μ-EDM with rotating tool**, and **electrochemical micromachining of shape memory alloys** have appeared in the last two cycles — high weightage.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Conventional Processes - EDM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT404 - MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Non-Conventional Processes - EDM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Electrical Discharge Machining (EDM)** is a non-conventional, thermo-electric material removal process in which the shape of a workpiece is produced by means of a controlled, recurring series of electrical discharges (sparks) between a **tool electrode (cathode)** and a **workpiece electrode (anode)** submerged in a **dielectric fluid**. The spark produces localized temperatures of the order of **8,000 °C to 12,000 °C**, which melts and vaporizes a microscopic volume of material from both electrodes, irrespective of their mechanical hardness.

In KTU terminology, EDM belongs to the family of **Non-Traditional / Non-Conventional Machining (NCM) Processes** — processes that remove material using forms of energy other than (or in addition to) sharp wedge-shaped cutting tools. The defining features that make EDM a non-conventional process are:

1. **No mechanical contact** between tool and workpiece.
2. **Hardness-independent material removal** — even hardened tool steels, carbides, titanium, and exotic alloys can be machined in their fully hardened state.
3. **Direct thermal energy** is used to erode material.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Lightning in a Test Tube"

Imagine a tiny, controlled **lightning bolt** firing thousands of times per second between two metal plates submerged in oil. Each lightning strike instantly vaporizes a microscopic speck of metal. Because the strikes happen millions of times in sequence, slowly but surely, a precise cavity matching the shape of the upper "negative" plate is carved into the lower plate. That, in essence, is EDM.

| Lightning (Nature) | EDM (Industry) |
|---|---|
| Uncontrolled, destructive spark | Controlled spark at micro-scale |
| Strikes highest point | Strikes nearest conductive surface |
| Air as medium | Dielectric oil/deionized water as medium |
| Instant vaporization of material | Instant melting + vaporization of metal |

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board Favourites)

> [!NOTE]  
> These exact phrases appear frequently in KTU 2-mark and 3-mark questions. Memorise the wording.

- **Spark / Discharge**: A discrete pulse of electrical energy crossing the inter-electrode gap.
- **Gap (Inter-Electrode Gap, IEG)**: The microscopic distance (typically **0.01 mm to 0.05 mm**) maintained between tool and workpiece.
- **Dielectric Fluid**: An electrically insulating liquid (EDM oil, kerosene, or deionized water) that ionizes to allow spark generation and then rapidly de-ionizes to flush away debris.
- **MRR (Material Removal Rate)**: Volume of workpiece material removed per unit time, expressed in **mm³/min**.
- **TWR (Tool Wear Ratio)**: Ratio of volumetric tool wear to volumetric workpiece removal.
- **Recast Layer**: A thin, re-solidified, often micro-cracked layer (≈ 2–50 μm thick) left on the EDM-cut surface.
- **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)**: The micro-structurally altered layer beneath the recast layer.
- **Overcut / Side Gap**: Lateral difference between the cavity produced and the tool electrode size.
- **Critical Current / Critical Pulse On-Time**: Boundary settings below which sparking becomes unstable.

## 1.4 Why EDM Exists — The Engineering Need

Conventional machining fails to economically cut:
- **Hardened tool steels** (HRC 60+) — drill bits, dies.
- **Carbides, ceramics, exotic alloys** (Inconel, Waspaloy, titanium).
- **Internal cavities with sharp internal corners** and complex 3D profiles.
- **Very small, deep holes** (micro-holes with high aspect ratio).

EDM fills this gap. It is the **workhorse** of tool-and-die making, aerospace blade machining, and medical implant manufacturing.

## 1.5 Salient Process Characteristics

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU High-Yield Point**: Examiners often ask — *"List any four characteristics of EDM."*

- Material removal is independent of mechanical properties (hardness, strength).
- Only **electrically conductive** materials can be machined.
- Tool electrode must be made of an **electrically conductive material** (graphite, copper, brass, tungsten).
- Process produces a **recast layer** and **micro-cracks** on the finished surface.
- No mechanical force on the workpiece — ideal for thin, delicate parts.
- **High accuracy** (≈ ± 0.01 mm) and good surface finish (Ra 0.2–3.2 μm achievable).
- Process is **slow** compared to conventional milling of soft materials.
- Dielectric must be regularly filtered and degassed.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of a Single Spark (Step-by-Step)

The EDM process repeats a single, identical event — the **spark cycle** — thousands of times per second. Each cycle has four well-defined stages:

### Stage 1 — Dielectric Ionization (Breakdown)
When voltage is applied across the IEG, the dielectric fluid is normally an insulator. However, as the gap becomes small (≈ 10–50 μm) and the field strength exceeds the dielectric strength (≈ **3 × 10⁶ V/m** for EDM oil), the dielectric molecules ionize. The fluid becomes a transient **plasma channel** of electrons and ions.

### Stage 2 — Plasma Channel Formation
A narrow conductive channel forms between tool and workpiece. Current density in this channel reaches **10⁴ to 10⁶ A/mm²**. The channel is only a few microns in diameter.

### Stage 3 — Material Erosion (Vaporization & Melting)
Temperatures in the channel range from **8,000 °C to 12,000 °C** (often quoted as ≈ **10,000 °C** in textbooks). This intense heat:
- **Vaporizes** a small volume of metal at the discharge point.
- **Melts** a surrounding halo of material.
- Creates a tiny hemispherical **crater** (typically 2–10 μm deep) on both electrodes.

### Stage 4 — Debris Flushing & De-ionization
When the pulse is switched **OFF**, the plasma collapses. The dielectric rushes back into the gap, **quenching** the molten debris into tiny spherical particles (called **EDM sludge** or **debris**). The dielectric also cools the electrodes and re-establishes its insulating property — ready for the next pulse.

> [!NOTE]  
> The cycle is governed by **Pulse-On Time (T_on)**, **Pulse-Off Time (T_off)**, and **Pulse Interval (T_p)**. The ratio T_on : T_off critically affects surface finish and MRR.

## 2.2 Process Architecture — What an EDM Machine Actually Does

A working EDM system must perform **four simultaneous jobs** during every spark:

| Function | Hardware Responsible |
|---|---|
| Generate controlled DC pulses | **Pulse Generator (Power Supply)** |
| Hold tool at correct gap | **Servo Control System** (advances tool as material erodes) |
| Force dielectric through gap | **Dielectric Circulation System** (pump, filter, nozzles) |
| Translate tool path to cavity | **CNC / NC Axis Drives** (X, Y, Z, sometimes C) |

The **servo system** is the brain: it monitors gap voltage. If voltage rises (gap too large), it advances the tool. If voltage drops (short circuit/imminent), it retracts. This maintains the **critical spark gap** continuously.

## 2.3 Process Parameters — The "Knobs" of EDM

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> KTU loves tabular questions asking to *"Explain the effect of process parameters on MRR and surface roughness."* Memorize the directions of these effects.

| Parameter | Effect on **MRR** | Effect on **Surface Roughness (Ra)** | Effect on **Tool Wear** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Increase **discharge current (I)** | ↑ Increases sharply | ↑ Rougher (deeper craters) | ↑ Increases |
| Increase **pulse-on time (T_on)** | ↑ Increases | ↑ Rougher | ↑ Increases (more heat) |
| Increase **pulse-off time (T_off)** | ↓ Slightly decreases (less sparking/sec) | ↓ Slightly better | ↓ Lower |
| Increase **open-circuit voltage (V)** | ↑ Slight increase | ↑ Slight increase | ↑ Slight increase |
| Increase **duty cycle (τ = T_on / (T_on+T_off))** | ↑ Increases | ↑ Rougher | ↑ Increases |
| Use **negative polarity** (tool = cathode) | ↑ Higher MRR | — | ↓ Lower tool wear (preferred) |
| Better **flushing / dielectric flow** | ↑ Removes debris, prevents short | ↓ Better finish | ↓ Lower wear |
| **Graphite tool** vs **copper tool** | Graphite allows higher currents | — | Graphite has lower wear at high I |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> All formulas below are **routinely asked** in KTU numerical problems. Master them.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$MRR = \dfrac{V_w}{t}$$ | Volume of workpiece removed per minute | mm³/min |
| 2 | $$TWR = \dfrac{V_t}{V_w}$$ | Volumetric tool wear ratio (dimensionless) | — |
| 3 | $$\eta = \dfrac{V_w}{V_w + V_t}$$ | Volumetric efficiency of material utilization | — |
| 4 | $$E = V \cdot I \cdot T_{on}$$ | Energy dissipated in one spark | Joules (J) |
| 5 | $$MRR = k \cdot E \cdot f$$ | MRR proportional to spark energy × frequency | mm³/min |
| 6 | $$\text{Crater Volume} = \dfrac{\pi d^2 h}{12}$$ | Volume of a single hemispherical crater | μm³ |
| 7 | $$Ra \approx k' \cdot I^{0.4} \cdot T_{on}^{0.3}$$ | Empirical surface roughness relation | μm |
| 8 | $$\text{Recast Thickness} \propto I \cdot T_{on}$$ | Heat input per pulse controls recast | μm |
| 9 | $$Duty\ Cycle\ \tau = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$$ | Fraction of time spark is active | dimensionless |
| 10 | $$v_w = k_v \cdot I \cdot \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on}+T_{off}}$$ | Linear cutting speed (Wire-EDM) | mm/min |

**Where:**
- $V_w$ = volume of workpiece removed (mm³)
- $V_t$ = volume of tool worn (mm³)
- $t$ = machining time (min)
- $V$ = discharge voltage (V), $I$ = peak current (A)
- $T_{on}, T_{off}$ = pulse on/off time (μs)
- $f$ = pulse frequency (Hz)
- $d, h$ = crater diameter and depth
- $k, k', k_v$ = process constants

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Typical EDM Application |
|---|---|
| Tool & Die Making | Cavities in forging dies, plastic injection moulds, drawing dies |
| Aerospace | Cooling holes in super-alloy turbine blades, blade profiles |
| Medical | Micro-features in titanium implants, surgical instruments |
| Electronics | Micro-holes in semiconductor lead frames, narrow slits |
| Automotive | Hardened gear teeth, die-cast die repair |
| Defence | Shaped-charge liner machining, exotic alloy components |

> [!NOTE]  
> Modern **Wire-EDM (WEDM)** uses a continuously travelling brass/zinc-coated wire (0.02–0.30 mm diameter) as the electrode, allowing 2D profile cutting with tolerances of **± 0.002 mm**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Volume of a Single Crater

The fundamental assumption of EDM is that **one spark removes one crater-shaped volume** of material. The crater is modelled as a hemisphere.

**Step 1:** Volume of a full sphere of diameter $d$ is $\dfrac{\pi d^3}{6}$.

**Step 2:** Volume of a hemisphere is half the sphere.

$$V_{hemi} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{6} = \frac{\pi d^3}{12}$$

**Step 3:** However, EDM craters are typically **flat-bottomed** — the depth $h$ is not equal to the radius $d/2$. Empirical studies show $h \approx d/3$ to $d/4$. A commonly used textbook approximation is:

$$V_{crater} = \frac{\pi d^2 h}{12}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $h = d/3$ (a common simplification):

$$V_{crater} = \frac{\pi d^2}{12} \cdot \frac{d}{3} = \frac{\pi d^3}{36}$$

**Step 5:** If $N$ sparks strike a surface in time $t$, the total volume removed is:

$$V_w = N \cdot V_{crater} = N \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{36}$$

**Step 6:** The number of sparks per unit time is the **pulse frequency** $f$ (Hz). So $N = f \cdot t$:

$$V_w = f \cdot t \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{36}$$

**Step 7:** Therefore the **MRR** becomes:

$$MRR = \frac{V_w}{t} = f \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{36}$$

This proves MRR scales with the **cube of the crater diameter**, which in turn scales with spark energy.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — MRR from Spark Energy

**Step 1:** The energy delivered in a single spark of voltage $V$, peak current $I$, and duration $T_{on}$ is:

$$E = V \cdot I \cdot T_{on} \quad [\text{Joules}]$$

**Step 2:** Of this energy, only a fraction $\alpha$ is used to actually remove material; the rest is lost to the dielectric, tool, and surroundings. So **effective material-removal energy** is:

$$E_{eff} = \alpha \cdot V \cdot I \cdot T_{on}$$

**Step 3:** From thermodynamic principles, the volume of metal melted/vaporized is:

$$V_{crater} = \frac{E_{eff}}{\rho \cdot (C_p \Delta T + L_f + L_v)}$$

where $\rho$ is density, $C_p$ specific heat, $\Delta T$ temperature rise, $L_f$ latent heat of fusion, and $L_v$ latent heat of vaporization. Combining all material constants into $K$:

$$V_{crater} = K \cdot E = K \cdot \alpha \cdot V \cdot I \cdot T_{on}$$

**Step 4:** If pulse frequency is $f$ sparks per second:

$$MRR = 60 \cdot f \cdot V_{crater} = 60 \cdot K \cdot \alpha \cdot V \cdot I \cdot T_{on} \cdot f \quad [\text{mm}^3/\text{min}]$$

**Step 5:** This explains the KTU rule: *"MRR is directly proportional to discharge current, pulse-on time, and pulse frequency."*

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Empirical Surface Roughness

**Step 1:** Surface roughness $R_a$ is proportional to the size of the craters left on the surface. Larger craters → rougher surface.

**Step 2:** Crater size scales with **peak current** raised to a fractional power and with **pulse-on time** raised to a fractional power (experimentally determined exponents).

**Step 3:** Therefore:

$$R_a = k' \cdot I^{\beta_1} \cdot T_{on}^{\beta_2}$$

**Step 4:** Standard textbook exponents are $\beta_1 = 0.4$ and $\beta_2 = 0.3$, giving:

$$R_a = k' \cdot I^{0.4} \cdot T_{on}^{0.3} \quad [\mu m]$$

**Step 5:** This is a direct, KTU-acceptable empirical formula for surface roughness.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** In an EDM operation, the following data is given:
- Discharge current $I = 25$ A
- Pulse-on time $T_{on} = 100\ \mu s = 100 \times 10^{-6}$ s
- Pulse-off time $T_{off} = 50\ \mu s$
- Discharge voltage $V = 60$ V
- Crater diameter $d = 200\ \mu m = 0.2$ mm
- Crater depth $h = 50\ \mu m = 0.05$ mm
- Pulse frequency $f = 2000$ Hz

Find: (a) Energy per spark, (b) MRR in mm³/min, (c) Duty cycle.

**Solution:**

**(a) Energy per spark**

$$E = V \cdot I \cdot T_{on} = 60 \times 25 \times (100 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$E = 1500 \times 10^{-6} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{J} = 1.5\ \text{mJ}$$

**(b) MRR**

$$V_{crater} = \frac{\pi d^2 h}{12} = \frac{\pi \times (0.2)^2 \times 0.05}{12}$$

$$V_{crater} = \frac{3.1416 \times 0.04 \times 0.05}{12} = \frac{0.006283}{12} = 5.236 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{mm}^3$$

$$MRR = V_{crater} \times f \times 60 = 5.236 \times 10^{-4} \times 2000 \times 60$$

$$MRR = 62.83\ \text{mm}^3/\text{min}$$

**(c) Duty cycle**

$$\tau = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} = \frac{100}{100 + 50} = \frac{100}{150} = 0.667\ (66.7\%)$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparison of EDM Variants

| Feature | Die-Sinking EDM (Sinker / Ram EDM) | Wire-EDM (WEDM) | Hole-Drilling EDM |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tool electrode | Shaped graphite/copper electrode | Travelling brass wire (0.02–0.30 mm) | Rotating/fixed tubular electrode |
| Cavity produced | 3D cavity matching tool | 2D through-cut profile | Small deep hole |
| Application | Die cavities, moulds | Stamping dies, profiles, slits | Cooling holes, start holes for WEDM |
| Tolerance | ± 0.01 mm | ± 0.002 mm | ± 0.02 mm |
| Surface roughness Ra | 0.8 – 3.2 μm | 0.2 – 1.6 μm | 0.8 – 3.2 μm |
| MRR | Moderate | High for thin slits | Low (small electrode) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 EDM System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram shows how the four sub-systems of an EDM machine interact during a single spark. This is the **exact style of diagram** that earns full marks in KTU descriptive answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PWR[Power Supply Module]
        A1[Pulse Generator]
        A2[Discharge Capacitor Bank]
        A3[Voltage Current Regulator]
    end

    subgraph SRV[Servo Control Module]
        B1[Gap Voltage Sensor]
        B2[Comparator Circuit]
        B3[Servo Motor Driver]
    end

    subgraph DIE[Dielectric Circulation Module]
        C1[Dielectric Reservoir Tank]
        C2[Filter and Cooler Unit]
        C3[High Pressure Pump]
        C4[Flushing Nozzles]
    end

    subgraph GAP[Inter Electrode Gap IEG]
        D1[Tool Electrode Cathode]
        D2[Workpiece Anode]
        D3[Spark Discharge Channel]
        D4[Dielectric Fluid in Gap]
    end

    A1 -->|High Frequency Pulses| D3
    A2 -->|Stores Energy| D3
    A3 -->|Sets V and I| A1

    B1 -->|Reads Gap Voltage| D3
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 -->|Advances or Retracts| D1

    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C4 -->|Injects Clean Dielectric| D4
    D4 -->|Carries Debris Out| C2

    D1 -.->|Vaporized Tool Particles| D4
    D2 -.->|Vaporized Workpiece Particles| D4
```

## 4.2 Spark Cycle — Sequence of Events

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Stage 1: Dielectric Ionization] --> B[Stage 2: Plasma Channel Forms]
    B --> C[Stage 3: Material Melting and Vaporization]
    C --> D[Stage 4: Pulse OFF Dielectric Flushes Debris]
    D -->|After T_off| A
```

## 4.3 EDM Process Flowchart — Engineering Decision Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start Part Design]) --> Q1{Is Material Electrically Conductive?}
    Q1 -->|No| Out1([Use ECM or Laser])
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is Hardness Greater than HRC 50?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Is Feature Internal Cavity?}
    Q2 -->|No| Out2([Use Conventional Milling])
    Q3 -->|Yes| Sinker[Use Die Sinking EDM]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Is Feature a 2D Through Cut?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| WEDM[Use Wire EDM]
    Q4 -->|No| Q5{Is Feature a Small Deep Hole?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| Drill[Use EDM Hole Drilling]
    Q5 -->|No| Sinker
```

## 4.4 Tool and Workpiece Geometry Schematic (Textual Layout)

```
        +----------------------+
        |    TOOL ELECTRODE    |     <-- Shaped negative
        |    (Graphite / Cu)   |         of the desired cavity
        |    [   CATHODE  -  ] |
        +---/\/\/\/\/\/\/\/---+     <-- Servo feed
              | |  |  | |              controlled
         ~~~  | |  |  | |  ~~~     <-- Dielectric flow
         ~~~  GAP ≈ 0.025 mm  ~~~
              | |  |  | |
        +---/\/\/\/\/\/\/\/---+     <-- Spark zone
        |   WORKPIECE         |
        |   (Hardened Steel)  |
        |   [   ANODE  +   ]  |
        +----------------------+
              |  |  |  |  |
              ▼  ▼  ▼  ▼  ▼
        (Material eroded as craters)
```

> [!NOTE]  
> In your KTU answer sheet, draw this layout in pencil. Label the gap (IEG), the polarity, the dielectric arrows, and the direction of tool feed. Full marks = labelled diagram + working explanation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the principle of Electrical Discharge Machining (EDM) with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
EDM is a non-conventional material removal process that uses **controlled electrical sparks** between a tool electrode (cathode) and the workpiece (anode), both submerged in a **dielectric fluid**. A high-frequency pulsed DC supply is applied. When the gap voltage ionizes the dielectric, a **plasma channel** forms, generating temperatures of **8,000–12,000 °C**. This melts and vaporizes a tiny volume of metal from both electrodes, producing a crater. The pulse is switched off, the dielectric flushes the debris away, and the cycle repeats thousands of times per second. A **servo mechanism** keeps the inter-electrode gap (≈ 0.025 mm) constant by advancing the tool as the workpiece erodes. **(3 marks: 1 for principle, 1 for spark description, 1 for role of dielectric & servo).**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**List four characteristics of EDM. Why is EDM classified as a non-conventional process?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Four characteristics: (i) No mechanical contact between tool and workpiece, (ii) Material removal independent of mechanical hardness, (iii) Only electrically conductive materials can be machined, (iv) A recast layer and heat-affected zone are produced on the workpiece surface. EDM is non-conventional because it does not use a sharp wedge-shaped cutting tool and does not rely on mechanical shearing; it uses **thermal energy from electrical discharges** to erode material, which traditional machining cannot do on fully hardened, exotic, or brittle materials. **(3 marks).**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, each Part B question carries **14 marks** and offers a choice between two questions. Each sub-part is typically **7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Q. (a)** With a neat schematic, explain the construction and working of a **Die-Sinking EDM**. Discuss the role of the dielectric fluid. (7 marks)

**Q. (b)** Explain the following EDM performance measures: **MRR, TWR, and Surface Roughness**. Discuss how discharge current affects each. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — (a) Construction and Working of Die-Sinking EDM

**Construction (3 marks):**
A die-sinking EDM consists of:
1. **Pulse generator / power supply** — provides high-frequency DC pulses (50 V to 250 V, 1 A to 400 A peak).
2. **Tool holder (ram)** — holds a pre-shaped electrode (graphite or copper) that is the **mirror image** of the desired cavity.
3. **Work tank** — holds the workpiece and is filled with dielectric oil.
4. **Dielectric circulation system** — pump, filter, and nozzles.
5. **Servo control** — automatically advances the tool to maintain spark gap.
6. **CNC axes** — X, Y, Z (and sometimes C for rotation) to machine complex 3D cavities.

**Working (3 marks):**
The shaped tool is brought close to the workpiece under servo control. A pulsed DC voltage is applied. Each pulse causes a spark in the dielectric, vaporizing a micro-crater of material. The tool is progressively advanced into the workpiece. After thousands of pulses per second, the workpiece is eroded into a cavity **exactly matching the tool shape**. The servo maintains the IEG throughout.

**Role of dielectric (1 mark):**
- Acts as an **insulator** until voltage exceeds breakdown strength → enables spark.
- **Quenches** the plasma between pulses, allowing de-ionization.
- **Flushes** eroded debris (sludge) out of the gap.
- **Cools** the electrodes.

#### Model Solution — (b) MRR, TWR, and Surface Roughness

**MRR (2 marks):**
Material Removal Rate is the volume of workpiece metal removed per minute, $MRR = V_w / t$, in mm³/min. It increases **sharply** with discharge current because higher $I$ produces larger, deeper craters, and spark energy $E = V I T_{on}$ rises linearly with $I$.

**TWR (2 marks):**
Tool Wear Ratio is $TWR = V_t / V_w$, the volume of tool worn per unit volume of workpiece removed. With increasing current, TWR initially decreases (higher metal-removal efficiency) but rises at very high currents because the tool also experiences intense thermal loading. **Negative polarity** (tool = cathode) is preferred because it reduces tool wear by ~ 30–50 %.

**Surface Roughness (2 marks):**
$R_a$ is governed by crater dimensions. Empirically, $R_a \propto I^{0.4} \cdot T_{on}^{0.3}$. Higher $I$ → larger, deeper craters → higher $R_a$ (rougher). To get a mirror finish (Ra < 0.4 μm), use low current, short $T_{on}$, and finishing-grade parameters.

**Final mark split (1 mark):** Neat table summarising trends of $I$ on MRR, TWR, $R_a$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2022]`

**Q. (a)** What are the main **process parameters of EDM**? Explain the effect of pulse-on time, pulse-off time, and duty cycle on the process. (7 marks)

**Q. (b)** Describe **Wire-EDM** with a labelled sketch. List its advantages over die-sinking EDM. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — (a) Process Parameters of EDM

**Main parameters (2 marks):**
1. Open-circuit voltage
2. Peak discharge current
3. Pulse-on time ($T_{on}$)
4. Pulse-off time ($T_{off}$)
5. Duty cycle $\tau = T_{on} / (T_{on} + T_{off})$
6. Polarity
7. Dielectric flow rate and flushing pressure
8. Electrode material

**Effect of $T_{on}$ (2 marks):**
Longer $T_{on}$ → more energy per spark → deeper, larger craters → **higher MRR**, **rougher surface**, and **more tool wear**. Short $T_{on}$ is used for finishing.

**Effect of $T_{off}$ (1.5 marks):**
Longer $T_{off}$ → dielectric has more time to flush debris and de-ionize → **better surface finish**, **lower tool wear**, but **lower MRR** (fewer sparks per second).

**Effect of duty cycle (1.5 marks):**
Higher $\tau$ → more sparking time per cycle → **higher MRR** but **rougher surface**. For roughing, $\tau$ ≈ 0.7–0.8; for finishing, $\tau$ ≈ 0.3–0.5.

#### Model Solution — (b) Wire-EDM

**Description (3.5 marks):**
In Wire-EDM, a **thin continuously travelling wire** (brass, zinc-coated brass, or molybdenum, diameter 0.02–0.30 mm) acts as the tool electrode. The wire is fed from spools, passes through the workpiece, and is collected on a take-up spool. The workpiece is submerged in deionized water, which serves as the dielectric. CNC-controlled X-Y table moves the workpiece to cut the desired 2D profile, while the wire remains vertical. Sparks erode a narrow slit through the workpiece.

**Sketch (1.5 marks):**
The textual diagram from Section 4.4 (with wire replacing the shaped electrode) is acceptable. Label: wire spools, wire guides, work tank, dielectric inlet, X-Y table, taper control head.

**Advantages over die-sinking EDM (2 marks):**
1. **No need to manufacture a shaped electrode** → lower setup cost, faster lead time.
2. **Through-cutting** is possible in a single pass.
3. **Higher accuracy** (± 0.002 mm) and finer surface finish (Ra 0.2 μm).
4. Can machine **very small internal corners** because wire has no wear-induced corner radius.
5. **Lower recast layer** and better metallurgical integrity.
6. Suitable for **hard materials, thin sheets, and complex 2D profiles**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in EDM questions:**
> 1. Writing "EDM uses mechanical force to cut metal" — **WRONG**. It uses **thermal energy from sparks**. Examiners deduct full marks.
> 2. Confusing **MRR with TWR** — MRR is about workpiece, TWR is about tool wear ratio.
> 3. Forgetting to mention the **role of dielectric** — at least 1 mark is reserved for this in nearly every question.
> 4. Not stating the **temperature range (8,000–12,000 °C)** in the principle question — this is a 1-mark keyword.
> 5. Drawing a diagram without labelling the **gap, electrodes, dielectric, and spark zone** — Examiner deducts 1 mark for unlabelled sketches.
> 6. Confusing **EDM with ECM** — ECM is **electrochemical** (no spark, no heat, no recast). EDM is **electro-thermal**.
> 7. Writing "Wire-EDM uses a stationary wire" — wrong; the wire is **continuously moving** to prevent breakage.
> 8. Stating that EDM can machine **ceramics and glass** — wrong if they are **non-conductive**. Only **electrically conductive** materials can be EDM-ed.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — print this on the back of your answer sheet.**

- ✅ EDM = **Electro-Discharge Machining** (or **Electrical Discharge Machining**). Both names accepted by KTU.
- ✅ Energy form: **Thermal** (from electrical spark). NOT mechanical.
- ✅ Temperature in spark: **8,000–12,000 °C** (≈ 10,000 °C).
- ✅ Inter-Electrode Gap (IEG): **0.01 – 0.05 mm**, servo-controlled.
- ✅ Tool: **conductive** (graphite, copper, brass, tungsten). Tool is **cathode (−)** for low wear.
- ✅ Workpiece: must be **electrically conductive**.
- ✅ Dielectric: **EDM oil / kerosene** (sinker) or **deionized water** (wire-EDM).
- ✅ Four stages of spark: Ionization → Plasma → Erosion → Flush.
- ✅ MRR ↑ with $I$, $T_{on}$, frequency. MRR ↓ with $T_{off}$ (slightly).
- ✅ $R_a \propto I^{0.4} \cdot T_{on}^{0.3}$ — empirical roughness formula.
- ✅ Recast layer thickness $\propto I \cdot T_{on}$.
- ✅ TWR = $V_t / V_w$. Lower TWR is better.
- ✅ Wire-EDM uses **continuously moving wire**, deionized water dielectric, ± 0.002 mm accuracy.
- ✅ Die-sinking EDM uses **shaped electrode**, EDM oil dielectric, 3D cavity production.
- ✅ EDM produces **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** and **recast layer** — must be removed for fatigue-critical parts.
- ✅ Common applications: **dies, moulds, turbine blade holes, medical implants, micro-holes**.
- ✅ EDM **cannot** machine **glass, ceramics, plastics** (non-conductive).
- ✅ Competing processes: **ECM** (chemical), **LBM** (laser), **AJM** (abrasive), **USM** (ultrasonic).
- ✅ Remember the **four sub-systems**: Pulse generator, Servo, Dielectric circulation, CNC axes.
- ✅ Numericals: practice $E = V I T_{on}$, $MRR = k E f$, $V_{crater} = \pi d^2 h / 12$, $\tau = T_{on}/(T_{on}+T_{off})$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
