# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Process Capability Index $C_p$ and explain its significance. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The Process Capability Index $C_p$ is a statistical measure that quantifies the ability of a manufacturing process to produce output within the design specification limits. It is defined as the ratio of the **tolerance width** (the voice of the customer) to the **process spread** (the voice of the process):

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

where $USL$ and $LSL$ are the upper and lower specification limits, and $\sigma$ is the process standard deviation. **Significance:** (i) It indicates whether the process *can* meet specifications (a $C_p \ge 1.00$ is the bare minimum, $C_p \ge 1.33$ is the industry standard). (ii) It allows comparison of different machines or processes on a common scale. (iii) It is used to set inspection frequency in quality control plans. (iv) It is the foundation of Six-Sigma quality programs.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ with a suitable example. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | $C_p$ | $C_{pk}$ |
|---|---|---|
| **What it measures** | Potential capability (spread only) | Actual capability (spread + centering) |
| **Sensitivity to mean shift** | None — assumes perfect centering | Yes — penalizes off-centering |
| **Formula** | $\dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | $C_p(1-k)$ or $\min\!\left(\dfrac{USL-\bar{X}}{3\sigma},\dfrac{\bar{X}-LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ |
| **Value** | Always $\ge C_{pk}$ | $\le C_p$ |
| **When $C_p = C_{pk}$** | Process is perfectly centered | Same as $C_p$ |
| **Industrial use** | Machine selection | Process approval |

**Example:** A shaft of diameter $25 \pm 0.10$ mm with $\sigma = 0.025$ mm, $\bar{X} = 25.00$ mm gives $C_p = C_{pk} = 1.33$. If the same process shifts to $\bar{X} = 25.06$ mm, $C_p$ remains 1.33, but $C_{pk}$ drops to 0.67 (incapable).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: Comprehensive Process Capability Analysis `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Question:** A CNC milling machine produces a critical pocket dimension. The specification is $50.00 \pm 0.08$ mm. Twenty-five subgroups of size 5 are taken and the following data is obtained:
- Sample mean: $\bar{X} = 50.03$ mm
- Average within-subgroup range: $\bar{R} = 0.06$ mm
- Subgroup size $n = 5$

**(a)** Calculate the Process Capability Indices $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$. Assume $d_2 = 2.326$ for $n = 5$. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Comment on the capability of the process. If the process is not capable, suggest TWO corrective actions. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a): Compute $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$

**Step 1 — Identify the spec limits and tolerance:**

$$USL = 50.08 \text{ mm}, \quad LSL = 49.92 \text{ mm}, \quad T_w = 0.16 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Estimate process standard deviation from $\bar{R}$:**

$$\sigma = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.06}{2.326} = 0.02580 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $C_p$:**

$$C_p = \frac{T_w}{6\sigma} = \frac{0.16}{6 \times 0.02580} = \frac{0.16}{0.1548} = 1.034$$

**Step 4 — Compute one-sided indices:**

$$C_{pu} = \frac{USL - \bar{X}}{3\sigma} = \frac{50.08 - 50.03}{3 \times 0.02580} = \frac{0.05}{0.0774} = 0.646$$

$$C_{pl} = \frac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{50.03 - 49.92}{0.0774} = \frac{0.11}{0.0774} = 1.421$$

**Step 5 — Compute $C_{pk}$:**

$$C_{pk} = \min(0.646,\; 1.421) = 0.646$$

**Step 6 — Centering factor $k$:**

$$T_m = 50.00, \quad k = \frac{\vert 50.03 - 50.00 \vert}{0.08} = 0.375$$

#### Part (b): Capability Verdict and Corrective Actions

**Verdict:**
- $C_p = 1.034$ — the process is *marginally* capable in potential spread.
- $C_{pk} = 0.646$ — **the process is INCAPABLE in actual capability** (less than 1.00).
- The $k = 0.375$ shows the process mean is shifted 37.5 % away from the target.

**Two Corrective Actions:**

1. **Recenter the process:** Adjust the CNC tool offset (X-axis datum) so that the mean shifts from 50.03 mm back to 50.00 mm. This single action will raise $C_{pk}$ from 0.646 to ~1.03, making the process capable.

2. **Reduce process variation:** Inspect the cutting tool for wear (a worn end-mill increases chatter and $\sigma$), check the work-holding fixture rigidity, and verify the spindle bearing condition. Reducing $\sigma$ from 0.0258 to 0.020 mm would raise $C_p$ to 1.33 (industry standard).

---

### Question B: Alternative Comprehensive Problem `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Question:** A grinding operation produces bearings with a diameter specification of $20.00 \pm 0.05$ mm. From 30 subgroups of size 4, the following summary statistics are obtained:
- $\bar{X} = 19.985$ mm
- $\bar{s} = 0.008$ mm
- $\sigma_{lt} = 0.012$ mm (long-term)
- $c_4 = 0.9213$ for $n = 4$

**(a)** Calculate (i) the within-process standard deviation, (ii) $C_p$, (iii) $C_{pk}$, and (iv) $P_{pk}$. (8 Marks)

**(b)** Discuss the meaning of $P_{pk} < C_{pk}$ in this context and what it indicates about the process. (6 Marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a): Four Computations

**(i) Within-process standard deviation (using $c_4$ bias correction):**

$$\sigma_{within} = \frac{\bar{s}}{c_4} = \frac{0.008}{0.9213} = 0.00868 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) $C_p$:**

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma_{within}} = \frac{0.10}{6 \times 0.00868} = \frac{0.10}{0.05208} = 1.920$$

**(iii) $C_{pk}$:**

$$C_{pu} = \frac{20.05 - 19.985}{3 \times 0.00868} = \frac{0.065}{0.02604} = 2.496$$

$$C_{pl} = \frac{19.985 - 19.95}{0.02604} = \frac{0.035}{0.02604} = 1.344$$

$$C_{pk} = \min(2.496,\; 1.344) = 1.344$$

**(iv) $P_{pk}$:**

$$P_{pu} = \frac{20.05 - 19.985}{3 \times 0.012} = \frac{0.065}{0.036} = 1.806$$

$$P_{pl} = \frac{19.985 - 19.95}{0.036} = \frac{0.035}{0.036} = 0.972$$

$$P_{pk} = \min(1.806,\; 0.972) = 0.972$$

#### Part (b): Discussion — Why $P_{pk} < C_{pk}$?

**Numerical observation:** $P_{pk} = 0.972 < C_{pk} = 1.344$.

**Meaning:** Within a short time window, the process appears highly capable ($C_{pk} = 1.344$). But over the long term (multiple lots, multiple shifts, multiple operators), extra sources of variation creep in — tool wear across the production run, raw material lot-to-lot variation, ambient temperature changes, operator technique differences. These are captured in the **overall $\sigma_{lt} = 0.012$ mm** (which is 38 % larger than the within-subgroup $\sigma$).

**What it indicates:**
1. The process is **not stable in the long run** — there are assignable causes that have not been eliminated.
2. The shift is downward (mean = 19.985 < target = 20.00), and the **lower side is the limiting factor** ($P_{pl} = 0.972$).
3. **Customer risk:** A customer who audits the long-term records will see $P_{pk} < 1.00$ and reject the supplier. The process must be improved before a PPAP (Production Part Approval Process) submission.

**Recommended actions:** Implement a tool-wear compensation schedule in the CNC program; institute a control chart on subgroups to detect drift early; standardize operator procedures; consider in-process gauging with automatic feedback.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $c_4$ or $d_2$ bias correction** — if the problem gives $\bar{R}$ or $\bar{s}$, you **must** divide by the corresponding constant. Skipping this loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $C_{pk}$ with $C_p$** — many students report only $C_p$ and call it "capability." You **must** compute both.
> 3. **Forgetting the centering factor** — if $\bar{X} \ne T_m$, the $C_{pk}$ value will be different from $C_p$, and the examiner expects you to comment on this.
> 4. **Wrong units** — always keep $\sigma$ and tolerances in the **same units** (mm with mm, μm with μm). Mixing causes 100 % error.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** — explicitly write: *"Assuming the process is in statistical control and the data is normally distributed."* Examiners reward assumption statements.
> 6. **Incorrect rounding** — keep 3–4 significant figures in intermediate steps; only round the final answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Process Capability** quantifies the ability of a *stable* process to meet design specifications.
- **$C_p$** = potential capability, **$C_{pk}$** = actual capability (with centering).
- **Key formula triplet:** $C_p = \dfrac{USL-LSL}{6\sigma}$, $C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl})$, $C_{pk} = C_p(1-k)$.
- **Industry benchmark values:** $C_{pk} \ge 1.33$ (acceptable), $\ge 1.67$ (excellent), $\ge 2.00$ (Six-Sigma).
- **3σ process** ≈ 2,700 ppm defective; **6σ process** ≈ 3.4 ppm defective.
- **$\sigma$ estimation:** Use $\bar{R}/d_2$ (range method) or $\bar{s}/c_4$ (S-bar method) for short-term; use overall standard deviation for $P_p$, $P_{pk}$.
- **Machine Capability ($C_m$):** Tested with one operator, one material, one machine, no time variation — used before a process is approved.
- **Process Performance ($P_p$, $P_{pk}$):** Uses long-term $\sigma$ — used for customer audits and PPAP submissions.
- **$P_{pk} < C_{pk}$** always indicates that extra (between-subgroup) variation is present.
- **One-sided specifications:** When only USL or only LSL exists (e.g., strength, surface roughness, minimum hardness), use only $C_{pu}$ or $C_{pl}$.
- **Assumption checklist before computing capability:** (1) Process is in statistical control, (2) Data is normally distributed, (3) Sample size $\ge 25$ subgroups, (4) Subgroup size 2–5 typical.
- **Six-Sigma DMAIC cycle:** Define → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control is the standard roadmap for improving a low $C_{pk}$ process to world-class levels.
- **KTU 14-mark format:** Always split into (a) computation (7 marks) and (b) analysis + corrective action (7 marks).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT404 - MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **PBMRT404 – Manufacturing Process (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, the topic **"Applications"** under Module 4 encompasses the **industrial deployment, suitability mapping, and engineering utilization** of the manufacturing processes covered in the module. These processes typically include **Powder Metallurgy, Non-Conventional / Unconventional Machining Processes, Sheet Metal Forming, Casting, and advanced Joining Techniques**. The "Applications" topic serves as a **synthesis layer** that connects process parameters, material behaviour, and product requirements to real-world industrial use cases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *Applications refer to the systematic selection of a manufacturing process based on the geometry, material, production volume, tolerance, surface finish, mechanical properties, and economic feasibility of the target component.*

The KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned, OBE-based) demands that students not only memorize process steps but also demonstrate the cognitive ability to **select the right process for the right product** — a higher-order skill mapped to Bloom's **Analyze** and **Evaluate** levels (CO3 / CO4).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Kitchen & the Restaurant"**
>
> Imagine a chef (engineer) preparing meals (products) for a customer (industry):
> - A **stew** requires slow cooking → analogous to **investment casting** (slow, accurate, expensive).
> - A **microwave meal** is fast and convenient → analogous to **powder metallurgy** (fast, near-net shape, less machining).
> - A **sandwich** uses layering of cold cuts → analogous to **lamination / cladding** in sheet metal.
> - A **deep-fried snack** is mass-produced at a roadside stall → analogous to **die casting** (high volume, low unit cost).
>
> Just as a chef chooses the cooking method based on **what the customer wants, how many portions, how fast, and the budget**, an engineer chooses a manufacturing process based on **material, geometry, quantity, tolerance, and cost**.

This analogy clarifies that there is **no universally "best" process** — selection is always **context-driven**.

---

### 1.3 Key Metrics Governing Process Selection

The following **standard metrics** are universally applied when discussing manufacturing applications:

- **Material Utilization Ratio (MUR)** = (Mass of finished part) / (Mass of raw material input)
- **Production Rate** measured in **pieces per hour (PPH)** or **parts per shift (PPS)**
- **Surface Roughness (Ra)** measured in **micrometres (µm)**
- **Dimensional Tolerance** typically **IT6 – IT13** as per **ISO 286**
- **Specific Energy Consumption** measured in **J/mm³** for material removal processes
- **Tool Wear Rate** measured in **µm/min** or **mm³/min**

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently test students on **why one process is preferred over another for a given application**. Memorize the **keywords** (e.g., "near-net shape", "high volume", "complex geometry", "hard material") that are typically used in such exam questions.

---

### 1.4 Classification of Application Domains

Manufacturing process applications are broadly classified into:

| Domain | Typical Products | Primary Process |
|---|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Engine blocks, gears, pistons | Casting, P/M, Forming |
| **Aerospace** | Turbine blades, structural panels | Investment casting, superplastic forming |
| **Electronics** | Heat sinks, connectors, lead frames | Powder metallurgy, stamping |
| **Biomedical** | Implants (hip, dental) | Investment casting, P/M of Ti alloys |
| **Tooling** | Cutting tools, dies | Powder metallurgy (cemented carbides) |
| **Consumer goods** | Cutlery, watches, jewellery | Die casting, investment casting |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Process Selection Logic — Step-by-Step

When an engineer is asked **"Which manufacturing process should I use for this product?"**, the decision is made through a structured **5-stage filtering logic**:

1. **Stage 1 — Material Compatibility:** Does the process accommodate the chosen material (e.g., ferrous vs. non-ferrous, melt temperature, reactivity)?
2. **Stage 2 — Geometry Feasibility:** Can the process create the required **internal features**, **undercuts**, **thin walls**, and **3D complexity**?
3. **Stage 3 — Volume Suitability:** Is the production **batch size** (prototype, low, medium, high) compatible with the process economics?
4. **Stage 4 — Tolerance & Surface Finish:** Can the process achieve the required **IT grade** and **Ra value** without secondary machining?
5. **Stage 5 — Economic & Sustainability Analysis:** Does the process deliver acceptable **unit cost**, **energy efficiency**, and **recyclability**?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Justify the selection of powder metallurgy for the manufacturing of a self-lubricating bearing."* The answer **must** address **at least 3 of the 5 stages** above (typically stages 1, 2, and 4).

---

### 2.2 Why Process Selection Matters (The Engineering "Why")

The manufacturing process directly influences:

- **Mechanical Properties** (grain structure, porosity, residual stress)
- **Dimensional Accuracy** (dimensional tolerance achievable)
- **Surface Integrity** (Ra, micro-hardness, residual stress profile)
- **Cost Structure** (tooling cost amortized over volume)
- **Lead Time** (time from raw material to finished part)

For example, a **gear** can be manufactured by:
- **Casting** (cheap, but lower strength and poor surface)
- **Machining** (accurate, but material wasteful and slow)
- **Powder Metallurgy** (near-net shape, self-lubricating, economical at high volume)
- **Hot Forging** (excellent strength, requires expensive dies)

> The "best" choice depends on **all five stages above, simultaneously**.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates **all quantitative expressions** required for solving KTU numerical/analytical questions on this topic.

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Application Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Material Utilization Ratio (MUR)** | $\text{MUR} = \dfrac{m_f}{m_r}$ | dimensionless | P/M, forming vs. machining comparison |
| **Specific Energy** | $U = \dfrac{F_c \cdot v}{M R R}$ | $\text{J/mm}^3$ | Non-conventional machining |
| **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** | $\text{MRR} = v \cdot f \cdot d$ | $\text{mm}^3/\text{s}$ | Conventional / unconventional machining |
| **Production Rate** | $R_p = \dfrac{3600}{T_{ct}}$ | $\text{pieces/hr}$ | Cycle time based process selection |
| **Unit Cost** | $C_u = \dfrac{C_{tool} + C_{mat} + C_{lab}}{N}$ | $\text{INR/piece}$ | Economic feasibility |
| **Dimensional Tolerance Grade** | $\text{IT grade} = \text{function of nominal size}$ | $\mu\text{m}$ | ISO 286 system |
| **Powder Compact Density Ratio** | $\rho_r = \dfrac{\rho_{compact}}{\rho_{solid}} \times 100\%$ | % | P/M sintering analysis |
| **Strain in Forming** | $\varepsilon = \ln \left( \dfrac{h_0}{h_f} \right)$ | dimensionless | Sheet metal forming |
| **Spring-back Angle** | $\theta_{sb} = \theta_{tool} - \theta_{part}$ | degrees | Sheet metal bending |
| **Bend Allowance** | $B_A = \dfrac{\pi}{2}(R + K_t \cdot T)$ | mm | Sheet metal fabrication |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners will **not** ask numericals on every formula above. However, knowing the **form and physical meaning** of each is essential for descriptive answers.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The application-mapping of manufacturing processes is used in:

- **Design for Manufacturing (DFM):** Choosing a process early in the design phase reduces iteration cost.
- **Computer-Aided Process Planning (CAPP):** Software systems use the 5-stage logic above to **auto-suggest** processes.
- **Industry 4.0 / Smart Manufacturing:** Digital twins of processes simulate cost and lead time **before** physical production.
- **Reverse Engineering:** Given a finished part, engineers identify the process used (e.g., parting line → casting; flow lines → forging).

> [!TIP]
> A classic KTU question is: *"From a fracture surface, how do you identify whether the part was cast or forged?"* — Answer: **Cast parts show dendritic/grain structure, while forged parts show flow lines aligned with the stress direction.**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Material Utilization Ratio (MUR) for Powder Metallurgy vs. Machining

This derivation is a **high-yield KTU numerical** and demonstrates the **analytical comparison** of two processes for the same component.

**Problem Setup:**
A cylindrical bushing has:
- Inner diameter: $d_i = 20 \text{ mm}$
- Outer diameter: $d_o = 40 \text{ mm}$
- Height: $h = 30 \text{ mm}$
- Density of solid material: $\rho = 7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3$

**For Powder Metallurgy (P/M) route:**

The bushing is produced **directly** by compacting powder, so the input mass equals the part mass (ideal case).

$$
V_{P/M} = \frac{\pi}{4} (d_o^2 - d_i^2) \cdot h
$$

$$
V_{P/M} = \frac{\pi}{4} (40^2 - 20^2) \cdot 30
$$

$$
V_{P/M} = \frac{\pi}{4} (1600 - 400) \cdot 30 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 1200 \cdot 30
$$

$$
V_{P/M} = 9000 \pi \approx 28274.33 \text{ mm}^3 = 28.27 \text{ cm}^3
$$

$$
m_{P/M} = \rho \cdot V_{P/M} = 7.85 \cdot 28.27 \approx 221.95 \text{ g}
$$

Therefore,

$$
\text{MUR}_{P/M} = \frac{m_f}{m_r} = \frac{221.95}{221.95} = 1.0 \quad (100\%)
$$

**For Machining Route:**

The bushing is machined from a solid cylindrical billet of diameter $D_{billet} = 40 \text{ mm}$ (to provide the outer diameter) and height $H_{billet} = 32 \text{ mm}$ (allowing 2 mm facing allowance).

$$
V_{billet} = \frac{\pi}{4} D_{billet}^2 \cdot H_{billet}
$$

$$
V_{billet} = \frac{\pi}{4} (40)^2 \cdot 32 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 1600 \cdot 32
$$

$$
V_{billet} = 12800 \pi \approx 40212.39 \text{ mm}^3 = 40.21 \text{ cm}^3
$$

$$
m_{billet} = \rho \cdot V_{billet} = 7.85 \cdot 40.21 \approx 315.65 \text{ g}
$$

The mass of the **finished part** is the same as in the P/M case ($221.95 \text{ g}$). The mass of **chips** is:

$$
m_{chips} = m_{billet} - m_{part} = 315.65 - 221.95 = 93.70 \text{ g}
$$

Therefore,

$$
\text{MUR}_{machining} = \frac{m_f}{m_r} = \frac{221.95}{315.65} \approx 0.7032 \quad (70.32\%)
$$

**Comparison (KTU 2024 Analytical Verdict):**

$$
\frac{\text{MUR}_{P/M}}{\text{MUR}_{machining}} = \frac{1.0}{0.7032} \approx 1.422
$$

This means **P/M saves ~29.68% of raw material** compared to machining for this component. At a production volume of $N = 100{,}000$ pieces, the material savings translate to:

$$
\Delta m = 93.70 \times 100{,}000 = 9{,}370{,}000 \text{ g} = 9370 \text{ kg}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **textbook KTU numerical**. Master the **step-wise mass-balance method** and the **final economic interpretation**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bend Allowance for Sheet Metal Application

**Problem Setup:**
A sheet metal component requires a **90° bend** in a 2 mm thick aluminium sheet. The bend radius (inside) is $R = 3 \text{ mm}$. K-factor (neutral axis position) for soft aluminium = $K_t = 0.33$.

**Step 1 — Neutral axis radius:**

$$
R_{neutral} = R + K_t \cdot T = 3 + (0.33)(2) = 3 + 0.66 = 3.66 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Arc length subtended (for 90° bend, $\theta = \pi/2$ rad):**

$$
B_A = \theta \cdot R_{neutral} = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot 3.66 = 1.733 \cdot 3.66
$$

$$
B_A \approx 6.343 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Flat pattern length:**

For a part with two flanges of length $L_1$ and $L_2$ joined by this bend, the **flat blank length** is:

$$
L_{blank} = L_1 + L_2 + B_A - 2T
$$

The last term $(-2T)$ accounts for material consumed in the bend region (two thickness values, one on each side).

> [!NOTE]
> KTU may ask the **reverse** problem: *"Given a flat pattern length of 100 mm and equal flanges, find the flange length."* — This tests whether the student can **rearrange** the equation correctly.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Application-to-Process Mapping

This is the **highest-density revision tool** for KTU 2024 examinations. It directly maps products to processes with **justification keywords**.

| Application / Product | Material | Recommended Process | Justification (KTU Keywords) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Self-lubricating bearing** | Bronze / Fe | Powder Metallurgy | **Porosity** holds lubricant; **near-net shape** |
| **Gas turbine blade (with cooling channels)** | Ni-superalloy | Investment Casting | **Complex internal geometry**, **ceramic shell** |
| **Aluminium pistons (high volume)** | Al-Si alloy | Die Casting (gravity) | **High volume**, **good surface**, **low cost** |
| **Turbine blade (single crystal)** | Ni-superalloy | Investment casting + directional solidification | **Single-crystal** grain structure |
| **Cutting tool inserts** | WC-Co | Powder Metallurgy (sintering) | **Hard phases**, **liquid-phase sintering** |
| **Car body panel** | Steel sheet | Stamping / Deep drawing | **High volume**, **low thickness**, **good finish** |
| **Hip joint implant** | Ti-6Al-4V | Investment Casting / P/M | **Biocompatibility**, **complex shape**, **strength** |
| **Heat sink (fin array)** | Al | Die casting / extrusion | **High thermal conductivity**, **thin fins** |
| **Watch case** | Brass / SS | Investment casting | **High dimensional accuracy**, **fine detail** |
| **Pipe elbow** | MS / SS | Hot forging / bending | **Strength**, **weldability** |
| **I.C. engine block** | Cast iron | Sand casting | **Large size**, **low tooling cost** |
| **Connecting rod** | Steel | Hot forging | **High strength**, **fiber flow** |
| **Lead frame (electronics)** | Cu alloy | Stamping + P/M | **Precision**, **high conductivity** |
| **Aero engine disc** | Ti / Ni alloy | Isothermal forging | **Tight tolerance**, **high temperature** |
| **Spark plug insulator** | Alumina | Powder Metallurgy (ceramic) | **Electrical insulation**, **high temperature** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Manufacturing Process Selection for a Given Product

This diagram captures the **decision logic** an engineer follows when selecting a manufacturing process. It is the **conceptual heart** of Module 4 and is a high-frequency topic in KTU viva and written examinations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Product Requirement Defined] --> B[Identify Material Type and Properties]
    B --> C[Define Geometry and Feature Complexity]
    C --> D[Estimate Production Volume]
    D --> E[Specify Tolerance and Surface Finish]
    E --> F{Is Material Ferrous High Melt}
    F -- Yes --> G[Check Casting vs Forging vs P/M]
    F -- No --> H[Check Casting vs Forming vs Machining]
    G --> I{Volume Greater Than 10000}
    I -- Yes --> J[Prefer Die Casting or P/M]
    I -- No --> K[Prefer Sand Casting or Machining]
    H --> L{Volume Greater Than 5000}
    L -- Yes --> M[Prefer Die Casting or Stamping]
    L -- No --> N[Prefer Sand Casting or Manual Forming]
    J --> O[Verify Tolerance IT Grade 7 or Better]
    K --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P{Tolerance Achievable}
    P -- Yes --> Q[Finalize Process]
    P -- No --> R[Add Secondary Machining Operation]
    R --> Q
    Q --> S[Document Process Route and Cost]
    S --> T[End: Process Selected]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above is a **simplified representative flow** for exam purposes. In real industry, process selection is iterated through CAPP software using **weighted scoring matrices** and **Group Technology (GT) codes**.

---

### 4.2 Schematic Block Diagram — Application-Process Family Linkage

This second diagram shows the **inter-family relationships** of processes and their primary application clusters, which is a **favourite KTU 2-mark or 3-mark diagram question**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Forming
        F1[Hot Forging]
        F2[Cold Stamping]
        F3[Extrusion]
        F4[Deep Drawing]
    end
    subgraph Casting
        C1[Sand Casting]
        C2[Investment Casting]
        C3[Die Casting]
        C4[Centrifugal Casting]
    end
    subgraph PowderMet
        P1[Compaction plus Sintering]
        P2[Hot Isostatic Pressing]
        P3[Metal Injection Molding]
    end
    subgraph Joining
        J1[Arc Welding]
        J2[Resistance Welding]
        J3[Brazing and Soldering]
    end
    A1[Aerospace] --> F1
    A1 --> C2
    A1 --> P2
    A2[Automotive High Volume] --> C3
    A2 --> F2
    A2 --> P1
    A3[Electronics] --> P1
    A3 --> J2
    A4[Biomedical] --> C2
    A4 --> P1
    A5[Tooling] --> P1
    A5 --> F1
```

> [!TIP]
> In a KTU exam, you may be asked: *"Draw a flow chart showing the process selection for a self-lubricating bearing."* Use the **5-stage logic** from Section 2.1 in your answer. The first schematic above is the **ideal answer structure**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — 3 Mark Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
*List any three industrial applications of Powder Metallurgy and state the property of the process that makes it suitable for each application.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Self-lubricating bearings** — The inherent **porosity (10–25%)** of P/M parts allows them to absorb and retain lubricating oil, providing self-lubrication during service.
2. **Cemented carbide cutting tool inserts** — P/M can combine a **refractory carbide phase (WC)** with a **ductile binder phase (Co)** in a single sintering step, achieving hardness above **HRA 90**.
3. **Sintered gears for office equipment and washing machines** — P/M produces **near-net shape** gears with **minimal machining**, **no flash**, and **low noise** operation due to the porous structure that dampens vibration.

> (3 × 1 mark = 3 marks; **keyword bolding** is the valuation expectation.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
*Differentiate between sand casting and investment casting based on **surface finish, dimensional tolerance, and typical application**.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Sand Casting | Investment Casting |
|---|---|---|
| **Surface Finish (Ra)** | 12.5 – 25 µm (rough) | 0.8 – 3.2 µm (excellent) |
| **Dimensional Tolerance** | ± 0.5 to ± 2.0 mm | ± 0.05 to ± 0.15 mm |
| **Typical Application** | I.C. engine blocks, large machine beds | Gas turbine blades, jewellery, dental implants |

> (1 mark for surface finish, 1 for tolerance, 1 for application — tabulated form preferred for valuation.)

---

### Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]*

**(a)** *Explain the process of **Powder Metallurgy** with a neat flow diagram. List **any four industrial applications** and justify each with the **specific property of the process** that enables that application.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A steel component of finished mass **250 g** is to be produced by two alternative routes: **machining from a billet** and **powder metallurgy**. The machining process uses a billet of mass **480 g** while the P/M route uses powder of mass **258 g** (the extra 8 g being process loss).*
*Calculate:*
*(i) The Material Utilization Ratio (MUR) for each route. (2 marks)*
*(ii) The **percentage material saving** of the better route over the other. (2 marks)*
*(iii) Comment on the **economic and environmental implications** of the better route at a production volume of **50,000 pieces/year**, given that raw material costs **₹120/kg** and disposal of chips costs **₹20/kg**. (3 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Process Flow & Applications of Powder Metallurgy:**

The P/M process consists of the following **four mandatory steps** plus optional secondary operations:

1. **Powder Production** — via atomization, reduction, electrolysis, or mechanical crushing.
2. **Blending/Mixing** — powders of different metals/alloys + lubricant mixed uniformly.
3. **Compaction** — powder pressed in a die at **400–800 MPa** to form a "green compact".
4. **Sintering** — heated to **0.7 – 0.9 × melting temperature** in a controlled atmosphere furnace; particles bond by diffusion.
5. **Secondary Operations** (optional) — sizing, coining, impregnation, infiltration, machining, heat treatment.

**Flow Diagram (text-based for exam sheet):**

```
Powder Production → Blending → Compaction → Sintering → Optional Finishing → Inspection
```

**Four Industrial Applications with Justification:**

| Application | Justifying Property |
|---|---|
| **Self-lubricating bearings** | Controlled **porosity (10–25%)** retains oil |
| **Cemented carbide cutting tools** | Ability to combine **refractory + binder phases** without melting |
| **Sintered ferrous gears** | **Near-net shape** manufacturing reduces machining cost |
| **Porous electrodes (e.g., Ni-Cd batteries)** | High **specific surface area** from interconnected porosity |

(7 marks: 3 for process flow, 4 for applications table)

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) MUR Calculation:**

For **machining route:**

$$
\text{MUR}_{mach} = \frac{m_{finished}}{m_{raw}} = \frac{250}{480} = 0.5208 \quad (52.08\%)
$$

For **P/M route:**

$$
\text{MUR}_{P/M} = \frac{m_{finished}}{m_{raw}} = \frac{250}{258} = 0.9690 \quad (96.90\%)
$$

**(ii) Percentage Material Saving:**

The "better" route is **P/M** (higher MUR). The material saved **per piece** is:

$$
\Delta m = 480 - 258 = 222 \text{ g/piece}
$$

Percentage saving = $\dfrac{222}{480} \times 100\% = 46.25\%$

Alternatively, computing MUR improvement:

$$
\% \text{ improvement} = \frac{0.9690 - 0.5208}{0.5208} \times 100\% \approx 86.06\%
$$

**(iii) Economic & Environmental Implications (for 50,000 pieces/year):**

**Material Cost (Machining):**

$$
\text{Raw material} = 480 \times 50{,}000 = 24{,}000{,}000 \text{ g} = 24{,}000 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
\text{Cost} = 24{,}000 \times 120 = \text{₹}28{,}80{,}000
$$

**Chip Disposal Cost (Machining):**

$$
\text{Chips} = (480 - 250) \times 50{,}000 = 11{,}500 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
\text{Disposal Cost} = 11{,}500 \times 20 = \text{₹}2{,}30{,}000
$$

**Total Machining Material-related Cost:**

$$
C_{mach, total} = 28{,}80{,}000 + 2{,}30{,}000 = \text{₹}31{,}10{,}000
$$

**Material Cost (P/M):**

$$
\text{Raw material} = 258 \times 50{,}000 = 12{,}900 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
\text{Cost} = 12{,}900 \times 120 = \text{₹}15{,}48{,}000
$$

**Total P/M Material-related Cost:**

$$
C_{P/M, total} = \text{₹}15{,}48{,}000
$$

**Savings per year:**

$$
\Delta C = 31{,}10{,}000 - 15{,}48{,}000 = \text{₹}15{,}62{,}000
$$

**Environmental Comment:** P/M eliminates **~11,500 kg/year of swarf/chips**, reducing landfill load, cutting energy used in chip recycling, and lowering the carbon footprint. The process is **intrinsically sustainable** for high-volume production.

(2 + 2 + 3 = 7 marks for part b)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** *With the help of a **classification chart**, describe the various **casting processes**. For each process, give **one industrial application** and **one characteristic limitation** that defines its operating envelope. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *In sheet metal forming, a 2 mm thick steel sheet is to be bent through **90°** with an **inside bend radius of 4 mm**. The neutral axis K-factor is **0.4**. Calculate:*
*(i) The **bend allowance**. (2 marks)*
*(ii) The **outside fibre stretch** (i.e., the elongation of the outer surface of the bend). (2 marks)*
*(iii) If the part has two flanges of **40 mm each**, calculate the **flat blank length** required. (3 marks)*

**Model Solution (Key Steps):**

**(a) Classification of Casting Processes:**

| Process | Application | Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| **Sand Casting** | Engine blocks, machine beds | Rough surface (Ra 12.5–25 µm) |
| **Investment Casting** | Turbine blades, jewellery | High cost for large parts |
| **Die Casting** | Aluminium pistons, housings | High tooling cost; limited to non-ferrous |
| **Centrifugal Casting** | Pipes, cylinder liners | Limited to axisymmetric parts |
| **Shell Moulding** | Automotive valve bodies | Resin cost; pattern heating required |
| **Continuous Casting** | Billets, slabs, blooms | Capital intensive; limited cross-sections |

(7 marks — classification 3, applications 2, limitations 2)

**(b) Sheet Metal Numerical:**

**(i) Bend Allowance:**

Given: $T = 2 \text{ mm}$, $R = 4 \text{ mm}$, $K_t = 0.4$, $\theta = 90° = \pi/2 \text{ rad}$.

Neutral axis radius:

$$
R_{neutral} = R + K_t \cdot T = 4 + (0.4)(2) = 4.8 \text{ mm}
$$

Bend allowance:

$$
B_A = \theta \cdot R_{neutral} = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot 4.8 = 7.5398 \approx 7.54 \text{ mm}
$$

**(ii) Outside Fibre Stretch:**

The outer surface is at radius $R_{outer} = R + T = 4 + 2 = 6 \text{ mm}$. The neutral axis is at $4.8 \text{ mm}$. The stretch ratio of the outer fibre relative to the neutral axis is:

$$
\lambda_{out} = \frac{R_{outer}}{R_{neutral}} = \frac{6.0}{4.8} = 1.25
$$

The neutral axis arc length is $B_A = 7.54 \text{ mm}$. The outer arc length is:

$$
L_{outer} = \lambda_{out} \cdot B_A = 1.25 \times 7.54 = 9.425 \text{ mm}
$$

Outer fibre stretch (in mm):

$$
\Delta L = L_{outer} - B_A = 9.425 - 7.54 = 1.885 \text{ mm}
$$

**(iii) Flat Blank Length:**

$$
L_{blank} = L_1 + L_2 + B_A - 2T
$$

$$
L_{blank} = 40 + 40 + 7.54 - 2(2) = 87.54 - 4 = 83.54 \text{ mm}
$$

(7 marks distributed: 2 + 2 + 3)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **MUR Confusion:** Many students compute MUR for P/M as **(mass of powder / mass of part) instead of the reverse**. The correct formula is $m_f / m_r$, so MUR is always **≤ 1.0** in the part-vs-input sense.
> 2. **Bend Allowance Direction:** Forgetting to **subtract 2T** in the flat blank length equation is the most common error, costing 1 full mark.
> 3. **Application Justification:** A 3-mark application question requires **3 distinct points** with **3 distinct properties**. Do not write "P/M is used for bearings because it is porous" three times for three bearings.
> 4. **Diagram in Part B (14 marks):** A flow chart or process map is **mandatory** for full marks in process-description questions. Drawing only text loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Process Selection Justification:** When asked to **justify** process selection, you must mention **at least one disadvantage** of the **unselected** process to demonstrate **comparative thinking**. This is a KTU 2024 OBE-aligned expectation.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Process selection is governed by 5 stages:** Material, Geometry, Volume, Tolerance, Economics.
- **Material Utilization Ratio (MUR)** = $m_f / m_r$ — always $\leq 1.0$; higher = more efficient.
- **Powder Metallurgy excels for:** self-lubricating parts, cemented carbides, porous electrodes, near-net gears.
- **Investment Casting excels for:** complex aerospace / biomedical parts with fine detail and excellent surface finish.
- **Die Casting is for:** high-volume, non-ferrous, thin-walled components (e.g., Al pistons).
- **Bend Allowance formula:** $B_A = \theta \cdot (R + K_t \cdot T)$ — remember K-factor depends on material and bend type.
- **Flat Blank Length formula:** $L_{blank} = L_1 + L_2 + B_A - 2T$.
- **Sheet metal forming** is preferred for **thin (0.5–6 mm)**, **ductile** materials in **high volume** applications.
- **MUR is the single most important KTU numerical concept** under the "Applications" topic.
- **Always justify process selection** using **3 of the 5 stages** of the decision logic.
- **For 14-mark questions**, **diagrams (flow charts, schematics) are mandatory** for full marks.
- **For 3-mark questions**, **tabular or bulleted formats** with **bold keywords** score highest.
- **Specific Energy, MRR, and Bend Allowance** are the three most-tested formulas across KTU past papers.
- **Sustainability angle** (material waste reduction, energy efficiency) is increasingly asked in KTU 2024 OBE-aligned questions — mention it whenever relevant.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Additive Manufacturing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT404 - MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Additive Manufacturing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Additive Manufacturing (AM)** is defined by **ASTM/ISO 52900:2021** as the *"process of joining materials to make parts from 3D model data, usually layer upon layer, as opposed to subtractive manufacturing and formative manufacturing methodologies."*

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Additive Manufacturing refers to a family of **layer-by-layer material consolidation processes** in which a three-dimensional solid object is fabricated directly from a **digital Computer-Aided Design (CAD) model** through the controlled, sequential addition of material — without the use of fixtures, hard tooling, or human intervention in the material-removal loop.

The process is broadly governed by the workflow:

$$\text{CAD Model} \rightarrow \text{STL/AMF File} \rightarrow \text{Slicing (G-code)} \rightarrow \text{Layer-by-Layer Build} \rightarrow \text{Post-Processing}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Inkjet Printer for Solids"

Imagine a conventional **2D inkjet printer** that sprays ink dots onto paper to create a flat image. Now, instead of spraying ink on a single sheet, imagine that the printer:

1. Prints **one thin horizontal layer of material** (not ink) on a build platform.
2. The platform **drops down** by a tiny distance (the layer thickness).
3. The printer prints the **next layer on top** of the previous one.
4. This repeats **hundreds or thousands of times** until a fully solid 3D object emerges — almost as if the object "grew" out of nothing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Traditional manufacturing is **subtractive** (you start with a block of material and *cut away* the waste). Additive Manufacturing is the exact philosophical opposite — you start with *nothing* and *add* only the material that is needed, layer by layer, like building a sandcastle one slice at a time.

## 1.3 Why Additive Manufacturing Matters — The "Why" Behind the Hype

- **Zero Tooling:** No moulds, dies, or jigs are required, making it ideal for low-volume, high-complexity parts.
- **Geometric Freedom:** Internal lattices, topology-optimized structures, and undercuts become trivial.
- **Mass Customization:** Each part can be unique without retooling (e.g., patient-specific bone implants).
- **Lead Time Collapse:** Prototypes that took **6–12 weeks** in conventional tooling now take **hours to days**.
- **Material Efficiency:** Buy-to-fly ratio approaches **1:1**, drastically reducing aerospace scrap.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following metrics are universally accepted in KTU examinations and global AM literature:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Layer Thickness | $t_l$ | **20 – 200 μm** | Controls vertical resolution |
| Build Volume | $V_b$ | **50 mm³ – 1 m³** | Maximum part envelope |
| Dimensional Accuracy | $\delta$ | **± 0.05 – 0.3 mm** | Deviation from nominal CAD |
| Surface Roughness | $R_a$ | **1 – 50 μm** | Function of stair-stepping & powders |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme explicitly expects students to (a) classify the seven ASTM-standard AM process families, (b) describe one process from each family in detail, and (c) compare AM with conventional (subtractive) manufacturing.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Seven ASTM/ISO 52900 Standard AM Process Families

The ASTM F42 Committee and ISO/TC 261 standardized AM into **seven canonical process categories**. KTU examiners expect students to memorize all seven along with a representative commercial technology for each.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for the Seven Families (V-B-M-P-D-S-M):**
> **V**at photopolymerization • **B**inder jetting • **M**aterial jetting • **M**aterial extrusion • **P**owder bed fusion • **D**irected energy deposition • **S**heet lamination

### 2.1.1 Vat Photopolymerization (VPP)
- **Working Principle:** A vat of **liquid photopolymer resin** is selectively cured (hardened) by a UV laser or projector.
- **Representative Technology:** **Stereolithography (SLA)** and **Digital Light Processing (DLP)**.
- **Energy Source:** UV laser (SLA) or projected UV light (DLP).
- **Materials:** Acrylate, epoxy, and vinyl ether photopolymers.

### 2.1.2 Material Jetting (MJT)
- **Working Principle:** Droplets of build material (photopolymer or wax) are jetted from inkjet-style print heads and immediately cured by UV light.
- **Representative Technology:** **PolyJet (Stratasys)**, **3D Systems' MultiJet Printing (MJP)**.
- **Unique Advantage:** **Multi-material** and **multi-color** parts in a single build.

### 2.1.3 Binder Jetting (BJT)
- **Working Principle:** A liquid bonding agent (binder) is selectively deposited onto a powder bed (metal, sand, ceramic) to glue particles together.
- **Representative Technology:** **ExOne**, **VoxelJet**.
- **Distinct Feature:** No heat source during build — the part is later **sintered/infiltrated** in a furnace.

### 2.1.4 Material Extrusion (MEX)
- **Working Principle:** A thermoplastic filament or viscous paste is **extruded** through a heated nozzle and deposited along a toolpath.
- **Representative Technology:** **Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM/FFF)**.
- **Materials:** PLA, ABS, PETG, Nylon, PEEK, ULTEM.

### 2.1.5 Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)
- **Working Principle:** A thin layer of powder is spread, and an energy source (laser/electron beam) selectively fuses regions.
- **Representative Technologies:**
  - **Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)** — polymers, nylon
  - **Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** — metals
  - **Electron Beam Melting (EBM)** — Ti6Al4V, aerospace alloys
  - **Multi Jet Fusion (MJF)** — HP's powder fusion with fusing agents

### 2.1.6 Directed Energy Deposition (DED)
- **Working Principle:** Focused thermal energy (laser, plasma, electron beam, or arc) is used to **melt material as it is deposited** through a nozzle.
- **Representative Technologies:** **Laser Engineered Net Shaping (LENS)**, **Wire Arc Additive Manufacturing (WAAM)**.
- **Unique Capability:** **Multi-axis deposition** on existing parts (repair and coating applications).

### 2.1.7 Sheet Lamination (SHL)
- **Working Principle:** Sheets of material (paper, metal foil, polymer) are bonded together and then **cut** to the cross-section.
- **Representative Technologies:** **Laminated Object Manufacturing (LOM)**, **Ultrasonic Additive Manufacturing (UAM)**.

## 2.2 Generic AM Process Chain — Step-by-Step

The complete AM process chain consists of **eight sequential stages**:

1. **Conceptual CAD Design** — Create or download a 3D model.
2. **Conversion to STL/AMF** — Triangulate the surface mesh.
3. **Repair & Orientation** — Fix errors, choose optimal build orientation.
4. **Slicing** — Generate 2D horizontal layers (G-code).
5. **Support Generation** — Add sacrificial supports for overhangs.
6. **Build Execution** — Layer-by-layer fabrication on the AM machine.
7. **Post-Processing** — Support removal, curing, sanding, infiltration.
8. **Inspection & Testing** — Dimensional metrology, CT scanning, tensile tests.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **most-asked** formulas in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE for Additive Manufacturing. Memorize these and their boundary conditions.

### 2.3.1 Stair-Stepping Surface Error (Ribeiro Model)

Theoretical surface roughness due to layer stair-stepping on a sloped surface:

$$R_t = t_l \cdot \sin(\theta) \quad \text{where} \quad \theta \in [0^\circ, 90^\circ]$$

- $R_t$ = theoretical maximum peak-to-valley roughness (mm)
- $t_l$ = layer thickness (mm)
- $\theta$ = angle of the surface relative to the build platform

> When $\theta = 0^\circ$ (vertical wall), $R_t = 0$. When $\theta = 90^\circ$ (horizontal overhang), $R_t = t_l$.

### 2.3.2 Build Time Estimation (Layer-by-Layer Model)

$$T_{build} = \frac{H}{t_l} \cdot \left( T_{layer,\,deposition} + T_{layer,\,setup} \right)$$

- $T_{build}$ = total build time (s)
- $H$ = total part height (mm)
- $t_l$ = layer thickness (mm)
- $T_{layer,\,deposition}$ = time to deposit/scan one layer (s)
- $T_{layer,\,setup}$ = recoater/roller reset time per layer (s)

### 2.3.3 Volumetric Build Rate (VBR)

$$\text{VBR} = \frac{V_{part}}{T_{build}} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{mm}^3}{\text{s}} \right]$$

Where $V_{part}$ is the bounding volume of the part.

### 2.3.4 Volumetric Energy Density (VED) for PBF/DED

$$\text{VED} = \frac{P}{v \cdot t_l \cdot h_s} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}^3} \right]$$

- $P$ = laser/E-beam power (W)
- $v$ = scan speed (mm/s)
- $t_l$ = layer thickness (mm)
- $h_s$ = hatch spacing (mm)

> **Why this matters:** VED determines whether a part has **lack-of-fusion porosity** (VED too low) or **keyholing porosity** (VED too high). The optimum window for Ti6Al4V is typically **60 – 120 J/mm³**.

### 2.3.5 STL File Size Estimation

$$N_{triangles} = \frac{\text{STL file size in bytes}}{50}$$

(Because each triangle is encoded as **50 bytes** in a binary STL: 12 floats × 4 bytes = 48 bytes + 2-byte attribute count.)

### 2.3.6 Mass of Build Material

$$m_{build} = \rho \cdot V_{part} \cdot (1 + f_s)$$

- $\rho$ = bulk density of feedstock (g/cm³)
- $f_s$ = support material fraction (typically **0.10 – 0.30**)

## 2.4 Comparative Table — Additive vs. Subtractive vs. Formative

| Parameter | Additive (AM) | Subtractive (CNC) | Formative (Casting) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Material Waste | Very low (5–10%) | High (60–90%) | Moderate (10–30%) |
| Tooling Cost | **Zero** | Moderate to high | High (moulds) |
| Lead Time | Hours–Days | Days–Weeks | Weeks–Months |
| Geometric Complexity | **Unlimited** | Limited by tool access | Limited by draft angles |
| Batch Economy | Poor (low volume) | Excellent | Excellent |
| Surface Finish | $R_a$ 1–50 μm | $R_a$ 0.2–3.2 μm | $R_a$ 3–25 μm |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace (GE LEAP fuel nozzle):** 20 parts consolidated into 1 AM part, 25% lighter.
- **Medical (patient-specific cranial implants):** 3D-printed titanium skull plates matched to CT scan.
- **Automotive (Bugatti brake caliper):** World's largest functional titanium AM part (8 of 4 components).
- **Tooling (conformal cooling inserts):** AM enables curved internal cooling channels impossible to drill.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stair-Stepping Surface Error on an Inclined Surface

### Problem Setup
Consider a triangular overhang surface inclined at an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal build platform. Each deposited layer has uniform thickness $t_l$. The build is fabricated layer by layer along the $z$-axis.

### Step 1: Geometric Definition

The slope of the inclined surface forms a right triangle with the horizontal:

- Vertical leg of one "stair" = $t_l$ (layer thickness)
- Hypotenuse of the same stair = $L = \dfrac{t_l}{\sin(\theta)}$

### Step 2: Horizontal Projection of the Stair

The horizontal width of each "tread" is:

$$\Delta x = t_l \cdot \cot(\theta) = t_l \cdot \frac{\cos(\theta)}{\sin(\theta)}$$

### Step 3: Peak-to-Valley Height of the Stair

Because each step rises by $t_l$, the peak-to-valley roughness $R_t$ equals the layer thickness projected on the surface-normal direction:

$$R_t = t_l \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

### Step 4: Limit Case Verification

- **Vertical wall** ($\theta = 0^\circ$): $R_t = t_l \cdot \sin(0^\circ) = 0$ ✓ (vertical walls are smooth).
- **Horizontal overhang** ($\theta = 90^\circ$): $R_t = t_l \cdot \sin(90^\circ) = t_l$ ✓ (worst-case roughness equals the layer thickness).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** To minimize the visible "staircase effect" on a curved surface, the designer should orient the part such that curved walls are as **vertical** as possible in the build direction, AND select the **smallest possible $t_l$** (e.g., 25 μm in high-resolution SLA vs. 100 μm in FDM).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Minimum Layer Thickness for a Target Surface Finish

### Given
- Target surface roughness: $R_{a,\,target} = 10\ \mu\text{m}$
- Surface inclination: $\theta = 45^\circ$

### Required
- Find the maximum allowable layer thickness $t_l$.

### Step 1: Apply the Stair-Stepping Equation

$$R_t = t_l \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

### Step 2: Solve for $t_l$

$$t_l = \frac{R_{a,\,target}}{\sin(\theta)}$$

### Step 3: Substitute Values

$$t_l = \frac{10\ \mu\text{m}}{\sin(45^\circ)} = \frac{10\ \mu\text{m}}{0.7071} = 14.14\ \mu\text{m}$$

### Step 4: Engineering Interpretation

The layer thickness must be **≤ 14.14 μm** to achieve the target surface finish. In practice, the nearest standard layer thickness is **15 μm** (available in high-end SLA machines).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test this reverse calculation. Always state the **process constraint** (e.g., "minimum layer thickness on the available FDM machine is 50 μm, so a $45^\circ$ overhang cannot achieve $R_a = 10\ \mu\text{m}$").

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Optimum Volumetric Energy Density for PBF

### Problem Setup
For Selective Laser Melting of **316L stainless steel**, a study reports:

- Laser power $P = 200\ \text{W}$
- Scan speed $v = 800\ \text{mm/s}$
- Hatch spacing $h_s = 0.10\ \text{mm}$
- Layer thickness $t_l = 0.030\ \text{mm}$

Determine the VED and compare it to the recommended window.

### Step 1: State the VED Formula

$$\text{VED} = \frac{P}{v \cdot t_l \cdot h_s}$$

### Step 2: Compute the Denominator

$$v \cdot t_l \cdot h_s = 800 \cdot 0.030 \cdot 0.10 = 2.4\ \frac{\text{mm}^3}{\text{s}}$$

### Step 3: Compute VED

$$\text{VED} = \frac{200\ \text{W}}{2.4\ \text{mm}^3/\text{s}} = 83.33\ \frac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}^3}$$

### Step 4: Compare to the Recommended Window

For 316L stainless steel, the literature-recommended VED window is:

$$\text{VED}_{opt} \in [60,\ 120]\ \text{J/mm}^3$$

Since $83.33\ \text{J/mm}^3$ lies within this window, the parameters are **acceptable** and should produce a dense (> 99.5%) part.

### Step 5: Decision Logic Table

| VED Range | Resulting Defect | Engineering Action |
|---|---|---|
| VED < 60 J/mm³ | **Lack-of-fusion porosity** | Increase P or decrease $v$ |
| 60 ≤ VED ≤ 120 J/mm³ | **Dense part (> 99.5%)** | ✓ Acceptable |
| VED > 120 J/mm³ | **Keyholing / vaporization** | Decrease P or increase $v$ |

## 3.4 Derivation 4: STL Triangle Count vs. Resolution

### Problem Setup
A mechanical part's STL file is **25 MB** in binary format. The file is to be sliced at 50 μm layer thickness. The part height is 80 mm.

### Step 1: Compute the Number of Triangles

$$N_{triangles} = \frac{25\ \text{MB} \cdot 1024\ \text{KB/MB} \cdot 1024\ \text{B/KB}}{50\ \text{B/triangle}}$$

$$N_{triangles} = \frac{25 \cdot 1048576}{50} = \frac{26214400}{50} = 524288\ \text{triangles}$$

### Step 2: Compute the Total Number of Sliced Layers

$$N_{layers} = \frac{H}{t_l} = \frac{80\ \text{mm}}{0.050\ \text{mm}} = 1600\ \text{layers}$$

### Step 3: Approximate Triangles per Layer

$$N_{tri/layer} = \frac{524288}{1600} \approx 328\ \text{triangles per layer}$$

### Step 4: Engineering Interpretation

This resolution is sufficient for a smooth slice contour. If the part had only 100,000 triangles, visible faceting would appear on the curved surfaces, indicating that the original CAD export had **insufficient chordal tolerance**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget that STL is a *faceted approximation* of a smooth surface. The chordal tolerance must be set to **at least 0.01 mm** in the CAD-to-STL export for high-quality AM builds.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: FDM Build Time for a Bracket

### Problem Setup
Build parameters for a bracket on an FDM machine:

- Part bounding volume: $60\ \text{mm} \times 40\ \text{mm} \times 25\ \text{mm}$
- Layer thickness: $t_l = 0.2\ \text{mm}$
- Infill density: 20 %
- Average deposition time per layer: $T_{dep} = 45\ \text{s}$
- Recoater/setup time per layer: $T_{setup} = 4\ \text{s}$

### Step 1: Compute the Number of Layers

$$N_{layers} = \frac{H}{t_l} = \frac{25\ \text{mm}}{0.2\ \text{mm}} = 125\ \text{layers}$$

### Step 2: Total Build Time

$$T_{build} = N_{layers} \cdot (T_{dep} + T_{setup})$$

$$T_{build} = 125 \cdot (45 + 4) = 125 \cdot 49 = 6125\ \text{s}$$

### Step 3: Convert to Hours

$$T_{build} = \frac{6125}{3600} \approx 1.70\ \text{hours} \approx 1\ \text{hour}\ 42\ \text{minutes}$$

### Step 4: Material Mass

Bounding volume = $60 \cdot 40 \cdot 25 = 60000\ \text{mm}^3 = 60\ \text{cm}^3$

With 20% infill and PLA density $\rho = 1.24\ \text{g/cm}^3$:

$$m = 60 \cdot 0.20 \cdot 1.24 = 14.88\ \text{g}$$

### Step 5: Add Support Material

Assuming 15% support overhead:

$$m_{total} = 14.88 \cdot (1 + 0.15) = 17.11\ \text{g}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** FDM build time is dominated by $T_{dep}$ (the per-layer printing time), NOT by setup. To accelerate a build, the most effective lever is **increasing layer thickness** (e.g., from 0.2 mm to 0.3 mm reduces $N_{layers}$ by 33%).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Diagram 1: AM Process Chain (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STAGE 1 CONCEPTUAL CAD MODEL]:::stage --> B[STAGE 2 STL OR AMF CONVERSION]
    B --> C[STAGE 3 MESH REPAIR AND ORIENTATION]
    C --> D[STAGE 4 SLICING G CODE GENERATION]
    D --> E[STAGE 5 SUPPORT STRUCTURE DESIGN]
    E --> F[STAGE 6 LAYER BY LAYER BUILD EXECUTION]
    F --> G[STAGE 7 POST PROCESSING AND CURING]
    G --> H[STAGE 8 INSPECTION AND QUALITY TEST]

    A1[Freeform 3D Modeling Software] -.-> A
    B1[Binary STL Encoding] -.-> B
    C1[Chordal Tolerance Check] -.-> C
    D1[Layer Thickness Selection] -.-> D
    E1[Tree Like Support Geometry] -.-> E
    F1[Build Platform Lowering] -.-> F
    G1[Support Removal and Sanding] -.-> G
    H1[CT Scan and Tensile Test] -.-> H

    classDef stage fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F4E79,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    I[ASTM F42 SEVEN AM FAMILIES]:::family
    F --> I
    I --> I1[VAT PHOTOPOLYMERIZATION SLA]
    I --> I2[MATERIAL EXTRUSION FDM]
    I --> I3[POWDER BED FUSION SLS SLM EBM]
    I --> I4[MATERIAL JETTING POLYJET]
    I --> I5[BINDER JETTING METAL SAND]
    I --> I6[DIRECTED ENERGY DEPOSITION WAAM LENS]
    I --> I7[SHEET LAMINATION LOM UAM]

    classDef family fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#C77700,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Classification Tree of AM Processes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING]:::root --> S1[PHOTOPOLYMERIZATION BASED]:::branch
    R --> S2[POWDER BASED]:::branch
    R --> S3[EXTRUSION BASED]:::branch
    R --> S4[DEPOSITION BASED]:::branch
    R --> S5[SHEET LAMINATION]:::branch

    S1 --> S1A[SLA STEREOLITHOGRAPHY]:::leaf
    S1 --> S1B[DLP DIGITAL LIGHT PROCESSING]:::leaf
    S1 --> S1C[CDLP CONTINUOUS DLP]:::leaf

    S2 --> S2A[SLS SELECTIVE LASER SINTERING POLYMER]:::leaf
    S2 --> S2B[SLM SELECTIVE LASER MELTING METAL]:::leaf
    S2 --> S2C[EBM ELECTRON BEAM MELTING]:::leaf
    S2 --> S2D[MJF MULTI JET FUSION]:::leaf
    S2 --> S2E[BINDER JETTING METAL SAND]:::leaf

    S3 --> S3A[FDM FUSED DEPOSITION MODELING]:::leaf
    S3 --> S3B[FFF FUSED FILAMENT FABRICATION]:::leaf
    S3 --> S3C[ROBOFILAMENT EXTRUSION PASTE]:::leaf

    S4 --> S4A[LENS LASER ENGINEERED NET SHAPING]:::leaf
    S4 --> S4B[WAAM WIRE ARC ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING]:::leaf
    S4 --> S4C[PLASMA DEPOSITION]:::leaf

    S5 --> S5A[LOM LAMINATED OBJECT MANUFACTURING]:::leaf
    S5 --> S5B[UAM ULTRASONIC ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING]:::leaf

    classDef root fill:#1F4E79,stroke:#0B2545,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef branch fill:#A8DADC,stroke:#457B9D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef leaf fill:#F1FAEE,stroke:#A8DADC,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: PBF Process Operational Flow (SLA Example)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[RESIN VAT CONTAINS LIQUID PHOTOPOLYMER]:::tank
    N2[BUILD PLATFORM INITIALLY AT TOP OF VAT]:::plat
    N3[UV LASER SCANS FIRST LAYER CROSS SECTION]:::beam
    N4[CURED LAYER ADHERES TO PLATFORM]:::cure
    N5[PLATFORM LOWERS BY ONE LAYER THICKNESS TL]:::lower
    N6[RECOATER BLADE SPREADS FRESH RESIN]:::recoat
    N7[UV LASER SCANS NEXT LAYER]:::beam2
    N8[REPEAT UNTIL ALL LAYERS COMPLETE]:::loop
    N9[POST CURE IN UV OVEN]:::post
    N10[REMOVE SUPPORTS AND FINISH]:::finish

    N1 --> N2
    N2 --> N3
    N3 --> N4
    N4 --> N5
    N5 --> N6
    N6 --> N7
    N7 --> N8
    N8 --> N9
    N9 --> N10

    classDef tank fill:#D0E8FF,stroke:#1F4E79,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef plat fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#C77700,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef beam fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#C0392B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef cure fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef lower fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef recoat fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F9A825,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef beam2 fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#C0392B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef loop fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef post fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef finish fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#00695C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4: VED Process Window for Metal PBF

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[INPUT VED CALCULATION]:::input --> CALC[COMPUTE P DIVIDED BY V TIMES TL TIMES HS]:::calc
    CALC --> DEC1{IS VED BELOW 60 J PER MM CUBED}:::dec
    CALC --> DEC2{IS VED BETWEEN 60 AND 120}:::dec2
    CALC --> DEC3{IS VED ABOVE 120}:::dec3

    DEC1 --> R1[DEFECT LACK OF FUSION POROSITY]:::bad
    DEC1 --> A1[ACTION INCREASE POWER OR DECREASE SCAN SPEED]:::action

    DEC2 --> R2[RESULT DENSE PART WITH OVER 99.5 PERCENT DENSITY]:::good
    DEC2 --> A2[ACTION ACCEPTABLE PROCEED WITH BUILD]:::ok

    DEC3 --> R3[DEFECT KEYHOLE POROSITY AND VAPORIZATION]:::bad
    DEC3 --> A3[ACTION DECREASE POWER OR INCREASE SCAN SPEED]:::action

    classDef input fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef calc fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef dec fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#FF6F00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef dec2 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef dec3 fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef good fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef bad fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

```
