# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mark Scheme Note:** Part A questions are 3-mark direct-concept questions. Each model answer below is calibrated to the **2–3 sentence** length expected in valuation.

### Question 1: Define Additive Manufacturing and list the seven ASTM standard process families. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Additive Manufacturing (AM), as per **ASTM/ISO 52900**, is the *"process of joining materials to make parts from 3D model data, usually layer upon layer."* The seven process families are: **(1) Vat Photopolymerization, (2) Material Jetting, (3) Binder Jetting, (4) Material Extrusion, (5) Powder Bed Fusion, (6) Directed Energy Deposition, and (7) Sheet Lamination.**

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between STL and AMF file formats used in AM. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
**STL (Standard Tessellation Language)** is the de-facto industry standard, representing geometry as a triangulated mesh using only 3D coordinates and a normal vector. It supports neither colour nor material assignment. **AMF (Additive Manufacturing File Format)** is the newer ISO/ASTM standard (ISO/ASTM 52915) that supports **colour, material gradients, lattice microstructures, and curved triangulated surfaces** (using $C^0$-continuous triangles), thereby eliminating the stair-stepping error of pure planar STL facets.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is divided into two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students answer EITHER the full Question A OR the full Question B.

---

### ❑ Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) Explain the Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) process with a neat schematic. Discuss its advantages, limitations, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:** FDM (also called Fused Filament Fabrication, FFF) is a **Material Extrusion (MEX)** process where a thermoplastic filament is fed into a heated liquefier, melted, and extruded through a nozzle in the form of a fine bead. The bead is deposited along a toolpath defined by the slicer software, layer by layer, on a build platform. After each layer, the build platform **lowers by the layer thickness $t_l$** (typically 0.1–0.3 mm), and the next layer is deposited on top of the previously solidified one.

**Process Schematic Description:**
- Filament spool feeds filament into the **liquefier head** (200–250 °C).
- A **stepper motor-driven extruder gear** pushes the filament.
- The **heated nozzle** (diameter 0.2–0.8 mm) deposits the molten bead.
- The **X-Y gantry** moves the nozzle along the slicer-defined contour.
- The **build platform** moves down in **Z** after each layer.
- **Support structures** are deposited from a second nozzle (or same nozzle) for overhangs > 45°.

**Advantages:**
- **Low capital cost** (entry-level machines start at ₹50,000).
- **Wide material range** (PLA, ABS, PETG, Nylon, PEEK).
- **Simple, robust, office-friendly** with minimal post-processing.
- Suitable for **large parts** (1 m³ industrial machines available).

**Limitations:**
- **Anisotropic strength** (Z-direction strength ~ 50% of XY).
- **Visible stair-stepping** on curved surfaces.
- **Slow build speed** compared to SLA/SLS.
- **Limited resolution** (nozzle diameter sets horizontal resolution).

**Typical Applications:** Conceptual prototypes, jigs and fixtures, low-volume end-use parts, architectural models, educational kits.

---

#### (b) A cylindrical part of diameter 40 mm and height 60 mm is to be built on an FDM machine with a layer thickness of 0.25 mm. The average time to deposit each layer is 32 s, and the recoater/reset time is 5 s. Estimate: (i) total number of layers, (ii) total build time, (iii) build rate in mm³/s. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Number of Layers**

$$N_{layers} = \frac{H}{t_l} = \frac{60\ \text{mm}}{0.25\ \text{mm}} = 240\ \text{layers}$$

**Step 2: Time per Layer**

$$T_{layer} = T_{dep} + T_{setup} = 32 + 5 = 37\ \text{s/layer}$$

**Step 3: Total Build Time**

$$T_{build} = N_{layers} \cdot T_{layer} = 240 \cdot 37 = 8880\ \text{s}$$

Converting to hours:

$$T_{build} = \frac{8880}{3600} \approx 2.467\ \text{hours} \approx 2\ \text{hours}\ 28\ \text{minutes}$$

**Step 4: Part Volume and Build Rate**

$$V_{part} = \pi \cdot r^2 \cdot H = \pi \cdot (20)^2 \cdot 60 = 24000\pi \approx 75398.2\ \text{mm}^3$$

$$\text{VBR} = \frac{V_{part}}{T_{build}} = \frac{75398.2}{8880} \approx 8.49\ \frac{\text{mm}^3}{\text{s}}$$

**Final Answers:** (i) **240 layers**, (ii) **8880 s ≈ 2 h 28 min**, (iii) **8.49 mm³/s**.

---

### ❑ Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) Explain the Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) process in detail. Compare it with SLA and FDM. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**SLS Working Principle:** Selective Laser Sintering is a **Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)** process. A thin layer of polymer powder (typically **polyamide/Nylon-12**) is spread across the build platform by a roller or blade. A **CO₂ laser** (10–50 W) selectively scans the cross-section, raising the powder temperature near its **melting point** (for Nylon-12, ~ 175 °C). The powder in the heated zone **fuses** (sinters) with the surrounding powder. After each layer, the platform lowers by $t_l$ (typically 0.1 mm), a fresh layer of powder is spread, and the next layer is sintered on top. The unfused powder acts as a **natural support** for overhangs and complex geometries.

**Process Parameters:**
- Laser power: 30–60 W
- Scan speed: 5,000–15,000 mm/s
- Bed temperature: 170–180 °C (just below melting)
- Layer thickness: 100–120 μm
- Atmosphere: Nitrogen (to prevent oxidation)

**Comparison Table (Full 7-Mark Answer):**

| Parameter | SLS | SLA | FDM |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Source | CO₂ laser | UV laser | Heated nozzle |
| Material State | Powder | Liquid resin | Filament |
| Support | Powder bed (natural) | Vertical pillars | Vertical pillars |
| Strength | Isotropic | Isotropic | Anisotropic |
| Surface Finish | $R_a$ 5–15 μm | $R_a$ 1–5 μm | $R_a$ 10–50 μm |
| Speed | Moderate | Slow | Very slow |
| Cost (machine) | ₹ 50 L – 5 Cr | ₹ 10 L – 1 Cr | ₹ 50 K – 50 L |
| Material Variety | Limited (PA12, TPU) | Limited (resins) | Wide (PLA, ABS, PEEK) |

**Conclusion:** SLS is the **best compromise** between part strength, geometric complexity, and finish, but requires higher capital investment than FDM.

---

#### (b) A Selective Laser Melting build uses the following parameters: $P = 250\ \text{W}$, $v = 1000\ \text{mm/s}$, $h_s = 0.12\ \text{mm}$, $t_l = 0.040\ \text{mm}$. Determine the Volumetric Energy Density (VED) and comment on the suitability of the process window. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: State the VED Formula**

$$\text{VED} = \frac{P}{v \cdot t_l \cdot h_s}$$

**Step 2: Compute the Denominator**

$$v \cdot t_l \cdot h_s = 1000 \cdot 0.040 \cdot 0.12 = 4.8\ \frac{\text{mm}^3}{\text{s}}$$

**Step 3: Compute VED**

$$\text{VED} = \frac{250}{4.8} \approx 52.08\ \frac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}^3}$$

**Step 4: Comment on Process Window**

For most metallic alloys (e.g., **Inconel 718, Ti6Al4V, 316L stainless steel**), the recommended VED window is:

$$\text{VED}_{opt} \in [60,\ 120]\ \text{J/mm}^3$$

Since the computed VED (**52.08 J/mm³**) is **below 60 J/mm³**, the part will likely suffer from **lack-of-fusion porosity** — a defect where consecutive layers or adjacent scan tracks are not fully fused, leaving elongated voids along the build direction.

**Step 5: Corrective Action**

To bring VED into the acceptable window, the engineer should:
- **Increase laser power** (e.g., from 250 W to 300 W), or
- **Decrease scan speed** (e.g., from 1000 to 800 mm/s), or
- **Decrease hatch spacing** (e.g., from 0.12 to 0.10 mm).

Recomputing with $P = 300\ \text{W}$:

$$\text{VED}_{new} = \frac{300}{4.8} = 62.5\ \frac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}^3} \quad ✓\ \text{Acceptable}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the units** when substituting into the VED formula. Always state "W", "mm/s", "mm", and "J/mm³" explicitly.
> 2. **Not commenting** on the process window. Computing the number is only 3 marks; *interpreting* it for porosity is the remaining 4 marks.
> 3. **Confusing lack-of-fusion with keyholing.** Low VED = lack-of-fusion; High VED = keyholing. Students often swap these.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> The following 20-point summary is the **last-look revision** that KTU toppers use the night before ESE.

- [x] **ASTM Definition:** AM = layer-by-layer joining of materials from 3D model data.
- [x] **Seven Families (Mnemonic V-B-M-P-D-S-M):** Vat, Binder, Material, Material, Powder, Directed, Sheet.
- [x] **SLA** = Vat Photopolymerization; **FDM** = Material Extrusion; **SLS/SLM** = Powder Bed Fusion.
- [x] **STL File** = 50 bytes/triangle; binary format is industry standard.
- [x] **Stair-Stepping Equation:** $R_t = t_l \cdot \sin(\theta)$.
- [x] **Build Time:** $T_{build} = N_{layers} \cdot (T_{dep} + T_{setup})$.
- [x] **VED Formula:** $\text{VED} = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot t_l \cdot h_s}$, optimum 60–120 J/mm³ for metals.
- [x] **Lack-of-fusion** = VED too low; **Keyholing** = VED too high.
- [x] **FDM Limitations:** Anisotropy, slow speed, visible layer lines.
- [x] **SLS Advantage:** Unsintered powder = natural support, no support design needed.
- [x] **SLA Limitation:** Requires extensive support structures and post-curing.
- [x] **DED Process:** Capable of multi-axis deposition and part repair.
- [x] **Binder Jetting:** No heat during build; requires post-sintering furnace.
- [x] **AM vs CNC:** AM = 5–10% waste, no tooling; CNC = 60–90% waste, requires tooling.
- [x] **AMF vs STL:** AMF supports colour, materials, and curved triangles; STL is geometry-only.
- [x] **Build Orientation:** Vertical walls minimize stair-stepping; overhangs > 45° need supports.
- [x] **Industrial AM Examples:** GE LEAP nozzle (DED), Bugatti caliper (SLM), HP MJF (PA12).
- [x] **Material Range:** Polymers (FDM, SLA, SLS), Metals (SLM, EBM, DED, BJT), Ceramics (BJT).
- [x] **Build Volume:** Small (SLA ~ 145 × 145 × 175 mm) → Large (industrial SLS ~ 700 × 800 × 500 mm).
- [x] **Post-Processing:** Support removal, sanding, infiltration, UV-curing, HIP (Hot Isostatic Pressing) for critical metal parts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rapid Prototyping
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 4: Rapid Prototyping – KTU Premium Study Notes

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT404 – MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Rapid Prototyping

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Rapid Prototyping (RP)**, also known as **Additive Manufacturing (AM)** or **Solid Free-form Fabrication (SFF)**, is defined by the **American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM F2792)** as *“a process of joining materials to make objects from 3D model data, usually layer upon layer, as opposed to subtractive manufacturing methodologies.”*

In the KTU 2024 manufacturing curriculum, Rapid Prototyping is the **layer-by-layer additive fabrication of a physical prototype directly from a Computer-Aided Design (CAD) model**, without the need for part-specific tooling, fixtures, or human-intensive machining operations.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):** Rapid Prototyping is a class of technologies that can automatically construct physical models from Computer-Aided Design (CAD) data, by adding successive layers of material — solid, liquid, or powder — and converting them into a finished, three-dimensional prototype.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **wedding cake with 50 horizontal tiers** — but instead of frosting, each tier is a thin slice of plastic, metal, or resin. A chef (the RP machine) reads a digital recipe (the CAD file) and **adds one perfect slice at a time**, gluing each new slice to the one beneath it. After 200–500 slices, a fully formed 3D object emerges.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this revolutionary?** Traditional manufacturing is **subtractive** — you start with a solid block and *cut away* waste (like sculpting a statue from marble). Rapid Prototyping is **additive** — you *add* only what is needed (like a 3D pen drawing in mid-air, layer by layer). This eliminates tooling, drastically reduces lead time, and allows geometries impossible to machine.

### 1.3 Core Terminology & Standard Metrics

| **Term** | **Definition / Standard Value** |
|---|---|
| **Layer Thickness (z-resolution)** | Typically **0.05 mm to 0.30 mm**; defines vertical accuracy. |
| **Build Volume** | Maximum envelope (X × Y × Z) — e.g., **250 × 250 × 300 mm** for desktop FDM. |
| **STL File Format** | **Standard Tessellation Language** — the de-facto file format (triangulated mesh). |
| **Lead Time Reduction** | From weeks (conventional tooling) to **hours (RP)**. |
| **Part Accuracy (Tolerance)** | Typically **±0.1 mm** for industrial RP systems. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Generic Process Chain of Rapid Prototyping (KTU High-Yield)

The complete workflow of any RP system follows **seven canonical steps** that KTU examiners frequently test:

1. **CAD Model Creation** — The product is modeled in 3D CAD software (SolidWorks, CATIA, Creo, Fusion 360).
2. **STL File Conversion** — The CAD model is mathematically tessellated into thousands of small triangles and exported as an `.STL` file.
3. **Slicing** — Specialized **slicer software** (Cura, Simplify3D, Magics) mathematically divides the 3D model into **horizontal cross-sectional layers** of uniform thickness `t`.
4. **Build Parameter Setting** — Layer thickness, infill density, scan speed, laser power, and orientation are configured.
5. **Layer-by-Layer Fabrication** — The RP machine physically constructs the part, one layer at a time, by selectively solidifying/depositing material.
6. **Post-Processing** — Support structure removal, curing (for resins), sanding, polishing, painting, or infiltration.
7. **Verification & Testing** — Dimensional inspection, functional testing, and approval for the next stage (tooling or production).

---

### 2.2 Classification of Rapid Prototyping Processes (ASTM F2792)

The KTU syllabus focuses on the **seven ASTM-recognized AM process families**. Below is the high-yield table:

| **ASTM Category** | **Process Example** | **Material State** | **Joining Mechanism** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Vat Photopolymerization** | Stereolithography (SLA) | Liquid resin | UV laser photopolymerization |
| **Material Extrusion** | Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) | Thermoplastic filament | Heated nozzle extrusion |
| **Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)** | Selective Laser Sintering (SLS), SLM, EBM | Metal/Polymer powder | Laser/Electron beam melting/sintering |
| **Material Jetting** | PolyJet, MultiJet Modeling | Photopolymer droplets | UV curing of jetted droplets |
| **Binder Jetting** | 3D Printing (3DP) | Powder + liquid binder | Drop-on-demand binder deposition |
| **Sheet Lamination** | Laminated Object Manufacturing (LOM) | Paper/Metal sheets | Adhesive bonding + laser cutting |
| **Directed Energy Deposition (DED)** | Laser Metal Deposition (LMD) | Metal powder/wire | Focused thermal energy melting |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Mathematical Foundations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These equations are routinely tested in **KTU 2024 numerical problems** and **derivations** for the Rapid Prototyping module.

| **#** | **Formula / Equation** | **Meaning of Variables** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_z = \dfrac{H}{t}$ | $N_z$ = Number of layers, $H$ = Part height, $t$ = Layer thickness | dimensionless |
| 2 | $T_{build} = \dfrac{V}{R_d} + T_{setup}$ | $T_{build}$ = Total build time, $V$ = Part volume, $R_d$ = Deposition rate, $T_{setup}$ = Pre/post time | minutes / hours |
| 3 | $V_{part} = \pi r^2 H$ | Volume of a simple cylindrical part | mm³ |
| 4 | $A_{layer} = f(x,y) \approx \sum A_i$ | Cross-sectional area of each layer | mm² |
| 5 | $\eta_{material} = \dfrac{\rho_{used}}{\rho_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Material utilization efficiency (often **> 90 %** in AM vs. < 30 % in machining) | % |
| 6 | $E_d = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | $E_d$ = Volumetric energy density, $P$ = Laser power, $v$ = Scan speed, $h$ = Hatch spacing, $t$ = Layer thickness | J/mm³ |
| 7 | $T_{post} = T_{build} \times k_{post}$ | $T_{post}$ = Post-processing time, $k_{post}$ = Empirical factor (≈ 0.1 – 0.3) | minutes / hours |
| 8 | $C_{part} = C_{material} + C_{machine} + C_{labor}$ | Total part cost components | ₹ / USD |
| 9 | $\Delta z = \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \cdot NA}$ | Vertical (z-axis) optical resolution in photopolymerization | µm |
| 10 | $R_{infill} = \dfrac{V_{infill}}{V_{solid}} \times 100\%$ | Infill ratio for FDM parts (typically 10–60 %) | % |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Bugatti, BMW, and Tesla use metal SLM/EBM for **lightweight lattice structures** in brake calipers, impellers, and exhaust manifolds (50–70 % weight reduction).
- **Biomedical Engineering:** Custom **patient-specific cranial implants**, dental crowns, and orthopedic scaffolds are 3D-printed directly from CT/MRI scan data.
- **Aerospace:** GE Aviation uses **Direct Metal Laser Melting (DMLM)** to print a single-piece fuel nozzle that replaces an assembly of 18 parts (25 % lighter, 5× more durable).
- **Tooling:** Rapid Tooling (RT) uses RP patterns to cast molds in days instead of weeks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Build Time Estimation for Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM)

The KTU 2024 board frequently asks for derivations of build time. Below is the **complete, step-by-step derivation** showing every algebraic transition.

> **Problem Statement:** Derive an expression for the total build time of a rectangular cuboid part (dimensions $L \times W \times H$) fabricated using FDM, given layer thickness $t$, deposition rate $R_d$ (mm³/s), and a fixed machine overhead $T_{setup}$ (seconds).

---

**Step 1 — Identify the geometry of the part.**
The part is a rectangular cuboid of length $L$, width $W$, and height $H$ (all in mm). Its volume is the product of the three dimensions.

$$
V_{part} = L \times W \times H
$$

**Step 2 — Determine the number of layers.**
The build is performed in horizontal layers of uniform thickness $t$ along the z-axis. Therefore, the number of layers $N_z$ is the part height divided by the layer thickness.

$$
N_z = \frac{H}{t}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the volume to be deposited per layer.**
Assuming a **100 % solid infill** for simplicity, the volume of material in a single layer is the cross-sectional area of the part times the layer thickness.

$$
V_{layer} = L \times W \times t
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the pure deposition time (without overhead).**
The deposition rate $R_d$ is the volume of material the extruder deposits per unit time. The total deposition time $T_{dep}$ is the total part volume divided by the deposition rate.

$$
T_{dep} = \frac{V_{part}}{R_d} = \frac{L \times W \times H}{R_d}
$$

**Step 5 — Alternative expression using layer count.**
By substituting $V_{layer}$ and $N_z$, we obtain an equivalent expression that explicitly counts layer-based deposition time.

$$
T_{dep} = N_z \times \frac{V_{layer}}{R_d} = \frac{H}{t} \times \frac{L \times W \times t}{R_d} = \frac{L \times W \times H}{R_d}
$$

**Step 6 — Incorporate the infill ratio correction.**
For a realistic FDM part, the infill ratio $R_{infill}$ (a value between 0 and 1) reduces the actual deposited volume, since the interior is hollow lattice rather than solid material.

$$
T_{dep,actual} = \frac{V_{part} \times R_{infill}}{R_d}
$$

**Step 7 — Add the fixed machine overhead time.**
The total build time includes the pre-processing, bed heating, calibration, and final cooling time — collectively $T_{setup}$.

$$
T_{build} = T_{dep,actual} + T_{setup}
$$

**Step 8 — Final compact equation (KTU Board-Answer Format).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{build} &= \frac{V_{part} \times R_{infill}}{R_d} + T_{setup} \\
T_{build} &= \frac{L \cdot W \cdot H \cdot R_{infill}}{R_d} + T_{setup}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Study of Major RP Processes (KTU Most-Asked Table)

This comparison matrix is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** in KTU 2024 board exams. Memorize it thoroughly.

| **Parameter** | **SLA (Stereolithography)** | **FDM (Fused Deposition)** | **SLS (Selective Laser Sintering)** | **LOM (Laminated Object)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ASTM Category** | Vat Photopolymerization | Material Extrusion | Powder Bed Fusion | Sheet Lamination |
| **Material Form** | Liquid photopolymer resin | Thermoplastic filament (ABS, PLA) | Powder (Nylon, Polyamide) | Paper / metal sheet rolls |
| **Energy Source** | UV Laser (He-Cd / solid-state) | Thermal (heated nozzle ~ 200–250 °C) | CO₂ Laser | CO₂ Laser + heated roller |
| **Layer Thickness** | **0.05 – 0.15 mm** | **0.10 – 0.30 mm** | **0.10 – 0.20 mm** | **0.15 – 0.50 mm** |
| **Support Structure** | **Required** (for overhangs) | **Required** | **Not required** (powder self-supports) | **Not required** |
| **Surface Finish** | **Excellent** (smoothest) | Moderate (stair-stepping) | Moderate (grainy) | Poor (delamination lines) |
| **Mechanical Strength** | Brittle, low for thin walls | Anisotropic, moderate | **Highest (near-isotropic)** | Low (wood-like for paper) |
| **Build Speed** | Moderate | Slow for complex geometry | Fast | Very fast for large parts |
| **Material Cost** | **High** (resin) | **Low** (filament) | Moderate | **Lowest** (paper) |
| **Typical Accuracy** | **± 0.05 mm** | ± 0.20 mm | ± 0.10 mm | ± 0.25 mm |
| **Best Application** | Investment casting patterns, jewelry, dental | Concept models, low-cost prototyping | Functional prototypes, end-use parts | Sand casting patterns, large mock-ups |

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Volumetric Energy Density for Laser-Based PBF

This derivation is critical for **Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)** and **Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** systems.

**Step 1 — Define the volumetric energy density.**
The volumetric energy density $E_d$ is the total laser energy delivered per unit volume of powder.

$$
E_d = \frac{\text{Total Energy}}{\text{Volume Melted}} = \frac{P \cdot t_{exposure}}{V_{melted}}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the exposure time in terms of scan speed.**
The exposure time $t_{exposure}$ for a single scan line of length $L_s$ at scan speed $v$ is:

$$
t_{exposure} = \frac{L_s}{v}
$$

**Step 3 — Express the melted volume.**
The volume melted by a single laser scan is the cross-section of the melt pool times the scan length.

$$
V_{melted} = h \cdot t \cdot L_s
$$

where $h$ = hatch spacing and $t$ = layer thickness.

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_d &= \frac{P \cdot (L_s / v)}{h \cdot t \cdot L_s} \\
E_d &= \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation.**
For a successful SLS/SLM process in metal, $E_d$ must lie in a **process window** of typically **60 – 200 J/mm³**. Too low → lack of fusion; too high → keyhole porosity and balling.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: The Generic Rapid Prototyping Process Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Product Idea] --> B[Step 1: 3D CAD Modeling]
    B --> C[Step 2: STL File Conversion]
    C --> D[Step 3: Slicing Software]
    D --> E[Step 4: Build Parameter Setup]
    E --> F[Step 5: Layer-by-Layer Fabrication]
    F --> G[Step 6: Post-Processing]
    G --> H{Inspection Pass?}
    H -->|Yes| I[End: Approved Prototype]
    H -->|No| J[Redesign Loop]
    J --> B
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Classification of Rapid Prototyping Processes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[RAPID PROTOTYPING PROCESSES] --> B[Solid Based]
    A --> C[Liquid Based]
    A -->[Powder Based]
    A --> D[Sheet Based]

    B --> B1[LOM Laminated Object Manufacturing]
    B --> B2[FDM Fused Deposition Modeling]

    C --> C1[SLA Stereolithography]
    C --> C2[PolyJet Material Jetting]

    P --> P1[SLS Selective Laser Sintering]
    P --> P2[SLM Selective Laser Melting]
    P --> P3[EBM Electron Beam Melting]
    P --> P4[BJ Binder Jetting 3DP]

    D --> D1[UAM Ultrasonic Additive Manufacturing]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Functional Architecture: Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) Subsystems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FIL[Filament Spool] --> EXT[Heated Extruder Nozzle 200 to 250 deg C]
    EXT --> DEPO[Material Deposition on Bed]
    BED[Heated Build Platform 60 to 110 deg C] --> DEPO
    DEPO --> LAY[Layer Formation Thickness 0.1 to 0.3 mm]
    LAY --> ZMOVE[Z Axis Stepper Movement Up by One Layer]
    ZMOVE --> REPEAT{More Layers?}
    REPEAT -->|Yes| EXT
    REPEAT -->|No| POST[Post Processing and Support Removal]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Stereolithography (SLA) Optical Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LASER[UV Laser Source 355 nm] --> MIRROR1[Scanning Mirror X Axis]
    MIRROR1 --> MIRROR2[Scanning Mirror Y Axis]
    MIRROR2 --> VAT[Resin Vat with Liquid Photopolymer]
    VAT --> CURE[Selective Photopolymerization on Surface]
    CURE --> PLAT[Elevator Platform Moves Down by Layer Thickness]
    PLAT --> CURE
    CURE --> FINAL[Finished Cured 3D Part]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above are **block-level functional architecture** representations — they map the **flow of material, energy, and information** in each RP system rather than photorealistic physical drawings, ensuring crystal-clear KTU board comprehension.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
> **Define Rapid Prototyping. List any four advantages of Rapid Prototyping over conventional manufacturing.**

**Model Answer:**
Rapid Prototyping is the **layer-by-layer additive fabrication of physical models directly from 3D CAD data**, without using part-specific tooling.

**Four advantages:**
1. **Drastically reduced lead time** (days/weeks reduced to hours).
2. **No tooling required** — ideal for low-volume, customized parts.
3. **Geometric complexity is free** — internal channels and lattice structures are easily achievable.
4. **Design iteration is fast and inexpensive** — modifications to the STL file are immediate.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Internal Assessment]**
> **Differentiate between Subtractive and Additive Manufacturing.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Subtractive** | **Additive (RP)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Removes material from a solid block | Adds material layer by layer |
| **Material Waste** | High (chips/swarf) | Minimal (only what is needed) |
| **Tooling** | Required (cutters, fixtures) | Not required |
| **Setup Cost** | High | Low |
| **Best For** | High-volume, simple parts | Complex, low-volume, customized parts |
| **Examples** | Milling, turning, grinding | SLA, FDM, SLS |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Part B, Q3(A). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Past Paper, 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Stereolithography (SLA) process** in detail. Discuss the role of photopolymer resin and the post-curing step. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Derive an expression for the **build time** of a rectangular part of dimensions 100 mm × 80 mm × 50 mm built using FDM, given a layer thickness of 0.2 mm, deposition rate of 5 mm³/s, 20 % infill, and a setup time of 15 minutes. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Stereolithography (SLA) — Process Explanation:**

1. **Working Principle:** SLA uses a focused **UV laser** (typically 355 nm wavelength) to selectively cure and harden a vat of **liquid photopolymer resin** point-by-point and layer-by-layer.

2. **Step-by-Step Process:**
   - The build platform is initially positioned just below the resin surface.
   - A **wiper blade** spreads a fresh layer of liquid resin.
   - The **UV laser** is directed via **galvanometer-controlled X-Y scanning mirrors** to trace the cross-section of the part.
   - Wherever the laser strikes, **photopolymerization** occurs, solidifying the resin.
   - The platform descends by one layer thickness (typically 0.05–0.15 mm).
   - The next layer is cured on top of the previous one.
   - This process repeats for hundreds to thousands of layers.

3. **Role of Photopolymer Resin:** The resin contains **photoinitiators** that trigger molecular cross-linking under UV light, converting liquid monomer chains into a rigid 3D polymer network. Common resins include **epoxy and acrylate-based** photopolymers.

4. **Post-Curing Step:** After build completion, the green part is:
   - Rinsed in **isopropyl alcohol (IPA)** to remove uncured resin.
   - Placed in a **UV oven chamber** for 30–60 minutes to achieve full polymerization.
   - This improves mechanical strength, dimensional stability, and surface hardness.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given Data:**
- $L = 100$ mm, $W = 80$ mm, $H = 50$ mm
- $t = 0.2$ mm
- $R_d = 5$ mm³/s
- $R_{infill} = 0.20$ (20 %)
- $T_{setup} = 15 \text{ min} = 900$ s

**Step 1 — Calculate the part volume.**

$$
V_{part} = L \times W \times H = 100 \times 80 \times 50 = 400{,}000 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the actual deposited volume (with infill).**

$$
V_{deposited} = V_{part} \times R_{infill} = 400{,}000 \times 0.20 = 80{,}000 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the deposition time.**

$$
T_{dep} = \frac{V_{deposited}}{R_d} = \frac{80{,}000}{5} = 16{,}000 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to hours for board-friendly presentation.**

$$
T_{dep} = \frac{16{,}000}{3600} \approx 4.44 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 5 — Add the setup time to obtain total build time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{build} &= T_{dep} + T_{setup} \\
T_{build} &= 16{,}000 \text{ s} + 900 \text{ s} \\
T_{build} &= 16{,}900 \text{ s} \approx 4.69 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** The total FDM build time is **approximately 16,900 seconds (≈ 4.69 hours)**.

---

> **Part B, Q3(B). [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper, 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** With a schematic diagram, describe the **Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM)** process. List its **advantages and limitations**. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Compare the **ASTM F2792 process categories** of Vat Photopolymerization, Material Extrusion, and Powder Bed Fusion in terms of material state, energy source, support structure, and accuracy. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM):**

**Principle:** FDM is an **extrusion-based additive manufacturing** process in which a thermoplastic filament is heated above its melting point and extruded through a nozzle to build a part layer-by-layer.

**Working:**
1. A thermoplastic filament (ABS, PLA, Nylon, TPU) is fed from a spool into a **heated liquefier head** (~ 200–250 °C).
2. The molten material is extruded through a fine nozzle (0.2–0.8 mm) onto a **heated build platform** (~ 60–110 °C to prevent warping).
3. The nozzle moves in the X-Y plane following the toolpath generated by the slicer.
4. After completing one layer, the platform (or nozzle) moves up by the layer thickness along the Z-axis.
5. The next layer is deposited and bonds to the previous one by thermal fusion.

**Advantages:**
- **Low cost** of both machines and materials.
- **Easy operation** — ideal for office and educational environments.
- **Wide range of materials** (ABS, PLA, Nylon, polycarbonate).
- **No chemical post-curing** required.
- **Recyclable** support material in some systems (e.g., Stratasys SR-30).

**Limitations:**
- **Anisotropic mechanical properties** (weakest along Z).
- **Visible stair-stepping** on curved surfaces.
- **Slow** for large or complex parts.
- **Limited to thermoplastics** in most consumer machines.
- **Support structures** are mandatory for overhangs > 45°.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

| **Parameter** | **Vat Photopolymerization (SLA)** | **Material Extrusion (FDM)** | **Powder Bed Fusion (SLS/SLM)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Material State** | Liquid photopolymer resin | Solid thermoplastic filament | Powder (polymer/metal) |
| **Energy Source** | UV laser (355 nm) | Thermal heating (heater) | CO₂ laser (SLS) / fiber laser (SLM) |
| **Support Structure** | Required for overhangs | Required for overhangs > 45° | **Not required** (powder self-supports) |
| **Accuracy** | **± 0.05 mm** (highest) | ± 0.20 mm | ± 0.10 mm |
| **Surface Finish** | Excellent | Moderate (stair-stepping) | Moderate (grainy) |
| **Tensile Strength** | 35–65 MPa | 30–50 MPa | 40–70 MPa |
| **Speed** | Moderate | Slow | Fast for many parts |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Pitfall Alert – Common Mark-Deduction Errors**
> 1. **Do not confuse SLM (Selective Laser Melting) with SLS (Selective Laser Sintering).** SLM fully melts the metal powder, while SLS only sinters polymer/nylon powder. Many students write "laser melts" for both — lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Always state the ASTM category** alongside the process name (e.g., *“SLA — Vat Photopolymerization”*). Examiners reward this with a 0.5–1 mark bonus.
> 3. **Unit mismatches** in build time problems: convert minutes to seconds (or vice versa) **before** adding. Mixing units is the #1 cause of zero-credit numerical answers.
> 4. **In FDM diagrams**, always label the **heater block temperature** and the **build platform temperature** — many students omit these and lose 1 mark each.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Fire Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Rapid-Prototyping Revision — Memorize These:**

- ✅ **Definition (must memorize):** RP = layer-by-layer additive fabrication directly from 3D CAD data.
- ✅ **ASTM F2792** classifies AM into **7 categories** — Vat Photopolymerization, Material Extrusion, Powder Bed Fusion, Material Jetting, Binder Jetting, Sheet Lamination, Directed Energy Deposition.
- ✅ **Generic process chain (7 steps):** CAD → STL → Slicing → Parameter setup → Build → Post-processing → Inspection.
- ✅ **STL file = Standard Tessellation Language** — triangulated mesh representation of the 3D model.
- ✅ **SLA (Stereolithography):** Liquid resin + UV laser + photopolymerization → **highest accuracy, support required**.
- ✅ **FDM (Fused Deposition):** Thermoplastic filament + heated nozzle → **low cost, anisotropic, supports required**.
- ✅ **SLS (Selective Laser Sintering):** Powder + CO₂ laser → **no supports needed, functional parts, near-isotropic strength**.
- ✅ **SLM (Selective Laser Melting):** **Metal powder + fiber laser** → fully melted → aerospace/medical end-use parts.
- ✅ **LOM (Laminated Object Manufacturing):** Paper/metal sheets + adhesive + laser cutting → **cheapest, good for large patterns**.
- ✅ **3DP (Binder Jetting):** Powder + liquid binder → color prototypes, sand casting molds.
- ✅ **Master formula:** $T_{build} = \dfrac{V_{part} \times R_{infill}}{R_d} + T_{setup}$
- ✅ **Volumetric energy density (laser PBF):** $E_d = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ — process window **60–200 J/mm³** for metals.
- ✅ **Number of layers formula:** $N_z = \dfrac{H}{t}$
- ✅ **Layer thickness range:** **0.05 mm (SLA) – 0.50 mm (LOM)** — lower thickness = better finish, longer build time.
- ✅ **Material utilization in AM is > 90 %** vs. < 30 % in conventional machining — a frequent 2-mark KTU question.
- ✅ **Applications:** Automotive (lightweight parts), Aerospace (fuel nozzle, turbine blades), Biomedical (implants, scaffolds), Tooling (investment casting patterns).
- ✅ **Support structures** are needed in SLA and FDM, **not** in SLS (powder bed is self-supporting).
- ✅ **Post-processing steps:** Support removal, resin draining, IPA rinsing, UV post-curing, sanding, infiltration, painting.

---

> **End of Module 4 – Rapid Prototyping Notes**
