topics.title: Stereo-lithography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT404 - MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stereo-lithography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Stereo-lithography (SLA)**, also known as **Stereolithography Apparatus**, is a **vat photopolymerization** based **Additive Manufacturing (AM)** process, classified under the **ISO/ASTM 52900** standard (Category: *Material Extrusion* → correction: *Vat Photopolymerization*). In this process, a focused **Ultra-Violet (UV) laser beam** selectively scans and **photo-cures (hardens)** a liquid **photopolymer resin** in a vat, building a solid three-dimensional object in a **layer-by-layer** fashion, with each layer thickness typically ranging from **0.05 mm to 0.20 mm**.

The process was invented by **Chuck Hull in 1984** and patented in **1986 (US Patent 4,575,330)**, making it the **first commercial 3D printing technology** ever developed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Stereo-lithography is a 14-mark favorite in KTU ESE. The examiner expects the student to draw a neat process schematic, explain the working principle, derive Jacob's curing depth equation, and discuss the role of key process parameters (laser power, scan speed, hatch spacing, layer thickness).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Magical Honeycomb Cake"

Imagine you have a tank filled with **liquid honey** (the photopolymer resin) and a **magical flashlight** (the UV laser) that can instantly turn the honey solid wherever it shines — but only on the **top surface**. 

To build a 3D cake:
1. You first slice a digital cake into hundreds of thin horizontal sheets (the **CAD slicing**).
2. You pour a fresh layer of honey on top (the **recoater blade**).
3. You shine the flashlight along the exact outline of the *first slice* of the cake — that slice hardens instantly.
4. The platform drops down a tiny bit (one slice thickness), a new layer of honey flows in, and you trace the *second* slice.
5. Repeat thousands of times — and at the end, you lift out a fully solid cake, just from light.

That is exactly how stereo-lithography transforms **pixels of light into a real 3D object**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term Glossary for KTU:**
> - **Photopolymer:** A liquid resin that undergoes **photo-polymerization** (chain reaction solidification) when exposed to UV light of specific wavelength (~355 nm for solid-state lasers).
> - **Vat:** The reservoir containing the liquid resin.
> - **Recoater / Wiper Blade:** A mechanical blade that spreads a fresh, uniform resin layer on top of the build platform.
> - **Build Platform (Elevator):** A vertically moving stage that dips the part into the resin vat.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics at a Glance

| Metric | Typical Value (SLA) |
|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | **± 0.1 mm (high)** |
| **Layer Thickness** | **0.05 – 0.20 mm** |
| **Laser Wavelength** | **355 nm (UV solid-state Nd:YVO₄)** |
| **Build Volume (typical)** | **250 × 250 × 250 mm³** |
| **Part Density Achieved** | **> 99 %** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Stereo-lithography Process — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The working of SLA can be broken down into **6 sequential stages**:

1. **CAD Model Creation:** A 3D solid model is built in CAD software (e.g., CATIA, SolidWorks) and exported as **.STL** (Standard Tessellation Language) file.
2. **Slicing:** The STL file is sliced by a **slicer software** into 2D cross-sectional layers of predetermined thickness ($\Delta z$).
3. **Resin Filling & Recoating:** The vat is filled with liquid photopolymer. A **recoater blade** sweeps across the vat to deposit a uniform, thin, fresh layer of resin on the build platform (or on the previously cured layer).
4. **Selective UV Curing (Scanning):** The **galvanometer-controlled UV laser** scans the surface of the resin along the contour of the current 2D slice, following the **scan vectors** computed by the slicer. Wherever the laser hits, the resin **photo-polymerizes** and solidifies.
5. **Platform Translation:** After a layer is fully cured, the **build platform** lowers by exactly **one layer thickness ($\Delta z$)** into the vat, allowing fresh liquid resin to flow over the cured layer.
6. **Repetition & Post-Processing:** Steps 3–5 repeat until the part is complete. The part is then lifted, **rinsed with isopropyl alcohol (IPA)** to remove uncured resin, and **post-cured in a UV oven** for full mechanical strength.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Stage

- **Why slicing?** Because SLA cannot fabricate the entire 3D shape in one go — it must reconstruct it from 2D information, which is the fundamental philosophy of all additive manufacturing.
- **Why UV laser?** UV light carries enough **photon energy** to break the **photo-initiator molecules** in the resin, triggering a free-radical chain reaction that converts liquid monomers into a cross-linked solid polymer.
- **Why scan vectors?** Each slice is too complex to be drawn in one continuous motion; the scanner breaks it into **hatches** (parallel scan lines) and **contours** (outer/inner boundaries).

> [!NOTE]
> **Photo-polymerization Reaction (simplified):**
> A **photo-initiator** (e.g., BAPO — Phenylbis(2,4,6-trimethylbenzoyl)phosphine oxide) absorbs a UV photon → produces **free radicals** → radicals attack **C=C double bonds** of monomers → monomers link into long **polymer chains** → liquid turns to solid.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table contains every formula a KTU student must memorize for this topic.

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning of Symbols | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$C_d = D_p \, \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E}{E_c}\right)$$ | $C_d$ = Curing depth (mm); $D_p$ = Penetration depth of resin (mm); $E$ = Laser exposure energy (mJ/cm²); $E_c$ = Critical exposure energy (mJ/cm²) | **Jacob's Equation (1987)** — the most important SLA formula. Predicts how deep a layer cures given the laser energy. |
| 2 | $$E = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h_s}$$ | $P$ = Laser power (mW); $v$ = Scan speed (mm/s); $h_s$ = Hatch spacing (mm) | Calculates the energy density delivered per unit area. |
| 3 | $$v = \dfrac{P}{E \cdot h_s}$$ | (Rearranged form of #2) | Used to set scan speed for desired energy. |
| 4 | $R = \Delta z / t_{layer}$ | $R$ = Build rate; $\Delta z$ = Layer thickness; $t_{layer}$ = Time per layer | Production throughput calculation. |
| 5 | $\eta_{vol} = V_{part} / V_{build} \times 100\%$ | $\eta_{vol}$ = Volumetric efficiency; $V_{part}$ = Part volume; $V_{build}$ = Build envelope volume | Material utilization indicator. |
| 6 | $D_p = 1/\alpha$ | $D_p$ = Penetration depth; $\alpha$ = Resin absorption coefficient | Links optical property of resin to cure geometry. |
| 7 | $E_c = E_0 \cdot e^{\alpha \cdot z_0}$ | $E_c$ = Critical exposure at surface; $E_0$ = Threshold for gel point; $z_0$ = Inhibition depth (oxygen-inhibited) | Explains why a minimum energy is required to overcome oxygen inhibition. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **penetration depth ($D_p$)** with **curing depth ($C_d$)**. Remember: $D_p$ is a **material property** (constant for a given resin), while $C_d$ is a **process response** (depends on the laser energy supplied).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Prototyping:** Automotive (Maruti, Tata Motors) and aerospace (HAL, Boeing) use SLA to print concept models in **24–48 hours** instead of waiting weeks for CNC-machined prototypes.
- **Jewellery & Dental:** Investment casting patterns for gold crowns and orthodontic aligners (e.g., **Invisalign** uses SLA-like processes).
- **Microfluidics & MEMS:** Lab-on-chip devices with channel sizes as small as **50 µm**.
- **Medical Implants:** Patient-specific anatomical models for pre-surgical planning (e.g., skull models for neurosurgery at SCTIMST, Trivandrum — a KTU-relevant institution).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Jacob's Curing Depth Equation (KTU's Most Repeated Derivation)

This is **the derivation** that appears in almost every KTU ESE question paper on SLA. We will derive it line by line.

### 3.1.1 Starting Assumption — Beer's Law of Light Absorption

When a UV laser beam of intensity $I_0$ enters the photopolymer resin, its intensity $I$ at a depth $z$ below the surface decreases exponentially because the resin absorbs photons. This is given by the **Beer–Lambert Law**:

$$
I(z) = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha z}
$$

where $\alpha$ is the **absorption coefficient** of the photopolymer resin (units: $\text{mm}^{-1}$).

### 3.1.2 Definition of Penetration Depth ($D_p$)

The **penetration depth** is defined as the depth at which the laser intensity falls to $\frac{1}{e}$ (≈ 36.8 %) of its surface value. Mathematically:

$$
I(D_p) = \dfrac{I_0}{e}
$$

Substituting into Beer's law:

$$
\dfrac{I_0}{e} = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha D_p}
$$

Dividing both sides by $I_0$:

$$
\dfrac{1}{e} = e^{-\alpha D_p}
$$

Taking natural logarithm on both sides:

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{e}\right) = -\alpha D_p
$$

Since $\ln(1/e) = -1$:

$$
-1 = -\alpha D_p
$$

Solving for $D_p$:

$$
\boxed{D_p = \dfrac{1}{\alpha}}
$$

Thus we have established the inverse relationship between $D_p$ and $\alpha$.

### 3.1.3 The Energy Required for Photo-polymerization

For the resin to cure (turn solid), it must absorb a **minimum critical energy**, $E_c$, per unit volume of material. The total energy $E$ delivered to a depth $z$ is:

$$
E(z) = I(z) \cdot t_{exp} = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha z} \cdot t_{exp}
$$

where $t_{exp}$ is the **laser exposure time** at a given point.

### 3.1.4 Defining the Critical Cure Condition

Curing will just barely occur when the absorbed energy at depth $C_d$ equals the critical energy:

$$
E(C_d) = E_c
$$

Substituting:

$$
I_0 \, e^{-\alpha C_d} \cdot t_{exp} = E_c
$$

Rearranging:

$$
e^{-\alpha C_d} = \dfrac{E_c}{I_0 \, t_{exp}} = \dfrac{E_c}{E}
$$

where $E = I_0 \, t_{exp}$ is the **surface exposure energy** (energy per unit area on the top surface).

### 3.1.5 Solving for $C_d$

Taking natural logarithm on both sides:

$$
-\alpha C_d = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_c}{E}\right)
$$

Using the logarithmic identity $\ln(a/b) = -\ln(b/a)$:

$$
-\alpha C_d = -\ln\!\left(\dfrac{E}{E_c}\right)
$$

Cancelling the negative signs and substituting $D_p = 1/\alpha$:

$$
\boxed{C_d = D_p \, \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E}{E_c}\right)}
$$

This is **Jacob's Equation**, the cornerstone of SLA process analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation of Jacob's Equation:**
> - $C_d$ depends **logarithmically** on the energy ratio $E/E_c$. Doubling the energy does **not** double the cure depth — it adds only $D_p \ln(2) \approx 0.693 D_p$ extra depth.
> - To get a thicker single-layer cure, you can either **increase $E$** (higher laser power or slower scan) or **choose a resin with higher $D_p$** (more transparent, less absorbing).
> - To get a thin, sharp cure (for fine resolution), use a resin with **low $D_p$** (highly absorbing) and operate **just above** $E_c$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Numerical)

**Problem:** A photopolymer resin has $D_p = 0.15 \text{ mm}$ and $E_c = 9 \text{ mJ/cm}^2$. If the laser delivers an exposure of $E = 81 \text{ mJ/cm}^2$, find the curing depth $C_d$.

### Step 1: Identify given values

$$
D_p = 0.15 \text{ mm}, \quad E_c = 9 \text{ mJ/cm}^2, \quad E = 81 \text{ mJ/cm}^2
$$

### Step 2: Apply Jacob's equation

$$
C_d = D_p \, \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E}{E_c}\right)
$$

### Step 3: Substitute

$$
C_d = 0.15 \times \ln\!\left(\dfrac{81}{9}\right) = 0.15 \times \ln(9)
$$

### Step 4: Evaluate $\ln(9)$

$$
\ln(9) = 2 \ln(3) = 2 \times 1.0986 = 2.1972
$$

### Step 5: Final answer

$$
C_d = 0.15 \times 2.1972 \approx 0.3296 \text{ mm} \approx 0.33 \text{ mm}
$$

So the laser cures to a depth of **0.33 mm**, meaning if the layer thickness is set to 0.20 mm, there is enough margin to ensure proper inter-layer bonding.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Numerical Tip:** Most KTU problems use powers of 3 (e.g., 9, 27, 81) for the energy ratio so that $\ln$ evaluates to a clean integer multiple of $\ln 3 \approx 1.099$. Practice recognizing these "KTU-friendly" numbers.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Process Parameters on $C_d$

| Process Parameter | Direction of Change | Effect on $C_d$ | Physical Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Laser Power ($P$) ↑** | Increase | $C_d$ increases | More photons per second → higher $E$ |
| **Scan Speed ($v$) ↑** | Increase | $C_d$ decreases | Laser spends less time on each point → lower $E$ |
| **Hatch Spacing ($h_s$) ↑** | Increase | No direct effect, but over-cure overlap reduces | $E$ is per area, not per hatch |
| **Layer Thickness ($\Delta z$) ↑** | Increase | Needs higher $E$ to maintain bonding | Thicker layer needs deeper cure |
| **Resin $D_p$ ↑** (more transparent) | Increase | $C_d$ increases for same $E$ | Photons reach deeper before absorption |
| **Resin $E_c$ ↑** (harder to cure) | Increase | $C_d$ decreases for same $E$ | More energy is wasted just to start curing |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SLA Process Block Diagram (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start: CAD Model] --> A2[STL File Export]
    A2 --> A3[Slicer Software]
    A3 --> A4[Layer Slice Data]
    A4 --> A5[Galvanometer Control Unit]
    A5 --> A6[UV Laser Nd YVO4 355nm]
    A6 --> A7[Scanning Mirrors X Y]
    A7 --> A8[Vat of Liquid Photopolymer]
    A8 --> A9[Recoater Blade Sweeps New Layer]
    A9 --> A10[Build Platform Lower Delta Z]
    A10 --> A11{All Slices Cured?}
    A11 -- No --> A9
    A11 -- Yes --> A12[Part Removal from Platform]
    A12 --> A13[IPA Rinse to Remove Resin]
    A13 --> A14[UV Post Cure Oven 60 to 80 min]
    A14 --> A15[Finished SLA Part Ready]
    A15 --> A16[End]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an SLA Machine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CONTROL[Control Subsystem]
        CMP[Industrial PC with Slicer]
        SCN[Sliced Scan Vector Generator]
        CMP --> SCN
    end
    subgraph OPTICAL[Optical Subsystem]
        LSR[UV Laser Source 355nm]
        GALV[Galvanometer Scanner X and Y]
        FOC[F Theta Focusing Lens]
        LSR --> GALV
        GALV --> FOC
    end
    subgraph MECH[Mechatronic Subsystem]
        VAT[Resin Vat]
        REC[Recoater Blade]
        PLT[Z Elevator Build Platform]
        VAT --> REC
        REC --> PLT
    end
    subgraph PROC[Process Chamber]
        RES[Liquid Photopolymer]
        CUR[Solidified Cured Layer]
        RES --> CUR
    end
    SCN --> GALV
    CMP --> PLT
    CMP --> REC
    FOC --> RES
    CUR --> PLT
```

## 4.3 Schematic Showing "Top-Down" vs "Bottom-Up" SLA Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TOPDOWN[Top Down Configuration Classical]
        TD1[UV Laser from above] --> TD2[Scans on Resin Top Surface]
        TD2 --> TD3[Platform moves downward into Vat]
        TD3 --> TD4[Used in large industrial systems]
    end
    subgraph BOTTOMUP[Bottom Up Configuration Modern]
        BU1[UV Laser from below] --> BU2[Passes through Transparent Vat Bottom]
        BU2 --> BU3[Platform moves upward out of Vat]
        BU3 --> BU4[Used in desktop Formlabs and Peopoly]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The examiner frequently asks students to draw the schematic of an SLA machine. Always label: **(1)** UV laser, **(2)** Galvo-mirror, **(3)** F-θ lens, **(4)** Recoater, **(5)** Vat, **(6)** Build platform with direction of motion indicated. A neat, well-labeled diagram carries **3–4 marks** by itself.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Stereo-lithography. Mention any two of its industrial applications. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]**

**Model Answer:**

Stereo-lithography (SLA) is a **vat photopolymerization** based additive manufacturing process in which a **UV laser** selectively photo-cures a **liquid photopolymer resin** layer by layer to build a solid 3D object from a CAD model. (2 marks)

**Two industrial applications:**
1. **Rapid prototyping** in the automotive industry (concept models of car dashboards, bumpers).
2. **Investment casting patterns** in the jewellery and dental industries (crowns, bridges, ornaments). (1 mark for any two correct applications)

> [!NOTE]
> Award full marks even if the student writes "model-making" or "medical implants" as an application.

---

### Q2. List the four main process parameters that affect the curing depth in SLA. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

The four main process parameters affecting the curing depth $C_d$ are:
1. **Laser Power ($P$)** — higher power gives more energy.
2. **Scan Speed ($v$)** — slower scan gives longer exposure time.
3. **Hatch Spacing ($h_s$)** — controls overlap between adjacent scan lines.
4. **Layer Thickness ($\Delta z$)** — must be less than the curing depth for bonding.

(1 mark for naming any three, ½ mark each; 3 marks for all four with brief explanation)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the working principle of the **Stereo-lithography process** with a neat, labeled schematic diagram. Discuss the role of the **photo-initiator** in the resin. [7 Marks] **[KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle (5 marks for explanation + diagram):**

1. A **CAD model** of the desired part is created and converted into an **STL file**, which is then sliced into 2D cross-sections by the slicer software.
2. A vat is filled with a **liquid photopolymer resin** containing dissolved **photo-initiators** (e.g., BAPO, TPO).
3. A **recoater blade** spreads a uniform, thin layer (typically 0.1 mm) of resin onto the **build platform** (or onto the previously cured layer).
4. A **UV laser beam** (wavelength ~355 nm from a frequency-tripled Nd:YVO₄ solid-state laser) is directed by a pair of **galvanometer-controlled scanning mirrors** through an **F-θ lens** onto the resin surface.
5. The laser traces the **scan vectors** (contour + hatches) of the current slice. Wherever it strikes, the **photo-initiator absorbs the UV photon** and generates **free radicals**.
6. These free radicals attack the **C=C double bonds** of the **acrylate/epoxy monomers**, initiating **chain-growth polymerization**. The liquid monomers link into a cross-linked **solid polymer network**.
7. After a layer is fully cured, the **build platform lowers by one layer thickness** $\Delta z$, fresh resin flows in, and the next layer is cured.
8. The process repeats until the entire part is built. The part is then removed, **rinsed with IPA**, and **post-cured in a UV chamber** to achieve full mechanical strength.

**Schematic (to be drawn in exam):**

```
        UV Laser (top)
            |
        [Galvo Mirror X-Y]
            |
        [F-θ Lens]
            |
        ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~  <- Recoated resin layer (to be cured)
        [//// Cured ////]  <- Previously cured layer
        [//// Cured ////]
            |
        [Build Platform]  (moves DOWN by Δz)
        =====================  <- Vat bottom
```

**Role of the Photo-initiator (2 marks):**

The photo-initiator is a **specially designed organic molecule** (e.g., BAPO, Irgacure 819) that has a strong **absorption band in the UV region (~355 nm)**. When it absorbs a UV photon, it undergoes **homolytic bond cleavage** to produce **reactive free radicals**. These radicals are the **catalysts** that attack the monomer's C=C bonds and start the polymerization chain reaction. Without a photo-initiator, the resin would remain liquid even under intense UV exposure, since pure monomers cannot directly absorb UV light efficiently.

---

**(b)** Derive **Jacob's equation** for curing depth in stereo-lithography. A photopolymer resin has a penetration depth of $D_p = 0.20 \text{ mm}$ and a critical exposure $E_c = 12 \text{ mJ/cm}^2$. If the laser exposure is $E = 108 \text{ mJ/cm}^2$, calculate the curing depth. [7 Marks] **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Step 1: By the **Beer–Lambert law**, the intensity at depth $z$ in the resin is:

$$
I(z) = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha z}
$$

Step 2: The **penetration depth** $D_p$ is defined as the depth at which $I = I_0 / e$, which gives:

$$
D_p = \dfrac{1}{\alpha}
$$

Step 3: The energy delivered to depth $z$ for an exposure time $t_{exp}$ is:

$$
E(z) = I(z) \cdot t_{exp} = I_0 \, t_{exp} \cdot e^{-\alpha z} = E \cdot e^{-\alpha z}
$$

Step 4: Curing occurs when the energy at depth $C_d$ just equals the critical energy $E_c$:

$$
E \cdot e^{-\alpha C_d} = E_c
$$

Step 5: Rearranging and taking the natural log:

$$
e^{-\alpha C_d} = \dfrac{E_c}{E}
$$

$$
-\alpha C_d = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_c}{E}\right) = -\ln\!\left(\dfrac{E}{E_c}\right)
$$

Step 6: Substituting $1/\alpha = D_p$:

$$
\boxed{C_d = D_p \, \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E}{E_c}\right)}
$$

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

Given: $D_p = 0.20 \text{ mm}$, $E_c = 12 \text{ mJ/cm}^2$, $E = 108 \text{ mJ/cm}^2$.

$$
C_d = 0.20 \times \ln\!\left(\dfrac{108}{12}\right) = 0.20 \times \ln(9)
$$

Since $\ln(9) = 2.1972$:

$$
C_d = 0.20 \times 2.1972 = 0.4394 \text{ mm} \approx \mathbf{0.44 \text{ mm}}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the various **subsystems of an SLA machine**. Mention the function of the **galvanometer scanner** and the **F-θ lens**. [7 Marks] **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**Subsystems of an SLA Machine (block diagram, 4 marks):**

```
[CAD/CAM PC]  →  [Slicer Software]  →  [Galvanometer Controller]
                                            |
                                            ↓
[UV Laser Source]  →  [Beam Shaper]  →  [Galvo X-Y Mirrors]  →  [F-θ Lens]  →  [Resin Vat]
                                                                                  ↑
                            [Build Platform Z-axis]  ←  [Stepper Motor Controller] ┘
                                            ↓
                                  [Post-Process: IPA + UV Cure]
```

The four key subsystems are:
1. **Optical Subsystem:** UV laser, beam expander, galvanometer scanner, F-θ lens.
2. **Mechatronic Subsystem:** Build platform, Z-axis stepper, recoater blade.
3. **Control Subsystem:** Industrial PC, slicer, scan vector generator, motor drivers.
4. **Process Chamber:** Vat, resin, cured part stack.

**Function of the Galvanometer Scanner (1.5 marks):**

A **galvanometer scanner** consists of two small, fast-rotating mirrors mounted on servo motors (one for the X-axis, one for the Y-axis). It **deflects the stationary UV laser beam** at very high speeds (up to several m/s) to "draw" the 2D pattern of each slice on the resin surface. This eliminates the need to physically move the heavy laser source.

**Function of the F-θ Lens (1.5 marks):**

The **F-θ (F-theta) lens** is a specialized flat-field scanning lens. It ensures that the laser beam is **focused to a constant spot size** across the entire build area. Without an F-θ lens, the focal point would curve (barrel distortion) and energy density would vary, causing non-uniform curing. The F-θ lens delivers a **linear relationship** between the mirror scan angle $\theta$ and the spot position $y$ on the focal plane: $y = f \cdot \theta$, ensuring uniform energy delivery.

---

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages, limitations, and applications** of stereo-lithography. Compare it briefly with **Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM)**. [7 Marks] **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages of SLA (3 marks):**
1. **High accuracy and excellent surface finish** — layer lines are barely visible; surface roughness Ra as low as 0.5 µm.
2. **Wide material range** — many photopolymers available (tough, clear, flexible, biocompatible, castable, high-temperature).
3. **Complex internal geometries** — since the resin is liquid, delicate lattices, internal channels, and hollow structures can be built without support issues inside the part.
4. **Fast for small parts** — can produce a small detailed part in 1–3 hours.

**Limitations of SLA (2 marks):**
1. **Limited mechanical strength** — cured photopolymers are brittle and not as strong as injection-molded plastics or FDM parts in engineering polymers like ABS.
2. **Post-processing required** — IPA wash and UV post-cure add time and labour.
3. **Resin is toxic and expensive** — uncured resin is a skin/eye irritant; resin costs ₹8,000–₹15,000/litre.
4. **Long post-cure leads to dimensional shrinkage (~1–2 %).**

**Applications (1 mark):** Automotive prototyping, dental aligners, jewellery patterns, medical anatomical models, microfluidics, investment casting, optics (transparent parts).

**Comparison with FDM (1 mark):**

| Parameter | SLA | FDM |
|---|---|---|
| **State of material** | Liquid photopolymer | Solid thermoplastic filament |
| **Energy source** | UV laser | Hot-end extruder (heat) |
| **Surface finish** | Very smooth | Visible layer lines |
| **Mechanical strength** | Moderate, brittle | High (ABS, Nylon, PC) |
| **Material cost** | High (resin) | Low (filament) |
| **Support structure** | Minimal | Mandatory for overhangs |
| **Speed** | Slower | Faster |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** When comparing SLA with FDM, students frequently **omit the state of the material** and **the energy source** from their answer. Always state these as the *first* line of comparison — they are the two most fundamental differences. Also, do **not** write "SLA is better than FDM" or vice-versa in a KTU answer — the examiner expects an **objective** comparison, not a value judgement. Writing subjective opinions leads to a **0.5–1 mark deduction**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullet Points for KTU Viva / ESE:**

- **Inventor:** Chuck W. Hull, **1984**, patented 1986, **3D Systems Corporation**.
- **Classification:** ISO/ASTM 52900 — **Vat Photopolymerization**.
- **Working principle:** UV laser cures liquid photopolymer layer by layer.
- **Typical laser:** Frequency-tripled **Nd:YVO₄**, wavelength **355 nm**.
- **Key reaction:** **Photo-polymerization** triggered by **photo-initiator** (e.g., BAPO, TPO).
- **Jacob's Equation:** $C_d = D_p \ln(E/E_c)$ — **must be memorized and derivable**.
- **$D_p$** = penetration depth (material property, in mm).
- **$E_c$** = critical exposure (minimum energy to start curing).
- **Energy delivered:** $E = P / (v \cdot h_s)$ — laser power per unit scan rate and hatch spacing.
- **Subsystems:** Optical, Mechatronic, Control, Process Chamber.
- **Galvanometer scanner:** Two fast X-Y mirrors deflect the laser beam.
- **F-θ lens:** Maintains constant spot size across build area.
- **Post-processing:** **IPA rinse** + **UV oven post-cure**.
- **Shrinkage:** ~1–2 % after post-cure.
- **Supports:** Required for overhangs > 45° in SLA (similar to FDM).
- **Materials:** Acrylate-based and epoxy-based photopolymers.
- **Build orientation:** Affects strength (Z-axis is weaker due to layer interfaces).
- **Resolution:** ~25–50 µm in X-Y, ~50–100 µm in Z.
- **Key competitors:** FDM (FFF), SLS, MJF, DLP, PolyJet — but **SLA** is the **original** 3D printing process.
- **Common KTU answer opener:** "SLA is a vat photopolymerization process that uses a UV laser to selectively cure a photopolymer resin layer by layer, following the 2D contours generated by slicing a 3D CAD model."
- **Common KTU diagram labels (must appear in any sketch):** UV laser, galvo mirror, F-θ lens, recoater, vat, build platform (Z-axis), direction of platform motion (downward), cured layer stack.
- **Numerical safety check:** Always verify $E > E_c$ before applying Jacob's equation; if $E \le E_c$, the resin will not cure and $C_d = 0$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LIGA Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBMRT404 - MANUFACTURING PROCESS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** LIGA Process # 🏭 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **LIGA** is a German acronym derived from the three core steps of the process: **L**ithographie (Lithography), **G**alvanoformung (Electroforming/Electroplating), and **A**bformung (Molding). It is a **microfabrication technology** used to manufacture high-aspect-ratio **micro-electro-mechanical systems (MEMS)** structures with feature heights ranging from **several micrometers to several millimeters** and lateral resolutions down to the sub-micrometer range. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the LIGA process is classified as a **non-conventional / advanced microfabrication process** under the umbrella of *micro-manufacturing technologies*. It is specifically designed to produce **3D microstructures** that combine extreme **structural heights (≥ 100 µm)** with **sub-micron lateral precision** — a feat that conventional surface-micromachining or bulk-micromachining (wet/dry etching) cannot achieve economically. > [!NOTE] > **Key Performance Metrics of the LIGA Process (Industry Standards):** > - **Structural Height:** 10 µm – 3000 µm > - **Lateral Resolution:** 0.2 µm – 1 µm > - **Aspect Ratio:** Up to **100:1** (and beyond, theoretically > 500:1) > - **Sidewall Roughness:** < 50 nm (using X-ray lithography) > - **Material Compatibility:** Polymers, metals (Ni, Cu, Au), ceramics, alloys ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: "The Miniature Skyscraper Builder"** 🏗️ > > Imagine you want to build a **perfectly vertical miniature skyscraper** on a flat plate, with a height of 1 cm but a width of only 10 micrometers (about 1/10th the diameter of a human hair). A normal chisel or saw would snap such a thin, tall structure. LIGA solves this by: > 1. **Pouring a liquid plastic (resist)** onto a base plate — think of this as pouring cake batter into a baking tray. > 2. **Using a powerful "shadow projector" (X-rays)** from a synchrotron to "cook" (cross-link) only the parts you want to keep, leaving the rest as soft, unexposed batter. > 3. **Washing away the soft batter** with a developer — just like carving a canal in jelly, leaving tall, thin standing walls. > 4. **Plating a metal "skeleton"** into these canals using electrochemistry — like electroplating gold onto a ring, but filling deep trenches. > 5. **Using the metal skeleton as a mold insert** to mass-produce thousands of plastic copies by injection molding or hot embossing. > > The result: **cheap, mass-producible micro-parts** that retain the original's nanoscale precision. ## 1.3 Historical Context & Why LIGA Exists Conventional photolithography is limited to feature depths of only a few microns because **UV light** is absorbed by the photoresist near the top surface, leaving deeper layers underdeveloped. The LIGA process overcomes this by employing **high-energy, parallel X-ray beams from a synchrotron radiation source**, which possess extremely short wavelengths (typically **0.2 – 0.5 nm**) and immense penetration depth, enabling uniform exposure of resist layers hundreds of microns thick. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Not Just Use Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE)?** While DRIE can achieve aspect ratios of ~50:1 in silicon, it is **material-limited** (only works on silicon), produces **tapered sidewalls (scalloping)**, and has **high capital cost**. LIGA is **material-agnostic** for the final product and produces **perfectly vertical, smooth sidewalls**, making it indispensable for metallic, ceramic, and polymer microstructures. --- # ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three (Sometimes Four) Stages of LIGA The classical LIGA process is a sequential, multi-stage manufacturing workflow. Each stage has a distinct physical principle and engineering function. ### **Stage 1: L — Lithographie (X-Ray Deep Lithography)** This is the **pattern-definition stage**, where the geometric template is created. 1. **Substrate Preparation:** A conductive substrate (typically a **titanium or gold-coated silicon wafer**, or a polished metal plate) is cleaned and primed with an adhesion promoter. 2. **Resist Coating:** A thick layer of **PMMA (Polymethyl Methacrylate)** or a negative-tone resist (e.g., SU-8) is bonded/cast onto the substrate. Thickness can vary from **10 µm to 3000 µm**. 3. **X-Ray Exposure:** The resist is irradiated through a **mask** (similar to a photolithography mask, but with X-ray absorber patterns of **gold or tungsten** on a thin membrane of **beryllium, silicon, or graphite**). 4. **Development:** Exposed (or unexposed, depending on tone) resist is dissolved in a chemical developer (e.g., **GG developer**: 2-(2-butoxyethoxy)ethanol, morpholine, ethanolamine, and DI water for PMMA). > [!NOTE] > **Why X-rays from a synchrotron?** > - Wavelength: **λ ≈ 0.1 – 1 nm** (much shorter than UV's 365 nm) → negligible diffraction at micron-scale features. > - High photon energy (**1 – 20 keV**) → deep penetration through thick resist with minimal absorption variation. > - High collimation (parallel beam) → straight, vertical sidewalls. ### **Stage 2: G — Galvanoformung (Electroforming)** This is the **metal-filling stage**, where the lithographic template is converted into a metallic tool. 1. The developed substrate (now containing resist cavities down to the conductive base layer) is immersed in an **electrolytic bath** (e.g., **nickel sulfamate** solution for Ni deposition, or **copper sulfate** for Cu). 2. The conductive base layer acts as the **cathode**, and a metal plate acts as the **anode**. 3. **Faraday's laws of electrolysis** govern metal ion reduction, and metal atoms are deposited preferentially inside the cavities from the bottom upward. 4. Process parameters: **current density 1 – 20 A/dm²**, **pH 3.5 – 4.5**, **temperature 40 – 60 °C**. 5. Deposition continues until metal **overfills** the cavities. ### **Stage 3: A — Abformung (Molding)** This is the **replication stage**, where the metal mold is used for mass production. 1. The overplated metal layer is **lapped and polished** to create a flat surface (the "primary" or "master" mold). 2. This mold is then used in one of several replication techniques: - **Hot Embossing** (thermoplastic polymers like PMMA, COC, PC) - **Injection Molding** (high-volume production) - **Reaction Injection Molding (RIM)** for ceramics/polymers 3. A **secondary metallic mold insert** can also be made from the first plastic replica via a second electroforming step (called the "**LIGA family tree**" or **"Lost Mold" process**). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Optional Stage 4 — Sacrificial Layer Release / Assembly:** > After molding, the parts are demolded, and any sacrificial layers (used to define movable MEMS elements) are etched away, followed by dicing, packaging, and assembly. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | **Parameter** | **Formula / Expression** | **Units** | **Engineering Significance** | |---------------|--------------------------|-----------|------------------------------| | **Aspect Ratio (AR)** | $AR = \dfrac{H}{W}$ | dimensionless | Quality metric for microstructure verticality | | **Synchrotron Wavelength** | $\lambda = \dfrac{h \cdot c}{E_{photon}}$ | nm, Å | Determines diffraction limit; $h$ = Planck's constant, $c$ = speed of light | | **Photon Energy** | $E_{photon} = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda(\text{nm})}$ | eV | Penetration depth into resist | | **X-Ray Mass Attenuation Coefficient** | $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu \rho t}$ | cm⁻¹, g/cm³, cm | Beer–Lambert law for resist absorption; $\mu/\rho$ = mass attenuation | | **Resist Exposure Dose Threshold** | $D_{min} \approx 1\text{–}4 \text{ kJ/cm}^3$ | J/cm³ | Minimum energy to fully crosslink/cleave PMMA | | **Sidewall Angle** | $\theta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta W}{H}\right)$ | degrees | Ideally $90°$ for LIGA; $\Delta W$ = lateral undercut | | **Electroplating Rate (Faraday)** | $v = \dfrac{j \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | µm/min | $j$ = current density, $M$ = molar mass, $n$ = electrons, $F$ = Faraday constant | | **Mold Insert Cycle Time** | $t_{cycle} = t_{fill} + t_{cool} + t_{eject}$ | seconds | Determines throughput in replication | | **Replication Factor** | $N_{replicas} = \dfrac{T_{tool}}{t_{cycle}}$ | parts | Number of parts per mold lifetime | | **LIGA Resolution Limit** | $R_{min} \approx \sqrt{\lambda \cdot d}$ | µm | Rayleigh-type criterion; $d$ = resist thickness | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL FORMULA — Electroplating Rate (Faraday's First Law in Engineering Form):** > > The deposition thickness rate of metal per unit time, derived directly from Faraday's laws of electrolysis, is given by: > > $$v = \frac{j \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$ > > Where: > - $v$ = linear deposition rate (m/s) > - $j$ = current density (A/m²) > - $M$ = molar mass of deposited metal (kg/mol) > - $n$ = number of electrons exchanged in the reduction reaction > - $F$ = Faraday's constant $\approx$ **96485 C/mol** > - $\rho$ = density of deposited metal (kg/m³) ## 2.3 Why This Matters in Engineering > [!NOTE] > **Industrial & Research Applications of LIGA:** > - **Automotive:** Fuel injector nozzles, pressure sensors (Bosch, Siemens). > - **Telecommunications:** RF switches, optical fiber alignment structures. > - **Medical:** Microfluidic devices, drug-delivery microreservoirs, neural probes. > - **Aerospace:** High-aspect-ratio inertial sensors, gyroscopes. > - **Consumer Electronics:** Inkjet printer heads (Hewlett-Packard pioneered LIGA for this). > - **X-Ray Optics:** LIGA-fabricated nickel zone plates for synchrotron beamlines. --- # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Process Analysis > [!NOTE] > The following analytical matrix compares the four major microfabrication techniques. This is a **highly-tested comparative question** in KTU examinations and a favorite of evaluators. | **Attribute** | **LIGA (X-Ray)** | **DRIE (Silicon)** | **UV Photolithography** | **Micro-EDM** | |---------------|------------------|---------------------|--------------------------|----------------| | **Max Aspect Ratio** | **100:1 to 500:1** | 30:1 to 50:1 | 2:1 to 5:1 | 10:1 to 20:1 | | **Lateral Resolution** | **0.2 – 1 µm** | 1 – 5 µm | 0.5 – 5 µm | 5 – 20 µm | | **Structural Depth** | **10 µm – 3000 µm** | 5 – 500 µm | 0.1 – 5 µm | 10 – 500 µm | | **Sidewall Quality** | **Excellent (Ra < 50 nm)** | Scalloped | Good | Recast layer present | | **Material Freedom** | **Polymers, metals, ceramics** | Silicon only | Photoresist only | Conductive only | | **Capital Cost** | Very High (synchrotron) | High | Low | Medium | | **Production Scale** | Mass via molding | Wafer-batch | Mass | Prototype/Low | | **Sidewall Angle** | **~90° (vertical)** | 88°–92° | Tapered | Variable | ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Electroplating Rate Calculation > [!NOTE] > **Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern):** > In a LIGA process, **nickel (Ni)** is to be electroplated into PMMA resist cavities of depth **500 µm**. The electroplating bath operates at a current density of **5 A/dm²**. Calculate the time required to completely fill the cavities. > > **Given Data:** > - Current density, $j$ = 5 A/dm² = 50 A/m² > - Target depth, $H$ = 500 µm = $5 \times 10^{-4}$ m > - Molar mass of Ni, $M$ = 58.69 g/mol = 0.05869 kg/mol > - Valence (electrons exchanged), $n$ = 2 (Ni²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Ni) > - Density of Ni, $\rho$ = 8900 kg/m³ > - Faraday's constant, $F$ = 96485 C/mol **Step 1 — Apply Faraday's First Law (Engineering Form):** $$\begin{aligned} v &= \frac{j \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho} \\ v &= \frac{50 \, \text{A/m}^2 \times 0.05869 \, \text{kg/mol}}{2 \times 96485 \, \text{C/mol} \times 8900 \, \text{kg/m}^3} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Compute Numerator:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Numerator} &= 50 \times 0.05869 = 2.9345 \, \text{A} \cdot \text{kg} / (\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{mol}) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Compute Denominator:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Denominator} &= 2 \times 96485 \times 8900 \\ &= 1.7174 \times 10^9 \, \text{C} \cdot \text{kg} / (\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{mol}) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Compute Linear Deposition Rate:** $$\begin{aligned} v &= \frac{2.9345}{1.7174 \times 10^9} \, \text{m/s} \\ v &= 1.7086 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m/s} \\ v &\approx 1.71 \, \text{nm/s} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Convert to µm/hour for engineering utility:** $$\begin{aligned} v &= 1.7086 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m/s} \times \frac{10^6 \, \mu m}{1 \, m} \times \frac{3600 \, s}{1 \, h} \\ v &= 6.151 \, \mu m / h \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6 — Time Required to Fill 500 µm Cavity:** $$\begin{aligned} t &= \frac{H}{v} = \frac{500 \, \mu m}{6.151 \, \mu m / h} \\ t &= 81.29 \, h \\ t &\approx 3.4 \, \text{days} \end{aligned}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Interpretation:** The fill time is governed linearly by the inverse of current density. Doubling the current density to **10 A/dm²** halves the fill time to ~40.6 h. However, excessively high current densities cause **dendritic growth, hydrogen embrittlement, and poor surface finish** — a classic KTU-style trade-off question. ## 3.3 X-Ray Penetration Depth Derivation The intensity of X-rays transmitted through a resist of thickness $t$ is given by the **Beer–Lambert Law**: $$\begin{aligned} I &= I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu \rho t} \end{aligned}$$ Where: - $I_0$ = incident X-ray intensity - $\mu$ = mass attenuation coefficient (cm²/g) — depends on photon energy and resist composition - $\rho$ = density of resist (g/cm³) - $t$ = resist thickness (cm) **Practical LIGA Rule of Thumb:** For PMMA at synchrotron photon energies of **2 – 10 keV**, the term $\mu \rho$ is small enough that exposure dose varies by less than 10% across a 500 µm thick resist, ensuring **uniform sidewall development** — this is the fundamental advantage over UV lithography. > [!NOTE] > **Critical Dose Relationship:** The minimum resist exposure dose is related to the transmitted intensity and exposure time $t_e$ by: > > $$D_{abs} = I \cdot t_e \geq D_{min}$$ > > For PMMA, $D_{min} \approx 1 \text{ kJ/cm}^3$ (for bond cleavage in positive tone). Below this threshold, the resist remains undeveloped. --- # 📊 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of the LIGA Process ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Substrate Preparation
Conductive Base Layer Au or Ti] --> B[Resist Casting
PMMA Layer 10 to 3000 micrometer] B --> C[Synchrotron X-Ray Exposure
Through X-Ray Mask] C --> D[Chemical Development
GG Developer Removes Soluble Regions] D --> E[Resist Mold Cavities Formed
Aspect Ratio up to 100 colon 1] E --> F[Electroforming Stage
Nickel or Copper Deposition via Faraday] F --> G[Metal Overplate and Lapping
Flat Primary Mold Created] G --> H[Molding Stage Selection] H --> I1[Hot Embossing] H --> I2[Injection Molding] H --> I3[Reaction Injection Molding] I1 --> J[Polymer Micro Parts Demolded] I2 --> J I3 --> J J --> K[Optional Secondary Electroforming
for Metallic Inserts] K --> L[Final Micro Component
Ready for Assembly and Packaging] ``` ## 4.2 X-Ray Mask Architecture (LIGA Sub-System) ```mermaid flowchart LR M1[Synchrotron Beam Source
Electron Storage Ring] --> M2[Be or Si Membrane
Thickness 2 to 5 micrometer] M2 --> M3[Au or W Absorber Pattern
Thickness 10 to 30 micrometer] M3 --> M4[PMMA Resist on Conductive Substrate] M4 --> M5[Patterned Resist Profile] ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow — LIGA Process Stages with Parameters ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Stage L Lithography] --> P2[X-Ray Photon Energy 1 to 20 keV] P1 --> P3[Resist Type PMMA Positive Tone] P1 --> P4[Resolution 0.2 micrometer] P2 --> P5[Stage G Galvanoformung] P3 --> P5 P4 --> P5 P5 --> P6[Current Density 1 to 20 A per dm squared] P5 --> P7[Ni or Cu Electrolyte] P5 --> P8[Filling Rate 1 to 10 micrometer per hour] P6 --> P9[Stage A Abformung] P7 --> P9 P8 --> P9 P9 --> P10[Cycle Time 30 to 300 seconds] P9 --> P11[Mold Lifetime 1 million plus cycles] P10 --> P12[Mass Produced Micro Component] P11 --> P12 ``` --- # 🎓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 1]** *(3 Marks)* > **Q: Define the acronym "LIGA" and state the three primary stages of the process.** > > **Model Answer:** > LIGA stands for **L**ithographie (Lithography), **G**alvanoformung (Electroforming), and **A**bformung (Molding). The three stages are: **(1)** X-ray deep lithography to pattern a thick resist; **(2)** electroforming to deposit metal into the resist cavities; **(3)** molding to mass-produce replicas from the metallic mold. *(3 marks — full credit)* > **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 2]** *(3 Marks)* > **Q: Why is synchrotron X-ray radiation used in the LIGA process instead of conventional UV light?** > > **Model Answer:** > Synchrotron X-rays have very **short wavelengths (0.1 – 1 nm)**, providing diffraction-limited resolution at sub-micron scales, and **high photon energy (1 – 20 keV)** enabling deep penetration through thick resist layers (100s of µm) with uniform exposure. UV light is absorbed near the resist surface, limiting depth to a few microns. *(3 marks)* ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice > **[KTU University Exam - Model Question A]** *(14 Marks)* > > **(a)** Explain in detail the **X-ray lithography stage** of the LIGA process, including mask design, resist selection, and the role of synchrotron radiation. *(7 marks)* > > **(b)** Describe the **electroforming (Galvanoformung) stage** with a neat sketch of the setup. Derive the expression for the linear deposition rate using Faraday's law. *(7 marks)* ### Model Solution — Part (a): The X-ray lithography stage begins with the **substrate preparation**, where a polished metal or silicon wafer is coated with a thin conductive adhesion layer (typically **Au or Ti, ~20 nm**). A thick layer of **PMMA (positive-tone resist)** is then cast or laminated onto the substrate to a controlled thickness ranging from **10 µm to 3000 µm** depending on the application. An **X-ray mask** is fabricated separately: a thin membrane of **beryllium (2 – 5 µm)** or silicon is patterned with a high-Z absorber (gold or tungsten) of thickness 10 – 30 µm. The mask membrane is transparent to X-rays while the absorber blocks them. The resist-coated substrate and mask are loaded into a **synchrotron beamline**, where parallel X-ray photons of energy **1 – 20 keV** pass through the mask and selectively expose the PMMA. In positive-tone PMMA, exposed regions undergo **chain scission** (degradation of long polymer chains into short fragments), making them soluble in the GG developer. After exposure, the substrate is immersed in a **chemical developer** that dissolves the exposed regions, leaving behind the unexposed PMMA as the structural template with **near-vertical sidewalls** and **aspect ratios exceeding 100:1**. The development process is diffusion-limited and must be carefully timed to avoid undercutting. *(7 marks — full credit if mask, resist, exposure, and development are discussed)* ### Model Solution — Part (b): The electroforming stage converts the polymer resist template into a **robust metallic mold** through electrolytic deposition. The developed substrate (now with cavities exposing the conductive base) is immersed in an **electrolytic bath** — typically a **nickel sulfamate solution** (Ni(NH₂SO₃)₂) for nickel deposition, maintained at **40 – 60 °C** and **pH 3.5 – 4.5**. The conductive base layer is connected to the **negative terminal (cathode)** of a DC power supply, while a nickel plate serves as the **anode**. When current is applied (current density **1 – 20 A/dm²**), Ni²⁺ ions migrate to the cathode and reduce to metallic Ni atoms, filling the cavities **from the bottom upward**. **Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis** states that the mass $m$ of substance deposited is directly proportional to the charge $Q$ passed: $$m = \frac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \frac{j \cdot A \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$ The **linear deposition rate** $v$ (rate of thickness increase) is obtained by dividing the mass by the cross-sectional area $A$ and the density $\rho$: $$\begin{aligned} v = \frac{m}{A \cdot \rho} = \frac{j \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho} \end{aligned}$$ Differentiating with respect to time gives the **instantaneous rate of thickness build-up**: $$\boxed{v = \frac{j \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}}$$ This is the **standard KTU formula for electroplating rate**. Deposition continues until the metal **overfills** the cavities, and the excess is then lapped and polished to produce a flat primary mold surface. *(7 marks)* > **[KTU University Exam - Model Question B — Alternative Choice]** *(14 Marks)* > > **(a)** Discuss the **molding (Abformung) stage** of LIGA in detail, comparing hot embossing and injection molding. What are the typical cycle times and part tolerances? *(7 marks)* > > **(b)** Compare the **LIGA process** with **Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE)** for high-aspect-ratio microfabrication across at least six parameters. State two industrial applications where LIGA is uniquely preferred. *(7 marks)* ### Model Solution — Part (a): The molding (Abformung) stage is the **mass-replication phase** of LIGA, where the metallic primary mold (typically Ni) is used to produce thousands to millions of identical polymer micro-parts. Two principal replication techniques are used: **Hot Embossing:** A thermoplastic polymer pellet (PMMA, COC, PC, or PEEK) is placed on the mold, heated above its **glass transition temperature** ($T_g$), and pressed under high mechanical pressure (**5 – 50 bar**) using a hydraulic press. The polymer flows into the mold cavities, is cooled below $T_g$, and demolded. Typical **cycle times: 60 – 300 seconds**; achievable tolerances: **± 1 – 5 µm**. Hot embossing is best suited for **small-to-medium production volumes** and high-precision prototyping. **Injection Molding:** Polymer granules are melted in a heated barrel (**200 – 350 °C**) and injected under high pressure (**500 – 1500 bar**) into a closed mold cavity. Cooling solidifies the part, and it is ejected. **Cycle times: 5 – 30 seconds**, supporting **high-volume production** (>10⁶ parts/mold). Tolerances are typically **± 5 – 20 µm** due to higher process dynamics. A **secondary electroforming step** can also be performed on a polymer replica to create a **"daughter" metal insert**, enabling the production of metallic micro-parts at scale. *(7 marks — awarded for covering both techniques with cycle times, tolerances, and applications)* ### Model Solution — Part (b): | **Parameter** | **LIGA** | **DRIE** | |---------------|----------|----------| | **Max Aspect Ratio** | 100:1 to 500:1 | 30:1 to 50:1 | | **Lateral Resolution** | 0.2 – 1 µm | 1 – 5 µm | | **Material Compatibility** | Polymers, metals, ceramics, alloys | Silicon only | | **Sidewall Roughness** | < 50 nm (very smooth) | Scalloping (50 – 500 nm) | | **Sidewall Verticality** | ~90° (excellent) | 88° – 92° (slight taper) | | **Capital Cost** | Very high (synchrotron) | High (ICP/RIE tool) | | **Production Mode** | Mass via molding | Wafer-batch (~100s per wafer) | **Two Industrial Applications Where LIGA is Uniquely Preferred:** 1. **Automotive fuel injector nozzles** — require metallic (Ni) components with sub-100 µm features and high aspect ratios for precise spray patterns; DRIE cannot produce metal parts. 2. **X-ray optical components (zone plates)** — require extremely high aspect ratios (>100:1) in gold or nickel for synchrotron beam focusing, infeasible with DRIE. *(7 marks)* > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing the order of stages** — LIGA follows L → G → A strictly. Reversing the order is an instant 2-mark deduction. > 2. **Forgetting the synchrotron** — Students often say "X-ray lithography" without specifying synchrotron radiation. The KTU 2024 rubric awards marks for identifying the source. > 3. **Skipping the unit in electroplating rate derivations** — Always include units (µm/h or nm/s) in the final answer; KTU strictly penalizes missing units. > 4. **Not relating the molding stage to mass production** — A common mistake is to treat the three stages as equal in cost/time. Examiners expect students to highlight that the **molding stage is what makes LIGA economically viable** for mass production. > 5. **Mixing up LIGA with LIGA-like processes** — SU-8 photolithography and UV-LIGA are *related but not identical*; explicitly mention the differences (X-ray source, dose, resolution). ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **📌 High-Density Revision Checklist:** > - ✅ LIGA = **L**ithographie + **G**alvanoformung + **A**bformung. > - ✅ Uses **synchrotron X-rays** (λ = 0.1 – 1 nm, E = 1 – 20 keV) for deep lithography. > - ✅ **PMMA** is the most common positive-tone resist (thickness 10 – 3000 µm). > - ✅ X-ray mask uses **Au or W absorber** on **Be or Si membrane**. > - ✅ Electroforming uses **Faraday's law**: $v = \dfrac{j \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$. > - ✅ Typical current density: **1 – 20 A/dm²**; pH: **3.5 – 4.5**; temperature: **40 – 60 °C**. > - ✅ **Hot embossing** = small batches, ±1 – 5 µm; **Injection molding** = high volume, ±5 – 20 µm. > - ✅ Aspect ratios: LIGA = **100:1 to 500:1**; DRIE = **30:1 to 50:1**; UV = **2:1 to 5:1**. > - ✅ LIGA is **material-agnostic**; DRIE is **silicon-only**. > - ✅ Industrial uses: **fuel injectors, inkjet heads, X-ray optics, neural probes, microfluidics**. > - ✅ Primary advantage of LIGA: **decouples high-cost mask/fabrication from low-cost mass replication**. > - ✅ Beer–Lambert law governs X-ray penetration: $I = I_0 e^{-\mu \rho t}$. > - ✅ Minimum exposure dose for PMMA: **~1 kJ/cm³**. > - ✅ Sidewall verticality of LIGA: **~90°**, sidewall roughness **< 50 nm**. > - ✅ **Secondary electroforming** of polymer replica = "daughter mold" for metallic parts. > - ✅ Developed by **Forschungszentrum Karlsruhe (Germany)** in the 1980s. ---
